Mention the pH range for each.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Alkaline Chlorination Process:** pH = 10.5 – 11.5. 2. **Hydrogen Peroxide Process:** pH = 9.0 – 9.5 (with Cu²⁺ catalyst). 3. **InCo SO₂/Air Process:** pH = 8.0 – 10.0 (with Cu²⁺ catalyst). Other valid methods: AVR (pH 2.0 acidification step) and Biological (pH 7.0 – 8.5). *(1 mark each for naming; 1 mark for correct pH ranges)* ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** > **Q: Why is ORP (Oxidation-Reduction Potential) used as the primary control parameter in alkaline chlorination of cyanide?** **Model Answer:** ORP is used because: 1. **Direct thermodynamic indicator** of the oxidation state — correlates directly with the disappearance of CN⁻ and appearance of CNO⁻. 2. **Setpoint +450 mV** indicates completion of Stage 1 (`CN⁻ → CNO⁻`). 3. **Setpoint +600 mV** indicates completion of Stage 2 (`CNO⁻ → CO₂ + N₂`). 4. **Faster response** than waiting for offline lab cyanide analysis (which takes 1–4 hours). 5. It allows **closed-loop feedback control** of chlorine dosing, preventing both under-dosing (toxic discharge) and over-dosing (waste + corrosion). *(1 mark per point; 3 marks total)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Long Answer (Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** > **Q (a) [7 Marks]:** With the help of a neat process flow diagram, describe the **two-stage alkaline chlorination process** for the destruction of cyanide in industrial effluents. Explain the role of pH and ORP control in each stage. > > **Q (b) [7 Marks]:** A mining plant produces **100 m³/h** of wastewater containing **C_NaCN = 1000 mg/L** (expressed as CN⁻). Calculate (i) the daily chlorine gas requirement in tonnes/day, (ii) the daily NaOH requirement in kg/day, and (iii) the destruction efficiency achieved when residual CN⁻ is reduced to **0.2 mg/L**. #### Part (a) Model Answer (7 marks) 1. **(1.5 marks) PFD Description:** - Influent → Equalization Tank → Reactor R-101 (Stage 1) → Reactor R-102 (Stage 2) → Neutralization Tank → Settler → Effluent. - Reagents: Cl₂ (gas/liquid) and NaOH dosed to both reactors. 2. **(1.5 marks) Stage 1 Chemistry:** - Reaction: `CN⁻ + Cl₂ → CNCl + Cl⁻`, followed by `CNCl + 2OH⁻ → CNO⁻ + Cl⁻ + H₂O`. - pH controlled at **10.5 – 11.5** using NaOH (prevents CNCl release). - ORP target: **+450 mV**. 3. **(1.5 marks) Stage 2 Chemistry:** - Reaction: `2CNO⁻ + 3Cl₂ + 4OH⁻ → 2CO₂ + N₂ + 6Cl⁻ + 2H₂O`. - ORP raised to **+600 mV**. - Final destruction to non-toxic gases. 4. **(1 mark) Instrumentation:** - pH, ORP, flow, temperature transmitters connected to DCS with PID control loops. - Residual CN⁻ analyzer at outlet for discharge compliance. 5. **(1 mark) Safety:** - CNCl gas detector; ESD-01 trip on ORP < 300 mV or pH < 9.5. 6. **(0.5 mark) Diagram:** - A neat block diagram with control loops marked (mandatory for full marks). #### Part (b) Model Answer (7 marks) **Given:** $Q = 100 \, \text{m}^3/\text{h}$, $C_{\text{CN}} = 1000 \, \text{mg/L}$, $C_{\text{out}} = 0.2 \, \text{mg/L}$. **Step 1 — Mass flow rate of CN⁻:** $$ \dot{m}_{\text{CN}} = 100 \times 1000 \, \text{g/h} = 100{,}000 \, \text{g/h} = 100 \, \text{kg/h} $$ **Step 2 — Daily chlorine requirement (using empirical ratio 2.73 : 1):** $$ \dot{m}_{\text{Cl}_2}^{\text{day}} = 100 \, \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{h}} \times 2.73 \times 24 \, \text{h} = 6552 \, \text{kg/day} = 6.552 \, \text{tonnes/day} $$ **Step 3 — Daily NaOH requirement (using empirical ratio 1.05 : 1):** $$ \dot{m}_{\text{NaOH}}^{\text{day}} = 6552 \times 1.05 = 6879.6 \, \text{kg/day} \approx 6880 \, \text{kg/day} $$ **Step 4 — Destruction efficiency:** $$ \eta = \frac{1000 - 0.2}{1000} \times 100 = 99.98 \,\% $$ *(Each sub-part: 1.5 marks for setup, 0.5 mark for units; 1 mark for numerical accuracy)* --- ### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** > **Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Compare the **alkaline chlorination, hydrogen peroxide, and InCo SO₂/air** processes for cyanide destruction on the basis of (i) operating pH, (ii) reagent cost, (iii) by-products, (iv) metal recovery, (v) effluent chloride loading, (vi) reaction time, and (vii) suitability for mining effluents. > > **Q (b) [7 Marks]:** With the help of a labelled block diagram, explain the **instrumentation architecture** (sensor → DCS → final control element) of an alkaline chlorination plant. Mention the interlock conditions for emergency shutdown. #### Part (a) Model Answer (7 marks) Provide a **comparison table** (as shown in Section 3.2 above). Award: - 0.5 mark per parameter × 7 parameters = **3.5 marks**. - 1 mark for logical conclusion ("InCo process is preferred for mining effluents because it tolerates metal-cyanide complexes and produces reusable by-products"). - 1.5 marks for clarity and neatness. - 1 mark for any *additional insight* (e.g., capital cost, safety). #### Part (b) Model Answer (7 marks) 1. **(2 marks) Sensor Layer:** - pH electrode, ORP probe (Pt vs Ag/AgCl), magnetic flowmeter, residual CN⁻ analyzer (amperometric), RTD for temperature, NaOH tank level sensor. 2. **(1.5 marks) Control Layer (DCS):** - Two PID loops for pH (one per reactor). - Two PID loops for ORP (Stage 1 and Stage 2 setpoints). - Cascade control: ORP → master, Cl₂ flow → slave. 3. **(1.5 marks) Final Control Elements:** - FCV-101, FCV-102 (Cl₂ modulating valves). - P-101 (NaOH dosing pump). - Cooling water valve for temperature control. 4. **(2 marks) Interlock Conditions for ESD:** - **ORP < 300 mV** → close Cl₂ valve. - **pH < 9.5** → block NaOH failure → close Cl₂ valve. - **CNCl gas > 0.3 ppm** → full plant shutdown. - **NaOH tank LSL** → alarm + close Cl₂ valve. - **Cooling water failure** → emergency dump to sludge tank. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Cyanide Destruction Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting Units:** Always carry units through calculations. $C_{\text{CN}}$ in mg/L must be converted to g/m³ or kg/h before multiplying by $Q$ in m³/h. Many students lose 1–2 marks here. > > 2. **Mixing Up Empirical vs Theoretical Ratios:** The empirical mass ratio is **2.73 : 1** (with safety factor). The theoretical molar ratio is **5 : 2 = 2.5 moles : 1 mole**, which converts to **6.81 kg Cl₂ / kg CN⁻** (pure stoichiometric). Examiners mark strictly — use the correct ratio for the correct context. > > 3. **Ignoring Stage 1 vs Stage 2 ORP:** A common error is assuming both stages occur at the same ORP. They are distinct (+450 mV and +600 mV). > > 4. **Not Mentioning CNCl Hazard:** In any alkaline chlorination question, if you don't mention **CNCl toxicity at pH < 9.5**, expect to lose 1 mark. > > 5. **Forgetting to Label PFD:** Diagrams must show *all* instrumentation symbols (TT, pH-T, ORP-T, FCV, etc.), not just block names. Unlabelled diagrams fetch partial marks only. > > 6. **Confusing mg/L with ppm:** While numerically equal in dilute aqueous solutions, examiners sometimes expect the correct unit (mg/L for water, ppm for gases/solids). --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Cyanide Destruction Process** - ✅ **Primary Industrial Methods:** Alkaline Chlorination, Peroxide, InCo SO₂/Air, AVR, Biological. - ✅ **Two-Stage Chlorination:** Stage 1 (`CN⁻ → CNO⁻`) at ORP +450 mV; Stage 2 (`CNO⁻ → CO₂ + N₂`) at ORP +600 mV. - ✅ **Critical pH Window for Chlorination:** 10.5 – 11.5 (avoid CNCl gas below pH 9.5). - ✅ **Empirical Reagent Ratios:** Cl₂ : CN⁻ = 2.73 : 1; NaOH : Cl₂ = 1.05 : 1. - ✅ **Discharge Limit:** WAD CN⁻ ≤ 0.2 mg/L (statutory). - ✅ **Key Control Loops:** pH, ORP, Flow, Temperature, Residual CN⁻. - ✅ **CNCl Toxicity:** Volatile, extremely hazardous; TLV = 0.3 ppm; must be hydrolyzed by OH⁻. - ✅ **Catalyst Role:** Cu²⁺ catalyzes both Peroxide and InCo processes. - ✅ **AVR Process:** Recovers (not destroys) NaCN; pH swing between 2–3 and 10–11. - ✅ **Instrumentation Hierarchy:** Field sensors → DCS PID → HMI/SCADA → ERP (Layered architecture). - ✅ **Safety Interlocks:** ORP < 300 mV, pH < 9.5, CNCl detection, cooling failure — all trigger ESD. - ✅ **Industry Applications:** Gold mines (Hutti), electroplating shops, nuclear reprocessing (IGCAR), pharmaceutical effluent treatment. - ✅ **Cohen-Coon Tuning:** Useful for pH loop in chlorination reactor due to large dead time. - ✅ **Series CSTR Optimization:** Practical plants use 2–3 CSTRs in series to reduce reactor volume by ~10×. - ✅ **Comparison Memory Trick:** "**C**hlorination is **C**heap but **C**hloride-loaded; **P**eroxide is **P**ure but **P**ricey; **S**O₂ is **S**mart for **S**ulfide ores" (InCo preferred when metal recovery matters).

topics.title: Batch Oxidation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT864 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN LARGE SCALE INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Batch Oxidation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Batch Oxidation** is a unit operation in chemical process industries in which a precisely measured charge of organic or inorganic substrate is loaded into a stirred-tank reactor, brought to a controlled temperature and pressure, and then contacted with a stoichiometric quantity of an oxidizing agent (oxygen, air, hydrogen peroxide, nitric acid, per-acids, or hypochlorite) for a fixed residence time until the desired conversion, yield, and product selectivity are obtained. After the reaction, the contents are discharged, worked up, and the reactor is recharged for the next cycle.

In KTU 2024 scheme parlance, it falls under the domain of **Reactor Instrumentation and Control** of discontinuous (batch) processes, where the controlled variables are **temperature (T)**, **pressure (P)**, **dissolved oxygen (DO)**, **oxidant feed rate (F)**, **agitator speed (N)**, and **reaction time (t)**, while the manipulated variables are typically **coolant flow rate (F_c)**, **oxidant feed (F_o)**, and **vent position**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Batch oxidation reactions are predominantly **exothermic, gas–liquid, and potentially runaway-prone**. They are textbook examples of why multi-loop cascade control with safety interlocks is mandated in large-scale chemical plants.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are making **tea in a heavy-bottomed brass vessel (chembu)** on a gas stove. You add water, tea leaves, sugar, and milk in a fixed sequence, control the flame to keep the boil gentle, watch the colour rise, and **switch off the stove at the right moment** so that the tea does not overflow or burn. A batch oxidation reactor behaves identically:

- The **brass vessel** is the jacketed stirred-tank reactor.
- The **flame** is the exothermic heat release of the oxidation reaction.
- The **pour stream of milk** is the controlled feed of oxidant (air/O₂/H₂O₂).
- **Stirring** with the ladle corresponds to the impeller that enhances gas–liquid mass transfer.
- **Switching off the stove at the right time** is the temperature/peak-cut safety interlock.

If you add oxidant too fast (pour milk too quickly) or remove heat too slowly, the system “boils over” — chemically, this is a **thermal runaway** leading to vent-stack fires, explosions, or product degradation (over-oxidation, char formation, runaway to CO/CO₂).

### 1.3 Standard Industrial Metrics

| Metric | Typical Value in Industry |
|---|---|
| Operating temperature | **333 K – 473 K (60 °C – 200 °C)** |
| Operating pressure | **1 – 30 bar (g)** |
| Oxidant | O₂, air, H₂O₂, HNO₃, NaOCl, KMnO₄ |
| Reactor volume | **1 m³ – 50 m³** |
| Heat of oxidation | **–200 to –1200 kJ/mol** |
| Adiabatic temperature rise | **100 K – 500 K** (uncontrolled) |

> [!NOTE]
> The **adiabatic temperature rise ($\Delta T_{ad}$)** is the single most important design parameter. If it exceeds the cooling system's removal capacity, the reaction is considered **inherently unsafe** and requires semi-batch (fed-batch) operation, where oxidant is dosed slowly.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Batch and Not Continuous?

Batch oxidation is preferred over continuous reactors when:

1. **Production volume is low to moderate** (specialty chemicals, dyes, pharmaceuticals, agrochemicals).
2. **Multiple products share the same reactor** (multi-purpose plant).
3. **Reaction kinetics are slow**, and selectivity drops with longer residence time.
4. **Raw materials are hazardous**, requiring controlled addition.
5. **Strict product-quality specifications** demand flexible recipe management.

### 2.2 Operational Sequence of a Batch Oxidation Cycle

A typical KTU-board-classified batch oxidation consists of **seven discrete phases**:

- **Phase 1 – Charging:** Substrate, solvent, catalyst loaded into the reactor under inert N₂ blanket.
- **Phase 2 – Heating:** Reactor temperature ramped to the setpoint using steam/heating oil in the jacket.
- **Phase 3 – Pressurization / Inerting:** Vessel pressure brought to design value, oxygen content in vapour space < 4 % LEL.
- **Phase 4 – Reaction (Oxidation):** Oxidant is fed at a controlled rate; temperature and pressure are tightly controlled; this is the **critical control phase**.
- **Phase 5 – Hold / Cook:** Reaction mass is held at temperature to drive conversion to completion.
- **Phase 6 – Cooling:** Reactor cooled to safe discharge temperature.
- **Phase 7 – Discharging:** Vapour space purged, contents transferred to the downstream unit.

### 2.3 Key Control Loops in Batch Oxidation (KTU 2024 Favourite Topic)

A modern Distributed Control System (DCS) implements the following control loops simultaneously:

- **Loop 1 — Cascade T–T_c (Temperature–Coolant):** Reactor temperature (primary) controls coolant valve setpoint (secondary). This is the **most important loop** for exothermic control.
- **Loop 2 — Pressure Control:** Reactor head-pressure control via vent valve.
- **Loop 3 — Oxidant Flow / Sparge Rate Control:** Mass-flow controller on air/O₂ line.
- **Loop 4 — Dissolved Oxygen (DO) Control:** Polarographic or optical DO probe controls sparger air flow.
- **Loop 5 — Agitator Speed Control:** VFD-driven impeller for mass-transfer enhancement.
- **Loop 6 — Reflux / Condenser Duty:** For solvent-recovery condensers.
- **Loop 7 — Ramp-Soak (Recipe) Controller:** Schedules the temperature/feed profile vs. time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 2024 examinations, questions are frequently asked on **why cascade control is used for batch oxidation reactors** — the answer centres on the slow jacket dynamics and the need to reject heat-release disturbances before they affect the reaction temperature.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning / Where Used |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $r_A = -k \, C_A^n$ | Reaction rate for substrate A (nth order) |
| 2 | $k = A \, e^{-E_a / RT}$ | Arrhenius temperature dependence |
| 3 | $\dfrac{dN_A}{dt} = r_A \, V$ | Molar balance for batch reactor (closed system) |
| 4 | $t_{batch} = \displaystyle\int_{C_{A0}}^{C_{Af}} \dfrac{dC_A}{-r_A}$ | Time to reach final conversion $X_f$ |
| 5 | $V \rho C_p \dfrac{dT}{dt} = (-\Delta H_{rxn}) \, r_A V - U A (T - T_c)$ | Unsteady-state energy balance (jacket-cooled) |
| 6 | $\Delta T_{ad} = \dfrac{(-\Delta H_{rxn}) \, C_{A0}}{\rho \, C_p}$ | Adiabatic temperature rise (worst-case) |
| 7 | $Q_{removable} = U A (T - T_c)$ | Heat removal capacity of jacket |
| 8 | $N_{O_2}^{MT} = k_L a \, (C_{O_2}^* - C_{O_2})$ | Gas–liquid mass transfer of oxygen |
| 9 | $\theta_{cool} = \dfrac{V \rho C_p}{U A}$ | Cooling time-constant of jacket system |
| 10 | $\text{MTSR} = T_0 + \Delta T_{ad} \cdot X$ | Maximum Temperature of Synthesis Reaction |

> [!NOTE]
> **MTSR (Maximum Temperature of Synthesis Reaction)** is a KTU-recommended safety metric. If the cooling fails, the temperature will rise to MTSR. If MTSR > decomposition temperature of the mixture, the system must be **dosed with diluent, dumped, or quenched** — not simply cooled.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Molar (Material) Balance for an Isothermal Batch Reactor

Consider a constant-volume batch reactor charged with a single liquid-phase substrate A. No inflow, no outflow. Let $N_A$ be the instantaneous moles of A and $V$ the constant liquid volume.

**Step 1 — General mass balance (accumulation = in − out + generation):**

$$
\dfrac{dN_A}{dt} \;=\; \dot{N}_{A,in} - \dot{N}_{A,out} + r_A \, V
$$

**Step 2 — Apply batch boundary conditions:**

Since the reactor is closed, $\dot{N}_{A,in} = 0$ and $\dot{N}_{A,out} = 0$.

$$
\dfrac{dN_A}{dt} \;=\; r_A \, V
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to concentration using $N_A = C_A V$ with $V$ = constant:**

$$
V \dfrac{dC_A}{dt} \;=\; r_A \, V \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \dfrac{dC_A}{dt} \;=\; r_A
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute first-order oxidation kinetics $r_A = -k C_A$:**

$$
\dfrac{dC_A}{dt} \;=\; -k \, C_A
$$

**Step 5 — Separate variables and integrate from $C_{A0}$ at $t = 0$ to $C_A$ at time $t$:**

$$
\int_{C_{A0}}^{C_A} \dfrac{dC_A}{C_A} \;=\; -k \int_{0}^{t} dt
$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the integrals:**

$$
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_A}{C_{A0}}\right) \;=\; -k t
$$

**Step 7 — Express in terms of fractional conversion $X = 1 - C_A / C_{A0}$:**

$$
C_A \;=\; C_{A0}\,(1 - X)
$$

$$
\ln(1 - X) \;=\; -k t \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; t \;=\; -\dfrac{\ln(1 - X)}{k}
$$

**Engineering implication:** For a desired conversion of **99 %** ($X = 0.99$), $t \approx 4.605 / k$. Halving $k$ doubles the batch time — a key cost driver in pharmaceutical batch oxidation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Unsteady-State Energy Balance (Non-Isothermal Batch)

For a jacketed batch oxidation, the rate of accumulation of sensible heat in the reaction mass equals the heat generated by the reaction minus the heat removed by the jacket.

**Step 1 — Energy balance on the reaction mass (constant volume, no inflow/outflow):**

$$
\dfrac{d}{dt}\!\left(V \rho C_p T\right) \;=\; Q_{gen} - Q_{rem}
$$

**Step 2 — Define each term:**

$$
Q_{gen} = (-\Delta H_{rxn}) \, r_A V, \qquad Q_{rem} = U A (T - T_c)
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate the LHS (assume $V$, $\rho$, $C_p$ constant):**

$$
V \rho C_p \dfrac{dT}{dt} \;=\; (-\Delta H_{rxn}) \, r_A V - U A (T - T_c)
$$

**Step 4 — At steady state, $dT/dt = 0$ — gives the design equation for coolant temperature:**

$$
T_c^{ss} \;=\; T_{set} - \dfrac{(-\Delta H_{rxn}) \, r_A V}{U A}
$$

**Step 5 — Linearise for small perturbations $T = T_{set} + \theta$:**

$$
V \rho C_p \dfrac{d\theta}{dt} \;=\; (-\Delta H_{rxn}) \, V \!\left(\dfrac{\partial r_A}{\partial T}\right)_{T_{set}}\!\theta \;-\; U A \, \theta
$$

**Step 6 — Identify the cooling time constant $\theta_{cool}$ and the reaction "amplification" $K_R$:**

$$
\theta_{cool} \;=\; \dfrac{V \rho C_p}{U A}, \qquad K_R \;=\; (-\Delta H_{rxn}) \, V \!\left(\dfrac{\partial r_A}{\partial T}\right)_{T_{set}}
$$

**Step 7 — Closed-loop form (Newton's law of heating/cooling with internal heat source):**

$$
\theta_{cool}\,\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} \;=\; \left(K_R - U A\right) \theta
$$

**Engineering implication — Runaway Criterion:** The system is **unstable** if $K_R > U A$. In KTU 2024 parlance, this is called the **Semenov criterion** for thermal runaway.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Adiabatic Temperature Rise $\Delta T_{ad}$

The worst-case scenario is **complete cooling failure** during the most reactive phase. We need the temperature reached when **all unreacted substrate** reacts adiabatically.

**Step 1 — Start with the energy balance with $UA = 0$ (no cooling):**

$$
V \rho C_p \dfrac{dT}{dt} \;=\; (-\Delta H_{rxn}) \, r_A V
$$

**Step 2 — Use stoichiometry to relate heat released to moles reacted. For a complete conversion of $C_{A0}$:**

$$
dQ \;=\; (-\Delta H_{rxn}) \, V \, dC_A
$$

**Step 3 — Energy balance expressed in differential form:**

$$
V \rho C_p \, dT \;=\; (-\Delta H_{rxn}) \, V \, dC_A
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from initial state $(T_0, C_{A0})$ to final $(T_f, 0)$:**

$$
\int_{T_0}^{T_f} \rho C_p \, dT \;=\; (-\Delta H_{rxn}) \int_{C_{A0}}^{0} dC_A
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate both sides (assume $\rho$, $C_p$ constant):**

$$
\rho C_p (T_f - T_0) \;=\; (-\Delta H_{rxn}) \, C_{A0}
$$

**Step 6 — Define adiabatic temperature rise:**

$$
\Delta T_{ad} \;=\; T_f - T_0 \;=\; \dfrac{(-\Delta H_{rxn}) \, C_{A0}}{\rho \, C_p}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style):**

Given:
- Substrate: p-xylene → terephthalic acid
- $-\Delta H_{rxn} = 320$ kJ/mol
- $C_{A0} = 2.5$ kmol/m³
- $\rho = 950$ kg/m³, $C_p = 2.2$ kJ/(kg·K)

$$
\Delta T_{ad} \;=\; \dfrac{320\,000 \, \text{J/mol} \times 2500 \, \text{mol/m}^3}{950 \, \text{kg/m}^3 \times 2200 \, \text{J/(kg·K)}}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{ad} \;=\; \dfrac{8.0 \times 10^{8}}{2.09 \times 10^{6}} \;\approx\; 383 \, \text{K}
$$

This enormous value (383 K ≈ 110 °C rise) confirms why p-xylene oxidation is **always run as a semi-batch with air sparging**, not as a one-shot batch charge.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Design Considerations

| Design Parameter | Governing Equation | Typical Industrial Range | Failure Consequence |
|---|---|---|---|
| Jacket area $A$ | $UA \ge 1.25 \times Q_{gen,max}$ | 0.5 – 1.5 × vessel lateral area | Runaway |
| Coolant flow $F_c$ | $\Delta T_c = Q_{rem}/(\rho_c C_{pc} F_c)$ | 5 – 50 K approach | Loss of temperature control |
| Sparger air rate | $N_{O_2}^{MT} \ge r_{O_2} \cdot V$ | 0.1 – 2.0 vvm | Oxygen starvation, selectivity loss |
| Impeller $P/V$ | $P = N_p \rho N^3 D^5$ | 0.5 – 5 kW/m³ | Poor mass transfer |
| Pressure rating | $P_{MAWP} \ge 1.1 \times P_{max,ox}$ | 6 – 30 bar | Rupture disc activation |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics


### 4.4 Detailed P&ID-Inspired Schematic (Block-Level)

```mermaid

    FT1[Coriolis Mass Flow on O2 Line] --> FR1[Flow Recorder]

    end
    subgraph Control_Loops
        TR1 --> DCS1[DCS Recipe Controller]
        PR1 --> DCS1
        LR1 --> DCS1
        FR1 --> DCS1

    end

        DCS1 --> V1[O2 Control Valve]
        DCS1 --> V2[Coolant Control Valve]

    V1 --> R1
    V2 --> J1

```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define batch oxidation. List any three industrial examples where it is commonly employed.*

**Model Answer:**
Batch oxidation is a discontinuous chemical process in which a measured charge of substrate is reacted with a controlled quantity of an oxidising agent (O₂, air, H₂O₂, HNO₃, etc.) in a stirred-tank reactor for a fixed residence time, after which the product is discharged and the reactor recharged.

**Three industrial examples:**
1. **p-Xylene → Terephthalic acid** (used in PET manufacture) — Amoco/Mid-Century process.
2. **Cyclohexane → Cyclohexanone/Cyclohexanol (KA-oil)** — first step in Nylon-6,6 production.
3. **Toluene → Benzoic acid** — pharmaceutical intermediate.
*(Acceptable alternatives: cumene → cumene hydroperoxide, anthracene → anthraquinone, glucose → gluconic acid.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Why is cascade control preferred over single-loop PID for the temperature control of a batch oxidation reactor?*

**Model Answer:**
Single-loop PID control measures only the reactor temperature and manipulates the coolant valve directly. The jacket, however, exhibits slow thermal dynamics (time constant $\theta_{cool}$), large dead time, and nonlinear heat-transfer characteristics, which cause a single-loop controller to be sluggish and prone to overshoot.

Cascade control uses **two nested loops**:
- **Secondary (inner) loop:** jacket-outlet temperature → coolant valve (fast, eliminates valve nonlinearity and disturbances in coolant supply).
- **Primary (outer) loop:** reactor temperature → secondary loop setpoint (slow, handles reaction kinetics and load changes).

This structure rejects disturbances inside the jacket before they propagate to the reactor, gives a 3–10× improvement in disturbance rejection, and provides a smoother trajectory — critical for selectivity and safety in exothermic oxidation.

---

### Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

**Question A. [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

(a) **[7 Marks]** Derive the unsteady-state energy balance for a jacketed batch oxidation reactor. From this, obtain the **adiabatic temperature rise** expression and discuss its significance in process safety.

(b) **[7 Marks]** In the liquid-phase oxidation of p-xylene to terephthalic acid in a 5 m³ batch reactor, the following data are given:
- Initial concentration of p-xylene: $C_{A0} = 2.5$ kmol/m³
- Heat of reaction: $-\Delta H_{rxn} = 320$ kJ/mol
- Density of reaction mass: $\rho = 950$ kg/m³
- Specific heat: $C_p = 2.2$ kJ/(kg·K)
- Heat transfer coefficient × area: $UA = 8500$ kJ/(min·K)
- Reaction rate at operating temperature: $-r_A = k C_A$ with $k = 0.04$ min⁻¹
- Operating temperature: $T_{set} = 425$ K, Coolant temperature: $T_c = 320$ K

Calculate:
1. The adiabatic temperature rise.
2. The maximum heat generation rate at the start of the reaction.
3. Whether the system is in a thermally safe operating regime (compare $Q_{gen,max}$ with $Q_{rem,max}$).

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation** — Follow **Derivation 2** and **Derivation 3** in Section 3 above. The key result is:

$$
\Delta T_{ad} \;=\; \dfrac{(-\Delta H_{rxn}) \, C_{A0}}{\rho \, C_p}
$$

Its significance: it represents the worst-case temperature excursion if cooling fails. If $\Delta T_{ad} >$ (decomposition temperature − operating temperature), the batch is **inherently unsafe** and must be redesigned as a semi-batch with controlled oxidant dosing.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Adiabatic temperature rise:**

$$
\Delta T_{ad} \;=\; \dfrac{320\,000 \times 2500}{950 \times 2200} \;=\; \dfrac{8.0 \times 10^{8}}{2.09 \times 10^{6}} \;\approx\; 383 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum heat generation rate at $t = 0$ where $C_A = C_{A0}$:**

$$
Q_{gen,max} \;=\; (-\Delta H_{rxn}) \, (-r_A) \, V \;=\; 320\,000 \times 0.04 \times 60 \times 2500 \times 5
$$

$$
Q_{gen,max} \;=\; 320\,000 \times 150 \times 5 \;=\; 2.4 \times 10^{8} \text{ J/min} \;=\; 4000 \text{ kJ/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum heat removal capacity:**

$$
Q_{rem,max} \;=\; UA \,(T_{set} - T_c) \;=\; 8500 \times (425 - 320) \;=\; 892\,500 \text{ kJ/min} \;=\; 14\,875 \text{ kJ/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare and conclude:**

$Q_{rem,max}$ (14 875 kJ/s) ≫ $Q_{gen,max}$ (4000 kJ/s). The system is **thermally safe** at the start. The ratio $Q_{rem}/Q_{gen} \approx 3.7$ gives a healthy safety margin of ~3.7×.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Marks lost here:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** $k$ is in min⁻¹, $V$ in m³, $C_{A0}$ in kmol/m³. Convert all terms to consistent SI (J, s, mol, m³, K) before substituting. Many students lose 2–3 marks by mixing minutes and seconds.
> 2. **Sign of $\Delta H_{rxn}$:** Always write $(-\Delta H_{rxn})$ as a **positive** quantity in heat-generation formulas. Writing it as a negative number flips the sign of the answer.
> 3. **Forgetting the factor $V$:** Some students compute $Q_{gen}$ as $(-\Delta H_{rxn}) \cdot (-r_A)$ without multiplying by reactor volume $V$. This is a 2-mark deduction.

---

**Question B. [14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative]**

(a) **[7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **cascade T–T_c control strategy** for a jacketed batch oxidation reactor. Why is it preferred over simple feedback control?

(b) **[7 Marks]** Explain the **Semenov criterion for thermal runaway** in batch reactors. For a first-order exothermic reaction $A \to B$ with $k = 10^{13} e^{-12000/T}$ s⁻¹, $C_{A0} = 4$ kmol/m³, $(-\Delta H) = 90$ kJ/mol, $V = 2$ m³, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $C_p = 3$ kJ/(kg·K), $UA = 30\,000$ kJ/(min·K), and $T_c = 350$ K, determine whether the reactor can be operated safely at 400 K.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Cascade Control — Block Diagram & Justification**
Refer to the **mermaid block diagram in Section 4.2**. The two loops are:
- **Primary (slow):** TRC-100 compares reactor T with setpoint and outputs a setpoint for jacket-outlet temperature.
- **Secondary (fast):** TRC-101 compares jacket-outlet T with the setpoint from the primary and modulates the coolant valve.

Preferred because (i) secondary loop absorbs coolant supply disturbances, (ii) valve nonlinearity is linearised by the inner loop, (iii) faster correction reduces overshoot and protects selectivity.

**(b) Semenov Criterion:**

The criterion states that a thermal runaway occurs when the **heat-generation curve** is steeper than the **heat-removal line** at the operating point. Mathematically, the system is safe only if:

$$
UA \;>\; (-\Delta H) V C_{A0} \dfrac{E_a}{R T_{set}^{2}} \, k \, e^{-E_a / (R T_{set})}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute $k$ at 400 K:**

$$
k \;=\; 10^{13} e^{-12000 / 400} \;=\; 10^{13} \times e^{-30}
$$

$$
e^{-30} \;\approx\; 9.358 \times 10^{-14} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; k \;\approx\; 0.9358 \text{ s}^{-1}
$$

**Step 2 — Heat generation rate at start:**

$$
Q_{gen} \;=\; (-\Delta H) \, k \, C_{A0} \, V \;=\; 90\,000 \times 0.9358 \times 4000 \times 2
$$

$$
Q_{gen} \;=\; 90\,000 \times 7486.4 \;\approx\; 6.738 \times 10^{8} \text{ J/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Heat removal rate at 400 K:**

$$
Q_{rem} \;=\; UA \, (T - T_c) \;=\; 30\,000 \, \dfrac{\text{kJ}}{\text{min}\cdot\text{K}} \times 50 \, \text{K} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ min}}{60 \text{ s}}
$$

$$
Q_{rem} \;=\; \dfrac{30\,000 \times 50}{60} \;\approx\; 25\,000 \text{ kJ/s} \;=\; 2.5 \times 10^{7} \text{ J/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**

$Q_{gen} (6.74 \times 10^8 \text{ J/s}) \gg Q_{rem} (2.5 \times 10^7 \text{ J/s})$.
The reactor **cannot** be operated safely at 400 K. The operator must either reduce the operating temperature, increase coolant capacity, change the operating mode to semi-batch, or use a more selective catalyst.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Marks lost here:**
> 1. The Semenov criterion requires you to **derive the runaway condition** $UA > \text{(critical)}$, not just compute $Q_{gen}$ vs $Q_{rem}$. Marks are awarded for the *inequality statement*.
> 2. Always keep $E_a$ in **J/mol** and $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K). Using $R = 1.987$ cal/(mol·K) without unit conversion is a 1-mark deduction.
> 3. In the Arrhenius expression, ensure the **exponent is dimensionless** — i.e., $E_a / RT$ must be dimensionless. So $E_a$ must be in J/mol and $R$ in J/(mol·K).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Batch oxidation** = discontinuous, exothermic, gas–liquid, multi-phase reaction; reactor is a **jacketed stirred tank**.
- The most critical controlled variable is **reactor temperature**, controlled by a **cascade T–T_c loop** manipulating the coolant valve.
- Other loops: **pressure** (vent valve), **oxidant feed** (mass-flow controller), **DO** (sparger), **agitator speed** (VFD).
- **Semenov criterion** for runaway: heat generation must be < heat removal at all times. Equivalent condition: $UA >$ critical value derived from Arrhenius parameters.
- **Adiabatic temperature rise $\Delta T_{ad} = (-\Delta H) C_{A0} / (\rho C_p)$** quantifies the worst-case excursion under total cooling failure.
- **MTSR (Maximum Temperature of Synthesis Reaction)** = $T_0 + \Delta T_{ad} \cdot X$; if MTSR > decomposition temperature, immediate dump is mandatory.
- **Semi-batch (fed-batch) operation** is the default safe mode for highly exothermic oxidations (e.g., p-xylene, cyclohexane, acrolein).
- **Safety interlocks** trip on: T-high, P-high, DO out of range, coolant flow low, agitator failure, vapour-space O₂ above 4 %.
- **Degree of freedom** of the cascade loop = 2 (TT primary, TT secondary, CV coolant); two controllers, one valve.
- **KTU-favourite derivations:** (i) energy balance, (ii) $\Delta T_{ad}$ expression, (iii) Semenov runaway condition.
- **KTU-favourite numericals:** compute $\Delta T_{ad}$ for given $-\Delta H$, $C_{A0}$, $\rho$, $C_p$; compare $Q_{gen}$ vs $Q_{rem}$.
- **Common industrial processes:** p-xylene → TPA, cyclohexane → KA-oil, toluene → benzoic acid, cumene → CHP, acrolein from propylene, ethylene → ethylene oxide (although the last is continuous).
- **Green-chemistry trend:** replace stoichiometric oxidants (HNO₃, KMnO₄) with catalytic O₂/air; replace chlorinated solvents.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Always draw the cascade block diagram whenever a question mentions exothermic reactor control. Examiners award 2–3 marks for a clean, labelled diagram. Also, when calculating $\Delta T_{ad}$, **show units in every step** — it is a 1-mark giveaway that distinguishes a 13-mark answer from a 14-mark answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Continuous Oxidation
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT864 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN LARGE SCALE INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Continuous Oxidation

---

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Continuous Oxidation** is a steady-state, uninterrupted industrial chemical process in which a reactant stream (typically a gas or liquid) is continuously fed into a reactor where it undergoes an exothermic, oxidation reaction with an oxidizing agent (commonly air or pure oxygen) in the presence of a catalyst or at elevated temperatures, producing the desired oxidized product(s) while the unreacted feed and products are continuously withdrawn.

In the context of large-scale chemical industries, continuous oxidation is the workhorse unit operation for manufacturing foundational commodity chemicals such as **sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄)** via the Contact Process and **nitric acid (HNO₃)** via the Ostwald Process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Continuous Oxidation falls under Module 1 of PEICT864, emphasizing the integration of process chemistry with **Distributed Control Systems (DCS)**, multi-bed catalytic converter design, inter-stage cooling, and conversion-yield optimization via temperature and concentration feedback loops.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a busy highway toll booth system. Cars (reactant molecules) flow in continuously from one side, pass through a series of toll booths (catalyst beds), and exit on the other side as "processed" vehicles. Between toll booths, there are rest areas where cars cool down (inter-stage heat exchangers). The toll operator (control system) monitors the speed and density of cars and adjusts lane openings accordingly. If too many cars pile up, the system slows down the inflow or opens more lanes. This is exactly how continuous oxidation works — **feed in → react → cool → react again → product out**, all under strict automated supervision.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value | Significance |
|-----------|---------------|--------------|
| Adiabatic Flame Temperature of SO₂ oxidation | **~ 925 °C** | Peak temperature in 1st catalyst bed |
| Optimum inlet temperature (SO₂ → SO₃) | **~ 410–440 °C** | Catalyst activity onset |
| Standard heat of reaction (SO₂ + ½O₂ → SO₃) | **ΔH° = –98.9 kJ/mol** | Strongly exothermic |
| Activation energy (Pt catalyst, SO₂ oxidation) | **Eₐ ≈ 80 kJ/mol** | Indicates moderate catalyst dependency |
| Oxidation state change of nitrogen (NH₃ → NO) | **–3 → +2** | 5-electron transfer per N atom |
| Space velocity (industrial converter) | **300–600 h⁻¹** | Defines reactor throughput |

> [!NOTE]
> The negative ΔH° denotes that heat is **released** during the oxidation, requiring robust inter-stage heat removal to prevent catalyst sintering and equilibrium shift back toward reactants (Le Chatelier's principle).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

Continuous oxidation processes operate on a sequence of carefully engineered steps:

1. **Feed Preparation & Preheating** — The reactant gas (e.g., SO₂ from a sulfur burner or NH₃ from an ammonia plant) is mixed with a stoichiometric excess of air and preheated using the hot exit gas from the reactor (economizer heat exchange).
2. **Catalytic Conversion (Multi-Bed Converter)** — The preheated gas passes through multiple catalyst beds (typically 3–4 for SO₂ oxidation) where the exothermic reaction proceeds.
3. **Inter-Stage Cooling** — Between successive catalyst beds, the gas is cooled via heat exchangers to maintain the temperature within the optimum window dictated by the equilibrium curve and the rate curve.
4. **Product Absorption / Quenching** — The oxidized product (e.g., SO₃) is absorbed in a suitable solvent (98% H₂SO₄) to yield the final acid.
5. **Recycle & Purge** — Unreacted gases may be recycled after scrubbing to maximize conversion.

## 2.2 Why Multi-Bed Reactors?

- A **single adiabatic bed** would experience a steep temperature rise due to the large exotherm, causing the equilibrium to shift **backward** (lower conversion).
- By splitting the catalyst into multiple beds with inter-stage cooling, the temperature profile follows a "**zig-zag**" pattern that keeps each segment within the kinetic-optimum zone.
- This is governed by the intersection of the **equilibrium conversion curve** and the **reaction rate curve**.

## 2.3 Equilibrium & Rate Considerations

For the generic reversible oxidation:

$$
A + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \rightleftharpoons B \quad \Delta H < 0
$$

- **Lower temperature** → higher equilibrium conversion (favored kinetically and thermodynamically for forward reaction upon cooling).
- **Higher temperature** → faster rate, but lower equilibrium constant.
- The **optimum temperature** (T_opt) is found where the rate curve and equilibrium curve yield the maximum product of (rate × driving force).

The T_opt is given by:

$$
T_{opt} = \frac{T_{eq}}{1 + \frac{RT_{eq}}{E_a} \ln\left(\frac{E_a + RT_{eq}}{E_a - RT_{eq}}\right)^{-1}}
$$

> Where T_eq is the equilibrium temperature at the current conversion and E_a is the activation energy.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Equation | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units |
|----------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Equilibrium constant (van't Hoff) | $K_p = K_0 \exp\left(-\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT}\right)$ | $K_p$ dimensionless, $T$ in K |
| Rate expression (power law) | $-r_{SO_2} = k \cdot p_{SO_2} \cdot p_{O_2}^{0.5} / p_{SO_3}$ | $r$ in mol/(s·kg cat), $p$ in atm |
| Arrhenius rate constant | $k = A \exp(-E_a/RT)$ | $A$ pre-exponential factor |
| Conversion (single-pass) | $X = \frac{F_{in} - F_{out}}{F_{in}}$ | $F$ molar flow (mol/s) |
| Space velocity | $SV = \frac{Q_{feed}}{V_{reactor}}$ | $h^{-1}$ |
| Heat removal duty | $Q = \dot{m} C_p \Delta T$ | W (Watts) |
| Optimum inlet T (empirical) | $T_{in,opt} \approx T_{ignition} + 20\,^\circ C$ | °C |
| Pressure drop (Ergun eqn.) | $\frac{\Delta P}{L} = \frac{150 \mu (1-\varepsilon)^2}{d_p^2 \varepsilon^3} u_s + \frac{1.75 \rho (1-\varepsilon)}{d_p \varepsilon^3} u_s^2$ | Pa/m |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse the **equilibrium temperature** (T_eq) with the **optimum temperature** (T_opt). T_eq is where ΔG = 0 (no net reaction driving force); T_opt maximizes the actual reaction rate.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Sulfuric Acid Plants:** Continuous oxidation of SO₂ produces ~260 million tonnes/year worldwide — the largest volume industrial chemical.
- **Nitric Acid Plants:** Ostwald process oxidation of NH₃ over Pt-Rh gauze is the standard route, operating at ~900 °C with ~95% conversion per pass.
- **Environmental Control:** SCR (Selective Catalytic Reduction) for NO_x removal uses a similar catalytic oxidation-reduction principle.
- **Phthalic Anhydride, Maleic Anhydride:** Fixed-bed catalytic oxidation of ortho-xylene and butene respectively.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Equilibrium Conversion of SO₂ Oxidation

The reversible reaction:

$$
SO_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \rightleftharpoons SO_3 \quad \Delta H^\circ = -98.9 \text{ kJ/mol}
$$

For an initial mixture with molar ratio $SO_2 : O_2 = a : b$, the equilibrium constant based on partial pressures:

$$
K_p = \frac{p_{SO_3}}{p_{SO_2} \cdot p_{O_2}^{0.5}}
$$

Let $X_e$ = equilibrium fractional conversion of SO₂.

**Initial moles (basis = 1 mol SO₂):**

$$
n_{SO_2,0} = 1, \quad n_{O_2,0} = b, \quad n_{SO_3,0} = 0, \quad n_{N_2,0} = 3.76b
$$

**At equilibrium:**

$$
n_{SO_2} = 1 - X_e, \quad n_{O_2} = b - \frac{X_e}{2}, \quad n_{SO_3} = X_e
$$

Total moles at equilibrium:

$$
n_T = (1 - X_e) + \left(b - \frac{X_e}{2}\right) + X_e + 3.76b = 1 + 4.76b - \frac{X_e}{2}
$$

Mole fractions (assuming total pressure $P_t$):

$$
y_{SO_2} = \frac{1 - X_e}{n_T}, \quad y_{O_2} = \frac{b - X_e/2}{n_T}, \quad y_{SO_3} = \frac{X_e}{n_T}
$$

Substituting into $K_p$:

$$
K_p = \frac{X_e / n_T}{\left[(1 - X_e)/n_T\right] \cdot \left[(b - X_e/2)/n_T\right]^{0.5}} \cdot P_t^{-0.5}
$$

Simplifying by multiplying numerator and denominator by $n_T^{1.5}$:

$$
K_p = \frac{X_e}{(1 - X_e)(b - X_e/2)^{0.5}} \cdot \left(\frac{n_T}{P_t}\right)^{0.5}
$$

For a typical feed with $a = 1, b = 1.0$ (stoichiometric) and $P_t = 1$ atm, this reduces to:

$$
K_p = \frac{X_e}{(1 - X_e)(1 - X_e/2)^{0.5}} \quad \text{(at 1 atm)}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

**Problem:** For SO₂ oxidation at 700 °C, $K_p \approx 3.3$ atm⁻⁰·⁵. Feed composition: 7% SO₂, 11% O₂, 82% N₂. Total pressure 1.2 atm. Calculate the equilibrium conversion.

**Step 1: Convert to molar basis per 1 mol SO₂.**

Per 100 mol feed: 7 SO₂, 11 O₂, 82 N₂.
Normalizing to 1 mol SO₂: $b = 11/7 = 1.571$, $n_{N_2} = 82/7 = 11.714$.

**Step 2: Express K_p in terms of conversion.**

$$
K_p = \frac{X_e}{(1-X_e)\left(b - X_e/2\right)^{0.5}} \cdot \left(\frac{n_T}{P_t}\right)^{0.5}
$$

where $n_T = 1 + b + 11.714 - X_e/2 = 14.286 - X_e/2$.

**Step 3: Iterate** (try $X_e = 0.65$):

$$
n_T = 14.286 - 0.325 = 13.961
$$

$$
\left(\frac{n_T}{P_t}\right)^{0.5} = \left(\frac{13.961}{1.2}\right)^{0.5} = (11.634)^{0.5} = 3.411
$$

$$
b - X_e/2 = 1.571 - 0.325 = 1.246
$$

$$
K_p = \frac{0.65}{(0.35)(1.246)^{0.5}} \times 3.411 = \frac{0.65}{0.35 \times 1.116} \times 3.411 = 1.665 \times 3.411 = 5.68
$$

Too high → try $X_e = 0.55$:

$$
n_T = 14.286 - 0.275 = 14.011
$$

$$
\left(14.011/1.2\right)^{0.5} = (11.676)^{0.5} = 3.417
$$

$$
b - X_e/2 = 1.571 - 0.275 = 1.296, \quad (1.296)^{0.5} = 1.138
$$

$$
K_p = \frac{0.55}{(0.45)(1.138)} \times 3.417 = 1.075 \times 3.417 = 3.67
$$

Try $X_e = 0.53$:

$$
b - X_e/2 = 1.296 \text{ (approx)}, \quad K_p \approx 3.32 \approx 3.3 \text{ ✓}
$$

**Result: $X_e \approx 0.53$ (53%)** at 700 °C.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Note:** Students frequently forget the **P_t^(–0.5)** dependence, leading to a factor-of-1.1 error in conversion. Always carry pressure explicitly.

## 3.3 Derivation: Optimum Temperature Using Thiele–Lewis Analysis

For a reversible exothermic reaction, the optimum temperature $T_{opt}$ at a given conversion $X$ is found by maximizing the rate $r$:

$$
r = k(T) \cdot f(X) - k'(T) \cdot g(X)
$$

Setting $\dfrac{dr}{dT} = 0$:

$$
\frac{dr}{dT} = \frac{\partial r}{\partial k} \frac{dk}{dT} + \frac{\partial r}{\partial K} \frac{dK}{dT} = 0
$$

Substituting Arrhenius forms $k = A e^{-E/RT}$ and $K = K_0 e^{-\Delta H/RT}$, the optimum temperature is given implicitly by:

$$
\frac{E_a}{RT_{opt}^2} \cdot k(T_{opt}) f(X) - \frac{\Delta H}{RT_{opt}^2} \cdot k'(T_{opt}) g(X) = 0
$$

This yields the **classic zig-zag temperature profile** for multi-bed converters.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Multi-Bed SO₂ Converter with Inter-Stage Cooling (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SO2 Air Feed Stream] --> B[Preheater Economizer]
    B --> C[Bed 1 Catalyst V2O5]
    C --> D[Intercooler 1]
    D --> E[Bed 2 Catalyst V2O5]
    E --> F[Intercooler 2]
    F --> G[Bed 3 Catalyst V2O5]
    G --> H[Intercooler 3]
    H --> I[Bed 4 Catalyst V2O5]
    I --> J[SO3 Cooler]
    J --> K[Absorption Tower 98 percent H2SO4]
    K --> L[Product H2SO4 Storage]
    K --> M[Tail Gas Stack]

    TC1[Temperature Controller Bed1] -.-> C
    TC2[Temperature Controller Bed2] -.-> E
    TC3[Temperature Controller Bed3] -.-> G
    TC4[Temperature Controller Bed4] -.-> I
    FC1[Flow Controller Air Feed] -.-> A
    AC1[Analyzer SO2 IR Analy] -.feedback.-> FC1

    style A fill:#FFD580
    style K fill:#9CD2FF
    style L fill:#A8E6A1
    style TC1 fill:#FF9999
    style TC2 fill:#FF9999
    style TC3 fill:#FF9999
    style TC4 fill:#FF9999
    style FC1 fill:#FFCC99
    style AC1 fill:#FFCC99
```

## 4.2 Ammonia Oxidation Reactor (Ostwald Process) Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FeedPrep[Feed Preparation]
        A1[Liquid Ammonia Storage] --> A2[Ammonia Vaporizer]
        A3[Filtered Air Blower] --> A4[Air Preheater]
    end

    subgraph Reactor[Oxidation Reactor]
        A2 --> R1[Mixer Tee]
        A4 --> R1
        R1 --> R2[Pt Rh Gauze Catalyst Pack]
        R2 --> R3[Waste Heat Boiler]
    end

    R3 --> D1[NO Cooler]
    D1 --> D2[Oxidation Chamber NO to NO2]
    D2 --> D3[Absorption Tower H2O]
    D3 --> P1[Product HNO3 55 to 68 percent]
    D3 --> P2[Tail Gas]

    style R2 fill:#FF7F7F
    style P1 fill:#9CD2FF
    style A1 fill:#FFD580
```

## 4.3 Instrumentation & Control Loop Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Reactor Temperature Sensor RTD] --> T1[Transmitter 4 to 20 mA]
    T1 --> DCS[DCS Controller PID Block]
    P2[Inlet SO2 Concentration IR Analyzer] --> T2[Transmitter]
    T2 --> DCS
    P3[Catalyst Bed Delta T] --> T3[Transmitter]
    T3 --> DCS
    P4[Outlet Gas Flow Meter] --> T4[Transmitter]
    T4 --> DCS

    DCS --> V1[Control Valve Cold Shot Air Bypass]
    DCS --> V2[Control Valve Inlet SO2]
    DCS --> V3[Control Valve Inter Bed Cooling Water]

    HMI[Operator HMI Display] --- DCS
    ALM[Alarm Management Block] --- DCS

    style DCS fill:#FFCC66
    style HMI fill:#A8E6A1
    style ALM fill:#FF9999
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]
**List any three key features of a multi-bed catalytic converter used for continuous oxidation of SO₂ to SO₃.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Multiple catalyst beds (typically 3–4)** with inter-stage heat exchangers to maintain optimum temperature window between equilibrium and rate curves.
2. **Adiabatic operation** of each bed with intermediate cooling to prevent thermal sintering of the V₂O₅ catalyst.
3. **Inlet temperature around 410–440 °C** in the first bed to ensure catalyst ignition, with progressively lower temperatures (down to ~400 °C) in subsequent beds to leverage equilibrium shift.
4. **Use of V₂O₅ on SiO₂ or K₂SO₄-promoted support** for industrial-scale operation (replacing costly Pt).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]
**Why is the Ostwald process operated at atmospheric pressure rather than high pressure for NH₃ oxidation?**

**Model Answer:**
1. The forward reaction $4NH_3 + 5O_2 \rightarrow 4NO + 6H_2O$ proceeds with **increase in moles (10 → 10)** but the rate is extremely fast on Pt-Rh gauze (~millisecond contact time), so high pressure offers no kinetic benefit.
2. The **equilibrium is highly favorable** (>95% conversion) at high temperature (~900 °C), eliminating the need for pressure-driven conversion enhancement.
3. High pressure would require **thicker pressure vessels** for the Pt gauze reactor, increasing capital cost significantly.
4. The subsequent absorption step ($3NO_2 + H_2O \rightarrow 2HNO_3 + NO$) is **pressure-favored**, so compression is applied only at that stage.

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**(a)** Derive the expression for equilibrium conversion of SO₂ oxidation as a function of temperature, pressure, and initial molar ratio. Discuss the effect of pressure on conversion. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A double-contact double-absorption (DCDA) sulfuric acid plant processes 100,000 Nm³/hr of feed gas containing 9% SO₂, 12% O₂, and 79% N₂ at 1 atm. The first converter gives 70% conversion, and after intermediate absorption, the remaining gas gives an additional 90% conversion in the second converter. Calculate the **overall conversion** and the **daily production of H₂SO₄** (assume 100% absorption efficiency). **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution (Question A)

#### Part (a) — Derivation & Pressure Effect

**Derivation:** Starting from the reaction stoichiometry with initial moles $n_{SO_2,0}=1$, $n_{O_2,0}=b$, $n_{N_2,0}=3.76b$ (since air is 21% O₂, 79% N₂):

At conversion $X$:

$$
n_{SO_2} = 1-X, \quad n_{O_2} = b - \frac{X}{2}, \quad n_{SO_3} = X, \quad n_{N_2} = 3.76b
$$

$$
n_T = 1 + 4.76b - \frac{X}{2}
$$

The equilibrium relation:

$$
K_p = \frac{X}{(1-X)\left(b - X/2\right)^{0.5}} \cdot \left(\frac{n_T}{P_t}\right)^{0.5}
$$

**Effect of Pressure:** Since $\Delta n = -\frac{1}{2}$ (moles decrease), increasing $P_t$ **increases** equilibrium conversion. However, the dependence is weak ($P_t^{0.5}$), and practical considerations (vessel cost, catalyst mechanical strength) dictate operation near atmospheric pressure in industry.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:** Feed = 100,000 Nm³/hr at 9% SO₂.
Molar flow of SO₂ at STP (22.4 Nm³/kmol):

$$
F_{SO_2} = \frac{0.09 \times 100{,}000}{22.4} = 401.79 \text{ kmol/hr}
$$

**Overall conversion** (sequential product rule):

$$
X_{overall} = 1 - (1 - X_1)(1 - X_2) = 1 - (1 - 0.70)(1 - 0.90)
$$

$$
X_{overall} = 1 - (0.30 \times 0.10) = 1 - 0.03 = 0.97 = 97\%
$$

**Moles of SO₃ produced** (and absorbed to make H₂SO₄):

$$
F_{H_2SO_4} = F_{SO_2} \times X_{overall} = 401.79 \times 0.97 = 389.74 \text{ kmol/hr}
$$

**Daily production:**

$$
\text{Mass} = 389.74 \times 98 \times 24 = 916{,}541 \text{ kg/day} \approx 916.5 \text{ tonnes/day}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Students frequently apply $X_{overall} = X_1 + X_2 = 1.60$, which is **physically impossible**. Always use the sequential formula $X_{overall} = 1 - \prod(1-X_i)$.
> - Forgetting to multiply by **24 hours** in daily production loses 1 mark.
> - Using $22.4$ L/mol instead of $22.4$ Nm³/kmol loses 1 mark.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the working of a **multi-bed catalytic converter** for continuous oxidation of SO₂. Discuss why the temperature profile follows a zig-zag pattern. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The rate expression for SO₂ oxidation over a V₂O₅ catalyst is:

$$
-r_{SO_2} = \frac{k \cdot p_{SO_2} \cdot p_{O_2}^{0.5}}{p_{SO_3}}
$$

If $k = 0.05$ mol/(s·kg·atm^1.5) at 450 °C with $E_a = 80$ kJ/mol, and the partial pressures are $p_{SO_2} = 0.05$ atm, $p_{O_2} = 0.10$ atm, $p_{SO_3} = 0.03$ atm, calculate the rate at 450 °C and at 500 °C. Comment on the trend. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution (Question B)

#### Part (a) — Multi-Bed Converter & Zig-Zag Profile

A multi-bed catalytic converter consists of **3–4 adiabatic catalyst beds** stacked vertically with **inter-stage heat exchangers** between them. The cold feed enters the first bed at the **ignition temperature** (~410 °C). The exothermic reaction raises the temperature rapidly to a peak (~580–600 °C in the first bed). Before the gas enters the second bed, it is **cooled** in a heat exchanger (cold-shot cooler or heat exchanger with cold feed).

**Why zig-zag?**
- The first bed operates at **high temperature to maximize rate** (kinetic-controlled).
- The intermediate cooling brings the gas back to a temperature where **equilibrium conversion is favorable**.
- Each successive bed starts at a slightly lower temperature but achieves **incremental conversion**.
- The overall profile ensures that the gas always stays **between the equilibrium curve and the rate curve**, maximizing per-pass conversion (often >97% in DCDA plants).

#### Part (b) — Rate Calculation

**At T₁ = 450 °C = 723 K:**

$$
-r_{SO_2} = \frac{0.05 \times 0.05 \times (0.10)^{0.5}}{0.03} = \frac{0.05 \times 0.05 \times 0.3162}{0.03}
$$

$$
-r_{SO_2} = \frac{0.000791}{0.03} = 0.02635 \text{ mol/(s·kg cat)}
$$

**At T₂ = 500 °C = 773 K:**

Compute new $k_2$ using Arrhenius:

$$
k_2 = k_1 \exp\left[-\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)\right]
$$

$$
\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{723} - \frac{1}{773} = \frac{773 - 723}{723 \times 773} = \frac{50}{558879} = 8.946 \times 10^{-5} \text{ K}^{-1}
$$

$$
\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right) = \frac{80{,}000}{8.314} \times 8.946 \times 10^{-5} = 9622.6 \times 8.946 \times 10^{-5} = 0.8608
$$

$$
k_2 = 0.05 \times e^{-(-0.8608)} = 0.05 \times e^{0.8608} = 0.05 \times 2.365 = 0.1182 \text{ mol/(s·kg·atm}^{1.5}\text{)}
$$

(Note: Arrhenius $k$ increases with $T$; the negative sign is because we use $1/T_1 - 1/T_2$, which is positive when $T_2 > T_1$, but conventionally $k_2 = k_1 \exp[+\frac{E_a}{R}(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)]$.)

Now compute the rate:

$$
-r_{SO_2,2} = \frac{0.1182 \times 0.05 \times 0.3162}{0.03} = \frac{0.001869}{0.03} = 0.0623 \text{ mol/(s·kg cat)}
$$

**Trend:** Rate at 500 °C is **~2.36× higher** than at 450 °C, indicating strong temperature sensitivity in the kinetically-controlled region.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Students often forget that $p_{O_2}^{0.5}$ means **square root** of partial pressure, not direct multiplication.
> - Incorrectly writing the Arrhenius exponent sign ($+\frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \Delta(1/T)$) flips the rate trend. Verify: higher T → higher k.
> - Units of rate (mol/s/kg cat) must be explicitly stated; missing units lose 0.5 mark.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Continuous oxidation is a **steady-state exothermic catalytic process** (SO₂→SO₃, NH₃→NO).
- ✅ **Multi-bed converters** with inter-stage cooling are used to balance kinetics vs. equilibrium.
- ✅ Equilibrium constant follows **van't Hoff equation**: $K_p = K_0 \exp(-\Delta H^\circ/RT)$ — increases as $T$ decreases for exothermic reactions.
- ✅ **Optimum inlet temperature** ≈ ignition temperature + 20 °C (~410–440 °C for SO₂).
- ✅ **DCDA (Double Contact Double Absorption)** achieves >97% overall conversion vs. ~97% for SCSA — but with significantly higher capital cost.
- ✅ Ostwald process: Pt-Rh gauze (90:10), atmospheric pressure, ~900 °C, ~95% NH₃ conversion in ~10⁻³ s contact time.
- ✅ Rate expression has **product inhibition** by SO₃ (denominator contains $p_{SO_3}$).
- ✅ Pressure dependence: weak ($P_t^{0.5}$) → industry operates near 1 atm.
- ✅ Key control loops: **reactor temperature** (cascade to cooling medium flow), **inlet SO₂ concentration** (IR analyzer feedback to feed valve), **catalyst ΔT monitoring** (hot-spot detection).
- ✅ Catalyst: **V₂O₅/K₂SO₄ on SiO₂** for SO₂; **Pt-Rh gauze** for NH₃.
- ✅ Le Chatelier: Lower T + higher P → higher equilibrium conversion for SO₂ oxidation (Δn = –½).
- ✅ **DCS** monitors: bed temperatures, pressure drop (ΔP across bed), gas composition, flow rates.
- ✅ Common safety instruments: **O₂ analyzer in tail gas** (prevents explosive mixture), **temperature interlocks** on catalyst beds (high-T trip), **acid temperature alarms**.

---

> **End of Notes — Continuous Oxidation | PEICT864 Module 1 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chlorinator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT864 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN LARGE SCALE INDUSTRIES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Chlorinator # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Chlorinator** is a precisely engineered industrial instrumentation device designed to measure, regulate, and inject a controlled quantity of chlorine (in elemental gaseous form or as hypochlorite compounds) into a process stream—predominantly water—for the purposes of **disinfection**, **oxidation**, **bleaching**, or **biological growth control**. In the context of KTU's instrumentation framework, the chlorinator is treated as a closed-loop chemical dosing subsystem integrating primary sensors, controllers, final control elements, and safety interlocks. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A chlorinator is never studied as a standalone mechanical feeder; it is examined as an **integrated dosing loop** comprising metering, regulation, injection, and feedback. Questions in ESE typically focus on the working principle of *vacuum gas chlorinators*, the *control philosophy*, and the *residual chlorine feedback loop*. The formal definition per IS 10553 and AWWA standards is: > *"A chlorinator is an apparatus that introduces chlorine or chlorine-bearing compounds into water or wastewater at a measurable, controllable, and reproducible rate using vacuum, pressure, or positive-displacement principles."* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **medical IV drip** in a hospital, but on an industrial scale and dispensing **chlorine** instead of saline. The doctor (control system) decides how fast the drip should flow based on the patient's vitals (water quality parameters like residual chlorine, flow rate, turbidity). The IV set has a roller clamp (flow regulator/rotameter), a drip chamber (visual indicator), and a needle (ejector/diffuser injecting into the main line). If the patient's condition worsens, the drip rate is adjusted automatically by an infusion pump (PID controller). The chlorinator works exactly on this principle: - The **"patient"** is the water stream requiring disinfection. - The **"medicine"** is chlorine gas/solution. - The **"drip rate"** is the chlorine dose (mg/L or ppm). - The **"doctor"** is the PLC/DCS controller receiving feedback from residual chlorine analyzers and flow meters. ## 1.3 Classification of Chlorinators | Type | Feed Form | Principle | Typical Application | |------|-----------|-----------|---------------------| | **Gas Chlorinator** (Vacuum-type) | Cl₂ gas | Vacuum ejector | Large municipal WTP | | **Gas Chlorinator** (Pressure-type) | Cl₂ gas | Direct pressure | Legacy installations | | **Hypochlorinator** | NaOCl liquid | Positive displacement pump | Small-medium plants | | **Electrolytic Chlorinator** | On-site generated | Electrolysis of brine | Remote/safety-critical | | **Tablet Chlorinator** | Ca(OCl)₂ tablets | Erosion/dissolution | Rural/small flow | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metric:** Chlorine dose is universally expressed in **mg/L (equivalent to ppm)** for water. The CT value (Concentration × Time) is the governing disinfection parameter per WHO/AWWA guidelines. ## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standards - **Atomic mass of Cl:** 35.45 g/mol - **Molecular mass of Cl₂:** 70.90 g/mol - **Density of liquid Cl₂:** 1.56 g/cm³ at 0 °C - **Boiling point of Cl₂:** −34.04 °C - **Solubility of Cl₂ in water:** 6.3 g/L at 20 °C and 1 atm - **Standard dose range for potable water:** **0.2 – 1.0 mg/L** (free chlorine residual) - **WHO permissible residual:** 0.5 mg/L at consumer end # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Operational Principle – Step-by-Step Logic The most commonly examined variant is the **vacuum-operated gas chlorinator**. The working follows a strict sequential logic: ### Step 1: Gas Withdrawal from Cylinder Chlorine is stored as a liquefied gas under pressure (~6 bar at 20 °C) in tonner/cylinder containers. As gas is withdrawn, the latent heat of vaporization cools the cylinder, sustaining equilibrium vapor pressure. ### Step 2: Pressure Regulation The cylinder pressure (which varies with ambient temperature) is reduced to a stable working pressure (typically 1.5 bar) using a **pressure-regulating valve (PRV)** mounted on the manifold. ### Step 3: Vacuum Generation (Safety Principle) A **vacuum is intentionally created** in the chlorinator body using the motive water flow through a venturi ejector. This is the *defining safety feature*: if the ejector water fails, the vacuum collapses and the chlorine inlet valve **automatically closes**, preventing gas leakage. ### Step 4: Gas Flow Measurement The evacuated chlorine gas passes through a **variable-area flowmeter (rotameter)**, providing a visual indication of dosing rate. A **rate-of-flow controller (RFC)** automatically modulates a differential pressure (V-notch) valve to maintain setpoint. ### Step 5: Mixing & Injection The metered chlorine gas mixes with a small auxiliary water stream inside the chlorinator, forming a **chlorine solution (chlorine-water)** of known concentration. This solution is then drawn by vacuum into the **ejector (injector)**, where it is discharged into the main water line. ### Step 6: Feedback (Closed-Loop) A **residual chlorine analyzer** (typically amperometric or DPD colorimetric) measures downstream free chlorine. The 4–20 mA signal is fed back to a **PID controller**, which adjusts the chlorine feed rate to maintain a set residual (e.g., 0.5 ppm). ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units | |---|--------------------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $\text{Dose} = \frac{V_{Cl} \times C_{Cl}}{Q_{water}}$ | Basic chlorine dose equation | mg/L | | 2 | $\text{Chlorine Demand} = \text{Dose Applied} - \text{Residual}$ | Net chlorine consumed by impurities | mg/L | | 3 | $\text{CT Value} = C \times t$ | Disinfection efficacy parameter (Concentration × Contact Time) | mg·min/L | | 4 | $\text{Feed Rate} = Q_{water} \times D_{target}$ | Required chlorine feed rate | g/min or kg/day | | 5 | $Q_{Cl_2(gas)} = \frac{\text{Feed Rate (g/min)} \times R \times T}{M \times P}$ | Gas volume flow (ideal gas law) | L/min | | 6 | $\text{Residual} = \text{Dose} - \text{Demand}$ | Mass balance on chlorine | mg/L | | 7 | $\Delta P_{\text{ejector}} = \rho \left( \frac{v_2^2 - v_1^2}{2} \right)$ | Bernoulli-based ejector pressure recovery | Pa | | 8 | $\eta_{\text{disinfection}} = 1 - e^{-k \cdot CT}$ | Chick-Watson disinfection kinetics | dimensionless | | 9 | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t) dt + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$ | PID controller output (chlorine control loop) | % stroke | | 10 | $K = K_c \left( 1 + \frac{1}{\tau_I s} + \tau_D s \right)$ | Transfer function of PID controller | — | > [!NOTE] > **Valuation Tip:** Examiners award full marks when you clearly state **assumptions** (e.g., "assuming ideal gas behaviour, isothermal flow, fully mixed contact tank") before substituting into the gas-law equation. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry | Application | Typical Dose | |----------|-------------|--------------| | **Municipal Water Treatment** | Potable water disinfection | 0.5 – 1.5 mg/L | | **Power Plant Cooling Water** | Biofouling control | 0.5 – 2.0 mg/L | | **Pulp & Paper** | Bleaching (C/D stages) | 25 – 80 kg/ton of pulp | | **Wastewater (Tertiary)** | Effluent disinfection | 2 – 6 mg/L | | **Food & Beverage** | CIP (Clean-in-Place), bottle rinse | 50 – 200 mg/L | | **Petrochemical** | Hydrogen sulfide oxidation | Stoichiometric + 10% excess | | **Aquaculture / Swimming Pools** | Pathogen control | 1 – 3 mg/L residual | | **Semiconductor / Pharma** | Ultra-pure water systems | 0.1 – 0.5 mg/L | ## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Design Choice - **Why vacuum operation?** → *Safety*. Loss of vacuum = automatic shutoff. Pressure systems leak; vacuum systems suck in air, never leak outward. - **Why rotameter + RFC?** → *Visibility + Automaticity*. Operator sees the rate; controller compensates for cylinder pressure drop. - **Why ejector?** → *No moving parts in contact with chlorine*. Maintenance-free, no corrosion of mechanical parts. - **Why residual analyzer in feedback?** → *Process disturbances* (varying turbidity, demand) require adaptive dosing to maintain disinfection guarantee. # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Chlorine Gas Volume Required from Ideal Gas Law **Problem Statement:** A water treatment plant treats 50,000 m³/day of water requiring a chlorine dose of 2 mg/L. If chlorine is supplied as pure Cl₂ gas at 2.5 bar (gauge) and 30 °C, calculate the volume of gas consumed per day. ### Step 1: Convert absolute pressure Atmospheric pressure = 1.013 bar (approx. 1 bar). Absolute pressure = gauge + atmospheric = 2.5 + 1.013 = 3.513 bar. Convert to SI units: $P = 3.513 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$. ### Step 2: Convert temperature to Kelvin $T = 30 + 273.15 = 303.15 \text{ K}$. ### Step 3: Calculate mass of chlorine required per day Daily water flow: $Q_{water} = 50{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} = 50 \times 10^6 \text{ L/day}$. Dose $D = 2 \text{ mg/L}$. Mass of chlorine required: $$ m_{Cl_2} = Q_{water} \times D = (50 \times 10^6 \text{ L/day}) \times (2 \text{ mg/L}) $$ $$ m_{Cl_2} = 100 \times 10^6 \text{ mg/day} = 100 \text{ kg/day} $$ ### Step 4: Calculate moles of chlorine Molecular weight of Cl₂: $M = 70.90 \text{ g/mol} = 70.90 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/mol}$. $$ n = \frac{m_{Cl_2}}{M} = \frac{100 \text{ kg/day}}{70.90 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/mol}} = 1410.44 \text{ mol/day} $$ ### Step 5: Apply the Ideal Gas Law $$ PV = nRT $$ Solving for $V$: $$ V = \frac{nRT}{P} $$ Universal gas constant: $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$. $$ V = \frac{(1410.44) \times (8.314) \times (303.15)}{3.513 \times 10^5} $$ ### Step 6: Numerator evaluation Numerator = $1410.44 \times 8.314 \times 303.15$ $1410.44 \times 8.314 = 11{,}726.40$ (intermediate). $11{,}726.40 \times 303.15 = 3{,}554{,}795.16$ (J, since R in SI). ### Step 7: Final division $$ V = \frac{3{,}554{,}795.16}{3.513 \times 10^5} = 10.118 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} $$ ### Result $$\boxed{V_{Cl_2} \approx 10.12 \text{ m}^3/\text{day at 2.5 bar gauge, 30 °C}}$$ This volume is metered through the rotameter of the chlorinator, which is calibrated in **kg/day** of chlorine equivalent for operator convenience. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: PID Controller Tuning for Residual Chlorine Loop **Problem Statement:** A chlorinator control loop maintains residual chlorine at setpoint 0.6 mg/L. The process has a first-order lag with time constant $\tau_p = 120$ s and process gain $K_p = 0.4$ mg/(L·mg·L⁻¹) i.e. 0.4 ppm per ppm dose. The analyzer has a 15-second dead time. Compute Ziegler-Nichols PI tuning parameters. ### Step 1: Identify process parameters $K_p = 0.4$, $\tau_p = 120$ s, $L = 15$ s (dead time). ### Step 2: Apply Ziegler-Nichols open-loop (reaction curve) tuning for PI controller Proportional gain: $$ K_c = 0.9 \times \frac{\tau_p}{K_p \times L} = 0.9 \times \frac{120}{0.4 \times 15} $$ $$ K_c = 0.9 \times \frac{120}{6} = 0.9 \times 20 = 18 $$ ### Step 3: Integral time $$ \tau_I = 3.33 \times L = 3.33 \times 15 = 49.95 \text{ s} \approx 50 \text{ s} $$ ### Step 4: Derivative time (zero for PI) $$ \tau_D = 0 $$ ### Step 5: Verify with closed-loop performance For a PI controller, the closed-loop transfer function is: $$ \frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{K_c K_p \left(1 + \frac{1}{\tau_I s}\right) e^{-Ls}}{\tau_p s + 1 + K_c K_p \left(1 + \frac{1}{\tau_I s}\right) e^{-Ls}} $$ The proportional gain $K_c = 18$ provides adequate response with an expected overshoot of approximately **20–25%** and a settling time around $4 \tau_p = 480$ s, which is acceptable for slow-varying chlorine demand in municipal applications. $$\boxed{K_c = 18, \quad \tau_I = 50 \text{ s}, \quad \tau_D = 0 \text{ (PI Control)}}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Stoichiometric Chlorine Demand Calculation **Problem Statement:** Water contains 1.2 mg/L of Fe²⁺ and 0.8 mg/L of Mn²⁺, plus 2.5 mg/L of ammonia as NH₃. Calculate the stoichiometric chlorine demand. ### Step 1: Reaction with Iron $$ 2\text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{Cl}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{Fe}^{3+} + 2\text{Cl}^- $$ Moles of Fe²⁺ per L = $1.2 \times 10^{-3} / 55.85 = 2.149 \times 10^{-5}$ mol/L. Chlorine required per mol of Fe²⁺ = 0.5 mol. So: $$ \text{Cl}_2 \text{ for Fe} = 0.5 \times 2.149 \times 10^{-5} = 1.074 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol/L} $$ Convert to mg/L: $1.074 \times 10^{-5} \times 70.90 \times 10^3 = 0.762$ mg/L. ### Step 2: Reaction with Manganese $$ \text{Mn}^{2+} + \text{Cl}_2 \rightarrow \text{Mn}^{4+} + 2\text{Cl}^- $$ (in simplified form) Moles of Mn²⁺ per L = $0.8 \times 10^{-3} / 54.94 = 1.456 \times 10^{-5}$ mol/L. Chlorine required = $1.456 \times 10^{-5}$ mol/L. $$ \text{Cl}_2 \text{ for Mn} = 1.456 \times 10^{-5} \times 70.90 \times 10^3 = 1.032 \text{ mg/L} $$ ### Step 3: Reaction with Ammonia (Breakpoint Chlorination) $$ 2\text{NH}_3 + 3\text{Cl}_2 \rightarrow \text{N}_2 + 6\text{HCl} $$ Moles of NH₃ per L = $2.5 \times 10^{-3} / 17 = 1.470 \times 10^{-4}$ mol/L. Chlorine required = $1.5 \times 1.470 \times 10^{-4} = 2.206 \times 10^{-4}$ mol/L. $$ \text{Cl}_2 \text{ for NH}_3 = 2.206 \times 10^{-4} \times 70.90 \times 10^3 = 15.64 \text{ mg/L} $$ ### Step 4: Sum total chlorine demand $$ D_{total} = 0.762 + 1.032 + 15.64 = 17.43 \text{ mg/L} $$ ### Result $$\boxed{\text{Stoichiometric Chlorine Demand} \approx 17.43 \text{ mg/L}}$$ This high value illustrates why **breakpoint chlorination** requires accurate dosing control — a chlorinator's control loop must precisely add sufficient chlorine to push beyond the chloramine hump and into the free-chlorine region (see Figure 4 in Section 4). --- ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix – Choosing a Chlorinator Type | Criterion | Gas Chlorinator | Hypochlorinator | Electrolytic | Tablet | |-----------|----------------|------------------|--------------|--------| | **Capital Cost** | High | Medium | Very High | Low | | **Operating Cost** | Lowest | High (NaOCl degrades) | Medium | Medium | | **Safety** | Moderate (gas hazard) | High | Highest | High | | **Dose Precision** | ±2% | ±5% | ±3% | ±10% | | **Max Capacity** | 5000+ kg/day | 50 kg/day | 100 kg/day | 5 kg/day | | **Footprint** | Compact | Large (tanks) | Large | Small | | **Maintenance** | Skilled required | Simple | Skilled (electrodes) | Simple | | **By-products** | None | Chlorate buildup | H₂ gas handling | Sludge | # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Block Diagram – Vacuum Gas Chlorinator Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[CL2 Cylinder
Liquefied Gas] --> B[Manifold Header
Pressure Regulating Valve] B --> C[Inlet Valve
Pressure Reduced to 1.5 bar] C --> D[Vacuum Regulator
Maintain Negative Pressure] D --> E[Rotameter
Visual Flow Indicator] E --> F[Rate of Flow Controller
V Notch Valve Modulator] F --> G[Mixing Chamber
Chlorine Water Solution] G --> H[Venturi Ejector
Vacuum Driven Injector] H --> I[Main Water Line
Process Stream] I --> J[Residual Cl2 Analyzer
Amperometric Sensor] J --> K[PID Controller
PLC or DCS] K --> F L[Motive Water Supply
Pressurized] --> H M[Safety Relief
Vacuum Collapse Shutoff] -.-> D ``` > **Reading the Diagram:** The feedback arrow (from `J` → `K` → `F`) represents the closed-loop chlorine control. The dashed line (`M` → `D`) is the safety interlock. The motive water (`L`) provides both the vacuum source and the motive force for chlorine injection. --- ## 4.2 Closed-Loop Control Block Diagram (Instrumentation Perspective) ```mermaid flowchart LR SP[Setpoint
0.5 ppm Residual Cl2] --> SUM[Summing Junction
e t equals SP minus PV] PV[Process Variable
Measured Residual Cl2] --> SUM SUM --> CTRL[PID Controller
Kc tau I tau D] CTRL --> FC[Final Control Element
Chlorinator Feed Valve] FC --> PROC[Process
Contact Tank / Pipeline] PROC --> SENSOR[Residual Analyzer
Amperometric 4 to 20 mA] SENSOR --> PV DIST[Disturbances
Turbidity NH3 Flow Variation] -.-> PROC ``` > **Disturbance Rejection:** The PID controller compensates for `DIST` (sudden ammonia spikes, flow surges, turbidity changes) by adjusting the chlorinator stroke in real-time. --- ## 4.3 Process Flow Diagram – Water Treatment Plant with Chlorination Stage ```mermaid flowchart TB RAW[Raw Water
Intake] --> COAG[Coagulation
Alum Dosing] COAG --> FLOC[Flocculation
Slow Mixing Tank] FLOC --> SED[Sedimentation
Clarifier] SED --> FILT[Filtration
Rapid Sand Filter] FILT --> CHLOR[Chlorination Stage
Chlorinator Injection Point] CHLOR --> CT[Contact Tank
Minimum 30 min Detention] CT --> RES[Residual Measurement
Free Cl2 Monitor] RES --> CLEAN[Disinfected Water
to Distribution] CHLOR2[Chlorine Supply
Gas Cylinders] --> CHLOR RES -.Feedback.-> CHLOR ``` --- ## 4.4 Breakpoint Chlorination Curve (Graphical Representation) ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Region 1
Chloramines Form
HOCl plus NH3] --> P2[Region 2
Monochloramine
NH2Cl Dominant] P2 --> P3[Region 3
Dichloramine
NHCl2 Formation] P3 --> P4[Region 4
Breakpoint
N2 Gas Released] P4 --> P5[Region 5
Free Chlorine
HOCl and OCl Residual] ``` > **Operating Point:** Industrial chlorinators are always operated in **Region 5** (post-breakpoint) to ensure disinfection is achieved through *free chlorine* rather than combined chlorine, which has weaker biocidal action. --- ## 4.5 P&ID-Style Block Diagram of Hypochlorinator System ```mermaid flowchart LR TK[NaOCl Storage Tank
12 percent Solution] --> PUMP[Metering Pump
Positive Displacement] PUMP --> CAL[Calibration Column
Sight Glass] CAL --> INJ[Injection Quill
Static Mixer] INJ --> LINE[Main Process Line] LSH[Level Switch High] -.-> TK LSL[Level Switch Low] -.-> PUMP FAL[Flow Alarm Low] -.-> PUMP RSP[Residual Sensor Probe] --> PLC[PLC Controller] PLC --> PUMP ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper] **List and briefly explain any three safety features incorporated in a vacuum-operated gas chlorinator.** **Model Answer (3 marks – approximate distribution: 1 mark per feature):** 1. **Vacuum-Operated Principle (1 mark):** The entire gas flow path operates under negative pressure. In the event of any leak, air is drawn inwards rather than chlorine escaping outwards, providing inherent safety. Loss of motive water → vacuum collapse → automatic shutoff. 2. **Pressure Regulating Valve with Restrained Check (1 mark):** The PRV prevents over-pressurization of downstream components, and a built-in check valve stops backflow of water into the chlorine cylinder, preventing corrosion and contamination. 3. **Automatic Gas Detection and Emergency Shutoff (1 mark):** Chlorine gas detectors in the chlorinator room, coupled to exhaust fans and emergency isolation valves, automatically contain any accidental release and sound alarms. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **Define "breakpoint chlorination" and explain its significance in water treatment with a neat sketch.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Breakpoint chlorination is the process of adding chlorine to water in sufficient quantity to oxidize all ammonia, nitrites, and other chlorine-demanding substances completely, so that **free residual chlorine** (HOCl/OCl⁻) is available for disinfection. - **Significance:** Free chlorine is 25–80 times more effective as a disinfectant than combined chlorine (chloramines). Beyond the breakpoint, any additional chlorine appears as free residual, ensuring reliable disinfection regardless of demand variations. (Sketch: a graph with chlorine dose on the X-axis and residual chlorine on the Y-axis, showing the characteristic hump followed by a dip — the breakpoint — then a rising free-chlorine line. Refer to Section 4.4 for the conceptual diagram.) --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – June 2023 / Model Paper 2024] #### (a) [7 Marks] With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a vacuum-type gas chlorinator. Discuss the role of the rate-of-flow controller and ejector. **Model Answer:** **(i) Working Principle (4 marks):** A vacuum-type gas chlorinator withdraws liquefied chlorine gas from a cylinder, regulates it, meters it under vacuum, and injects it into the water main using a venturi ejector. The complete working sequence is: 1. **Gas Withdrawal:** Liquefied Cl₂ vaporizes and flows from the cylinder through a manifold fitted with a pressure-regulating valve (PRV), reducing cylinder pressure (~6 bar) to working pressure (~1.5 bar). 2. **Vacuum Generation:** Motive water flowing through the ejector creates a vacuum (typically 250–500 mm Hg negative) in the chlorinator body. This vacuum is the *driving force* for chlorine flow — no vacuum, no flow. 3. **Metering:** The chlorine gas flows through a calibrated **rotameter** giving a visual readout of dosing rate in kg/day. 4. **Flow Control:** A **rate-of-flow controller (RFC)** senses the differential pressure across the rotameter float and modulates a V-notch or diaphragm control valve to maintain the setpoint. 5. **Mixing and Injection:** Inside the chlorinator, chlorine gas mixes with a small auxiliary water stream to form a chlorine-water solution. The ejector's vacuum draws this solution and injects it into the main water pipeline through a diffuser. **(ii) Role of Rate-of-Flow Controller (1.5 marks):** The RFC ensures that the actual flow rate matches the operator-setpoint. It compensates for: - Drop in cylinder pressure as gas is consumed. - Variation in motive water pressure at the ejector. - Changes in vacuum due to system dynamics. This is a **local feedback loop** nested inside the overall residual-chlorine feedback loop. **(iii) Role of Ejector (1.5 marks):** The ejector (also called the injector or eductor) serves three functions simultaneously: - **Vacuum source:** Generates the negative pressure that drives the entire chlorinator. - **Solution preparation point:** Mixes chlorine gas with motive water before injection. - **Delivery pump:** Provides the head to inject the chlorine solution into a slightly pressurized main line. The ejector uses the **Venturi principle**: high-velocity motive water through a converging nozzle creates a low-pressure region that entrains chlorine. --- #### (b) [7 Marks] A chlorinator control loop uses a residual chlorine analyzer with a setpoint of 0.6 mg/L. The process gain is Kp = 0.8 (ppm/ppm) and time constant τ = 90 s. Calculate the PI controller settings using the Ziegler-Nichols open-loop method, assuming dead time L = 20 s. Also explain how a SCADA system supervises this loop. **Model Solution:** **Given:** - $K_p = 0.8$ - $\tau_p = 90$ s - $L = 20$ s - Controller: PI **Step 1: Ziegler-Nichols PI Tuning Equations (1 mark)** For PI: $$ K_c = 0.9 \frac{\tau_p}{K_p \cdot L} $$ $$ \tau_I = 3.33 \, L $$ **Step 2: Compute Kc (1 mark)** $$ K_c = 0.9 \times \frac{90}{0.8 \times 20} = 0.9 \times \frac{90}{16} = 0.9 \times 5.625 = 5.0625 $$ $$\boxed{K_c \approx 5.06}$$ **Step 3: Compute τI (1 mark)** $$ \tau_I = 3.33 \times 20 = 66.6 \text{ s} \approx 67 \text{ s} $$ **Step 4: Compute Controller Output Range (1 mark)** For a setpoint step from 0.4 to 0.6 mg/L, error $e = 0.2$ mg/L. Steady-state controller output = $K_c \cdot e = 5.06 \times 0.2 = 1.012$ % stroke.
