If the chlorinator has a stroke range of 0–100% corresponding to 0–10 kg/day chlorine feed, then the controller commands: $$ \text{Feed Rate} = \frac{1.012}{100} \times 10 = 0.1012 \text{ kg/day per 0.2 ppm error} $$ **Step 5: SCADA Supervision of the Loop (3 marks)** The SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition) system provides the following supervisory functions over the chlorinator loop: 1. **Real-Time Trend Display:** SCADA plots residual chlorine, flow rate, and chlorinator stroke (%) on HMI mimics for the operator. 2. **Setpoint Adjustment:** Operator can modify the residual setpoint remotely from the control room. 3. **Alarm Management:** High/low residual, cylinder low-pressure, analyzer fault, and motive-water failure alarms are configured in SCADA with priority levels (e.g., HH, H, L, LL). 4. **Data Logging & Reporting:** Historical data archived for regulatory compliance (e.g., daily compliance reports to Pollution Control Board). 5. **Auto/Manual Cascade:** SCADA allows switching between automatic PID mode and manual operator control during maintenance. 6. **Interlocks:** Prevents cylinder changeover while chlorine is being fed; enforces analyzer warm-up time before closing the loop. --- ### QUESTION B — [Alternative 14-Mark Question] #### (a) [7 Marks] Compare gas chlorination with hypochlorination. Under what conditions would each be preferred? Discuss the chemistry of chlorine in water. **Model Answer:** **Comparison Table (3 marks):** | Parameter | Gas Chlorination | Hypochlorination | |-----------|-------------------|-------------------| | **Chemical form** | Cl₂ gas | NaOCl liquid (12–15%) | | **Storage** | Pressurized cylinders (hazardous) | Tanks (non-pressurized) | | **Equipment cost** | High | Moderate | | **Operating cost** | Low (Cl₂ is cheap) | High (NaOCl degrades ~10%/month) | | **Safety** | Toxic gas risk | Splash hazard, lower toxicity | | **Capacity** | Up to 5000 kg/day | Up to 50 kg/day | | **Application** | Large municipal, industrial | Small communities, swimming pools | **Preference Criteria (2 marks):** - **Gas chlorination preferred when:** Plant capacity > 100 m³/h, long-term economy matters, trained operators available, on-site chlorine storage is feasible. - **Hypochlorination preferred when:** Capacity < 100 m³/h, community safety is paramount, intermittent operation, no trained gas-handling staff. **Chemistry of Chlorine in Water (2 marks):** When Cl₂ dissolves in water: $$ \text{Cl}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{HOCl} + \text{HCl} $$ Hypochlorous acid (HOCl) then dissociates: $$ \text{HOCl} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{OCl}^- $$ The equilibrium depends on pH: - At pH < 6: HOCl dominates (>90%) — strongest biocidal form. - At pH 6–8: mixture of HOCl and OCl⁻. - At pH > 9: OCl⁻ dominates — much weaker disinfectant (~100× less effective). For sodium hypochlorite: $$ \text{NaOCl} \rightarrow \text{Na}^+ + \text{OCl}^- $$ This directly provides hypochlorite ion; hence pH control is critical for effective chlorination. --- #### (b) [7 Marks] Explain the closed-loop chlorinator control scheme with a residual chlorine analyzer. Sketch the breakpoint chlorination curve and identify the operating zone. **Model Answer:** **Closed-Loop Control Scheme (4 marks):** The control loop operates as follows: 1. **Primary Measurement:** Residual chlorine analyzer (amperometric or DPD colorimetric) installed downstream of the contact tank measures free chlorine continuously and outputs a 4–20 mA signal corresponding to 0–2 mg/L. 2. **Setpoint Comparison:** A PID controller compares the measured value to the desired residual setpoint (typically 0.5 mg/L for potable water). 3. **Controller Action:** Based on error, the controller computes an output signal (0–100%) that is sent to the chlorinator's rate-of-flow controller. 4. **Chlorinator Response:** The chlorinator modulates chlorine gas/liquid feed rate accordingly. 5. **Disturbance Compensation:** When raw water quality changes (e.g., ammonia spike, turbidity surge), the analyzer reading drops, error increases, and chlorine feed is automatically increased to compensate. **Block-Level Flow:** $$\text{Setpoint} \rightarrow \text{Error Calculator} \rightarrow \text{PID} \rightarrow \text{Chlorinator} \rightarrow \text{Process} \rightarrow \text{Analyzer} \rightarrow \text{Feedback}$$ **Breakpoint Chlorination Curve (3 marks):** Refer to Section 4.4 — the curve has five regions: - **Region 1–3 (Pre-Breakpoint):** Chlorine reacts with ammonia to form mono- and dichloramines; residual rises, peaks, and then drops as chloramines are oxidized. - **Region 4 (Breakpoint):** All chloramines destroyed; nitrogen gas released; residual chlorine drops to near zero. - **Region 5 (Post-Breakpoint, Operating Zone):** Free chlorine (HOCl/OCl⁻) appears and rises linearly with added dose. **Operating Zone Identification:** Industrial chlorinators are operated in **Region 5**, typically at a dose 1–2 mg/L beyond the breakpoint, to ensure complete disinfection and a reliable free-chlorine residual (0.5–1.0 mg/L) at the consumer end. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing gas flow units:** Students often write "kg/hr" when the rotameter reads "kg/day" — read the problem statement carefully. > 2. **Forgetting absolute pressure:** When applying ideal gas law, ALWAYS add 1.013 bar to gauge pressure. Failure to do so loses 2–3 marks. > 3. **Wrong stoichiometry in breakpoint:** NH₃ requires 1.5 mol Cl₂ per mol NH₃ — students often mistakenly use 1:1. > 4. **Ignoring pH effect on chlorine speciation:** HOCl ↔ OCl⁻ equilibrium is pH-dependent; examiners explicitly test this. > 5. **PID tuning without justifying the choice of method:** Always name the tuning rule (Z-N, Cohen-Coon, Lambda) before applying its formula. > 6. **Skipping the motive-water description in ejector operation:** Examiners expect a Bernoulli/Venturi principle explanation, not just "it creates vacuum." --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Chlorinator (Module 1)** ### Core Concepts (Memorize) - Chlorinator = controlled chlorine dosing system with closed-loop feedback. - Two main categories: **Gas chlorinators** (vacuum/pressure) and **Liquid chlorinators** (hypochlorinators). - Vacuum operation is the *defining safety feature* of modern gas chlorinators. ### Key Components - Manifold + Pressure Regulating Valve (PRV) - Vacuum Regulator + Restrained Check Valve - Rotameter (visual flow indication) - Rate-of-Flow Controller (RFC) with V-notch valve - Mixing Chamber - Venturi Ejector (vacuum source + injector) - Residual Chlorine Analyzer (feedback sensor) - PID Controller + SCADA HMI ### Critical Equations - Dose: $D = V_{Cl} C_{Cl} / Q_{water}$ - Ideal Gas: $PV = nRT$ - Chick-Watson: $\eta = 1 - e^{-k CT}$ - PID Output: $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\, dt + K_d \dot{e}$ ### Process Chemistry - $\text{Cl}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{HOCl} + \text{HCl}$ - $\text{HOCl} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{OCl}^-$ (pH-dependent) - Ammonia demand: $2\text{NH}_3 + 3\text{Cl}_2 \rightarrow \text{N}_2 + 6\text{HCl}$ ### Standards & Numbers - **Free chlorine residual:** 0.5 mg/L (WHO) - **Contact time:** ≥ 30 minutes - **Cylinder pressure:** ~6 bar at 20 °C - **Working pressure:** ~1.5 bar - **Operating zone on breakpoint curve:** Region 5 (post-breakpoint) ### Industrial Applications - Municipal water treatment - Cooling water (power plants) - Pulp & paper bleaching - Wastewater effluent disinfection - Food & beverage CIP - Swimming pool sanitization ### Safety Considerations - Vacuum principle prevents leakage outward. - Automatic shutoff on vacuum collapse. - Chlorine gas detectors + emergency ventilation. - Neutralization scrubbers (NaOH) for accidental release. - Personal protective equipment (PPE) for cylinder handling. ### Control Loop Perspective - PID controller receives 4–20 mA from residual analyzer. - Output modulates chlorinator RFC. - SCADA supervises alarms, trends, interlocks. - Cascade control possible: flow rate as inner loop, residual as outer loop. ### KTU Exam Strategy - Always draw a **block diagram** with feedback arrow. - State **assumptions** before deriving. - Use **units consistently** (mg/L, kg/day, m³/day). - Mention **pH effect** when discussing chlorine chemistry. - Cite **standards** (WHO 0.5 mg/L, AWWA contact time) for full marks.

topics.title: Chemical Reduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT864 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN LARGE SCALE INDUSTRIES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Chemical Reduction # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Chemical Reduction** in the context of large-scale industrial processes refers to a class of chemical reactions in which an element or compound gains one or more electrons, resulting in a decrease in its oxidation state. In an industrial setting, this typically involves the removal of oxygen from a metal ore (oxide, hydroxide, or carbonate) to produce the pure metal or a lower-oxide form, or the addition of hydrogen to a substrate in refining operations. Mathematically, a general reduction half-reaction is expressed as: $$M^{n+} + ne^{-} \rightarrow M$$ where $M^{n+}$ is the oxidized species, $n$ is the number of electrons transferred, and $M$ is the reduced (metallic) product. The simultaneous reaction—the **oxidation half-reaction**—occurs at the reductant, and the combined pair constitutes a **redox (reduction-oxidation) reaction**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Chemical reduction in large-scale industries encompasses both **pyrometallurgical reduction** (using carbon, CO, H₂, or active metals as reductants at high temperature) and **hydrometallurgical/electrolytic reduction** (aqueous-phase or electrolysis-based processes). The instrumentation engineer is concerned with **temperature, pressure, stoichiometry, reductant feed rate, and off-gas composition**. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine a piece of rusty iron (iron oxide, Fe₂O₃) sitting in a furnace with coke (carbon, C). The oxygen atoms in the rust are "stolen" by the carbon, forming carbon dioxide (CO₂), and what remains is **pure metallic iron**. The carbon "robbed" the iron of its oxygen. That robbery—where one substance loses oxygen while another gains it—is **chemical reduction**. Think of it like this: - **Reduction = removal of oxygen, OR gain of electrons, OR gain of hydrogen** - **Oxidation = gain of oxygen, OR loss of electrons, OR loss of hydrogen** - A helpful mnemonic device: **OIL RIG** — **O**xidation **I**s **L**oss (of electrons), **R**eduction **I**s **G**ain (of electrons). A second analogy: Consider a sponge soaking up water. The sponge "takes on" something. Similarly, a substance undergoing reduction "takes on" electrons. In industry, this process is performed in **massive reactors** (blast furnaces, rotary kilns, fluidized bed reactors, electrolytic cells) where the engineer must precisely control temperature profiles, residence times, and reductant-to-ore ratios to achieve the target metallurgical yield. ## 1.3 Common Industrial Reductants and Their Roles | Reductant | Symbol/Formula | Typical Use | Operating Temperature | |-----------|----------------|-------------|----------------------| | Coke (Carbon) | C | Iron ore reduction (blast furnace) | **800 °C – 1600 °C** | | Carbon Monoxide | CO | Direct reduction, sponge iron | **700 °C – 1000 °C** | | Hydrogen | H₂ | Direct reduced iron (DRI), ammonia synthesis, desulfurization | **400 °C – 900 °C** | | Natural Gas / Methane | CH₄ | DRI in Midrex/Energiron plants | **800 °C – 1050 °C** | | Aluminum (Thermite) | Al | Chromium, manganese, molybdenum production | **> 2000 °C** (exothermic) | | Ferrous Sulfate / Na₂S₂O₄ | Chemical | Effluent treatment (Cr⁶⁺ → Cr³⁺) | **25 °C – 60 °C** | | Electric Current | e⁻ | Aluminum, zinc, copper electrowinning | **~ 950 °C (Al)** | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metric Note:** Industrial yields are typically reported as **percent metal recovery (% Recovery)**, defined as: > $$\% \text{ Recovery} = \frac{\text{Mass of metal obtained}}{\text{Mass of metal in feed ore}} \times 100$$ # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Thermodynamic Foundation of Reduction The feasibility of a reduction reaction is governed by the **Gibbs free energy change (ΔG)**, the **Ellingham diagram** concept, and the **equilibrium constant (K)**. For a reaction to be thermodynamically spontaneous at a given temperature, the following condition **must be satisfied**: $$\Delta G = \Delta H - T \Delta S < 0$$ where $\Delta H$ is the enthalpy change, $T$ is the absolute temperature (in **Kelvin, K**), and $\Delta S$ is the entropy change. The temperature at which $\Delta G = 0$ defines the **thermodynamic threshold** for the reaction. For an oxide reduction of the general form: $$M_xO_y + yC \rightarrow xM + yCO$$ the Ellingham diagram plots $\Delta G^{\circ}$ versus temperature for the formation of oxides. **A metal oxide can be reduced by a reductant whose oxide-formation line lies *below* that of the metal oxide** on the Ellingham diagram at the operating temperature. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters to an instrumentation engineer:** The Ellingham diagram is the *first-pass feasibility tool* before designing a temperature control loop. If the chosen reductant has a $\Delta G$ curve above the target oxide at the planned operating temperature, the reaction will not proceed—**no amount of instrumentation can fix bad thermodynamics**. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Application | |---|-------------------|------------|-------|-------------| | 1 | Reduction half-reaction (general) | $M^{n+} + ne^{-} \rightarrow M$ | — | Electrolytic winning | | 2 | Standard Electrode Potential | $E^{\circ}$ | **Volts (V)** | Predicting spontaneity; positive $E^{\circ}_{cell}$ = spontaneous redox | | 3 | Nernst Equation | $E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$ | V | Non-standard condition EMF | | 4 | Faraday's Law of Electrolysis | $m = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$ | grams | Mass of metal deposited | | 5 | Percent Recovery | $\frac{m_{out}}{m_{in}} \times 100$ | % | Process efficiency KPI | | 6 | Stoichiometric Air/Reductant Ratio | $R_{s} = \frac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}}$ | dimensionless | Combustion control | | 7 | Arrhenius Rate Law | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$ | s⁻¹ | Reduction kinetics | | 8 | Ellingham Threshold | $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$ | J/mol | Feasibility temperature | | 9 | Ellingham ΔG condition | $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ | J/mol | Spontaneity test | | 10 | Energy Balance (Reactor) | $Q = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ | J | Furnace heat duty | | 11 | Sinter strength (pellet) | $\sigma = \frac{F}{A}$ | N/m² | Burden permeability | | 12 | Off-gas CO/CO₂ ratio | $\eta_{CO} = \frac{\%CO}{\%CO + \%CO_2}$ | dimensionless | Indirect reduction index | | 13 | Stoichiometric O₂ demand | $V_{O_2} = \frac{22.4}{12}(C) + \frac{22.4}{32}(S) + \dots$ | Nm³ | Combustion control | | 14 | Residence Time | $\tau = \frac{V_{reactor}}{\dot{V}_{feed}}$ | s | Reactor sizing | | 15 | Space Velocity (GHSV) | $GHSV = \frac{\dot{V}_{gas}}{V_{catalyst}}$ | h⁻¹ | Catalytic reduction | > [!NOTE] > In the Nernst equation, the universal gas constant $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$ and Faraday's constant $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}$. These are **standard physical constants** and must be memorized for KTU exams. ## 2.3 Mechanism of Industrial Reduction The reduction pathway in a typical pyrometallurgical operation proceeds through **three sequential stages**, and the instrumentation engineer must monitor each: - **Stage 1 – Drying & Pre-heating:** The burden (ore + flux + reductant) is dried at 100 °C – 200 °C. **Control variable:** Burden moisture (infrared / capacitance sensor); stack gas humidity. - **Stage 2 – Indirect Reduction (wüstite stage):** $FeO + CO \rightarrow Fe + CO_2$. This is a **solid-gas reaction** dominated by the Boudouard equilibrium $2CO \rightleftharpoons CO_2 + C$. **Control variable:** Off-gas CO/CO₂ ratio (infrared analyzer). - **Stage 3 – Direct Reduction (smelting):** $FeO + C \rightarrow Fe + CO$. Highly endothermic, requires **tuyere-zone temperatures of 1500 °C – 2000 °C**. **Control variable:** Tuyere temperature (optical pyrometer / R-type thermocouple), hot-blast temperature, oxygen enrichment. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-world engineering utility:** The CO/CO₂ ratio in the top gas of a blast furnace is the **single most important indicator of thermal efficiency and reduction progress**. A target ratio of approximately **0.65 – 0.70** is maintained in modern practice. Drifting below 0.55 signals excessive direct reduction (carbon wasting); drifting above 0.80 signals insufficient reduction (iron losses in slag). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Faraday's Law of Electrolysis (for Electrolytic Reduction) Faraday's first law of electrolysis is **the** governing equation for electrolytic reduction processes such as aluminum smelting (Hall-Héroult), zinc electrowinning, and copper refining. The complete derivation from first principles is given below. ### Statement The mass $m$ of a substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electric charge $Q$ passed through the electrolyte. ### Step 1 — Define the charge The fundamental relationship between current, time, and charge is: $$Q = I \cdot t$$ where $I$ is the constant current in **Amperes (A)** and $t$ is the time of electrolysis in **seconds (s)**. ### Step 2 — Moles of electrons transferred Since one mole of electrons carries the Faraday constant of charge: $$Q = n_{e^-} \cdot F$$ where $n_{e^-}$ is the number of moles of electrons and $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}$. Equating the two expressions: $$I \cdot t = n_{e^-} \cdot F$$ ### Step 3 — Moles of metal deposited For the reduction half-reaction $M^{n+} + ne^- \rightarrow M$, one mole of metal requires $n$ moles of electrons. Therefore, the moles of metal deposited is: $$n_M = \frac{n_{e^-}}{n} = \frac{I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$ ### Step 4 — Convert to mass Multiplying by the molar mass $M$ (g/mol) of the metal: $$m = n_M \cdot M = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$ This is the working equation. Substituting numerical values yields the deposited mass in grams. > [!NOTE] > The equation in its **complete algebraic form** is the only acceptable form for KTU board answers. The following worked example is the **canonical examiner's test problem**. ### Worked Example (KTU-Type) **Question:** An electrolytic cell is used to refine copper from a CuSO₄ solution. A current of 2000 A is passed for 8 hours. Calculate the mass of copper deposited. (Molar mass of Cu = 63.5 g/mol, n = 2, F = 96,485 C/mol) **Solution:** Step 1 — Convert time to seconds: $$t = 8 \text{ h} \times 3600 \text{ s/h} = 28{,}800 \text{ s}$$ Step 2 — Apply Faraday's law: $$m = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F} = \frac{63.5 \times 2000 \times 28{,}800}{2 \times 96{,}485}$$ Step 3 — Compute numerator: $$63.5 \times 2000 = 127{,}000$$ $$127{,}000 \times 28{,}800 = 3.6576 \times 10^{9}$$ Step 4 — Compute denominator: $$2 \times 96{,}485 = 192{,}970$$ Step 5 — Final division: $$m = \frac{3.6576 \times 10^{9}}{1.9297 \times 10^{5}} \approx 18{,}955 \text{ g} \approx 18.96 \text{ kg}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Answer:** Approximately **18.96 kg of copper** is deposited. This matches the typical KTU numerical-answer style: a single boxed numerical value with units. ## 3.2 Derivation: Equilibrium Constant from Ellingham Data Given a metal oxide reduction: $$2 M O + C \rightarrow 2 M + CO_2$$ At equilibrium ($\Delta G^{\circ} = 0$): $$\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K_{eq}$$ Rearranging for $K_{eq}$: $$K_{eq} = e^{-\Delta G^{\circ} / RT}$$ If the Ellingham diagram gives $\Delta G^{\circ}_{MO}$ at temperature $T$, the engineer can compute the equilibrium partial-pressure ratio required to drive reduction to completion: $$\frac{P_{CO_2}}{P_{CO}} = e^{-\Delta G^{\circ} / RT}$$ > [!NOTE] > In the **Midrex direct reduction** process, the target off-gas ratio of H₂/CO is set to drive this ratio to the desired side of equilibrium, ensuring > 92% metallization of the iron ore pellets. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Reduction Process Selection | Process Type | Reductant | Reactor | Temp Range | Key Controlled Variable | Major Industrial Product | |--------------|-----------|---------|------------|-------------------------|--------------------------| | Blast Furnace (BF) | C (coke) + CO | Shaft furnace | **1200 – 2000 °C** | Hot-blast T, O₂ enrichment, top-gas CO/CO₂ | Pig iron | | Direct Reduced Iron (DRI) | CH₄ / H₂ | Shaft / Fluidized bed | **800 – 1050 °C** | Reformer T, gas flow | Sponge iron (for EAF) | | Hall-Héroult (Al) | Electric current | Electrolytic cell | **~ 950 °C** (cryolite melt) | Cell voltage (~ 4.2 V), anode-cathode distance | Aluminum | | Fluidized Bed Roasting | SO₂ (generated) | Fluidized bed | **600 – 900 °C** | Bed T, fluidization velocity | ZnO, sulfide ore calcines | | SCR (DeNOx) | NH₃ / Urea | Catalytic reactor | **300 – 450 °C** | NH₃ slip, NOₓ at outlet, GHSV | Clean flue gas | | Thermite Welding | Al powder | Open hearth | **> 2500 °C** | Ignition timing | Rail welding, Cr/Mn production | | Hydrometallurgical (Cu, Zn) | Fe, SO₂, H₂ | Leach tanks + SX/EW | **25 – 80 °C** | pH, ORP, solution potential | Cu cathode, Zn SHG | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Integrated Blast Furnace Reduction & Control Architecture The figure below shows a **Block-Level Functional Architecture** of a modern blast furnace instrumented for chemical reduction monitoring and control. Note: Mermaid is used here as the rendering engine for the process flow; all node labels are kept in **clean uppercase alphanumeric text** (no markdown formatting inside the label strings) to satisfy the diagram-compilation safeguard. ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["ORE BURDEN SILO
Fe2O3 PELLET SINTER LUMP ORE"] --> nodeB["SKIP CAR OR BELT CONVEYOR
CHARGING SYSTEM"] nodeC["COKE OVEN GAS COAL
FUEL STORAGE"] --> nodeD["COKE OVEN
DELAYED COKING"] nodeD --> nodeE["COKE STOCKHOUSE
SCREENING WEIGHING"] nodeE --> nodeF["TOWER CHARGER TOP OF FURNACE"] nodeB --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG["STACK SHAFT ZONE
INDIRECT REDUCTION 800 TO 1200 C"] nodeG --> nodeH["BOSH AND BELLY ZONE
DIRECT REDUCTION 1200 TO 1500 C"] nodeH --> nodeI["TUYERE AND HEARTH ZONE
SMELTING 1500 TO 2000 C"] nodeJ["HOT BLAST STOVES
COWPER 1100 C AIR"] --> nodeI nodeK["OXYGEN ENRICHMENT PLANT
VPSA OR CRYOGENIC"] --> nodeI nodeI --> nodeL["TAPPING NOTCH AND RUNNER
HOT METAL AND SLAG"] nodeL --> nodeM["TORPEDO LADLE
DESULFURIZATION STATION"] nodeI --> nodeN["TOP GAS DUCT
CO CO2 H2 N2 MIXTURE"] nodeN --> nodeO["GAS CLEANING
DUST SCRUBBER ESP"] nodeO --> nodeP["GAS HOLDER
RECOVERY TO STOVES"] nodeI --> nodeQ["BLOWER AND DUST COLLECTION
PRIMARY AND SECONDARY"] nodeL --> nodeR["SLAG GRANULATION
WATER QUENCH"] nodeQ --> nodeS["CONTROL ROOM DCS
HMI SCADA PLATFORM"] nodeP --> nodeS nodeJ --> nodeS nodeK --> nodeS nodeO --> nodeS nodeL --> nodeS nodeS --> nodeT["INSTRUMENTATION LAYER
T P FLOW LEVEL O2 CO CO2"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The vertical chain nodeA → nodeB → nodeF → nodeG → nodeH → nodeI → nodeL represents the **physical flow of burden and metal** through the furnace. The horizontal branches (nodeJ, nodeK, nodeN, nodeO) represent **input/output streams** (hot blast, oxygen, top gas). The nodeS hub is the **Distributed Control System (DCS)** that closes all control loops. ## 4.2 Closed-Loop Control Architecture for a Fluidized-Bed Reduction Reactor The following Mermaid block renders the **closed-loop control architecture** for a typical fluidized-bed direct-reduction reactor (such as a Circofer or FINMET plant). It illustrates how the instrumentation layer feeds the controller, which then actuates valves and variable-speed drives. ```mermaid flowchart LR node1["FEED ORE INLET
MASS FLOW TRANSMITTER FT"] --> node2["FLUIDIZED BED REACTOR
REDUCTION ZONE 850 C"] node2 --> node3["GAS DISTRIBUTOR PLATE
PRESSURE DROP DP TRANSMITTER"] node2 --> node4["BED TEMPERATURE
MULTIPOINT THERMOCOUPLE TT"] node2 --> node5["OFF GAS ANALYZER
CO CO2 H2O IR ANALYZER AT"] node3 --> node6["PID CONTROLLER PIC
PRESSURE DROP SETPOINT 10 KPA"] node4 --> node7["PID CONTROLLER TIC
BED TEMPERATURE SETPOINT 850 C"] node5 --> node8["RATIO CONTROLLER FFIC
H2 TO CO RATIO 1.5 TO 1"] node6 --> node9["CONTROL VALVE
FLUIDIZING GAS FLOW"] node7 --> node10["CONTROL VALVE
COOLING GAS INJECTION"] node8 --> node11["CONTROL VALVE node12["ACTUATED FIELD DEVICE"] node10 --> node12 node11 --> node12 node12 --> node2 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Instrumentation Symbol Convention Used (ISA 5.1):** The first letter denotes the **measured variable** (F = Flow, T = Temperature, P = Pressure, A = Analysis) and the succeeding letters denote the **function** (T = Transmitter, I = Indicator, C = Controller, V = Valve). For example, **TT** = Temperature Transmitter, **PIC** = Pressure Indicating Controller. ## 4.3 Signal Conditioning Path for an Off-Gas Analyzer The diagram below depicts the **signal chain** from the sampling probe inside the top-gas duct to the DCS display. In a blast furnace, the harsh dusty, hot, moist environment requires sophisticated sample-conditioning. ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["SAMPLING PROBE
FILTERED WATER COOLED SS316"] --> nodeB["PRIMARY FILTER
PARTICULATE REMOVAL 5 MICRON"] nodeB --> nodeC["CONDITIONER UNIT nodeD["SECONDARY FILTER nodeE["NDIR ANALYZER nodeF["NDIR ANALYZER nodeG["PARAMAGNETIC O2 nodeH["MULTIPLEXER AND A TO D nodeH nodeG --> nodeH nodeH --> nodeI["DCS PROCESSOR nodeJ["OPERATOR HMI nodeK["ALARM MANAGEMENT nodeL["ADVANCED CONTROL [!NOTE] > **Why NDIR (Non-Dispersive Infrared)?** CO and CO₂ absorb strongly at characteristic infrared wavelengths (CO: **4.67 μm**; CO₂: **4.26 μm**). NDIR analyzers are rugged, fast (T₉₀ < 2 s), and immune to the corrosive flue-gas matrix, making them the **industry standard for top-gas composition analysis**. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Short-Answer Conceptual **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q:** Define chemical reduction with one industrial example. List the three common industrial reductants. **Model Answer:** **Chemical reduction** is a chemical reaction in which a substance gains electrons, accompanied by a decrease in its oxidation state. In large-scale industries, it most commonly refers to the **removal of oxygen from a metal ore** to yield the free metal. **Industrial Example:** Reduction of hematite ore (Fe₂O₃) by carbon monoxide inside a blast furnace: $$Fe_2O_3 + 3CO \rightarrow 2Fe + 3CO_2$$ **Three Common Industrial Reductants:** 1. **Coke (Carbon, C)** — used in blast furnaces for iron and ferroalloy production. 2. **Hydrogen (H₂)** — used in direct reduced iron (DRI) plants and ammonia synthesis. 3. **Aluminum (Al)** — used in thermite reactions for chromium, manganese, and molybdenum. > [!NOTE] > **Mark allocation tip (3 marks):** Definition = 1 mark, Equation = 1 mark, Reductants list = 1 mark. Examiners *strictly* look for the correct symbol/notation in equations. ### Question 2: Short-Answer Conceptual **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Q:** State Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis. Write the expression for the mass of metal deposited. **Model Answer:** **Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis** states that *"the mass of a substance liberated at an electrode during electrolysis is directly proportional to the quantity of electric charge passed through the electrolyte."* **Mathematical Expression:** $$m = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$ where $m$ = mass of metal deposited (grams), $M$ = molar mass of the metal (g/mol), $I$ = current (A), $t$ = time (s), $n$ = number of electrons in the reduction half-reaction, and $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol**. > [!NOTE] > **Mark allocation tip (3 marks):** Statement = 1 mark, Formula = 1.5 marks, Definition of constants = 0.5 mark. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### Question Choice A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]** **Q (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **integrated blast furnace reduction process** and identify the **major instrumentation parameters** monitored at each zone. (7 marks) **Model Answer:** A modern blast furnace is a **shaft-type counter-current reactor** in which iron-bearing burden descends by gravity while ascending reducing gas (a mixture of CO, H₂, and N₂) reduces the ore progressively. The functional block diagram showing zone-wise instrumentation is given below. **Zone 1 — Stockhouse / Top Charging:** - **Function:** Storage, weighing, and charging of iron ore, sinter, pellets, and coke. - **Instruments:** Load cells (weighing), limit switches, level switches, and a skip-car position transmitter. **Zone 2 — Stack / Shaft (Indirect Reduction Zone):** - **Function:** The hematite (Fe₂O₃) is first reduced to magnetite (Fe₃O₄) and then to wüstite (FeO) by CO and H₂. - **Instruments:** Multiple thermocouples (K-type) at depths of 5 m, 10 m, 15 m, 20 m; burden descent radar; charging-sequence controller. **Zone 3 — Bosh / Belly (Direct Reduction & Melting):** - **Function:** Wüstite is reduced to metallic iron by solid carbon; the iron begins to melt. - **Instruments:** Sidewall thermocouples, gas-sampling probes for CO/CO₂ analysis, microwave cohesion meter. **Zone 4 — Tuyere / Hearth (Smelting):** - **Function:** Final melting of iron and slag; combustion of injected pulverized coal and oxygen. - **Instruments:** Tuyere pyrometers (optical, two-color), hot-blast temperature thermocouples, oxygen flow meters, top pressure transmitter. **Zone 5 — Tapping & Slag Handling:** - **Function:** Periodic tapping of hot metal and slag. - **Instruments:** Trough temperature IR camera, stopper-rod position transmitter, flow meters for granulation water. **Q (b)** A **Direct Reduced Iron (DRI)** plant uses a mixture of **75% H₂ and 25% CO** by volume to reduce iron oxide pellets at **900 °C**. The off-gas leaving the reactor contains **40% H₂, 18% CO, 30% H₂O, and 12% CO₂** (dry basis after water removal). Calculate: (i) the H₂/CO ratio in the inlet gas, (ii) the H₂/CO ratio in the dry off-gas, and (iii) the **percent utilization** of the reducing gas mixture. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Inlet H₂/CO ratio:** $$R_{inlet} = \frac{75\%}{25\%} = 3.00$$ **Step 2 — Dry off-gas H₂/CO ratio:** $$R_{outlet} = \frac{40\%}{18\%} \approx 2.22$$ **Step 3 — Percent utilization of reductant gas:** The reductant utilization is defined as the **mole fraction of H₂ and CO that was actually consumed** in the reduction. From the inlet and outlet compositions (assuming an inert-free basis for simplicity): The H₂ consumed is $75 - 40 = 35$ percentage points. The CO consumed is $25 - 18 = 7$ percentage points. Total consumed = $35 + 7 = 42$ percentage points. Total inlet reductant = $75 + 25 = 100$ percentage points. $$\eta_{reductant} = \frac{42}{100} \times 100 = 42\%$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students commonly confuse **"inlet H₂/CO ratio" with "stoichiometric H₂/CO ratio"**. The former is simply the arithmetic ratio of the feed gas composition; the latter is dictated by the reduction chemistry ($FeO + H_2 \rightarrow Fe + H_2O$ and $FeO + CO \rightarrow Fe + CO_2$ each require 1 mole of reductant per mole of FeO). Examiners deduct **2 marks** if the difference is not explicitly stated. --- ### Question Choice B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1]** **Q (a)** Explain the **Ellingham diagram** concept. How does it help the instrumentation engineer select a suitable reductant and operating temperature for an industrial reduction process? (7 marks) **Model Answer:** The **Ellingham diagram** is a plot of the **standard Gibbs free energy of formation ($\Delta G^{\circ}_f$)** of metal oxides (per mole of O₂ consumed) versus **absolute temperature (T)**. It is the *primary thermodynamic tool* for predicting the feasibility of oxide reduction. **Key Features:** 1. **Most oxide-formation lines have a positive slope**, because the entropy decreases when a gas (O₂) is consumed in forming a solid/liquid oxide. Hence higher temperatures *generally make oxide formation less spontaneous* — the lines slope upward. 2. **The carbon-formation line (2C + O₂ → 2CO) has a negative slope** because the reaction produces 2 moles of gas from 1 mole of gas, *increasing* entropy dramatically. **At approximately 700 °C, the carbon line crosses the lines of nearly all common metal oxides**, which is why **carbon (coke) is the universal industrial reductant for iron, zinc, lead, and tin** at high temperatures. 3. **A reductant R can reduce a metal oxide MO if the line for R's oxide lies *below* the line for MO at the operating temperature** — i.e., $\Delta G^{\circ}_{R\text{-oxide}} < \Delta G^{\circ}_{MO}$. **Selection Logic (Worked Example for Fe₂O₃ reduction):** At **1000 °C** (1273 K), the Ellingham diagram gives approximately: - $\Delta G^{\circ}_f$ for $2Fe + \tfrac{3}{2}O_2 \rightarrow Fe_2O_3$: approximately **−250 kJ/mol O₂** - $\Delta G^{\circ}_f$ for $2C + O_2 \rightarrow 2CO$: approximately **−350 kJ/mol O₂** Since the C→CO line is more negative (below the Fe₂O₃ line), carbon **can** reduce Fe₂O₃ at 1000 °C — a fact exploited in every blast furnace on Earth. **Utility to the Instrumentation Engineer:** - **Sets the lower bound on operating temperature** (e.g., 700 °C minimum for carbon-based reduction). - **Determines the required excess reductant** to drive $\Delta G$ strongly negative. - **Informs the off-gas composition target** (e.g., CO/CO₂ ratio) for closed-loop control. - **Predicts refractory selection**: at 1600 °C tuyere temperatures, only MgO-C or Al₂O₃-C refractories are stable — Ellingham analysis confirms this. **Q (b)** An **aluminum smelter (Hall-Héroult cell)** operates at a cell voltage of **4.2 V** with a current of **300 kA**. The current efficiency is **93%** and the molar mass of Al is **27 g/mol**. Calculate the **daily aluminum production** of one cell in kilograms. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Theoretical mass per Faraday:** The reduction half-reaction is $Al^{3+} + 3e^- \rightarrow Al$, so $n = 3$. $$m_{theoretical} = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F} = \frac{27 \times 300{,}000 \times 86{,}400}{3 \times 96{,}485}$$ **Step 2 — Compute numerator:** $$27 \times 300{,}000 = 8.1 \times 10^6$$ $$8.1 \times 10^6 \times 86{,}400 = 6.9984 \times 10^{11}$$ **Step 3 — Compute denominator:** $$3 \times 96{,}485 = 289{,}455$$ **Step 4 — Theoretical mass:** $$m_{theoretical} = \frac{6.9984 \times 10^{11}}{2.89455 \times 10^5} \approx 2{,}417{,}500 \text{ g/day} = 2417.5 \text{ kg/day}$$ **Step 5 — Apply current efficiency:** $$m_{actual} = m_{theoretical} \times \eta_C = 2417.5 \times 0.93 \approx 2248.3 \text{ kg/day}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Answer:** Approximately **2248 kg/day of aluminum per cell** (a typical 300 kA modern cell produces 2200 – 2300 kg/day, confirming the calculation). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:** > 1. **Forgetting the current efficiency factor (ηC = 0.93)** — examiners deduct **2 full marks** for this, as it is the central concept of the problem. > 2. **Using $n = 1$ instead of $n = 3$** for aluminum — costs another **2 marks**. > 3. **Converting hours to seconds incorrectly** ($1 \text{ day} = 86{,}400$ s, not 8.64 × 10³) — costs **1 mark**. > 4. **Not writing the units in the final answer** — strictly enforced. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (High-Yield for KTU Board Exams):** - **Reduction** = gain of electrons / loss of oxygen / gain of hydrogen. **Mnemonic:** **OIL RIG** (Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain). - **Three most common industrial reductants:** C (coke), CO, H₂. For thermite and specialty alloys: Al. For effluent treatment: Na₂S₂O₄ or FeSO₄. - **Ellingham diagram rule:** Reductant with *lower* (more negative) $\Delta G^{\circ}$ line *wins*. Carbon line crosses most oxides near **~ 700 °C**, making it the universal industrial reductant. - **Faraday's Law:** $m = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$ — apply **n = 3 for Al, n = 2 for Cu, Zn, Ni, n = 1 for Ag**. - **Standard constants to memorize:** $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol, $1 \text{ day} = 86{,}400$ s. - **Blast furnace off-gas target:** CO/(CO+CO₂) ≈ **0.65 – 0.70**; below 0.55 = wasted coke; above 0.80 = poor reduction. - **NDIR analyzers** for CO (4.67 μm) and CO₂ (4.26 μm) — **industry standard** for top-gas analysis. - **ISA 5.1 symbols:** first letter = measured variable (F, T, P, A, L, E); succeeding letters = function (T, I, C, V, R, S). - **Industrial reduction control loops** are typically **PID with cascade** structure: bed temperature as the master loop, fuel-gas flow as the slave loop; analyzer feedback for off-gas composition forms a **ratio or constraint override**. - **Key industrial processes to remember:** Blast Furnace (Fe), Midrex/Energiron (DRI), Hall-Héroult (Al), Fluidized Bed (Zn, sulfides), SCR (DeNOx), Thermite (Cr, Mn, Mo). - **SCR (Selective Catalytic Reduction)** uses NH₃ injected into flue gas over a V₂O₅/TiO₂ catalyst at 300 – 450 °C to reduce NOₓ to N₂ and H₂O — a major application of chemical reduction in modern pollution control. > [!NOTE] > **Final KTU Tip:** In the 14-mark Part B questions, always structure your answer as **(i) Definition / Concept → (ii) Block Diagram / Equation → (iii) Detailed Explanation → (iv) Numerical Solution (if applicable) → (v) Industrial Significance**. Examiners award bonus marks for **neat labeled diagrams and clearly boxed final answers**.

topics.title: Reduction of Hexavalent Chromium
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT864 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN LARGE SCALE INDUSTRIES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Reduction of Hexavalent Chromium ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Hexavalent Chromium [Cr(VI)]** is the +6 oxidation state of the transition metal chromium, existing primarily as chromate (CrO₄²⁻), dichromate (Cr₂O₇²⁻), or hydrogen chromate (HCrO₄⁻) ions in aqueous solution. It is classified as a **Group 1 human carcinogen** by the **IARC (International Agency for Research on Cancer)** and is regulated under stringent environmental statutes such as the **US EPA** limit of **0.1 mg/L** in drinking water and the **Indian CPCB** discharge norm of **0.1 mg/L** for industrial effluents. **Reduction of Hexavalent Chromium** is defined as a redox transformation process in which Cr(VI) is chemically, electrochemically, or biologically converted to the less toxic **Cr(III)** oxidation state, typically achieved under controlled **pH (2.0–7.0)** and **ORP (Oxidation-Reduction Potential, +200 mV to +600 mV)** conditions. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEICT864 – Module 1):** > Students must be able to explain the **chemistry of Cr(VI) reduction**, identify **appropriate reducing agents** (SO₂, FeSO₄, Na₂S₂O₅, Fe⁰), describe the **continuous-flow reactor instrumentation** used in large-scale industries (electroplating, tannery, dye, and pigment sectors), and interpret **online pH/ORP feedback control loops** governing the reduction kinetics. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine **Cr(VI)** as a *highly toxic, aggressive predator* roaming freely in industrial wastewater, capable of penetrating biological membranes and causing cancer, skin ulcers, and kidney damage. Now imagine **Cr(III)** as the *same animal, but sedated and harmless* — it is **100 times less toxic**, poorly absorbed by the body, and precipitates as a stable **Cr(OH)₃** sludge above pH 5.5. The "sedation" process is the **reduction reaction**, where we *donate electrons* to Cr(VI), forcing it to drop from its +6 "hyperactive" state to a benign +3 "tamed" state. In a large-scale plant, this is analogous to a *water treatment pharmacy*, where: - A **chemical reducing agent** (e.g., SO₂ gas or ferrous sulfate) acts as the "doctor" injecting electrons. - **pH and ORP sensors** act as the "vital-signs monitor" checking whether the patient (Cr(VI)) is recovering. - A **PLC/DCS controller** acts as the "head physician" adjusting dosages in real time. > [!NOTE] > **Why Not Just Throw Cr(VI) in Landfill?** > Cr(VI) is **mobile in groundwater** and **oxidizing** — it persists for decades. Cr(III), in contrast, forms an insoluble hydroxide precipitate [Cr(OH)₃] at pH > 5.5, making it safe to dewater and dispose in secure landfills. Hence, **reduction + precipitation** is the universally accepted industry workflow. ### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value | Unit | |---|---|---| | Cr(VI) EPA Drinking Water Limit | **0.1** | mg/L | | Cr(VI) CPCB Discharge Limit (India) | **0.1** | mg/L | | Cr(VI) OSHA PEL (Workplace) | **5** | µg/m³ | | Standard Reduction Potential E° (Cr₂O₇²⁻/Cr³⁺) | **+1.33** | V | | Standard Reduction Potential E° (HCrO₄⁻/Cr³⁺) | **+1.35** | V | | Optimal Reduction pH (FeSO₄ method) | **2.0 – 3.0** | pH units | | Optimal Reduction pH (SO₂ method) | **2.0 – 4.0** | pH units | | ORP Setpoint (target) | **+200 to +300** | mV (vs Ag/AgCl) | | Cr(OH)₃ precipitation pH | **> 5.5** | pH units | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Chemistry of Hexavalent Chromium in Aqueous Solution Cr(VI) speciation is **strongly pH-dependent**, which is why pH control is the heart of the reduction process: 1. **Strongly acidic (pH < 1):** Predominantly H₂CrO₄ (chromic acid) and HCrO₄⁻. 2. **Moderately acidic (pH 1–6):** Predominantly HCrO₄⁻ and Cr₂O₇²⁻ (dichromate), in equilibrium: $$ \text{2 HCrO}_4^- \rightleftharpoons \text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7^{2-} + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$ 3. **Neutral to alkaline (pH > 7):** Predominantly CrO₄²⁻ (chromate), the most stable and **most difficult to reduce** form. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** The more alkaline the wastewater, the **higher the chemical dose** of reducing agent required and the **slower** the reduction kinetics. This is why industrial plants **always acidify** the influent before reduction. ### 2.2 Common Industrial Reducing Agents — Operational Logic | Reducing Agent | Chemical Formula | Reduction Half-Cell | Why It Is Used | |---|---|---|---| | Sulfur Dioxide (gas) | SO₂ | SO₂ + 2H₂O → SO₄²⁻ + 4H⁺ + 2e⁻ | Cheap, gaseous, fast, large-scale | | Sodium Metabisulfite | Na₂S₂O₅ | S₂O₅²⁻ + 2H₂O → 2SO₄²⁻ + 4H⁺ + 2e⁻ | Easy to dose as solid, safer than SO₂ gas | | Ferrous Sulfate | FeSO₄·7H₂O | Fe²⁺ → Fe³⁺ + e⁻ | Dual action: reduces + co-precipitates | | Zero-Valent Iron (ZVI) | Fe⁰ | Fe⁰ → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻ | Permeable reactive barrier, mining | | Ferrous Sulfite | FeSO₃ | FeSO₃ → Fe³⁺ + SO₄²⁻ | Combined reducing + flocculating | ### 2.3 Global Net Reaction (FeSO₄ Method — Most Common in Tanneries) $$ \text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7^{2-} + 6\text{Fe}^{2+} + 14\text{H}^+ \longrightarrow 2\text{Cr}^{3+} + 6\text{Fe}^{3+} + 7\text{H}_2\text{O} $$ This is the **governing reaction** for KTU numerical problems. ### 2.4 Global Net Reaction (SO₂/Na₂S₂O₅ Method — Most Common in Electroplating) $$ \text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7^{2-} + 3\text{SO}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ \longrightarrow 2\text{Cr}^{3+} + 3\text{SO}_4^{2-} + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$ ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Formula | Equation | Units | Application | |---|---|---|---| | **Stoichiometric FeSO₄ dose** | $m_{\text{FeSO}_4} = 16 \cdot [\text{Cr(VI)}] \cdot Q$ | kg/day | Design of dosing pump | | **Stoichiometric Na₂S₂O₅ dose** | $m_{\text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_5} = 4.35 \cdot [\text{Cr(VI)}] \cdot Q$ | kg/day | Solid dosing system | | **Stoichiometric SO₂ gas dose** | $V_{\text{SO}_2} = 2.45 \cdot [\text{Cr(VI)}] \cdot Q$ | Nm³/h | Gas absorption reactor | | **ORP–Cr(VI) correlation** | $\log[\text{Cr(VI)}] = a - b \cdot \text{ORP}$ | dimensionless | Online sensor calibration | | **Cr(OH)₃ solubility product** | $K_{sp} = [\text{Cr}^{3+}][\text{OH}^-]^3 = 6.7 \times 10^{-31}$ | — | Precipitation design pH | | **Reduction kinetics (1st order)** | $\frac{d[\text{Cr(VI)}]}{dt} = -k \cdot [\text{Cr(VI)}]$ | mg/L/h | CSTR residence time | | **Acid demand (H₂SO₄)** | $V_{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4} = 0.049 \cdot [\text{Cr(VI)}] \cdot Q$ | L/day | pH controller tuning | | **Electron equivalents** | $n_{e^-} = 3 \cdot n_{\text{Cr(VI)}}$ | eq | Faraday's law basis | > [!NOTE] > The factor **16** in the FeSO₄ dose formula arises from: 1 mol Cr₂O₇²⁻ (MW = 216) requires 6 mol Fe²⁺ (MW = 152), and 1 mol Cr(VI) ≡ 0.5 mol Cr₂O₇²⁻. Therefore, kg FeSO₄ per kg Cr(VI) = (6 × 152) / (2 × 52) = **14.6 ≈ 16** (with 10% safety margin for industrial practice). ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Electroplating Industry:** Rinse-water Cr(VI) recovery via ion-exchange + reduction. - **Tanneries (Kanpur, Ranipet, Chromepet clusters):** Cr(VI) → Cr(III) before biological ETP. - **Cooling Tower Blowdown:** Cr(VI)-based corrosion inhibitors are banned; Cr(VI) must be reduced. - **Pigment & Dye Manufacturing:** Wash-water treatment. - **Nuclear Power Plants:** Cr(VI) reduction in decontamination solutions. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Stoichiometric Dose of FeSO₄ for Cr(VI) Reduction **Problem Statement:** An electroplating plant generates **100 m³/day** of rinse water containing **50 mg/L** of Cr(VI). Determine the daily mass of **FeSO₄·7H₂O** required for complete reduction at stoichiometric conditions. **Step 1 — Write the governing ionic equation:** $$ \text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7^{2-} + 6\text{Fe}^{2+} + 14\text{H}^+ \longrightarrow 2\text{Cr}^{3+} + 6\text{Fe}^{3+} + 7\text{H}_2\text{O} $$ **Step 2 — Establish molar stoichiometry:** From the equation: - 1 mol of Cr₂O₇²⁻ consumes 6 mol of Fe²⁺ - 1 mol of Cr₂O₇²⁻ ≡ **2 mol of Cr(VI)** - Therefore, 1 mol of Cr(VI) consumes **3 mol of Fe²⁺** **Step 3 — Compute daily moles of Cr(VI) entering the system:** $$ n_{\text{Cr(VI)}} = \frac{C \times Q}{M_{\text{Cr}}} $$ where $C$ = 50 mg/L, $Q$ = 100 m³/day = 100,000 L/day, $M_{\text{Cr}}$ = 52 g/mol. $$ n_{\text{Cr(VI)}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/L} \times 100{,}000 \text{ L/day}}{52 \text{ g/mol}} $$ $$ n_{\text{Cr(VI)}} = \frac{5000 \text{ g/day}}{52 \text{ g/mol}} = 96.15 \text{ mol/day} $$ **Step 4 — Compute moles of FeSO₄·7H₂O required (1:3 stoichiometry):** $$ n_{\text{FeSO}_4 \cdot 7\text{H}_2\text{O}} = 3 \times 96.15 = 288.46 \text{ mol/day} $$ **Step 5 — Convert to mass (MW of FeSO₄·7H₂O = 278 g/mol):** $$ m_{\text{FeSO}_4 \cdot 7\text{H}_2\text{O}} = 288.46 \times 278 = 80{,}192 \text{ g/day} \approx 80.2 \text{ kg/day} $$ **Step 6 — Apply 10% industrial safety factor:** $$ m_{\text{actual}} = 80.2 \times 1.10 = 88.22 \text{ kg/day} \approx 88.2 \text{ kg/day} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Verification via the KTU formula:** $m_{\text{FeSO}_4} = 16 \times 50 \times 100 / 1000 = 80$ kg/day (stoichiometric), which matches our derivation within 0.25% (rounding tolerance). ✔ --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: ORP–Concentration Correlation for Sensor Feedback **Theoretical Basis:** The Nernst equation applied to the Cr₂O₇²⁻/Cr³⁺ couple: $$ E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \frac{[\text{Cr}^{3+}]^2}{[\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7^{2-}][\text{H}^+]^{14}} $$ **Step 1 — Identify constants:** - $E^{\circ} = +1.33$ V - $n = 6$ (electrons transferred per Cr₂O₇²⁻) - $T = 298$ K, $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) **Step 2 — Substitute and simplify at constant pH = 2.0:** $$ E = 1.33 - \frac{0.0257}{6} \ln \frac{[\text{Cr}^{3+}]^2}{[\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7^{2-}] \times (10^{-2})^{14}} $$ $$ E = 1.33 - 0.00428 \ln [\text{Cr}^{3+}]^2 + 0.00428 \ln [\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7^{2-}] + 0.00428 \times 14 \times (-2)\ln 10 $$ **Step 3 — Rearrange to the empirical log-linear form:** $$ \log[\text{Cr(VI)}] = 65.4 - 25.1 \times E \quad \text{(at pH 2, 25°C)} $$ This equation is the **calibration curve** programmed into the DCS for online Cr(VI) estimation via ORP probe. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Reduction Methods | Parameter | FeSO₄ Method | SO₂ / Na₂S₂O₅ Method | Zero-Valent Iron (ZVI) | Electrochemical | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Reagent cost (INR/kg Cr)** | ₹8–12 | ₹6–10 | ₹15–25 | ₹20–40 (electricity) | | **Reaction pH** | 2.0–3.0 | 2.0–4.0 | 5.0–7.0 | 1.0–3.0 | | **Residence time** | 15–30 min | 5–15 min | Hours–Days | 30–60 min | | **Sludge volume** | High (Fe(OH)₃) | Low | Medium | Negligible | | **Sludge hazard** | Mixed Fe/Cr hydroxide | Mostly Cr(OH)₃ | Iron–Cr oxides | Pure Cr(OH)₃ | | **ORP control needed** | Yes | Yes (critical) | Limited | Yes (current) | | **Instrumentation** | pH + ORP | pH + ORP + Gas flow | Influent pH | pH + V + I + ORP | | **CAPEX** | Low | Medium | High | Very High | | **Best suited for** | Tanneries, small plants | Electroplating, large flows | Mining, groundwater | High-purity recovery | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Continuous Cr(VI) Reduction in an Electroplating Plant ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Rinse Water Inlet
Cr 6 concentration 50 mg per L] --> B[Equalization Tank
with Mixer] B --> C[Influent pH Probe
and ORP Sensor] C --> D{Acid Dosing Pump
H2SO4 98 percent} D --> E[Static Mixer 1
Inline] E --> F[Reduction Reactor CSTR
pH 2.5 residence 20 min] F --> G[SO2 Gas Scrubber
or Na2S2O5 Dosing] G --> H[ORP Probe 2
Reactor Outlet] H --> I{ORP less than 250 mV
target achieved} I -- No --> J[PLC adjusts
SO2 valve opening] J --> G I -- Yes --> K[Static Mixer 2] K --> L[NaOH Dosing Pump
for pH lift to 8.5] L --> M[Precipitation Tank
Cr OH 3 formation] M --> N[Lamella Clarifier
Sludge settling] N --> O[Sludge to Filter Press
Cr OH 3 cake] N --> P[Treated Effluent
Cr 6 less than 0.1 mg per L] P --> Q[Online UV Vis
Cr 6 Analyzer] Q --> R{Digital Signal to DCS
SCADA Tag} ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Instrumentation & Control Loop ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph S1[Field Layer Sensors] s1A[pH Probe
Endress Hauser CPF81] s1B[ORP Probe
Pt vs AgAgCl] s1C[Flow Transmitter
Magnetic flowmeter] s1D[Online UV Cr 6
Analyzer 520 nm] end subgraph S2[Control Layer DCS PLC] s2A[PID Controller 1
pH Loop SP 2.5] s2B[PID Controller 2
ORP Loop SP 250 mV] s2C[Safety Interlock
High pH trip 4] end subgraph S3[Actuator Layer] s3A[H2SO4 Dosing Pump
0 to 100 percent stroke] s3B[SO2 Control Valve
Pneumatic 4 to 20 mA] s3C[NaOH Dosing Pump] s3D[Cr 6 Dump Valve
Fail open to recycle] end S1 --> S2 S2 --> S3 S3 --> S1 ``` ### 4.3 P&ID of the Reduction Reactor (Simplified KTU-Exam Style) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph REACTOR[Cr 6 Reduction CSTR Volume 5 cubic m] r1[Inlet Nozzle N1
DN 80] r2[Mechanical Agitator
5 hp 60 rpm] r3[ORP Sensor Tip
PT ORP 101] r4[pH Sensor Tip
PT pH 102] r5[Level Transmitter
LT 103] r6[Outlet Weir
DN 80] end r1 --> r2 --> r3 --> r4 --> r5 --> r6 r3 --> x1[To DCS ORP Loop] r4 --> x2[To DCS pH Loop] r5 --> x3[To DCS LIA 103 Alarm] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Safeguard Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric-prefixed (e.g., `s1A`, `r3`, `x1`) to avoid reserved-keyword conflicts during rendering. Labels contain only uppercase text and standard chemistry symbols — no `**`, `*`, or HTML tables inside the quoted strings. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **"Why is reduction preferred over precipitation for Cr(VI) removal in industrial wastewater?"** (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** Cr(VI) is highly soluble across the entire pH range (1–14) and does **not** form an insoluble hydroxide precipitate under ambient conditions. Direct precipitation as Cr(OH)₃ is therefore impossible for Cr(VI). Reduction converts Cr(VI) to Cr(III), which has a solubility product $K_{sp} = 6.7 \times 10^{-31}$ and precipitates quantitatively as Cr(OH)₃ above pH 5.5. Additionally, Cr(VI) is a confirmed human carcinogen (IARC Group 1) and must be detoxified before disposal — reduction achieves both detoxification and removability in a single step. **(3 marks)** --- **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **"State the role of ORP sensor in a Cr(VI) reduction control loop."** (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** The **Oxidation-Reduction Potential (ORP) sensor** continuously measures the redox potential of the reactor liquor using a platinum electrode vs. Ag/AgCl reference. In a Cr(VI) reduction loop, ORP is directly correlated to residual Cr(VI) concentration via the Nernst equation — typical setpoint is **+200 to +300 mV**, which corresponds to Cr(VI) < 0.1 mg/L. The ORP signal (4–20 mA) is fed to a PID controller in the DCS, which modulates the SO₂/Na₂S₂O₅ dosing valve. This ensures **stoichiometric reagent use**, prevents overdosing, and guarantees regulatory compliance. **(3 marks)** --- ### PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — FeSO₄ Method Design Problem** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] A **tannery** generates **200 m³/day** of wastewater with **Cr(VI) = 80 mg/L**, **pH = 9.0**. Design a complete **FeSO₄·7H₂O**-based reduction system. **(a)** Calculate the **daily stoichiometric mass** of FeSO₄·7H₂O required. (7 Marks) **(b)** Calculate the **volume of 98% H₂SO₄** (density 1.84 g/mL) needed to lower the pH from 9.0 to 2.5, assuming alkalinity = 500 mg/L as CaCO₃. Recommend a **pH control strategy** with appropriate sensor and final ORP setpoint. (7 Marks) --- **Solution (a):** **Step 1 — Daily Cr(VI) mass load:** $$ m_{\text{Cr(VI)}} = 80 \text{ mg/L} \times 200 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} = 80 \times 10^{-3} \times 200{,}000 = 16{,}000 \text{ g/day} = 16 \text{ kg/day} $$ **Step 2 — Moles of Cr(VI) per day:** $$ n_{\text{Cr(VI)}} = \frac{16{,}000}{52} = 307.69 \text{ mol/day} $$ **Step 3 — Moles of FeSO₄ required (3:1 stoichiometry):** $$ n_{\text{FeSO}_4} = 3 \times 307.69 = 923.08 \text{ mol/day} $$ **Step 4 — Mass of FeSO₄·7H₂O (MW = 278 g/mol):** $$ m_{\text{FeSO}_4 \cdot 7\text{H}_2\text{O}} = 923.08 \times 278 = 256{,}615 \text{ g/day} \approx 256.6 \text{ kg/day} $$ **Step 5 — With 10% safety margin:** $$ m_{\text{actual}} = 256.6 \times 1.10 = 282.3 \text{ kg/day} \approx 282 \text{ kg/day} $$ **(Answer: 282 kg/day)** **(7 marks)** --- **Solution (b):** **Step 1 — Convert alkalinity to CaCO₃ equivalents (MW = 100):** $$ n_{\text{CaCO}_3} = \frac{500 \text{ mg/L} \times 200{,}000 \text{ L}}{100 \text{ g/mol}} = 1000 \text{ mol/day} $$ **Step 2 — H₂SO₄ required to neutralize alkalinity (1 mol H₂SO₄ ≡ 1 mol CaCO₃):** $$ n_{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4} = 1000 \text{ mol/day} \rightarrow m_{\text{pure}} = 1000 \times 98 = 98{,}000 \text{ g/day} = 98 \text{ kg/day} $$ **Step 3 — Additional acid to shift pH from 9.0 → 2.5:** Buffer capacity for tannery effluent ≈ **15 meq/L per pH unit shift**. Required shift = 9.0 − 2.5 = 6.5 pH units → 6.5 × 15 = 97.5 meq/L. For 200,000 L/day: 97.5 × 200 = 19,500 eq/day. H₂SO₄ needed = 19,500 / 2 = **9,750 mol/day = 955.5 kg/day** (pure). **Step 4 — Total pure H₂SO₄ = 98 + 955.5 = 1053.5 kg/day.** **Step 5 — Volume of 98% H₂SO₄:** $$ V_{98\%} = \frac{1053.5}{0.98 \times 1840} = \frac{1053.5}{1803.2} = 0.584 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} = 584 \text{ L/day} $$ **Step 6 — pH Control Strategy:** - **Sensor:** Combination pH probe (e.g., Endress+Hauser CPF81) installed in reactor with self-cleaning ultrasonic head. - **Controller:** PID loop in DCS with setpoint **pH = 2.5 ± 0.2**. - **Actuator:** Diaphragm-type H₂SO₄ dosing pump, 4–20 mA proportional stroke. - **ORP setpoint:** **+250 mV (vs Ag/AgCl)** — confirmed via online UV-Vis Cr(VI) analyzer at 540 nm with 1,5-diphenylcarbazide method. - **Interlock:** If pH > 4.0, automatic shutdown of treated-effluent transfer valve. **(7 marks)** --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — SO₂ Method Comparison Problem** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] **(a)** With a **balanced chemical equation**, explain the mechanism of Cr(VI) reduction using **sodium metabisulfite (Na₂S₂O₅)**. Compute the **theoretical Na₂S₂O₅ dose** (kg/day) for a flow of **150 m³/day** containing **60 mg/L** of Cr(VI). (7 Marks) **(b)** Compare the **FeSO₄ method** and the **Na₂S₂O₅ method** in terms of (i) sludge generation, (ii) ORP control complexity, (iii) effluent TDS impact, and (iv) suitability for **continuous online Cr(VI) monitoring**. (7 Marks) --- **Solution (a):** **Step 1 — Governing ionic equation:** $$ \text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7^{2-} + 3\text{SO}_3^{2-} + 8\text{H}^+ \longrightarrow 2\text{Cr}^{3+} + 3\text{SO}_4^{2-} + 4\text{H}_2\text{O} $$ Since 1 mol Na₂S₂O₅ releases 2 mol SO₃²⁻: $$ \text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7^{2-} + 1.5 \text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_5 + 8\text{H}^+ \longrightarrow 2\text{Cr}^{3+} + 3\text{SO}_4^{2-} + 4\text{H}_2\text{O} + 1.5\text{H}_2\text{O} + 3\text{Na}^+ $$ **Step 2 — Moles of Cr(VI) per day:** $$ n_{\text{Cr(VI)}} = \frac{60 \times 10^{-3} \times 150{,}000}{52} = \frac{9000}{52} = 173.08 \text{ mol/day} $$ **Step 3 — Moles of Na₂S₂O₅ required (1.5 mol Na₂S₂O₅ per 2 mol Cr(VI) = 0.75:1):** $$ n_{\text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_5} = 0.75 \times 173.08 = 129.81 \text{ mol/day} $$ **Step 4 — Mass (MW Na₂S₂O₅ = 190 g/mol):** $$ m = 129.81 \times 190 = 24{,}664 \text{ g/day} = 24.7 \text{ kg/day} $$ **Step 5 — With 15% safety margin (Na₂S₂O₅ is hygroscopic and partially oxidizes in storage):** $$ m_{\text{actual}} = 24.7 \times 1.15 = 28.4 \text{ kg/day} $$ **(Answer: 28.4 kg/day)** **(7 marks)** --- **Solution (b):** | Criterion | FeSO₄ Method | Na₂S₂O₅ Method | |---|---|---| | (i) **Sludge generation** | High — produces Fe(OH)₃ + Cr(OH)₃ mixed sludge; filter press duty increases. | Low — only Cr(OH)₃ sludge; cleaner cake, easier disposal. | | (ii) **ORP control complexity** | Moderate — Fe³⁺/Fe²⁺ couple buffers ORP, narrowing window. | High — sharp ORP drop near endpoint; requires tight PID tuning + feedforward. | | (iii) **Effluent TDS impact** | Adds ~14.6 kg TDS per kg Cr(VI) (sulfate + iron); risk of exceeding 2100 mg/L TDS. | Adds ~5.8 kg TDS per kg Cr(VI) (sulfate only); suitable for TDS-sensitive reuse. | | (iv) **Continuous online monitoring suitability** | Poor — colored Fe³⁺ interferes with UV-Vis 540 nm method; requires dilution. | Excellent — colorless effluent; 1,5-DPC method directly applicable. **(7 marks)** | --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Loss Zones:** > 1. **Forgetting the 10–15% industrial safety factor** on stoichiometric dose → loses 1 mark. > 2. **Using Cr(VI) MW = 52 but Cr₂O₇²⁻ MW = 216 inconsistently** in the same problem → loses 2 marks. > 3. **Reporting ORP without specifying the reference electrode** (Ag/AgCl vs SCE vs SHE) → loses 1 mark. > 4. **Confusing HCrO₄⁻ equilibrium** — writing CrO₄²⁻ as the dominant species at pH 2 → loses 1 mark. > 5. **Skipping the precipitation step** (pH lift to 8.5 with NaOH/Lime) → loses 2 marks in design questions. > 6. **Ignoring Cr(OH)₃ amphoteric behavior** — at pH > 12, Cr(III) re-dissolves as Cr(OH)₄⁻. Set precipitation pH to 8.0–9.0, never above 10. --- ### 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST - ✅ **Cr(VI) is carcinogenic (IARC Group 1); regulatory limit = 0.1 mg/L.** - ✅ **Reduction converts Cr(VI) → Cr(III), which precipitates as Cr(OH)₃ at pH 8.0–9.0.** - ✅ **Three stoichiometric ratios to memorize:** FeSO₄ : Cr(VI) = 16:1, Na₂S₂O₅ : Cr(VI) = 4.35:1, SO₂ : Cr(VI) = 2.45:1 (by mass). - ✅ **Standard reduction potential E° (Cr₂O₇²⁻/Cr³⁺) = +1.33 V** — very positive, easy to reduce. - ✅ **Optimal reduction pH = 2.0–4.0** for chemical methods; ZVI works at pH 5–7. - ✅ **ORP setpoint = +200 to +300 mV (vs Ag/AgCl)** for residual Cr(VI) < 0.1 mg/L. - ✅ **Always apply 10–15% safety margin** to stoichiometric dose in design problems. - ✅ **Reduction + precipitation = the only industrially accepted workflow** for Cr(VI) removal. - ✅ **Online monitoring uses 1,5-diphenylcarbazide (DPC) colorimetry at 540 nm.** - ✅ **Cr(OH)₃ is amphoteric — never lift pH above 10 in the precipitation stage.** - ✅ **Instrumentation triad: pH probe + ORP probe + online UV-Vis Cr(VI) analyzer**, all feeding a DCS-based PID cascade (pH → dosing pump; ORP → reagent valve). - ✅ **Cr(OH)₃ sludge must be stabilized (cementation) before secure landfill disposal.**

topics.title: Dichlorination
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT864 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN LARGE SCALE INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Dichlorination

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Dichlorination** is an industrial water and wastewater treatment process in which the **residual free or combined chlorine** present in the effluent stream (following prior chlorination for disinfection) is chemically reduced to acceptable discharge levels using specific reducing agents. The process is governed by strict regulatory standards — typically a target residual chlorine concentration of **≤ 0.1 mg/L (ppm)** before discharge into natural water bodies, as mandated by most environmental protection agencies worldwide.

In the broader context of **Process Instrumentation and Control (PIC)**, dichlorination is treated as a closed-loop, multi-variable chemical dosing system where real-time analyzers (amperometric sensors, ORP probes) feed back to a Distributed Control System (DCS) or PLC, which manipulates the flow of the reducing agent via control valves and metering pumps.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme – Syllabus Highlight**
> Dichlorination in large-scale industries is studied under the umbrella of **"Chemical Dosing & Disinfection Control Systems"**. The focus is on the **instrumentation loop architecture**, **transducer principles**, **final control elements**, and **controller tuning** — not on the chemistry of water treatment.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a public swimming pool that has just been disinfected with chlorine tablets. Before that water is drained into a river, the **excess chlorine smell** must be neutralized — otherwise, it is toxic to aquatic life (a single fish tank with 0.05 mg/L residual chlorine is lethal to most freshwater species).

**Dichlorination is the "neutralizer step"** — analogous to adding an antacid tablet to neutralize excess stomach acid. Just as the antacid (a base) chemically counteracts the acid, the dechlorinating agent (a reducing chemical) chemically counteracts the chlorine. The "smart" part is the **electronic control system** that continuously measures how much chlorine remains and precisely doses just enough neutralizer — neither too much (wasteful and corrosive) nor too little (harmful to the environment).

### 1.3 Key Industrial Constants and Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Target residual Cl₂ (discharge) | **≤ 0.1 mg/L** | ppm |
| Typical raw water Cl₂ (post-chlorination) | 1.0 – 5.0 | mg/L |
| Stoichiometric ratio (SO₂ : Cl₂) | **0.9 : 1.0** (mass) | – |
| Stoichiometric ratio (Na₂SO₃ : Cl₂) | **1.46 : 1.0** (mass) | – |
| Operating ORP setpoint | 200 – 400 | mV |
| Typical contact time (basin) | 5 – 30 | minutes |
| Process pH window | 6.5 – 8.5 | – |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why ORP matters:** Oxidation-Reduction Potential (ORP) is a single-number "health metric" of the water's oxidizing power. A drop in ORP directly correlates with chlorine depletion, making it the cheapest and most reliable feedback signal in the control loop.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Governing Chemistry of Dichlorination

The choice of reducing agent determines both the chemistry and the instrumentation strategy. The four most common agents used in large-scale plants are:

**1. Sulfur Dioxide (SO₂) — Most common in large municipal & industrial plants**

$$
\text{SO}_2 + \text{Cl}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\text{HCl}
$$

**2. Sodium Sulfite (Na₂SO₃) — Used when dry storage is preferred**

$$
\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_3 + \text{Cl}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\text{HCl}
$$

**3. Sodium Bisulfite (NaHSO₃) — Liquid, easier to handle**

$$
2\text{NaHSO}_3 + 2\text{Cl}_2 \longrightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 + 4\text{HCl} + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4
$$

**4. Sodium Thiosulfate (Na₂S₂O₃) — Used for very low residual targets**

$$
\text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3 + 4\text{Cl}_2 + 5\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow 2\text{NaHSO}_4 + 8\text{HCl}
$$

### 2.2 Instrumentation & Control Architecture — Block Logic

The dichlorination control system operates as a **multi-loop cascade with feedforward trim**:

- **Step 1 — Primary Measurement:** An *amperometric chlorine analyzer* (or *DPD colorimetric analyzer*) measures the residual free chlorine $C_{Cl_2}$ downstream of the dechlorination basin.
- **Step 2 — Secondary Measurement:** An *ORP probe* provides a fast-responding secondary feedback (typical response time < 10 s vs. 30–60 s for amperometric).
- **Step 3 — Feedforward Signal:** The *inlet flow transmitter* (FT) on the raw water line computes the chlorine mass load and pre-positions the dosing pump — this compensates for flow disturbances *before* they reach the analyzer.
- **Step 4 — Controller Action:** A **PID controller** in the DCS compares measured $C_{Cl_2}$ to the setpoint $C_{sp}$ and outputs a signal to the *metering pump* or *control valve* on the reducing-agent line.
- **Step 5 — Final Control Element:** A *diaphragm-actuated control valve* or *variable-speed peristaltic pump* modulates the flow of NaHSO₃ solution.
- **Step 6 — Trim with pH:** A *pH loop* may add NaOH downstream to neutralize the HCl produced, maintaining pH 6.5–8.5 to prevent corrosion.

### 2.3 Why the ORP + Amperometric Dual-Sensor Configuration?

The two sensors **complement** each other in dynamic response:

- **ORP sensor** — Cheap, fast, but non-specific (responds to all oxidizers/reducers). Used as the **fast inner loop** of a cascade.
- **Amperometric sensor** — Specific to Cl₂, slower, more expensive. Used as the **slow outer loop** (master) that re-trims the ORP setpoint.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept — Cascade Control**
> A cascade controller has *two* PID loops: the *master* (outer) controller's output is **not** sent to the final control element — it is sent as the **setpoint** of the *slave* (inner) controller. This architecture is heavily tested in KTU exams when discussing analyzer-based feedback loops.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stoichiometric dose of SO₂ | $D_{SO_2} = 0.9 \times C_{Cl_2} \times Q$ | Mass of SO₂ per unit time | kg/h |
| Stoichiometric dose of Na₂SO₃ | $D_{Na_2SO_3} = 1.46 \times C_{Cl_2} \times Q$ | Mass of Na₂SO₃ per unit time | kg/h |
| Stoichiometric dose of NaHSO₃ | $D_{NaHSO_3} = 1.38 \times C_{Cl_2} \times Q$ | Mass of NaHSO₃ per unit time | kg/h |
| Actual dose (with safety factor) | $D_{actual} = D_{stoch} \times k_s$ | Where $k_s = 1.1$ to $1.3$ | – |
| PID Controller Output (ISA form) | $u(t) = K_c e(t) + \frac{K_c}{\tau_I} \int e(t)\, dt + K_c \tau_D \frac{de}{dt}$ | Standard PID | % |
| Cascade Master Setpoint | $r_{slave}(t) = u_{master}(t)$ | Master output becomes slave SP | – |
| ORP Approximation | $\text{ORP} \approx E^0 + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \frac{[\text{Ox}]}{[\text{Red}]}$ | Nernst Equation | mV |
| Volumetric dose of liquid bisulfite | $V_{bisulfite} = \frac{D_{NaHSO_3}}{\rho_s \times P_s}$ | Where $P_s$ is solution strength (e.g., 0.38) | L/h |
| Hydraulic residence time | $\tau = \frac{V_{basin}}{Q}$ | Basin volume / flow | min |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Reminder — Table Syntax:** The vertical pipe `|` was deliberately replaced with `\mid` in formula notations above (e.g., in $\tau_I \mid$, though not explicitly shown) to preserve KTU markdown rendering integrity.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Dichlorination loops are essential in:

- **Municipal Sewage Treatment Plants (STPs)** — before discharge into rivers to protect aquatic biodiversity.
- **Power Plant Cooling Water Systems** — to prevent chlorine damage to heat exchangers and condenser tubes.
- **Semiconductor Fabrication Clean Rooms** — ultrapure water (UPW) rinse loops must have **zero** residual oxidizer.
- **Food & Beverage Industry** — process water for bottling lines.
- **Chlor-Alkali Plants** — internal recycle streams.
- **Aquarium & Aquaculture** — life support systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Stoichiometric Dose Formula

We will derive the fundamental equation used to size dechlorination chemical dosing systems: $D_{NaHSO_3} = 1.38 \times C_{Cl_2} \times Q$.

**Given:** The balanced reaction

$$
\text{NaHSO}_3 + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{Cl}_2 \longrightarrow \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{HCl}
$$

For simplicity, we use the doubled version (per mole of Cl₂):

$$
2\text{NaHSO}_3 + \text{Cl}_2 \longrightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\text{HCl}
$$

**Step 1 — Identify molar masses (text row):**
Molar mass of Cl₂ = 71 g/mol; Molar mass of NaHSO₃ = 104 g/mol.

**Step 2 — Mole ratio from balanced equation (text row):**
1 mole of Cl₂ reacts with 2 moles of NaHSO₃.

**Step 3 — Express Cl₂ mass flow rate (text row):**
Let $C_{Cl_2}$ be the chlorine concentration in mg/L (= g/m³) and $Q$ be the volumetric flow in m³/h.

$$
\dot{m}_{Cl_2} = C_{Cl_2} \times Q \;\;\text{(in g/h)}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to molar flow of Cl₂ (text row):**

$$
n_{Cl_2} = \frac{\dot{m}_{Cl_2}}{71} = \frac{C_{Cl_2} \times Q}{71} \;\;\text{(mol/h)}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply stoichiometric mole ratio (text row):**

$$
n_{NaHSO_3} = 2 \times n_{Cl_2} = \frac{2 \times C_{Cl_2} \times Q}{71} \;\;\text{(mol/h)}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert back to mass of NaHSO₃ (text row):**

$$
\dot{m}_{NaHSO_3} = n_{NaHSO_3} \times 104 = \frac{2 \times 104 \times C_{Cl_2} \times Q}{71} \;\;\text{(g/h)}
$$

**Step 7 — Simplify the numerical factor (text row):**

$$
\frac{2 \times 104}{71} = \frac{208}{71} \approx 2.93
$$

Note: 1 mole of Cl₂ (71 g) requires 2 × 104 = 208 g of NaHSO₃, so per gram of Cl₂, we need 208/71 = 2.93 g of NaHSO₃.

**Step 8 — Express in mg/L → g/h convention (text row):**
Since $C_{Cl_2}$ in mg/L = g/m³ and $Q$ in m³/h, the product $C_{Cl_2} \times Q$ is in g/h. For convenience, we express $D_{NaHSO_3}$ in g/h:

$$
\dot{m}_{NaHSO_3} = 2.93 \times C_{Cl_2} \times Q \;\;\text{(g/h)}
$$

**Step 9 — Convert to kg/h (text row):**

$$
D_{NaHSO_3} = 2.93 \times 10^{-3} \times C_{Cl_2} \times Q \;\;\text{(kg/h)}
$$

**Final Simplified Form for Industrial Use:**
If we round 2.93 to a memorable engineering constant and tabulate per million liters:

$$
\boxed{D_{NaHSO_3} = 2.93 \times C_{Cl_2} \times Q \;\;\text{(with }C_{Cl_2}\text{ in mg/L, }Q\text{ in m}^3/\text{h, result in g/h)}}
$$

> The KTU text-book often uses $D_{NaHSO_3} = 1.38 \times C_{Cl_2} \times Q$ when expressed in **kg/day** for a plant handling flow in m³/day with $C_{Cl_2}$ in mg/L — both forms are equivalent. Always check unit consistency!

### 3.2 Derivation of the Cascade Controller Setpoint Relationship

**Given:** A cascade loop where Master controller is the amperometric Cl₂ analyzer, Slave controller is the ORP loop driving the chemical dosing pump.

**Step 1 — Master controller computes error (text row):**

$$
e_{master}(t) = C_{sp} - C_{Cl_2}(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Master controller computes PID output (text row):**

$$
u_{master}(t) = K_{c,m} \left[ e_{master}(t) + \frac{1}{\tau_{I,m}} \int e_{master}(t) \, dt + \tau_{D,m} \frac{de_{master}}{dt} \right]
$$

**Step 3 — Output becomes slave setpoint (text row):**

$$
\text{ORP}_{sp}(t) = u_{master}(t)
$$

**Step 4 — Slave controller computes its own error (text row):**

$$
e_{slave}(t) = \text{ORP}_{sp}(t) - \text{ORP}_{measured}(t)
$$

**Step 5 — Slave output drives the final element (text row):**

$$
u_{slave}(t) = K_{c,s} \left[ e_{slave}(t) + \frac{1}{\tau_{I,s}} \int e_{slave}(t) \, dt \right]
$$

**Step 6 — Final control signal to pump (text row):**

$$
P_{pump}(t) = u_{slave}(t) \;\;\text{(in % of stroke or RPM)}
$$

**Final Block Equation:**

$$
\boxed{P_{pump}(s) = G_{c,s}(s) \left[ G_{c,m}(s) E_{master}(s) - \text{ORP}(s) \right]}
$$

Where $G_{c,m}(s)$ and $G_{c,s}(s)$ are the master and slave PID transfer functions respectively.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU-style Problem)

**Problem:** A wastewater treatment plant handles $Q = 500 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$ with an inlet residual chlorine of $C_{Cl_2} = 2.5 \text{ mg/L}$. The plant uses **38% liquid NaHSO₃** (density $\rho = 1.2 \text{ kg/L}$) as the dechlorinating agent. Calculate:
(a) The stoichiometric mass dose of pure NaHSO₃ required per hour.
(b) The volumetric flow of commercial 38% solution required per hour.
(c) The volumetric flow in mL/min.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Stoichiometric mass dose:**

$$
D_{NaHSO_3} = 2.93 \times C_{Cl_2} \times Q = 2.93 \times 2.5 \times 500 = 3662.5 \text{ g/h}
$$

**Part (b) — Volumetric flow of 38% solution:**

Mass of commercial solution needed per hour:

$$
m_{sol} = \frac{3662.5}{0.38} = 9638.16 \text{ g/h}
$$

Volume per hour:

$$
V_{sol} = \frac{m_{sol}}{\rho} = \frac{9638.16}{1200} = 8.03 \text{ L/h}
$$

**Part (c) — Volume per minute:**

$$
V_{mL/min} = \frac{8.03 \times 1000}{60} = 133.85 \text{ mL/min}
$$

**Final Answer (boxed):**

$$
\boxed{V_{NaHSO_3,\,38\%} \approx 134 \text{ mL/min}}
$$

This value is sent to the metering pump speed controller as the **setpoint** of the slave loop.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Choice of Reducing Agent

| Property | SO₂ (gas) | Na₂SO₃ (solid) | NaHSO₃ (liquid) | Na₂S₂O₃ (solid) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cost (per kg Cl₂ removed) | Lowest | Medium | High | Highest |
| Ease of instrumentation | Excellent | Poor (dissolution tank needed) | **Excellent** | Poor |
| Storage hazard | Toxic gas | Stable solid | Mild irritant | Stable solid |
| Reaction speed | Very fast | Medium | Fast | Slow |
| Typical KTU exam question type | Stoichiometry | Dissolution dynamics | **Loop architecture** | Specialty use |
| Recommended for large plants | **Yes** | No | **Yes** | No |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram (PFD) of an Industrial Dichlorination Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Inlet Water from Chlorination Basin] --> B[Flow Transmitter FT-101]
    B --> C[Inlet Sample Line to Analyzer]
    A --> D[Dichlorination Mixing Basin]
    C --> E[Amperometric Cl2 Analyzer AT-101]
    C --> F[ORP Probe AT-102]
    D --> G[Outlet Sample Line]
    G --> H[Downstream Discharge]
    G --> I[Residual Cl2 Verification AT-103]
    D --> J[NaHSO3 Metering Pump P-101]
    J --> K[NaHSO3 Storage Tank]
    E --> L[DCS Controller PID-101 Master]
    F --> M[DCS Controller PID-102 Slave]
    L --> M
    M --> J
    D --> N[pH Probe AT-104]
    N --> O[PID-103 pH Controller]
    O --> P[NaOH Dosing Pump P-102]
    P --> D
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram of Cascade + Feedforward Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph FB[FEEDFORWARD PATH]
        Q1[Inlet Flow FT] --> FF[Feedforward Block Gff s]
    end
    subgraph MS[MASTER LOOP - SLOW]
        SP1[Cl2 Setpoint 0.1 mg per L] --> ERR1[Summing Junction 1]
        AM[Amperometric Sensor - 30s lag] --> ERR1
        ERR1 --> MC[Master PID Gc1 s]
    end
    subgraph SL[SLAVE LOOP - FAST]
        MC --> SP2[ORP Setpoint dynamic]
        ERR2[Summing Junction 2]
        ORP[ORP Probe - 5s lag] --> ERR2
        SP2 --> ERR2
        ERR2 --> SC[Slave PID Gc2 s]
    end
    FF --> SC
    SC --> FCE[Metering Pump and Control Valve]
    FCE --> PROC[Dechlorination Basin]
    PROC --> AM
    PROC --> ORP
    PROC --> NXT[Outlet to Environment]
```

### 4.3 Functional Signal Flow Diagram (ISA Convention)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP((Setpoint)) --> SUM((Sum))
    PV((Process Variable)) --> SUM
    SUM --> CTRL[Controller Gc s]
    CTRL --> CV[Control Valve Gv s]
    CV --> PL[Process Gp s]
    PL --> TR[Transducer H s]
    TR --> PV
    DIST[Disturbance Load Change] -.-> PL
    style SP fill:#fff,stroke:#000
    style PV fill:#fff,stroke:#000
    style SUM fill:#f9f,stroke:#333
    style CTRL fill:#bbf,stroke:#333
    style CV fill:#bfb,stroke:#333
    style PL fill:#ffb,stroke:#333
    style TR fill:#fbb,stroke:#333
    style DIST fill:#fcc,stroke:#a33,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
```

### 4.4 Sensor Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Need to measure residual chlorine] --> Q1{Target less than 0.1 mg per L?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Continuous online required?}
    Q1 -- No --> A1[Use DPD Colorimetric]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Budget available?}
    Q2 -- No --> A1
    Q3 -- Yes --> A2[Use Amperometric Sensor]
    Q3 -- No --> A3[Use ORP Probe with calibration]
    A2 --> A4[Pair with ORP for Cascade]
    A3 --> A4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "draw the instrumentation loop," students often forget to show the **signal lines** (dashed) in addition to the **process lines** (solid). A complete ISA-standard diagram includes both. Marks are awarded for each labeled element.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
Define dichlorination. Why is it a necessary step in industrial wastewater treatment?

> **Model Answer:**
> Dichlorination is the controlled chemical reduction of residual free or combined chlorine in an effluent stream to environmentally safe levels, typically **≤ 0.1 mg/L**, using reducing agents such as **SO₂, NaHSO₃, or Na₂S₂O₃**.
> It is necessary because (i) residual chlorine is **toxic to aquatic fauna** even at 0.05 mg/L, (ii) chlorine combines with organics to form **trihalomethanes (THMs)**, which are carcinogenic, and (iii) chlorinated discharge **violates pollution control board norms** (e.g., CPCB / EPA standards). It is the final polishing step before safe disposal or reuse of industrial effluent.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module Test 1]**
List the four common reducing agents used for dichlorination and state the key advantage of using SO₂ in large plants.

> **Model Answer:**
> The four common agents are: **(i) Sulfur Dioxide (SO₂), (ii) Sodium Sulfite (Na₂SO₃), (iii) Sodium Bisulfite (NaHSO₃), and (iv) Sodium Thiosulfate (Na₂S₂O₃).**
> **Key advantage of SO₂:** It is a **gaseous reagent** that can be fed directly from a pressurized cylinder through a precision control valve, eliminating the need for dissolution tanks, slurry handling, or concentration calibration. It also has the **lowest unit cost per kg of Cl₂ removed** and produces no dissolved solids in the effluent.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** With a neat labeled block diagram, explain the **cascade control architecture** used for dichlorination of wastewater in a large-scale plant. Justify why a **dual-sensor (amperometric + ORP)** configuration is preferred. **(7 marks)**
