**(b)** A municipal STP handles $Q = 800 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$ of effluent with an inlet free chlorine concentration of $C_{Cl_2} = 3.2 \text{ mg/L}$. Using **38% liquid NaHSO₃** solution (density 1.2 kg/L), calculate:
  (i) The stoichiometric mass flow rate of pure NaHSO₃.
  (ii) The volumetric flow rate of the commercial 38% solution in L/h.
  (iii) The corresponding pump stroke setting in mL/min. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Answer (a):**
>
> **Block Diagram (text description for written exam):**
> The cascade control system has two nested PID loops. The **master (outer) loop** uses the slow but highly specific **amperometric chlorine analyzer (AT-101)** to measure residual Cl₂ downstream of the dechlorination basin. Its setpoint is fixed at **0.1 mg/L**. The controller output of this master loop is **not** sent to the final control element — it is dynamically assigned as the setpoint of the **slave (inner) loop**.
>
> The **slave loop** uses the fast-responding **ORP probe (AT-102)** (typical time constant 5–10 s) to track the master-generated setpoint. The slave controller's output drives the **metering pump (P-101)** that injects NaHSO₃ into the mixing basin.
>
> **Justification for dual-sensor configuration:**
> - **ORP sensor:** Fast response, low cost, ideal for the inner (high-bandwidth) loop, but **non-specific** — responds to all oxidizers and changes with pH.
> - **Amperometric sensor:** Specific to free Cl₂, accurate, but **slower** (lag 30–60 s) and more expensive. Ideal for the outer (low-bandwidth) loop that re-trims the ORP setpoint periodically.
> - The combination provides **both speed (ORP) and accuracy (amperometric)**, while also providing **sensor redundancy** for plant safety.
>
> **Model Answer (b):**
>
> **(i) Stoichiometric mass flow rate of pure NaHSO₃:**
> Using the derived formula $D_{NaHSO_3} = 2.93 \times C_{Cl_2} \times Q$ (g/h, with $C_{Cl_2}$ in mg/L and $Q$ in m³/h):
>
> $$
> D_{NaHSO_3} = 2.93 \times 3.2 \times 800 = 7500.8 \text{ g/h} \approx 7.50 \text{ kg/h}
> $$
>
> **(ii) Volumetric flow of 38% commercial solution:**
>
> $$
> m_{sol} = \frac{7500.8}{0.38} = 19739 \text{ g/h}
> $$
>
> $$
> V_{sol} = \frac{19739}{1200 \text{ g/L}} = 16.45 \text{ L/h}
> $$
>
> **(iii) Pump stroke setting in mL/min:**
>
> $$
> V_{mL/min} = \frac{16.45 \times 1000}{60} = 274.2 \text{ mL/min}
> $$
>
> **Final boxed answer:** $V_{pump} \approx 274 \text{ mL/min}$ — this value is the **setpoint** of the slave controller's output stage.

#### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Compare the use of **sulfur dioxide (SO₂)** and **sodium bisulfite (NaHSO₃)** as dechlorinating agents in large-scale industries. Discuss the corresponding **instrumentation requirements** for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **stoichiometric dose equation** for sulfur dioxide gas in terms of chlorine concentration $C_{Cl_2}$ (mg/L) and water flow rate $Q$ (m³/h). A plant uses SO₂ at a feed pressure of 2 bar and a control valve $C_v$ of 4.0. If the required dose is 2.5 kg/h, calculate the expected pressure drop across the valve. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Answer (a):**
>
> | Aspect | SO₂ (gas) | NaHSO₃ (liquid) |
> |---|---|---|
> | Phase | Gaseous, fed from cylinder | Liquid solution (typically 38%) |
> | Cost | Lowest (per kg Cl₂) | Higher |
> | Handling hazard | Toxic, requires scrubber & SCBA | Mild irritant, simpler handling |
> | Instrumentation | Mass flow controller (MFC) or **thermal mass flow meter** + control valve on gas line | **Diaphragm metering pump** with VFD or stroke control |
> | Response time | Very fast (gas) | Fast (liquid) |
> | Storage | Pressurized cylinder or bulk tanker | Polyethylene day tank |
> | Best suited for | Large municipal plants (>50 MLD) | Small/medium plants (<10 MLD) |
>
> **Instrumentation for SO₂:** Requires (i) **gas cylinder pressure regulator**, (ii) **thermal mass flow controller (MFC)** for accurate gas dosing, (iii) **solenoid shut-off valve** for emergency interlock, (iv) **leak detector (H₂S-style electrochemical sensor)** for safety, and (v) **scrubber pH control loop** on the vent line.
>
> **Instrumentation for NaHSO₃:** Requires (i) **day tank level transmitter** (LT, ultrasonic or differential pressure), (ii) **metering pump with VFD speed control** or **electronic stroke controller**, (iii) **calibration column** for stroke vs. flow verification, and (iv) **pulse-dampener** to smooth the dosing.
>
> **Model Answer (b):**
>
> **Derivation:**
> Balanced reaction: $\text{SO}_2 + \text{Cl}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \to \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\text{HCl}$
>
> 1 mole of Cl₂ (71 g) reacts with 1 mole of SO₂ (64 g).
>
> Mass ratio: $\frac{64}{71} \approx 0.9$
>
> Mass flow rate of SO₂:
>
> $$
> D_{SO_2} = 0.9 \times C_{Cl_2} \times Q \;\;\text{(g/h, with }C_{Cl_2}\text{ in mg/L, }Q\text{ in m}^3/\text{h)}
> $$
>
> **Numerical Calculation:**
> Required dose: $D = 2.5 \text{ kg/h} = 2500 \text{ g/h}$
> Inlet pressure: $P_1 = 2 \text{ bar} = 29.0 \text{ psia}$
> Use liquid-gas conversion via ideal gas law for SO₂ at NTP:
> Molar volume of SO₂ at NTP $\approx 22.4 \text{ L/mol}$ (molar mass 64 g/mol)
>
> Volumetric flow required:
>
> $$
> Q_{SO_2} = \frac{2500 \text{ g/h}}{64 \text{ g/mol}} \times 22.4 \text{ L/mol} = 875 \text{ L/h} \approx 14.58 \text{ L/min}
> $$
>
> Using the **valve sizing equation** (simplified SI form):
>
> $$
> Q = C_v \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P}{\rho}}
> $$
>
> Where $Q$ is in m³/h, $\Delta P$ in bar, and $\rho$ in kg/m³ (SO₂ gas ≈ 2.26 kg/m³ at STP).
>
> Solving for $\Delta P$:
>
> $$
> 0.01458 \text{ m}^3/\text{min} = 0.875 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} = C_v \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P}{2.26}}
> $$
>
> $$
> 0.875 = 4.0 \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P}{2.26}}
> $$
>
> $$
> \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P}{2.26}} = 0.21875
> $$
>
> $$
> \frac{\Delta P}{2.26} = 0.04785
> $$
>
> $$
> \boxed{\Delta P = 0.108 \text{ bar} \approx 10.8 \text{ kPa}}
> $$
>
> This $\Delta P$ corresponds to the **valve opening** in the cascade slave loop.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in Dichlorination Questions:**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency in stoichiometry** — Mixing mg/L with m³/h vs. g/L with L/h. Always **declare units explicitly** in every line.
> 2. **Confusing the role of the cascade master/slave** — Students often write that the master controller "directly" controls the pump. **No** — master's output is the **setpoint of the slave**.
> 3. **Forgetting to apply the 38% strength factor** when computing volumetric flow of commercial bisulfite. Many students compute pure NaHSO₃ volume and call it the answer.
> 4. **Drawing the loop without a feedback arrow** — A control loop without a clear **return signal from sensor to controller** is marked down 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Ignoring pH control** — Dichlorination produces HCl, which depresses pH and may corrode downstream piping. A complete answer must mention the **pH trim loop**.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Dichlorination = **chemical reduction of residual Cl₂** to ≤ **0.1 mg/L** using SO₂, Na₂SO₃, NaHSO₃, or Na₂S₂O₃.
- ✅ **SO₂** is the cheapest and most common in **large** plants; **NaHSO₃** is preferred when gas handling is restricted.
- ✅ Stoichiometric mass ratios: **SO₂:Cl₂ = 0.9**, **Na₂SO₃:Cl₂ = 1.46**, **NaHSO₃:Cl₂ = 2.93** (all by mass).
- ✅ **Cascade control** uses an **amperometric master loop** + **ORP slave loop** + **metering pump** as final element.
- ✅ The **ORP setpoint is dynamic** — it is **not fixed**, but **generated by the master controller's output**.
- ✅ **Feedforward** from the inlet flow transmitter **pre-positions** the pump to handle load changes faster than feedback alone.
- ✅ A **pH control loop** is mandatory downstream to neutralize HCl byproduct.
- ✅ Hydraulic residence time in the dechlorination basin is typically **5–30 minutes**.
- ✅ For an exam, **always**: (i) write balanced equation, (ii) compute moles, (iii) apply stoichiometric ratio, (iv) convert to mass, (v) apply solution strength factor, (vi) express final answer with units.
- ✅ Key regulatory limit to memorize: **residual Cl₂ ≤ 0.1 mg/L** at discharge.
- ✅ Sensor selection rule: **Amperometric** = accuracy; **ORP** = speed; **DPD colorimetric** = lab verification; **pH** = process safety.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Neutralisation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT864 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN LARGE SCALE INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Neutralisation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Neutralisation** in the context of large-scale process industries is defined as a controlled chemical unit operation in which an acidic stream (proton donor, H⁺ ion source) and an alkaline/basic stream (proton acceptor, OH⁻ ion source) are brought into intimate contact in a Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR) to yield a salt and water, with the primary control objective being the maintenance of a desired **pH setpoint** of the effluent stream.

The governing stoichiometric reaction is:

$$H^{+} + OH^{-} \rightarrow H_{2}O \quad \Delta H = -55.84 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

In industrial process control nomenclature, neutralisation is classified as a **highly nonlinear, gain-varying, time-varying pH control problem** because the steady-state titration curve exhibits an extremely steep sigmoidal (S-shaped) characteristic near the equivalence point.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Neutralisation is one of the canonical benchmark problems for studying **process nonlinearity, dead-time dominance, and adaptive control strategies**. It is universally used by KTU examiners to test a student's ability to recognize why a fixed-gain PID controller fails on a strongly nonlinear plant.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car on a road where the steering sensitivity suddenly changes every few meters — *light* on straight stretches, *extremely sensitive* on sharp curves, and *sluggish* on slopes. If you use the same hand-force on the steering wheel throughout, the car will weave erratically. This is *exactly* the problem with pH neutralisation control:

- The **acid stream** is the moving car.
- The **base/reagent addition valve** is the steering wheel.
- The **pH meter** downstream is your eyes (feedback sensor).
- The **titration curve** is the road whose curvature keeps changing.

Near neutrality (pH = 7), a **tiny drop of reagent** causes a **huge swing** in pH — the curve is almost vertical. Away from neutrality, you must pour **copious amounts of reagent** to move pH even slightly. This dramatic gain variation is what makes pH control the "evergreen" challenging problem in process control.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants to Remember:**
> - Ionic product of water: $K_w = [H^+][OH^-] = 1.0 \times 10^{-14}$ at **25 °C**
> - Neutral pH: **pH = 7.0** at 25 °C (pure water)
> - Faraday constant: $F = 96485$ **C/mol**
> - Gas constant: $R = 8.314$ **J/(mol·K)**

---

### 1.3 Why Neutralisation Matters in Large-Scale Industries

| Industry | Acid Stream | Base Stream | Target pH | Criticality |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Wastewater Treatment | Sulfuric/HCl waste | Lime (Ca(OH)₂) or NaOH | 6.5 – 7.5 | EPA environmental discharge |
| Petroleum Refining | Spent caustic | Sulfuric acid | 6 – 9 | Corrosion prevention |
| Pharmaceutical | Process effluents | NaOH/KOH | 5 – 8 | API stability |
| Food & Beverage | Citric/lactic acid | NaOH | 3.5 – 7 | Taste & shelf life |
| Pulp & Paper | Black liquor acid | White liquor (NaOH + Na₂S) | 10 – 12 | Kraft process recovery |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Chemistry of Neutralisation — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Dissociation of Water**
Water undergoes auto-ionisation:

$$H_2O \rightleftharpoons H^{+} + OH^{-}$$

with equilibrium constant $K_w = [H^+][OH^-] = 10^{-14}$ at 25 °C.

**Step 2 — Definition of pH and pOH**

$$pH = -\log_{10}[H^+] \qquad pOH = -\log_{10}[OH^-]$$

and at 25 °C, $pH + pOH = 14$.

**Step 3 — Strength of Acids and Bases**
- **Strong acid** (HCl, H₂SO₄, HNO₃): completely dissociates → $[H^+] = C_a$
- **Weak acid** (acetic, citric): partial dissociation → governed by $K_a$
- **Strong base** (NaOH, KOH): completely dissociates → $[OH^-] = C_b$
- **Weak base** (NH₄OH): partial dissociation → governed by $K_b$

**Step 4 — Henderson–Hasselbalch Equation (Weak Acid/Base Systems)**

$$pH = pK_a + \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{[A^-]}{[HA]}\right)$$

This is the **workhorse equation** for buffer design and weak-acid neutralisation.

**Step 5 — Material Balance in a Neutralising CSTR**
For a single-stream system with feed acid concentration $C_a$ and reagent base concentration $C_b$:

$$V\frac{dC}{dt} = q_a C_a - q_b C_b - r\,V$$

where $r$ is the reaction rate (instantaneous for acid-base reactions, so $r$ is effectively governed by ionic equilibrium).

### 2.2 The Steady-State Titration Curve — The "Bump"

The single most important graphical concept in pH control is the **titration curve**, plotting effluent pH versus reagent flow rate $q_b$ at constant acid flow $q_a$.

For a **strong acid–strong base** system (e.g., HCl + NaOH), the curve is a sharp **sigmoidal S-curve**:

- **Region I (pH < 3):** Excess acid dominates; curve is nearly flat.
- **Region II (3 < pH < 11):** The "**bump**" — slope is enormous. A fractional change in $q_b$ of $\sim 10^{-4}$ can swing pH by 8 units.
- **Region III (pH > 11):** Excess base dominates; curve flattens again.

The **process gain** $K_p = \dfrac{\Delta \text{pH}}{\Delta q_b}$ therefore varies over **6 to 8 decades** — a fact that single-handedly defeats any fixed-gain PID controller.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The process gain $K_p$ is not a constant. It is a function of the operating point:
> $$K_p(q_b) = \frac{\partial \text{pH}}{\partial q_b}\bigg|_{\text{steady state}}$$
> Near the equivalence point, $K_p \to \infty$. This is the mathematical reason classical linear control fails.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Engineering Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | pH definition | $pH = -\log_{10}[H^+]$ | Direct pH conversion | dimensionless |
| 2 | Water ionic product | $K_w = [H^+][OH^-] = 10^{-14}$ | Equilibrium check at 25 °C | (mol/L)² |
| 3 | Henderson–Hasselbalch | $pH = pK_a + \log([A^-]/[HA])$ | Buffer design | dimensionless |
| 4 | CSTR mass balance | $V\frac{dC}{dt}=q_{in}C_{in}-q_{out}C_{out}-rV$ | Reactor dynamics | mol/s |
| 5 | Strong acid titration | $pH = -\log(C_a - C_b)$ | Direct pH from normality | mol/L |
| 6 | Equivalent point condition | $C_a q_a = C_b q_b$ | Stoichiometric feed ratio | mol/s |
| 7 | Process gain (linearised) | $K_p = \dfrac{d(\text{pH})}{dq_b} = \dfrac{-1}{\ln(10)\,[H^+]}\cdot\dfrac{d[H^+]}{dq_b}$ | Controller tuning | pH per (L/s) |
| 8 | Electrochemical (Nernst) | $E = E_0 - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\dfrac{[\text{Red}]}{[\text{Ox}]}$ | pH probe voltage | Volts |
| 9 | Glass electrode slope | $S = \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{F}$ | pH probe calibration | mV/pH |
| 10 | Time constant of CSTR | $\tau = V/q$ | Mixing dynamics | seconds |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of Neutralisation Control

- **Wastewater treatment plants (WWTPs):** Continuous neutralisation of industrial effluents before discharge into rivers; failure results in fish kills and regulatory penalties.
- **Pharmaceutical API manufacture:** Final pH adjustment is often the last unit operation before crystallisation/drying; off-spec pH causes batch rejection.
- **Flue-gas desulphurisation (FGD):** Scrubber liquor pH must be controlled in the 5.5–6.0 range for optimal SO₂ absorption with limestone slurry.
- **Bioreactor pH control:** Living cells produce lactic/acetic acid; a precise neutralisation loop keeps pH within ±0.05 units for optimal growth.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Deriving the Titration Curve Equation (Strong Acid + Strong Base)

**Setup:** A CSTR of volume $V$ receives acid stream with flow $q_a$, concentration $C_a$, and base stream $q_b$, concentration $C_b$. Effluent concentration of H⁺ is denoted $[H^+]$. Reaction is instantaneous.

**Step 1:** Steady-state mass balance on $H^+$ (neglecting $H^+$ from water auto-ionisation, valid when $[H^+] > 10^{-6}$ M):

$$q_a C_a - q_b C_b = q_e [H^+]$$

where $q_e = q_a + q_b$ is effluent flow. Rearranging:

$$[H^+] = \frac{q_a C_a - q_b C_b}{q_a + q_b}$$

**Step 2:** Taking $q_a$ and $C_a$, $C_b$ as constants, define the stoichiometric equivalent base flow:

$$q_{b,\text{eq}} = \frac{q_a C_a}{C_b}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into Step 1 to express $[H^+]$ in terms of deviation from stoichiometry:

$$\boxed{\;[H^+] = C_b \cdot \frac{q_{b,\text{eq}} - q_b}{q_a + q_b}\;}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the pH definition $pH = -\log_{10}[H^+]$:

$$pH(q_b) = -\log_{10}\!\left[C_b \cdot \frac{q_{b,\text{eq}} - q_b}{q_a + q_b}\right]$$

**Step 5:** Differentiate w.r.t. $q_b$ to obtain the **process gain** $K_p(q_b)$:

$$\frac{d(\text{pH})}{dq_b} = \frac{-1}{\ln(10)} \cdot \frac{1}{[H^+]} \cdot \frac{d[H^+]}{dq_b}$$

Computing $\dfrac{d[H^+]}{dq_b}$ from Step 3:

$$\frac{d[H^+]}{dq_b} = -C_b \cdot \frac{q_a + q_b - (q_{b,\text{eq}} - q_b)}{(q_a + q_b)^2} = -C_b \cdot \frac{2q_b + q_a - q_{b,\text{eq}}}{(q_a + q_b)^2}$$

**Step 6:** At the equivalence point $q_b = q_{b,\text{eq}}$, the numerator becomes $q_a + q_{b,\text{eq}}$ but $[H^+] \to 0$ (dominated by water auto-ionisation, $\approx 10^{-7}$ M). The ratio therefore **blows up to infinity**:

$$\lim_{q_b \to q_{b,\text{eq}}} K_p(q_b) = \lim_{[H^+] \to 10^{-7}} \frac{1}{[H^+]} = 10^{7} \; \text{M}^{-1}$$

This mathematically proves the **infinite process gain** at the equivalence point.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Dynamic Model of pH in a CSTR (Linearised)

**Step 1:** Start from the dynamic mass balance (dropping the quasi-steady ionic product for clarity):

$$V\frac{d[H^+]}{dt} = q_a C_a - q_b C_b - (q_a + q_b)[H^+]$$

**Step 2:** Define state $x = [H^+]$, input $u = q_b$, and disturbance $d = q_a$. Output $y = \text{pH} = -\log_{10} x$.

**Step 3:** Linearise about an operating point $(x_0, u_0, d_0)$ using Taylor expansion to first order:

$$\frac{d(\Delta x)}{dt} = -\frac{(q_{a0} + q_{b0})}{V}\Delta x - \frac{C_b + x_0}{V}\Delta u + \frac{C_a - x_0}{V}\Delta d$$

**Step 4:** Transfer function from $u = q_b$ to $x = [H^+]$:

$$\frac{\Delta X(s)}{\Delta U(s)} = -\frac{(C_b + x_0)/V}{s + (q_{a0} + q_{b0})/V}$$

**Step 5:** Define the time constant $\tau = V/(q_{a0}+q_{b0})$ and DC gain $K_x = -(C_b+x_0)/(q_{a0}+q_{b0})$.

$$\frac{\Delta X(s)}{\Delta U(s)} = \frac{K_x}{\tau s + 1}$$

**Step 6:** Map from $[H^+]$ to pH (output linearisation):

$$\Delta y = \Delta(\text{pH}) = -\frac{1}{x_0 \ln 10}\,\Delta x$$

Combining:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{\Delta \text{pH}(s)}{\Delta Q_b(s)} = \frac{-1}{x_0 \ln 10} \cdot \frac{K_x}{\tau s + 1} = \frac{K_p}{\tau s + 1}\;}$$

with $K_p = \dfrac{C_b + x_0}{x_0 \ln(10)(q_{a0}+q_{b0})}$. Note that $K_p$ depends on the operating point $x_0$, which is the **fundamental nonlinearity**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Nernst Equation for a Glass pH Electrode

**Step 1:** The glass electrode develops a potential proportional to hydrogen-ion activity:

$$E = E^0 - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\frac{a_{\text{Red}}}{a_{\text{Ox}}}$$

**Step 2:** For the H⁺/H₂ half-cell, $n = 1$ and the activity of the reduced form (H₂) is fixed by reference, giving:

$$E = E^0 + \frac{RT}{F}\ln[H^+] = E^0 - \frac{2.303\,RT}{F}\,\text{pH}$$

**Step 3:** The **slope** of the glass electrode at temperature $T$ (Kelvin):

$$S = \frac{2.303\,RT}{F}$$

**Step 4:** Numerical evaluation at $T = 298.15$ K:

$$\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298.15}{96485} \\
&= \frac{5708.2}{96485} \\
&= 0.05916 \text{ V/pH unit}
\end{aligned}$$

So a **1-unit pH change** produces a **59.16 mV** change at 25 °C — the canonical "Nernst slope" used to calibrate every industrial pH probe.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Why PID Alone Fails

| Plant Regime | Operating pH | $K_p$ (approx.) | PID Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|
| Far acidic | pH ≈ 2 | 0.001 pH/(mL/s) | Sluggish, large offset |
| Near equivalence | pH ≈ 7 | 10⁶ pH/(mL/s) | Wild oscillation, instability |
| Far alkaline | pH ≈ 12 | 0.001 pH/(mL/s) | Sluggish, large offset |

**Conclusion:** A single fixed-gain PID tuned for the flat region **will never** stabilise the bump region, and vice versa.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

 pH

    │                      ●●

  8 ┤                   ●              here. This is the "bump".

  7 ┤ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ● ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ Equivalence Point (pH=7)

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
*Define neutralisation. Why is it considered a highly nonlinear control problem in large-scale industries?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Neutralisation is the unit operation in which an acid stream and a base stream are reacted in a controlled CSTR to produce a salt and water, with the goal of maintaining a desired pH in the effluent. It is considered a highly nonlinear control problem because the steady-state titration curve exhibits an S-shaped sigmoidal characteristic in which the process gain $K_p$ varies over six to eight decades — being very small far from the equivalence point and tending to infinity at the equivalence point. Consequently, a fixed-gain linear controller (such as a conventional PID) cannot provide stable, tight control over the entire operating range. **[3 marks]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Exam]**
*State the Henderson–Hasselbalch equation. Mention its significance in neutralisation control of weak-acid systems.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Henderson–Hasselbalch equation is:
$$pH = pK_a + \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{[A^-]}{[HA]}\right)$$
Its significance in neutralisation control lies in the fact that for weak-acid (or weak-base) systems, the titration curve is much flatter around the equivalence point than for strong-acid systems, giving a finite and manageable process gain. The equation allows the engineer to design buffer compositions and predict the pH after a given dose of reagent, which is essential for controller tuning and setpoint selection in pharmaceutical and food-industry neutralisation processes. **[3 marks]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Exam, Question A]**

**(a)** Derive the steady-state relationship between effluent pH and reagent base flow rate for a strong-acid / strong-base neutralisation CSTR. Show that the process gain tends to infinity at the equivalence point. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the closed-loop pH control scheme for an industrial neutraliser. Identify the role of each block. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Q3(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Define variables.** Let $q_a$ = acid volumetric flow, $C_a$ = acid normality, $q_b$ = base flow (manipulated), $C_b$ = base normality, $V$ = reactor volume, $q_e = q_a + q_b$ = effluent flow. **[1 mark]**

**Step 2 — Mass balance on H⁺ at steady state:**

$$q_a C_a - q_b C_b = q_e [H^+]$$

$$[H^+] = \frac{q_a C_a - q_b C_b}{q_a + q_b}$$ **[1 mark]**

**Step 3 — Equivalence condition:** $q_{b,\text{eq}} = \dfrac{q_a C_a}{C_b}$. Substitute:

$$[H^+] = C_b \cdot \frac{q_{b,\text{eq}} - q_b}{q_a + q_b}$$ **[1 mark]**

**Step 4 — Apply pH definition:**

$$pH = -\log_{10}\!\left[C_b \cdot \frac{q_{b,\text{eq}} - q_b}{q_a + q_b}\right]$$ **[1 mark]**

**Step 5 — Differentiate to obtain process gain:**

$$K_p(q_b) = \frac{d(\text{pH})}{dq_b} = \frac{-1}{\ln(10) \cdot [H^+]} \cdot \frac{d[H^+]}{dq_b}$$ **[1 mark]**

**Step 6 — Show the blow-up at $q_b = q_{b,\text{eq}}$.** As $q_b \to q_{b,\text{eq}}$, the numerator of $[H^+]$ vanishes, so $[H^+] \to 10^{-7}$ M (water auto-ionisation). Therefore:

$$K_p \to \frac{1}{\ln(10) \cdot 10^{-7}} \to \infty$$ **[2 marks]**

This proves that the process gain is unbounded at the equivalence point, mathematically establishing the nonlinearity of the plant.

---

#### Model Solution to Q3(b) — 7 Marks

The standard closed-loop pH control loop consists of the following blocks, in series:

1. **Setpoint generator (SP):** Provides the desired pH (e.g., 7.0) as a 4–20 mA signal. **[1 mark]**
2. **pH sensor (AIT):** A glass-reference electrode pair producing a Nernstian voltage $E = E^0 - 0.05916 \cdot \text{pH}$ volts at 25 °C. **[1 mark]**
3. **Transmitter (AIT):** Linearises and converts the mV signal to a standardised 4–20 mA current loop. **[1 mark]**
4. **PID controller:** Computes error $e = \text{SP} - \text{PV}$ and outputs a control signal using $u(t) = K_c\!\left(e + \dfrac{1}{T_i}\!\int e\,dt + T_d \dfrac{de}{dt}\right)$. **[1 mark]**
5. **Current-to-Pressure (I/P) converter:** Converts 4–20 mA to 3–15 psi pneumatic signal for the control valve (or to a VFD command for a metering pump). **[1 mark]**
6. **Reagent control valve / pump:** Modulates $q_b$ — the final control element. **[1 mark]**
7. **CSTR (Plant):** The neutralising reactor where mixing and reaction occur; its output pH closes the loop via the sensor. **[1 mark]**

The functional flow: **SP → Comparator → PID → I/P → Valve → CSTR → pH Sensor → AIT → Comparator.**

---

#### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning for Q3

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks in Q3:**
> 1. **Forgetting to mention the auto-ionisation of water** ($[H^+] = 10^{-7}$ M) when arguing that $K_p \to \infty$. Many students stop at $[H^+] = 0$, which is mathematically incomplete; the correct limit is governed by $K_w$.
> 2. **Omitting units** in the mass balance. Always state that $C_a, C_b$ are in eq/L (or mol/L for monoprotic systems).
> 3. **Drawing the block diagram without labelling the signal types** (4–20 mA, 3–15 psi, mV). KTU examiners award 1 mark specifically for signal-type annotation.
> 4. **Writing only the formula without a sentence explaining its physical meaning** in Part (a). Each formula must be accompanied by a one-line justification.

---

### 5.3 Question B (Internal Choice Alternative, 14 Marks)

**Q4. [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Supplementary Exam, Question B]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the *gain-scheduling* and *adaptive control* strategies used to handle the nonlinearity of neutralisation processes. Compare their advantages and limitations. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A wastewater neutraliser handles an acid stream of normality $C_a = 0.1$ N at $q_a = 10$ L/s using NaOH of normality $C_b = 1.0$ N. Compute (i) the stoichiometric equivalent base flow, (ii) the effluent pH when $q_b = 11$ L/s, and (iii) the effluent pH when $q_b = 9$ L/s. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Q4(a) — 7 Marks

**Gain Scheduling:**

Gain scheduling is a nonlinear control technique in which the controller parameters ($K_c$, $T_i$, $T_d$) are **pre-computed as functions of the operating point** (typically the measured pH itself). The measured pH is used to look up a table or evaluate a function that gives the appropriate PID gains. Implementation steps: **[2 marks]**

1. Linearise the plant at multiple operating points along the titration curve.
2. Tune a PID at each operating point (e.g., using Ziegler–Nichols or IMC).
3. Store the gain triplets as a lookup table indexed by pH.
4. During operation, interpolate between table entries as pH moves.

**Advantages:** Simple to implement in modern DCS; no online parameter estimation needed; deterministic. **[1 mark]**
**Limitations:** Requires an exhaustive open-loop identification campaign; cannot adapt to *changing* plant dynamics (e.g., when feed concentration drifts). **[1 mark]**

**Adaptive Control:**

Adaptive control continuously identifies the plant parameters online (using recursive least squares, for example) and updates the controller gains in real time using a chosen tuning law (e.g., MIT rule, Lyapunov-based, or model-reference adaptive control). **[2 marks]**

**Advantages:** Tracks slow drift in feed conditions; handles changing pH curve shapes; gives tighter control than gain scheduling on truly time-varying plants. **[0.5 mark]**
**Limitations:** Complex to commission; convergence not guaranteed during transients; sensitive to sensor noise; safety interlocks are mandatory to prevent runaway. **[0.5 mark]**

The KTU-typical comparison table:

| Feature | Gain Scheduling | Adaptive Control |
|---|---|---|
| Online computation | None (lookup) | Continuous |
| Commissioning effort | High | Very High |
| Adapts to drift | No | Yes |
| DCS Implementation | Trivial (function blocks) | Custom code |
| Stability guarantees | Yes (per region) | Conditional |

---

#### Model Solution to Q4(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $C_a = 0.1$ N, $q_a = 10$ L/s, $C_b = 1.0$ N.

**(i) Stoichiometric equivalent base flow:** **[2 marks]**

$$q_{b,\text{eq}} = \frac{q_a C_a}{C_b} = \frac{10 \times 0.1}{1.0} = 1.0 \text{ L/s}$$

**(ii) Effluent pH at $q_b = 11$ L/s (excess base):** **[2.5 marks]**

$$[H^+] = C_b \cdot \frac{q_{b,\text{eq}} - q_b}{q_a + q_b} = 1.0 \cdot \frac{1.0 - 11}{10 + 11} = \frac{-10}{21} \text{ N}$$

Negative $[H^+]$ means the solution is basic, so we must compute $[OH^-]$ instead:

$$[OH^-] = \frac{q_b C_b - q_a C_a}{q_a + q_b} = \frac{11 \times 1.0 - 10 \times 0.1}{21} = \frac{10}{21} = 0.476 \text{ N}$$

$$pOH = -\log_{10}(0.476) = 0.322$$

$$pH = 14 - 0.322 = 13.68$$

**(iii) Effluent pH at $q_b = 9$ L/s (excess acid):** **[2.5 marks]**

$$[H^+] = C_b \cdot \frac{q_{b,\text{eq}} - q_b}{q_a + q_b} = 1.0 \cdot \frac{1.0 - 9}{10 + 9} = \frac{-8}{19}$$

Since this is negative, $[H^+]$ from excess acid is:

$$[H^+] = \frac{q_a C_a - q_b C_b}{q_a + q_b} = \frac{1.0 - 0.9}{19} = \frac{0.1}{19} = 5.263 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N}$$

$$pH = -\log_{10}(5.263 \times 10^{-3}) = 2.28$$

**Final Answers:** (i) $q_{b,\text{eq}} = 1.0$ L/s; (ii) pH = **13.68**; (iii) pH = **2.28**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **Q4(b) Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error on $[H^+]$** when $q_b > q_{b,\text{eq}}$. A negative $[H^+]$ is physically meaningless; you must switch to $[OH^-]$ arithmetic and use $pH = 14 - pOH$.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 14** — this is only valid at 25 °C. State this assumption explicitly.
> 3. **Mixing normality and molarity** — for monoprotic strong acids/bases, 1 N = 1 M, but the examiner will penalise if you don't say so.
> 4. **Rounding too early.** Keep 3–4 significant figures through the calculation to avoid propagating error.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Neutralisation = acid + base → salt + water**, controlled to maintain a target **pH setpoint** in the effluent.
- The governing reaction $H^+ + OH^- \to H_2O$ is **instantaneous and exothermic** ($\Delta H = -55.84$ kJ/mol).
- The **titration curve is sigmoidal** with a near-vertical "bump" at the equivalence point; the process gain $K_p$ varies over **6–8 decades**.
- **Mass balance**: $V\dfrac{d[H^+]}{dt} = q_a C_a - q_b C_b - (q_a + q_b)[H^+]$.
- **Steady-state effluent concentration**: $[H^+] = C_b \cdot \dfrac{q_{b,\text{eq}} - q_b}{q_a + q_b}$.
- **Henderson–Hasselbalch** ($pH = pK_a + \log\dfrac{[A^-]}{[HA]}$) governs **weak-acid** neutralisation.
- **Glass-electrode Nernst slope** at 25 °C is **59.16 mV per pH unit**.
- **Fixed-gain PID fails** because of extreme gain variation; successful strategies include:
  - **Gain-scheduling PID** (lookup of $K_c, T_i, T_d$ vs. pH)
  - **Adaptive control** (online parameter estimation)
  - **Feedforward + feedback trim** (using acid-flow transmitter for anticipatory correction)
  - **Split-range control** (using both acid and reagent valves for bi-directional pH correction)
  - **Nonlinear model-based control** (e.g., exact feedback linearisation)
- **Equivalence condition**: $q_{b,\text{eq}} = \dfrac{q_a C_a}{C_b}$; always compute this **first** when solving numerical pH problems.
- **Water auto-ionisation** ($K_w = 10^{-14}$) sets the floor: $[H^+]$ cannot fall below $10^{-7}$ M (i.e., pH cannot exceed 7) from acid-side imbalance alone — excess base must be computed via $pOH$.
- **Industrial applications**: wastewater treatment, FGD scrubbers, pharmaceutical API pH adjustment, Kraft pulp recovery, food and beverage, and bioreactor pH control.
- **Time constant of a well-mixed CSTR**: $\tau = V/q_e$, typically **30 s to 5 min** in industrial neutralisers.
- **KTU examiners love** asking for: (i) the **steady-state titration equation derivation**, (ii) the **infinite-gain proof**, and (iii) **numerical pH computation** under excess acid or excess base conditions. Practise all three thoroughly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Valve Rangeability Required
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT864 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN LARGE SCALE INDUSTRIES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Valve Rangeability Required ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Valve Rangeability (R)** is defined as the ratio of the maximum controllable flow rate ($Q_{max}$) to the minimum controllable flow rate ($Q_{min}$) that a control valve can effectively regulate while maintaining stable, linear, and predictable process control. Mathematically, it is expressed as: $$R = \frac{Q_{max}}{Q_{min}}$$ In large-scale process industries, rangeability is a **critical sizing parameter** because real processes never operate at a single fixed flow rate. They experience: - **Startup and shutdown transients** - **Load variations** (e.g., 30% to 110% of design capacity) - **Product grade transitions** (e.g., switching from high-viscosity to low-viscosity crude in refineries) - **Setpoint changes** dictated by upstream/downstream unit demands ### 1.2 Two Fundamental Categories of Rangeability | # | Type | Symbol | Physical Meaning | |---|------|--------|------------------| | 1 | **Inherent Rangeability** | $R_{inh}$ | Capability of the bare valve tested under constant pressure drop | | 2 | **Installed Rangeability** | $R_{inst}$ | Actual operating range in a real piping system where ΔP varies with flow | > [!NOTE] > **Inherent Rangeability** is a property of the **valve trim alone** (manufacturer's specification), typically ranging from **30:1 to 100:1** depending on valve type. **Installed Rangeability** is always **less than or equal to** the inherent value because of parasitic pressure losses in piping and fittings. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Household Tap with a Pressure Tank > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a water tap connected to an overhead tank. When the tank is **full**, even with the tap barely open, water gushes out because of high pressure. When the tank is **nearly empty**, even with the tap fully open, only a trickle comes out. Now compare this with a tap connected directly to a **municipal pump** at constant high pressure — the flow is more predictable across all opening positions. The **first scenario is "installed" condition** (pressure drops as flow changes), and the **second is "inherent" test condition** (constant pressure). The control valve behaves identically — its true capability shrinks once bolted into a real pipeline. ### 1.4 Why Rangeability is "Required" in Industries The phrase *"Valve Rangeability Required"* in Module 1 emphasizes that the engineer must **calculate the rangeability demanded by the process** and then **select a valve whose installed rangeability meets or exceeds that demand**. Common industrial scenarios demanding high rangeability: - **Boiler feedwater control** in a 500 MW thermal power plant: 25% to 100% load range - **Reactor coolant flow** in a petrochemical cracking unit: turndown ratio of 20:1 - **Distillation column reflux** control: 10:1 to 50:1 depending on feed composition ### 1.5 Standard Reference Metrics (Bold Emphasis) - **Inherent rangeability** for **globe valves (linear trim)**: **30:1 to 50:1** - **Inherent rangeability** for **equal-percentage trim**: **50:1 to 100:1** - **Quick-opening valves**: **10:1 to 20:1** - **Typical process turndown ratio**: **4:1 to 20:1** - **Standard control valve inherent rangeability benchmark**: **50:1** ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Physics of Rangeability — Step-by-Step Logic **Step 1 — The Fundamental Flow Equation** All throttling control valves obey the ISA-75.01 standard flow equation: $$Q = N \cdot C_v \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P_v}{s_g}}$$ where each term has a precise role: - $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (m³/h) - $N$ = fractional valve opening (dimensionless, 0 to 1) - $C_v$ = valve flow coefficient (the metric defining valve capacity) - $\Delta P_v$ = pressure drop **across the valve** (bar or kPa) - $s_g$ = specific gravity of the fluid (water = 1.0) **Step 2 — Recognizing the Pressure-Drop Coupling** The control valve sits inside a piping network. The total system head equation is: $$\Delta P_{total} = \Delta P_v + \Delta P_{system}$$ The **system pressure drop** is approximately proportional to the square of flow (Darcy-Weisbach relation): $$\Delta P_{system} = K_{sys} \cdot Q^2$$ where $K_{sys}$ is a constant depending on pipe roughness, length, and fittings. **Step 3 — The Critical Observation** > [!IMPORTANT] > Because the pump or compressor supplies a **fixed $\Delta P_{total}$**, and $\Delta P_{system}$ **rises quadratically** with $Q$, the pressure drop available at the valve ($\Delta P_v$) must **decrease** as flow increases. This is the root cause of why **installed rangeability < inherent rangeability** in typical systems. **Step 4 — Definition of Maximum and Minimum Cv** The valve manufacturer specifies two boundary $C_v$ values: - $C_{v,max}$ = flow coefficient at full open ($N = 1$) - $C_{v,min}$ = flow coefficient at the smallest lift where the valve still produces a stable, linear, and controllable change in flow (typically $N \approx 0.05$ to $0.10$) **Step 5 — Combining Steps to Define Inherent Rangeability** Under **inherent test conditions**, the manufacturer holds $\Delta P_v$ constant. So: $$Q_{max,inh} = C_{v,max} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P_{test}}{s_g}} \quad ; \quad Q_{min,inh} = C_{v,min} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P_{test}}{s_g}}$$ The $\sqrt{\Delta P_{test}/s_g}$ terms cancel, leaving: $$R_{inh} = \frac{Q_{max,inh}}{Q_{min,inh}} = \frac{C_{v,max}}{C_{v,min}}$$ **Step 6 — Extending to Installed Conditions** In the real plant, the pressure drop is **not constant**: $$Q_{max,inst} = C_{v,max} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P_{v,max}}{s_g}} \quad ; \quad Q_{min,inst} = C_{v,min} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P_{v,min}}{s_g}}$$ Therefore: $$R_{inst} = \frac{Q_{max,inst}}{Q_{min,inst}} = \frac{C_{v,max}}{C_{v,min}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P_{v,max}}{\Delta P_{v,min}}} = R_{inh} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P_{v,max}}{\Delta P_{v,min}}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Result:** Since in any practical pumping system $\Delta P_{v,min} > \Delta P_{v,max}$, the ratio $\Delta P_{v,max} / \Delta P_{v,min} < 1$, forcing $R_{inst} < R_{inh}$. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | |---|----------|---------|----------------|-------| | 1 | **Inherent Rangeability** | $R_{inh} = C_{v,max} / C_{v,min}$ | Ratio of valve flow coefficients at full open and minimum controllable lift | dimensionless | | 2 | **Installed Rangeability** | $R_{inst} = R_{inh} \cdot \sqrt{\Delta P_{v,max} / \Delta P_{v,min}}$ | Actual operating range in a real piping system | dimensionless | | 3 | **Required (Process) Rangeability** | $R_{req} = Q_{process,max} / Q_{process,min}$ | Turndown ratio demanded by operating conditions | dimensionless | | 4 | **Valve Flow Equation** | $Q = N \cdot C_v \cdot \sqrt{\Delta P_v / s_g}$ | Standard ISA-75.01 sizing equation | m³/h | | 5 | **System Pressure Drop** | $\Delta P_{sys} = K_{sys} \cdot Q^2$ | Parabolic system curve | kPa | | 6 | **Head Balance** | $\Delta P_{total} = \Delta P_v + \Delta P_{sys}$ | Pump head splits between valve and pipe losses | kPa | | 7 | **Valve ΔP at Max Flow** | $\Delta P_{v,max} = \Delta P_{total} - K_{sys} \cdot Q_{max}^2$ | Pressure drop at upper end of operating range | kPa | | 8 | **Valve ΔP at Min Flow** | $\Delta P_{v,min} = \Delta P_{total} - K_{sys} \cdot Q_{min}^2$ | Pressure drop at lower end of operating range | kPa | | 9 | **Suitability Check** | $R_{inst} \geq R_{req}$ | Required condition for valve selection | — | | 10 | **Effective Cv** | $C_{v,eff} = Q / \sqrt{\Delta P_v / s_g}$ | Used in valve sizing for actual process conditions | — | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Valuation Note:** When writing the installed rangeability formula, **never** substitute $\Delta P_{v,min} / \Delta P_{v,max}$ — this is a common inversion error that flips the inequality direction and inflates the answer. ### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields | Industry | Application | Why Rangeability Matters | |----------|-------------|--------------------------| | **Thermal Power Plants** | Boiler feedwater control, HP/LP bypass valves | Load swings from 30% to 100% require wide flow modulation | | **Petrochemical Refineries** | Crude preheat trains, catalytic cracker slide valves | Different crude grades change viscosity and flow demand | | **Oil & Gas Pipeline Stations** | Pressure let-down stations, anti-surge valves | Compressor turndown forces wide flow variation | | **HVAC in Large Buildings** | Chilled water control valves | Seasonal load changes of 4:1 to 10:1 | | **Pulp & Paper Mills** | Stock flow to headbox, chemical additive metering | Consistency changes alter flow demand | | **Steel Plants** | Blast stove combustion air, cooling water valves | Reversing regenerative cycles demand turndown of 15:1 | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Installed Rangeability Formula We begin from the **ISA standard valve flow equation** and the **system head balance**, then combine them algebraically with full transparency. **Given:** - $Q = N \cdot C_v \cdot \sqrt{\Delta P_v / s_g}$ ... (Equation 1) - $\Delta P_{total} = \Delta P_v + K_{sys} \cdot Q^2$ ... (Equation 2) **Step 1 — Solve Equation 2 for $\Delta P_v$ at the two extreme flow rates.** At $Q = Q_{max}$ (valve fully open, $N = 1$): $$\Delta P_{v,max} = \Delta P_{total} - K_{sys} \cdot Q_{max}^2$$ At $Q = Q_{min}$ (valve at minimum controllable lift, $N = N_{min}$): $$\Delta P_{v,min} = \Delta P_{total} - K_{sys} \cdot Q_{min}^2$$ **Step 2 — Express the actual flow at each extreme using Equation 1.** $$Q_{max,inst} = 1 \cdot C_{v,max} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P_{v,max}}{s_g}}$$ $$Q_{min,inst} = N_{min} \cdot C_{v,min} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P_{v,min}}{s_g}}$$ **Step 3 — Form the ratio $Q_{max,inst} / Q_{min,inst}$.** $$\frac{Q_{max,inst}}{Q_{min,inst}} = \frac{C_{v,max} \cdot \sqrt{\Delta P_{v,max} / s_g}}{N_{min} \cdot C_{v,min} \cdot \sqrt{\Delta P_{v,min} / s_g}}$$ **Step 4 — Cancel the common terms $s_g$ and the $C_v$ definitions.** Because inherent rangeability is defined as $R_{inh} = C_{v,max} / C_{v,min}$, and at the minimum controllable lift the inherent test gives a flow ratio of $1 / N_{min}$ under constant ΔP, the term $N_{min}$ is absorbed into $R_{inh}$. Therefore: $$\frac{Q_{max,inst}}{Q_{min,inst}} = \frac{C_{v,max}}{C_{v,min}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P_{v,max}}{\Delta P_{v,min}}}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute the definition of inherent rangeability.** $$\boxed{R_{inst} = R_{inh} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P_{v,max}}{\Delta P_{v,min}}}}$$ This is the master equation. Note that $R_{inst}$ is the **installed (effective) rangeability** that a valve can deliver in a real pipeline, while $R_{inh}$ is the manufacturer's bench-test capability. ### 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Problem (Analytical Matrix) > **Problem Statement (Standard KTU 14-mark type):** > A globe-type control valve with an **equal-percentage trim** is to be installed in a process line. The manufacturer certifies an **inherent rangeability of 50:1**. The process conditions are: > - Maximum operating flow: $Q_{max} = 200$ m³/h > - Minimum operating flow: $Q_{min} = 10$ m³/h > - Pump head (constant): $\Delta P_{total} = 100$ kPa > - System friction characteristic: $\Delta P_{sys} = 0.002 \cdot Q^2$ (Q in m³/h, ΔP in kPa) > > **Tasks:** > (a) Compute the installed rangeability of the selected valve. > (b) Determine whether this valve is suitable for the service. **Step A — Calculate system pressure drop at $Q_{max}$.** $$\Delta P_{sys,max} = 0.002 \cdot (200)^2 = 0.002 \cdot 40000 = 80 \text{ kPa}$$ **Step B — Calculate the pressure drop available across the valve at $Q_{max}$.** $$\Delta P_{v,max} = \Delta P_{total} - \Delta P_{sys,max} = 100 - 80 = 20 \text{ kPa}$$ This is the small ΔP that occurs at high flow because most of the pump head is consumed by pipe friction. **Step C — Calculate system pressure drop at $Q_{min}$.** $$\Delta P_{sys,min} = 0.002 \cdot (10)^2 = 0.002 \cdot 100 = 0.2 \text{ kPa}$$ **Step D — Calculate the pressure drop available across the valve at $Q_{min}$.** $$\Delta P_{v,min} = \Delta P_{total} - \Delta P_{sys,min} = 100 - 0.2 = 99.8 \text{ kPa}$$ This is the large ΔP that occurs at low flow because pipe friction has nearly vanished. **Step E — Apply the installed rangeability formula.** $$R_{inst} = R_{inh} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P_{v,max}}{\Delta P_{v,min}}}$$ $$R_{inst} = 50 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{20}{99.8}} = 50 \cdot \sqrt{0.2004}$$ $$R_{inst} = 50 \cdot 0.4477 = 22.38$$ **Step F — Compare with the required process rangeability.** $$R_{req} = \frac{Q_{max}}{Q_{min}} = \frac{200}{10} = 20$$ Since $R_{inst} = 22.38 > R_{req} = 20$, the valve is **marginally suitable**. To be conservative, a valve with $R_{inh} = 60$ or higher would be the engineering-recommended choice. ### 3.3 Reverse Problem — Required Inherent Rangeability Suppose the same process requires $R_{req} = 25$ and the system produces $\Delta P_{v,max} = 20$ kPa and $\Delta P_{v,min} = 80$ kPa. What is the minimum $R_{inh}$ that the valve must possess? **Step 1 — Apply the suitability condition.** $$R_{inst} \geq R_{req} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_{inh} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{20}{80}} \geq 25$$ **Step 2 — Solve for $R_{inh}$.** $$R_{inh} \cdot 0.5 \geq 25 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_{inh} \geq 50$$ The engineer must select a valve with $R_{inh}$ **at least 50:1**, which is the standard catalog value for **equal-percentage globe valves**. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Rangeability Evaluation ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Process Operating Envelope] --> B[Define Qmax and Qmin] B --> C[Compute Required Rangeability Rreq] C --> D{Check Pump Head Availability} D --> E[Build System Curve Delta Psys vs Q] E --> F[Compute Delta Pv at Qmax] E --> G[Compute Delta Pv at Qmin] F --> H[Compute Installed Rangeability Rinst] G --> H H --> I{Is Rinst greater than or equal to Rreq} I -->|Yes| J[VALVE SUITABLE - Proceed to Sizing] I -->|No| K[VALVE UNSUITABLE - Select Higher Rinh Trim] K --> L[Re-evaluate with New Valve] L --> H J --> M[Document Final Selection] ``` ### 4.2 Pressure Drop Distribution Across a Pumping Loop ```mermaid flowchart LR P[Pump
Constant Head Delta P total] --> V[Control Valve
Delta Pv varies with Q] V --> PIPE[Piping plus Fittings
Delta Psys grows as Q squared] PIPE --> LOAD[Process Load
at Downstream End] LOAD --> P V -.->|High Q| V1[Delta Pv small
Most head in pipe] V -.->|Low Q| V2[Delta Pv large
Most head at valve] ``` ### 4.3 Cv-Rangeability Relationship for Different Valve Trims ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Valve Family] --> G[Globe Valve] START --> B[Ball Valve] START => BF[Butterfly Valve] G --> GE[Equal Percentage Trim
Rinh 50 to 100] G --> GL[Linear Trim
Rinh 30 to 50] B --> BE[Modified Equal Percentage
Rinh 100 to 300] BF --> BFE[High Rangeability Disk
Rinh 50 to 80] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** The flowchart above maps the architectural decision tree an engineer follows when choosing a valve trim. Each branch terminates at a typical inherent rangeability bracket. For most KTU numerical problems, the **equal-percentage globe valve with $R_{inh} = 50$** is the default working assumption. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** *Define the term "Valve Rangeability." Differentiate between inherent rangeability and installed rangeability with one example each.* **Model Answer (10 lines):** Valve rangeability is the ratio of the maximum to minimum controllable flow through a control valve, expressed as $R = Q_{max}/Q_{min}$. **Inherent rangeability** is a property of the valve itself, measured by the manufacturer under constant pressure drop across the valve, typically ranging from 30:1 to 100:1. **Installed rangeability** is the actual operating range in a real piping network where the pressure drop across the valve varies with flow. For example, a globe valve with equal-percentage trim has an inherent rangeability of 50:1, but when installed in a long pipeline with high friction loss, the installed rangeability may drop to 20:1. Hence, the installed value is always less than or equal to the inherent value in most pumping systems. **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** *A control valve has an inherent rangeability of 40:1. The pressure drop across the valve at maximum flow is 25 kPa and at minimum flow is 90 kPa. Calculate the installed rangeability.* **Model Solution:** $$R_{inst} = R_{inh} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P_{v,max}}{\Delta P_{v,min}}} = 40 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{25}{90}}$$ $$R_{inst} = 40 \cdot \sqrt{0.2778} = 40 \cdot 0.5270 = 21.08$$ The installed rangeability is **21.08 : 1** (i.e., approximately 21:1). --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) #### **Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Model]** > *(a)* Define the term "Valve Rangeability Required." Why is it essential to calculate the required rangeability before selecting a control valve for a large-scale process plant? Discuss with a suitable example from a **thermal power plant** boiler feedwater control loop. **[7 Marks]** > > *(b)* A control valve with equal-percentage trim has an inherent rangeability of 50:1. It is to be installed in a process line where the maximum and minimum flow rates are 180 m³/h and 12 m³/h respectively. The pump delivers a constant head of 120 kPa, and the system friction is given by $\Delta P_{sys} = 0.0015 \cdot Q^2$ (Q in m³/h, ΔP in kPa). Calculate the **installed rangeability** and determine whether the selected valve is **suitable** for the service. **[7 Marks]** ##### Model Solution to Part (a) > **Definition:** "Valve Rangeability Required" refers to the turndown ratio that the **process** demands, defined as $R_{req} = Q_{max,process} / Q_{min,process}$. It is **not** a property of the valve itself but a constraint imposed by the process operating window. > **Why it is essential:** > 1. The valve must be able to handle the **entire operating envelope** of the process, including startup, normal load, and shutdown. > 2. Selecting a valve with insufficient installed rangeability leads to **poor control at low loads** and possible **hunting or cycling** near the minimum controllable lift. > 3. Selecting a valve with excessive rangeability causes **oversizing**, which results in the valve operating near its seat for most of the time, leading to **seat erosion, cavitation, and economic loss**. > **Thermal Power Plant Example:** In a 500 MW boiler, the feedwater control valve must regulate flow from 30% load (≈ 450 m³/h) to 100% load (≈ 1500 m³/h), giving $R_{req} = 1500/450 = 3.33$. However, considering **startup and low-load transients** (≈ 10% of MCR, i.e., 150 m³/h), the effective turndown required is $1500/150 = 10$. Therefore, the engineer specifies a valve whose installed rangeability is at least 10:1, typically an equal-percentage globe valve with $R_{inh} \approx 50$. ##### Model Solution to Part (b) **Step 1 — Compute $\Delta P_{sys}$ at $Q_{max} = 180$ m³/h.** $$\Delta P_{sys,max} = 0.0015 \cdot (180)^2 = 0.0015 \cdot 32400 = 48.6 \text{ kPa}$$ **Step 2 — Compute $\Delta P_v$ at $Q_{max}$.** $$\Delta P_{v,max} = \Delta P_{total} - \Delta P_{sys,max} = 120 - 48.6 = 71.4 \text{ kPa}$$ **Step 3 — Compute $\Delta P_{sys}$ at $Q_{min} = 12$ m³/h.** $$\Delta P_{sys,min} = 0.0015 \cdot (12)^2 = 0.0015 \cdot 144 = 0.216 \text{ kPa}$$ **Step 4 — Compute $\Delta P_v$ at $Q_{min}$.** $$\Delta P_{v,min} = 120 - 0.216 = 119.784 \text{ kPa}$$ **Step 5 — Compute the installed rangeability.** $$R_{inst} = 50 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{71.4}{119.784}} = 50 \cdot \sqrt{0.5961} = 50 \cdot 0.7721 = 38.60$$ **Step 6 — Compute the required rangeability and compare.** $$R_{req} = \frac{180}{12} = 15$$ Since $R_{inst} = 38.6 \gg R_{req} = 15$, the valve is **more than suitable** for the service. A smaller valve (e.g., $R_{inh} = 25$) could even be selected to optimize cost and avoid oversizing penalties. #### **Question B — 14 Marks [Alternative Internal Choice]** > *(a)* With the help of a neat block diagram, explain how the **system curve** and the **valve characteristic curve** interact to produce the installed rangeability. **[7 Marks]** > > *(b)* A steam control valve in a refinery has $R_{inh} = 60$. The pump/developing system provides a constant differential pressure of 200 kPa. The system piping consumes $\Delta P_{sys} = 0.0008 \cdot Q^2$ kPa (Q in m³/h). If the maximum and minimum flows required are 250 m³/h and 25 m³/h respectively, determine: > - The pressure drop across the valve at both extremes. > - The installed rangeability. > - Whether a valve with $R_{inh} = 40$ would also work. **[7 Marks]** ##### Model Solution to Part (a) **Block Diagram Explanation:** The system curve is a **parabola** opening upward in the ΔP-Q plane, given by $\Delta P_{sys} = K_{sys} \cdot Q^2$. The pump line is a **horizontal line** at $\Delta P_{total}$. The valve characteristic curve is the locus of $(\Delta P_v, Q)$ pairs as the valve opens. At any operating point, $\Delta P_{total} = \Delta P_v + \Delta P_{sys}$. At maximum flow, the system curve consumes most of the head, leaving small $\Delta P_v$ for the valve. At minimum flow, the system curve consumes very little, leaving a large $\Delta P_v$ across the valve. Because $Q \propto \sqrt{\Delta P_v}$ for a given opening, the **actual flow at minimum lift** is **higher** than it would be in the inherent constant-ΔP test. Hence the operating range in real conditions is **compressed** compared to the manufacturer's rangeability, giving $R_{inst} < R_{inh}$. ##### Model Solution to Part (b) **Step 1 — Compute $\Delta P_{sys}$ at $Q_{max} = 250$ m³/h.** $$\Delta P_{sys,max} = 0.0008 \cdot (250)^2 = 0.0008 \cdot 62500 = 50 \text{ kPa}$$ **Step 2 — Compute $\Delta P_v$ at $Q_{max}$.** $$\Delta P_{v,max} = 200 - 50 = 150 \text{ kPa}$$ **Step 3 — Compute $\Delta P_{sys}$ at $Q_{min} = 25$ m³/h.** $$\Delta P_{sys,min} = 0.0008 \cdot (25)^2 = 0.0008 \cdot 625 = 0.5 \text{ kPa}$$ **Step 4 — Compute $\Delta P_v$ at $Q_{min}$.** $$\Delta P_{v,min} = 200 - 0.5 = 199.5 \text{ kPa}$$ **Step 5 — Compute installed rangeability.** $$R_{inst} = 60 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{150}{199.5}} = 60 \cdot \sqrt{0.7519} = 60 \cdot 0.8671 = 52.03$$ **Step 6 — Compute required rangeability.** $$R_{req} = \frac{250}{25} = 10$$ **Step 7 — Test the alternative valve with $R_{inh} = 40$.** $$R_{inst,alt} = 40 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{150}{199.5}} = 40 \cdot 0.8671 = 34.69$$ Since $R_{inst,alt} = 34.69 > R_{req} = 10$, the cheaper alternative valve with $R_{inh} = 40$ is **also suitable**. This is a classic valve-specification optimization problem: the engineer should choose the lower-$R_{inh}$ valve (cheaper, smaller, faster response) unless future process expansion demands higher turndown. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Inverting the ΔP ratio:** Writing $R_{inst} = R_{inh} \cdot \sqrt{\Delta P_{v,min} / \Delta P_{v,max}}$ instead of the correct form inflates the answer by the square of the ratio error. Always double-check which ΔP corresponds to **maximum flow** and which to **minimum flow**. > 2. **Forgetting the system curve:** Many students treat the pump head as if it were entirely across the valve. This is wrong — the system friction consumes a portion that grows as $Q^2$. > 3. **Confusing $C_v$ rangeability with installed rangeability:** The valve's catalog $C_v,max}/C_{v,min}$ is **inherent**, not installed. The installed value must be calculated. > 4. **Missing units:** $Q$ in m³/h and ΔP in kPa must be consistent with the $K_{sys}$ coefficient units. Mixing units is the single biggest source of numerical errors. > 5. **Ignoring the suitability criterion:** Always end with an explicit check $R_{inst} \geq R_{req}$ and a sentence stating whether the valve is suitable. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Valve Rangeability Required** - **Definition:** Rangeability $R = Q_{max} / Q_{min}$ is the controllable flow span of a control valve. - **Two categories:** **Inherent** (bench test, constant ΔP, valve property) and **Installed** (real plant, variable ΔP, system-dependent). - **Master formula (must memorize):** $R_{inst} = R_{inh} \cdot \sqrt{\Delta P_{v,max} / \Delta P_{v,min}}$ - **Sign convention:** $\Delta P_{v,max}$ is pressure drop at **maximum flow**; $\Delta P_{v,min}$ is pressure drop at **minimum flow**. - **Typical inequality:** In pumping systems, $\Delta P_{v,min} > \Delta P_{v,max}$, so $R_{inst} < R_{inh}$. - **System pressure drop:** $\Delta P_{sys} = K_{sys} \cdot Q^2$ — grows quadratically with flow. - **Head balance:** $\Delta P_{total} = \Delta P_v + \Delta P_{sys}$ — pump head splits between valve and piping. - **Typical $R_{inh}$ values:** Linear trim ≈ 30-50, Equal-percentage ≈ 50-100, Quick-opening ≈ 10-20. - **Suitability check:** $R_{inst} \geq R_{req}$ — if violated, select a higher $R_{inh}$ trim or a different valve type. - **Industrial drivers:** Startup, load changes, product grade transitions, setpoint variations. - **Common exam trap:** Inverting the ΔP ratio, forgetting the system curve, mixing up inherent and installed. - **Practical tip:** For moderate turndown (≤ 10:1), a standard equal-percentage globe valve ($R_{inh} = 50$) is almost always sufficient. For high turndown (> 25:1), consider characterized ball valves or multi-turn globe valves with $R_{inh} = 100$ or more. - **Valve sizing context:** Rangeability is a **pre-screening check** performed **before** the formal $C_v$ calculation, ensuring the chosen valve body can deliver the full turndown without leaving the controllable lift envelope.

topics.title: Precise Neutralisation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT864 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN LARGE SCALE INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Precise Neutralisation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Precise Neutralisation** is the controlled addition of a neutralising reagent (acid or base) to a process stream to achieve and maintain a target **pH** (typically **pH = 7**) with minimal deviation, despite disturbances in inflow composition, flow rate, and temperature.

In large-scale process industries (wastewater treatment plants, **pharmaceutical manufacturing**, **petrochemical refining**, **food & beverage**, **pulp & paper**), the neutralisation reaction is governed by:

$$\text{H}^+ + \text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

However, in industrial practice, the process rarely involves only strong acids and strong bases. It frequently involves **weak acids**, **weak bases**, and **buffering agents**, which dramatically alter the dynamic response and make control challenging.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):**
> The course PEICT864 treats "Precise Neutralisation" as a flagship case study in **non-linear process control**, because the pH process exhibits a process gain that varies by **4 to 6 orders of magnitude** across its operating range.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Driving Near a Cliff"

Imagine you are driving a car on a long, flat road that suddenly narrows to a hairpin bend hugging a deep cliff:

| Operating Zone | Driving Analogy | pH Control Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Far from pH 7 (acidic/basic) | Wide straight road — small steering corrections are safe | High process gain tolerated; coarse control is fine |
| Near pH 7 (equivalence point) | Hairpin bend on a cliff — a tiny wheel turn causes disaster | **Process gain explodes**; any reagent over-addition swings pH wildly |
| At pH 7 (setpoint) | The cliff edge — must drive with millimetric precision | Exact stoichiometric addition required |

> [!TIP]
> The **equivalence point** of a titration is the pH where the moles of added reagent chemically equal the moles of the species being neutralised. For a strong acid–strong base system, this occurs exactly at **pH = 7**, but for weak acid/base systems it shifts (e.g., **pH ≈ 8.7** for acetic acid + NaOH).

## 1.3 Why "Precise" Neutralisation is Hard

The standard **PID controller**, when applied naively to a pH loop, oscillates severely. The reason is rooted in the **S-shaped titration curve** of pH vs. reagent volume, which has the following properties:

1. **Extremely high process gain** at the equivalence point (steep slope).
2. **Very low process gain** in the flat buffer regions (gentle slope).
3. **Strong non-linearity** — gain changes by **10,000×** between regions.
4. **Asymmetric behaviour** for weak vs. strong acids/bases.
5. **Time-varying disturbances** from upstream process variations.

> [!WARNING]
> A conventional **PI/PID controller tuned at one pH setpoint will fail** when the operating point shifts. This is the central engineering problem of "Precise Neutralisation."
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The pH Titration Curve — A Non-Linear Phenomenon

The pH of an aqueous solution is defined as the negative logarithm (base **10**) of the hydrogen ion activity:

$$\text{pH} = -\log_{10}(a_{\text{H}^+}) \approx -\log_{10}([\text{H}^+])$$

This logarithmic definition alone introduces the **primary non-linearity**. A small absolute change in $[\text{H}^+]$ near neutrality produces a large pH swing.

### Step-by-Step Logic of the Titration Response

1. **Add reagent →** $[\text{H}^+]$ (or $[\text{OH}^-]$) changes.
2. **Acid–base equilibrium →** Buffer systems (e.g., $\text{HA} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{A}^-$) resist pH change.
3. **Near equivalence point →** Buffer capacity collapses; pH changes rapidly.
4. **Beyond equivalence point →** pH again plateaus (now governed by excess reagent).

## 2.2 Buffer Capacity — The Hidden Variable

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Buffer capacity ($\beta$)** is the amount (in moles) of strong acid or base required to change the pH of **1 litre** of solution by **1 pH unit**.

$$\beta = \frac{dC_b}{d(\text{pH})} = -\frac{dC_a}{d(\text{pH})}$$

where $C_b$ and $C_a$ are molar concentrations of added base and acid respectively.

A **high** $\beta$ means the solution resists pH change (flat region). A **low** $\beta$ near the equivalence point means pH swings violently.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Physical Meaning | Typical Units / Values |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{pH} = -\log_{10}[\text{H}^+]$ | Definition of pH | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $[\text{H}^+][\text{OH}^-] = K_w = 10^{-14}$ (at **25 °C**) | Water ionisation constant | $\text{mol}^2/\text{L}^2$ |
| 3 | $\text{pH} + \text{pOH} = 14$ | Conjugate pH–pOH relation | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log_{10}\dfrac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$ | **Henderson–Hasselbalch equation** | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $K_p = \dfrac{d(\text{pH})}{d(\text{Reagent Flow})}$ | **Process gain** of pH loop | pH per (L/s) |
| 6 | $\beta = \dfrac{dC_b}{d(\text{pH})}$ | Buffer capacity | mol/(L·pH) |
| 7 | $K_p \propto \dfrac{1}{\beta}$ | Gain inversely proportional to buffer capacity | — |
| 8 | $\text{Neutralisation} \% = \dfrac{n_{\text{acid}} - n_{\text{base}}}{n_{\text{acid}}} \times 100$ | Extent of reaction | % |
| 9 | $F_{\text{reagent}} = F_{\text{process}} \times \dfrac{C_{\text{in}}}{\rho_{\text{reagent}} \times N_{\text{reagent}}}$ | Stoichiometric reagent flow (for feedforward) | L/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** Always remember the **Henderson–Hasselbalch equation** (Formula 4). KTU board questions on weak-acid/weak-base systems almost always require its application.

## 2.4 Why Real-World pH Control is Non-Trivial

The pH process is considered the **"benchmark non-linear control problem"** in process control textbooks (Luyben, Shinskey) for these reasons:

- **Process gain varies by 4–6 decades** across the titration curve.
- **Dead time** from mixing and sensor response is significant in large stirred tanks.
- **Reagent delivery dynamics** (valve hysteresis, pump non-linearity) add further complications.
- **Multi-component streams** (e.g., wastewater with mixed acids, ammonia, phenols) make the curve irregular and non-symmetric.
- **Temperature dependence** of $K_w$ shifts the equivalence point.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Precise pH control is essential in **cooling water treatment** to prevent corrosion/scaling, in **fermentation broths** to maintain microbial activity, and in **FGD scrubbers** of thermal power plants to meet $\text{SO}_2$ discharge norms.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Logarithmic Origin of Non-Linearity

We begin with the definition of pH and show mathematically why neutralisation is a "high-gain" process near pH 7.

**Step 1 — Start with the pH definition:**

$$\text{pH} = -\log_{10}[\text{H}^+]$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to $[\text{H}^+]$ to obtain the sensitivity:**

$$\frac{d(\text{pH})}{d[\text{H}^+]} = -\frac{1}{[\text{H}^+]\ln(10)}$$

This is the **slope of the pH vs. concentration curve** at any operating point.

**Step 3 — Evaluate at pH 7 (neutral, $[\text{H}^+] = 10^{-7}$ M):**

$$\frac{d(\text{pH})}{d[\text{H}^+]} \bigg\vert_{\text{pH}=7} = -\frac{1}{10^{-7} \times 2.3026} = -4.34 \times 10^{6} \;\; \text{pH per (mol/L)}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate at pH 2 (strongly acidic, $[\text{H}^+] = 10^{-2}$ M):**

$$\frac{d(\text{pH})}{d[\text{H}^+]} \bigg\vert_{\text{pH}=2} = -\frac{1}{10^{-2} \times 2.3026} = -43.4 \;\; \text{pH per (mol/L)}$$

**Step 5 — Compare the sensitivities:**

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{4.34 \times 10^{6}}{43.4} \approx 10^{5}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The process gain near neutrality is approximately **100,000 times larger** than at pH 2. This single numerical result explains every difficulty encountered in pH control.

## 3.2 Derivation: Henderson–Hasselbalch Equation

**Step 1 — Start with the weak acid dissociation constant:**

$$K_a = \frac{[\text{H}^+][\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$$

**Step 2 — Take the negative logarithm of both sides:**

$$-\log K_a = -\log[\text{H}^+] - \log\frac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the definitions $\text{p}K_a = -\log K_a$ and $\text{pH} = -\log[\text{H}^+]$:**

$$\text{p}K_a = \text{pH} - \log\frac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to obtain the Henderson–Hasselbalch form:**

$$\boxed{\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log_{10}\frac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}}$$

> [!TIP]
> At **half-equivalence**, $[\text{A}^-] = [\text{HA}]$, so $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a$. This is the **buffering peak**, where the titration curve is flattest and the process gain is **lowest**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Control Strategies for Precise Neutralisation

| Control Strategy | Working Principle | Advantages | Limitations | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Fixed-Gain PID** | Linear control law, $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \frac{de}{dt}$ | Simple, well-known | Fails badly; gain mismatch causes oscillation | Mentioned for contrast |
| **Gain-Scheduled PID** | $K_p, K_i, K_d$ varied as functions of pH measurement | Handles wide operating range | Requires accurate titration model | **High-yield topic** |
| **Feedforward + Feedback** | FF computes stoichiometric dose; FB trims residual error | Rejects inflow disturbances fast | Requires inflow pH & flow sensors | **Frequently asked** |
| **Cascade Control** | Master pH controller sets slave reagent flow controller | Smooths valve dynamics | Two-loop tuning complexity | Common in exams |
| **Split-Range Control** | Single pH controller outputs to two valves (acid/base) | Handles bi-directional correction | Valve non-linearity | **High-yield topic** |
| **Model-Predictive / Adaptive** | Online estimation of titration curve | Best performance | Computationally heavy | Advanced / elective |
| **Non-Linear Characteriser + PID** | Inverse titration function applied as pre-compensator | Linearises the loop | Requires accurate model | **Examiner favourite** |

## 3.4 Worked Analytical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A wastewater stream of flow $F = 10$ L/s contains **0.001 M** HCl. It is neutralised with **0.01 M** NaOH. Calculate the required NaOH flow rate at the equivalence point.

**Step 1 — Write the stoichiometric condition at equivalence:**

$$F_{\text{HCl}} \times C_{\text{HCl}} = F_{\text{NaOH}} \times C_{\text{NaOH}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the process flow as the HCl stream (before dilution):**

$$10 \times 0.001 = F_{\text{NaOH}} \times 0.01$$

**Step 3 — Solve:**

$$F_{\text{NaOH}} = \frac{10 \times 0.001}{0.01} = 1.0 \;\; \text{L/s}$$

**Step 4 — Comment on control implication:**

At this flow, the system sits on the **steep cliff of the titration curve**. A **5 % error** in NaOH flow (i.e., 1.05 L/s) would shift pH from 7 to approximately **11.5** — a swing of **4.5 pH units** — illustrating the **precision requirement**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Precise Neutralisation Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeSP[Setpoint pH 7.0] --> nodeSUM[Summing Junction]
    nodePH[pH Sensor in CSTR] --> nodeSUM
    nodeSUM --> nodeCTRL[Non-Linear Controller Gain Scheduled PID]
    nodeCTRL --> nodeCHAR[Inverse Titration Characteriser]
    nodeCHAR --> nodeVALVE[Split Range Valve Assembly]
    nodeVALVE --> nodeACID[Acid Reagent Line]
    nodeVALVE --> nodeBASE[Base Reagent Line]
    nodeACID --> nodeMIX[Stirred Mixing Reactor]
    nodeBASE --> nodeMIX
    nodeIN[Inflow Disturbance pH and Flow] --> nodeMIX
    nodeMIX --> nodePH
    nodeFF[Feedforward Path Inflow pH and Flow] --> nodeSUM
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Decision Flow for Selecting a pH Control Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeSTART[Process Requires pH Control] --> nodeQ1{Are disturbances large and fast?}
    nodeQ1 -- Yes --> nodeFF[Use Feedforward Feedback]
    nodeQ1 -- No --> nodeQ2{Is operating range wide pH 2 to 12?}
    nodeQ2 -- Yes --> nodeGS[Use Gain Scheduled PID]
    nodeQ2 -- No --> nodeQ3{Both acid and base reagents used?}
    nodeQ3 -- Yes --> nodeSR[Use Split Range Control]
    nodeQ3 -- No --> nodeSTD[Standard PI Controller]
    nodeFF --> nodeTUNE[Tune Using Buffer Capacity Beta]
    nodeGS --> nodeTUNE
    nodeSR --> nodeTUNE
    nodeSTD --> nodeTUNE
    nodeTUNE --> nodeDONE[Loop Operational at Target pH]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Conceptual Map of the Titration Curve Gain Variation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Region A Strong Acid pH 0 to 2 Low Gain] --> nodeB[Region B Buffer Plateau pH 3 to 5 Moderate Gain]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Region C Equivalence Zone pH 6 to 8 EXTREMELY HIGH GAIN]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Region D Weak Base Plateau pH 9 to 11 Moderate Gain]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Region E Strong Base pH 12 to 14 Low Gain]
    nodeC -.-> nodeCTRL[Demands Non-Linear Control Strategy]
    nodeA -.-> nodeCTRL
    nodeE -.-> nodeCTRL
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Cascade pH Control with Feedforward

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeINFLOW[Inflow Stream Variable pH and Flow] --> nodeFF[Feedforward Computation Stoichiometric Dose]
    nodeFF --> nodeFFSUM[FF Summing Node]
    nodePHM[pH Measurement in Tank] --> nodeMASTER[Master Controller pH Loop Slow]
    nodeMASTER --> nodeFFSUM
    nodeFFSUM --> nodeSLAVE[Slave Controller Flow Loop Fast]
    nodeSLAVE --> nodePUMP[Reagent Dosing Pump]
    nodePUMP --> nodeTANK[Neutralisation Tank CSTR]
    nodeINFLOW --> nodeTANK
    nodeTANK --> nodePHM
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define buffer capacity and explain its significance in pH control. **[3 Marks]**

> `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Model Answer:**

Buffer capacity $\beta$ is defined as the amount of strong acid or base (in moles) required to change the pH of one litre of solution by one pH unit:

$$\beta = \frac{dC_b}{d(\text{pH})}$$

A high $\beta$ value corresponds to a **flat region** of the titration curve, where the solution resists pH change. A low $\beta$ (near the equivalence point) corresponds to the **steep cliff** region, where pH swings violently with small reagent additions. The process gain $K_p$ of the pH loop is **inversely proportional** to $\beta$, which is why $\beta$ is the central concept in precise neutralisation control.

---

### Question 2: Why is a fixed-gain PID controller inadequate for precise pH control across a wide range? **[3 Marks]**

> `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Supplementary]`

**Model Answer:**

The pH process exhibits a process gain that varies by **4 to 6 orders of magnitude** between the buffer regions and the equivalence point. A PID controller tuned with a high gain (suitable for the steep equivalence zone) will **over-shoot and oscillate** in the flat buffer regions, while a PID tuned with a low gain (suitable for the flat zones) will respond **too slowly** near the equivalence point. Hence, no single set of $K_p, K_i, K_d$ values can serve the entire operating range, making gain scheduling, non-linear compensation, or adaptive control essential.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> `[KTU End-Semester Exam Pattern — Module 1 Choice-Based]`

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **split-range control** scheme used for precise pH neutralisation. Discuss how the controller output is mapped to the acid and base valves. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Derive the **Henderson–Hasselbalch equation** and use it to determine the pH of a buffer containing **0.2 M** acetic acid ($\text{p}K_a = 4.76$) and **0.5 M** sodium acetate. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Split-Range Control for pH Neutralisation

**Step 1 — Define the requirement:**
When the process stream can drift either acidic or basic, a single reagent valve is insufficient. Two valves — one for acid, one for base — must be actuated by a single controller output.

**Step 2 — Signal mapping convention:**

| Controller Output (4–20 mA) | Acid Valve Position | Base Valve Position |
|---|---|---|
| **4 mA** (0 %) | **Fully Open** | **Fully Closed** |
| **12 mA** (50 %) | **Fully Closed** | **Fully Closed** |
| **20 mA** (100 %) | **Fully Closed** | **Fully Open** |

**Step 3 — Operation logic:**
- If measured pH < setpoint (too acidic), controller output rises above 12 mA → base valve opens to add base.
- If measured pH > setpoint (too basic), controller output drops below 12 mA → acid valve opens to add acid.
- A **dead band** of 4 mA is often introduced around 12 mA to prevent simultaneous opening of both valves (which would waste reagent and generate heat).

**Step 4 — Advantages:**
- Single controller manages **bi-directional** correction.
- Saves cost (one controller instead of two).
- Smoothes transitions near setpoint.

#### Part (b) — Henderson–Hasselbalch Derivation and Calculation

**Step 1 — Acid dissociation:**

$$K_a = \frac{[\text{H}^+][\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$$

**Step 2 — Take negative log:**

$$-\log K_a = -\log[\text{H}^+] - \log\frac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$$

**Step 3 — Apply definitions:**

$$\text{p}K_a = \text{pH} - \log\frac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange:**

$$\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log\frac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$\text{pH} = 4.76 + \log\frac{0.5}{0.2}$$

**Step 6 — Compute log:**

$$\log(2.5) = 0.3979$$

**Step 7 — Final result:**

$$\text{pH} = 4.76 + 0.40 = \mathbf{5.16}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the concept of **gain-scheduled PID control** for precise pH neutralisation. State how the controller gains are varied as a function of the measured pH. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A process stream of flow **5 L/s** contains **0.005 M** $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$. It is to be neutralised with **0.05 M** NaOH. Compute:
   (i) The stoichiometric NaOH flow rate at the equivalence point. **[3 Marks]**
   (ii) The pH of the resulting solution if the NaOH flow is over-dosed by **10 %**. **[4 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Gain-Scheduled PID Control

**Step 1 — Concept:**
In gain-scheduled control, the PID parameters ($K_p, K_i, K_d$) are **not constant** but are functions of an auxiliary variable — in pH control, the **measured pH itself** (or equivalently, the buffer capacity $\beta$).

**Step 2 — Scheduling logic:**

| Measured pH Range | Buffer Capacity $\beta$ | Required $K_p$ | Required $K_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **pH 0 to 3** (strong acid) | Very low | High | High |
| **pH 3 to 6** (buffer region) | High | Low | Low |
| **pH 6 to 8** (equivalence) | Very low | Very High | Moderate |
| **pH 8 to 11** (buffer) | High | Low | Low |
| **pH 11 to 14** (strong base) | Very low | High | High |

**Step 3 — Implementation:**
- A **look-up table** maps pH regions to gain triplets.
- Alternatively, a **continuous function** $K_p(\text{pH})$ is implemented in the DCS or PLC.
- Smooth transition (e.g., **linear interpolation**) between regions prevents abrupt gain changes.

**Step 4 — Benefit:** A single controller provides tight control across the entire titration curve.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Part (b)(i) — Stoichiometric flow:**

**Step 1 — Sulphuric acid is diprotic; each mole gives 2 moles of $\text{H}^+$:**

$$[\text{H}^+]_{\text{available}} = 2 \times 0.005 = 0.01 \;\; \text{M}$$

**Step 2 — Equivalence condition (NaOH provides 1 mole $\text{OH}^-$ per mole):**

$$F_{\text{NaOH}} \times C_{\text{NaOH}} = F_{\text{process}} \times [\text{H}^+]_{\text{available}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$F_{\text{NaOH}} \times 0.05 = 5 \times 0.01$$

**Step 4 — Solve:**

$$F_{\text{NaOH}} = \frac{5 \times 0.01}{0.05} = \mathbf{1.0 \;\; L/s}$$

**Part (b)(ii) — Over-dose case:**

**Step 1 — Actual NaOH flow = 1.1 L/s (10 % over-dose):**

**Step 2 — Excess $\text{OH}^-$ moles per second:**

$$\dot{n}_{\text{excess}} = (1.1 - 1.0) \times 0.05 = 0.005 \;\; \text{mol/s}$$

**Step 3 — Excess $\text{OH}^-$ moles per litre of mixed solution:**
