Approximating total flow $\approx 5 + 1.1 = 6.1$ L/s:

$$[\text{OH}^-]_{\text{excess}} = \frac{0.005 \times 1}{6.1} \times \frac{1}{1} = 8.197 \times 10^{-4} \;\; \text{M}$$

> *(Simplified per-litre basis)*

**Step 4 — Compute pOH:**

$$\text{pOH} = -\log(8.197 \times 10^{-4}) = 3.086$$

**Step 5 — Compute pH:**

$$\text{pH} = 14 - 3.086 = \mathbf{10.91}$$

**Step 6 — Observation:** A mere **10 % over-dose** caused the pH to jump from **7** to **10.91** — a **3.9 pH unit deviation**, vividly demonstrating the non-linearity.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully!**
>
> 1. **Unit mismatch in stoichiometry:** Forgetting that $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ is **diprotic** and contributes 2 $\text{H}^+$ per molecule is the single most common mistake — a 2-mark penalty every time.
> 2. **Sign error in Henderson–Hasselbalch:** Students frequently write $\log([\text{HA}]/[\text{A}^-])$ instead of $\log([\text{A}^-]/[\text{HA}])$, producing the **complementary** pH value.
> 3. **Forgetting temperature:** $K_w = 10^{-14}$ strictly holds at **25 °C**. At 50 °C, $K_w \approx 10^{-13.26}$. Examiners may award an extra mark for stating the temperature assumption.
> 4. **Skipping the block diagram in split-range questions:** Drawing the **signal mapping table** alone is insufficient — a **block diagram** is mandatory for full marks.
> 5. **Oversimplifying the gain-scheduling table:** Examiners expect at least **3 distinct regions** with quantitative reasoning, not a vague "controller adjusts itself."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorise the following high-yield items before entering the exam hall.**

- **pH definition:** $\text{pH} = -\log[\text{H}^+]$; **pH + pOH = 14** at 25 °C.
- **Titration curve shape:** Flat (buffer) → **steep cliff (equivalence)** → flat (excess) — an **S-curve**.
- **Buffer capacity $\beta$:** $dC_b/d(\text{pH})$; **inversely proportional** to process gain $K_p$.
- **Henderson–Hasselbalch equation:** $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log([\text{A}^-]/[\text{HA}])$.
- **Process gain variation:** $\sim \mathbf{10^{4} \text{ to } 10^{5} \times}$ across operating range — the root cause of all pH control problems.
- **Half-equivalence point:** $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a$; this is where the titration curve is **flattest**.
- **Equivalence point:** pH = 7 for strong acid + strong base; **shifts** for weak systems.
- **Split-range control:** Maps a single controller output (4–20 mA) to **two valves** (acid and base) with **12 mA** as the neutral midpoint.
- **Gain-scheduled PID:** $K_p, K_i, K_d$ varied as functions of measured pH using a **look-up table** or **continuous function**.
- **Feedforward control:** Computes stoichiometric reagent dose from **inflow pH, inflow concentration, and flow rate** — rejects disturbances **before** they affect the tank pH.
- **Cascade control:** Master pH controller sets the setpoint of a **slave flow controller** on the reagent line — improves dynamic response and rejects valve hysteresis.
- **Key industrial applications:** Wastewater treatment, FGD scrubbers, fermentation, cooling water, pharmaceutical API manufacture, food pH adjustment.
- **Diprotic acid trap:** Always multiply concentration by the **number of ionisable $\text{H}^+$** per molecule (e.g., ×2 for $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$, ×3 for $\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$).
- **Temperature correction:** $K_w$ changes with $T$; state the assumed temperature in derivations for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single Reagent Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT864 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN LARGE SCALE INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Single Reagent Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Single Reagent Control** is a process control strategy in which **one** manipulating variable (typically a chemical reagent flow rate) is used to regulate **one** controlled process variable, where the relationship between the two is governed by an **S-shaped (sigmoidal) titration-type characteristic curve**. The reagent either neutralizes, reacts with, or otherwise alters the chemical composition of the process stream to bring the controlled variable to its setpoint.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Highlight**
> Single reagent control is most commonly applied to **pH control** (using either a strong acid *or* a strong base, but not both), **chlorination control**, **dechlorination using SO₂**, and **coagulant dosing** in water-treatment plants. In each case, the process exhibits **extreme nonlinearity** — the process gain changes by several orders of magnitude across the operating range.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE] **Plain-English Analogy**
> Imagine filling a wide-brimmed drinking glass to *exactly* the top using a single water tap. When the glass is nearly empty, even opening the tap fully for a long time barely raises the water level — the glass is *insensitive* (low gain). But when the glass is one sip away from overflowing, a *tiny* additional drop causes a huge change — it is *super-sensitive* (high gain). Single reagent control behaves identically: near the stoichiometric (equivalence) point, a fraction of a percent change in reagent flow causes a *pH swing of many units*, while far from that point, even large reagent changes produce only minor pH changes.

## 1.3 Why "Single" Reagent?

In many industrial situations only **one** reagent is available, permissible, or economical:
- Wastewater treatment plant adds **only H₂SO₄** (no base) to acidic effluent.
- A drinking-water plant feeds **only Cl₂** (no dechlorination reagent) for disinfection.
- An absorption tower feeds **only NaOH** to scrub an acidic gas.

The "single" qualifier also distinguishes this scheme from **two-reagent (dual) control**, in which both acid *and* base may be added around the setpoint to reduce the effective gain.

## 1.4 Industrial Examples

| Application | Reagent | Controlled Variable |
|---|---|---|
| Acidic effluent neutralization | NaOH | pH |
| Alkaline waste neutralization | H₂SO₄ / HCl | pH |
| Water disinfection | Cl₂ | Free residual chlorine |
| Dechlorination | SO₂ | Total residual chlorine |
| Boiler-feed oxygen scavenging | Hydrazine / DEHA | Dissolved O₂ |
| Flocculation | Alum (Al₂(SO₄)₃) | Turbidity / charge neutralization |

## 1.5 Key Physical / Numerical Metrics

- **pH** is defined as $\mathrm{pH} = -\log_{10}[H^{+}]$, dimensionless, range 0–14.
- **Water ion product**: $K_{w} = [H^{+}][OH^{-}] = 1.0 \times 10^{-14}$ at **25 °C**.
- **Process gain** for pH near neutrality can exceed $\mathbf{10^{6}}$ (%pH change per %reagent change) — one of the most demanding control problems in industry.
- **Stoichiometric flow** is the reagent flow that exactly neutralizes the process stream: $F_{A,\text{stoich}} = F_{S}\,C_{B}/C_{A}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Single Reagent Control Loop

The generic feedback loop has the following elements:

1. **Setpoint** (desired pH or chlorine residual)
2. **Comparator / Error Detector**
3. **Controller** (usually PID, often with nonlinear compensation)
4. **Final Control Element** (modulating valve / metering pump)
5. **Reagent Addition Point** (mixing tee or reaction tank)
6. **Process** (continuously-stirred tank or in-line mixer)
7. **Sensor** (pH electrode, amperometric chlorine probe, etc.)
8. **Transmitter** (signal conditioning, 4–20 mA output)

> [!NOTE] **The "Why" Behind Each Block**
> - The **pH electrode** itself behaves as a high-impedance voltage source with a Nernst slope of **59.16 mV/pH unit at 25 °C** — its dynamics are typically modeled as a **first-order lag** with time constant $\tau_{e}$ in the range 5–30 s.
> - The **mixing tank** introduces additional dead time $\theta$ (residence time) and a first-order lag.
> - The **control valve** often has equal-percentage characteristic to compensate for the high process gain near setpoint.

## 2.2 The S-Shaped Titration Curve (Static Characteristic)

The static relationship between reagent flow $F_{A}$ and controlled variable (pH) is a classic **sigmoid**. Key features:

- **Plateau regions** at very low pH (excess acid) and very high pH (excess base): the curve is nearly flat — **low static gain**.
- **Equivalence (neutralization) point** near pH = 7: the curve is nearly vertical — **very high static gain**.
- The transition between plateaus can occur over a reagent flow change of **less than 1 %** — the central challenge of single reagent control.

> [!TIP] **Memory Aid**
> "S-curve = Steep at the middle, Shallow at the sides." The controller *sees* a process whose sensitivity is constantly changing.

## 2.3 Mathematical Model of pH Process (Strong Acid / Strong Base)

Let:
- $F_{S}$ = process stream volumetric flow rate (L/min)
- $F_{A}$ = reagent (strong acid) volumetric flow rate (L/min)
- $C_{B}$ = concentration of strong base in the process stream (mol/L)
- $C_{A}$ = concentration of strong acid reagent (mol/L)

**Charge balance on the well-mixed effluent:**

$$
[H^{+}] + [Na^{+}] = [OH^{-}] + [Cl^{-}]
$$

Because NaOH and HCl are fully dissociated, $[Na^{+}] = (F_{S}\,C_{B})/(F_{S}+F_{A})$ and $[Cl^{-}] = (F_{A}\,C_{A})/(F_{S}+F_{A})$.

Define the **excess acid** (in mol per unit total flow):

$$
\Delta \;=\; \frac{F_{A}\,C_{A} - F_{S}\,C_{B}}{F_{S}+F_{A}}
$$

- If $\Delta > 0$ → solution is **acidic**: $[H^{+}] = \Delta$ and
  $$
  \mathrm{pH} = -\log_{10}\Delta
  $$
- If $\Delta < 0$ → solution is **basic**: $[OH^{-}] = -\Delta$ and
  $$
  \mathrm{pH} \;=\; 14 + \log_{10}(-\Delta)
  $$
- If $\Delta = 0$ → exact neutralization (theoretically pH = 7; in practice, pH ≈ 7 ± 0.3 depending on ionic strength and temperature).

When $F_{A} \ll F_{S}$ (typical industrial case, $F_{A}/F_{S}<0.01$), the denominator simplifies to $F_{S}$, giving:

$$
\Delta \;\approx\; \frac{F_{A}\,C_{A} - F_{S}\,C_{B}}{F_{S}}
$$

## 2.4 Process Gain (Static Sensitivity) — The Most Important Derivation

Differentiating the pH expression with respect to $F_{A}$ in the **excess-acid region**:

$$
K_{p} \;=\; \frac{d\,\mathrm{pH}}{dF_{A}} \;=\; \frac{d}{dF_{A}}\left[-\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{F_{A}C_{A}-F_{S}C_{B}}{F_{S}}\right)\right]
$$

Applying the chain rule ($d(-\log_{10}x)/dx = -1/(x\,\ln 10)$):

$$
\boxed{\;K_{p} \;=\; -\frac{C_{A}}{2.303\,F_{S}\,[H^{+}]}\;}
$$

This is the **single most important formula in single-reagent pH control**. It tells us:

- At **pH = 3** ($[H^{+}] = 10^{-3}$): $|K_{p}|$ is small → easy to control.
- At **pH = 7** ($[H^{+}] = 10^{-7}$): $|K_{p}|$ is **10 000 times larger** → the controller must be retuned, or instability results.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Engineering Insight**
> The process gain changes by **four orders of magnitude** over just four pH units. A linear controller tuned for the flat region will be sluggish; the *same* controller tuned for the steep region will oscillate violently. This is the central design problem.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Region of Validity |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathrm{pH} = -\log_{10}[H^{+}]$ | Definition of pH | $0 \le \mathrm{pH} \le 14$ |
| 2 | $K_{w} = [H^{+}][OH^{-}] = 1.0\times10^{-14}$ | Water ion product at **25 °C** | Dilute aqueous, **25 °C** |
| 3 | $F_{A,\text{stoich}} = F_{S}\,C_{B}/C_{A}$ | Stoichiometric reagent flow | 1 : 1 neutralization |
| 4 | $\Delta = (F_{A}C_{A} - F_{S}C_{B})/(F_{S}+F_{A})$ | Excess acid per unit total flow | Steady state |
| 5 | $\mathrm{pH} = -\log_{10}\Delta$ | pH in excess-acid region | $\Delta > 0$ |
| 6 | $\mathrm{pH} = 14 + \log_{10}(-\Delta)$ | pH in excess-base region | $\Delta < 0$ |
| 7 | $K_{p} = -C_{A}/(2.303\,F_{S}\,[H^{+}])$ | Static process gain | Excess-acid region |
| 8 | $E = 59.16\,( \mathrm{pH}_{s} - \mathrm{pH}_{r})\ \mathrm{mV}$ | Nernst electrode output | **25 °C** |
| 9 | $G_{p}(s) = K_{p}\,e^{-\theta s}/(\tau s + 1)$ | Typical FOPDT process model | Approximate |
| 10 | $\mathrm{pH} = 7 + \tfrac{1}{2}\log_{10}(C_{B}/C_{A})$ | pH at slight excess (log approx.) | Far from equivalence |

> [!NOTE]
> In row 7 use $\lvert K_{p}\rvert$ for magnitude. In row 9 the dead time $\theta$ comes from the mixing tank residence time $\theta = V/F_{S}$ and $\tau$ from mixing dynamics.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

- **Effluent Treatment Plants (ETP)**: pH must be brought to 6.5–8.5 before discharge under Pollution Control Board norms.
- **Pharmaceutical / Fermentation**: pH tightly controlled (often ±0.05) to maintain biological activity.
- **FGD Scrubbers**: Limestone-slurry pH influences SO₂ removal efficiency.
- **Semiconductor Wet Benches**: pH control with single reagent is common; gain-scheduled controllers are standard.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stoichiometric Reagent Flow

**Statement:** A process stream of flow $F_{S}$ containing strong base at concentration $C_{B}$ must be neutralized with a strong-acid reagent of concentration $C_{A}$. Derive the stoichiometric flow rate $F_{A,\text{stoich}}$.

**Step 1 — Write the neutralization reaction (1 : 1 stoichiometry):**

$$
H^{+} \;+\; OH^{-} \;\longrightarrow\; H_{2}O
$$

**Step 2 — Equate molar flow rates of $H^{+}$ and $OH^{-}$ at the equivalence point:**

$$
\underbrace{F_{A,\text{stoich}}\;C_{A}}_{\text{molar flow of }H^{+}} \;=\; \underbrace{F_{S}\;C_{B}}_{\text{molar flow of }OH^{-}}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the unknown reagent flow:**

$$
\boxed{\;F_{A,\text{stoich}} \;=\; \frac{F_{S}\,C_{B}}{C_{A}}\;}
$$

**Step 4 — Units check:** (L/min)(mol/L) = (L/min)(mol/L) ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — pH as a Function of Reagent Flow (Far from Equivalence)

**Statement:** For $F_{A} \ll F_{S}$ derive the closed-form pH–flow relationship in both the excess-acid and excess-base regions.

**Step 1 — Define total flow and excess acid $\Delta$:**

$$
F_{T} = F_{S}+F_{A}, \qquad \Delta = \frac{F_{A}C_{A}-F_{S}C_{B}}{F_{S}+F_{A}}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the approximation $F_{A}\ll F_{S} \Rightarrow F_{T}\approx F_{S}$:**

$$
\Delta \;\approx\; \frac{F_{A}C_{A}-F_{S}C_{B}}{F_{S}}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the region:**

If $F_{A}C_{A} > F_{S}C_{B}$ then $\Delta>0$ and $[H^{+}]=\Delta$, so:

$$
\mathrm{pH} \;=\; -\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{F_{A}C_{A}-F_{S}C_{B}}{F_{S}}\right)
$$

If $F_{A}C_{A} < F_{S}C_{B}$ then $\Delta<0$ and $[OH^{-}]=-\Delta$, and using $K_{w}$:

$$
\mathrm{pH} \;=\; 14 + \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{F_{S}C_{B}-F_{A}C_{A}}{F_{S}}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Symmetry check:** if we define $\delta \equiv F_{A}C_{A}-F_{S}C_{B}$, then for equal $|\delta|$ on either side the two expressions must differ by exactly $14-2\,\mathrm{pH}_{|\delta|}$ — physically, equal excess on either side gives symmetric pH offsets from neutrality.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Static Process Gain $K_{p}$ (Detailed)

**Statement:** Starting from $\mathrm{pH}=-\log_{10}([H^{+}])$, derive the static gain with respect to $F_{A}$ in the excess-acid region.

**Step 1 — Write pH explicitly:**

$$
\mathrm{pH} \;=\; -\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{F_{A}C_{A}-F_{S}C_{B}}{F_{S}}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Use the calculus identity $d(\log_{10}x)/dx = 1/(x\,\ln 10)$:**

$$
\frac{d\,\mathrm{pH}}{dF_{A}} \;=\; -\frac{1}{\ln 10}\cdot\frac{1}{\left(\frac{F_{A}C_{A}-F_{S}C_{B}}{F_{S}}\right)}\cdot\frac{d}{dF_{A}}\!\left(\frac{F_{A}C_{A}-F_{S}C_{B}}{F_{S}}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate the inner term. Note $F_{S},C_{A},C_{B}$ are constants:**

$$
\frac{d}{dF_{A}}\!\left(\frac{F_{A}C_{A}-F_{S}C_{B}}{F_{S}}\right) \;=\; \frac{C_{A}}{F_{S}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back. Recognize $[H^{+}]=(F_{A}C_{A}-F_{S}C_{B})/F_{S}$:**

$$
K_{p} \;=\; \frac{d\,\mathrm{pH}}{dF_{A}} \;=\; -\frac{1}{\ln 10}\cdot\frac{1}{[H^{+}]}\cdot\frac{C_{A}}{F_{S}} \;=\; -\frac{C_{A}}{2.303\,F_{S}\,[H^{+}]}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical form using $\mathrm{pH}$:**

Since $[H^{+}] = 10^{-\mathrm{pH}}$:

$$
\boxed{\;K_{p} \;=\; -\,\frac{C_{A}}{2.303\,F_{S}}\cdot 10^{\,\mathrm{pH}}\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Reading the Formula**
> The magnitude of $K_{p}$ grows **exponentially** with pH. Doubling the pH increases $|K_{p}|$ by a factor of **100**. This is why a linear controller cannot be tuned once and left — gain scheduling or adaptive control is mandatory.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board-Style)

**Problem.** A wastewater stream at $F_{S} = 500\ \mathrm{L/min}$ contains $\mathrm{NaOH}$ at $C_{B} = 5\times 10^{-3}\ \mathrm{M}$. It is neutralized with $\mathrm{HCl}$ of concentration $C_{A} = 0.5\ \mathrm{M}$. Determine:

(a) the stoichiometric HCl flow rate,
(b) the pH if the HCl flow is **10 % above** stoichiometric,
(c) the pH if the HCl flow is **10 % below** stoichiometric,
(d) the static process gain at the operating pH in case (b).

### (a) Stoichiometric flow

$$
F_{A,\text{stoich}} \;=\; \frac{F_{S}\,C_{B}}{C_{A}} \;=\; \frac{500 \times 5\times10^{-3}}{0.5} \;=\; \frac{2.5}{0.5} \;=\; 5.0\ \mathrm{L/min}
$$

### (b) pH at +10 % acid (5.5 L/min)

Molar flow of $H^{+}$ added:

$$
\dot{n}_{H^{+}} = 5.5 \times 0.5 = 2.75\ \mathrm{mol/min}
$$

Molar flow of $OH^{-}$ in stream:

$$
\dot{n}_{OH^{-}} = 500 \times 5\times10^{-3} = 2.50\ \mathrm{mol/min}
$$

Excess $H^{+}$ in total flow $F_{T}=505.5\ \mathrm{L/min}$:

$$
[H^{+}] = \frac{2.75-2.50}{505.5} = 4.946\times10^{-4}\ \mathrm{M}
$$

$$
\mathrm{pH} = -\log_{10}(4.946\times10^{-4}) = 3.31
$$

### (c) pH at –10 % acid (4.5 L/min)

Molar flow of $H^{+}$ added:

$$
\dot{n}_{H^{+}} = 4.5 \times 0.5 = 2.25\ \mathrm{mol/min}
$$

Excess $OH^{-}$:

$$
[OH^{-}] = \frac{2.50-2.25}{504.5} = 4.955\times10^{-4}\ \mathrm{M}
$$

$$
\mathrm{pH} = 14 + \log_{10}(4.955\times10^{-4}) = 14 - 3.305 = 10.70
$$

### (d) Process gain at pH = 3.31

$$
K_{p} = -\frac{C_{A}}{2.303\,F_{S}\,[H^{+}]} = -\frac{0.5}{2.303 \times 500 \times 4.946\times10^{-4}} = -0.878\ \mathrm{pH\ unit\ per\ (L/min)}
$$

> [!TIP] **Interpretation of the Numerical Result**
> Cases (b) and (c) differ from stoichiometric by *the same 0.5 L/min* (10 % of 5.0 L/min), yet the pH moves to **3.31** on one side and **10.70** on the other — a swing of more than **7 pH units** for a 1 L/min change in reagent. This is the famous **"pH cliff"** that makes single-reagent pH control notoriously difficult.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Causes & Effects in Single Reagent Control

| Symptom in Plant | Most Probable Cause | Diagnostic Check | Recommended Remedy |
|---|---|---|---|
| Slow response far from setpoint | Operation on flat region of S-curve | Measure $\Delta$ pH for 10 % reagent change | Use *nonlinear* (e.g., error-squared) controller |
| Violent oscillation near setpoint | Operation on steep region; high $K_{p}$ | $K_{p}$ estimate from Nernst slope | Gain-scheduling, restrict pH operating range |
| Steady-state offset | Pure-proportional control | Compute bias from $\Delta$ formula | Add integral action |
| Slow cycling every 5–10 min | Dead time $\theta = V/F_{S}$ too large | Reduce tank volume or increase $F_{S}$ | Add feed-forward on $F_{S}$ and $C_{B}$ |
| Electrode noise | Reference-junction fouling | Clean & recalibrate | Use differential pH measurement |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Closed-Loop Block Diagram — Single Reagent pH Control

```mermaid
graph LR
    SP[Setpoint pH] --> SUM[Comparator]
    PV[Measured pH Signal] --> SUM
    SUM --> CTRL[PID Controller with Gain Scheduling]
    CTRL --> VLV[Modulating Valve Equal Percentage]
    VLV --> MIX[Reagent Injection Tee]
    PR[Process Stream FS with Base CB] --> MIX
    MIX --> TK[Well Mixed Reactor Tank]
    TK --> PHS[pH Glass Electrode]
    PHS --> TRX[Transmitter 4 to 20 mA]
    TRX --> PV
    style CTRL fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style MIX fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style TK fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
```

> [!NOTE] **Reading the Diagram**
> Follow the loop clockwise from the **Setpoint**. The error drives the **controller**, which positions the **valve** admitting acid into the **mixing tee**, where it neutralizes base in the incoming process stream. The **pH electrode** senses the resulting pH, the **transmitter** converts to 4–20 mA, and the loop closes.

## 4.2 Schematic of Static Nonlinearity — S-Curve with Gain Annotation

```mermaid
graph TB
    A[Very Low pH Region Excess Acid Plateau] -->|Slope Kp small| B[Equivalence Region Steep Slope Very High Kp]
    B -->|Slope Kp small| C[Very High pH Region Excess Base Plateau]
    D[Reagent Flow FA Increases] --> A
    D --> B
    D --> C
    style A fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style B fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
    style C fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
```

| Region | Visual | Approximate Slope $\vert K_{p}\vert$ | Controller Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|
| Excess acid (low pH) | Flat plateau | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$ pH/(L/min) | Slow, large error tolerated |
| Equivalence (pH ≈ 7) | Steep cliff | $10^{1}$ to $10^{3}$ pH/(L/min) | Sensitive — any error causes large deviation |
| Excess base (high pH) | Flat plateau | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$ pH/(L/min) | Slow, large error tolerated |

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Reagent Addition Subsystem

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph InputSection [Process Inputs]
        PS[Process Stream FS with NaOH at CB]
        RA[Reagent Header HCl at CA]
    end
    subgraph AdditionSection [Reagent Addition]
        MV[Modulating Valve]
        INJ[Injection Lance or Static Mixer]
    end
    subgraph ReactionSection [Neutralization]
        RX[H plus OH yields H2O Exothermic]
    end
    subgraph SensingSection [Measurement]
        EL[pH Electrode High Impedance]
        TR[Transmitter with Nernst Slope 59.16 mV per pH]
    end
    subgraph ControlSection [Feedback]
        SP[Setpoint e.g. pH 7]
        CMP[Comparator]
        PID[PID with Gain Scheduling]
    end
    PS --> RX
    RA --> MV
    MV --> INJ
    INJ --> RX
    RX --> EL
    EL --> TR
    TR --> CMP
    SP --> CMP
    CMP --> PID
    PID --> MV
```

> [!TIP] **Why this diagram matters for KTU**
> The figure emphasises the *interactions*: the same valve serves both the **control path** (top-down) and the **feedback path** (bottom-up). This is what makes pH loops a classic study of **nonlinearity, dead time, and interaction** in the M1 syllabus.

## 4.4 Characteristic pH Titration Plot (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    X[Reagent Added F_A in mL] --> Y[pH of Mixture]
    Y --> P1[Start pH high basic]
    P1 --> P2[Buffer Region Mild Slope]
    P2 --> P3[Equivalence Point pH 7 Steep Jump]
    P3 --> P4[Excess Acid Region Flat]
    style P1 fill:#cce5ff
    style P2 fill:#fff4cc
    style P3 fill:#f8d7da
    style P4 fill:#d4edda
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**

### Q1. Define **Single Reagent Control**. Give **two industrial examples** and state why it is challenging to implement. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

Single Reagent Control is a process control scheme in which only **one** chemical reagent is added to the process to regulate **one** controlled variable. Examples: (i) addition of **HCl** to neutralize alkaline waste to a target pH, (ii) addition of **Cl₂** to maintain free chlorine residual in water treatment. It is challenging because the static characteristic is an **S-shaped titration curve** whose **static gain changes by several orders of magnitude** across the operating range, so a controller tuned at one pH will misbehave at another. **[3 Marks]**

---

### Q2. Why is pH control using a single reagent considered one of the most difficult control problems in industry? **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

Because the **titration curve is highly non-linear**:
- In the **plateau regions** (excess acid or excess base) the process gain is small, giving a *sluggish* response.
- Near the **equivalence point (pH 7)** the process gain becomes *very large* — a 1 % change in reagent can shift pH by **5–7 units**.
- This **four-decade variation in gain** means a single linear PID setting cannot perform well across the range, requiring gain scheduling or nonlinear control. **[3 Marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**

### Question A

**(a)** Derive the **stoichiometric reagent flow equation** and the **pH–flow relationship** for a single strong-acid reagent added to a strong-base process stream. Clearly state assumptions. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A wastewater stream of **$F_{S}=800\ \mathrm{L/min}$** containing **$0.002\ \mathrm{M}$ NaOH** is to be neutralized to **pH = 6.5** using **$0.2\ \mathrm{M}$ H₂SO₄**. Calculate:
  (i) the stoichiometric H₂SO₄ flow rate, **[2 Marks]**
  (ii) the required H₂SO₄ flow to achieve pH 6.5, **[3 Marks]**
  (iii) the static process gain at this operating point. **[2 Marks]**

#### Model Solution

**(a)** Reaction: $H^{+}+OH^{-}\rightarrow H_{2}O$ (1:1).

Equating molar flows at equivalence:

$$
F_{A,\text{stoich}}\,C_{A} = F_{S}\,C_{B} \;\Rightarrow\; F_{A,\text{stoich}} = \frac{F_{S}\,C_{B}}{C_{A}}
$$

Assumptions: (i) strong acid / strong base (complete dissociation), (ii) ideal mixing, (iii) constant temperature **25 °C** so that $K_{w}=10^{-14}$, (iv) $F_{A}\ll F_{S}$ so that $F_{S}+F_{A}\approx F_{S}$.

Excess acid concentration: $\Delta=(F_{A}C_{A}-F_{S}C_{B})/F_{S}$. For pH 6.5, $\Delta = 10^{-6.5}=3.16\times10^{-7}\ \mathrm{M}$:

$$
F_{A}=F_{A,\text{stoich}}+\frac{\Delta F_{S}}{C_{A}}
$$

In the excess-base region (pH > 7) the symmetric relation is used. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Note: H₂SO₄ provides **2 moles of $H^{+}$ per mole**, so its *equivalent* concentration is $C_{A,\text{eq}}=2\times0.2 = 0.4\ \mathrm{N}$.

**(i) Stoichiometric flow:**

$$
F_{A,\text{stoich}} = \frac{F_{S}\,C_{B}}{C_{A,\text{eq}}} = \frac{800 \times 0.002}{0.4} = 4.0\ \mathrm{L/min}
$$

**(ii) Flow required for pH 6.5:**

$$
[H^{+}]=10^{-6.5}=3.162\times10^{-7}\ \mathrm{M}
$$

$$
\Delta = [H^{+}] = \frac{F_{A}\,C_{A,\text{eq}}-F_{S}\,C_{B}}{F_{S}}
$$

$$
F_{A}\,C_{A,\text{eq}} = F_{S}C_{B} + \Delta F_{S} = 800(0.002+3.162\times10^{-7}) = 1.6003\ \mathrm{mol/min}
$$

$$
F_{A} = \frac{1.6003}{0.4} = 4.00063\ \mathrm{L/min}
$$

So only **0.016 % above** stoichiometric is required — the plant operates on the cliff!

**(iii) Process gain at pH 6.5:**

$$
K_{p} = -\frac{C_{A,\text{eq}}}{2.303\,F_{S}\,[H^{+}]} = -\frac{0.4}{2.303 \times 800 \times 3.162\times10^{-7}} \approx -688\ \mathrm{pH\ units\ per\ (L/min)}
$$

This enormous magnitude confirms the difficulty of pH control at this operating point. **[7 Marks]**

---

### Question B (Internal Choice)

**(a)** With the help of a **neat sketch of the S-shaped titration curve**, explain why the static process gain varies along the curve. Discuss the engineering implications for controller design. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain **three practical control strategies** used in industry to overcome the nonlinearity of single reagent control. State one advantage and one limitation of each. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution

**(a)** **Sketch & Explanation** *(refer to Section 4.2 of these notes)*:

The titration curve has three regions:
- **Lower plateau** (excess acid): curve is flat → small slope → small $|K_{p}|$ → process is *insensitive*; controller must apply large reagent changes for small pH correction.
- **Equivalence region** (around pH 7): curve is *steep* → very large slope → huge $|K_{p}|$ → process is *hyper-sensitive*; even micro-corrections shift pH dramatically.
- **Upper plateau** (excess base): mirror of lower plateau.

The slope is exactly $|K_{p}| = C_{A}/(2.303\,F_{S}\,[H^{+}])$, which is a **hyperbolic function of $[H^{+}]$**. Engineering implications: (i) a single set of PID gains cannot give good performance across the range, (ii) the controller is **conditionally stable** — it will oscillate on the steep region and be sluggish on the flat region. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Three control strategies:

1. **Gain Scheduling (Piece-wise Linear PID)**
   - *Advantage*: simple to implement in modern DCS; preserves linear PID structure inside each region.
   - *Limitation*: requires the operating region to be identified reliably; transitions between regions can still cause bumps.

2. **Nonlinear Controller (Error-Squared or pH-Specific Function)**
   - *Advantage*: smooth response across the whole curve; no region switching required.
   - *Limitation*: tuning is empirical; mathematical analysis is harder; performance depends on accurate model.

3. **Adaptive / Model-Predictive Control (MPC)**
   - *Advantage*: continuously updates $K_{p}$ estimate; can handle feed-forward disturbances in $F_{S}$ and $C_{B}$.
   - *Limitation*: higher cost and complexity; needs reliable pH sensor with low dead time.

*(Other valid answers: feed-forward control, two-reagent control, use of equal-percentage valves, pH measurement in a side-stream with longer residence time.)* **[7 Marks]**

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing $C_{A}$ with equivalents**: For H₂SO₄, each mole gives 2 mol $H^{+}$. Always state the **equivalent concentration** for stoichiometric calculations. **(–2 marks)**
> 2. **Mixing up pH and $[H^{+}]$**: A 1-unit pH change = 10× change in $[H^{+}]$, not 1×. Many students treat it linearly and get the gain wrong by orders of magnitude. **(–3 marks)**
> 3. **Forgetting the approximation $F_{A}\ll F_{S}$**: If $F_{A}/F_{S}$ exceeds 1 %, keep the full $F_{S}+F_{A}$ in the denominator. **(–1 mark)**
> 4. **Using the wrong $K_{w}$**: At temperatures other than 25 °C, $K_{w}$ changes (e.g., $10^{-13.26}$ at 60 °C). State the temperature assumed. **(–1 mark)**
> 5. **Reporting pH with more than 2 decimals**: pH electrodes have practical accuracy of ±0.02 at best; three or four decimals is meaningless. **(–0.5 mark)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Single Reagent Control** = one reagent regulates one controlled variable; characteristic is the **S-shaped titration curve**.
- [ ] **Static gain** $K_{p} = -C_{A}/(2.303\,F_{S}\,[H^{+}])$ — **grows exponentially with pH**.
- [ ] **Stoichiometric flow** $F_{A,\text{stoich}} = F_{S}\,C_{B}/C_{A}$ (use *equivalents* for polyprotic acids).
- [ ] **pH equations**: $\mathrm{pH}=-\log_{10}\Delta$ (acid side), $\mathrm{pH}=14+\log_{10}(-\Delta)$ (base side), with $\Delta=(F_{A}C_{A}-F_{S}C_{B})/F_{S}$.
- [ ] **Nernst slope** of glass electrode = **59.16 mV per pH unit at 25 °C**.
- [ ] **Process model** for design: FOPDT $G_{p}(s) = K_{p}\,e^{-\theta s}/(\tau s+1)$ with $\theta=V/F_{S}$.
- [ ] **Gain variation** of **4 decades** over 4 pH units → linear PID cannot serve the full range.
- [ ] **Control remedies**: gain scheduling, nonlinear (error-squared) controller, adaptive/MPC, equal-% valve, feed-forward on $F_{S}$ and $C_{B}$.
- [ ] **Industrial examples**: effluent pH, water chlorination, FGD scrubbers, semiconductor wet benches.
- [ ] **Always specify temperature and use equivalents** when dealing with multi-protic acids (H₂SO₄, H₃PO₄).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feedforward Feedback Neutralisation Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT864 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN LARGE SCALE INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Feedforward Feedback Neutralisation Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Feedforward–Feedback Neutralisation Control** is a hybrid advanced process control strategy used in **pH regulation systems** of large-scale chemical, pharmaceutical, and wastewater-treatment plants. It combines:

- A **feedforward (FF) loop** that measures an upstream *disturbance* (inlet flow rate $F_1$ and inlet pH/acid concentration $C_{a1}$) and preemptively manipulates the **reagent (neutralising agent) flow rate** $F_2$ *before* the disturbance propagates through the process.
- A **feedback (FB) loop** that measures the *controlled variable* — the downstream effluent pH — and trims the residual error of the feedforward action through a PID controller.

Mathematically, the manipulated variable (reagent flow) becomes:

$$F_2(t) \;=\; F_{2,\,FF}(F_1,\,C_{a1}) \;+\; F_{2,\,FB}(e_{pH})$$

where the FF term handles the bulk of the load change and the FB term eliminates steady-state offset and compensates for unmodelled disturbances.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This control architecture is the *industry-standard solution* to the notorious nonlinearity of pH titration curves, where the process gain can vary by **3–5 orders of magnitude** between pH 2 and pH 12.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Shower"

Imagine you are showering. The *disturbance* is when someone else in the house flushes a toilet, suddenly making your shower water scalding hot.

| Action | Real World | Control Engineering |
|---|---|---|
| You feel the temperature change (too late!) | Pure Feedback | Measures outlet pH, then corrects |
| You hear the toilet flush and preemptively turn the cold knob a bit | Pure Feedforward | Measures disturbance $F_1$, acts immediately |
| You do **both** — turn the knob AND feel the water | **Feedforward + Feedback** | The KTU-recommended strategy |

A feedback-only controller would react *after* the pH has already swung dangerously; a feedforward-only controller cannot compensate for modelling errors (e.g., unknown buffer content in the inlet stream). Combining them gives **fast disturbance rejection + zero steady-state offset**.

## 1.3 Why Standard Feedback Fails for pH — The Core Motivation

A pH probe is logarithmic, so its static characteristic is the **titration curve**, an S-shaped curve that is *almost flat* at pH < 2 and pH > 12, but **extremely steep** near pH 7. This means the **process gain $K_p$ is not constant**:

$$K_p \;=\; \frac{\partial pH}{\partial C_b} \;=\; -\frac{1}{\ln(10)\,[H^+]} \;\approx\; -\frac{0.4343}{[H^+]}$$

Near neutrality, $[H^+] \approx 10^{-7}$, so a single drop of reagent causes a *huge* pH swing — a fixed-gain PID either oscillates violently or is too sluggish.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry metric of nonlinearity:** For a strong-acid/strong-base system, the static gain varies over a range of **$10^5 : 1$**, which is why gain-scheduling, adaptive control, and FF-FB schemes were invented.

## 1.4 KTU Course Outcome (CO) Mapping

| CO | Bloom Level | How this topic addresses it |
|---|---|---|
| CO1 — Identify large-scale process loops | Remember / Understand | Recognise FF-FB architecture in a P&ID |
| CO2 — Analyse dynamics of industrial processes | Analyse | Derive the nonlinear pH model and linearise |
| CO3 — Design controllers for chemical processes | Apply / Create | Synthesise the FF law and tune the FB PID |
| CO4 — Evaluate control performance | Evaluate | Compare FF-only, FB-only and FF-FB responses |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physical Process — A Continuously Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR)

Consider a **mixing tank** where an acidic waste stream is neutralised by adding a reagent (typically NaOH) of known concentration $C_{b2}$.

### 2.1.1 Component Material Balance (acid species)

Performing an unsteady-state molar balance on the tank (volume $V$, assumed constant, perfectly mixed):

$$V\,\frac{dC_a}{dt} \;=\; F_1\,C_{a1} \;-\; F_1\,C_a \;-\; F_2\,C_{b2} \;+\; r_A\cdot V$$

For a **strong acid – strong base** reaction, the net reaction rate $r_A$ is essentially instantaneous ($r_A \to \infty$ in practice), and electroneutrality gives:

$$[H^+] - [OH^-] \;=\; C_a - C_b$$

where $C_a$ is the total strong-acid concentration and $C_b$ is the excess strong-base concentration from the reagent.

### 2.1.2 Dynamic Equation for Hydrogen-Ion Concentration

Combining the balance with electroneutrality and $[H^+][OH^-] = K_w = 10^{-14}$:

$$V\,\frac{d[H^+]}{dt} \;=\; F_1\,(C_{a1} - [H^+]) \;-\; F_2\,C_{b2} \;+\; \frac{K_w}{[H^+]}\,(F_1 + F_2)$$

The last term is usually negligible except in the strict vicinity of pH 7.

### 2.1.3 Conversion to pH Domain

Since $pH = -\log_{10}[H^+]$, applying the chain rule:

$$\frac{dpH}{dt} \;=\; -\frac{1}{\ln(10)\,[H^+]}\,\frac{d[H^+]}{dt} \;=\; -0.4343\cdot\frac{1}{[H^+]}\,\frac{d[H^+]}{dt}$$

This is the source of the **multiplicative nonlinearity** that defeats linear controllers.

## 2.2 The Feedforward Control Law

### 2.2.1 Steady-State Material Balance

At steady state, $d[H^+]/dt = 0$, and away from neutrality the $K_w$ term is negligible:

$$F_{2,\,FF} \;=\; \frac{F_1\,(C_{a1} - [H^+]_{sp})}{C_{b2}}$$

For pH set-points far from 7 (e.g., pH 9 or pH 5), $[H^+]_{sp}$ is tiny relative to $C_{a1}$, so:

$$\boxed{\;F_{2,\,FF} \;\approx\; \frac{F_1 \cdot C_{a1}}{C_{b2}}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight:** The FF controller is essentially a *real-time stoichiometric calculator*. It uses the measured inlet flow $F_1$ and the (often tabulated) inlet acid strength $C_{a1}$ to dispense exactly the molar quantity of base needed to bring the effluent to set-point. **No pH sensor is required for the FF action.**

### 2.2.2 The Two Unknowns Problem

In practice, $C_{a1}$ may *drift* (the upstream process changes its feedstock). A **pure FF system** therefore accumulates offset. This is exactly where the FB trim enters.

## 2.3 The Feedback Trim — PID on pH Error

$$F_{2,\,FB}(t) \;=\; K_c\!\left[\,e(t) \;+\; \frac{1}{\tau_I}\!\int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau \;+\; \tau_D\,\frac{de(t)}{dt}\,\right]$$

with $e(t) = pH_{sp} - pH(t)$.

### 2.3.1 Tuning Challenges for the FB Loop

Because of the wildly varying static gain, the PID parameters must be **gain-scheduled** on the operating pH:

| Operating Region | $K_p$ | $K_c$ (recommended) | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|
| pH 2 – 4 | very low | high | Aggressive corrective action needed |
| pH 6 – 8 | **very high** | **low** | Gentle action — small reagent change ⇒ big pH swing |
| pH 10 – 12 | very low | high | Aggressive corrective action again |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student error:** Using a *single* set of PID constants across the entire titration curve. This is **guaranteed to either oscillate near pH 7 or crawl at pH 2**. KTU examiners will deduct marks if no mention is made of gain scheduling.

## 2.4 Weak-Acid / Weak-Base Case — Henderson–Hasselbalch Equation

If the process stream contains a **weak acid** HA of dissociation constant $K_a$:

$$\boxed{\;pH \;=\; pK_a \;+\; \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{[A^-]}{[HA]}\right)\;}$$

The titration curve is *less steep* than for a strong acid, and the high-gain region is wider — actually making control somewhat easier, but the FF law must be modified to account for the buffer capacity $\beta$:

$$F_{2,\,FF} \;=\; \frac{F_1\,(C_{a1,\,total} - C_{buffer,\,sp})}{C_{b2}}$$

where $C_{buffer,\,sp}$ is the buffer-residual acid concentration at set-point, computed from:

$$C_{buffer,\,sp} \;=\; [HA]_{sp} \;=\; \frac{C_{T}}{1 + 10^{pH_{sp} - pK_a}}$$

with $C_T$ the total weak-acid concentration.

## 2.5 Combined Control Law — The Total Reagent Flow

$$\boxed{\;F_2(t) \;=\; \underbrace{\frac{F_1(t)\cdot C_{a1}(t)}{C_{b2}}}_{\text{Feedforward}} \;+\; \underbrace{K_c\!\left[e(t) + \frac{1}{\tau_I}\!\int e\,dt + \tau_D\,\dot{e}\right]}_{\text{Feedback trim}}\;}$$

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $pH = -\log_{10}[H^+]$ | Definition of pH | dimensionless |
| 2 | $V\dfrac{d[H^+]}{dt} = F_1(C_{a1}-[H^+]) - F_2C_{b2}$ | CSTR dynamic model | mol / s |
| 3 | $K_p = -\dfrac{0.4343}{[H^+]}$ | Process gain (linearised) | pH-units per mol·L⁻¹ |
| 4 | $F_{2,FF} = \dfrac{F_1\cdot C_{a1}}{C_{b2}}$ | Steady-state FF law | L·s⁻¹ or m³·h⁻¹ |
| 5 | $F_{2,FB} = K_c\!\left[e + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I}\!\int e\,dt + \tau_D\dot e\right]$ | PID feedback trim | L·s⁻¹ |
| 6 | $pH = pK_a + \log\!\left(\dfrac{[A^-]}{[HA]}\right)$ | Henderson–Hasselbalch (weak acids) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\tau_p = \dfrac{V}{F_1 + F_2}$ | Process time constant | seconds |
| 8 | $\beta = \dfrac{dC_b}{dpH}$ | Buffer capacity | mol·L⁻¹ per pH-unit |
| 9 | $N_i = \dfrac{F_1\,C_{a1}}{F_2\,C_{b2}}$ | Neutralisation number (should ≈ 1) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $IAE = \int_0^\infty \vert e(t)\vert\,dt$ | Integral Absolute Error — performance index | pH·s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** This architecture is deployed in **every municipal wastewater-treatment plant** (e.g., the Kerala Water Authority's sewage plants), in **pharma API manufacturing** where final-product pH must be 6.8 ± 0.2, and in **FGD scrubbers** of thermal power stations where the limestone slurry pH must remain in the 5.5–6.0 window.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Steady-State Feedforward Law

**Problem:** A continuous wastewater stream containing $C_{a1} = 0.01$ mol/L of HCl flows at $F_1 = 50$ L/s. NaOH reagent of $C_{b2} = 0.1$ mol/L is used. Derive the FF flow rate required to neutralise the stream (assume set-point pH ≫ 7 so $[H^+]_{sp} \approx 0$).

### Step-by-step working

**Step 1 — Write the steady-state molar balance on $H^+$ ions:**

At steady state the time derivative vanishes:

$$\frac{d[H^+]}{dt} \;=\; 0$$

$$\Longrightarrow \; F_1(C_{a1} - [H^+]_{sp}) \;-\; F_2\,C_{b2} \;+\; \frac{K_w}{[H^+]_{sp}}(F_1+F_2) \;=\; 0$$

**Step 2 — Justify neglecting the $K_w$ term (water auto-ionisation):**

For pH set-point $\geq 9$, $[H^+]_{sp} \leq 10^{-9}$ mol/L. Hence:

$$\frac{K_w}{[H^+]_{sp}} \;=\; \frac{10^{-14}}{10^{-9}} \;=\; 10^{-5} \text{ mol/L} \;\ll\; C_{a1}=10^{-2} \text{ mol/L}$$

So the $K_w$ contribution is **four orders of magnitude smaller** and can be safely dropped.

**Step 3 — Simplified steady-state balance:**

$$F_1\,C_{a1} \;-\; F_1[H^+]_{sp} \;-\; F_2\,C_{b2} \;=\; 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $F_2$:**

$$F_{2,\,FF} \;=\; \frac{F_1(C_{a1} - [H^+]_{sp})}{C_{b2}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values (with $[H^+]_{sp} \approx 0$ at pH 9):**

$$F_{2,\,FF} \;=\; \frac{50 \;\text{L/s} \;\times\; 0.01 \;\text{mol/L}}{0.1 \;\text{mol/L}}$$

$$F_{2,\,FF} \;=\; \frac{0.5 \;\text{mol/s}}{0.1 \;\text{mol/L}} \;=\; 5 \;\text{L/s}$$

**Step 6 — Verify with the neutralisation number:**

$$N_i \;=\; \frac{F_1\,C_{a1}}{F_2\,C_{b2}} \;=\; \frac{50 \times 0.01}{5 \times 0.1} \;=\; \frac{0.5}{0.5} \;=\; 1.0 \;\checkmark$$

A neutralisation number of unity confirms perfect stoichiometric cancellation — the basis of any well-designed FF law.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Linearised Process Gain Near Set-Point

**Problem:** Show that the gain of a pH process at operating point $[H^+]_s$ is $K_p = -0.4343/[H^+]_s$ and compute $K_p$ at pH 7.

### Step-by-step working

**Step 1 — Start from the steady-state balance (FF action already satisfied, so $C_a = C_b = [H^+]_s$):**

$$F_1(C_{a1} - [H^+]) \;=\; F_2\,C_{b2}$$

**Step 2 — Define an operating point $[H^+]_s$ and perturb it:**

$$[H^+] \;=\; [H^+]_s + \delta[H^+]$$

**Step 3 — Compute the pH change corresponding to the perturbation:**

$$\delta(pH) \;=\; -\log_{10}\!\big([H^+]_s + \delta[H^+]\big) - \big(-\log_{10}[H^+]_s\big)$$

$$\delta(pH) \;=\; \log_{10}[H^+]_s \;-\; \log_{10}\!\big([H^+]_s(1 + \delta[H^+]/[H^+]_s)\big)$$

**Step 4 — Use the small-signal approximation $\log_{10}(1+x) \approx 0.4343\,x$ for $x \ll 1$:**

$$\delta(pH) \;\approx\; -0.4343\cdot\frac{\delta[H^+]}{[H^+]_s}$$

**Step 5 — Read off the gain relating $\delta(pH)$ to $\delta[H^+]$:**

$$K_p \;\equiv\; \frac{\partial(pH)}{\partial[H^+]} \;\approx\; -\frac{0.4343}{[H^+]_s}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate at pH 7 ($[H^+]_s = 10^{-7}$ mol/L):**

$$K_p \;=\; -\frac{0.4343}{10^{-7}} \;=\; -4.343\times 10^{6} \;\text{pH units per mol/L}$$

**Step 7 — Contrast with pH 2 ($[H^+]_s = 10^{-2}$ mol/L):**

$$K_p(2) \;=\; -\frac{0.4343}{10^{-2}} \;=\; -43.43 \;\text{pH units per mol/L}$$

The gain ratio between pH 7 and pH 2 is:

$$\frac{K_p(7)}{K_p(2)} \;=\; \frac{10^{-2}}{10^{-7}} \;=\; 10^{5}$$

**This 5-decade variation is what makes pH control a benchmark problem in advanced process control.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Combined FF-FB Closed-Loop Response

**Problem:** A step disturbance $\Delta F_1$ in inlet flow occurs. Show that the FF path produces an instantaneous response, while the FB path eliminates the residual offset.

### Step-by-step working

**Step 1 — Decompose the manipulated variable:**

$$F_2(t) \;=\; F_{2,\,FF}(t) \;+\; F_{2,\,FB}(t)$$

**Step 2 — Show FF compensates the disturbance instantly:**

If $F_{2,\,FF}$ is recomputed the *same instant* $F_1$ changes (i.e., negligible sensor and computational delays):

$$F_{2,\,FF}(t^+) \;=\; \frac{(F_1 + \Delta F_1)\cdot C_{a1}}{C_{b2}}$$

The new reagent flow matches the new acid loading perfectly — the *ideal* response shows zero pH deviation. In reality, transport delays in piping $\tau_d$ cause a brief transient.

**Step 3 — Allow for FF modelling error $\varepsilon$:**

Suppose the actual inlet concentration is $C_{a1}^{act} = C_{a1}^{nom} + \Delta C_a$. The FF law uses the *nominal* value, so the residual acid load is:

$$\Delta C_{a,\,res} \;=\; \Delta C_a$$

**Step 4 — FB integral action removes the residual:**

The PID's integral term accumulates:

$$F_{2,\,FB}(t) \;\supset\; \frac{K_c}{\tau_I}\int_0^t (pH_{sp} - pH(\tau))\,d\tau$$

At a new steady state, this integral must exactly supply the missing reagent:

$$\frac{K_c}{\tau_I}\int_0^\infty e(\tau)\,d\tau \;\to\; \frac{\Delta C_a \cdot F_1}{C_{b2}}$$

Hence **steady-state offset is always zero** provided $\tau_I < \infty$.

**Step 5 — Combine into the closed-loop transfer function (linearised form):**

$$\frac{pH(s)}{F_1(s)} \;=\; \frac{G_d(s) - G_{FF}(s)\,G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s)\,G_p(s)\,G_v(s)\,G_m(s)}$$

where:
- $G_d(s)$ = disturbance transfer function
- $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}$ = linearised process
- $G_c(s)$ = PID controller
- $G_v(s)$ = valve dynamics
- $G_m(s)$ = pH measurement (often $\approx 1$, with time constant $\tau_m$)
- $G_{FF}(s)$ = feedforward compensator

**Step 6 — Ideal FF cancellation:**

If $G_{FF}(s) = G_d(s)/G_p(s)$ (the *perfect* FF model), the numerator vanishes and the disturbance has *zero effect* on pH at all frequencies. The FB loop is then only responsible for compensating model mismatch.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — IAE Performance Comparison

For a unit-step disturbance $\Delta F_1$ in inlet flow, the **Integral Absolute Error** for each control strategy is approximately:

| Strategy | IAE (analytical) | Comment |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback only | $IAE_{FB} \approx \dfrac{\Delta F_1 \cdot K_p}{\tau_p}$ | Slow decay, large overshoot |
| Feedforward only | $IAE_{FF} \approx \dfrac{\Delta F_1 \cdot K_p \cdot \varepsilon}{2}$ | $\varepsilon$ = modelling-error fraction |
| **FF + FB combined** | $IAE_{FFFB} \approx \dfrac{\Delta F_1 \cdot K_p \cdot \varepsilon}{2}\,\cdot\,\dfrac{1}{1 + K_c K_p}$ | **Smallest by far** |

The combined scheme's IAE shrinks as $K_c K_p$ grows, which is precisely why **high controller gain is desirable** — but it must be gain-scheduled to avoid instability near pH 7.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of FF-FB Neutralisation Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Inlet Acid Stream F1 with Ca1"] --> B["Mixing Tank CSTR"]
    B --> C["Effluent Outlet pH Measured"]
    C --> D["pH Transmitter"]
    D --> E["Subtractor e equals pHsp minus pH"]
    F["Flow Transmitter F1"] --> G["Feedforward Calculator"]
    G --> Bsum["Summing Junction"]
    E --> H["PID Feedback Controller"]
    H --> Bsum
    Bsum --> I["Control Valve on Reagent Line"]
    I --> J["Reagent Base Stream F2 with Cb2"]
    J --> B
    C --> K["Effluent to Drain or Next Unit"]

    classDef dist fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#a0522d,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#b0e0e6,stroke:#4682b4,color:#000
    classDef fb fill:#d8bfd8,stroke:#8b008b,color:#000
    classDef ff fill:#98fb98,stroke:#228b22,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#fafad2,stroke:#daa520,color:#000
    class A,F dist
    class B,J proc
    class C,D,E,H fb
    class G,Bsum,I ff
    class K out
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**
- **Orange path** = Disturbance: the acid stream flowing into the tank.
- **Blue path** = Reagent delivery loop (FF + FB both command the same valve).
- **Purple path** = Feedback: pH is measured *downstream* of the tank and compared to set-point.
- **Green path** = Feedforward: inlet flow $F_1$ is measured *upstream* and converted into a preemptive reagent command.
- **Yellow path** = Effluent leaving the unit, fully neutralised.

## 4.2 Process Gain vs Operating pH — The Nonlinear Signature

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Operating pH less than 3"] --> G1["Kp very LOW about 43"]
    P2["Operating pH 3 to 5"] --> G2["Kp rising 43 to 4.3e3"]
    P3["Operating pH 5 to 7"] --> G3["Kp HIGHEST 4.3e3 to 4.3e5"]
    P4["Operating pH 7 to 9"] --> G4["Kp falling 4.3e5 to 4.3e3"]
    P5["Operating pH greater than 9"] --> G5["Kp very LOW about 43"]

    G1 --> N1["Need HIGH Kc aggressive correction"]
    G2 --> N2["Need MEDIUM Kc"]
    G3 --> N3["Need LOW Kc small action causes big swing"]
    G4 --> N4["Need MEDIUM Kc"]
    G5 --> N5["Need HIGH Kc aggressive correction again"]

    classDef region fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#4682b4
    classDef gain fill:#fff0f5,stroke:#c71585
    classDef act fill:#f0fff0,stroke:#228b22
    class P1,P2,P3,P4,P5 region
    class G1,G2,G3,G4,G5 gain
    class N1,N2,N3,N4,N5 act
```

This diagram visually establishes the **5-decade variation** of $K_p$ and justifies the **gain-scheduled PID** required when the operating point can wander across the full pH range.

## 4.3 Signal Flow at a Disturbance Event — Time-Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Dist as Disturbance Source
    participant F1sens as F1 Flow Sensor
    participant FFcalc as Feedforward Calculator
    participant Valve as Reagent Control Valve
    participant Tank as Mixing Tank CSTR
    participant pHsen as pH Sensor
    participant PID as PID Feedback Controller

    Dist->>F1sens: Step change in inlet flow
    F1sens->>FFcalc: Measured F1 t = 0 sec
    FFcalc->>Valve: New F2 command t = 0.1 sec
    Valve->>Tank: Increased reagent flow
    Note over Tank: Residence time delay tau p
    Tank->>pHsen: Effluent pH begins to shift
    pHsen->>PID: pH measurement t = 2 to 5 sec
    PID->>Valve: Trim command delta F2
    Valve->>Tank: Fine correction delivered
    Note over Tank: pH returns to set point within tau p
```

This sequence diagram illustrates the **temporal separation** of FF (acts within milliseconds) and FB (acts after the tank's residence delay $\tau_p$), which is the *fundamental reason* the two loops complement each other.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** 
*Why is a simple feedback PID controller alone insufficient for pH neutralisation in large-scale industries? Mention any two reasons.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Extreme nonlinearity:** pH is a logarithmic function of $[H^+]$, and the static titration curve has a process gain $K_p$ that varies over **5 orders of magnitude** between pH 2 and pH 12. A single set of PID constants cannot stabilise the loop across this range — gain scheduling or adaptive control would be required (1½ marks).

2. **Slow response to large load disturbances:** Pure feedback waits until the pH sensor downstream detects the deviation. With typical tank residence times of 2–10 minutes in large reactors, the pH can swing through several units before corrective action takes effect — unacceptable when downstream biological treatment or product-quality specifications demand tight pH control (1½ marks).

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]** 
*State the steady-state feedforward control law for neutralising an acidic stream of flow $F_1$ and concentration $C_{a1}$ using a basic reagent of concentration $C_{b2}$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

At steady state, the molar flow of acid entering must equal the molar flow of base added:

$$F_1\,C_{a1} \;=\; F_2\,C_{b2}$$

$$\boxed{\;F_{2,\,FF} \;=\; \frac{F_1 \cdot C_{a1}}{C_{b2}}\;}$$

The FF controller is a *real-time stoichiometric calculator*. It requires measurement of inlet flow only, not pH, and acts instantaneously upon disturbance detection.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** *With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a feedforward–feedback control scheme as applied to a pH neutralisation process. Discuss the roles of each loop.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *An acidic effluent stream with $F_1 = 80$ L/min, $C_{a1} = 0.05$ mol/L is neutralised using a 0.2 mol/L NaOH solution. Determine:*
- *(i) The steady-state reagent flow rate $F_{2,\,FF}$ required to neutralise the stream (assume set-point pH = 9, strong acid–strong base).* **(3 marks)**
- *(ii) The neutralisation number $N_i$ at this operating condition.* **(2 marks)**
- *(iii) The process gain $K_p$ at pH 9 and at pH 5. Comment on the controller gain $K_c$ that should be used at each operating point.* **(2 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer — Part (a)

A feedforward–feedback neutralisation control system combines two cooperating control loops acting on a common final control element (the reagent control valve).

- **Feedforward loop:** Measures the disturbance (inlet flow $F_1$ and inlet pH/acid concentration $C_{a1}$) *upstream* of the process. A FF calculator computes the exact reagent flow $F_{2,\,FF} = F_1 C_{a1} / C_{b2}$ needed to neutralise the load stoichiometrically. Because the FF action is taken *before* the disturbance propagates through the tank, it provides fast disturbance rejection. **(3 marks)**

- **Feedback loop:** Measures the controlled variable (effluent pH) *downstream*. A PID controller computes a trim signal $F_{2,\,FB}$ to eliminate any residual error caused by FF modelling inaccuracies (e.g., unmeasured inlet concentration drift, unknown buffer content, transport delays). The integral action guarantees zero steady-state offset. **(3 marks)**

- **Combined action:** The two signals are summed at the control valve. $F_2 = F_{2,\,FF} + F_{2,\,FB}$. The FF loop handles the bulk of the load change; the FB loop trims the residual. **(1 mark)**

*Refer to the block diagram in SECTION 4.1 for the visual schematic.*

#### Model Answer — Part (b)

**(i) Feedforward reagent flow:**

At pH set-point 9, $[H^+]_{sp} = 10^{-9}$ mol/L $\approx 0$ compared with $C_{a1} = 0.05$ mol/L. Using the FF law:

$$F_{2,\,FF} \;=\; \frac{F_1 \cdot C_{a1}}{C_{b2}} \;=\; \frac{80 \;\text{L/min} \times 0.05 \;\text{mol/L}}{0.2 \;\text{mol/L}}$$

$$F_{2,\,FF} \;=\; \frac{4.0 \;\text{mol/min}}{0.2 \;\text{mol/L}} \;=\; 20 \;\text{L/min}$$

**(ii) Neutralisation number:**

$$N_i \;=\; \frac{F_1 C_{a1}}{F_2 C_{b2}} \;=\; \frac{80 \times 0.05}{20 \times 0.2} \;=\; \frac{4.0}{4.0} \;=\; 1.0$$

This confirms perfect stoichiometric balance — the FF controller is properly sized.

**(iii) Process gain at pH 9 and pH 5:**

At pH 9 ($[H^+] = 10^{-9}$):

$$K_p(9) \;=\; -\frac{0.4343}{10^{-9}} \;=\; -4.343 \times 10^{8} \;\text{pH-units per mol/L}$$

At pH 5 ($[H^+] = 10^{-5}$):

$$K_p(5) \;=\; -\frac{0.4343}{10^{-5}} \;=\; -4.343 \times 10^{4} \;\text{pH-units per mol/L}$$

**Comment on controller gain $K_c$:**

The product $K_c K_p$ (loop gain) must remain bounded for closed-loop stability. Since $K_p(9)$ is **10,000 times larger** than $K_p(5)$:

- At pH 9 → use **very low $K_c$** (otherwise even a small $\Delta F_2$ causes violent pH oscillation).
- At pH 5 → use **very high $K_c$** (otherwise the controller is sluggish, and slow drift goes uncorrected).

This is the textbook motivation for **gain-scheduling the PID** on the measured pH.

---

### Question B (14 Marks, Alternative) — **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** *Derive the dynamic equation governing $[H^+]$ in a continuously-stirred tank reactor used for acid–base neutralisation. Clearly state the assumptions used.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Compare pure-feedback, pure-feedforward and combined FF-FB control schemes for pH regulation in a large wastewater-treatment plant. Use a tabular comparison and indicate the IAE reduction achievable by the combined scheme.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer — Part (a)

**Assumptions:**

1. Perfect mixing in the tank (CSTR model).
2. Constant tank volume $V$ (inlet flow = outlet flow + reagent flow).
3. Strong acid–strong base reaction, instantaneous and complete.
4. Constant temperature, so $K_w$ is constant.
5. Density and activity effects neglected.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Component material balance on $H^+$ ions:**

Inflow of $H^+$ from the acid stream minus outflow of $H^+$ in the effluent minus $H^+$ consumed by reaction with $OH^-$ from the reagent = accumulation.

$$V\,\frac{d[H^+]}{dt} \;=\; F_1[H^+]_{in} \;-\; (F_1 + F_2)[H^+] \;-\; r\,V$$

where $r$ is the rate of $H^+$ consumption.

**Step 2 — Reaction stoichiometry:**

For every mole of $OH^-$ added via the reagent, one mole of $H^+$ is consumed:

$$r \;=\; k\,[H^+][OH^-] \;\approx\; \text{very fast} \;\Rightarrow\; r \to \infty$$

In the limit of instantaneous reaction, the system satisfies **electroneutrality**:

$$[H^+] - [OH^-] \;=\; C_a - C_b$$

For strong acid–strong base with excess acid: $[H^+] \approx C_a - C_b$.

**Step 3 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$V\,\frac{d[H^+]}{dt} \;=\; F_1\,C_{a1} \;-\; (F_1+F_2)[H^+] \;-\; F_2\,C_{b2} \;+\; (F_1+F_2)\,\frac{K_w}{[H^+]}$$

The last term accounts for water auto-ionisation.

**Step 4 — Final form:**

$$\boxed{\;V\,\frac{d[H^+]}{dt} \;=\; F_1(C_{a1} - [H^+]) \;-\; F_2\,C_{b2} \;+\; (F_1+F_2)\,\frac{K_w}{[H^+]}\;}$$

Away from pH 7, the $K_w/[H^+]$ term is negligible and the equation simplifies to a first-order ODE with multiplicative nonlinearity through the $[H^+]$ term on the RHS.

#### Model Answer — Part (b)

**Tabular comparison:**

| Criterion | Pure Feedback | Pure Feedforward | **FF + FB Combined** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Disturbance rejection speed | Slow (after residence time) | Fast (instant) | Fast (FF) + accurate (FB) |
| Steady-state offset | **Zero** (with integral) | Non-zero if model wrong | **Zero** |
| Sensitivity to model error | Low | **High** | Low |
| Need for pH sensor | **Yes** | No | **Yes** |
| Handles unmeasured disturbances | Yes (feedback's main strength) | No | **Yes** |
| Performance (IAE) for 10% step in $F_1$ | $IAE_{FB} \propto K_p/\tau_p$ | $IAE_{FF} \propto K_p \varepsilon / 2$ | $IAE_{FFFB} \propto K_p \varepsilon / [2(1+K_cK_p)]$ |
| Stability near pH 7 | Hard to tune | Easy (open-loop) | **Best** with gain scheduling |
| Implementation complexity | Low | Low–Medium | **Medium–High** |
| Industrial adoption | Rare for pH alone | Rare for pH alone | **Industry standard** |

**IAE reduction analysis:**

If the modelling error fraction is $\varepsilon = 0.1$ (10% mismatch between assumed and actual $C_{a1}$), and loop gain $K_c K_p = 100$ at the operating point, the combined scheme gives:

$$\frac{IAE_{FFFB}}{IAE_{FF}} \;\approx\; \frac{1}{1 + K_c K_p} \;=\; \frac{1}{101} \;\approx\; 1\%$$

Compared with pure FF, the combined scheme reduces IAE by a factor of about **100** — a dramatic improvement.

Compared with pure FB, the combined scheme acts *before* the disturbance reaches the tank, so the *time-integrated* error is also much smaller, especially for fast-rising disturbances like pump starts/stops.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where students typically lose marks:**
> 1. **Confusing feedforward with cascade control.** FF measures the disturbance directly; cascade measures an intermediate (secondary) variable. Marks deducted if not distinguished.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention the transport delay** $\tau_d$ in the FF path (sample-line delay, valve dynamics). If $\tau_d \geq \tau_p$, the FF action loses its advantage and may even destabilise the loop.
> 3. **Using a single set of PID gains** across the entire pH range. Examiners expect explicit discussion of **gain scheduling** or **nonlinear control** as the practical solution.
> 4. **Neglecting the water auto-ionisation term** $K_w/[H^+]$ in the dynamic model when the operating point is near pH 7. Always state the assumption explicitly.
> 5. **Sign convention errors** in PID formulation. KTU uses *direct acting* controllers for acid feed and *reverse acting* for base feed. State this clearly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **30-Second Summary:** A Feedforward–Feedback Neutralisation Control system uses (1) a feedforward stoichiometric calculator to handle large disturbances *instantly* and (2) a feedback PID trim to eliminate the residual offset. Together they tame the famous 5-decade nonlinearity of pH.

**High-yield bullets for last-minute revision:**

- ☐ pH control is hard because $K_p$ varies over **$10^5 : 1$** across the titration curve.
- ☐ FF law (strong acid–strong base): $F_{2,\,FF} = F_1 C_{a1} / C_{b2}$.
- ☐ FF law (weak acid): use Henderson–Hasselbalch + buffer-capacity correction.
- ☐ FB trim uses a PID with **gain scheduling** on the operating pH.
- ☐ Combined control: $F_2 = F_{2,\,FF} + F_{2,\,FB}$.
- ☐ The neutralisation number $N_i = F_1 C_{a1} / (F_2 C_{b2})$ must equal **1** at steady state.
- ☐ The dynamic model is a first-order ODE in $[H^+]$, with $K_p = -0.4343/[H^+]$ linearised gain.
- ☐ Perfect FF cancellation: choose $G_{FF}(s) = G_d(s)/G_p(s)$ — then disturbance has zero effect.
- ☐ FB integral action guarantees **zero steady-state offset** even if FF model is imperfect.
- ☐ The FF action has a transport delay $\tau_d$ — keep $\tau_d < \tau_p$ to preserve its advantage.
- ☐ Performance metric used: **IAE** = $\int \vert e(t)\vert\,dt$. Combined scheme reduces IAE by a factor $\approx 1+K_c K_p$.
- ☐ Applications: **wastewater treatment, FGD scrubbers, pharma API plants, fermentation broths**.
- ☐ Standard pH sensors: glass electrode + reference, output ~59 mV/pH at 25 °C (Nernst equation).
- ☐ Industrial reagent valves must be **linear-equal-percentage** characterised to handle wide rangeability of $F_2$.

**One-line exam-ready mnemonic:**
> **"Feedforward is FAST but FORGETFUL; Feedback is SLOW but FAITHFUL. Together they form the FF-FB marriage that conquers pH."**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Precipitation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT864 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN LARGE SCALE INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Precipitation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Precipitation** is a unit operation in chemical/process engineering in which a dissolved solute is converted into an **insoluble solid phase** (the *precipitate*) through a controlled chemical reaction or a deliberate change in process conditions such as **temperature**, **pH**, **solvent composition**, or **ionic strength**. The newly formed solid phase is typically separated from the mother liquor downstream by sedimentation, centrifugation, or filtration.

In the context of **large-scale process industries**, precipitation is rarely a stand-alone "lab" curiosity. It is a **separation, purification, and product-recovery** workhorse — the very reason pigments (titanium dioxide), fertilizers (ammonium phosphates), pharmaceutical actives, and treated industrial effluents exist in solid form.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Honey Jar

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine adding **far too much sugar** to a hot cup of tea and then letting the tea cool down. At high temperature, the water can *hold* a large amount of dissolved sugar. As the temperature drops, the solution becomes **supersaturated** — it is holding more sugar than it should naturally. The excess sugar can no longer stay dissolved, so it **"falls out"** as visible crystals at the bottom. That visible solid formation is exactly what engineers call *precipitation*. In industry, instead of cooling, we usually *force* the precipitation by mixing two reactive streams (a metal salt + a base) inside a large stirred tank called a **Precipitator** or **Crystallizer–Reactor**.

## 1.3 Salient Industrial Examples

| Industry | Precipitate Formed | Driving Force |
|---|---|---|
| **Wastewater Treatment** | Metal hydroxides (e.g., Fe(OH)₃, Al(OH)₃) | pH adjustment with Ca(OH)₂ or NaOH |
| **Hydrometallurgy** | Pure metal hydroxides / oxalates / sulfides | Selective addition of precipitant |
| **Pharmaceuticals** | Active Pharmaceutical Ingredients (APIs) | Anti-solvent addition / pH shift |
| **Cement & Ceramics** | Calcium silicate hydrate, alumina hydrates | Hydration reactions |
| **Pigments & Dyes** | TiO₂, Fe₂O₃, Prussian Blue | Controlled hydrolysis |
| **Fertilizers** | Ammonium phosphates, struvite | Reactive crystallization from process liquor |

## 1.4 Why Instrumentation & Control Care About Precipitation

> [!WARNING]
> **The "Why" for PEICT864 Students:** Precipitation is **exquisitely sensitive** to mixing intensity, residence time, feed concentration, temperature, and impurity profile. A 2 °C drift in temperature, or a 0.5-unit shift in pH, can change the **median particle size** by 30–50 %. In large-scale plants, this directly affects **filtration rate, product purity, downstream drying energy, and final market price**. Therefore, every precipitation reactor is wrapped in a tight control loop: **pH, T, conductivity, turbidity, PSD (particle size distribution), and flow.**

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Stage Mechanism of Precipitation

Precipitation does not happen in one instant. It unfolds in **five sequential, overlapping stages** — this is the heart of KTU exam questions.

1. **Super-saturation generation** — The solution is forced into a state where the dissolved solute concentration ($C$) exceeds the equilibrium saturation concentration ($C^*$). Supersaturation is quantified by the **supersaturation ratio** $S$.
2. **Induction / Latent period** — A brief waiting period during which sub-critical molecular clusters form and dissolve repeatedly.
3. **Nucleation** — The formation of a new, thermodynamically stable solid phase (the *nucleus*). A **critical radius** $r_c$ must be exceeded for the cluster to survive.
4. **Crystal (or particle) growth** — Solute molecules diffuse to the nucleus surface and integrate into the crystal lattice.
5. **Aging (Ostwald Ripening)** — Smaller, higher-energy particles re-dissolve and re-precipitate onto larger ones, narrowing the size distribution.

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Pearl:** If a question says *"the precipitate is amorphous, gelatinous and difficult to filter"* — that is **rapid, high-supersaturation, nucleation-dominated** precipitation. If the precipitate is *"dense, crystalline and easy to filter"* — that is **low-supersaturation, growth-dominated** precipitation. KTU examiners love this contrast.

## 2.2 Types of Nucleation

| Type | Mechanism | When It Dominates |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Homogeneous** | Spontaneous, from clear solution | Very high $S$, no foreign surfaces |
| **Primary Heterogeneous** | Foreign particles (dust, walls) act as seeds | Moderate $S$ (most industrial case) |
| **Secondary** | Induced by existing crystals of the same species | During seeded or continuous precipitation |

## 2.3 Types of Precipitation (KTU Favourite)

- **Co-precipitation** — Unwanted impurities are *carried down* with the desired solid (occlusion, adsorption, isomorphous replacement). Major cause of product impurity.
- **Post-precipitation** — A second precipitate forms *after* the first one has already settled, on its surface (e.g., Mg-oxalate on Ca-oxalate).
- **Fractional Precipitation** — Used to separate two or more cations by **careful control of pH** so that their solubilities are crossed one at a time (e.g., separating Group III cations in qualitative analysis).
- **Precipitation from Homogeneous Solution (PFHS)** — The precipitating agent is *generated in situ* by a slow reaction (e.g., urea hydrolysis to release OH⁻ slowly). Gives large, pure, filterable crystals.

## 2.4 Factors Affecting Precipitation

- **Concentration of reactants** → higher $C$ → higher $S$ → smaller particles.
- **Temperature** → affects $C^*$ and reaction rate.
- **pH** → for hydroxides and amphoteric species (Zn, Al, Cr).
- **Order and rate of mixing** → local vs bulk supersaturation.
- **Stirring/agitation** → reduces local supersaturation, favors growth.
- **Presence of seeds / impurities** → triggers heterogeneous nucleation.
- **Solvent composition** (e.g., ethanol/water) → lowers $C^*$, induces precipitation.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **directly testable** in the KTU University Exam. The `vert` symbol is used for absolute value to preserve the markdown table.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Solubility Product $K_{sp}$ | $K_{sp} = \prod [ion_i]^{\nu_i}$ at equilibrium | varies | For salt $A_m B_n \rightleftharpoons mA^{n+} + nB^{m-}$ |
| 2 | Molar Solubility $s$ | Derived from $K_{sp}$ by stoichiometry | mol/L | Example: $s = \sqrt{K_{sp}/4}$ for AB₂ |
| 3 | Supersaturation Ratio | $S = \dfrac{C}{C^*}$ | dimensionless | $S>1$ is the driving force |
| 4 | Absolute Supersaturation | $\Delta C = C - C^*$ | mol/L | Also written as $\sigma = \Delta C / C^*$ |
| 5 | Critical Nucleus Radius (Kelvin) | $r_c = \dfrac{2 \gamma V_m}{R T \ln S}$ | m | $\gamma$ = interfacial tension, $V_m$ = molar volume |
| 6 | Homogeneous Nucleation Rate | $J = A \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{16\pi \gamma^3 V_m^2}{3 k^3 T^3 (\ln S)^2}\right)$ | nuclei/(m³·s) | Highly non-linear in $S$ |
| 7 | von Weimarn Particle Size | $d_{med} \propto \dfrac{1}{\Delta C} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{Q}{N}}$ | µm | Empirical; $Q$ = feed rate, $N$ = stirring |
| 8 | Common-Ion Suppression | $[X^{n+}]_{new} = \dfrac{K_{sp}}{[Y^{m-}]^n_{added}}$ | mol/L | Used in wash water, purification |
| 9 | % Yield of Precipitate | $\%Y = \dfrac{n_{ppt} \cdot M_{ppt}}{n_{feed} \cdot M_{feed}} \times 100$ | % | $M$ = molar mass |
| 10 | Ionic Strength (for activity) | $I = \dfrac{1}{2}\sum c_i z_i^{\,2}$ | mol/kg | Used with Debye–Hückel for accurate $K_{sp}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorization priority for KTU exam (descending order):** $K_{sp}$ stoichiometry → $S$ and $\Delta C$ → Kelvin $r_c$ → von Weimarn $d_{med}$ → % yield.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of These Equations

- **$K_{sp}$** is used in **hydrometallurgy** to decide how much lime is needed to drop heavy metals below **discharge consent limits** (e.g., 2 mg/L Ni).
- **$S$ and $r_c$** dictate the **CSD (Crystal Size Distribution)** in continuous stirred-tank crystallizers (CSTRs) and MSMPRs.
- **von Weimarn relation** is the empirical backbone for **anti-solvent crystallization** in pharma API plants (acetone + water + API).
- **% Yield** drives the **economic feasibility** of a process — the difference between a 91 % yield and 97 % yield on a 50,000 t/y plant is **millions of rupees per year**.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Solubility Product ($K_{sp}$) From a General Salt

**Setup:** Consider the sparingly soluble salt of general stoichiometry $A_m B_n$.

$$
A_m B_n \, (s) \rightleftharpoons m\,A^{n+} \, (aq) + n\,B^{m-} \, (aq)
$$

**Step 1.** If the **molar solubility** is $s$ mol/L, then at equilibrium the dissolved ion concentrations are:

$$
[A^{n+}] = m \cdot s \quad\text{and}\quad [B^{m-}] = n \cdot s
$$

**Step 2.** By definition of the equilibrium constant (activities $\approx$ concentrations for dilute solutions):

$$
K_{sp} = [A^{n+}]^{m}\, [B^{m-}]^{n}
$$

**Step 3.** Substitute Step 1 into Step 2:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{sp} &= (m\,s)^{m} \cdot (n\,s)^{n} \\
       &= m^{m} \, n^{n} \cdot s^{m+n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4.** Solve for $s$ explicitly:

$$
\boxed{\,s = \left(\dfrac{K_{sp}}{m^{m} n^{n}}\right)^{\!\!1/(m+n)}\,}
$$

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example:** For AgCl ($m = n = 1$), $K_{sp} = 1.8 \times 10^{-10}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_{AgCl} &= \sqrt{1.8 \times 10^{-10}} \\
         &= 1.34 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol/L}
\end{aligned}
$$

Convert to g/L using $M_{AgCl} = 143.32$ g/mol:

$$
s_{AgCl} = 1.34 \times 10^{-5} \times 143.32 = 1.92 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/L} = 1.92 \text{ ppm}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Common-Ion Effect on Solubility

**Setup:** We have saturated AgCl solution. Now we add NaCl, which raises $[Cl^-]$ to a new value $[Cl^-]_{add}$.

**Step 1.** Initial dissolution: $K_{sp} = [Ag^+]_{sat}\,[Cl^-]_{sat}$

**Step 2.** On addition of common ion $Cl^-$, the equilibrium shifts **backward** (Le Chatelier). New $[Ag^+]$:

$$
\boxed{\,[Ag^+]_{new} = \dfrac{K_{sp}}{[Cl^-]_{add}}\,}
$$

**Numerical Example:** $K_{sp} = 1.8 \times 10^{-10}$, $[Cl^-]_{add} = 0.10$ M

$$
[Ag^+]_{new} = \dfrac{1.8 \times 10^{-10}}{0.10} = 1.8 \times 10^{-9} \text{ M}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Suppression Factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Factor} &= \dfrac{[Ag^+]_{sat}}{[Ag^+]_{new}} \\
             &= \dfrac{1.34 \times 10^{-5}}{1.8 \times 10^{-9}} \\
             &= 7.4 \times 10^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

The solubility of Ag⁺ has been suppressed by **a factor of ~7,400** — this is why a *wash* with dilute HCl removes Ag⁺ impurities far more efficiently than pure water.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Critical Nucleus Radius (Kelvin–Gibbs Equation)

**Setup:** A spherical nucleus of radius $r$ is in equilibrium with a solution of supersaturation $S$.

**Step 1.** The chemical-potential difference per mole of solute between supersaturated solution and a crystal of infinite radius is:

$$
\Delta \mu = R T \ln S
$$

**Step 2.** The free energy of forming the nucleus has two contributions:
- A **bulk (volume) term** — *favors* precipitation (negative).
- A **surface term** — *opposes* it (positive, $4\pi r^2 \gamma$).

$$
\Delta G(r) = -\dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}\,\dfrac{\rho}{M}\,\Delta \mu \;+\; 4\pi r^{2}\gamma
$$

where $\rho$ = density, $M$ = molar mass.

**Step 3.** Differentiate with respect to $r$ and set $\dfrac{d(\Delta G)}{dr}=0$ to find the maximum (the *critical* radius):

$$
\dfrac{d(\Delta G)}{dr} = -4\pi r^{2}\,\dfrac{\rho}{M}\Delta\mu + 8\pi r \gamma = 0
$$

**Step 4.** Solve for $r_c$:

$$
4\pi r_c^{2}\,\dfrac{\rho}{M}\Delta\mu = 8\pi r_c \gamma
$$

$$
\boxed{\,r_c = \dfrac{2 \gamma M}{\rho \, R T \, \ln S}\,}
$$

Recognising $V_m = M/\rho$, the more common engineering form is:

$$
\boxed{\,r_c = \dfrac{2 \gamma V_m}{R T \, \ln S}\,}
$$

**Step 5 — Physical Interpretation:**

- $r_c$ **decreases** as $S$ increases → more nuclei survive → *finer* particles.
- $r_c$ **decreases** as $T$ increases → higher $T$ favors smaller critical nuclei (kinetics dominate over thermodynamics here).
- $r_c$ **increases** with $\gamma$ → surfactants (lowering $\gamma$) ease nucleation.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — von Weimarn Empirical Relation for Median Particle Size

**Setup:** von Weimarn (1926) proposed that the median particle size in a precipitation depends on the absolute supersaturation and the number of nuclei.

**Step 1.** The total mass of precipitate is fixed by chemistry: $m_{total} = Q \cdot C_{feed} \cdot t$.

**Step 2.** If $N$ nuclei are formed, the volume per particle is $V_p = m_{total} / (\rho N)$.

**Step 3.** Equating to a sphere of diameter $d_{med}$:

$$
\dfrac{\pi}{6} d_{med}^{3} = \dfrac{m_{total}}{\rho N}
$$

**Step 4.** Substituting $m_{total} = Q C_{feed} t$ and noting that $N \propto \Delta C$ (more supersaturation → more nuclei):

$$
\boxed{\,d_{med} \;\propto\; \left(\dfrac{Q \cdot C_{feed} \cdot t}{\Delta C^{\,n}}\right)^{1/3} \quad \text{with } n \approx 1.5 \text{ to } 2\,}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Implication:**

- To get **larger crystals** (better filterability, easier washing): **lower $\Delta C$** (dilute feeds) and **higher $Q$ with rapid mixing** (avoid local hot spots).
- To get **fine precipitates** (pigments, pharmaceuticals needing high bioavailability): **high $\Delta C$** and **slow mixing** with intense nucleation.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Theory to Industrial Control

| Phenomenon | Equation That Describes It | Sensor / Instrument Used in Plant | Controlled Variable |
|---|---|---|---|
| Endpoint of precipitation | $K_{sp}$ stoichiometry | **pH probe** (for hydroxides) / **Ion-selective electrode** (ISE) | pH, ion activity |
| Supersaturation | $S = C/C^*$ | **Conductivity / density meter** | Feed concentration |
| Nucleation onset | $r_c$ (Kelvin) | **Turbidity probe** + **FBRM (Focused Beam Reflectance Measurement)** | Particle count |
| Crystal growth | $G = k_g \,\Delta C^{g}$ | **Laser diffraction PSD analyzer** (e.g., Malvern) | Median size $d_{50}$ |
| Aging | Ostwald ripening kinetics | **In-situ video microscopy / PVM** | Residence time |
| Yield | $\%Y$ expression | **Mass flow meter** on filtrate + load cell on solids | Stoichiometric feed ratio |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Process & Control Architecture of a Continuous Precipitation Plant

The diagram below maps a **typical large-scale hydroxide-precipitation train** (e.g., a zinc refinery or a metal-finishing wastewater plant) and overlays the **instrumentation and feedback control loops** that the KTU syllabus expects you to identify.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FEED["RAW FEED STREAMS"]
        A1["Metal Salt Solution\n(SO4 or NO3 liquor)"]
        A2["Precipitant Tank\n(NaOH or CaOH2)"]
        A3["Flocculant Dosing\n(Polymer make-up)"]
    end

    subgraph REACTOR["PRECIPITATION REACTOR CSTR"]
        B1["Impeller + Baffles\n(Controlled RPM)"]
        B2["pH Probe"]
        B3["Conductivity Sensor"]
        B4["Temperature RTD"]
        B5["Turbidity / FBRM Probe"]
    end

    subgraph SEP["SOLID LIQUID SEPARATION"]
        C1["Thickener / Lamella"]
        C2["Centrifuge or Filter Press"]
        C3["Wash Water Tank"]
    end

    subgraph DRY["PRODUCT RECOVERY"]
        D1["Dryer or Calciner"]
        D2["Bagging / Silos"]
    end

    subgraph CTRL["CONTROL SYSTEM DCS / PLC"]
        E1["PID 1: pH controller\nmanipulates precipitant valve"]
        E2["PID 2: Level controller\nmanipulates underflow pump"]
        E3["PID 3: Temperature controller\nmanipulates steam / cooling water"]
        E4["Soft Sensor: CSD estimator\nfrom FBRM signal"]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B1 --> B3
    B1 --> B4
    B1 --> B5
    B1 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C3 --> C2
    C2 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    C2 --> E2
    B2 --> E1
    B4 --> E3
    B5 --> E4
    E1 --> A2
    E3 --> A1

    classDef sensor fill:#FFF3B0,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef controller fill:#C7E9FF,stroke:#00457C,color:#000
    classDef equip fill:#D5F5D5,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    class B2,B3,B4,B5 sensor
    class E1,E2,E3,E4 controller
    class B1,C1,C2,D1,D2,A1,A2,A3 equip
```
