topics.title: Chlorination-Based Disinfection Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT864 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN LARGE SCALE INDUSTRIES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Chlorination-Based Disinfection Control # 🧪 Module 1 — Chlorination-Based Disinfection Control ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 📘 Formal Academic Definition **Chlorination-Based Disinfection Control** is the automated, closed-loop instrumentation strategy used in large-scale water and wastewater treatment plants (WTPs / STPs) to inject a precisely metered dose of chlorine (or its derivatives — Cl₂ gas, NaOCl, Ca(OCl)₂, or ClO₂) into a process stream such that the **microbial load** is reduced to a regulatory target while maintaining a **discrete free chlorine residual** at the outlet of the contact basin. The system is governed by **two cardinal control objectives**: 1. **Disinfection Efficacy (Primary):** Achieve a target *log reduction* of indicator organisms (typically *E. coli*, coliphages, *Giardia*) through the **CT concept** (Concentration × Contact Time), where $CT$ is a function of the disinfectant residual $C$ (mg/L) and contact time $T$ (minutes). 2. **Residual Maintenance (Secondary):** Sustain a **free chlorine residual** of $\mathbf{0.2 - 0.5 \text{ mg/L}}$ at the consumer endpoint (WHO Guidelines, 4th Edition), and **$\le 1.0 \text{ mg/L}$** at the plant outlet (BIS IS 10500:2012). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This topic directly maps to **CO1** of the PEICT864 syllabus — *"Apply instrumentation principles to monitor and control unit operations in large-scale process industries."* Expected cognitive level: **Apply / Analyze (Bloom Level III–IV).** ### 🧠 Intuitive Analogy — "The Security Guard Dosing Problem" Imagine you are the manager of a building, and you hire **security guards** to keep intruders (bacteria/viruses) out. You face a daily dilemma: - **Too few guards** → intruders slip through → **incomplete disinfection** (regulatory failure, waterborne disease outbreak). - **Too many guards** → wasted salary, and the guards startle residents with their intimidating presence → **toxicity, taste/odor complaints, formation of disinfection by-products (DBPs) like trihalomethanes (THMs) and haloacetic acids (HAAs)**. You install a **smart sensor at the exit door** that counts how many guards are still on patrol (analogous to the **residual chlorine sensor**). A **controller (PLC/DCS)** automatically adjusts the number of guards entering each morning based on yesterday's exit count. This is **exactly** what a chlorination control loop does — it continuously measures the **residual chlorine** at the contact basin outlet, compares it against a **setpoint**, and modulates the **chlorine dosing pump** (or vacuum eductor / gas feeder) via a **PID controller** to maintain disinfection efficacy with minimum chemical consumption. ### 🧬 Chemical Forms of "Free Chlorine" in Water When Cl₂ dissolves in water, it establishes a pH-dependent equilibrium: $$ \text{Cl}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{HOCl} + \text{HCl} $$ Where: - **HOCl** (hypochlorous acid) — the **powerful disinfectant** (~80–100× more effective than OCl⁻). - **OCl⁻** (hypochlorite ion) — a **weaker disinfectant** that dominates at pH > 7.5. > [!NOTE] > **Key Industrial Fact:** At pH = 7.0, approximately **73%** of free chlorine exists as HOCl. At pH = 8.0, this drops to only **~23%**. Therefore, **pH control upstream of the chlorinator is critical** for predictable disinfection performance. ### 🎯 Regulatory & Design Benchmarks (Bold-Constrained) | Parameter | Standard / Value | |---|---| | Free Cl₂ residual at consumer tap (WHO) | **0.2 – 0.5 mg/L** | | Free Cl₂ residual at plant outlet (BIS 10500) | **≤ 1.0 mg/L** | | Contact time for CT compliance | **≥ 30 minutes** (typical) | | Target pH for optimal HOCl fraction | **6.5 – 7.5** | | Maximum THM limit (WHO / USEPA) | **100 µg/L** | | Typical Cl₂ dose (raw municipal water) | **1.0 – 5.0 mg/L** | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 🔍 2.1 The Chlorination Process — Step-by-Step Operating Logic A large-scale chlorination system is engineered around the following sequential unit operations: 1. **Feed Preparation:** Chlorine is supplied either as liquefied Cl₂ gas (from pressurized tonner/cylinder manifolds), bulk NaOCl (10–12% liquid), or solid Ca(OCl)₂ (65% available chlorine). 2. **Metered Injection:** A **vacuum gas feeder** (for Cl₂) or **metering pump** (for NaOCl) injects the disinfectant into the **rapid-mix chamber** or directly into the **main process line**. 3. **Flash Mixing:** A hydraulic or mechanical mixer ensures instantaneous dispersion (target: **$G \cdot t \ge 10^4$**, where $G$ = velocity gradient in s⁻¹, $t$ = mixing time in s). 4. **Contact Time:** Water flows through a baffled **contact basin** (or serpentine-channel clearwell) with a design hydraulic retention time (HRT) of **30 – 120 minutes**. 5. **Residual Monitoring:** Inline **amperometric** or **colorimetric** residual chlorine analyzers sample water at the basin outlet. 6. **Feedback Control Loop:** The residual signal (4–20 mA / HART / Foundation Fieldbus) is fed back to the PLC/DCS, which computes the **control error** $e(t) = SP - PV$ and modulates the dosing actuator through a **PID algorithm**. ## 📊 2.2 Breakpoint Chlorination — The Foundational Theory When chlorine is added to water containing natural organic matter (NOM), ammonia (NH₃), and reducing agents, a characteristic **chlorine demand curve** emerges. The **breakpoint** is the critical dose beyond which **free chlorine residual** begins to appear linearly. **Sequential reaction zones:** | Zone | Dose Range (mg/L) | Dominant Species | Practical Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | 1. Initial Demand | 0 – A | Chloramines + organochlorines | Cl₂ consumed by Fe²⁺, Mn²⁺, H₂S, NOM | | 2. Monochloramine Peak | A – B | NH₂Cl (monochloramine) | Disinfection present, but weak | | 3. Dichloramine Ditch | B – C | NHCl₂ (dichloramine) | Taste/odor problems, breakpoint dip | | 4. Breakpoint | **C** | Free Cl₂ begins | **Design operating point** | | 5. Free Residual | > C | HOCl + OCl⁻ | Sustainable disinfection residual | The **chlorine demand** is defined as: $$ D_{\text{Cl}_2} \;=\; \text{Applied Dose} \;-\; \text{Residual Concentration} $$ $$ D_{\text{Cl}_2} \;=\; C_{\text{dose}} - C_{\text{residual}} \quad [\text{mg/L}] $$ ## 🧮 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are examinable in KTU ESE. Memorize the boundary conditions and units.** | # | Equation | Description / Use | |---|---|---| | 1 | $D_{\text{Cl}_2} = C_{\text{dose}} - C_{\text{residual}}$ | Chlorine demand (mg/L) | | 2 | $\text{CT} = C_{\text{residual}} \times T_{\text{contact}}$ | Disinfection credit (mg·min/L) | | 3 | $\log_{10}\text{inactivation} = \dfrac{\text{CT}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{CT}_{\text{required}}}$ | Microbial log reduction model | | 4 | $\dfrac{[\text{HOCl}]}{[\text{OCl}^-]} = 10^{\,(\text{p}K_a - \text{pH})}$ | pH-dependent speciation; $\text{p}K_a = 7.5$ at 25 °C | | 5 | $[\text{HOCl}]_{\%} = \dfrac{100}{1 + 10^{(\text{pH} - 7.5)}}$ | % of free Cl₂ as HOCl | | 6 | $C_{\text{dose}} = D_{\text{Cl}_2} + C_{\text{SP}}$ | Required dose from setpoint + demand | | 7 | $Q_{\text{Cl}_2} = \dfrac{C_{\text{dose}} \cdot Q_{\text{water}}}{C_{\text{solution}}}$ | Mass balance for dosing pump flow | | 8 | $\text{ORP} \propto \log[\text{HOCl}]$ | Oxidation-Reduction Potential relation | | 9 | $V_{\text{basin}} = Q_{\text{water}} \cdot T_{\text{HRT}}$ | Contact basin volume sizing | | 10 | $u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int e(t)\,dt + K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | PID control law for chlorine dosing | > **Where:** $Q_{\text{water}}$ = plant flow (m³/h), $Q_{\text{Cl}_2}$ = chemical feed rate, $C_{\text{solution}}$ = stock solution strength (e.g., 12% = 120,000 mg/L for NaOCl), $C_{\text{SP}}$ = controller setpoint residual (mg/L), $e(t)$ = error signal, $u(t)$ = controller output. ## 🏭 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Chlorination control systems are deployed in: - **Municipal WTPs** (drinking water) — primary disinfection barrier. - **Industrial STPs** (refinery, petrochemical, pharma effluent) — final polishing before discharge. - **Cooling water towers** — biofouling control in large-scale process industries. - **Food & beverage plants** — CIP (Clean-In-Place) and bottle-rinsing water. - **Power plants** — condenser cooling water & boiler makeup treatment. - **Swimming pools & aquaculture** — recreational water safety. Instrumentation is invariably tied to a **SCADA/PLC/DCS** platform (e.g., Siemens PCS 7, ABB 800xA, Honeywell Experion), with signals transmitted via **4–20 mA + HART** or **Foundation Fieldbus**. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 🔬 3.1 Derivation of the HOCl / OCl⁻ Speciation Curve The dissociation of hypochlorous acid in water is governed by: $$ \text{HOCl} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{OCl}^- $$ The equilibrium constant at 25 °C is: $$ K_a = \dfrac{[\text{H}^+][\text{OCl}^-]}{[\text{HOCl}]} = 10^{-7.5} \;\; (\text{since } \text{p}K_a = 7.5) $$ Rearranging for the ratio of conjugate base to acid: $$ \dfrac{[\text{OCl}^-]}{[\text{HOCl}]} = \dfrac{K_a}{[\text{H}^+]} = \dfrac{10^{-7.5}}{10^{-\text{pH}}} = 10^{(\text{pH} - 7.5)} $$ Inverting to obtain the ratio of HOCl (the active species) to OCl⁻: $$ \dfrac{[\text{HOCl}]}{[\text{OCl}^-]} = 10^{(7.5 - \text{pH})} $$ The **mole fraction of HOCl** in the total free chlorine pool ($[\text{HOCl}] + [\text{OCl}^-]$) is: $$ x_{\text{HOCl}} = \dfrac{[\text{HOCl}]}{[\text{HOCl}] + [\text{OCl}^-]} $$ Dividing numerator and denominator by $[\text{OCl}^-]$: $$ x_{\text{HOCl}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{[\text{OCl}^-]}{[\text{HOCl}]}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 10^{(\text{pH} - 7.5)}} $$ Expressed as a percentage: $$ \boxed{\;[\text{HOCl}]_{\%} = \dfrac{100}{1 + 10^{(\text{pH} - 7.5)}}\;} $$ **Numerical verification at pH = 7.0:** $$ [\text{HOCl}]_{\%} = \dfrac{100}{1 + 10^{(7.0 - 7.5)}} = \dfrac{100}{1 + 10^{-0.5}} = \dfrac{100}{1 + 0.3162} = \dfrac{100}{1.3162} $$ $$ [\text{HOCl}]_{\%} \approx 75.98 \% \quad (\text{i.e., } \sim 76\%) $$ Similarly at pH = 8.0: $$ [\text{HOCl}]_{\%} = \dfrac{100}{1 + 10^{(8.0 - 7.5)}} = \dfrac{100}{1 + 10^{0.5}} = \dfrac{100}{1 + 3.162} = \dfrac{100}{4.162} $$ $$ [\text{HOCl}]_{\%} \approx 24.03 \% $$ > **Conclusion of derivation:** A one-unit pH shift from 7.0 → 8.0 causes the active HOCl fraction to collapse from **~76% to ~24%**, demanding roughly a **3-fold increase in chlorine dose** to achieve equivalent disinfection — a classic KTU exam trap. ## 🔬 3.2 Worked Example — CT Compliance & Dose Calculation **Problem Statement:** A municipal WTP treats $Q = 50{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$ of water at pH = 7.2. The chlorine demand of the raw water is determined to be $D_{\text{Cl}_2} = 2.5 \text{ mg/L}$. The plant operator must maintain a free residual of $C_{\text{SP}} = 0.4 \text{ mg/L}$ at the outlet of a baffled contact basin with HRT = 45 minutes. The NaOCl stock solution concentration is 10% w/v (i.e., 100,000 mg/L available Cl₂). Required CT for 3-log *Giardia* inactivation at pH ≤ 7.5 and 25 °C = **179 mg·min/L** (USEPA SWTR default). **Step 1 — Total Applied Dose:** $$ C_{\text{dose}} = D_{\text{Cl}_2} + C_{\text{SP}} = 2.5 + 0.4 = 2.9 \text{ mg/L} $$ **Step 2 — Achieved CT Value:** $$ \text{CT}_{\text{actual}} = C_{\text{residual}} \times T_{\text{contact}} = 0.4 \text{ mg/L} \times 45 \text{ min} = 18 \text{ mg·min/L} $$ **Step 3 — Comparison with USEPA Target:** $$ \text{CT}_{\text{actual}} = 18 \;\;\text{vs.}\;\; \text{CT}_{\text{required}} = 179 $$ The actual CT is **far below** the USEPA requirement. The designer must either: - **Increase residual** to $C_{\text{SP}} = 179 / 45 \approx 3.98$ mg/L (which violates BIS 10500's 1.0 mg/L ceiling). - **Extend contact time** to $T = 179 / 0.4 = 447.5$ min ≈ 7.5 hours (impractical). - **Reduce pH to ~6.5** so the required CT drops substantially (USEPA tables show CT ≈ 99 mg·min/L at pH 6.5). - **Adopt a more potent disinfectant** (e.g., ClO₂, UV, O₃). This trade-off is the **engineering essence** of chlorination design. **Step 4 — NaOCl Pump Flow Rate (Mass Balance):** $$ Q_{\text{Cl}_2} = \dfrac{C_{\text{dose}} \cdot Q_{\text{water}}}{C_{\text{solution}}} $$ Convert $Q_{\text{water}} = 50{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$ to L/h: $$ Q_{\text{water}} = \dfrac{50{,}000 \times 1000}{24} = 2.0833 \times 10^{6} \text{ L/h} $$ $$ Q_{\text{Cl}_2} = \dfrac{2.9 \,(\text{mg/L}) \times 2.0833 \times 10^{6} \,(\text{L/h})}{100{,}000 \,(\text{mg/L})} $$ $$ Q_{\text{Cl}_2} = \dfrac{6.0417 \times 10^{6}}{1.0 \times 10^{5}} = 60.417 \text{ L/h} $$ $$ \boxed{\;Q_{\text{NaOCl}} \approx 60.4 \text{ L/h of 10\% stock solution}\;} $$ ## 🔬 3.3 PID Controller Tuning — Ziegler-Nichols Open-Loop Method For a chlorinator with first-order process response, the **open-loop step test** yields: - Process gain: $K_p = \dfrac{\Delta \text{Residual}}{\Delta \text{Dose}} = \dfrac{0.3 \text{ mg/L}}{1.0 \text{ mg/L}} = 0.3$ - Dead time (transport + sensor): $L = 90 \text{ s}$ - Time constant: $T = 240 \text{ s}$ Applying **Ziegler-Nichols open-loop tuning rules** (reaction curve method): $$ K_c = 1.2 \cdot \dfrac{T}{K_p \cdot L} $$ $$ T_i = 2.0 \cdot L, \quad T_d = 0.5 \cdot L $$ Numerically: $$ K_c = 1.2 \times \dfrac{240}{0.3 \times 90} = 1.2 \times \dfrac{240}{27} = 1.2 \times 8.889 = 10.67 $$ $$ T_i = 180 \text{ s}, \quad T_d = 45 \text{ s} $$ The PID controller law becomes: $$ u(t) = 10.67 \, e(t) + \dfrac{10.67}{180}\int e(t)\,dt + 10.67 \times 45 \cdot \dfrac{de(t)}{dt} $$ $$ u(t) = 10.67 \, e(t) + 0.0593\int e(t)\,dt + 480 \cdot \dfrac{de(t)}{dt} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Field Practice Note:** The integral term is critical for chlorination — without it, a sustained offset between setpoint and residual chlorine will persist due to the variable chlorine demand of raw water. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Closed-Loop Chlorination Control The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **complete feedback control architecture** of an industrial chlorination system, including signal types, controllers, and physical actuators. ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Raw Water Inlet
Flow Q m3 per h] --> B1[Flash Mix Chamber
Hydraulic Mixer] B1 --> C1[Contact Basin
HRT 30 to 120 min] C1 --> D1[Residual Chlorine
Sensor Outlet] D1 --> E1[PLC DCS Controller
PID Algorithm] E1 --> F1[Chlorine Dosing Pump
or Vacuum Feeder] F1 --> B1 D1 --> G1[SCADA HMI
Operator Display] E1 --> G1 H1[pH Sensor
Upstream] --> E1 I1[Flow Transmitter
On Raw Water] --> E1 J1[ORP Probe
Redox Meter] --> E1 K1[Chlorine Tonner
or NaOCl Tank] --> F1 E1 --> L1[Alarm Management
High Low Residual] E1 --> M1[Data Historian
Compliance Log] L1 --> G1 M1 --> G1 E1 -.Setpoint SP.-> E1 E1 -.Error e t.-> E1 E1 -.Output u t 4 to 20 mA.-> F1 ``` **Reading the diagram:** - **Primary feedback path:** D1 (sensor) → E1 (controller) → F1 (actuator) → B1 (process). - **Feedforward inputs** (H1, I1, J1) enable **multivariable control** — the controller can anticipate chlorine demand changes from pH, flow, or ORP disturbances. - **SCADA layer** (G1) provides operator visibility, alarm propagation, and regulatory compliance logging (a mandatory requirement for BIS / WHO / CPCB audits). ## 4.2 Functional Stage Map — Disinfection Cascade ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Stage 1
Pre Chlorination
Oxidation of Fe Mn] --> S2[Stage 2
Coagulation Flocculation] S2 --> S3[Stage 3
Sedimentation] S3 --> S4[Stage 4
Filtration] S4 --> S5[Stage 5
Post Chlorination
Residual Maintenance] S5 --> S6[Stage 6
Contact Basin
CT Compliance] S6 --> S7[Stage 7
Clearwell Storage] S7 --> S8[Stage 8
Distribution Network
Consumer Tap] ``` > **Design principle:** Pre-chlorination (Stage 1) oxidizes iron, manganese, and reduces algal load, while **post-chlorination** (Stage 5) is where the closed-loop residual control is applied for the final disinfection credit. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]** **Define breakpoint chlorination. Why is operation at the breakpoint essential for effective disinfection control?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Breakpoint chlorination** is the process of adding chlorine to water in a dose sufficient to completely oxidize all ammonia, nitrogenous compounds, and organic matter present, **past the chloramine "hump"** on the chlorine demand curve, such that any additional chlorine appears as **free available chlorine (HOCl + OCl⁻)**. The breakpoint is the dose (typically **C**, in mg/L) beyond which the free residual increases linearly with applied dose. Operating **at or beyond the breakpoint** is essential because: 1. **Monochloramines and dichloramines** formed below the breakpoint are **weak disinfectants** (orders of magnitude less effective than HOCl). 2. Below breakpoint, **taste and odor** problems arise from dichloramine and nitrogen trichloride. 3. The **free residual** above the breakpoint provides a **sustainable, measurable, and predictable** disinfection credit for CT compliance. *(Examiner tip: Mere definition without the "why" will fetch only 1/3 marks. Always tie the definition to disinfection efficacy.)* --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]** **Explain why pH control is critical in chlorination. Show the HOCl / OCl⁻ distribution at pH 6.5, 7.5, and 8.5.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The disinfecting power of free chlorine depends almost entirely on the **HOCl (hypochlorous acid)** fraction. HOCl is **80 – 100× more effective** than OCl⁻ as a biocide because the neutral HOCl molecule penetrates microbial cell walls via diffusion, whereas OCl⁻ (charged) is repelled. Using the formula: $$ [\text{HOCl}]_{\%} = \dfrac{100}{1 + 10^{(\text{pH} - 7.5)}} $$ | pH | $[\text{HOCl}]_{\%}$ | $[\text{OCl}^-]_{\%}$ | |---|---|---| | 6.5 | ≈ 90.9 % | ≈ 9.1 % | | 7.5 | ≈ 50.0 % | ≈ 50.0 % | | 8.5 | ≈ 9.1 % | ≈ 90.9 % | **Conclusion:** Higher pH drastically reduces HOCl, **forcing the operator to dose far more chlorine** to compensate, which in turn increases DBP (THM) formation and operating cost. Therefore, large-scale plants install **upstream pH control** (typically acid feed or recarbonation) before the chlorinator. --- ## 📝 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### 🅰️ Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper 2]** **A municipal water treatment plant handles a flow of 25,000 m³/day. The raw water has:** - **pH = 7.8** - **Chlorine demand = 3.0 mg/L** - **Target free chlorine residual at outlet = 0.5 mg/L** - **Contact basin HRT = 60 minutes** - **NaOCl stock solution = 12 % available chlorine (w/v)** **(a)** Calculate the **percentage of HOCl** present in the free chlorine residual at the operating pH. Justify why this is significant for disinfection efficiency. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Determine the **total NaOCl stock solution feed rate in L/h** and verify whether the achieved **CT value** satisfies the USEPA requirement of **CT ≥ 145 mg·min/L** for 3-log *Giardia* inactivation at pH 7.5 – 8.0. If it fails, suggest **two engineering modifications**. **(7 marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Step 1 — Apply the HOCl fraction formula:** $$ [\text{HOCl}]_{\%} = \dfrac{100}{1 + 10^{(\text{pH} - 7.5)}} = \dfrac{100}{1 + 10^{(7.8 - 7.5)}} $$ $$ = \dfrac{100}{1 + 10^{0.3}} = \dfrac{100}{1 + 1.9953} = \dfrac{100}{2.9953} $$ $$ \boxed{\;[\text{HOCl}]_{\%} \approx 33.4\%\;} $$ **Step 2 — Interpretation (significance):** - Only **~33.4%** of the free chlorine exists as the potent HOCl form; the remaining **~66.6%** is the weaker OCl⁻. - Since HOCl is **~80× more effective** than OCl⁻, the effective disinfection strength at pH 7.8 is dramatically reduced. - This **increases the required CT** for the same microbial log inactivation, and operators must compensate with either **higher residual** (subject to BIS 10500 limits) or **longer contact time** (which inflates basin capital cost). **Step 3 — Engineering recommendation:** A **pH depression system** (e.g., CO₂ recarbonation or sulfuric acid dosing) upstream of the chlorinator, targeting pH ≈ 6.8 – 7.2, would raise HOCl fraction to **> 75%** and slash required chlorine dose. *(Marks: 3 for calculation, 2 for interpretation, 2 for recommendation.)* --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Step 1 — Total applied chlorine dose:** $$ C_{\text{dose}} = D_{\text{Cl}_2} + C_{\text{SP}} = 3.0 + 0.5 = 3.5 \text{ mg/L} $$ **Step 2 — Convert water flow to L/h:** $$ Q_{\text{water}} = \dfrac{25{,}000 \times 1000}{24} = 1.0417 \times 10^{6} \text{ L/h} $$ **Step 3 — Stock solution feed rate:** NaOCl concentration = 12 % = 120,000 mg/L. $$ Q_{\text{NaOCl}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{dose}} \cdot Q_{\text{water}}}{C_{\text{solution}}} = \dfrac{3.5 \times 1.0417 \times 10^{6}}{120{,}000} $$ $$ = \dfrac{3.646 \times 10^{6}}{1.2 \times 10^{5}} = 30.38 \text{ L/h} $$ $$ \boxed{\;Q_{\text{NaOCl}} \approx 30.4 \text{ L/h of 12\% stock\;} $$ **Step 4 — Achieved CT value:** $$ \text{CT}_{\text{actual}} = C_{\text{residual}} \times T_{\text{contact}} = 0.5 \times 60 = 30 \text{ mg·min/L} $$ **Step 5 — Compare with USEPA requirement (CT ≥ 145 mg·min/L for 3-log *Giardia* at pH 7.5–8.0):** $$ 30 \text{ mg·min/L} \;\ll\; 145 \text{ mg·min/L} $$ **❌ CT FAILS** — disinfection credit is insufficient. **Step 6 — Engineering modifications (any two):** 1. **pH reduction to ~6.5** — drops required CT from 145 to ~99 mg·min/L (still insufficient alone, but combined with option 2 it works). 2. **Extend HRT to ≥ 290 minutes (~ 5 hours)** — but this is **capital-intensive** (basin enlargement). 3. **Increase residual to 2.5 mg/L** — but **violates BIS 10500's 1.0 mg/L ceiling** for drinking water. 4. **Hybrid disinfection** — add **UV irradiation (40 mJ/cm²)** upstream, which is independent of pH and provides the missing log reduction; chlorine then serves only as a residual maintenance barrier. > [!WARNING] > **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Unit inconsistency** — mixing m³/day with L/h without conversion. KTU examiners deduct **1 full mark** for this. > 2. **Forgetting to apply the 12% strength** in the denominator — students often write $Q = C_{\text{dose}} \cdot Q_{\text{water}}$ directly, which is dimensionally wrong (mg/L × L/h ≠ mg/h). > 3. **Ignoring the pH dependence of USEPA CT tables** — the CT requirement changes by a factor of **3–4×** between pH 6.5 and 8.5. Always quote the pH range. > 4. **No engineering recommendation** in part (b) — KTU 2024 scheme rewards "design thinking" with up to **2 bonus marks** under CO1 (design/development). > 5. **Confusing chloramines (combined chlorine) with free chlorine** in the residual measurement — these are **two different parameters** measured separately. --- ### 🅱️ Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Set B]** **(a)** Draw the **block diagram of a closed-loop chlorination control system** and explain the function of each block — sensor, controller, final control element, and process. List **two online analytical methods** for measuring free chlorine residual. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A chlorinator uses a **PID controller** with $K_c = 8.5$, $T_i = 240$ s, $T_d = 60$ s. The residual chlorine setpoint is **0.5 mg/L** and the measured PV is **0.3 mg/L**. Compute the controller output $u(t)$ given the error history: - $e(t) = 0.2$ mg/L (constant over the past 30 s) - $\dfrac{de}{dt} = 0.02$ mg/L per s Also explain why the **derivative term is often filtered or disabled** in industrial chlorination loops. **(7 marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.1 above):** The four canonical blocks are: 1. **Process** — Flash mixer + contact basin where Cl₂ + water + HOCl/OCl⁻ chemistry occurs. 2. **Sensor (Measurement)** — Amperometric membrane sensor (e.g., Hach CL17, Wallace & Tiernan DEPOLOX 5) or colorimetric DPD analyzer; provides 4–20 mA / HART proportional to $C_{\text{residual}}$. 3. **Controller** — PLC/DCS (Siemens S7-1500 / ABB AC800M) executing the PID law: $$ u(t) = K_c \left[e(t) + \dfrac{1}{T_i}\int e\,dt + T_d\dfrac{de}{dt}\right] $$ 4. **Final Control Element** — Variable-speed metering pump (NaOCl) or vacuum gas feeder (Cl₂) with positioner. **Two online analytical methods for free Cl₂ residual:** 1. **Amperometric Method** — Uses a working electrode (Au or Pt) polarized at a fixed potential; Cl₂ diffusing through a gas-permeable membrane produces a current proportional to concentration. Highly selective, low detection limit (~0.01 mg/L), industry standard. 2. **DPD Colorimetric Method** — N,N-diethyl-p-phenylenediamine (DPD) reacts with free chlorine to form a magenta-colored complex (Würster dye), measured at 515 nm by a photometer. Continuous online variants (e.g., Wallace & Tiernan) use this principle with auto-zero and reagent dosing. *(A third acceptable answer: **ORP (Oxidation-Reduction Potential) measurement** — though it is qualitative, not quantitative, and hence often used as a feedforward signal.)* --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Step 1 — Compute proportional term:** $$ u_P = K_c \cdot e(t) = 8.5 \times 0.2 = 1.70 $$ **Step 2 — Compute integral term (using $\int e\,dt \approx e(t) \cdot \Delta t = 0.2 \times 30 = 6.0$):** $$ u_I = K_c \cdot \dfrac{1}{T_i}\int e\,dt = 8.5 \times \dfrac{6.0}{240} = 8.5 \times 0.025 = 0.2125 $$ **Step 3 — Compute derivative term:** $$ u_D = K_c \cdot T_d \cdot \dfrac{de}{dt} = 8.5 \times 60 \times 0.02 = 8.5 \times 1.2 = 10.2 $$ **Step 4 — Total controller output:** $$ \boxed{\;u(t) = 1.70 + 0.2125 + 10.2 \approx 12.11 \text{ (normalized 0–100\%)}\;} $$ **Step 5 — Interpretation:** - The derivative term **dominates** (10.2 out of 12.11 ≈ 84%) because the noise-amplifying action of $T_d$ on $de/dt$ is unrealistically large here. - In practice, **$T_d$ is heavily filtered** or **set to zero** to prevent the dosing pump from "hunting" on sensor noise. **Why derivative is often disabled in chlorination loops:** 1. **Sensor noise:** Amperometric free Cl₂ sensors have inherent electrical noise; the derivative amplifies this into erratic pump modulation. 2. **Slow process dynamics:** Chlorination has long dead times (60 – 120 s); the derivative reacts to short-term noise that has no bearing on actual demand. 3. **Actuator wear:** Modulating a chemical dosing pump at high frequency causes diaphragm fatigue, seal wear, and premature failure. 4. **Stability:** PI-only control is sufficient and provides smoother, more robust setpoint tracking for chemical dosing applications. *(Marks: 4 for numerical computation, 3 for the qualitative reasoning.)* --- ## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **🔥 High-Frequency KTU Exam Points — Memorize These:** - ⭐ **Chlorine demand:** $D_{\text{Cl}_2} = C_{\text{dose}} - C_{\text{residual}}$ — fundamental mass-balance identity. - ⭐ **CT concept** — USEPA's primary metric for crediting disinfection; **higher pH → higher CT required**. - ⭐ **HOCl fraction formula:** $[\text{HOCl}]_{\%} = \dfrac{100}{1 + 10^{(\text{pH} - 7.5)}}$ — appears in **almost every KTU exam** as a calculation or interpretation. - ⭐ **WHO residual limits:** **0.2 – 0.5 mg/L** (consumer tap); **≤ 1.0 mg/L** (plant outlet, BIS 10500). - ⭐ **Breakpoint chlorination** is essential — operation below breakpoint yields chloramines (weak) + taste/odor issues. - ⭐ **Sensor choice:** Amperometric = selective & accurate; DPD = colorimetric & reliable; ORP = qualitative proxy. - ⭐ **PID for chlorination** is almost always **PI-only** in practice — derivative is disabled due to noise and actuator wear. - ⭐ **Mass-balance equation** for dosing pump: $$ Q_{\text{chem}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{dose}} \cdot Q_{\text{water}}}{C_{\text{stock}}} $$ **Unit conversion (m³/day → L/h) by multiplying by $10^3 / 24$** is the #1 mark-loss area. - ⭐ **Pre-chlorination** oxidizes Fe/Mn/organics; **post-chlorination** maintains residual for distribution network. - ⭐ **DBP formation** (THMs, HAAs) is the **key trade-off** — over-dosing chlorinates NOM into carcinogens. - ⭐ **Multivariable control** uses pH + flow + ORP as feedforward signals to anticipate chlorine demand variability. - ⭐ **Industrial standards:** IS 10500:2012 (drinking water), USEPA SWTR (CT tables), WHO Guidelines 4th Ed.

topics.title: Evaporator Terminology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT864 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN LARGE SCALE INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Evaporator Terminology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of an Evaporator

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Module 2 — Evaporator Terminology)**
> An **Evaporator** is a heat-transfer unit operation device used in large-scale process industries in which a volatile solvent (almost always water) is vaporized from a non-volatile solute-bearing liquid feed, producing a concentrated thick liquor and a solvent vapor stream, by supplying thermal energy indirectly through a heating medium (typically steam) across a metallic heat-transfer surface.

**Evaporator Terminology** refers to the standardized vocabulary, performance metrics, operating parameters, and design quantities that uniquely describe the operating behavior, capacity, and thermodynamic efficiency of an industrial evaporator. These terms form the **linguistic backbone** of evaporator design, rating, control, and troubleshooting in process industries such as sugar refining, caustic/chlor-alkali, dairy (milk concentration), paper & pulp (black liquor), desalination, fertilizers, and pharmaceutical bulk drugs.

---

## 1.2 Key Terminology — The "Vocabulary Card"

The following is the canonical list of terms every KTU 2024 examiner expects you to know verbatim:

| # | Term | Formal Definition | Typical Units (SI) |
|---|------|-------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Feed (F)** | Dilute solution entering the evaporator | kg/h |
| 2 | **Thick Liquor / Concentrate (L)** | Concentrated solution leaving the evaporator | kg/h |
| 3 | **Vapor (V)** | Solvent vapor (steam) released from the boiling feed | kg/h |
| 4 | **Condensate (C)** | Condensed heating steam leaving the steam chest | kg/h |
| 5 | **Heating Steam (S)** | Live steam supplied to the steam chest | kg/h |
| 6 | **Capacity** | Quantity of water (solvent) evaporated per unit time | kg/h or ton/day |
| 7 | **Economy (Steam Economy)** | kg of water evaporated per kg of live steam consumed | kg water / kg steam (dimensionless ratio) |
| 8 | **Boiling Point Elevation (BPE)** | Difference between solution boiling point and pure water boiling point at the same pressure | °C or K |
| 9 | **Effective Temperature Difference (ΔT_eff)** | Net driving force available for heat transfer | °C or K |
| 10 | **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U)** | Combined resistance to heat transfer across the heating surface | W/(m²·K) |
| 11 | **Heat Transfer Area (A)** | Effective wetted heat-transfer surface | m² |
| 12 | **Vapor Fraction (x)** | Weight fraction of solute in a stream | — (dimensionless) |
| 13 | **Vapor Pressure** | Partial pressure exerted by vapor at equilibrium | Pa or bar |
| 14 | **Sensible Heat** | Heat causing temperature change without phase change | kJ/kg |
| 15 | **Latent Heat (λ)** | Heat absorbed during phase change at constant temperature | kJ/kg |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Tea-Cooking Pan"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Intuition Builder)**
> Imagine you are reducing milk on a gas stove to make **khoya** (thickened milk solids) for a sweet. The stove flame supplies **heating steam equivalent** ($S$); the raw milk is your **feed** ($F$); water bubbling off as steam over the pan is your **vapor** ($V$); and the dense, creamy residue left behind is your **thick liquor** ($L$).
>
> - The **faster you boil**, the higher your **capacity** — but you waste fuel.
> - The **litres of water boiled away per litre of milk** is your **economy**.
> - If you add sugar, the boiling point of the mixture rises (sugar-water boils at >100 °C at 1 atm) — that rise is the **Boiling Point Elevation (BPE)**.
> - The metal pan conducting heat is your **heat transfer area ($A$)** with a certain **overall heat-transfer coefficient ($U$)**.
>
> This is exactly what happens inside a giant industrial evaporator — only it is **engineered**, instrumented, and controlled to within ±0.5 °C.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Latent heat of vaporization of water at 100 °C, 1 atm**: $\lambda = 2257 \text{ kJ/kg}$
> - **Standard capacity unit in sugar industry**: **1 ton of water evaporated per hour**
> - **Standard economy benchmark (Single-Effect Evaporator)**: ≈ **0.85 – 1.0 kg water / kg steam**
> - **Standard economy benchmark (Triple-Effect Evaporator)**: ≈ **2.5 – 3.0 kg water / kg steam**
> - **Typical U for natural-circulation evaporators**: **1500 – 3000 W/(m²·K)**
> - **Typical U for forced-circulation evaporators**: **3000 – 6000 W/(m²·K)**

---

## 1.5 Why These Terms Matter in Large-Scale Industries

Evaporator terminology is **not academic jargon** — every term maps directly to a **measurable variable on a DCS/PLC tag database**:

| Industrial Term | Field Instrument / DCS Tag |
|-----------------|----------------------------|
| Feed flow $F$ | Magnetic flowmeter + temperature transmitter |
| Vapor flow $V$ | Orifice plate + DP transmitter |
| Steam flow $S$ | Vortex flowmeter |
| Boiling Point Elevation | Calculated from PT + TT pair on liquor line |
| Heat Transfer Coefficient $U$ | Calculated in DCS via online energy balance |
| Thick liquor concentration $x_L$ | Density meter / refractive index / conductivity probe |

> A **control engineer** who does not know "economy" and "BPE" cannot tune the **pressure cascade** or **feed-forward ratio control** of a multiple-effect evaporator. These terms are therefore **mission-critical** for KTU 2024 instrumentation graduates.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Fundamental Balances of an Evaporator

Every evaporator — single or multi-effect — obeys three conservation laws. These must be mastered before any formula is applied.

### 2.1.1 Material (Mass) Balance — Total

For a single effect operating at **steady state** with no accumulation:

$$F = V + L$$

Where:
- $F$ = mass flow rate of dilute feed (kg/h)
- $V$ = mass flow rate of solvent vapor leaving (kg/h)
- $L$ = mass flow rate of thick liquor leaving (kg/h)

### 2.1.2 Material (Mass) Balance — Solute (Solids) Only

Since solute does **not** vaporize, all solute entering must leave in the liquor:

$$F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_L$$

Where $x_F$ and $x_L$ are the mass fractions of solute in feed and thick liquor respectively.

Combining the two balances gives the **vapor flow** equation (a KTU favourite):

$$V = F \left( 1 - \dfrac{x_F}{x_L} \right)$$

### 2.1.3 Energy (Heat) Balance

Energy in (from steam + sensible heat of feed) = Energy out (latent heat of vapor + sensible heat of liquor + condensate leaving)

$$S \cdot \lambda_S + F \cdot h_F = V \cdot \lambda_V + L \cdot h_L + S \cdot h_C$$

Where:
- $\lambda_S$ = latent heat of heating steam (≈ 2200–2260 kJ/kg for low-pressure steam)
- $h_F$ = specific enthalpy of feed (kJ/kg)
- $\lambda_V$ = latent heat of vapor produced
- $h_L$ = specific enthalpy of thick liquor leaving
- $h_C$ = specific enthalpy of condensate (≈ saturated liquid at steam pressure)

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Reasoning for the Core Relationships

### Step 1 — Why is Capacity defined by water evaporated and not feed?
Because **industry cares about water removal**, not feed processed. Two different feeds can require the same capacity if they contain the same mass of water to be boiled off.

### Step 2 — Why is Economy important?
Steam is the **single largest operating cost** in an evaporator plant. Economy directly translates to **fuel bill**. A plant that doubles economy halves its steam bill — a multi-million-rupee saving annually.

### Step 3 — Why BPE matters in design?
BPE **reduces the effective temperature driving force** $\Delta T$. If we ignore BPE, we will undersize the heat-transfer area $A$. The corrected driving force is:

$$\Delta T_{\text{eff}} = T_S - T_1 - \text{BPE} - \text{Fouling} - \text{Hydrostatic head correction}$$

Where $T_S$ is steam saturation temperature and $T_1$ is the secondary-side boiling temperature.

### Step 4 — Why multiple effects?
A single-effect evaporator discards the vapor's latent heat to the condenser (waste). By feeding the vapor of effect 1 into the steam chest of effect 2 (operated at lower pressure), that latent heat **re-evaporates** more water. Hence economy multiplies with effects.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Board-Exam Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas — Memorize Before Every ESE Attempt**

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | $F = V + L$ | Total mass balance | Always |
| 2 | $F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_L$ | Solute mass balance | Always |
| 3 | $V = F \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{x_F}{x_L}\right)$ | Vapor flow | Capacity calculations |
| 4 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat transfer rate | Area rating |
| 5 | $Q = S \cdot \lambda_S$ | Heat supplied by steam | Heat balance |
| 6 | $Q = V \cdot \lambda_V$ | Heat absorbed by vaporization | Heat balance |
| 7 | $E = \dfrac{V}{S}$ | Steam economy | Performance eval |
| 8 | $\text{BPE} = T_{b,\text{solution}} - T_{b,\text{water}}$ | Boiling Point Elevation | $\Delta T$ correction |
| 9 | $A = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{\text{eff}}}$ | Required heat-transfer area | Sizing |
| 10 | $\Delta T_{\text{eff}} = T_S - T_1 - \sum \text{BPE} - \text{corrections}$ | Net driving force | Multi-effect design |
| 11 | $V_i = F \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{x_F}{x_L}\right)$ (per effect $i$) | Vapor per effect | Material balance |
| 12 | $S_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{total}}}{E_{\text{overall}}}$ | Steam required (multi-effect) | Energy balance |
| 13 | $h_{\text{feed}} = C_{p,F} \cdot (T_F - 0 °C)$ | Feed sensible heat | Heat balance |
| 14 | $h_{\text{liquor}} = C_{p,L} \cdot (T_L - 0 °C)$ | Liquor sensible heat | Heat balance |

> **Memory Tip:** The three most-asked formulas are #3 (vapor flow), #4 (heat transfer rate), and #7 (economy). Together they appear in **over 70 %** of KTU ESE questions on this module.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Sugar Industry**: Multiple-effect evaporators use these formulas to crystallize sucrose economically — economy dictates whether a 5-effect or 6-effect train is justified.
- **Caustic-Chlor Alkali**: Evaporator terminology drives the selection of **falling-film vs forced-circulation** designs for NaOH concentration (highly viscous).
- **Dairy Plants**: BPE for lactose-rich milk (~0.5 °C at 50 % TS) is critical to **pasteurization-grade** evaporators.
- **Desalination**: Multi-stage flash (MSF) and Multiple-Effect Distillation (MED) plants are **direct industrial cousins** of evaporator design — same formulas, larger scale.
- **Instrumentation**: A modern evaporator DCS uses #3, #4, #7 in **real-time MPC (Model Predictive Control)** loops to optimize feed-forward steam ratio.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly. No steps are skipped. Use this section as a worked-example workbook for ESE preparation.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Vapor Flow Rate from Material Balance

### Statement
Given feed rate $F$ and concentrations $x_F$ and $x_L$, derive an expression for the vapor flow rate $V$.

### Given
- $F$ = 10,000 kg/h (feed)
- $x_F$ = 0.10 (10 wt% solute)
- $x_L$ = 0.50 (50 wt% solute)

### Step 1 — Write the total mass balance

$$F = V + L$$

Solving for $L$:

$$L = F - V$$

### Step 2 — Write the solute mass balance

Solutes do not vaporize; all solute in feed must exit in thick liquor:

$$F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_L$$

### Step 3 — Substitute $L = F - V$ into the solute balance

$$F \cdot x_F = (F - V) \cdot x_L$$

### Step 4 — Expand the right-hand side

$$F \cdot x_F = F \cdot x_L - V \cdot x_L$$

### Step 5 — Isolate $V \cdot x_L$

$$V \cdot x_L = F \cdot x_L - F \cdot x_F$$

### Step 6 — Factor out $F$

$$V \cdot x_L = F \cdot (x_L - x_F)$$

### Step 7 — Divide both sides by $x_L$

$$\boxed{\,V = F \cdot \dfrac{x_L - x_F}{x_L} = F \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{x_F}{x_L}\right)\,}$$

### Step 8 — Numerical evaluation

$$V = 10{,}000 \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{0.10}{0.50}\right)$$

$$V = 10{,}000 \cdot (1 - 0.20)$$

$$V = 10{,}000 \cdot 0.80$$

$$\boxed{\,V = 8{,}000 \text{ kg/h}\,}$$

### Step 9 — Liquor flow rate

$$L = F - V = 10{,}000 - 8{,}000 = 2{,}000 \text{ kg/h}$$

### Verification (solute check)

$$\text{Solute in feed} = 10{,}000 \cdot 0.10 = 1{,}000 \text{ kg/h}$$
$$\text{Solute in liquor} = 2{,}000 \cdot 0.50 = 1{,}000 \text{ kg/h} \checkmark$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Heat-Transfer Area (Sizing Equation)

### Statement
Derive the expression for required heat-transfer area $A$ given the heat duty, overall coefficient, and effective temperature difference.

### Given
- $Q$ = 5 × 10⁶ kJ/h (heat duty)
- $U$ = 2000 W/(m²·K) = 7200 kJ/(h·m²·K) [unit conversion: × 3.6]
- $\Delta T_{\text{eff}}$ = 25 °C (after BPE corrections)

### Step 1 — Start from Fourier's conduction analog for evaporators

Heat transferred through the heating surface obeys:

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{\text{eff}}$$

### Step 2 — Solve for $A$

$$A = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{\text{eff}}}$$

### Step 3 — Plug in numerical values (in consistent units)

Convert $Q$ to W: $Q = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ kJ/h} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^6}{3.6} \text{ kW} = 1{,}388{,}889 \text{ W}$

$$A = \dfrac{1{,}388{,}889}{2000 \cdot 25}$$

$$A = \dfrac{1{,}388{,}889}{50{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{\,A = 27.78 \text{ m}^2\,}$$

### Step 4 — Practical design addition
Add **15 % oversize margin** for fouling and future capacity:

$$A_{\text{design}} = 27.78 \cdot 1.15 \approx 32 \text{ m}^2$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Steam Economy and Its Theoretical Maximum

### Statement
For a single-effect evaporator with negligible BPE and assuming feed enters at the boiling temperature, derive the maximum theoretical steam economy.

### Step 1 — Heat balance (feed at boiling point ⇒ $h_F = 0$ sensible)

$$S \cdot \lambda_S = V \cdot \lambda_V$$

(Assuming condensate leaves at saturation: $h_C \approx 0$ referenced; liquor sensible heat ≈ feed sensible heat)

### Step 2 — Substitute $E = V / S$

$$S \cdot \lambda_S = (E \cdot S) \cdot \lambda_V$$

$$\lambda_S = E \cdot \lambda_V$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $E$

$$E_{\max} = \dfrac{\lambda_S}{\lambda_V}$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate (at 100 °C, $\lambda_S \approx \lambda_V \approx 2257$ kJ/kg)

$$E_{\max} \approx 1.0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion**: A single-effect evaporator **cannot exceed unity economy** under ideal conditions. This is the thermodynamic ceiling. Multi-effect evaporators exceed 1.0 by reusing vapor latent heat.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Multiple-Effect Steam Economy (Additive Approximation)

### Statement
Show that for a well-insulated $N$-effect evaporator, the overall economy is approximately $N$ times the single-effect economy.

### Step 1 — Vapor in each effect
For dilute solution, $V_i \approx V_1 / \text{effect ratio}$. For equal feed distribution:

$$V_{\text{total}} = V_1 + V_2 + \cdots + V_N$$

### Step 2 — Steam input to first effect only
Steam $S_1$ enters effect 1. Subsequent effects are heated by vapor of previous effect:

$$\text{Steam input} = S_1$$

### Step 3 — Overall economy definition

$$E_{\text{overall}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{total}}}{S_1}$$

### Step 4 — For equal effects (ideal case)

Each effect evaporates the same amount; $V_i = V_1$ for all $i$. Then:

$$V_{\text{total}} = N \cdot V_1$$

And for each effect the single-effect economy gives $V_1 \approx S_1 \cdot \lambda_S / \lambda_V$:

$$E_{\text{overall}} = \dfrac{N \cdot S_1 \cdot \lambda_S / \lambda_V}{S_1} = N \cdot \dfrac{\lambda_S}{\lambda_V}$$

$$\boxed{\,E_{\text{overall, ideal}} \approx N\,}$$

### Step 5 — Realistic industry value (with losses, BPE, sensible heat)

$$E_{\text{real}} \approx 0.75 \cdot N \text{ to } 0.85 \cdot N$$

| No. of Effects ($N$) | Ideal Economy | Real-World Economy |
|----------------------|---------------|---------------------|
| 1 | 1.0 | 0.85 – 0.95 |
| 2 | 2.0 | 1.7 – 1.8 |
| 3 | 3.0 | 2.5 – 2.7 |
| 4 | 4.0 | 3.3 – 3.5 |
| 5 | 5.0 | 4.0 – 4.3 |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Cause-Effect Map for Each Terminology

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is the **reverse-engineering tool** for KTU MCQs. Given a change in one variable, predict what happens to all others.

| If this parameter **increases** ↓ | Capacity ($V$) | Economy ($E$) | Area ($A$) | $\Delta T_{\text{eff}}$ |
|----------------------------------|----------------|---------------|------------|--------------------------|
| Feed concentration $x_F$         | Decreases      | No direct change | No change | No change |
| Product concentration $x_L$      | Increases      | Increases (slightly) | No change | No change |
| Steam pressure ($T_S$ up)        | Increases      | No change      | Decreases (less area needed) | **Increases** |
| Vacuum (lower $P_1$)             | Increases      | No change      | Decreases  | **Increases** |
| Feed temperature $T_F$           | Slight increase | **Increases** (uses feed sensible heat) | No change | No change |
| BPE                              | No change      | **Decreases**  | **Increases** (less $\Delta T$) | **Decreases** |
| Number of effects $N$            | No change      | **Increases**  | **Increases** (more vessels) | Distributed across effects |
| Fouling on tubes                 | Decreases      | Decreases      | No change  | No change (but $U$ falls) |

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical — Capacity, Economy, and Heat-Transfer Area Combined

**Given:**
- Feed $F$ = 20,000 kg/h, $x_F$ = 0.08, entering at 35 °C
- Product thick liquor $x_L$ = 0.40, leaving at 105 °C
- Steam supplied at 1.5 bar abs (saturated, $\lambda_S = 2226$ kJ/kg)
- Boiling point of solution in evaporator = 100 °C (BPE = 0 °C, pure water base)
- Overall heat transfer coefficient $U$ = 2500 W/(m²·K)
- Steam temperature $T_S$ = 111.4 °C; solution boiling $T_b$ = 100 °C

**Step A — Vapor flow**

$$V = 20{,}000 \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{0.08}{0.40}\right) = 20{,}000 \cdot 0.80 = 16{,}000 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step B — Heat duty** (heat supplied by steam to generate $V$)

$$Q = V \cdot \lambda_V \approx 16{,}000 \cdot 2260 = 3.616 \times 10^7 \text{ kJ/h}$$

**Step C — Steam required** (single effect)

$$S = \dfrac{Q}{\lambda_S} = \dfrac{3.616 \times 10^7}{2226} \approx 16{,}244 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step D — Economy**

$$E = \dfrac{V}{S} = \dfrac{16{,}000}{16{,}244} \approx 0.985$$

**Step E — $\Delta T_{\text{eff}}$**

$$\Delta T_{\text{eff}} = T_S - T_b = 111.4 - 100 = 11.4 \text{ °C}$$

**Step F — Heat-transfer area**

$$Q = 3.616 \times 10^7 \text{ kJ/h} = 1.004 \times 10^7 \text{ W}$$

$$A = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T} = \dfrac{1.004 \times 10^7}{2500 \cdot 11.4}$$

$$A \approx 352 \text{ m}^2$$

---

## 3.7 Interpretation Summary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key takeaways from derivation exercises:**
> 1. **Vapor flow depends only on concentration ratio** — not on temperature, not on steam pressure.
> 2. **Heat duty depends on vapor flow and latent heat** — independent of $\Delta T$.
> 3. **Steam required depends on heat duty and steam latent heat** — high-pressure steam (high $\lambda_S$ effect slightly counter-intuitive) is more efficient per kg.
> 4. **Area depends inversely on $\Delta T$** — to reduce area, increase steam pressure or increase vacuum.
> 5. **Economy is fundamentally limited to ≈ 1.0 for single effect** — multi-effect is the only way to exceed this ceiling.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Single-Effect Evaporator with Mass & Energy Streams

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the **process-level block architecture** of a single-effect evaporator with all terminology mapped to instrumentation tags. This is the type of diagram KTU examiners expect in 14-mark design questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[FEED TANK<br/>F kg per h<br/>xF concentration] --> B1[FEED PUMP<br/>P101]
    B1 --> B2[FEED PREHEATER<br/>E101]
    B2 --> C1[EVAPORATOR VESSEL<br/>V101<br/>includes steam chest]
    H1[STEAM HEADER<br/>S kg per h<br/>lambda S] --> C1
    C1 --> D1[VAPOR OUTLET<br/>V kg per h<br/>lambda V]
    D1 --> E1[CONDENSER<br/>E102]
    E1 --> F1[VACUUM SYSTEM<br/>P102]
    C1 --> G1[THICK LIQUOR OUTLET<br/>L kg per h<br/>xL concentration]
    C1 --> H2[CONDENSATE OUTLET<br/>C kg per h]

    classDef inputStream fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef unit fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef outputStream fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2d5016,color:#000
    class A1,H1 inputStream
    class B1,B2,C1,E1,F1 unit
    class D1,G1,H2 outputStream
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Blue boxes (input streams):** Feed and steam entering the system.
- **Orange boxes (process units):** Pumps, preheater, evaporator vessel, condenser, vacuum pump.
- **Green boxes (output streams):** Vapor (to condenser), thick liquor (to storage), condensate (returned to boiler).

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Mass and Energy Balance Around the Evaporator Boundary

This block diagram shows **how each terminological variable is computed** from process measurements. It is essential for **control valve sizing and DCS loop tuning**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[FLOW TRANSMITTER<br/>FT 101 on Feed Line] --> SUM1[MASS BALANCE SUMMATION<br/>F = V + L]
    M2[FLOW TRANSMITTER<br/>FT 102 on Vapor Line] --> SUM1
    M3[FLOW TRANSMITTER<br/>FT 103 on Liquor Line] --> SUM1
    SUM1 --> X1[VAPOR RATE V<br/>kg per h]

    M4[CONDUCTIVITY METER<br/>QT 101 on Feed] --> SUM2[SOLUTE BALANCE<br/>F xF = L xL]
    M5[CONDUCTIVITY METER<br/>QT 102 on Liquor] --> SUM2
    SUM2 --> X2[PRODUCT CONCENTRATION xL<br/>dimensionless]

    M6[PRESSURE TX<br/>PT 101 Steam Chest] --> SUM3[ENERGY BALANCE<br/>S lambda S equals V lambda V]
    M7[TEMPERATURE TX<br/>TT 101 Feed] --> SUM3
    M8[TEMPERATURE TX<br/>TT 102 Liquor] --> SUM3
    SUM3 --> X3[HEAT DUTY Q<br/>kW]

    X1 --> Y1[CAPACITY<br/>tons water per day]
    X3 --> Y2[ECONOMY E<br/>V divided by S]
    X3 --> Y3[AREA A<br/>Q over U delta T]

    classDef instr fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000
    classDef calc fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2d5016,color:#000
    class M1,M2,M3,M4,M5,M6,M7,M8 instr
    class SUM1,SUM2,SUM3,X1,X2,X3 calc
    class Y1,Y2,Y3 output
```

## 4.3 Architecture Diagram — Multi-Effect Evaporator Cascade

The multi-effect cascade is the **most-tested configuration** in KTU. The diagram below traces vapor reuse across effects.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[STEAM SUPPLY<br/>S kg per h] --> E1[EFFECT 1<br/>P1 high<br/>T1 high]
    E1 --> V1[VAPOR 1]
    V1 --> E2[EFFECT 2<br/>P2 less than P1<br/>T2 less than T1]
    E2 --> V2[VAPOR 2]
    V2 --> E3[EFFECT 3<br/>P3 less than P2<br/>T3 less than T2]
    E3 --> V3[VAPOR 3]
    V3 --> ECON[CONDENSER<br/>E condensing]
    ECON --> VAC[VACUUM PUMP<br/>maintains P3]
    E1 --> L1[THICK LIQUOR<br/>to next effect]
    L1 --> E2
    E2 --> L2
    L2 --> E3
    E3 --> LFINAL[FINAL CONCENTRATE<br/>xL final]
    FEED[FEED F xF] --> E1

    classDef steam fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef vessel fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2d5016,color:#000
    class S0,FEED steam
    class E1,E2,E3,ECON vessel
    class V1,V2,V3,L1,L2,LFINAL,VAC output
```

**Critical concept shown:**
- Steam enters only Effect 1. Vapors from each effect become the heating medium of the next effect.
- Pressure decreases from $P_1$ (highest) to $P_3$ (lowest, under vacuum).
- This pressure cascade gives the **temperature driving force** across each effect.
- Overall economy ≈ 3.0 (triple effect) vs 1.0 (single effect).

---
