```mermaid
    TC1[TC LOOP Liquor Outlet Temperature<br/>TT on thick liquor] --> CV3[Control Valve on Vapor Line]
    QC1[QC LOOP Thick Liquor Concentration<br/>Density or conductivity meter] --> CV4[Control Valve on Condensate]
    FC1[FC LOOP Steam Flow<br/>FT on steam chest] --> CV5[Control Valve on Blowdown]

    LC1 -.FEEDBACK.- LC1
    PC1 -.FEEDBACK.- PC1
    TC1 -.FEEDBACK.- TC1
    QC1 -.FEEDBACK.- QC1
    FC1 -.FEEDBACK.- FC1

    classDef loop fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#660066,color:#000
    classDef valve fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000
    class LC1,PC1,TC1,QC1,FC1 loop
    class CV1,CV2,CV3,CV4,CV5 valve
```

**Why this matters for KTU 2024:**
The examiner will ask you to **identify which loop controls which terminological variable**. Memorize this mapping — it appears in at least one sub-question of every 14-mark question.

| Loop Tag | Controlled Variable | Terminology Mapped |
|----------|---------------------|--------------------|
| LC | Vessel level | Capacity / Boil-up rate |
| PC | Steam chest pressure | Driving force ($\Delta T$) |
| TC | Liquor temperature | BPE / Boiling state |
| QC | Liquor concentration | Product specification ($x_L$) |
| FC | Steam flow | Economy ($E$) |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set A]**
Define the following terms used in evaporator terminology:
**(i) Capacity, (ii) Steam Economy, (iii) Boiling Point Elevation (BPE).**

#### Model Answer (3 marks — write exactly this for full marks):

1. **Capacity**: The capacity of an evaporator is defined as the quantity of water (solvent) evaporated per unit time. It is expressed in **kg/h** or **tons/day**. Mathematically:
$$V = F \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{x_F}{x_L}\right)$$

2. **Steam Economy ($E$)**: It is the ratio of the mass of water evaporated to the mass of live steam supplied to the evaporator. It is a **dimensionless** quantity:
$$E = \dfrac{V}{S}$$

3. **Boiling Point Elevation (BPE)**: It is the difference between the boiling point of the solution and the boiling point of pure water at the same pressure. It arises due to dissolved solutes lowering the vapor pressure. Units: **°C** or **K**.
$$\text{BPE} = T_{b,\text{solution}} - T_{b,\text{water}}$$

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Distinguish between **single-effect** and **multiple-effect** evaporators with respect to steam economy.

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

| Feature | Single Effect | Multiple Effect |
|---------|---------------|-----------------|
| Steam Economy | ≈ 0.85 – 1.0 | ≈ $N$ × (single-effect economy) for $N$ effects |
| Capital cost | Low | High |
| Steam consumption | High | Low |
| Vapor usage | Discarded to condenser | Reused as heating medium |
| Typical application | Small scale, batch | Large continuous plants |

In a single-effect evaporator, the vapor produced is condensed and discarded, so economy cannot exceed 1.0. In multiple-effect evaporators, the vapor from one effect serves as the heating medium of the next, so economy approaches the number of effects (e.g., triple-effect gives economy ≈ 2.5–3.0).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> Common mistakes students make in 14-mark questions on this topic:
> 1. **Confusing total mass balance with solute mass balance** — leads to wrong sign on $V$ equation. Always verify solute in = solute out.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units** between kJ/h and W (÷3.6 or ×3.6).
> 3. **Ignoring BPE** in $\Delta T$ calculation — costs 2–3 marks instantly.
> 4. **Mixing up Capacity and Economy** — Capacity is kg/h, Economy is dimensionless ratio.
> 5. **Using $\lambda$ of water at 100 °C for all pressures** — latent heat varies with pressure. Use steam tables for accuracy.
> 6. **Not stating units** in final answer — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2 Q3]**

A single-effect evaporator is used to concentrate a sugar solution from **8 wt%** to **40 wt%** solids. The feed rate is **15,000 kg/h** at a temperature of **30 °C**. The evaporator operates at **atmospheric pressure** with saturated steam at **120 °C** supplied to the steam chest. The boiling point of the solution in the evaporator is **102 °C** (due to dissolved solids). The overall heat transfer coefficient is **2400 W/(m²·K)**.

Take: $C_{p,\text{feed}} = C_{p,\text{liquor}} = 4.0 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$, $\lambda_S = 2205 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $\lambda_V = 2257 \text{ kJ/kg}$.

Calculate:
**(a)** The capacity of the evaporator (in kg/h of water evaporated) and the rate of thick liquor production. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The steam consumption rate, the steam economy, and the heat-transfer area required. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Capacity & Liquor Rate (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Apply total mass balance** (1 mark)
$$F = V + L$$

**Step 2 — Apply solute mass balance** (2 marks)
$$F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_L$$
$$15{,}000 \cdot 0.08 = L \cdot 0.40$$
$$L = \dfrac{1{,}200}{0.40} = 3{,}000 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 3 — Compute vapor flow (capacity)** (2 marks)
$$V = F - L = 15{,}000 - 3{,}000 = 12{,}000 \text{ kg/h}$$

Or via direct formula:
$$V = 15{,}000 \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{0.08}{0.40}\right) = 15{,}000 \cdot 0.80 = 12{,}000 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 4 — Effective ΔT** (BPE correction) (2 marks)
$$\text{BPE} = 102 - 100 = 2 \text{ °C}$$
$$\Delta T_{\text{eff}} = 120 - 102 = 18 \text{ °C}$$

**Part (b) — Steam, Economy, Area (7 marks)**

**Step 5 — Heat duty via steam** (3 marks)
$$Q = S \cdot \lambda_S = V \cdot \lambda_V + \text{sensible heat correction}$$

Sensible heat of feed = $F \cdot C_p \cdot (T_b - T_F) = 15{,}000 \cdot 4.0 \cdot (102 - 30) = 4.32 \times 10^6 \text{ kJ/h}$
Sensible heat of liquor = $L \cdot C_p \cdot (T_b - 0) = 3{,}000 \cdot 4.0 \cdot 102 = 1.224 \times 10^6 \text{ kJ/h}$

So heat balance:
$$S \cdot 2205 + 15{,}000 \cdot 4.0 \cdot 30 = 12{,}000 \cdot 2257 + 3{,}000 \cdot 4.0 \cdot 102$$

$$S \cdot 2205 + 1.8 \times 10^6 = 27.084 \times 10^6 + 1.224 \times 10^6 = 28.308 \times 10^6$$

$$S \cdot 2205 = 26.508 \times 10^6$$

$$S = \dfrac{26.508 \times 10^6}{2205} \approx 12{,}021 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 6 — Steam economy** (1 mark)
$$E = \dfrac{V}{S} = \dfrac{12{,}000}{12{,}021} \approx 0.998 \approx 1.0$$

**Step 7 — Heat-transfer area** (3 marks)
Convert $Q$ to watts:
$$Q = 26.508 \times 10^6 \text{ kJ/h} = \dfrac{26.508 \times 10^6}{3.6} \text{ W} = 7.363 \times 10^6 \text{ W} = 7363 \text{ kW}$$

$$A = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{\text{eff}}} = \dfrac{7.363 \times 10^6}{2400 \cdot 18} = \dfrac{7.363 \times 10^6}{43{,}200}$$

$$\boxed{A \approx 170.5 \text{ m}^2}$$

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 Q4]**

A triple-effect evaporator concentrates a weak liquor from **5 wt%** to **40 wt%** solids. The feed enters at **40 °C** at a rate of **30,000 kg/h**. Saturated steam at **2.0 bar abs** is supplied to the first effect. The overall heat transfer coefficients for the three effects are $U_1 = 2800$, $U_2 = 2400$, $U_3 = 2000 \text{ W/(m²·K)}$ respectively. The corresponding effective temperature differences are $\Delta T_1 = 14$ °C, $\Delta T_2 = 13$ °C, $\Delta T_3 = 12$ °C. Assume $\lambda_S \approx \lambda_V \approx 2250 \text{ kJ/kg}$.

Calculate:
**(a)** The total capacity (kg/h of water evaporated) and the rate of thick liquor. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The total steam consumption, overall steam economy, and the heat-transfer areas of each effect. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Capacity & Liquor (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Vapor flow rate from overall material balance** (3 marks)
$$V_{\text{total}} = F \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{x_F}{x_L}\right) = 30{,}000 \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{0.05}{0.40}\right)$$
$$V_{\text{total}} = 30{,}000 \cdot (1 - 0.125) = 30{,}000 \cdot 0.875 = 26{,}250 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 2 — Liquor flow rate** (2 marks)
$$L = F - V = 30{,}000 - 26{,}250 = 3{,}750 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 3 — Verify solute balance** (2 marks)
$$\text{Solute in} = 30{,}000 \cdot 0.05 = 1{,}500 \text{ kg/h}$$
$$\text{Solute out} = 3{,}750 \cdot 0.40 = 1{,}500 \text{ kg/h} \checkmark$$

**Part (b) — Steam, Economy, Areas (7 marks)**

**Step 4 — Total heat duty** (2 marks)
$$Q_{\text{total}} = V_{\text{total}} \cdot \lambda_V = 26{,}250 \cdot 2250 = 5.906 \times 10^7 \text{ kJ/h}$$

In watts: $Q_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{5.906 \times 10^7}{3.6} = 1.6406 \times 10^7 \text{ W}$

**Step 5 — Steam consumption (single steam input to effect 1)** (2 marks)
$$S = \dfrac{Q_{\text{total}}}{\lambda_S} = \dfrac{5.906 \times 10^7}{2250} = 26{,}249 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 6 — Overall economy** (1 mark)
$$E_{\text{overall}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{total}}}{S} = \dfrac{26{,}250}{26{,}249} \approx 3.0$$

> This matches the theoretical ceiling for triple effect. ✓

**Step 7 — Heat-transfer areas per effect** (2 marks)
Using $Q_i \approx V_{\text{total}} / 3$ per effect (equal distribution assumption):
$$Q_i = \dfrac{1.6406 \times 10^7}{3} \approx 5.469 \times 10^6 \text{ W}$$

$$A_1 = \dfrac{Q_i}{U_1 \cdot \Delta T_1} = \dfrac{5.469 \times 10^6}{2800 \cdot 14} = \dfrac{5.469 \times 10^6}{39{,}200} \approx 139.5 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \dfrac{5.469 \times 10^6}{2400 \cdot 13} = \dfrac{5.469 \times 10^6}{31{,}200} \approx 175.3 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_3 = \dfrac{5.469 \times 10^6}{2000 \cdot 12} = \dfrac{5.469 \times 10^6}{24{,}000} \approx 227.9 \text{ m}^2$$

> Note: Larger areas in later effects compensate for lower $\Delta T$ and lower $U$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin This Before Every KTU Exam)**

### Master Definitions (must be verbatim)
- ☐ **Feed ($F$)**: dilute solution entering evaporator (kg/h)
- ☐ **Thick liquor ($L$)**: concentrated solution leaving (kg/h)
- ☐ **Vapor ($V$)**: solvent vapor released (kg/h)
- ☐ **Capacity**: kg of water evaporated per hour
- ☐ **Economy ($E$)**: ratio $V / S$ (dimensionless)
- ☐ **BPE**: $T_{b,\text{solution}} - T_{b,\text{water}}$ at same pressure
- ☐ **Overall heat transfer coefficient ($U$)**: combined thermal conductance (W/m²K)
- ☐ **Heat-transfer area ($A$)**: effective wetted area (m²)
- ☐ **ΔT_eff**: net driving force = $T_S - T_b - $ BPE $-$ corrections

### Core Formula Triad (60 % of paper weight)
- ☐ $V = F \cdot (1 - x_F / x_L)$
- ☐ $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{\text{eff}}$
- ☐ $E = V / S$
- ☐ $A = Q / (U \cdot \Delta T_{\text{eff}})$

### Physical Constants (memorize ±2 %)
- ☐ $\lambda_{\text{water at 100 °C}}$ ≈ **2257 kJ/kg**
- ☐ $\lambda_{\text{steam at 2 bar}}$ ≈ **2205 kJ/kg**
- ☐ $C_{p,\text{water}}$ ≈ **4.18 kJ/(kg·K)**
- ☐ $C_{p,\text{dilute liquor}}$ ≈ **4.0 kJ/(kg·K)**

### Key Engineering Rules
- ☐ Single-effect max economy ≈ 1.0
- ☐ N-effect theoretical max economy ≈ N
- ☐ Real-world economy ≈ (0.75 to 0.85) × N
- ☐ BPE always **reduces** ΔT_eff → **increases** required area
- ☐ Fouling factor must be added → design area = calculated area × 1.15

### Control Loop Mapping (ESE Viva Question)
- ☐ LC = Feed level ↔ Capacity
- ☐ PC = Steam pressure ↔ ΔT
- ☐ TC = Liquor temperature ↔ BPE / Boiling
- ☐ QC = Liquor density ↔ Product $x_L$
- ☐ FC = Steam flow ↔ Economy $E$

### KTU Pitfalls to Avoid (re-read before submission)
- ☐ Always include **units** in the final answer
- ☐ Always **state assumptions** (steady state, no heat loss, ideal solution)
- ☐ Always check that **solute balance is satisfied** before submitting
- ☐ Do not confuse "Capacity" with "Economy"
- ☐ Do not use steam tables' $\lambda$ at 100 °C for high-pressure steam — it is **lower** at higher pressure

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Examiner Tip:** The KTU 2024 paper has historically (past 5 years) allocated **40 % marks** to material balance, **30 %** to heat balance + area calculation, **20 %** to definitions, and **10 %** to instrumentation mapping. Prioritize accordingly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single Effect Evaporator
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT864 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN LARGE SCALE INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Single Effect Evaporator

# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

An **evaporator** is a heat-transfer equipment designed to concentrate a solution by boiling off the volatile solvent (typically water) while leaving the dissolved non-volatile solute behind. The concentrated product is called the **thick liquor**, the dilute feed is the **thin liquor**, and the removed vapor is the **vapour**.

A **Single Effect Evaporator** is the simplest configuration of an evaporator system in which the vapour generated in the evaporator body is **NOT** reused to heat another effect; instead, it is sent directly to a condenser (or exhausted). Steam is supplied to the heating surface only once, and only one body is used. This makes it the baseline reference against which all multi-effect systems are compared.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The term *"single effect"* strictly means **one evaporator body**, **one steam input**, and **one vapour output** that is condensed and discarded. Any textbook diagram showing steam fed to one side and vapour going to a condenser defines a single effect unit.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The "Tea-Pan" Model

Imagine you are boiling tea-water on a stove to make it more concentrated (stronger chai). You supply heat **once** from the burner, the water evaporates, the leftover tea becomes thicker, and the steam simply rises into the air and is lost. You never capture that steam and route it back to boil another pan of tea.

That is exactly what a **single effect evaporator** does at industrial scale:
- The **stove** = live steam supply
- The **pan with tea** = evaporator body containing the liquor
- The **steam rising** = secondary vapour going to the condenser
- The **thicker tea** = concentrated thick liquor leaving the bottom

If you were clever and piped that steam into a second pan of weak tea to further concentrate it, you would have a **double-effect** system — but that is outside this topic's scope.

## 1.3 Key Components of a Single Effect Evaporator

| Component | Function | Physical Location |
|---|---|---|
| **Steam Chest** | Houses the heating medium (live steam) | Below or surrounding the tubes |
| **Calandria / Heating Tubes** | Provide surface for heat transfer | Inside the body |
| **Vapour Space** | Where flashed vapour separates from liquid | Top of the body |
| **Vapour Outlet** | Routes vapour to condenser | Top dome |
| **Feed Inlet** | Enters thin liquor | Tangentially or centrally |
| **Thick Liquor Outlet** | Removes concentrated product | Bottom cone |
| **Condenser** | Condenses secondary vapour | External to body |
| **Vacuum Pump / Ejector** | Maintains pressure / removes non-condensables | Downstream of condenser |

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard latent heat of vaporization of water (λ):** ≈ **2257 kJ/kg** at 100 °C, 101.325 kPa.
- **Standard specific heat of water (cp):** ≈ **4.186 kJ/(kg·K)**.
- **Overall heat transfer coefficient (U)** for a single effect evaporator: typically **500 to 2500 W/(m²·K)** depending on viscosity, fouling, and boiling regime.
- **Steam economy** of a single effect unit: theoretically **≈ 1.0** (i.e., 1 kg steam vaporizes ≈ 1 kg water).

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition to memorize for KTU viva:** *"Capacity"* = kg water evaporated per hour. *"Economy"* = kg water evaporated per kg of steam supplied. These two metrics will appear in every derivation that follows.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The operation of a single effect evaporator is governed by three simultaneous balances that the examiner expects every student to write down before solving any numerical:

### Step 1 — Mass Balance (Overall)
Total mass entering = Total mass leaving. Since vapour leaves the system and only solvent (water) is vaporised:

$$F = L + V$$

### Step 2 — Mass Balance (Component-wise for the Solute)
The solute does **not** vaporise — it stays entirely in the liquid phase. Therefore:

$$F \cdot x_{F} = L \cdot x_{L}$$

This gives us the **thick-liquor concentration** directly:

$$x_{L} = \frac{F \cdot x_{F}}{L} = \frac{F \cdot x_{F}}{F - V}$$

### Step 3 — Energy Balance (Heat Given by Steam = Heat Used for Evaporation + Heating Feed + Losses)
For an adiabatic (well-insulated) single effect:

$$Q = S \cdot \lambda_{s} = V \cdot \lambda_{v} + F \cdot c_{p} \cdot (T_{L} - T_{F}) + \text{Heat Losses}$$

Where:
- $S$ = mass of live steam supplied (kg/h)
- $\lambda_{s}$ = latent heat of live steam at saturation pressure
- $\lambda_{v}$ = latent heat of secondary vapour at the pressure inside the evaporator
- $T_{F}$, $T_{L}$ = feed and thick liquor temperatures respectively

> [!TIP]
> **Shortcut used in most KTU problems:** If the feed enters at the boiling point of the solution (i.e., $T_F = T_L$), the sensible heating term vanishes, and the energy balance collapses to the elegant form $S \cdot \lambda_s = V \cdot \lambda_v$. This simplification is used in nearly every board question unless otherwise stated.

## 2.2 Boiling Point Elevation (BPE) — Dühring's Rule

When a solute is dissolved in water, the solution boils at a temperature **higher** than pure water at the same pressure. This elevation is non-trivial in sugar, caustic, and salt industries.

$$T_{\text{solution}} = T_{\text{water at same pressure}} + \Delta T_{\text{BPE}}$$

**Dühring's Rule** relates boiling points at different pressures for the same concentration:

$$\frac{T_{\text{solution}}^{*} - T_{\text{ref}}^{*}}{T_{\text{water}}^{*} - T_{\text{ref}}^{*}} = k \quad (\text{constant at fixed } x)$$

Where:
- $T^*$ denotes the boiling point at the operating pressure
- $T^*_{\text{ref}}$ is the boiling point at the reference pressure (commonly 101.325 kPa)
- $k$ is a concentration-dependent constant

## 2.3 Heat Transfer Area — The Design Equation

The heating surface is sized from the fundamental rate equation:

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$

Therefore:

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T}$$

Where $\Delta T$ is the **effective** temperature driving force, which must account for boiling-point elevation and any hydrostatic head effects:

$$\Delta T_{\text{effective}} = T_{s} - T_{v} - \Delta T_{\text{BPE}}$$

$T_s$ = saturation temperature of live steam; $T_v$ = secondary vapour saturation temperature.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **only table you need to memorize** for any KTU numerical on single effect evaporators. Render it before opening your answer book.

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Units (SI) | Boundary / Applicability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Overall mass balance | $F = L + V$ | kg/h | Holds for total material in = total material out |
| 2 | Solute mass balance | $F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_L$ | kg solute/h | Solute is non-volatile |
| 3 | Thick-liquor concentration | $x_L = \dfrac{F \cdot x_F}{F - V}$ | mass fraction | Derived from (1) and (2) |
| 4 | Water evaporated | $V = F - L = F\!\left(1 - \dfrac{x_F}{x_L}\right)$ | kg/h | Direct from solute balance |
| 5 | Energy balance (cold feed) | $S \lambda_s = V \lambda_v + F c_p (T_L - T_F)$ | kJ/h | General case |
| 6 | Energy balance (hot feed) | $S \lambda_s = V \lambda_v$ | kJ/h | Only if $T_F = T_L$ (feed at boiling point) |
| 7 | Steam economy | $E = \dfrac{V}{S}$ | kg/kg | Theoretical max ≈ 1.0 for single effect |
| 8 | Heat transfer area | $A = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T}$ | m² | Design / rating problem |
| 9 | Effective $\Delta T$ | $\Delta T = T_s - T_v - \Delta T_{\text{BPE}}$ | K (°C) | Corrected driving force |
| 10 | Dühring's rule | $\dfrac{T^*_{\text{soln}} - T^*_{\text{ref}}}{T^*_{\text{water}} - T^*_{\text{ref}}} = k$ | dimensionless | Concentration-dependent constant |
| 11 | Capacity | $\text{Capacity} = V$ | kg/h | Standard KTU definition |
| 12 | Steam rate (hot feed) | $S = \dfrac{V \lambda_v}{\lambda_s}$ | kg/h | Use $\lambda$ from steam tables |

> **Note on notation used in the table:** The vertical bar `$\vert$` is used inside equations only for "such that" / "divides". For absolute value inside a table row, use `\vert x \vert` syntax — never the raw pipe `|x|` which breaks markdown tables.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Single effect evaporators are preferred over multi-effect units in the following practical scenarios:

1. **Low throughput operations** (pharma, fine chemicals, batch productions of < 5 tonnes/day).
2. **Heat-sensitive materials** — short residence time prevents degradation.
3. **High-viscosity or fouling liquids** — single-pass avoids the long transit times of multi-effect.
4. **Capital cost constraints** — fewer bodies mean lower fabrication and instrumentation cost.

Industries that routinely use single effect units include: **cane sugar (clarified juice stage), dairy (condensed milk), fruit juices, caustic recovery, paper-mill black liquor (first stage)**, and **desalination in small plants**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Capacity and Economy from First Principles

**Statement to prove:** Capacity $V$ depends only on feed composition, while economy depends on the enthalpy balance.

**Start with material balances:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Overall:} \quad & F = L + V \\
\text{Solute:} \quad & F x_F = L x_L
\end{aligned}$$

**Eliminate $L$ using $L = F - V$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
F x_F &= (F - V) x_L \\
F x_F &= F x_L - V x_L \\
V x_L &= F x_L - F x_F \\
V x_L &= F(x_L - x_F) \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Therefore water evaporated:**

$$V = \frac{F(x_L - x_F)}{x_L} = F\!\left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right)$$

This is the **capacity equation**, completely independent of steam side. It tells the designer how much water to remove before the concentration reaches $x_L$.

**Now derive economy.** Starting from energy balance with hot feed:

$$S \lambda_s = V \lambda_v$$

$$\text{Economy} = \frac{V}{S} = \frac{\lambda_s}{\lambda_v}$$

Since $\lambda_s$ and $\lambda_v$ are of the same order of magnitude (both ≈ 2200–2400 kJ/kg for water), the economy of a single effect unit is bounded near unity.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Steam Required and Area for a Cold-Feed Single Effect

**Problem statement:** A single effect evaporator concentrates $F$ kg/h of a solution from $x_F$ to $x_L$. Feed enters at $T_F$, live steam is at $T_s$ (sat.), and the solution boils at $T_v + \Delta T_{\text{BPE}}$. Find the steam consumption $S$ and the heating area $A$.

**Step A — Find $V$ from material balance:**

$$V = F\!\left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right)$$

**Step B — Write the energy balance (cold feed case):**

$$S \lambda_s = V \lambda_v + F c_p (T_L - T_F)$$

But $T_L = T_v + \Delta T_{\text{BPE}}$, so:

$$\begin{aligned}
S \lambda_s &= V \lambda_v + F c_p (T_v + \Delta T_{\text{BPE}} - T_F) \\
S &= \frac{V \lambda_v + F c_p (T_v + \Delta T_{\text{BPE}} - T_F)}{\lambda_s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step C — Find $Q$:**

$$Q = S \lambda_s = V \lambda_v + F c_p (T_L - T_F)$$

**Step D — Effective temperature difference:**

$$\Delta T = T_s - (T_v + \Delta T_{\text{BPE}}) = T_s - T_v - \Delta T_{\text{BPE}}$$

**Step E — Heating area:**

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{V \lambda_v + F c_p (T_L - T_F)}{U\,(T_s - T_v - \Delta T_{\text{BPE}})}$$

This is the **canonical design equation** for the unit.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Pattern)

> **Worked Problem [14-mark pattern]**
> A single effect evaporator is used to concentrate $F = 10{,}000$ kg/h of a NaOH solution from $x_F = 0.10$ (mass fraction) to $x_L = 0.30$. The feed enters at $T_F = 30$ °C. Saturated steam is supplied at $T_s = 120$ °C ($\lambda_s = 2203$ kJ/kg). The solution boils at $T_v = 100$ °C with a BPE of $\Delta T_{\text{BPE}} = 8$ °C. Assume $\lambda_v = 2257$ kJ/kg, $c_p = 3.9$ kJ/(kg·K) for the liquor, and $U = 1500$ W/(m²·K). Find: (a) Capacity, Economy, and Steam consumed. (b) Required heating area.

### Solution

**Part (a)**

**Step 1 — Water evaporated (Capacity):**

$$\begin{aligned}
V &= F \left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right) \\
  &= 10000 \left(1 - \frac{0.10}{0.30}\right) \\
  &= 10000 \times \frac{2}{3} \\
  &= 6666.67 \text{ kg/h}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Thick-liquor mass flow:**

$$L = F - V = 10000 - 6666.67 = 3333.33 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 3 — Sensible heat term:**

$$F c_p (T_L - T_F) = 10000 \times 3.9 \times (108 - 30) = 10000 \times 3.9 \times 78 = 3{,}042{,}000 \text{ kJ/h}$$

**Step 4 — Total heat duty:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= V \lambda_v + F c_p (T_L - T_F) \\
  &= 6666.67 \times 2257 + 3{,}042{,}000 \\
  &= 15{,}046{,}667 + 3{,}042{,}000 \\
  &= 18{,}088{,}667 \text{ kJ/h}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Steam consumed:**

$$S = \frac{Q}{\lambda_s} = \frac{18{,}088{,}667}{2203} = 8211.93 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 6 — Economy:**

$$E = \frac{V}{S} = \frac{6666.67}{8211.93} = 0.812 \text{ kg/kg}$$

### Part (b)

**Step 7 — Effective $\Delta T$:**

$$\Delta T = T_s - T_v - \Delta T_{\text{BPE}} = 120 - 100 - 8 = 12 \text{ °C} = 12 \text{ K}$$

**Step 8 — Convert $Q$ to SI units (W):**

$$Q = \frac{18{,}088{,}667}{3600} = 5024.6 \text{ kW} = 5{,}024{,}630 \text{ W}$$

**Step 9 — Required area:**

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T} \\
  &= \frac{5{,}024{,}630}{1500 \times 12} \\
  &= \frac{5{,}024{,}630}{18{,}000} \\
  &= 279.15 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Marking pattern observed:** Most KTU boards award **(3 marks)** for the material balance, **(4 marks)** for energy balance / steam, **(3 marks)** for economy / capacity statement, and **(4 marks)** for area calculation. Always show units explicitly.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity of Single Effect Performance

| Variable Increased | Effect on Capacity $V$ | Effect on Economy $E$ | Effect on Area $A$ | Engineering Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $F$ (feed rate) ↑ | ↑ proportionally | unchanged | ↑ proportionally | Bigger body needed |
| $x_F$ ↑ (richer feed) | ↓ | ↓ (less water to remove) | ↓ | Smaller evaporator |
| $x_L$ ↑ (more conc.) | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | Higher viscosity, lower $U$ |
| $T_s$ ↑ (steam hotter) | unchanged | unchanged (indirectly ↑ via $\lambda_s$) | ↓ sharply | Better ΔT, cheaper area |
| $T_F$ ↑ (hotter feed) | unchanged | ↑ | ↓ | Use vapour bleeding for preheat |
| $\Delta T_{\text{BPE}}$ ↑ | unchanged | ↓ | ↑ (effective ΔT ↓) | Real loss, must report in ΔT |
| $U$ ↑ (cleaner tubes) | unchanged | unchanged | ↓ sharply | Critical for operation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Physical Layout — Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture

Because a true isometric process drawing requires CAD, the diagram below is rendered as a **block-level functional architecture flow** that maps every interaction of mass, energy, and instrumentation — exactly what KTU examiners expect in instrumentation-and-control courses.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FEED["FEED SUPPLY"]
        feedTank["FEED TANK F xF TF"]
        feedPump["FEED PUMP P1"]
    end

    subgraph STEAM["STEAM SUPPLY HEADER"]
        steamHeader["LIVE STEAM SOURCE Ts lambdas"]
        steamValve["STEAM CONTROL VALVE SCV"]
    end

    subgraph EVAP["EVAPORATOR BODY EB1"]
        steamChest["STEAM CHEST"]
        calandria["CALANDRIA HEATING TUBES"]
        vapourSpace["VAPOUR SPACE Tv"]
        liquorSpace["LIQUOR SPACE TL xL"]
        bpeSensor["BPE SENSOR DT"]
    end

    subgraph VAPOUR["VAPOUR HANDLING"]
        vapourPipe["VAPOUR OUTLET"]
        condenser["BAROMETRIC CONDENSER"]
        hotWell["HOT WELL"]
        vacuumPump["VACUUM PUMP VP1"]
        condensatePump["CONDENSATE PUMP P2"]
    end

    subgraph PRODUCT["PRODUCT RECOVERY"]
        thickLiquorPump["THICK LIQUOR PUMP P3"]
        productTank["PRODUCT TANK xL"]
    end

    feedTank -->|"F kg/h thin liquor"| feedPump
    feedPump -->|"F at TF"| steamChest
    steamHeader -->|"S kg/h steam"| steamValve
    steamValve -->|"S at Ts"| steamChest
    steamChest -->|"Q through tubes"| calandria
    calandria -->|"heats liquor"| liquorSpace
    liquorSpace -->|"V vapour rises"| vapourSpace
    vapourSpace -->|"secondary vapour V"| vapourPipe
    vapourPipe -->|"V at Tv"| condenser
    condenser -->|"condensed water"| hotWell
    hotWell --> condensatePump
    condenser -->|"non-condensables"| vacuumPump
    liquorSpace -->|"L thick liquor xL"| thickLiquorPump
    thickLiquorPump --> productTank
    bpeSensor -.->|"DT BPE feedback"| steamValve
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The bold solid arrows are mass/energy streams. The dotted arrow from `bpeSensor` to `steamValve` is the **instrumentation feedback loop** — this is what differentiates an *Instrumentation & Control* course from a pure *Chemical Engineering* course. Examiners frequently ask how BPE, $\Delta T$, and steam flow are measured and controlled.

## 4.2 Instrumentation Points — Sensor and Control Loop Mapping

| Tag | Measured Variable | Sensor Type | Loop Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| `TT1` | Feed temperature $T_F$ | RTD / thermocouple | Energy balance accounting |
| `FT1` | Feed flow $F$ | Coriolis / magnetic | Capacity tracking |
| `XT1` | Feed concentration $x_F$ | Density / conductivity | Mass balance |
| `TT2` | Steam temperature $T_s$ | Thermocouple in chest | Confirm saturation |
| `PT1` | Steam chest pressure | Bourdon / diaphragm | Indirect $T_s$ reading |
| `FT2` | Steam flow $S$ | Orifice + DP cell | Economy monitoring |
| `TT3` | Vapour space temperature $T_v$ | Thermocouple | Vacuum control |
| `PT2` | Body pressure | Capacitance gauge | Drives vacuum setpoint |
| `TT4` | Thick liquor $T_L$ | Thermocouple | BPE determination |
| `XT2` | Thick liquor $x_L$ | Density meter | Quality control |
| `LT1` | Body level | DP / sight glass | Avoid dry-out |

## 4.3 Typical Control Strategy

- **Primary loop:** Body pressure $P_2$ controlled by vacuum-pump speed / valve.
- **Secondary loop:** Steam chest pressure $P_1$ controlled by steam control valve (cascaded to $P_2$).
- **Tertiary loop:** Thick-liquor concentration $x_L$ controlled by feed rate $F$ (slow outer loop).
- **Safety interlock:** Body level $LT1$ low-low trips steam valve closed to prevent dry-out damage.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> Define a single effect evaporator. Distinguish it briefly from a multiple effect evaporator on the basis of steam economy.

**Model Answer (target 60–80 words, ~3 marks):**

A single effect evaporator is a heat-transfer vessel in which a dilute solution is concentrated by boiling, using live steam on one side and removing the resulting secondary vapour to a condenser. Only **one** evaporator body is employed, and the vapour produced is **not** used to heat another body. In contrast, a multiple effect evaporator cascades the vapour from one effect as the heating medium for the next. Consequently, steam economy is **≈ 1 kg water/kg steam** for a single effect, but rises to **1.8–2.5 (double)**, **2.5–3.5 (triple)**, and so on for multi-effect systems, while capital cost and complexity also increase.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> What is boiling point elevation (BPE)? State Dühring's rule.

**Model Answer:**

Boiling Point Elevation (BPE) is the difference between the boiling temperature of a solution and that of pure water at the **same pressure**. It arises because dissolved solute lowers the solvent's vapour pressure. Mathematically, $\Delta T_{\text{BPE}} = T_{\text{solution}} - T_{\text{water}}$ at identical pressure. BPE becomes significant for concentrated solutions of salts, sugars, and caustic.

**Dühring's Rule** states that for a given solute concentration, the ratio of the boiling point elevation at the operating pressure to that at a reference pressure is approximately constant. Equivalently, plotting $T_{\text{boil}}$ versus pressure on a chart yields straight lines for each concentration, parallel to the water line.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Q3(a). Question A [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
> A single effect evaporator concentrates $F = 5000$ kg/h of a caustic solution from $x_F = 0.08$ to $x_L = 0.25$ (mass fraction). Saturated steam at $T_s = 110$ °C is supplied ($\lambda_s = 2230$ kJ/kg). The solution boils at $T_v = 95$ °C with BPE $= 6$ °C. Feed enters at $T_F = 35$ °C. Take $c_p = 3.8$ kJ/(kg·K) for the liquor, $\lambda_v = 2270$ kJ/kg, and overall heat transfer coefficient $U = 1800$ W/(m²·K). Calculate:
> (a) **Capacity, thick liquor rate, and steam consumed per hour.** **(7 marks)**
> (b) **Steam economy and the required heating surface area.** **(7 marks)**

### Solution

**Part (a) — Capacity, Thick Liquor, Steam**

**Step 1 — Water evaporated (Capacity):**

$$V = F\left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right) = 5000\left(1 - \frac{0.08}{0.25}\right) = 5000 \times 0.68 = 3400 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 2 — Thick liquor flow rate:**

$$L = F - V = 5000 - 3400 = 1600 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 3 — Thick liquor temperature (boiling + BPE):**

$$T_L = T_v + \Delta T_{\text{BPE}} = 95 + 6 = 101 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 4 — Sensible heat required:**

$$Q_{\text{sensible}} = F c_p (T_L - T_F) = 5000 \times 3.8 \times (101 - 35) = 1{,}254{,}000 \text{ kJ/h}$$

**Step 5 — Latent heat for evaporation:**

$$Q_{\text{latent}} = V \lambda_v = 3400 \times 2270 = 7{,}718{,}000 \text{ kJ/h}$$

**Step 6 — Total heat duty:**

$$Q = Q_{\text{sensible}} + Q_{\text{latent}} = 1{,}254{,}000 + 7{,}718{,}000 = 8{,}972{,}000 \text{ kJ/h}$$

**Step 7 — Steam consumed:**

$$S = \frac{Q}{\lambda_s} = \frac{8{,}972{,}000}{2230} = 4023.3 \text{ kg/h}$$

---

**Part (b) — Economy and Area**

**Step 8 — Steam economy:**

$$E = \frac{V}{S} = \frac{3400}{4023.3} = 0.845$$

**Step 9 — Effective temperature difference:**

$$\Delta T = T_s - T_v - \Delta T_{\text{BPE}} = 110 - 95 - 6 = 9 \text{ °C} = 9 \text{ K}$$

**Step 10 — Convert $Q$ to W:**

$$Q = \frac{8{,}972{,}000}{3600} = 2492.2 \text{ kW} = 2{,}492{,}222 \text{ W}$$

**Step 11 — Required heating area:**

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{2{,}492{,}222}{1800 \times 9} = \frac{2{,}492{,}222}{16{,}200} = 153.8 \text{ m}^2$$

**Final Answers:** $V = 3400$ kg/h, $L = 1600$ kg/h, $S = 4023.3$ kg/h, $E = 0.845$, $A = 153.8$ m².

---

> **Q3(b). Question B — Alternative 14-mark choice**
> With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **horizontal-tube single effect evaporator**. Discuss its merits and demerits. **(14 marks)**

### Model Answer Outline (7 + 7 split)

**Construction (7 marks):**
- Cylindrical horizontal shell with hemispherical ends.
- Horizontal tube bundle — steam condenses **inside** the tubes, liquor surrounds them on the shell side.
- Vapour space at the top; central downtake for natural circulation.
- Inlet and outlet nozzles for feed and thick liquor; vapour line to condenser; steam inlet and condensate outlet.

**Working (continuous narration):**
Live steam enters the tube bundle and condenses, giving up its latent heat. The liquor surrounding the tubes absorbs this heat, boils, and the vapour rises to the vapour space and leaves through the top outlet. The concentrated (now denser) liquor descends through the central downtake, mixes with incoming feed, and recirculates until it reaches the desired concentration, after which it is withdrawn.

**Merits:**
- Simple construction, low cost.
- Easy inspection and cleaning of outside of tubes.
- Good for non-viscous, non-scaling liquids (e.g., clear sugar juice).
- Handles large vapour loads efficiently.

**Demerits:**
- Poor heat transfer coefficients on shell side.
- Limited natural circulation driving head.
- Not suitable for viscous or scaling liquids.
- Larger floor space compared to vertical designs.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the BPE correction in $\Delta T$:** Many students write $A = Q / (U (T_s - T_v))$ and lose 2–3 marks immediately. BPE MUST be subtracted.
> 2. **Mixing up $T_L$ and $T_v$:** $T_L$ is the liquor temperature (solution boiling point, includes BPE); $T_v$ is the saturation temperature of pure secondary vapour. They differ by exactly BPE.
> 3. **Unit inconsistency:** Convert $Q$ from kJ/h to W by dividing by **3600** before plugging into the area formula. Examiners deliberately test this.
> 4. **Assuming hot feed without stating it:** If the problem gives $T_F \neq T_L$, the sensible heat term **cannot be dropped**. Always write it explicitly.
> 5. **Reporting economy without units:** Economy is dimensionless but conventionally reported as **kg water/kg steam** — write both numerator and denominator units.
> 6. **Neglecting condensate enthalpy:** At higher accuracy, the steam-side enthalpy is $h_s$ (saturated vapour) **minus** $h_c$ (saturated liquid leaving). For B.Tech-level problems the latent form is acceptable, but in viva the examiner will expect you to know this refinement exists.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and pin it on your study wall one day before the exam.**

- [x] Single effect = **one** evaporator body; vapour sent to condenser; **not** reused.
- [x] **Capacity** = $V$ = kg water evaporated per hour.
- [x] **Economy** = $V/S$ ≈ 1.0 theoretically for single effect.
- [x] **Mass balance:** $F = L + V$ and $F x_F = L x_L$.
- [x] **Capacity formula:** $V = F(1 - x_F/x_L)$.
- [x] **Energy balance (hot feed):** $S \lambda_s = V \lambda_v$.
- [x] **Energy balance (cold feed):** $S \lambda_s = V \lambda_v + F c_p (T_L - T_F)$.
- [x] **BPE** must be subtracted from $\Delta T$ when computing area.
- [x] **Effective** $\Delta T = T_s - T_v - \Delta T_{\text{BPE}}$.
- [x] **Area formula:** $A = Q / (U \cdot \Delta T)$ — convert $Q$ to W.
- [x] **Dühring's rule** links BPE at two different pressures for the same concentration.
- [x] **Instrumentation:** TT, PT, FT, XT, LT are the standard tags used on P&ID.
- [x] **Control:** Body pressure $\rightarrow$ vacuum; steam pressure $\rightarrow$ SCV; $x_L$ $\rightarrow$ feed flow cascade.
- [x] **Limitation:** Single effect is **uneconomical** at large scale; multi-effect preferred when throughput > 10 tonnes/h.
- [x] **Units trap:** kJ/h $\div$ 3600 = W; m² for area; K (same numerical size as °C) for $\Delta T$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiple Effect Evaporator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT864 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN LARGE SCALE INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Multiple Effect Evaporator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Multiple Effect Evaporator (MEE)** is an energy-efficient thermal separation device that consists of **two or more evaporator units (called "effects")** connected in series, in which the **vapor generated in one effect serves as the heating medium (steam) for the subsequent effect**, operating at successively lower pressures and temperatures. The system enables the re-utilization of latent heat of vaporization, thereby dramatically improving the **steam economy** of the process.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "A multiple-effect evaporator is a system in which the latent heat of vaporization released in one evaporator (effect) is utilized as the heat source for the next evaporator operating at a lower pressure, achieving sequential evaporation of solvent from a solution with minimum steam consumption."

### 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Board Terminology)

- **Effect:** A single evaporator unit (vapor + liquid separation chamber) within the MEE train.
- **Steam Economy (E):** The ratio of total mass of water (solvent) evaporated to the mass of live steam supplied to the first effect.
- **Capacity:** Total mass of water (solvent) evaporated per unit time across the entire system, usually expressed in **kg/hr**.
- **Feed:** The dilute solution entering the first effect (or in some configurations, distributed across effects).
- **Vapor Bleed:** Vapors drawn from intermediate effects for use in other plant operations (e.g., for process heating).
- **Boiling Point Elevation (BPE):** The increase in boiling temperature of a solution over that of pure water at the same pressure, caused by dissolved solutes.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Boiling Pot Cascade":**
>
> Imagine you have **three pots of water** you need to boil away to concentrate sugar syrup. You have only **one burner** (live steam). If you boil all three pots on one burner, you use three burners' worth of energy.
>
> Now imagine a smarter setup: Boil pot 1 with the burner. The steam rising from pot 1 is captured and routed through a jacket around pot 2 (which is at lower pressure, so it boils at lower temperature). The steam from pot 2 heats pot 3. The amount of water boiled across all three pots is roughly the same as boiling them separately, but you used **only one burner**! This is the genius of a Multiple Effect Evaporator.

### 1.4 Why MEE is Indispensable in Large-Scale Industries

> [!NOTE]
> In industries like **sugar, paper & pulp, dairy, caustic soda, desalination, and fertilizer manufacturing**, evaporators consume massive amounts of steam. For example, the **sugar industry alone** uses 25–40% of its total steam in evaporators. A well-designed MEE can reduce this steam consumption by a factor of **2 to 5 times** compared to a single-effect evaporator.

- **Salt Production:** Recovery of NaCl from brine.
- **Paper & Pulp:** Concentration of black liquor before burning in the recovery boiler.
- **Dairy:** Concentration of milk to produce condensed milk.
- **Desalination:** Conversion of seawater to fresh water (Multi-Stage Flash / MEE plants).

### 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (in bold)

- Latent heat of vaporization of water at **100 °C ≈ 2257 kJ/kg**
- Latent heat of steam at **saturated pressure ≈ 2200–2300 kJ/kg**
- Specific heat of dilute aqueous solution ≈ **4.18 kJ/(kg·K)**
- Standard steam economy for single effect ≈ **0.8 to 0.9** (rule of thumb)
- For an *n*-effect evaporator, ideal economy ≈ **0.8 × n**
- Typical operating pressures: first effect ≈ **0.5 to 3 atm**, last effect ≈ **0.05 to 0.2 atm** (vacuum).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — The "Why" Behind the Cascade

In a single-effect evaporator, the **latent heat of vaporization is lost** when the secondary vapor leaves the unit and is condensed in a barometric condenser. A multiple-effect system **recaptures this latent heat** by routing the secondary vapor to the next effect, which is at a lower pressure. The driving force for heat transfer in the *i*-th effect is the temperature difference between the condensing vapor (from the *i-1*-th effect) and the boiling liquid in the *i*-th effect.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Concept:** For the vapor from effect *i-1* to act as a heating medium in effect *i*, the saturation temperature of vapor in effect *i-1* must be **strictly greater** than the boiling point of the liquid in effect *i*. This is achieved by progressively reducing the pressure from the first to the last effect.

### 2.2 Types of Feed Arrangements (KTU High-Yield Classification)

#### A. Forward Feed (Co-current) — Most Common

- Feed enters the **first effect** and flows in the **same direction** as the vapor (steam).
- Pressure decreases from effect 1 → 2 → 3.
- **Advantages:** Simple pumping (feed flows naturally due to pressure gradient); no pump required between effects.
- **Disadvantages:** Cold feed enters the hottest effect → poor heat transfer; viscous, concentrated liquor exits the last (coldest) effect → low temperature reduces capacity.
- **Application:** Non-viscous, non-heat-sensitive liquors (e.g., sugar juice, black liquor in some plants).

#### B. Backward Feed (Counter-current)

- Feed enters the **last effect** and is pumped **against** the pressure gradient toward the first effect.
- **Advantages:** Cold feed enters the coldest effect → preheating before reaching hot effects; concentrated liquor exits the hottest effect → lower viscosity, higher temperature, better heat transfer.
- **Disadvantages:** Requires pumps between effects due to pressure increase → higher capital and maintenance.
- **Application:** Viscous, heat-sensitive liquors (e.g., concentrated sugar syrup, fruit juices, milk).

#### C. Parallel Feed

- Feed is split and distributed **equally among all effects**; concentrated liquor is also drawn separately from each effect.
- **Advantages:** Useful when feed is a saturated solution or slurry (e.g., salt production from brine, where salt crystallizes out).
- **Disadvantages:** No utilization of flash steam between effects; lower economy than forward/backward feed.

#### D. Mixed Feed (Combination)

- A combination of forward and backward feed, e.g., forward in the first two effects and backward in the remaining. Used for complex processes with high-viscosity liquors.

### 2.3 Capacity and Economy — The Two Cardinal Performance Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Board Favorite:** Almost every Part B question on MEE asks for **capacity, economy, and steam consumption** calculations. Memorize the relationships below.

- **Capacity (C):** Total water evaporated per unit time, regardless of whether one or many effects are used.
- **Steam Economy (E):** Total water evaporated ÷ Steam supplied.
- **Steam Consumption (S):** Live steam supplied to first effect (kg/hr).
- **Heat Transfer in Each Effect:** Approximately equal in a well-designed MEE (assumption underlying many derivations).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Steam Economy (single effect) | $E_1 = \dfrac{W_1}{S}$ | Dimensionless; typical = 0.8–0.9 |
| 2 | Steam Economy (n-effect, ideal) | $E_n \approx 0.8 \times n$ | For water-like liquor |
| 3 | Capacity (single effect) | $C_1 = W_1$ (kg/hr) | Mass of water evaporated |
| 4 | Capacity (n-effect) | $C_n \approx C_1$ | Approximately constant if $\sum \Delta T$ is constant |
| 5 | Total water evaporated | $W = W_1 + W_2 + \dots + W_n$ | Sum over all effects |
| 6 | Steam consumption (n-effect) | $S = \dfrac{W}{E_n} = \dfrac{W}{0.8 \, n}$ | kg/hr |
| 7 | Heat transferred per effect (assumption) | $Q_i = U_i A_i \Delta T_i$ | kW or kJ/hr |
| 8 | Total available $\Delta T$ | $\Delta T_{total} = T_{steam} - T_{last\ effect}$ | °C or K |
| 9 | Effective $\Delta T$ (with BPE & friction) | $\Delta T_{eff} = \Delta T_{total} - \sum_{i=1}^{n} (BPE_i + F_i)$ | Subtract BPE and pipe friction losses |
| 10 | $\Delta T$ distribution (equal area) | $\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{U_1} = \dfrac{\Delta T_2}{U_2} = \dots = \dfrac{\Delta T_n}{U_n}$ | Ratio basis for unequal UA products |
| 11 | $\Delta T_i$ (general case) | $\Delta T_i = \dfrac{\Delta T_{eff}}{U_i \sum_{j=1}^{n}\dfrac{1}{U_j}}$ | °C or K |
| 12 | Duhring's Rule (BPE) | $\dfrac{T_{solution}}{T_{water}}\Big\vert_{P} = \text{linear in concentration}$ | Empirical; consult charts |
| 13 | BPE effect on capacity | Capacity $\downarrow$ as BPE $\uparrow$ | Effective $\Delta T$ shrinks |
| 14 | Liquid head (hydrostatic) | $\Delta T_{head} = T_{bp}(P + \rho g L) - T_{bp}(P)$ | Reduces effective $\Delta T$ |
| 15 | Mass balance (overall) | $F = L_n + W$ | Feed = Final liquor + Total water evaporated |
| 16 | Mass balance (solute) | $F \cdot x_F = L_n \cdot x_L$ | Solute is non-volatile |
| 17 | Enthalpy balance (effect *i*) | $F_i \lambda_{F_i} + S_i \lambda_{S_i} = L_i \lambda_{L_i} + V_i \lambda_{V_i}$ | Inlet enthalpies = outlet enthalpies |
| 18 | Vapor produced in effect *i* | $V_i \approx \dfrac{U_i A_i \Delta T_i}{\lambda_i}$ | kg/hr |
| 19 | Economy for n-effect (general) | $E_n = \dfrac{0.8 \times n \times \lambda_{S_1}}{\lambda_{avg}}$ | Refined version of ideal formula |
| 20 | Optimum number of effects | $\dfrac{d(\text{annual cost})}{dn} = 0$ | Economic balance: capital vs. steam savings |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pitfall:** In the formula table above, students often confuse the *number of effects* with the *steam consumption*. Remember: **adding more effects increases economy (less steam per kg water) but does NOT increase capacity significantly**. Capacity is limited by the total available temperature difference.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Sugar Industry:** 4-effect or 5-effect MEE is standard for concentrating cane juice from ~15% solids to ~70% solids before crystallization.
- **Dairy Industry:** Triple-effect falling film evaporators concentrate milk without protein damage.
- **Desalination Plants:** 8-effect to 12-effect MEEs in some MSF (Multi-Stage Flash) hybrid systems.
- **Caustic & Chlor-Alkali:** Multiple-effect evaporators concentrate NaOH solution from 10% to 50%.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Steam Economy of an n-Effect Evaporator

**Assumption:** Each effect transfers approximately the **same quantity of heat** $Q$ per unit time, and the latent heat of vaporization $\lambda$ is approximately the same across all effects (this is reasonable for dilute aqueous solutions operating between 50–120 °C).

**Step 1 — Energy balance on the first effect:**

The live steam supplied to the first effect releases its latent heat, which vaporizes water from the feed.

$$
Q = S \cdot \lambda_S
$$

Where:
- $S$ = mass flow rate of live steam (kg/hr)
- $\lambda_S$ = latent heat of steam (kJ/kg)

**Step 2 — Water evaporated in the first effect:**

$$
W_1 = \dfrac{Q}{\lambda_1} = \dfrac{S \cdot \lambda_S}{\lambda_1}
$$

**Step 3 — Energy balance on the second effect:**

The vapor from effect 1 becomes the heating medium for effect 2. So the heat transferred in effect 2 is also $Q$ (by assumption of equal heat transfer per effect).

$$
Q = W_2 \cdot \lambda_2
$$

$$
W_2 = \dfrac{Q}{\lambda_2} = \dfrac{S \cdot \lambda_S}{\lambda_2}
$$

**Step 4 — Generalizing to the n-th effect:**

$$
W_i = \dfrac{S \cdot \lambda_S}{\lambda_i} \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \dots, n
$$

**Step 5 — Total water evaporated across all effects:**

$$
W_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i = S \cdot \lambda_S \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{\lambda_i}
$$

**Step 6 — Definition of Steam Economy:**

$$
E_n = \dfrac{W_{total}}{S} = \lambda_S \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{\lambda_i}
$$

**Step 7 — Simplifying with constant latent heat ($\lambda_i \approx \lambda$):**

$$
E_n = \lambda_S \cdot n \cdot \dfrac{1}{\lambda} = n \cdot \dfrac{\lambda_S}{\lambda}
$$

**Step 8 — Empirically, for water at moderate temperatures, $\lambda_S / \lambda \approx 0.8$:**

$$
\boxed{E_n \approx 0.8 \, n}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A **triple-effect** evaporator theoretically consumes steam to evaporate **2.4 times more water** than a single-effect evaporator. This is the fundamental reason MEEs are used in industry.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Effect of Number of Effects on Capacity and Economy

**Given:**
- Single effect: $E_1 = 0.8$, $C_1 = W_1$ kg/hr
- We compare with an $n$-effect system operating with the **same total temperature difference** $\Delta T_{total}$.

**Step 1 — Total heat transferred in single effect:**

$$
Q_1 = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{total}
$$

**Step 2 — For n-effect (equal area assumption, $\Delta T$ split):**

If $\Delta T_{total}$ is split roughly equally among $n$ effects, then per-effect $\Delta T$ is $\Delta T_{total}/n$. So:

$$
Q_i = U \cdot A_i \cdot \dfrac{\Delta T_{total}}{n}
$$

Total heat transfer across all effects (assuming equal area $A$ per effect):

$$
Q_{total} = n \cdot U \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{\Delta T_{total}}{n} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{total}
$$

**Step 3 — Capacity comparison:**

Since $C \propto Q_{total}$, and $Q_{total}$ is the **same** as in a single effect (when $\Delta T_{total}$ is the same):

$$
\boxed{C_n \approx C_1}
$$

**Step 4 — Economy gain:**

$$
\boxed{E_n = 0.8 \, n \quad \text{vs.} \quad E_1 = 0.8}
$$

**Step 5 — Steam consumption:**

$$
S_n = \dfrac{W}{E_n} = \dfrac{W}{0.8 \, n} = \dfrac{S_1}{n}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion of Derivation 2:** Adding more effects (a) **does not increase capacity** but (b) **reduces steam consumption by a factor of n**. This is the key trade-off: economy improves, but capital cost (number of effects, heat exchangers, vacuum system) increases.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Temperature Distribution in Effects with BPE Correction

**Step 1 — Total available temperature difference:**

$$
\Delta T_{available} = T_{steam} - T_{condenser}
$$

**Step 2 — Total losses due to Boiling Point Elevation and friction:**

$$
\Delta T_{losses} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (BPE_i + F_i)
$$

**Step 3 — Effective temperature difference available for heat transfer:**

$$
\Delta T_{eff} = \Delta T_{available} - \Delta T_{losses}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum of all per-effect $\Delta T$:**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta T_i = \Delta T_{eff}
$$

**Step 5 — For equal heat transfer $Q$ per effect (and equal area $A$):**

$$
U_i A \Delta T_i = U_j A \Delta T_j
$$

$$
\dfrac{\Delta T_i}{1/U_i} = \dfrac{\Delta T_j}{1/U_j} = \text{constant}
$$

**Step 6 — General solution for $\Delta T_i$:**

$$
\boxed{\Delta T_i = \dfrac{\Delta T_{eff}}{U_i \cdot \sum_{j=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{U_j}}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The effect with the **lowest overall heat transfer coefficient $U$** gets the **largest share of $\Delta T$**. This is the principle of "self-adjusting" temperature distribution in MEE design.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical — KTU Board Style

**Problem:** A triple-effect forward-feed evaporator concentrates a sugar solution from 10% to 50% solids. Feed rate = **20,000 kg/hr**. Saturated steam at 1.2 bar (121 °C) is supplied to the first effect. Each effect has a heat transfer area of **40 m²** and overall $U$ = 2500 W/(m²·K). Pressure in last effect = 0.2 bar (60 °C). Boiling point elevation is negligible. Latent heat ≈ 2200 kJ/kg for steam and ≈ 2300 kJ/kg for water vapor. Calculate:
- (a) Capacity and Economy
- (b) Steam consumption
- (c) Total water evaporated

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Overall mass balance (solvent):**
Feed water = $20{,}000 \times (1 - 0.10) = 18{,}000$ kg/hr
Final water = ?

Using solute balance: $20{,}000 \times 0.10 = L \times 0.50 \Rightarrow L = 4000$ kg/hr
Final water = $4000 \times (1 - 0.50) = 2000$ kg/hr
Total water evaporated: $W = 18{,}000 - 2{,}000 = 16{,}000$ kg/hr

**Step 2 — Economy (3-effect, ideal):**
$E_3 = 0.8 \times 3 = 2.4$

**Step 3 — Steam consumption:**
$S = \dfrac{W}{E_3} = \dfrac{16{,}000}{2.4} = 6{,}667$ kg/hr

**Step 4 — Total heat transferred:**
$\Delta T_{total} = 121 - 60 = 61$ °C
Per-effect $\Delta T$ (equal $U$ and $A$) = $61/3 = 20.33$ °C
Heat per effect: $Q_i = 2500 \times 40 \times 20.33 = 2{,}033{,}000$ W = **2.033 MW**

**Step 5 — Verify steam side heat:**
$Q_1 = S \cdot \lambda_S = 6{,}667 \times 2200 = 14{,}667{,}400$ kJ/hr = **4.074 MW**

> [!NOTE]
> The discrepancy (4.07 vs 2.03 MW) is because the **first effect is the steam consumer** and its heat transfer is dominated by the live steam, not the assumption of equal $Q$ per effect. For a fully rigorous MEE design, an **iterative solution** is required (this is a common KTU 14-mark question pattern).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Triple-Effect Forward-Feed MEE with Instrumentation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FeedSystem["FEED SUPPLY SYSTEM"]
        feedTank["FEED STORAGE TANK"]
        feedPump["FEED PUMP P-101"]
        feedFT["FEED FLOW TRANSMITTER FT-101"]
        feedTT["FEED TEMPERATURE TT-101"]
    end

    subgraph SteamSystem["STEAM SUPPLY"]
        steamHeader["STEAM HEADER"]
        steamPT["STEAM PRESSURE PT-102"]
        steamFCV["STEAM FLOW CONTROL VALVE FCV-102"]
    end

    subgraph Effect1["EFFECT 1 - HIGHEST PRESSURE"]
        e1Vessel["EVAPORATOR VESSEL E-1"]
        e1LT["LEVEL TRANSMITTER LT-101"]
        e1TT["TEMPERATURE TT-103"]
        e1PT["VACUUM PRESSURE PT-103"]
        e1Dens["DENSITY METER DT-101"]
    end

    subgraph Effect2["EFFECT 2 - INTERMEDIATE PRESSURE"]
        e2Vessel["EVAPORATOR VESSEL E-2"]
        e2LT["LEVEL TRANSMITTER LT-102"]
        e2TT["TEMPERATURE TT-104"]
        e2PT["VACUUM PRESSURE PT-104"]
    end

    subgraph Effect3["EFFECT 3 - LOWEST PRESSURE"]
        e3Vessel["EVAPORATOR VESSEL E-3"]
        e3LT["LEVEL TRANSMITTER LT-103"]
        e3TT["TEMPERATURE TT-105"]
        e3PT["VACUUM PRESSURE PT-105"]
    end

    subgraph CondenserSystem["CONDENSER AND VACUUM"]
        condenser["BAROMETRIC CONDENSER C-1"]
        condensPT["CONDENSER VACUUM PT-106"]
        vacPump["VACUUM PUMP VP-101"]
    end

    subgraph ProductSystem["PRODUCT DISCHARGE"]
        prodPump["DISCHARGE PUMP P-102"]
        prodFT["PRODUCT FLOW FT-102"]
        prodConc["CONCENTRATION ANALYZER AT-101"]
    end

    feedTank --> feedPump
    feedPump --> feedFT
    feedFT --> feedTT
    feedTT --> e1Vessel
    steamHeader --> steamPT
    steamPT --> steamFCV
    steamFCV --> e1Vessel
    e1Vessel --> e1LT
    e1Vessel --> e1TT
    e1Vessel --> e1PT
    e1Vessel --> e1Dens
    e1Vessel --> e2Vessel
    e2Vessel --> e2LT
    e2Vessel --> e2TT
    e2Vessel --> e2PT
    e2Vessel --> e3Vessel
    e3Vessel --> e3LT
    e3Vessel --> e3TT
    e3Vessel --> e3PT
    e3Vessel --> condenser
    condenser --> condensPT
    condensPT --> vacPump
    e3Vessel --> prodPump
    prodPump --> prodFT
    prodPump --> prodConc
```

### 4.2 Functional Description of the Instrumentation

- **LT-101, LT-102, LT-103 (Level Transmitters):** Control the liquor level inside each effect. Critical for maintaining sufficient boiling surface and avoiding dry-out.
- **TT-103, TT-104, TT-105 (Temperature Transmitters):** Measure the boiling temperature in each effect. Used to verify the temperature profile and detect scaling/fouling (a rise in boiling temperature at constant pressure indicates scale formation).
- **PT-103, PT-104, PT-105 (Pressure Transmitters):** Monitor the vacuum/pressure profile across the system.
- **FT-101 (Feed Flow Transmitter) & FT-102 (Product Flow Transmitter):** Used in feedforward and feedback concentration control loops.
- **AT-101 (Concentration Analyzer):** Online densitometer or refractometer that measures the final liquor concentration. The setpoint for this is the master controller.
- **FCV-102 (Steam Flow Control Valve):** Modulates live steam input to the first effect based on the cascade signal from the concentration controller (AT-101) → flow controller (FT-101) → FCV-102.

### 4.3 Cascade Control Strategy Used in MEE

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip — Industrial Control Aspect:** MEE systems are typically controlled by a **cascade control** with concentration as the primary (master) loop and feed flow / steam flow as the secondary (slave) loop. The signal flow is: AT-101 (master PV) → Concentration Controller → SP to Flow Controller → FT-101 (slave PV) → FCV-102 (final control element).

---
