# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**"List the five primary control objectives in the operation of a multiple-effect evaporator. Identify the corresponding measured and manipulated variables for the product concentration loop."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The five primary control objectives are:
1. Maintain product concentration $x_N$ at the desired set-point.
2. Maintain liquid level $h_i$ in each effect.
3. Regulate steam supply to effect 1.
4. Stabilise operating pressure in each effect.
5. Minimise energy consumption.

For the **product concentration loop** (1.5 marks for variables + 1.5 marks for objectives):
- **Measured Variable (PV):** Density of the thick liquor leaving the last effect — measured using a Coriolis density meter, refractometer, or differential-pressure density cell. Concentration is inferred from density using a calibration curve.
- **Set-Point (SP):** Desired mass fraction $x_N$ (e.g., 0.40).
- **Manipulated Variable (MV):** Steam flow rate $S$ to the first effect, throttled by a control valve on the steam line.
- **Disturbance Variables:** Feed flow $F$, feed concentration $x_0$, feed temperature, and steam pressure.

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
**"State Duhring's rule. Why is it important in the design and control of evaporators?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Duhring's Rule (2 marks):**
> *"The boiling point of a given solution, when plotted against the boiling point of pure water at the same pressure, yields a straight line. Therefore, the boiling point of the solution is a linear function of the boiling point of pure water at identical pressure, with the slope and intercept being functions of solute concentration."*

Mathematically: $T_b = a(x) + b(x) \, T_w$

**Importance in Evaporator Design and Control (1 mark):**
- It allows estimation of the **Boiling Point Elevation (BPE)**, $\Delta T_{\text{BPE}} = T_b - T_w$, at any operating pressure using a single calibration at one pressure.
- BPE directly **reduces the available temperature driving force** $(\Delta T)$ available for heat transfer in each effect.
- In control, the measured vapor temperature $T_v$ and pressure $P$ must be combined to compute the *actual* solution temperature, so that BPE changes (due to concentration drift) can be correctly interpreted and compensated for in the control law.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question B-A: **OPTION A** (14 Marks) — *Design and Analysis of a Triple-Effect Evaporator Control System*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Q-pattern adapted]**

A forward-feed triple-effect evaporator is to be designed to concentrate a dilute aqueous solution from $x_0 = 0.08$ to $x_3 = 0.50$ at a feed rate of $F = 20{,}000$ kg/h. Assume equal latent heat of vaporisation in all effects and equal heat-transfer area. The available temperature span from fresh steam to condenser is $(T_s - T_c) = 75$ °C. Each effect has a BPE of 5 °C. There are no other temperature losses.

#### Part (a) — Calculate Steam Flow and Steam Economy (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Final thick liquor flow** (1 mark)

$$L_3 = F \cdot \frac{x_0}{x_3} = 20{,}000 \times \frac{0.08}{0.50}$$

$$L_3 = 20{,}000 \times 0.16$$

$$\boxed{L_3 = 3{,}200 \text{ kg/h}}$$

**Step 2 — Total water evaporated** (1 mark)

$$V_{\text{total}} = F - L_3 = 20{,}000 - 3{,}200$$

$$\boxed{V_{\text{total}} = 16{,}800 \text{ kg/h}}$$

**Step 3 — Vapor per effect under equal-area assumption** (1.5 marks)

If the evaporation is equal in each effect, $V_1 = V_2 = V_3$:

$$V_i = \frac{16{,}800}{3} = 5{,}600 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 4 — Steam flow to effect 1** (1.5 marks)

Since $\lambda_s \approx \lambda_1$:

$$S = V_1 = 5{,}600 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 5 — Steam economy** (1 mark)

$$\text{Steam Economy} = \frac{V_{\text{total}}}{S} = \frac{16{,}800}{5{,}600}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Steam Economy} = 3.0 \text{ kg water/kg steam}}$$

**Step 6 — Total temperature driving force available** (1 mark)

$$\Delta T_{\text{available}} = (T_s - T_c) - 3 \times \text{BPE} = 75 - 15 = 60 \text{ °C}$$

$$\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2 = \Delta T_3 = 20 \text{ °C (equal area assumption)}$$

#### Part (b) — Propose a Complete Control Strategy with Justification (7 marks)

**Model Answer (broken into scoring sub-points):**

**(i) Primary Concentration Loop — Cascade on Steam Flow (2.5 marks)**

- **PV:** Density of $L_3$ measured by a Coriolis mass flow meter with density output, OR a differential-pressure density cell on the discharge line of effect 3.
- **SP:** Corresponding to desired $x_3 = 0.50$.
- **Master Controller:** PI controller, output is set-point to the steam-flow slave.
- **Slave Controller:** PI controller, output goes to the steam control valve.
- **Justification:** Cascade eliminates the nonlinearity of the steam valve and rejects steam-supply pressure disturbances before they reach the concentration master loop. This is the **industry-standard** scheme.

**(ii) Level Control in Each Effect (1.5 marks)**

- Each effect has a $\Delta P$ level transmitter connected to a PI controller that throttles the thick-liquor outflow valve ($L_1$, $L_2$, $L_3$).
- For effect 1, the level loop output is the **feed flow $F$** (or a fraction of it).
- For effects 2 and 3, the level loops manipulate the inter-effect liquor transfer valves.
- **Justification:** Stable level is essential for stable boiling. Without level control, fluctuations in $h_i$ alter hydrostatic head, change $\Delta T_{\text{loss}}$, and disturb the concentration loop.

**(iii) Pressure / Temperature Control in Each Effect (1.5 marks)**

- Pressure transmitters on the vapor head of each effect feed PI controllers that throttle the vapor line going to the next effect (or to the condenser for effect 3).
- **Justification:** Pressure directly determines boiling temperature via the Clausius-Clapeyron relation, hence the temperature driving force $\Delta T_i$. Stable pressure → stable heat-transfer rate.

**(iv) Feedforward from Feed Flow $F$ (1.5 marks)**

- The feed flow transmitter output is multiplied by a feedforward gain $K_{ff}$ and added to the steam-flow controller set-point.
- **Justification:** A 10 % rise in $F$ will, at steady state, demand a 10 % rise in $S$ to maintain $x_N$. Feedforward applies this correction *instantaneously*, while feedback from $x_N$ would only correct after the analyser delay (often 1–5 minutes).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often forget to specify the **type of controller action** (direct vs. reverse). For the concentration loop, the controller must be **REVERSE acting** — when $x_N$ rises above set-point, the controller must *decrease* $S$. The steam-flow slave must be **DIRECT acting** when its PV is steam flow. Similarly, level loops on the discharge side of an effect are typically **DIRECT acting**. Examiners deduct **1 mark per missing action** specification.

---

### Question B-B: **OPTION B** (14 Marks) — *Dynamic Modelling and PID Tuning of the Concentration Loop*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2, Q-pattern adapted]**

The product concentration loop of a double-effect evaporator has been experimentally step-tested. A 2 % increase in steam flow $S$ caused the product concentration $x_N$ to rise by 0.5 % (mass fraction) at steady state. The response was first-order with a time constant of $\tau = 360$ s, and the analyser introduced a dead time of $t_d = 45$ s.

#### Part (a) — Develop the FOPDT Transfer Function and Compute the Process Gain (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute the steady-state process gain** (2 marks)

$$K_p = \frac{\Delta x_N \, (\text{as fraction})}{\Delta S \, (\text{as fraction of full scale})}$$

$$K_p = \frac{+0.005}{+0.02}$$

$$\boxed{K_p = 0.25 \text{ (dimensionless, per unit steam change)}}$$

**Step 2 — Write the FOPDT transfer function** (2 marks)

$$G_p(s) = \frac{K_p \, e^{-t_d s}}{\tau s + 1} = \frac{0.25 \, e^{-45 s}}{360 s + 1}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the normalised dead time** (1.5 marks)

$$\theta = \frac{t_d}{\tau} = \frac{45}{360} = 0.125$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Cohen-Coon tuning rule for a PI controller** (1.5 marks)

The Cohen-Coon PI settings for a FOPDT process are:

$$K_c = \frac{1}{K_p} \cdot \frac{\tau}{t_d} \left( 0.9 + \frac{\theta}{12} \right)$$

$$\tau_I = t_d \cdot \frac{30 + 3\theta}{9 + 20\theta}$$

Substituting $\tau = 360$, $t_d = 45$, $\theta = 0.125$, $K_p = 0.25$:

$$K_c = \frac{1}{0.25} \cdot \frac{360}{45} \left( 0.9 + \frac{0.125}{12} \right)$$

$$K_c = 4 \cdot 8 \cdot (0.9 + 0.0104)$$

$$K_c = 32 \cdot 0.9104$$

$$\boxed{K_c \approx 29.1}$$

$$\tau_I = 45 \cdot \frac{30 + 0.375}{9 + 2.5}$$

$$\tau_I = 45 \cdot \frac{30.375}{11.5}$$

$$\tau_I = 45 \cdot 2.6413$$

$$\boxed{\tau_I \approx 118.9 \text{ s}}$$

#### Part (b) — Discuss the Limitations of the FOPDT Model and Suggest Improvements (7 marks)

**Model Answer (broken into scoring sub-points):**

**(i) Linearity Assumption (2 marks):** The FOPDT model assumes the process is **linear and time-invariant**. In reality, the relationship $x_N = F x_0 / (F - S)$ is **nonlinear** — the process gain $K_p = F x_0 / (F - S)^2$ increases sharply as $S$ approaches $F$. A controller tuned at one operating point will be sluggish or unstable at another. **Improvement:** Use **gain scheduling** — different PI tunings for different operating regions.

**(ii) Single-Loop Assumption (2 marks):** The FOPDT model treats steam $S$ as the only input. In reality, the loop is **coupled** to feed flow $F$, feed concentration $x_0$, and pressures in each effect. The other loops (level, pressure) interact with the concentration loop. **Improvement:** Use a **multivariable controller (MPC)** or at least add **feedforward compensation** from $F$ and $x_0$.

**(iii) Neglect of Slow Drift (1.5 marks):** FOPDT assumes constant parameters, but **fouling** of heat-transfer surfaces causes $U$ to drift slowly over weeks. This causes the optimal $K_c$ to change with time. **Improvement:** Implement **adaptive tuning** or periodic re-tuning based on $U$ estimation.

**(iv) Analyser Dead Time (1.5 marks):** The 45 s analyser dead time dominates the loop's performance. **Improvement:** Replace the slow density analyser with an **inferential sensor** — compute $x_N$ from measurements of $T_b$, $P$, and $F$ using a software model based on Duhring's rule. This eliminates most of the dead time and can dramatically improve closed-loop performance.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (B-B):** A common mistake is to **omit the dead-time term $e^{-t_d s}$** and use ordinary PI tuning formulas meant for first-order *no-delay* processes. The Cohen-Coon formula explicitly requires $t_d > 0$. If you forget the dead time, the loop will oscillate at start-up and you will lose **at least 2 marks**. Also, **report the controller action** (direct/reverse) — concentration loop is reverse-acting.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ An evaporator is a **heat-transfer device** that concentrates a solution by boiling off the solvent. The control goal is to maintain **product concentration, levels, pressures, and flows** simultaneously.
- ✅ The five canonical control loops are: **(1) concentration/density, (2) level in each effect, (3) pressure in each effect, (4) steam flow, (5) feed flow**.
- ✅ The **primary** (quality) loop is concentration; it is typically configured as a **cascade** with the steam-flow loop as the slave.
- ✅ **Feedforward control** from feed flow $F$ to steam set-point $S$ is the most common enhancement, giving a **feedforward + feedback** structure.
- ✅ **Multiple-effect evaporators** use the vapor from one effect as the heating medium for the next, multiplying the **steam economy** by approximately the number of effects.
- ✅ **Boiling Point Elevation (BPE)** is the increase in boiling point of a solution over pure water at the same pressure; it is predicted by **Duhring's Rule**: $T_b = a(x) + b(x) T_w$.
- ✅ BPE **reduces the available $\Delta T$** for heat transfer; the total available driving force is $(T_s - T_c) - \sum \text{BPE}_i - \sum \text{losses}_i$.
- ✅ The energy balance on each effect is $Q_i = V_i \lambda_i = U_i A_i \Delta T_i$.
- ✅ The mass balance on each effect is $F_i = V_i + L_i$, and the solute balance is $F_i x_{i-1} = L_i x_i$.
- ✅ Final thick liquor flow: $L_N = F (1 - x_0 / x_N)$. Total water evaporated: $V_{\text{total}} = F - L_N$.
- ✅ **Steam economy** for an $N$-effect evaporator is theoretically $N$ (kg water per kg steam); in practice it is 0.7N to 0.9N.
- ✅ Concentration loop transfer function: $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p e^{-t_d s}}{\tau s + 1}$, where $K_p = F x_0 / (F - S)^2$.
- ✅ **Controller action:** concentration loop → **REVERSE**; steam flow slave → **DIRECT**; discharge level → **DIRECT**; feed flow → **REVERSE** (typically).
- ✅ **Fouling, BPE changes, and analyser dead time** are the three most common causes of poor evaporator control in industry.
- ✅ For complex plants with strong interactions, use **multivariable (MPC)** control. For simple plants, cascade + feedforward is sufficient.
- ✅ **Exam tip:** Always state **measured variable, set-point, manipulated variable, controller action, and controller type (P/PI/PID)** for every loop you describe — missing any one of these costs marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feedback Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT864 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN LARGE SCALE INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Feedback Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Feedback Control** is a control mechanism in which the output of a system is measured, fed back (returned) to the input side, and compared with the desired reference (setpoint) value. The resulting **error signal** — the difference between the reference and the measured output — is then used by a controller to drive the actual output toward the desired value.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (aligned with NEP 2020 outcome-based pedagogy), feedback control belongs to the broader family of **Closed-Loop Control Systems** (also called **Automatic Control Systems**), as opposed to **Open-Loop Systems** which have no self-correcting action.

Mathematically, the governing relationship at the comparator (summing junction) is:

$$
e(t) = r(t) - c(t)
$$

where:
- $e(t)$ = error signal
- $r(t)$ = reference (setpoint) input
- $c(t)$ = measured/process output

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of driving a car on a highway at 80 km/h.**
> - **Open-loop:** You press the accelerator to a fixed position and hope the car stays at 80 km/h regardless of slope, wind, or load. It will not.
> - **Closed-loop (Feedback):** You constantly look at the **speedometer** (sensor), compare it with **80 km/h** (setpoint), and adjust the accelerator (actuator). If the car slows down on a hill, you press harder; on a downhill, you ease off. The *feedback* (speedometer reading) automatically *corrects* your action.

This is exactly how industrial feedback control works: **Measure → Compare → Correct → Repeat**.

### 1.3 Why Feedback Control is Indispensable in Large-Scale Industries

| Industrial Challenge | Role of Feedback Control |
|---|---|
| Disturbances (load changes, ambient temperature) | Rejects disturbances automatically |
| Parameter drift (aging of equipment) | Maintains desired output regardless of plant variation |
| Setpoint changes (different product grades) | Tracks new references accurately |
| Safety and stability | Prevents runaway conditions in reactors, boilers, turbines |
| Product quality consistency | Tight regulation of temperature, pressure, flow, level |

### 1.4 Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop — Key Distinctions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Students must clearly differentiate **Open-Loop** and **Closed-Loop** systems — this is a guaranteed 3-mark question.

| Parameter | Open-Loop System | Closed-Loop (Feedback) System |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback path | **Absent** | **Present** |
| Error detection | No | Yes (via comparator) |
| Disturbance rejection | Poor / None | Excellent |
| Accuracy | Low (depends on calibration) | High (self-correcting) |
| Stability | Generally stable | Can become unstable if poorly tuned |
| Complexity | Simple, cheap | Complex, costly |
| Bandwidth | Wide (fast) | Limited by feedback dynamics |
| Examples | Toaster, washing machine (timer), traffic light | Boiler drum level, distillation column temp., servo motor |

### 1.5 Types of Feedback

1. **Negative Feedback** — The feedback signal is *subtracted* from the reference. This is the **overwhelmingly dominant** form in process control because it:
   - Reduces error,
   - Improves stability,
   - Linearizes system behaviour,
   - Reduces sensitivity to parameter variations.

2. **Positive Feedback** — The feedback signal is *added* to the reference. Used rarely (e.g., oscillators, regenerative circuits), and can drive a system to instability if unchecked.

> [!WARNING]
> In **large-scale industries (chemical, petrochemical, power)**, **negative feedback is used in virtually every regulatory control loop**. Positive feedback is generally a fault condition to be avoided.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Components of a Feedback Control Loop

A feedback control loop is composed of five essential building blocks:

1. **Process / Plant (G)** — The physical system to be controlled (e.g., heat exchanger, chemical reactor, distillation column). Its dynamics are described by a **Transfer Function** $G_p(s)$.

2. **Sensor / Measuring Element (H)** — Measures the controlled variable (temperature, pressure, flow, level, composition) and converts it into a comparable signal (commonly a 4–20 mA current loop in industry).

3. **Comparator / Summing Junction** — Subtracts the measured feedback signal from the reference to produce the **error signal** $e(t)$.

4. **Controller (Gc)** — The "brain" of the loop. Implements a control law (P, PI, PD, or PID) to convert the error into a corrective **manipulated variable (MV)**.

5. **Final Control Element / Actuator** — A control valve, variable speed drive, or thyristor that physically changes the process input (steam flow, coolant flow, fuel flow, electrical power).

### 2.2 Block Diagram Algebra — Fundamental Rules

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Block diagram reduction is essential for deriving the closed-loop transfer function. Master these rules — they appear every semester.

| Rule | Block Diagram | Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **Cascade (series)** | $X \to [G_1] \to [G_2] \to Y$ | $Y/X = G_1 \cdot G_2$ |
| **Parallel** | Two blocks fed by same input, outputs summed | $Y/X = G_1 \pm G_2$ |
| **Feedback loop** | Forward path $G$, feedback path $H$ | Closed-loop TF $= \dfrac{G}{1 \pm GH}$ |
| **Moving a summing junction beyond a block** | Re-architecture | Equivalent block remains $G$ |
| **Moving a take-off point beyond a block** | Re-architecture | Equivalent block remains $G$ |

### 2.3 Derivation of the Closed-Loop Transfer Function (Negative Feedback)

The standard unity-feedback closed-loop transfer function is derived in Section 3 below. The general form for a non-unity feedback is:

$$
T(s) = \frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}
$$

with negative feedback, and:

$$
T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 - G(s)H(s)}
$$

with positive feedback.

### 2.4 Standard First-Order and Second-Order Closed-Loop Forms

For a first-order plant $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$ with unity feedback and proportional controller $K_c$, the closed-loop transfer function is:

$$
T(s) = \frac{K_c K / (1 + K_c K)}{\frac{\tau}{1+K_c K} s + 1}
$$

This shows how the feedback loop:
- **Reduces DC gain** from $K$ to $\dfrac{K_c K}{1+K_c K}$,
- **Reduces time constant** from $\tau$ to $\dfrac{\tau}{1+K_c K}$ → **faster response**.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the following formulas — they are guaranteed to appear in KTU Board Examinations.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Error signal | $e(t) = r(t) - c(t)$ | Time domain definition |
| 2 | Laplace-domain error | $E(s) = R(s) - C(s)$ | Used in analysis |
| 3 | Open-loop TF | $G_{OL}(s) = G_c(s) \, G_p(s) \, G_v(s)$ | Product of all forward blocks |
| 4 | Loop gain | $L(s) = G(s)\,H(s)$ | Product of forward & feedback path |
| 5 | Closed-loop TF (negative FB) | $T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ | Standard form |
| 6 | Closed-loop TF (positive FB) | $T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 - G(s)H(s)}$ | Rare, unstable tendency |
| 7 | Unity negative feedback | $T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$ | When $H(s)=1$ |
| 8 | Error TF | $\dfrac{E(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{1}{1+G(s)H(s)}$ | For unity $H=1$: $\dfrac{1}{1+G(s)}$ |
| 9 | Characteristic equation | $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ | Roots determine stability |
| 10 | Sensitivity (steady-state) | $S_G^T = \dfrac{\partial T/T}{\partial G/G} = \dfrac{1}{1+GH}$ | Feedback reduces sensitivity |
| 11 | Steady-state error (step input) | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1+K_p}$ | $K_p = \lim_{s\to 0} G(s)H(s)$ |
| 12 | Steady-state error (ramp input) | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{K_v}$ | $K_v = \lim_{s\to 0} s G(s)H(s)$ |
| 13 | PID controller output | $u(t) = K_c\!\left[e + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I}\!\int e\,dt + \tau_D \dfrac{de}{dt}\right]$ | Classical form |
| 14 | PID transfer function | $G_c(s) = K_c\!\left[1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I s} + \tau_D s\right]$ | Laplace form |
| 15 | PI transfer function | $G_c(s) = K_c\!\left[1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I s}\right]$ | Eliminates steady-state error |
| 16 | PD transfer function | $G_c(s) = K_c\!\left[1 + \tau_D s\right]$ | Improves transient response |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Feedback control is the **backbone of modern process industries**:
- **Oil refineries** — distillation column temperature, reflux flow, top/bottom product composition.
- **Power plants** — boiler drum level, steam temperature, generator frequency.
- **Chemical plants** — reactor temperature, pH, concentration.
- **Paper mills** — basis weight, moisture, caliper.
- **Cement, steel, glass** — furnace temperature, oxygen concentration.

Without feedback, none of these industries could achieve the **product consistency, safety, and energy efficiency** required at modern production scales.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Closed-Loop Transfer Function (Negative Feedback)

We derive the canonical relation from first principles using block diagram algebra.

**Step 1 — Identify the forward path and the feedback path.**

Let the forward-path transfer function be $G(s)$ (containing controller, plant, and actuator combined). Let the feedback-path transfer function be $H(s)$ (containing the sensor).

**Step 2 — Write the input/output relation of each block.**

The actuator input is the controller output, which is the error $E(s)$ multiplied by the controller $G_c(s)$. For brevity, we group controller + plant + actuator into one block $G(s) = G_c(s)\,G_p(s)\,G_v(s)$.

Therefore:
- Actuator input = controller output
- Process output: $C(s) = G(s)\cdot U_c(s)$
- Feedback signal: $B(s) = H(s)\cdot C(s)$

**Step 3 — Apply the comparator equation.**

At the summing junction, the feedback is **subtracted** from the reference:

$$
E(s) = R(s) - B(s)
$$

Substituting $B(s) = H(s)\,C(s)$:

$$
E(s) = R(s) - H(s)\,C(s)
$$

**Step 4 — Express the controller output.**

$$
U_c(s) = G_c(s)\cdot E(s) \quad \text{(but in our grouped form } C(s) = G(s) \cdot E(s)\text{)}
$$

Wait — when we group everything, the controller is *inside* $G(s)$, so:

$$
C(s) = G(s)\cdot E(s)
$$

**Step 5 — Combine.**

$$
C(s) = G(s)\bigl[R(s) - H(s)\,C(s)\bigr]
$$

Expand the bracket:

$$
C(s) = G(s)\,R(s) - G(s)\,H(s)\,C(s)
$$

Bring the $C(s)$ terms to the left-hand side:

$$
C(s) + G(s)\,H(s)\,C(s) = G(s)\,R(s)
$$

Factor out $C(s)$ on the left:

$$
C(s)\,\bigl[1 + G(s)\,H(s)\bigr] = G(s)\,R(s)
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the closed-loop transfer function $T(s) = C(s)/R(s)$.**

$$
T(s) = \frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)\,H(s)}
$$

This is the **canonical closed-loop transfer function for negative feedback** — a relation every KTU student must memorize.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check (unity feedback, $H=1$):** $T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1+G(s)}$. For a DC gain check, set $s=0$: if $G(0)=K$, then $T(0) = K/(1+K)$, which is less than 1 (i.e., <100%) — consistent with the *load regulation* property of feedback.

### 3.2 Derivation — Sensitivity of Closed-Loop to Plant Variation

Define sensitivity $S_G^T = \dfrac{\partial T / T}{\partial G / G}$.

**Step 1.** Differentiate $T(s) = G/(1+GH)$ with respect to $G$:

$$
\frac{\partial T}{\partial G} = \frac{(1+GH) - G\cdot H}{(1+GH)^2} = \frac{1}{(1+GH)^2}
$$

**Step 2.** Compute the logarithmic derivative:

$$
S_G^T = \frac{G}{T} \cdot \frac{\partial T}{\partial G}
$$

Substituting $T = G/(1+GH)$:

$$
S_G^T = \frac{G\,(1+GH)}{G} \cdot \frac{1}{(1+GH)^2}
$$

Simplify:

$$
S_G^T = \frac{1}{1+G(s)H(s)}
$$

**Interpretation:** A large loop gain $GH$ makes $|S_G^T| \ll 1$, meaning the closed-loop behaviour is *insensitive* to plant variations — a key reason feedback is so valuable in industry where plant parameters drift with age, temperature, and throughput.

### 3.3 Derivation — Steady-State Error for a Step Input (Type-0 System)

For a unity-feedback system with forward TF $G(s)$, the steady-state error to a unit step input $R(s) = 1/s$ is given by the **Final Value Theorem**.

**Step 1.** Error transfer function:

$$
\frac{E(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{1}{1+G(s)}
$$

**Step 2.** Error in s-domain:

$$
E(s) = \frac{1}{1+G(s)} \cdot \frac{1}{s}
$$

**Step 3.** Apply the Final Value Theorem:

$$
e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s\,E(s) = \lim_{s\to 0} \frac{s}{s\,[1+G(s)]} = \frac{1}{1+G(0)}
$$

Define the **position error constant** $K_p = G(0) = \lim_{s\to 0} G(s)$.

$$
\boxed{\,e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1+K_p}\,}
$$

**Interpretation:** If $K_p$ is large (as in a well-tuned feedback loop with integral action), $e_{ss} \to 0$. **Adding integral action drives steady-state error to zero** for step inputs — this is the central reason PI controllers dominate process industries.

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Worked Example — Closed-Loop TF of a Temperature Control Loop

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example (typical KTU exam-style problem):**
> A temperature control loop has the following components:
> - Process (heater + sensor): $G_p(s) = \dfrac{2}{5s+1}$
> - PI controller: $G_c(s) = 1.5\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{10s}\right)$
> - Sensor: $H(s) = 0.8$ (non-unity feedback, scale factor)
>
> **Find the closed-loop transfer function $T(s) = C(s)/R(s)$.**

**Step 1 — Combine the forward path.**

$$
G(s) = G_c(s)\cdot G_p(s) = 1.5\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{10s}\right)\cdot \frac{2}{5s+1}
$$

Simplify the PI controller:

$$
G_c(s) = 1.5\cdot \frac{10s+1}{10s} = \frac{1.5(10s+1)}{10s} = \frac{15s+1.5}{10s}
$$

Therefore:

$$
G(s) = \frac{15s+1.5}{10s}\cdot\frac{2}{5s+1} = \frac{2(15s+1.5)}{10s(5s+1)} = \frac{30s+3}{10s(5s+1)}
$$

Simplify:

$$
G(s) = \frac{3(10s+1)}{10s(5s+1)} = \frac{3(10s+1)}{50s^2+10s}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the closed-loop formula.**

$$
T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1+G(s)H(s)}
$$

Compute $G(s)H(s)$:

$$
G(s)H(s) = \frac{3(10s+1)\cdot 0.8}{10s(5s+1)} = \frac{2.4(10s+1)}{10s(5s+1)} = \frac{24s+2.4}{50s^2+10s}
$$

**Step 3 — Add 1 to $G(s)H(s)$:**

$$
1 + G(s)H(s) = 1 + \frac{24s+2.4}{50s^2+10s} = \frac{50s^2+10s + 24s + 2.4}{50s^2+10s}
$$

Combine numerator terms:

$$
1 + G(s)H(s) = \frac{50s^2 + 34s + 2.4}{50s^2 + 10s}
$$

**Step 4 — Form $T(s)$:**

$$
T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1+G(s)H(s)} = \frac{\dfrac{3(10s+1)}{10s(5s+1)}}{\dfrac{50s^2+34s+2.4}{50s^2+10s}}
$$

Cross-multiply (denominator of $T$ = denominator of $G$ × numerator of $1+GH$, etc.):

$$
T(s) = \frac{3(10s+1)(50s^2+10s)}{(10s)(5s+1)(50s^2+34s+2.4)}
$$

Simplify the numerator: $(10s+1)(50s^2+10s) = 500s^3 + 100s^2 + 50s^2 + 10s = 500s^3 + 150s^2 + 10s$.

$$
T(s) = \frac{3(500s^3+150s^2+10s)}{10s(5s+1)(50s^2+34s+2.4)} = \frac{1500s^3+450s^2+30s}{10s(5s+1)(50s^2+34s+2.4)}
$$

Cancel $10s$:

$$
T(s) = \frac{150s^3 + 45s^2 + 3}{(5s+1)(50s^2+34s+2.4)}
$$

This is the closed-loop transfer function. The characteristic polynomial $(5s+1)(50s^2+34s+2.4)$ determines stability (must have all roots with negative real parts for BIBO stability).

---

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define feedback control system. Differentiate between open-loop and closed-loop systems.**

**Model Answer:**

A **feedback control system** is one in which the output is continuously measured, compared with the desired reference, and the resulting error is used to generate a corrective control action.

| Feature | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop (Feedback) |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback path | Absent | Present |
| Disturbance rejection | None | Automatic |
| Accuracy | Low | High |
| Stability | Inherently stable | Requires careful design |
| Cost / complexity | Low | Higher |
| Example | Electric toaster | Boiler level controller |

*(3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**State the Mason's gain formula and write the closed-loop transfer function for a unity negative feedback system.**

**Model Answer:**

**Mason's Gain Formula:**

$$
T = \frac{1}{\Delta} \sum_{k=1}^{N} P_k \, \Delta_k
$$

where $P_k$ is the path gain of the $k^{th}$ forward path, $\Delta$ is the determinant of the graph, and $\Delta_k$ is the cofactor obtained by removing loops touching path $k$.

**Closed-loop TF for unity negative feedback:**

$$
T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}
$$

where $G(s)$ is the forward-path transfer function. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students frequently lose marks by (1) forgetting to write the **comparator equation explicitly** before reduction, (2) missing the **sign convention** (negative vs positive feedback), and (3) failing to mention the **physical meaning** of each block. Always **draw the block diagram first**, label every signal, and write the comparator equation as your starting step.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) (7 Marks)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the basic elements of a feedback control system. State the role of each element in the operation of an industrial temperature control loop.

**(b) (7 Marks)** For the block diagram shown below, derive the overall closed-loop transfer function $C(s)/R(s)$.

> *Forward path: $G_1(s) = \dfrac{1}{s+1}$, $G_2(s) = \dfrac{2}{s}$, $G_3(s) = \dfrac{5}{s+2}$ in cascade; inner feedback loop with $H_1(s)=1$; outer feedback with $H_2(s)=0.5$.*

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**(a) (7 Marks) — Basic Elements of a Feedback Control System:**

A feedback control system consists of the following elements (each must be explained):

1. **Reference Input / Setpoint (R):** The desired value of the controlled variable (e.g., 350 °C for a reactor).

2. **Comparator / Summing Junction:** Produces the actuating error $e(t) = r(t) - b(t)$, where $b(t)$ is the feedback signal.

3. **Controller (Gc):** Implements a control law — most commonly PID — to convert error into a corrective signal. In industry, this resides in a DCS (Distributed Control System) or PLC.

4. **Final Control Element / Actuator (Gv):** A pneumatic/hydraulic/electric device (control valve, VFD, thyristor) that physically adjusts the manipulated variable (steam, fuel, coolant flow).

5. **Process / Plant (Gp):** The physical system being controlled — e.g., the heat exchanger whose temperature we wish to regulate.

6. **Sensor / Transmitter (H):** Measures the controlled variable (RTD, thermocouple, orifice + DP cell) and provides a standardized feedback signal (typically 4–20 mA).

7. **Disturbance (D):** Any unwanted input affecting the process (ambient temperature changes, feed composition variation, fouling).

**Industrial Temperature Control Loop — Role of Each Element:**

In a reactor temperature control loop:
- **Setpoint** = desired reactor temperature (e.g., 200 °C).
- **Sensor (RTD)** measures reactor temperature and transmits 4–20 mA back to DCS.
- **DCS Controller** compares PV with SP and computes PID output.
- **Control Valve** modulates steam (or coolant) flow into the heating jacket.
- **Reactor (process)** responds with new temperature.
- **Disturbances:** feed temperature changes, exothermic reaction heat release.

*(7 marks — full marks awarded for diagram + explanation + industrial example)*

---

**(b) (7 Marks) — Derivation of Closed-Loop Transfer Function:**

**Step 1 — Identify the inner loop.**

The inner feedback loop comprises $G_1(s)$, $G_2(s)$, $G_3(s)$ in cascade with feedback $H_1(s)=1$.

The forward path of the inner loop is:

$$
G_{\text{inner}}(s) = G_1(s)\cdot G_2(s)\cdot G_3(s) = \frac{1}{s+1}\cdot\frac{2}{s}\cdot\frac{5}{s+2}
$$

$$
G_{\text{inner}}(s) = \frac{10}{s(s+1)(s+2)}
$$

**Step 2 — Reduce the inner unity-feedback loop:**

$$
G_{\text{inner,cl}}(s) = \frac{G_{\text{inner}}(s)}{1 + G_{\text{inner}}(s)\cdot H_1(s)} = \frac{\dfrac{10}{s(s+1)(s+2)}}{1 + \dfrac{10}{s(s+1)(s+2)}}
$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $s(s+1)(s+2)$:

$$
G_{\text{inner,cl}}(s) = \frac{10}{s(s+1)(s+2) + 10}
$$

Expand the cubic: $s(s+1)(s+2) = s(s^2+3s+2) = s^3 + 3s^2 + 2s$.

$$
G_{\text{inner,cl}}(s) = \frac{10}{s^3 + 3s^2 + 2s + 10}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply outer feedback with $H_2(s)=0.5$:**

$$
T(s) = \frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G_{\text{inner,cl}}(s)}{1 + G_{\text{inner,cl}}(s)\cdot H_2(s)}
$$

Substitute:

$$
T(s) = \frac{\dfrac{10}{s^3+3s^2+2s+10}}{1 + \dfrac{10}{s^3+3s^2+2s+10}\cdot 0.5}
$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $s^3+3s^2+2s+10$:

$$
T(s) = \frac{10}{s^3+3s^2+2s+10 + 5}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,T(s) = \frac{10}{s^3 + 3s^2 + 2s + 15}\,}
$$

*(7 marks — full marks for clear steps, correct expansion, and final simplified form)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a) (7 Marks)** Explain the effect of negative feedback on:
   (i) Overall gain,
   (ii) Sensitivity to plant parameter variations,
   (iii) Disturbance rejection,
   (iv) Bandwidth and stability.

**(b) (7 Marks)** A unity-feedback system has forward path transfer function:

$$
G(s) = \frac{K(s+3)}{s(s+1)(s+5)}
$$

Determine:
   (i) The type of the system.
   (ii) The static error constants $K_p$, $K_v$, $K_a$.
   (iii) The steady-state errors for unit step, unit ramp, and unit parabolic inputs.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**(a) (7 Marks) — Effects of Negative Feedback:**

**(i) Overall gain:** Reduces the open-loop gain by a factor of $(1 + GH)$. For a unity-feedback system with $H=1$:

$$
T(0) = \frac{K}{1+K} \quad \text{(reduced from } K\text{)}
$$

This is a deliberate trade-off — gain is sacrificed for accuracy and disturbance rejection.

**(ii) Sensitivity:** Reduced by a factor of $(1 + GH)$:

$$
S_G^T = \frac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)}
$$

For $|GH| \gg 1$, sensitivity approaches zero — closed-loop performance becomes nearly independent of plant drift.

**(iii) Disturbance rejection:** A disturbance $D(s)$ entering the plant produces an output:

$$
\frac{C(s)}{D(s)} = \frac{G_2(s)}{1 + G_1(s)G_2(s)H(s)}
$$

Large loop gain minimizes the effect of $D$.

**(iv) Bandwidth and stability:** Negative feedback *extends* bandwidth (useful for fast-tracking applications), but if loop gain is excessive, poles may cross into the right-half plane → instability. Proper compensation (PID tuning) is essential.

*(7 marks)*

---

**(b) (7 Marks) — Static Error Constants:**

**(i) System type:** Count the number of pure integrators (poles at origin) in $G(s)$. There is one factor $1/s$, so the system is **Type 1**.

**(ii) Static error constants:**

$$
K_p = \lim_{s \to 0} G(s) = \lim_{s\to 0} \frac{K(s+3)}{s(s+1)(s+5)} = \frac{K\cdot 3}{0} = \infty
$$

$$
K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} s\cdot G(s) = \lim_{s\to 0} \frac{K(s+3)}{(s+1)(s+5)} = \frac{K \cdot 3}{1 \cdot 5} = \frac{3K}{5}
$$

$$
K_a = \lim_{s \to 0} s^2 \cdot G(s) = \lim_{s\to 0} \frac{s\cdot K(s+3)}{(s+1)(s+5)} = 0
$$

**(iii) Steady-state errors:**

For **Type 1** system:

- Unit step input → $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1+K_p} = \dfrac{1}{1+\infty} = 0$
- Unit ramp input → $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{K_v} = \dfrac{5}{3K}$
- Unit parabolic input → $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{K_a} = \dfrac{1}{0} = \infty$

*(7 marks — full marks for correct identification of system type, application of limit formulas, and final numerical results)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Feedback Control**

- **Definition:** Feedback control = measure output, compare with reference, correct via controller.
- **Negative feedback dominates industry** because it stabilizes, linearizes, and rejects disturbances.
- **Closed-loop TF (negative feedback):** $T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$
- **Closed-loop TF (positive feedback):** $T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 - G(s)H(s)}$
- **Comparator equation:** Always start with $e(t) = r(t) - c(t)$ before any block diagram reduction.
- **Block diagram reduction rules:** Series multiply, parallel add/subtract, feedback formula.
- **Characteristic equation:** $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ — roots govern stability.
- **Sensitivity reduction:** $S_G^T = \dfrac{1}{1+GH}$ — large loop gain ⇒ low sensitivity.
- **Steady-state error formulas (unity FB):**
  - Step input: $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1+K_p}$, with $K_p = \lim_{s\to 0}G(s)$.
  - Ramp input: $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{K_v}$, with $K_v = \lim_{s\to 0}sG(s)$.
  - Parabolic input: $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{K_a}$, with $K_a = \lim_{s\to 0}s^2 G(s)$.
- **System type:** Number of pure integrations ($1/s$ poles) in $G(s)$.
  - Type 0 → finite step error, infinite ramp error.
  - Type 1 → zero step error, finite ramp error, infinite parabolic error.
  - Type 2 → zero step & ramp errors, finite parabolic error.
- **Controllers:**
  - **P-only:** Reduces error but never eliminates it; faster response, less stable at high gain.
  - **PI:** Eliminates steady-state error for steps; widely used in process industries.
  - **PD:** Improves transient response, never eliminates steady-state error.
  - **PID:** Combines all three — the workhorse of large-scale industrial control.
- **Industrial applications:** Boiler drum level, distillation temperature, pH control, furnace pressure, flow regulation, reactor temperature, paper machine basis weight.
- **Pitfalls to avoid in KTU exam:**
  - Forgetting the **sign** in the feedback formula.
  - Skipping the **block diagram** in long answers.
  - Not specifying whether feedback is **unity** or **non-unity**.
  - Writing the final closed-loop TF without showing intermediate simplifications.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Notes — Module 2 / Topic: Feedback Control**
> **Course:** PEICT864 — Instrumentation and Control in Large Scale Industries
> **Scheme:** KTU 2024 (NEP 2020 Aligned)

topics.title: Cascade Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT864 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN LARGE SCALE INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cascade Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cascade Control** is a multi-loop (specifically, two-loop) advanced control strategy in which the output (manipulated variable) of one controller — the **Primary (Master / Outer) Controller** — is dynamically used as the **remote setpoint** for a second controller — the **Secondary (Slave / Inner) Controller**. The two control loops are nested, with the inner (secondary) loop enclosed within the outer (primary) loop.

Mathematically, the cascade architecture employs **two measurement transmitters**, **two feedback controllers**, and **two process transfer functions** operating in a hierarchical, nested configuration. The inner loop is deliberately designed to be **substantially faster** than the outer loop — typically by a factor of **3 to 5 times** — to provide rapid rejection of disturbances that enter the secondary process before they can propagate into the primary (controlled) variable.

The two key process variables are:
- **Primary Variable (PV₁ / Controlled Variable):** The final process output that must be maintained at the desired setpoint (e.g., reactor temperature, column overhead composition).
- **Secondary Variable (PV₂ / Inner Variable):** An intermediate variable that is causally linked to the primary variable and responds quickly to the manipulated variable (e.g., jacket coolant flow, reflux flow, feed preheater outlet temperature).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Emphasis:** The board examiner typically frames cascade control as an *enhancement of single-loop feedback* used when a single-loop PID cannot adequately reject major measurable disturbances, or when the manipulated variable has a slow/non-linear effect on the controlled variable.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy: "The Cruise Control on a Truck Pulling a Trailer"

Imagine you are driving a heavy truck pulling a fragile trailer. Your goal is to keep the **truck's speed at exactly 60 km/h** (this is the **primary variable**). 

- **You** are the **primary controller** watching the speedometer (primary transmitter). You can only control the **gas pedal position**.
- The **truck's engine RPM** (secondary variable) responds almost **instantaneously** to gas pedal movement, and disturbances like wind, road incline, and trailer drag all first show up as RPM fluctuations **before** the truck's speed is noticeably affected.
- The **engine control unit (ECU)** is the **secondary controller**. It watches the RPM via a tachometer (secondary transmitter) and rapidly adjusts the fuel injection so that the RPM exactly matches *whatever target* you (the driver) demand via the gas pedal.
- In this analogy, **you are not directly commanding the engine's fuel flow** — you are simply telling the ECU *"I want 1800 RPM for 60 km/h."* The ECU then makes sure that, *no matter what disturbance hits the engine*, the RPM tracks 1800.

**Disturbance rejection hierarchy:**
- A disturbance hitting the **engine** (e.g., sudden headwind) → caught and annihilated by the **ECU (inner loop)** within milliseconds. **You never even notice it.**
- A disturbance that escapes the engine (e.g., long uphill grade affecting truck speed) → reaches the **speedometer** → **you (outer loop)** make a slow correction to the RPM demand.

This is **exactly** what cascade control achieves in a process plant: a fast inner loop absorbs the bulk of disturbances locally, leaving only a clean, slow, and well-behaved process for the outer loop to manage.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants, Design Ratios & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical KTU Value | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Time-constant ratio (τ₁ / τ₂)** | $\tau_1 \geq 3\tau_2$ to $5\tau_2$ | Inner loop must be **3–5× faster** than outer loop |
| **Secondary loop gain product** | $K_{c2} K_{p2} \approx \mathbf{0.5}$ to $\mathbf{1.0}$ | Inner loop critically/over-damped, fast response |
| **Secondary integral time** | $\tau_{I2} \approx \tau_{p2}$ | Inner loop integral ≈ inner process time constant |
| **Dead time ratio (L₂ / L₁)** | $L_2 < L_1$ ideally | Inner loop dead time must be **smaller** than outer |
| **Process type** | **SISO, Nested, Series** | Two single-loop controllers in series-nested arrangement |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection Criterion for Cascade:** A cascade control scheme is justified when (1) measurable disturbances can be detected *before* they affect the primary variable, and (2) the inner loop can be made substantially faster (lower time constant and dead time) than the outer loop.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The "How" and "Why" of Cascade Control

Cascade control is a structural, hierarchical enhancement of classical single-loop feedback. The following structured logic captures its operational anatomy:

- **Step 1 — Recognition of Limitation in Single-Loop Control**
  - **Why:** A single PID controller viewing only the primary variable cannot *anticipate* disturbances entering the secondary process. The disturbance must first corrupt the primary variable, be detected by the transmitter, and *only then* does the controller act.
  - **Consequence:** Long corrective action window → large deviation, poor ISE/IAE performance.
  - **How Cascade Helps:** The inner loop acts *immediately* on the disturbance, drastically reducing the deviation seen by the outer loop.

- **Step 2 — Identification of a Suitable Secondary Variable**
  - **Why:** We need an *intermediate* variable that is:
    (a) Causally downstream of the manipulated variable (so it can be controlled),
    (b) Causally upstream of the primary variable (so its control *indirectly* controls the primary variable),
    (c) Significantly **faster** (smaller τ and L) than the primary variable,
    (d) Reliably measurable.
  - **Examples:** Coolant *flow* as secondary variable for reactor *temperature* as primary variable; reflux *flow* for column *overhead temperature*; feed *preheater outlet temperature* for column *feed temperature*.

- **Step 3 — Construction of the Inner (Secondary) Loop**
  - **How:** Install a flow/pressure transmitter, a local PID controller, and close the loop on the secondary variable. Tune it **first**, with the primary controller placed in **MANUAL** mode and its output held constant.
  - **Why First?** The inner loop must be stable and fast *independently* before it can be treated as a "fast actuator" for the outer loop.

- **Step 4 — Construction of the Outer (Primary) Loop**
  - **How:** The primary controller's output (4–20 mA or 0–100%) becomes the **remote setpoint** of the secondary controller. The primary controller is then tuned with the secondary loop in **AUTOMATIC** mode.
  - **Why Last?** The secondary loop, once tuned, behaves approximately as a **first-order lag** with reduced effective dead time, simplifying primary loop tuning.

- **Step 5 — Performance Verification & Commissioning**
  - **How:** Step-test the plant and observe:
    (a) Disturbance rejection in the secondary loop (should be much faster than primary),
    (b) Setpoint tracking at the primary loop,
    (c) Stability margins (gain margin > 1.7, phase margin > 30°).

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are written in LaTeX and assume unity-gain transmitters ($G_{m1} = G_{m2} = 1$) unless stated otherwise. The "$\vert$" symbol in tables is rendered as $\mid$ to comply with the markdown table pipe-restriction rule.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Secondary (Inner) Closed-Loop TF** | $T_2(s) = \dfrac{G_{c2}(s)\, G_{p2}(s)}{1 + G_{c2}(s)\, G_{m2}(s)\, G_{p2}(s)}$ | Dimensionless, $\lim_{s\to 0} T_2(0) = 1$ for integral action in $G_{c2}$ |
| 2 | **Overall Closed-Loop TF (Y/R)** | $\dfrac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G_{c1}\, T_2\, G_{p1}}{1 + G_{c1}\, G_{m1}\, T_2\, G_{p1}}$ | Setpoint tracking response |
| 3 | **Primary Disturbance Rejection (Y/D₁)** | $\dfrac{Y(s)}{D_1(s)} = \dfrac{1}{1 + G_{c1}\, G_{m1}\, T_2\, G_{p1}}$ | $D_1$ enters **after** inner loop |
| 4 | **Secondary Disturbance Rejection (Y/D₂)** | $\dfrac{Y(s)}{D_2(s)} = \dfrac{G_{p1}}{(1 + G_{c1}\, G_{m1}\, T_2\, G_{p1})(1 + G_{c2}\, G_{m2}\, G_{p2})}$ | $D_2$ enters **inside** inner loop; denominator has *two* $(1 + \text{loop-gain})$ factors |
| 5 | **Inner Loop Equivalent Process (FOPDT approx.)** | $G_{p,eq} \approx K_{p2}\, e^{-L_2 s} \big/ (\tau_2 s + 1)$ for $\tau_2 \gg L_2$ | First-Order Plus Dead Time |
| 6 | **PI Controller (Industry Standard)** | $G_c(s) = K_c\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I s}\right)$ | $K_c$ = proportional gain, $\tau_I$ = integral time (min) |
| 7 | **Ziegler–Nichols Tuning (Reaction Curve)** | $K_c = \dfrac{1.2\,\tau_p}{K_p\,L}, \quad \tau_I = 2\,L$ | Open-loop step test, FOPDT fit |
| 8 | **Inner Loop Aggressive Tuning (Lambda $\lambda$ method)** | $K_{c2} = \dfrac{\tau_{p2}}{K_{p2}(\lambda_2 + L_2)}, \quad \tau_{I2} = \tau_{p2}$ | Set $\lambda_2 \approx 0.1\,\tau_{p2}$ for *fast* inner loop |
| 9 | **Outer Loop Lambda Tuning** | $K_{c1} = \dfrac{\tau_{p1}^{eq}}{K_{p}^{eq}(\lambda_1 + L_1^{eq})}, \quad \tau_{I1} = \tau_{p1}^{eq}$ | $\tau_{p1}^{eq}$ = effective process time after inner closure |
| 10 | **Time-Constant Separation Rule** | $\dfrac{\tau_1}{\tau_2} \geq 3 \quad \text{and} \quad \dfrac{L_1}{L_2} \geq 3$ | Ensures dynamic *decoupling* of loops; avoids loop interaction |

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility of Cascade Control

Cascade control is a **workhorse strategy** in continuous process industries. Common industrial deployments include:

1. **Jacketed CSTR Reactor Temperature Control**
   - Primary: Reactor temperature (slow, large τ).
   - Secondary: Jacket outlet (or inlet) temperature (faster, directly affected by steam/coolant valve).
   - *Disadvantage avoided:* Sudden coolant pressure fluctuations are absorbed by the inner temperature loop.

2. **Distillation Column Overhead Composition Control**
   - Primary: Tray temperature or online analyzer (slow, large L).
   - Secondary: Reflux drum level *or* reflux flow rate.
   - *Disadvantage avoided:* Reflux pump surges no longer propagate to composition.

3. **Steam-Heated Heat Exchanger Outlet Temperature**
   - Primary: Process outlet temperature.
   - Secondary: Steam flow rate.
   - *Disadvantage avoided:* Upstream steam pressure variation (a huge industrial nuisance) is neutralized by the flow loop.

4. **Boiler Drum Level–Flow Cascade**
   - Primary: Drum level (3-element control: level, steam flow, feedwater flow).
   - Secondary: Feedwater flow control valve.

5. **Compressor Surge / Antisurge Control**
   - Primary: Surge control valve position.
   - Secondary: Compressor differential pressure / flow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Cascade control is a form of *feedforward-feedback hybridization at the structural level.* The inner loop effectively converts a *non-linear, slow, dead-time-dominated* final element + secondary process into a *fast, well-behaved, near-linear* equivalent process. This dramatically improves the **disturbance rejection** and **robustness** of the outer loop.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Inner-Loop Transfer Function $T_2(s)$

We begin with the **secondary (inner) loop** in isolation. The block diagram is:

$$R_2(s) \longrightarrow \bigotimes \longrightarrow G_{c2}(s) \longrightarrow G_{v}(s) \longrightarrow G_{p2}(s) \longrightarrow X(s)$$
$$\hspace{4.5cm} \uparrow \hspace{3cm} \downarrow$$
$$\hspace{4.5cm} G_{m2}(s) \longleftarrow \hspace{3cm} \hspace{3cm}$$

where $R_2(s)$ is the *remote setpoint* (output of primary controller), $G_v(s)$ is the control valve, and $X(s)$ is the secondary variable.

**Step 1.1 — Define the control error in the secondary loop:**
$$E_2(s) = R_2(s) - G_{m2}(s)\, X(s)$$

**Step 1.2 — Write the controller output (after valve):**
$$M(s) = G_{c2}(s)\, G_v(s)\, E_2(s)$$

For analytical simplicity (and per KTU board convention), let $G_v(s) = 1$ (ideal valve, or absorb into $G_{c2}$). Then:
$$M(s) = G_{c2}(s)\, E_2(s)$$

**Step 1.3 — Write the process output:**
$$X(s) = G_{p2}(s)\, M(s) + D_2(s)$$
$$X(s) = G_{p2}(s)\, G_{c2}(s)\, E_2(s) + D_2(s)$$

**Step 1.4 — Substitute $E_2(s) = R_2(s) - G_{m2}(s)\, X(s)$:**
$$X(s) = G_{p2}(s)\, G_{c2}(s)\bigl[R_2(s) - G_{m2}(s)\, X(s)\bigr] + D_2(s)$$

**Step 1.5 — Expand and collect $X(s)$ terms on the left:**
$$X(s) = G_{p2}\, G_{c2}\, R_2 - G_{p2}\, G_{c2}\, G_{m2}\, X + D_2$$
$$X(s)\bigl[1 + G_{p2}\, G_{c2}\, G_{m2}\bigr] = G_{p2}\, G_{c2}\, R_2 + D_2$$

**Step 1.6 — Solve for $X(s)$:**
$$X(s) = \underbrace{\dfrac{G_{c2}\, G_{p2}}{1 + G_{c2}\, G_{m2}\, G_{p2}}}_{T_2(s)} R_2(s) + \underbrace{\dfrac{1}{1 + G_{c2}\, G_{m2}\, G_{p2}}}_{S_2(s)} D_2(s)$$

**Final Result — Inner-loop setpoint tracking TF:**
$$\boxed{\,T_2(s) = \dfrac{X(s)}{R_2(s)} = \dfrac{G_{c2}(s)\, G_{p2}(s)}{1 + G_{c2}(s)\, G_{m2}(s)\, G_{p2}(s)}\,}$$

> **Interpretation:** The inner loop has *unit DC gain* (assuming integral action in $G_{c2}$), meaning $X(s)$ tracks $R_2(s)$ in steady state with zero offset. This is the *single most important property* of the inner loop — it acts as a **faithful, fast follower** to the primary controller's commands.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Overall Closed-Loop Transfer Function $Y(s)/R(s)$

The **primary loop** views the inner closed-loop transfer $T_2(s)$ as its "manipulated pathway." The full cascade block diagram is:

$$R(s) \longrightarrow \bigotimes \longrightarrow G_{c1}(s) \longrightarrow T_2(s) \longrightarrow G_{p1}(s) \longrightarrow Y(s)$$
$$\hspace{3.8cm} \uparrow \hspace{5.5cm} \downarrow$$
$$\hspace{3.8cm} G_{m1}(s) \longleftarrow \hspace{5.5cm} \hspace{2cm}$$

**Step 2.1 — Define the primary error:**
$$E_1(s) = R(s) - G_{m1}(s)\, Y(s)$$

**Step 2.2 — Primary controller output equals the inner-loop setpoint:**
$$R_2(s) = G_{c1}(s)\, E_1(s)$$

**Step 2.3 — Inner-loop output drives the primary process:**
$$Y(s) = G_{p1}(s)\, X(s) + D_1(s) = G_{p1}(s)\, T_2(s)\, R_2(s) + D_1(s)$$

**Step 2.4 — Substitute $R_2(s)$:**
$$Y(s) = G_{p1}\, T_2\, G_{c1}\, E_1 + D_1$$
$$Y(s) = G_{p1}\, T_2\, G_{c1}\bigl[R - G_{m1}\, Y\bigr] + D_1$$

**Step 2.5 — Collect $Y(s)$ terms:**
$$Y + G_{p1}\, T_2\, G_{c1}\, G_{m1}\, Y = G_{p1}\, T_2\, G_{c1}\, R + D_1$$
$$Y\bigl[1 + G_{c1}\, G_{m1}\, T_2\, G_{p1}\bigr] = G_{c1}\, T_2\, G_{p1}\, R + D_1$$

**Step 2.6 — Solve for the closed-loop transfer functions:**

$$\boxed{\,\dfrac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G_{c1}(s)\, T_2(s)\, G_{p1}(s)}{1 + G_{c1}(s)\, G_{m1}(s)\, T_2(s)\, G_{p1}(s)}\,}$$

$$\boxed{\,\dfrac{Y(s)}{D_1(s)} = \dfrac{1}{1 + G_{c1}(s)\, G_{m1}(s)\, T_2(s)\, G_{p1}(s)}\,}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Secondary-Loop Disturbance Rejection $Y(s)/D_2(s)$

This is the **KTU favourite** — a disturbance entering *inside* the inner loop is rejected by **both** loops.

**Step 3.1 — Modify the inner-loop output to include $D_2$:**

From Section 3.1, Step 1.6:
$$X(s) = T_2(s)\, R_2(s) + S_2(s)\, D_2(s)$$

where $S_2(s) = \dfrac{1}{1 + G_{c2}\, G_{m2}\, G_{p2}}$.

**Step 3.2 — Substitute into the primary process equation:**
$$Y(s) = G_{p1}(s)\bigl[T_2(s)\, R_2(s) + S_2(s)\, D_2(s)\bigr] + D_1(s)$$

**Step 3.3 — Substitute $R_2(s) = G_{c1}(s)\bigl[R(s) - G_{m1}(s)\, Y(s)\bigr]$ and re-arrange (assuming $D_1 = 0$ and $R = 0$ for the disturbance analysis):**

$$Y = G_{p1}\, T_2\, G_{c1}\bigl[-G_{m1}\, Y\bigr] + G_{p1}\, S_2\, D_2$$
$$Y\bigl[1 + G_{c1}\, G_{m1}\, T_2\, G_{p1}\bigr] = G_{p1}\, S_2\, D_2$$

**Step 3.4 — Substitute $S_2(s)$:**

$$\boxed{\,\dfrac{Y(s)}{D_2(s)} = \dfrac{G_{p1}(s)}{(1 + G_{c1}\, G_{m1}\, T_2\, G_{p1})\,(1 + G_{c2}\, G_{m2}\, G_{p2})}\,}$$

> **Key Takeaway:** The denominator contains **two** characteristic polynomial factors. The factor $(1 + G_{c2}\, G_{m2}\, G_{p2})$ is the inner-loop characteristic equation and operates at **high frequency**, providing *rapid* disturbance rejection. The factor $(1 + G_{c1}\, G_{m1}\, T_2\, G_{p1})$ is the outer-loop characteristic equation operating at **low frequency** for steady-state accuracy. This *decoupled, two-timescale* structure is the mathematical heart of cascade control.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Numerical PI Tuning of Inner Loop (Lambda Method, FOPDT)

**Given:** $G_{p2}(s) = \dfrac{K_{p2}\, e^{-L_2 s}}{\tau_{p2} s + 1}$, with $K_{p2} = 2.0$, $\tau_{p2} = 0.5$ min, $L_2 = 0.1$ min.

**Step 4.1 — Choose a fast closed-loop time constant for the inner loop:**

For aggressive inner-loop performance, set $\lambda_2 = 0.2\,\tau_{p2} = 0.1$ min.

**Step 4.2 — Apply Lambda tuning formulas (IMC-derived):**

$$K_{c2} = \dfrac{\tau_{p2}}{K_{p2}\,(\lambda_2 + L_2)} = \dfrac{0.5}{2.0 \times (0.1 + 0.1)} = \dfrac{0.5}{0.4} = 1.25$$

$$\tau_{I2} = \tau_{p2} = 0.5 \text{ min}$$

**Step 4.3 — Write the PI controller transfer function:**

$$G_{c2}(s) = 1.25\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{0.5\,s}\right) = 1.25 + \dfrac{2.5}{s}$$

**Step 4.4 — Verify inner-loop stability (Bode/Gain margin check):**

The open-loop inner transfer function is:
$$L_2(s) = G_{c2}(s)\, G_{p2}(s) = 1.25\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{0.5\,s}\right)\!\cdot\!\dfrac{2.0\,e^{-0.1 s}}{0.5\,s + 1}$$

DC gain $= 1.25 \times 1 \times 2.0 = 2.5$ (high; aggressive — suitable for inner loop).

Cross-over frequency (approx., ignoring dead time): $\omega_c \approx \dfrac{1}{\lambda_2} = 10$ rad/min. Phase margin $\approx 70° - \dfrac{180°}{\pi}\times 0.1\times 10 = 70° - 57.3° \approx 13°$ — *marginal*. For safety, increase $\lambda_2$ to $0.3\,\tau_{p2} = 0.15$ min:

$$K_{c2} = \dfrac{0.5}{2.0\,(0.15 + 0.1)} = \dfrac{0.5}{0.5} = 1.0, \quad \tau_{I2} = 0.5 \text{ min}$$

Phase margin improves to $\approx 70° - 86° \times 0.1/0.15 \approx 23°$ — *acceptable for industrial cascade*. **This is the recommended KTU board-style answer.**

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Single-Loop vs. Cascade Control

| Performance Criterion | Single-Loop PID | Cascade Control | Improvement Factor |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Rejection of inner-loop disturbance $D_2$** | $Y/D_2 = G_{p1}\,G_{p2}/(1 + G_c G_m G_{p1} G_{p2})$ | $Y/D_2$ has *double* denominator | **~3–10× lower ISE** |
| **Rejection of $D_1$** | $Y/D_1 = 1/(1 + G_c G_m G_{p1} G_{p2})$ | Identical structure (outer loop dominates) | Marginal |
| **Setpoint tracking (rise time)** | Limited by total $\tau_{p1} + \tau_{p2} + L_1 + L_2$ | Outer loop sees only $\tau_{p,eq} + L_{eq}$, smaller | **~2–4× faster** |
| **Sensitivity to $K_{p2}$ non-linearity** | High (e.g., valve gain) | Linearized by inner loop | **Significantly lower** |
| **Tuning complexity** | 1 controller, 3 parameters | 2 controllers, 6 parameters (tuned sequentially) | More complex commissioning |
| **Hardware cost** | 1 transmitter, 1 controller | 2 transmitters, 2 controllers | Higher upfront cost |
| **Robustness to model error** | Lower | Higher (inner loop corrects) | **Substantially higher** |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete Cascade Control Architecture

The diagram below depicts the **nested, two-loop cascade architecture**, showing all signal paths, the two summing junctions, the two PID controllers, the two process blocks ($G_{p1}$ and $G_{p2}$), the two transmitters ($G_{m1}$ and $G_{m2}$), and both disturbance injection points ($D_1$ and $D_2$).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[/"Setpoint R(s)"/]
    S1(("Sum 1\nPrimary"))
    S2(("Sum 2\nSecondary"))
    Gc1["Gc1 Primary\nController"]
    Gc2["Gc2 Secondary\nController"]
    Gv["Control Valve\nGv approx 1"]
    Gp2["Gp2 Secondary\nProcess"]
    Gp1["Gp1 Primary\nProcess"]
    Gm1["Gm1 Primary\nTransmitter"]
    Gm2["Gm2 Secondary\nTransmitter"]
    Y[/"Primary Output Y(s)"/]
    X[/"Secondary Output X(s)"/]
    D1[/"D1 Outer\nDisturbance"/]
    D2[/"D2 Inner\nDisturbance"/]

    R --> S1
    S1 -- "E1" --> Gc1
    Gc1 -- "R2 Remote SP" --> S2
    S2 -- "E2" --> Gc2
    Gc2 --> Gv
    Gv --> Gp2
    Gp2 -- "X" --> X
    Gp2 -- "X" --> Gp1
    D2 -- "add" --> Gp2
    Gp1 -- "Y" --> Y
    D1 -- "add" --> Gp1
    Gp1 -- "Y feedback" --> Gm1
    Gm1 -- "to Sum 1" --> S1
    Gp2 -- "X feedback" --> Gm2
    Gm2 -- "to Sum 2" --> S2
```

**Reading the diagram (KTU standard signal-flow direction: left → right, top → bottom):**
- The **top path** (left to right) is the forward command path: $R \to G_{c1} \to G_{c2} \to G_v \to G_{p2} \to G_{p1} \to Y$.
- The **bottom path** is the primary feedback: $Y \to G_{m1} \to \Sigma_1$.
- The **middle path** is the secondary feedback: $X \to G_{m2} \to \Sigma_2$.
- $D_2$ is injected **between** $G_{p2}$ and the secondary transmitter, ensuring it must pass through the inner loop.
- $D_1$ is injected **between** $G_{p1}$ and the primary transmitter, downstream of the inner loop.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Inner-Loop Reduction (Engineering Simplification View)

When tuning the outer loop, the **entire inner loop is replaced by its closed-loop equivalent $T_2(s)$**, which is approximately a **first-order lag with small dead time** and unit DC gain. This block diagram is what the primary controller actually "sees."

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[/"Setpoint R(s)"/]
    S1(("Sum 1"))
    Gc1["Gc1 Primary\nPI Controller"]
    T2["T2 Inner Loop\nClosed-Loop TF\nApprox FOPDT"]
    Gp1["Gp1 Primary\nProcess"]
    Gm1["Gm1 Primary\nTransmitter"]
    Y[/"Output Y(s)"/]
    D1[/"D1 Disturbance"/]

    R --> S1
    S1 -- "E1" --> Gc1
    Gc1 -- "U" --> T2
    T2 -- "X" --> Gp1
    Gp1 --> Y
    D1 -- "injection" --> Gp1
    Gp1 -- "Y feedback" --> Gm1
    Gm1 --> S1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The primary loop in this reduced form is **mathematically equivalent to a single-loop feedback system** with the *effective process* being $T_2(s)\, G_{p1}(s)$. This is why cascade tuning can be performed as a *two-step sequential procedure*: first tune the inner loop (with primary in MANUAL), then tune the outer loop (with secondary in AUTO). The inner loop's fast, well-damped behaviour greatly simplifies the outer loop's tuning math.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Comparison: Single-Loop vs. Cascade (Block-Level View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SL["Single-Loop Control"]
        A1[/"R"/] --> SA(("Sum"))
        SA --> GA["Gc"]
        GA --> PA["Gp2 then Gp1\nin Series"]
        PA --> YA[/"Y"/]
        YA --> MA["Gm"]
        MA --> SA
        DD1[/"D enters here\nLate detection"/] -.-> PA
    end
    subgraph CS["Cascade Control"]
        B1[/"R"/] --> SB(("Sum Outer"))
        SB --> GB1["Gc1 Outer"]
        GB1 --> SB2(("Sum Inner"))
        SB2 --> GB2["Gc2 Inner"]
        GB2 --> PB2["Gp2\nFast Loop"]
        PB2 --> XB[/"X"/]
        XB --> PB1["Gp1\nSlow Loop"]
        PB1 --> YB[/"Y"/]
        YB --> MB1["Gm1"]
        MB1 --> SB
        XB --> MB2["Gm2"]
        MB2 --> SB2
        DD2[/"D2 enters here\nEarly detection"/] -.-> PB2
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Takeaway:** In single-loop control, the disturbance $D$ must traverse the *entire* process $G_{p2}G_{p1}$ before being detected. In cascade control, $D_2$ is detected *immediately* after $G_{p2}$ and corrected by $G_{c2}$ before propagating to $G_{p1}$. The **time-to-detect-and-correct** is reduced by a factor roughly equal to the time-constant ratio $\tau_1 / \tau_2$.
