## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define cascade control. State two essential conditions that must be satisfied for a process to be amenable to cascade control.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Cascade control is an advanced multi-loop control strategy in which the output of a *primary (master) controller* serves as the *remote setpoint* of a *secondary (slave) controller*. The two control loops are *nested*, with the secondary loop enclosed within the primary loop, designed to operate at a substantially faster time scale.

**Two Essential Conditions (1 mark):**
1. There must exist a measurable *secondary variable* that is causally linked to both the manipulated variable and the primary (controlled) variable, and which has a **significantly smaller time constant and dead time** than the primary variable.
2. The secondary loop, when closed, must be **stable and fast** enough to track setpoint changes demanded by the primary controller without introducing excessive lag or oscillation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*With a neat block diagram, explain the structure of a cascade control system. What is the role of the secondary controller?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Block Diagram (1.5 marks):** *[Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid block diagram — student must draw: Setpoint → Σ₁ → Gc1 → Σ₂ → Gc2 → Gp2 → (splits to) Gp1 → Y, with feedback from X via Gm2 to Σ₂ and from Y via Gm1 to Σ₁.]*

**Role of Secondary Controller (1.5 marks):**
1. **Local Disturbance Rejection:** It rapidly corrects disturbances entering the secondary process (e.g., steam pressure variations) before they propagate to the primary variable, providing a "first line of defence."
2. **Linearization & Actuation:** It linearizes the behaviour of the control valve and secondary process, presenting a clean, fast, near-first-order equivalent process to the primary controller.
3. **Setpoint Tracking:** It tracks the time-varying setpoint commanded by the primary controller, ensuring the secondary variable faithfully follows the primary controller's output.

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern Reminder:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. The student attempts *one* of the two alternatives provided. Detailed model solutions are given below.

---

## Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
*(a)* Derive the closed-loop transfer function of a cascade control system relating the primary output $Y(s)$ to (i) the setpoint $R(s)$ and (ii) a disturbance $D_1(s)$ entering the primary process. Clearly state all assumptions. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A jacketed CSTR has the following transfer functions:
$$G_{p1}(s) = \dfrac{2.0}{5s + 1} \quad \text{(reactor temperature dynamics)}$$
$$G_{p2}(s) = \dfrac{0.8}{0.4s + 1} \quad \text{(jacket temperature dynamics)}$$
$G_{m1} = G_{m2} = 1$. Design PI controllers $G_{c1}(s)$ and $G_{c2}(s)$ using the Lambda tuning method with $\lambda_2 = 0.2\,\tau_{p2}$ and $\lambda_1 = \tau_{p1}$. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Closed-Loop Transfer Functions [7 Marks]

**Assumptions stated (1 mark):**
- Unity-gain transmitters: $G_{m1} = G_{m2} = 1$.
- Ideal control valve: $G_v = 1$ (absorbed into $G_{c2}$).
- Linear, time-invariant, single-input single-output (SISO) processes.
- Both controllers are linear (PI or PID).
- Disturbance $D_1$ enters *additively* at the input of $G_{p1}$ (after the inner loop).

**Derivation of $Y(s)/R(s)$ (3 marks):**

From Section 3.2, the inner closed-loop transfer function is:
$$T_2(s) = \dfrac{G_{c2}\, G_{p2}}{1 + G_{c2}\, G_{m2}\, G_{p2}} = \dfrac{G_{c2}\, G_{p2}}{1 + G_{c2}\, G_{p2}}$$

The primary loop equation:
$$E_1 = R - G_{m1}\, Y = R - Y$$
$$R_2 = G_{c1}\, E_1$$
$$Y = G_{p1}\, T_2\, R_2 + D_1 = G_{p1}\, T_2\, G_{c1}\,(R - Y) + D_1$$

Rearranging:
$$Y\bigl[1 + G_{c1}\, T_2\, G_{p1}\bigr] = G_{c1}\, T_2\, G_{p1}\, R + D_1$$

$$\therefore \dfrac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G_{c1}(s)\, T_2(s)\, G_{p1}(s)}{1 + G_{c1}(s)\, T_2(s)\, G_{p1}(s)}$$

**Derivation of $Y(s)/D_1(s)$ (3 marks):**

From the same equation, isolating the $D_1$ term:

$$\therefore \dfrac{Y(s)}{D_1(s)} = \dfrac{1}{1 + G_{c1}(s)\, T_2(s)\, G_{p1}(s)}$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** The denominator $(1 + G_{c1} T_2 G_{p1})$ is the *outer-loop characteristic equation*. Setpoint tracking and $D_1$ rejection share the same closed-loop poles, but the numerators differ — $G_{c1} T_2 G_{p1}$ versus $1$. With integral action in $G_{c1}$, both numerators are designed to drive steady-state errors to zero.

---

#### Part (b) — PI Controller Design for the Jacketed CSTR [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Tune the inner loop (3 marks).**

Process: $G_{p2}(s) = \dfrac{0.8}{0.4s + 1}$ → $K_{p2} = 0.8$, $\tau_{p2} = 0.4$ min, $L_2 \approx 0$ (assumed negligible for first-order model).

Lambda value: $\lambda_2 = 0.2 \times 0.4 = 0.08$ min.

Lambda tuning formulas for FOPDT with $L_2 \to 0$:
$$K_{c2} = \dfrac{\tau_{p2}}{K_{p2}\,\lambda_2} = \dfrac{0.4}{0.8 \times 0.08} = \dfrac{0.4}{0.064} = 6.25$$
$$\tau_{I2} = \tau_{p2} = 0.4 \text{ min}$$

$$\therefore G_{c2}(s) = 6.25\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{0.4\,s}\right) = 6.25 + \dfrac{15.625}{s}$$

**Step 2: Compute the inner closed-loop transfer $T_2(s)$ (1 mark).**

$$T_2(s) = \dfrac{G_{c2}\, G_{p2}}{1 + G_{c2}\, G_{p2}} = \dfrac{6.25(1 + 1/0.4s) \times 0.8/(0.4s+1)}{1 + 6.25(1 + 1/0.4s) \times 0.8/(0.4s+1)}$$

After algebraic reduction (cancelling the pole-zero pair between $G_{c2}$ and $G_{p2}$):
$$T_2(s) \approx \dfrac{1}{\lambda_2\,s + 1} = \dfrac{1}{0.08\,s + 1}$$

*Engineering check:* DC gain = 1 ✓. The inner loop behaves as a **fast first-order lag with time constant 0.08 min** — 12.5× faster than $G_{p1}$. Excellent cascade ratio.

**Step 3: Tune the outer loop (3 marks).**

Effective process for outer loop: $T_2(s) \cdot G_{p1}(s) = \dfrac{1}{0.08s+1} \times \dfrac{2.0}{5s+1} = \dfrac{2.0}{(0.08s+1)(5s+1)}$

For tuning, approximate as FOPDT. The dominant pole is at $\tau_{p1} = 5$ min, and the inner loop is *fast enough* to be treated as a pure gain $\approx 1$. Effective FOPDT:
$$K_{p}^{eq} \approx 2.0, \quad \tau_{p}^{eq} \approx 5.0, \quad L^{eq} \approx 0$$

Lambda value: $\lambda_1 = \tau_{p1} = 5.0$ min (Lambda method, conservative for outer loop).

$$K_{c1} = \dfrac{\tau_{p}^{eq}}{K_{p}^{eq}\,\lambda_1} = \dfrac{5.0}{2.0 \times 5.0} = 0.5$$
$$\tau_{I1} = \tau_{p}^{eq} = 5.0 \text{ min}$$

$$\therefore G_{c1}(s) = 0.5\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{5.0\,s}\right) = 0.5 + \dfrac{0.1}{s}$$

**Final Design Summary:**

| Controller | $K_c$ | $\tau_I$ (min) | Closed-Loop Behaviour |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $G_{c1}$ (Primary, temperature) | **0.5** | **5.0** | Slow outer loop, $\tau_{cl,1} \approx 5$ min |
| $G_{c2}$ (Secondary, jacket) | **6.25** | **0.4** | Fast inner loop, $\tau_{cl,2} \approx 0.08$ min |

---

## Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
*(a)* Explain with justification **three industrial situations** in which cascade control is preferred over single-loop PID control. For each situation, identify the primary variable, the secondary variable, and the disturbance being rejected. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* For the cascade structure with $G_{p1}(s) = \dfrac{K_{p1}}{\tau_{p1} s + 1}$ and $G_{p2}(s) = \dfrac{K_{p2}}{\tau_{p2} s + 1}$ (no dead time), with PI controllers $G_{c1}(s) = K_{c1}(1 + 1/(\tau_{I1}s))$ and $G_{c2}(s) = K_{c2}(1 + 1/(\tau_{I2}s))$, derive the **overall characteristic equation** and the **disturbance rejection transfer function** $Y(s)/D_2(s)$ in the disturbance $D_2$ entering the inner loop. Comment on the *physical significance* of the double-factor denominator. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Three Industrial Cascade Control Applications [7 Marks]

**Application 1: Jacketed CSTR Reactor Temperature Control (2.5 marks)**
- **Primary Variable:** Reactor bulk temperature (controlled variable; e.g., 80 °C, exothermic reaction).
- **Secondary Variable:** Jacket (coolant) outlet temperature OR coolant flow rate.
- **Disturbance Rejected:** Sudden fluctuations in coolant supply pressure/temperature, or variations in cooling water header pressure. *Justification:* These disturbances corrupt the jacket temperature long before they affect the reactor temperature; the inner loop absorbs them.

**Application 2: Distillation Column Overhead Temperature / Composition (2.5 marks)**
- **Primary Variable:** Tray temperature near the top of the column (proxy for overhead composition) or online analyzer signal.
- **Secondary Variable:** Reflux flow rate (measured by orifice + flow transmitter).
- **Disturbance Rejected:** Reflux pump discharge pressure fluctuations, reflux composition disturbances, or upstream feed preheater upsets. *Justification:* Reflux flow has a direct, fast effect on top tray temperature; controlling it locally ensures smooth composition control.

**Application 3: Steam-Heated Heat Exchanger Product Outlet Temperature (2 marks)**
- **Primary Variable:** Process fluid outlet temperature from the heat exchanger.
- **Secondary Variable:** Steam flow rate to the heater.
- **Disturbance Rejected:** Steam supply pressure variations (very common in plant header systems). *Justification:* Steam pressure changes the steam flow through the control valve non-linearly; the inner flow loop linearizes the steam flow versus valve opening, and the outer temperature loop sees a clean, predictable process.

*(Equivalent alternative: furnace outlet temperature cascade with fuel flow as secondary variable; boiler drum level cascade with feedwater flow as secondary variable.)*

---

#### Part (b) — Derivation of Disturbance Rejection $Y(s)/D_2(s)$ [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Inner-loop transfer function (1 mark).**

With $G_{m2} = 1$, the inner closed-loop transfer function is:
$$T_2(s) = \dfrac{G_{c2}\, G_{p2}}{1 + G_{c2}\, G_{p2}}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the given forms (1 mark).**

$$G_{c2}\, G_{p2} = K_{c2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_{I2} s}\right)\!\cdot\!\dfrac{K_{p2}}{\tau_{p2} s + 1} = \dfrac{K_{c2} K_{p2} (\tau_{I2} s + 1)}{\tau_{I2} s\,(\tau_{p2} s + 1)}$$

Let $L_2(s) = G_{c2}\, G_{p2}$ for brevity. Then:
$$T_2(s) = \dfrac{L_2(s)}{1 + L_2(s)}$$

**Step 3: The overall loop gain for the outer loop (1 mark).**

The outer-loop "open-loop" transfer function (from the perspective of the primary summing junction) is:
$$L_1^{cascade}(s) = G_{c1}(s)\, T_2(s)\, G_{p1}(s) = G_{c1}(s)\, \dfrac{L_2(s)}{1 + L_2(s)}\, G_{p1}(s)$$

**Step 4: The overall characteristic equation (1 mark).**

$$\boxed{\,1 + G_{c1}(s)\, T_2(s)\, G_{p1}(s) = 0\,}$$

Or, equivalently, multiplying through by $(1 + L_2)$:
$$(1 + L_2) + G_{c1}\, L_2\, G_{p1} = 0$$
$$1 + L_2 + G_{c1}\, L_2\, G_{p1} = 0$$
$$1 + G_{c2}\, G_{p2} + G_{c1}\, G_{c2}\, G_{p2}\, G_{p1} = 0$$

**Step 5: Derive $Y(s)/D_2(s)$ (1.5 marks).**

From Section 3.3:
$$\dfrac{Y(s)}{D_2(s)} = \dfrac{G_{p1}(s)}{(1 + G_{c1}\, T_2\, G_{p1})\,(1 + G_{c2}\, G_{p2})}$$

Substituting the explicit forms:
$$\dfrac{Y(s)}{D_2(s)} = \dfrac{\dfrac{K_{p1}}{\tau_{p1} s + 1}}{\bigl[1 + G_{c1}\, T_2\, G_{p1}\bigr]\bigl[1 + G_{c2}\, G_{p2}\bigr]}$$

**Step 6: Physical significance of the double-factor denominator (1.5 marks).**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The denominator is a product of **two characteristic polynomials**, one for each loop. The factor $(1 + G_{c2}\, G_{p2})$ has roots that are the **inner-loop closed-loop poles**, located at **high frequency** (fast transients). The factor $(1 + G_{c1}\, T_2 G_{p1})$ has roots that are the **outer-loop closed-loop poles**, located at **lower frequency** (slow transients). The overall system thus exhibits a **two-timescale (singular-perturbation) response**:
> - The *first few seconds* of the disturbance response are dominated by the *inner loop* — the secondary controller acts almost instantaneously, attenuating $D_2$ at its source.
> - The *long-term* (steady-state) behaviour is determined by the *outer loop* — the primary controller drives the residual error to zero via integral action.
>
> This separation of timescales is the **fundamental reason cascade control is so effective**: a disturbance entering $D_2$ is suppressed at the *high-frequency* layer *before* it can corrupt the *low-frequency* primary variable. In a single-loop system, only one factor exists in the denominator, and the disturbance must propagate through the *entire* process before correction begins.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cause Mark Deductions in Cascade Control Questions (KTU Board Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Confusing the remote setpoint:** Students frequently write that the primary controller *directly* drives the valve. The primary controller's output is **not** sent to the valve — it is sent as the **setpoint input to the secondary controller**. The valve is driven by the *secondary* controller. *Penalty: 1–2 marks.*
>
> 2. **Drawing the block diagram with a single summing junction:** Cascade control has *two* summing junctions (one for each loop). Drawing only one is a fundamental structural error. *Penalty: 1–2 marks.*
>
> 3. **Omitting the assumption $G_{m1} = G_{m2} = 1$ in derivations:** Board examiners expect explicit assumption statements. Skipping them loses a "presentation" mark. *Penalty: 0.5–1 mark.*
>
> 4. **Writing the disturbance rejection $Y(s)/D_2(s)$ with only *one* $(1 + \text{loop-gain})$ factor in the denominator:** This is the most common algebraic error. The denominator **must** contain both the inner-loop and outer-loop characteristic factors. *Penalty: 1.5–2 marks.*
>
> 5. **Tuning the outer loop *first*:** The correct procedure is to tune the **inner loop first** (with the primary controller in MANUAL), and *only then* tune the outer loop (with the inner loop in AUTO). Reversing this order is a logical error. *Penalty: 1 mark.*
>
> 6. **Forgetting to specify the *choice* of secondary variable with justification:** A cascade design question that lists the primary variable but fails to justify *why* the chosen secondary variable is faster (smaller $\tau$ and $L$) loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 7. **Skipping the FOPDT approximation step:** When tuning with Lambda method, students must explicitly extract $K_p$, $\tau_p$, and $L$ from the given transfer function. Skipping this is considered "jumping to the answer." *Penalty: 1 mark.*

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Cascade Control — One-Page Summary for KTU Board Exam:**
>
> - [ ] **Definition:** Two nested loops; primary controller output = secondary controller's *remote setpoint*.
> - [ ] **Block Diagram:** Two summing junctions, two PID controllers, two transmitters, two process blocks, two disturbance points ($D_1$ outer, $D_2$ inner).
> - [ ] **Inner-loop TF:** $T_2(s) = G_{c2} G_{p2} / (1 + G_{c2} G_{m2} G_{p2})$; designed for **unit DC gain** and **fast response**.
> - [ ] **Overall Closed-Loop TF (Y/R):** $G_{c1} T_2 G_{p1} / (1 + G_{c1} G_{m1} T_2 G_{p1})$.
> - [ ] **Outer Disturbance Rejection (Y/D₁):** $1 / (1 + G_{c1} G_{m1} T_2 G_{p1})$ — single factor, controlled by outer loop only.
> - [ ] **Inner Disturbance Rejection (Y/D₂):** Has **TWO factors** in the denominator — one for each loop. The double-factor structure is the **mathematical signature** of cascade control.
> - [ ] **Tuning Order:** **Inner first** (primary in MANUAL), then **outer** (secondary in AUTO). This is non-negotiable.
> - [ ] **Lambda Tuning (PI):** $K_c = \tau_p / [K_p (\lambda + L)]$, $\tau_I = \tau_p$. For inner loop, use **aggressive $\lambda$** ($\lambda_2 \approx 0.2\tau_{p2}$). For outer loop, use **conservative $\lambda$** ($\lambda_1 \approx \tau_{p1}$).
> - [ ] **Selection Criteria:** Measurable, faster secondary variable exists; primary variable responds slowly to manipulated variable; major disturbances enter the secondary process.
> - [ ] **Time-Scale Separation:** $\tau_1 / \tau_2 \geq 3$ to $5$; $L_1 / L_2 \geq 3$. Ensures loop decoupling and predictable tuning.
> - [ ] **Industrial Examples:** CSTR jacket temperature, distillation reflux, steam-heated exchanger, boiler drum level, furnace outlet.
> - [ ] **Advantages:** Faster disturbance rejection (esp. for $D_2$); linearization of non-linear valves/processes; improved robustness; better setpoint tracking.
> - [ ] **Disadvantages:** Higher hardware cost (extra transmitter + controller); more complex commissioning; not effective for $D_1$-type disturbances.
> - [ ] **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Do *not* assume the inner-loop disturbance rejection is "perfect." It is improved, not eliminated; the outer loop still must correct the residual.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feedforward Control: Process Dynamics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT864 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN LARGE SCALE INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Feedforward Control: Process Dynamics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for process control, **Feedforward Control (FFC)** is a sophisticated *anticipatory* control strategy that measures one or more **measurable load disturbances** entering the process and, *before* they can perturb the **controlled variable (CV)**, computes and applies a corrective action via the **manipulated variable (MV)**.

Mathematically, for a process where the controlled variable $Y(s)$ is influenced by the manipulated variable $M(s)$ and the disturbance $D(s)$, the process is described by:

$$Y(s) = G_p(s)\, M(s) + G_d(s)\, D(s)$$

where $G_p(s)$ is the **process transfer function** and $G_d(s)$ is the **disturbance transfer function**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Feedforward control belongs to the family of **non-feedback** control. It is *complementary* to feedback — not a replacement. In industry (distillation columns, boiler drum level, heat exchangers), feedback + feedforward (combined as **Feedback-Feedforward control**) is the gold standard.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Weather-Ready Commuter"

Imagine you are driving to work. A **feedback** controller is like a driver who, *after* noticing he is late, presses the accelerator harder. He corrects **after** the error occurs.

A **feedforward controller**, in contrast, is like a commuter who *checks the weather forecast in the morning*. Seeing heavy rain predicted, he leaves home **30 minutes early** to compensate for the expected delay — *before* the error (lateness) ever occurs.

| Aspect | Feedback Control | Feedforward Control |
|---|---|---|
| Action timing | **Reactive** (after error) | **Anticipatory** (before error) |
| Needs CV measurement? | Yes | **No** |
| Needs disturbance measurement? | No | **Yes** |
| Stability issues? | Possible (loop tuning) | Generally stable (open-loop) |
| Perfect cancellation? | Asymptotic | Theoretical, requires perfect models |

### 1.3 Why "Process Dynamics" Matters in Feedforward

The phrase **"Process Dynamics"** in the topic title points to the central engineering challenge: the *process response* between the disturbance entering and the disturbance reaching the controlled variable has its **own dynamics** — characterized by **dead time ($\theta$)**, **time constants ($\tau$)**, and **process gain ($K_p$)**.

> [!NOTE]
> If we ignore process dynamics and design only a **static** feedforward controller (based purely on steady-state gains), the controller will be sluggish and produce transient errors. **Dynamic compensation** (Lead-Lag blocks) is therefore a critical KTU-exam-relevant concept.

### 1.4 Key Physical Quantities and Notation

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|
| $Y$ | Controlled Variable (CV) | °C, bar, ppm, m³/h |
| $M$ | Manipulated Variable (MV) | %, kg/h, A |
| $D$ | Load Disturbance | varies |
| $K_p$ | Process Steady-State Gain | dimensionless or unit ratio |
| $K_d$ | Disturbance Steady-State Gain | dimensionless or unit ratio |
| $\tau_p, \tau_d$ | Time constants | seconds / minutes |
| $\theta_p, \theta_d$ | Dead times / Transport delays | seconds / minutes |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The General Process Structure for FFC Design

For KTU examination purposes, every feedforward control problem is framed around a **two-input, one-output** process:

$$Y(s) = G_p(s)\, M(s) + G_d(s)\, D(s)$$

**Operational Decomposition (Step-by-Step Logic):**

1. **Step 1 — Identify the disturbance variable $D(s)$** that is *measurable, significant*, and *frequently varying*.
2. **Step 2 — Identify the manipulated variable $M(s)$** available to reject this disturbance (typically the same MV used by the feedback loop).
3. **Step 3 — Derive or obtain** the two transfer functions $G_p(s)$ and $G_d(s)$ from step tests, plant data, or first-principles modeling.
4. **Step 4 — Compute the ideal feedforward controller transfer function:**

$$G_{ff}(s) = -\frac{G_d(s)}{G_p(s)}$$

The **negative sign** ensures the FF controller applies *opposing* corrective action — when a positive disturbance occurs, the controller *reduces* $M$ (or vice versa) to cancel its effect.

5. **Step 5 — Implement static and dynamic compensation** based on the *form* of $G_{ff}(s)$.

### 2.2 Static Feedforward Control

The simplest implementation. We set $s = 0$ (steady-state condition) in $G_{ff}(s)$:

$$K_{ff} = -\frac{K_d}{K_p}$$

This produces an immediate, proportional correction with **no dynamic compensation**. Sufficient when:
- Process dynamics for $D$ and $M$ paths are **similar** ($\tau_p \approx \tau_d$).
- Dead times are negligible.
- The process is **non-integrating**.

### 2.3 Dynamic Feedforward Control — The Lead-Lag Compensator

In real plants, $G_p(s)$ and $G_d(s)$ differ in dynamics. After algebraic simplification, $G_{ff}(s)$ typically takes the form:

$$G_{ff}(s) = K_{ff}\,\frac{\tau_1 s + 1}{\tau_2 s + 1}\,\frac{1}{\tau_3 s + 1}\,e^{-\theta s}$$

A practical, physically-realizable form (called the **Lead-Lag plus Dead Time compensator**) used in DCS implementations is:

$$G_{ff}(s) = K_{ff}\,\frac{\tau_{lead}\, s + 1}{\tau_{lag}\, s + 1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" behind Lead-Lag:**
> - **Lead term** ($\tau_{lead} s + 1$) makes the controller *react faster* than the disturbance path — needed when the disturbance response is **slower** than the manipulated-variable response.
> - **Lag term** ($\tau_{lag} s + 1$) makes the controller *react slower* — needed when the disturbance path is **faster**.
> - Lead-Lag is **bounded** and physically realizable; ideal derivatives (poles at origin) are avoided.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application | Disturbance Measured |
|---|---|---|
| Petroleum Refining | Distillation column reflux | Feed composition, feed flow |
| Power Plants | Boiler drum level | Steam flow, feedwater flow |
| HVAC | Zone temperature control | Outside air temperature, occupancy |
| Chemical Reactors | Exothermic reactor temperature | Inlet coolant temperature |

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description / Boundary Condition | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Y(s) = G_p(s)\,M(s) + G_d(s)\,D(s)$ | Two-input single-output process model | varies |
| 2 | $G_{ff}(s) = -G_d(s) / G_p(s)$ | Ideal feedforward compensator | unitless ratio |
| 3 | $K_{ff} = -K_d / K_p$ | Static feedforward gain (steady state) | unitless |
| 4 | $G_{ff}(s) = K_{ff}\,\dfrac{\tau_1 s + 1}{\tau_2 s + 1}$ | Lead-Lag compensator (dynamic FF) | unitless |
| 5 | $G_{ff}(s) = K_{ff}\,\dfrac{\tau_{lead} s + 1}{\tau_{lag} s + 1}\,e^{-\theta s}$ | Lead-Lag with dead-time compensator | unitless |
| 6 | $\tau_{lead} = \tau_d$, $\tau_{lag} = \tau_p$ | Tuning rule: lead matches disturbance dynamics, lag matches process dynamics | seconds |
| 7 | $\theta_{ff} = \theta_p - \theta_d$ | Dead-time compensator argument | seconds |
| 8 | $E(s) = [G_p(s)\,G_{ff}(s) + G_d(s)]\,D(s)$ | Tracking error with ideal FF | varies |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip (High-Yield):** When the question says "design the static FF controller," you need only Formula (3). When it says "design with dynamic compensation," you must apply Formula (4) or (5) with the appropriate lead-lag tuning rule.

### 2.6 Mathematical Reality Check — Why Perfect Cancellation is Impossible

Even with a perfect $G_{ff}(s)$, model mismatch, unmeasured disturbances, and non-linearity guarantee that:

$$Y(s) = G_d(s)\,D(s) - G_p(s)\,\frac{G_d(s)}{G_p(s)}\,D(s) + \text{modeling error} \neq 0$$

This is why **feedforward is always paired with feedback** in industrial practice.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Ideal Feedforward Controller

We begin with the standard two-input process model. Our **design objective** is to find $G_{ff}(s)$ such that $Y(s) = 0$ for all $D(s)$.

**Step 1 — Write the augmented process equation:**

The manipulated variable in feedforward control is itself driven by the disturbance:

$$M(s) = G_{ff}(s)\, D(s)$$

Substituting this into the process equation:

$$Y(s) = G_p(s)\,[G_{ff}(s)\,D(s)] + G_d(s)\,D(s)$$

Factor out $D(s)$:

$$Y(s) = [G_p(s)\,G_{ff}(s) + G_d(s)]\, D(s)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the perfect-cancellation objective** $Y(s) = 0$ for all $D(s) \neq 0$:

$$G_p(s)\,G_{ff}(s) + G_d(s) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve algebraically for $G_{ff}(s)$:**

$$G_p(s)\,G_{ff}(s) = -G_d(s)$$

$$\boxed{G_{ff}(s) = -\frac{G_d(s)}{G_p(s)}}$$

This is the **fundamental KTU derivation**. Every static and dynamic FF design starts here.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Static Feedforward Gain

Assume both $G_p(s)$ and $G_d(s)$ are first-order-plus-dead-time (FOPDT):

$$G_p(s) = \frac{K_p\, e^{-\theta_p s}}{\tau_p s + 1}$$

$$G_d(s) = \frac{K_d\, e^{-\theta_d s}}{\tau_d s + 1}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute into the ideal FF formula:**

$$G_{ff}(s) = -\frac{K_d\, e^{-\theta_d s}}{K_p\, e^{-\theta_p s}} \cdot \frac{\tau_p s + 1}{\tau_d s + 1}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the steady-state assumption** ($s \rightarrow 0$, $e^{-\theta s} \rightarrow 1$, $(\tau s + 1) \rightarrow 1$):

$$\boxed{K_{ff} = -\frac{K_d}{K_p}}$$

The time-constants and dead times are *dropped* under static FF — this is precisely why static FF is **inadequate** for fast processes.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Lead-Lag Dynamic Compensator

For the dynamic FF, we retain the time-constant terms but **neglect dead times** (a common engineering approximation):

$$G_{ff}(s) \approx -\frac{K_d}{K_p}\,\frac{\tau_p s + 1}{\tau_d s + 1}$$

Identifying $\tau_1 = \tau_p$ and $\tau_2 = \tau_d$:

$$G_{ff}(s) = K_{ff}\,\frac{\tau_p s + 1}{\tau_d s + 1}$$

**Step 3a — If $\tau_d > \tau_p$:** The disturbance path is *slower* than the process path. We must **lead** the disturbance response.

$$\tau_{lead} = \tau_d, \qquad \tau_{lag} = \tau_p$$

**Step 3b — If $\tau_d < \tau_p$:** The disturbance path is *faster*. We must **lag** the controller response.

$$\tau_{lead} = \tau_p, \qquad \tau_{lag} = \tau_d$$

The dead-time compensator (when dead times are non-negligible):

$$\boxed{\theta_{ff} = \theta_p - \theta_d}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The dead-time compensator uses **future values of disturbance** via the *Smith Predictor-like* approach only if $\theta_{ff} < 0$ (i.e., $\theta_d > \theta_p$). In practice, dead times are typically handled by **delaying the FF signal** by $\theta_{ff}$ if positive, or by a **lead element** if negative.

---

### 3.4 Worked Analytical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem (from internal assessment pattern):**

A heat exchanger has the following FOPDT models:
- Process: $G_p(s) = \dfrac{0.8\,e^{-2s}}{3s + 1}$
- Disturbance: $G_d(s) = \dfrac{0.4\,e^{-1s}}{5s + 1}$

Design (a) the static feedforward controller, and (b) the dynamic Lead-Lag compensator.

**Solution — Part (a): Static FF**

Apply Formula (3):

$$K_{ff} = -\frac{K_d}{K_p} = -\frac{0.4}{0.8} = -0.5$$

So, $M_{ss}(s) = -0.5\,D(s)$.

**Solution — Part (b): Dynamic Lead-Lag FF**

Apply Formula (4) with dead times neglected:

$$G_{ff}(s) = -\frac{0.4}{0.8}\,\frac{3s + 1}{5s + 1} = -0.5\,\frac{3s + 1}{5s + 1}$$

Since $\tau_d = 5 > \tau_p = 3$, we choose:

$$\tau_{lead} = \tau_d = 5\,\text{min}, \quad \tau_{lag} = \tau_p = 3\,\text{min}$$

The dead-time compensator argument:

$$\theta_{ff} = \theta_p - \theta_d = 2 - 1 = +1\,\text{min}$$

A positive $\theta_{ff}$ means we **delay the FF signal by 1 min** to align with the process dynamics.

**Final Dynamic FF Controller:**

$$G_{ff}(s) = -0.5\,\frac{5s + 1}{3s + 1}\,e^{-s}$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Static vs Dynamic FF

| Property | Static FF | Dynamic FF (Lead-Lag) |
|---|---|---|
| Uses steady-state gains only | Yes | Yes |
| Compensates for $\tau$ mismatch | **No** | Yes |
| Compensates for $\theta$ mismatch | **No** | Yes (with $e^{-\theta s}$) |
| Implementation complexity | Trivial | Moderate (3 tuning params) |
| Typical industrial use | Slow processes | **Most industrial applications** |
| Exam weight in KTU | 3–5 marks | **8–14 marks** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Ideal Feedforward Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Dref[Disturbance D] --> SUMnode1((+))
    SUMnode1 --> Gd[Gd s Disturbance TF]
    Gd --> SUMnode2((+))
    SUMnode2 --> Gp[Gp s Process TF]
    Gp --> Yout[Controlled Variable Y]
    Dref --> Gff[Gff s Feedforward Controller]
    Gff --> Mnode[Manipulated Variable M]
    Mnode --> Gp
    classDef tf fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F4E79,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sum fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef io fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class Gd,Gp,Gff tf
    class SUMnode1,SUMnode2 sum
    class Dref,Mnode,Yout io
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Disturbance $D$ enters the process via the disturbance path $G_d(s)$.
- The same $D$ is *measured*, fed to the feedforward controller $G_{ff}(s)$, which computes the corrective $M$.
- $M$ enters the process via the manipulated-variable path $G_p(s)$.
- Both contributions sum to form $Y$.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Combined Feedback + Feedforward Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Setpoint Ysp] --> SUMfb((SUM))
    Ymeas[Measured Y] --> SUMfb
    SUMfb --> Ec[Error e]
    Ec --> Gc[Gc s Feedback Controller]
    Gc --> SUMff((SUM))
    Dref[Disturbance D] --> Gff2[Gff s Feedforward Controller]
    Gff2 --> SUMff
    SUMff --> Mnode[Manipulated Variable M]
    Mnode --> Gp[Gp s Process TF]
    Gp --> Yout2[Process Output Y]
    Dref --> Gd[Gd s Disturbance TF]
    Gd --> Yout2
    Yout2 --> Ymeas
    classDef fb fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#880E4F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ff fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef io fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class Gc fb
    class Gff2,Gd,ff tf
    class Gp,ff,Gff2 tf
    class SP,Ymeas,Yout2,Mnode,Dref io
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for Students:** The FF controller ($G_{ff}$) handles **measurable, frequent** disturbances. The FB controller ($G_c$) handles **everything else** — model errors, unmeasured disturbances, setpoint changes. The two control actions **add together** at the manipulated variable summing junction.

---

### 4.3 Step Response Visualization — Static vs Dynamic FF

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step Disturbance Applied] --> B{Controller Type}
    B -->|Static FF Only| C[Small initial error, slow settling]
    B -->|Dynamic FF with Lead-Lag| D[Initial overshoot/undershoot, fast settling]
    B -->|Ideal Perfect FF| E[Zero error - theoretical only]
    C --> F[Steady-state offset removed but transient persists]
    D --> G[Transient minimized by lead-lag tuning]
    E --> H[Requires perfect model - impossible in practice]
    classDef staticC fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    classDef dynC fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef idealC fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    class C,F staticC
    class D,G dynC
    class E,H idealC
```

---

### 4.4 Functional Architecture — Feedforward Design Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Identify measurable disturbances] --> S2[Step 2: Obtain Gp s and Gd s from step tests]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Compute ideal Gff s equals minus Gd divided by Gp]
    S3 --> S4{Static or Dynamic FF}
    S4 -->|Static| S5[Compute Kff equals minus Kd divided by Kp]
    S4 -->|Dynamic| S6[Design Lead-Lag: tau_lead and tau_lag from time constants]
    S6 --> S7[Add dead-time compensator: theta_ff equals theta_p minus theta_d]
    S5 --> S8[Step 5: Implement in DCS, tune on-line]
    S7 --> S8
    S8 --> S9[Step 6: Pair with feedback controller for residual error rejection]
    classDef ws fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    classDef ds fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,color:#000
    classDef fs fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000
    class S1,S2,S3,ws,S5 fs
    class S4,S6,S7,S8 ds
    class S9 fs
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A.1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Differentiate between feedback and feedforward control. When is feedforward preferred over feedback?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Basis | Feedback Control | Feedforward Control |
|---|---|---|
| Basis of action | Measures the **error** in the controlled variable | Measures the **disturbance** before it affects CV |
| Time of action | **After** deviation occurs (reactive) | **Before** deviation occurs (anticipatory) |
| Measurement needs | Requires CV measurement | Requires disturbance measurement |
| Stability | Stability analysis required | Open-loop, inherently stable |

Feedforward is preferred when:
1. The **disturbance is measurable** and frequent.
2. The process has **large dead times** or slow dynamics.
3. The **CV measurement is delayed** or expensive.
4. Strict control during **large load transients** is required.

*(1.5 marks for comparison table — minimum 3 rows; 1 mark for any two preference criteria; 0.5 mark for concluding sentence.)*

---

#### **Question A.2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: What is the role of a Lead-Lag compensator in dynamic feedforward control? Why is a pure derivative term avoided?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Lead-Lag compensator** is a physically realizable dynamic element added to the feedforward controller to account for mismatches between the **disturbance path dynamics** ($\tau_d, \theta_d$) and the **manipulated variable path dynamics** ($\tau_p, \theta_p$).

It has the standard form:

$$G_{lead-lag}(s) = \frac{\tau_{lead} s + 1}{\tau_{lag} s + 1}$$

- The **lead** term makes the controller react **faster** than the natural disturbance response — necessary when the disturbance dynamics are slower than the process dynamics.
- The **lag** term makes the controller react **slower** — necessary when the disturbance dynamics are faster than the process dynamics.

A **pure derivative** ($K_d s$) is avoided because:
1. It provides **unbounded gain** at high frequencies → amplifies measurement noise.
2. It is **physically unrealizable** (improper transfer function).
3. Lead-Lag gives a **bounded, realizable** approximation with tuning flexibility.

*(1 mark each for role of lead and lag; 1 mark for explaining avoidance of pure derivative.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question B (Option A) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the principle of **feedforward control** for a process with measurable disturbance. Derive the ideal feedforward controller transfer function. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A chemical reactor has the following transfer functions:
$$G_p(s) = \frac{2.5\,e^{-3s}}{4s + 1}, \qquad G_d(s) = \frac{1.2\,e^{-1s}}{6s + 1}$$
Design (i) a **static** feedforward controller and (ii) a **dynamic Lead-Lag** feedforward controller. Justify your choice of lead and lag time constants. *(7 marks)*

---

##### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**
Refer to Section 4.1. The student must show $D \rightarrow G_d \rightarrow Y$, and $D \rightarrow G_{ff} \rightarrow M \rightarrow G_p \rightarrow Y$, with $M$ and $G_d$ outputs summing at the output.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Step 1 — Process model:

$$Y(s) = G_p(s) M(s) + G_d(s) D(s)$$

Step 2 — Feedforward action:

$$M(s) = G_{ff}(s) D(s)$$

Step 3 — Substitute into process equation:

$$Y(s) = G_p(s) [G_{ff}(s) D(s)] + G_d(s) D(s)$$

$$Y(s) = [G_p(s) G_{ff}(s) + G_d(s)]\, D(s)$$

Step 4 — Apply perfect cancellation objective $Y(s) = 0$:

$$G_p(s) G_{ff}(s) + G_d(s) = 0$$

Step 5 — Solve for $G_{ff}(s)$:

$$\boxed{G_{ff}(s) = -\frac{G_d(s)}{G_p(s)}}$$

The negative sign indicates that the controller applies an *opposing* corrective action.

---

##### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Identify the gains and dynamics:**

From $G_p(s)$: $K_p = 2.5$, $\tau_p = 4$ min, $\theta_p = 3$ min.
From $G_d(s)$: $K_d = 1.2$, $\tau_d = 6$ min, $\theta_d = 1$ min.

**(i) Static Feedforward Controller (3 marks):**

Apply the steady-state formula:

$$K_{ff} = -\frac{K_d}{K_p} = -\frac{1.2}{2.5} = -0.48$$

Thus, $M_{ss}(s) = -0.48\, D(s)$.

**(ii) Dynamic Lead-Lag Feedforward Controller (4 marks):**

Apply the dynamic FF formula neglecting dead times:

$$G_{ff}(s) = -\frac{K_d}{K_p}\,\frac{\tau_p s + 1}{\tau_d s + 1} = -0.48\,\frac{4s + 1}{6s + 1}$$

Since $\tau_d = 6 > \tau_p = 4$, the disturbance path is **slower** than the process path. To compensate, the controller must **lead** the disturbance.

Choose:

$$\tau_{lead} = \tau_d = 6\,\text{min}, \quad \tau_{lag} = \tau_p = 4\,\text{min}$$

The dead-time compensator:

$$\theta_{ff} = \theta_p - \theta_d = 3 - 1 = +2\,\text{min}$$

Since $\theta_{ff} > 0$, we delay the FF signal by 2 min.

**Final Dynamic Feedforward Controller:**

$$\boxed{G_{ff}(s) = -0.48\,\frac{6s + 1}{4s + 1}\,e^{-2s}}$$

---

#### **Question B (Option B) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain with a block diagram the **combined feedback-feedforward control** scheme. Discuss why feedforward control alone is insufficient in practice. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A distillation column has:
$$G_p(s) = \frac{0.9\,e^{-4s}}{8s + 1}, \qquad G_d(s) = \frac{0.6\,e^{-2s}}{5s + 1}$$
Compute (i) the **static feedforward gain**, (ii) the **dynamic Lead-Lag compensator**, and (iii) the **steady-state error** when the disturbance $D$ changes by 5 units, using both static and dynamic FF. *(7 marks)*

---

##### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** Refer to Section 4.2. The student must clearly show:
- Feedback loop: $Y_{sp} \rightarrow \Sigma \rightarrow G_c \rightarrow M \rightarrow G_p \rightarrow Y$
- Feedforward path: $D \rightarrow G_{ff} \rightarrow \Sigma$ (summing with FB output)
- Disturbance path: $D \rightarrow G_d \rightarrow Y$

**Why FF Alone is Insufficient (4 marks):**

1. **Imperfect modeling:** Real plants are non-linear; FOPDT models are approximations.
2. **Unmeasured disturbances:** Only measurable disturbances are compensated; others require feedback.
3. **Model parameter drift:** $K_p, K_d, \tau_p, \tau_d$ change with operating conditions (aging, fouling).
4. **Dead-time and non-linearity:** $G_d/G_p$ is rarely exactly realizable.
5. **Setpoint tracking:** Feedforward alone cannot track setpoint changes — only disturbance rejection.

Therefore, **feedback is mandatory** for residual error correction.

---

##### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $K_p = 0.9$, $\tau_p = 8$, $\theta_p = 4$; $K_d = 0.6$, $\tau_d = 5$, $\theta_d = 2$.

**(i) Static FF Gain (2 marks):**

$$K_{ff} = -\frac{0.6}{0.9} = -0.667$$

**(ii) Dynamic Lead-Lag Compensator (3 marks):**

$$G_{ff}(s) = -0.667\,\frac{8s + 1}{5s + 1}$$

Since $\tau_d = 5 < \tau_p = 8$, the disturbance is **faster**. Hence:

$$\tau_{lead} = \tau_p = 8, \quad \tau_{lag} = \tau_d = 5$$

$$\theta_{ff} = \theta_p - \theta_d = 4 - 2 = +2\,\text{min delay}$$

$$G_{ff}(s) = -0.667\,\frac{8s + 1}{5s + 1}\,e^{-2s}$$

**(iii) Steady-State Error (2 marks):**

In steady state, $s = 0$. The closed-loop output with ideal FF (perfect model) is:

$$Y_{ss} = [G_p(0)\,G_{ff}(0) + G_d(0)]\,D_{ss}$$

$$Y_{ss} = [K_p\,K_{ff} + K_d]\,D_{ss} = [0.9 \times (-0.667) + 0.6]\,D_{ss}$$

$$Y_{ss} = [-0.6 + 0.6]\,D_{ss} = 0\,\text{(ideal)}$$

For $\Delta D = 5$:

$$Y_{ss} = 0 \quad \text{(theoretical steady-state error is zero)}$$

However, in practice, **dynamic mismatch** during the transient leaves a non-zero *Integral of Absolute Error (IAE)*. With static FF only:

$$Y_{ss}^{\text{static}} = 0 \quad \text{(steady state OK)}, \quad Y_{transient}^{\text{static}} \neq 0$$

With dynamic FF:

$$Y_{ss}^{\text{dynamic}} = 0, \quad Y_{transient}^{\text{dynamic}} \approx 0 \quad \text{(significantly reduced)}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign Error Trap (Most Common):** Students frequently *omit* the negative sign in $G_{ff} = -G_d/G_p$. This is a 1–2 mark deduction **every time**.
> 2. **Lead-Lag Inversion:** When $\tau_d > \tau_p$, students often incorrectly set $\tau_{lead} = \tau_p$. Remember: **lead matches the SLOWER path's time constant**.
> 3. **Dead-Time Sign:** If $\theta_{ff} > 0$, the FF signal is **delayed**. If $\theta_{ff} < 0$, a *lead* element is added. Mixing this up costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Static vs Dynamic Confusion:** A "static" answer in a "dynamic" question (or vice versa) loses 50% of marks on the design sub-part.
> 5. **Missing Justification:** Always *justify* your choice of $\tau_{lead}$ and $\tau_{lag}$ with reference to $\tau_d$ vs $\tau_p$. A bare formula without reasoning gets partial credit.

---

### 5.6 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Feedforward Control: Process Dynamics — Last-Minute Revision**

- ✅ **Core Formula:** $G_{ff}(s) = -G_d(s)/G_p(s)$ — *memorize the negative sign*.
- ✅ **Static Gain:** $K_{ff} = -K_d / K_p$ — used when dynamics are similar.
- ✅ **Dynamic Lead-Lag:** $G_{ff}(s) = K_{ff}\,(τ_1 s + 1)/(τ_2 s + 1)$ — used when dynamics differ.
- ✅ **Tuning Rule:** $\tau_{lead}$ matches the **slower** path's time constant; $\tau_{lag}$ matches the **faster**.
- ✅ **Dead Time:** $\theta_{ff} = \theta_p - \theta_d$; positive ⇒ delay, negative ⇒ lead element.
- ✅ **Process Model:** $Y(s) = G_p(s) M(s) + G_d(s) D(s)$ — always start here.
- ✅ **FFC + FBC:** Feedforward alone is **never sufficient**; always paired with feedback for residual error.
- ✅ **Measurable Disturbance:** Feedforward only works when $D$ can be measured — otherwise feedback is the only option.
- ✅ **Block Diagram:** Master the two diagrams — pure FF (Sec 4.1) and combined FF+FB (Sec 4.2).
- ✅ **Stability:** FFC is open-loop → inherently stable (but accuracy depends on model).
- ✅ **Static FF Limitations:** Cannot compensate for $\tau$ or $\theta$ mismatch.
- ✅ **Dynamic FF Advantages:** Compensates transient error, not just steady-state.
- ✅ **Lead-Lag Realizability:** Lead-Lag is a bounded, causal approximation of the ideal $G_{ff}$.
- ✅ **Typical Exam Pattern:** Design problem with given $G_p$ and $G_d$ — extract $K_p, K_d, \tau_p, \tau_d, \theta_p, \theta_d$ and apply formulas.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Dynamics with Feedback Trim
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT864 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN LARGE SCALE INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Process Dynamics with Feedback Trim

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Process Dynamics?

**Process Dynamics** is the study of how an industrial process responds to changes in its inputs (manipulated variables, disturbances) over time. It mathematically describes the *transient* and *steady-state* behavior of physical, chemical, or thermal systems commonly encountered in large-scale industries such as refineries, power plants, petrochemical units, and steel mills.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a process is modeled as a **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI)** system characterized by:
- **Process Gain ($K_p$)** — the ratio of steady-state change in output to the change in input.
- **Time Constant ($\tau$)** — the time required for the output to reach **63.2%** of its final steady-state value after a step input.
- **Dead Time / Transportation Lag ($\theta$)** — the time delay between an input change and the start of the output response.
- **Order of the Process ($n$)** — the number of energy-storage elements in series.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For Module 2, students must master the dynamic behavior of **first-order**, **second-order**, **integrating**, and **dead-time processes**, since these are the building blocks for designing any industrial controller, including a **Feedback Trim** configuration.

## 1.2 What is a Feedback Trim?

A **Feedback Trim** (also called **Feedback Trim Control** or **Split-Range with Secondary Feedback**) is an advanced control architecture in which a *secondary (slave/inner) feedback loop* continuously corrects or *trims* the output signal of a *primary (master/outer) controller* before it reaches the final control element.

The fundamental philosophy is:
> *"If you cannot measure or control the primary disturbance directly, measure its consequence downstream and use that signal to continuously correct the primary actuation."*

### Conceptual Analogy: The Cruise Control with Hill-Watch
Imagine driving a car with **cruise control** on a hilly road.
- The **primary controller** (cruise control) commands the throttle to maintain **set speed** ($100$ km/h).
- It does *not* directly know that the car is climbing a hill.
- A **secondary feedback sensor** measures the **engine load (fuel flow)**. When the engine works harder (hill climb), the trim signal *reduces* the throttle command slightly to prevent over-fueling and maintain efficiency.
- The secondary feedback *trims* the primary control action.

In a **large-scale industrial boiler**, this is exactly how **combustion control with O$_2$ trim** works:
- The primary loop controls **steam pressure (or temperature)** by adjusting the **fuel flow**.
- The secondary loop measures the **flue-gas oxygen (O$_2$) percentage** and *trims* the **air-flow** setpoint to maintain optimal excess air.
- This ensures complete combustion even when fuel composition changes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Board Standard):** *Feedback Trim is a multi-loop control strategy where the output of a primary controller is continuously modified by a secondary feedback signal derived from a downstream process variable, in order to compensate for disturbances not measured by the primary loop.*

## 1.3 Why is Feedback Trim Used in Large Scale Industries?

1. **Disturbance Rejection** — handles unmeasured load disturbances entering between the primary actuator and the controlled variable.
2. **Improved Setpoint Tracking** — corrects for nonlinearities and slow drifts in the primary loop.
3. **Process Safety** — limits excessive corrections that could damage equipment.
4. **Energy Efficiency** — keeps processes operating near their optimal point (e.g., excess air, excess oxygen).
5. **Model Uncertainty Compensation** — tolerates inaccurate process models used in the primary controller tuning.

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (Always Bolded in KTU Answers)

- **Steady-State Error ($e_{ss}$)** — the final offset between setpoint and process variable.
- **Settling Time ($t_s$)** — time to reach and stay within $\pm 5\%$ of the final value.
- **Rise Time ($t_r$)** — time to go from $10\%$ to $90\%$ of the final value.
- **Overshoot ($M_p$)** — peak excursion beyond the final value, expressed as a percentage.
- **Decay Ratio (DR)** — ratio of successive oscillation peaks; ideally $= 0.25$ for quarter-amplitude damping.
- **Integral of Absolute Error (IAE)** $= \int_0^\infty \vert e(t) \vert \, dt$.
- **Integral of Squared Error (ISE)** $= \int_0^\infty e^2(t) \, dt$.
- **Integral of Time-weighted Absolute Error (ITAE)** $= \int_0^\infty t \cdot \vert e(t) \vert \, dt$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Process Dynamics

Large-scale industrial processes are dynamically classified into four canonical types. Every KTU Module-2 problem is built from one (or a combination) of these.

### 2.1.1 First-Order Lag Process (Self-Regulating)
The most common process in industry: a single resistance–capacitance (RC) pair.

**Differential equation form:**
$$\tau \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K_p \cdot u(t)$$

**Transfer function:**
$$G_p(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{U(s)} = \frac{K_p}{\tau s + 1}$$

**Step response equation** (for a step of magnitude $M$ at $t=0$):
$$y(t) = K_p \cdot M \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> At $t = \tau$, $y(\tau) = 0.632 \, K_p M$ (i.e., $63.2\%$ of the final value).
> At $t = 3\tau$, $y \approx 0.95 \, K_p M$ (often called the *practical settling point*).
> At $t = 5\tau$, $y \approx 0.993 \, K_p M$ (the *true* steady state).

**Physical examples:** Thermal heating tank, single mixing tank, RC low-pass filter, level tank with pump.

### 2.1.2 First-Order Plus Dead Time (FOPDT) Process
A first-order process with pure transport delay $\theta$. The **single most important model** in industrial controller tuning.

$$G_p(s) = \frac{K_p \cdot e^{-\theta s}}{\tau s + 1}$$

**Physical examples:** Pipeline flow, conveyor-belt temperature sensor, any sensor placed far from the actuator.

### 2.1.3 Integrating Process (Non-Self-Regulating)
A pure integrator — once input is applied, the output ramps indefinitely.

**Differential equation form:**
$$\frac{dy(t)}{dt} = K_p \cdot u(t)$$

**Transfer function:**
$$G_p(s) = \frac{K_p}{s}$$

**Step response:**
$$y(t) = K_p \cdot M \cdot t$$

**Physical examples:** Liquid level in a tank with constant-outflow pump, pressure in a gas receiver with no relief, batch reactor temperature.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Integrating processes **must not** be controlled with proportional-only controllers unless a *reset* (integral) action or external feedback is added. Otherwise, a sustained offset develops.

### 2.1.4 Second-Order (Underdamped / Overdamped) Process
Two energy-storage elements in series.

$$G_p(s) = \frac{K_p}{\tau^2 s^2 + 2\zeta\tau s + 1}$$

where $\zeta$ is the **damping ratio** and $\omega_n = 1/\tau$ is the natural frequency.

**Step response characteristics:**

| Damping ratio | Response shape |
|:---:|:---|
| $\zeta = 0$ | Undamped pure sine wave |
| $0 < \zeta < 1$ | Underdamped — oscillations |
| $\zeta = 1$ | Critically damped — fastest non-oscillatory |
| $\zeta > 1$ | Overdamped — slow, no overshoot |
| $\zeta \to \infty$ | Approaches two first-order stages |

**Physical examples:** Two-tank series system, motor position control, suspension spring-damper.

## 2.2 The Feedback Trim Architecture (Detailed)

A feedback trim system has **two controllers** in **series**:
- **Master / Primary controller (PI or PID)** — receives the *primary measurement* $y_1$ and compares it to setpoint $r_1$.
- **Slave / Trim controller (usually PI or just P)** — receives the *secondary measurement* $y_2$ and computes a *trim* signal $T(s)$ that *adds to* the primary controller output.

### 2.2.1 Signal Flow (Mathematical)

Let the primary output of the master controller be $C_m(s)$, and the trim signal be $T(s)$. The final signal sent to the actuator is:

$$C(s) = C_m(s) + T(s)$$

The secondary feedback forms a *local* inner loop with its own controller $G_{c2}(s)$ acting on the error of the secondary measurement:

$$T(s) = G_{c2}(s) \cdot \left[ R_2(s) - Y_2(s) \right]$$

The **outer primary loop** is closed on $y_1$ through $G_{c1}(s)$ acting on the primary error $E_1(s) = R_1(s) - Y_1(s)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The trim controller does **not** control the secondary variable directly to a fixed setpoint. Instead, it continuously *corrects* the primary controller's output to keep the secondary variable near an *implicit* optimum. This is the **fundamental difference from cascade control**, where both loops have explicit, independent setpoints.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Process / Concept | Transfer Function | Key Parameters | Time-Domain Step Response |
|:---|:---:|:---|:---|
| First-Order Lag | $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{\tau s + 1}$ | $K_p$, $\tau$ | $y(t) = K_p M \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ |
| FOPDT | $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p e^{-\theta s}}{\tau s + 1}$ | $K_p$, $\tau$, $\theta$ | $y(t) = K_p M \left(1 - e^{-(t-\theta)/\tau}\right) \cdot u(t-\theta)$ |
| Second-Order | $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{\tau^2 s^2 + 2\zeta \tau s + 1}$ | $K_p$, $\tau$, $\zeta$ | Decays with period $T_d = \dfrac{2\pi \tau}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ |
| Integrating | $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{s}$ | $K_p$ | $y(t) = K_p M \cdot t$ |
| P Controller | $G_c(s) = K_c$ | $K_c$ | Output $\propto$ error |
| PI Controller | $G_c(s) = K_c \left( 1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I s} \right)$ | $K_c$, $\tau_I$ | Removes steady-state error |
| PID Controller | $G_c(s) = K_c \left( 1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I s} + \tau_D s \right)$ | $K_c$, $\tau_I$, $\tau_D$ | Best transient + zero offset |
| Ziegler–Nichols (Reaction Curve) | $K_c = \dfrac{1.2 \, \tau}{K_p \, \theta}$ | — | Quarter-amplitude decay |
| Cohen–Coon (Reaction Curve) | $K_c = \dfrac{1}{K_p} \dfrac{\tau}{\theta} \left( \dfrac{0.35 + \theta/\tau}{1 + 0.013 \, \theta/\tau} \right)$ | — | Faster than ZN |
| ITAE Criterion | $J = \int_0^\infty t \cdot \vert e(t) \vert \, dt$ | — | Minimizing $J$ gives robust tuning |
| Time-Constant Relation | $t_{63\%} = \tau$, $\quad t_{95\%} \approx 3\tau$, $\quad t_{99\%} \approx 5\tau$ | — | Empirical thumb rule |

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas above are **board-exam ready**. Memorize the FOPDT step response — it appears in nearly every KTU Module-2 numerical.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Industry | Primary Loop | Trim Variable | Purpose |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Power plant boiler | Steam pressure (fuel valve) | Flue-gas O$_2$ % | Combustion efficiency |
| Petroleum refinery | Distillate temperature | Tray temperature | Reduce energy use |
| Cement kiln | Burning-zone temperature | Kiln torque | Maintain clinker quality |
| HVAC | Zone temperature (air damper) | CO$_2$ in return air | Demand-controlled ventilation |
| Glass furnace | Glass temperature | Crown temperature | Prevent refractory damage |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners love to ask: *"In a boiler combustion control, which loop is primary and which is the trim? Justify."* — Always state that the **fuel/air-flow control is primary (fast inner loop)** and the **steam pressure is outer (slow, demand-driven)**. The O$_2$ trim adjusts the *air setpoint* to maintain stoichiometry.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Step Response of a First-Order Process

**Problem statement:** A first-order process $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{\tau s + 1}$ is subjected to a unit step input $U(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$. Derive the output $y(t)$.

**Step 1 — Write the output in the s-domain.**

The output of an LTI system is:
$$Y(s) = G_p(s) \cdot U(s)$$

Substituting:
$$Y(s) = \frac{K_p}{\tau s + 1} \cdot \frac{1}{s}$$

**Step 2 — Perform partial-fraction expansion.**

We rewrite:
$$Y(s) = \frac{K_p}{s(\tau s + 1)}$$

Decompose into two simple fractions:
$$\frac{K_p}{s(\tau s + 1)} = \frac{A}{s} + \frac{B}{\tau s + 1}$$

**Step 3 — Determine constants A and B.**

Multiply both sides by $s(\tau s + 1)$:
$$K_p = A(\tau s + 1) + B \cdot s$$

Set $s = 0$:
$$K_p = A(1) \implies A = K_p$$

Set $s = -\dfrac{1}{\tau}$:
$$K_p = A \cdot 0 + B \cdot \left( -\frac{1}{\tau} \right) \implies B = -K_p \tau$$

**Step 4 — Substitute A and B back into the expansion.**

$$Y(s) = \frac{K_p}{s} - \frac{K_p \tau}{\tau s + 1}$$

Rewrite the second term to match the standard Laplace pair:
$$\frac{K_p \tau}{\tau s + 1} = \frac{K_p}{s + 1/\tau}$$

Hence:
$$Y(s) = \frac{K_p}{s} - \frac{K_p}{s + 1/\tau}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the inverse Laplace transform.**

Using $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\!\left\{\dfrac{1}{s}\right\} = 1$ and $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\!\left\{\dfrac{1}{s+a}\right\} = e^{-a t}$:

$$y(t) = K_p - K_p \, e^{-t/\tau}$$

Therefore:
$$y(t) = K_p \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right)$$

For a step of magnitude $M$ instead of unity:
$$\boxed{y(t) = K_p M \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right)}$$

**Verification at key instants:**
- At $t = 0$: $y(0) = K_p M (1 - 1) = 0$ ✓
- At $t = \tau$: $y(\tau) = K_p M (1 - e^{-1}) = 0.632 \, K_p M$ ✓
- As $t \to \infty$: $y(\infty) = K_p M$ ✓

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Closed-Loop Transfer Function of a Feedback Trim System

**Setup:** Consider a feedback trim configuration with:
- Primary process $G_{p1}(s)$ controlled by master $G_{c1}(s)$, with measurement $H_1(s) = 1$.
- Trim process $G_{p2}(s)$ in series with $G_{p1}(s)$ from the actuator's standpoint.
- Trim controller $G_{c2}(s)$ measures the output of $G_{p2}$ via $H_2(s) = 1$.

**Block-diagram representation (textual):**
- Reference $R_1 \to (+) \to E_1 \to G_{c1} \to C_m$
- $C_m$ and trim output $T$ are summed $\to C \to G_{p1} \to Y_1$ (primary controlled variable)
- $Y_1$ is also input to $G_{p2} \to Y_2$ (secondary measurement)
- $R_2 \to (+) \to E_2 \to G_{c2} \to T$
- $Y_1$ feeds back to $E_1$, and $Y_2$ feeds back to $E_2$.

**Step 1 — Express the trim signal $T(s)$.**
$$T(s) = G_{c2}(s) \left[ R_2(s) - Y_2(s) \right]$$

**Step 2 — Express the controller output to the actuator.**
$$C(s) = C_m(s) + T(s) = G_{c1}(s) E_1(s) + G_{c2}(s) E_2(s)$$

**Step 3 — Express the primary output $Y_1(s)$.**
$$Y_1(s) = G_{p1}(s) \, C(s) = G_{p1}(s) \left[ G_{c1}(s) E_1(s) + G_{c2}(s) E_2(s) \right]$$

**Step 4 — Express the secondary output $Y_2(s)$.**
$$Y_2(s) = G_{p2}(s) \, G_{p1}(s) \, C(s)$$

**Step 5 — Express the primary error $E_1(s)$.**
$$E_1(s) = R_1(s) - Y_1(s)$$

**Step 6 — Express the secondary error $E_2(s)$.**
$$E_2(s) = R_2(s) - Y_2(s)$$

**Step 7 — Eliminate intermediate variables to get $\dfrac{Y_1}{R_1}$.**

Substituting $E_1$ and $E_2$ into $Y_1$ and $Y_2$, then rearranging:

$$Y_1 = G_{p1} G_{c1} R_1 - G_{p1} G_{c1} Y_1 + G_{p1} G_{c2} R_2 - G_{p1} G_{c2} G_{p2} G_{p1} C$$

Substitute $C$ recursively. For simplicity, assume $R_2 = 0$ (the most common case — secondary setpoint is implicit):

$$T(s) = -G_{c2}(s) \, G_{p2}(s) \, G_{p1}(s) \, C(s)$$

Let $L_1 = G_{c1} G_{p1}$ and $L_2 = G_{c2} G_{p2} G_{p1}$ be the two loop gains. Then:

$$C = G_{c1}(R_1 - Y_1) - L_2 \cdot C$$

Solving for $C$:
$$C (1 + L_2) = G_{c1} (R_1 - Y_1)$$

$$C = \frac{G_{c1}(R_1 - Y_1)}{1 + L_2}$$

But $Y_1 = G_{p1} C$, hence:
$$Y_1 = \frac{G_{c1} G_{p1} (R_1 - Y_1)}{1 + L_2}$$

$$Y_1 (1 + L_2) = L_1 (R_1 - Y_1)$$

$$Y_1 + L_2 Y_1 + L_1 Y_1 = L_1 R_1$$

$$Y_1 (1 + L_1 + L_2) = L_1 R_1$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{\frac{Y_1(s)}{R_1(s)} = \frac{G_{c1}(s) G_{p1}(s)}{1 + G_{c1}(s) G_{p1}(s) + G_{c1}(s) G_{c2}(s) G_{p1}(s) G_{p2}(s)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The denominator has **two loop-gain terms** — $L_1$ (primary loop) and $L_2$ (trim loop). The trim loop effectively *adds* damping/integrity to the closed loop, which is *why* feedback trim improves disturbance rejection.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Tuning a PI Controller for FOPDT using Ziegler–Nichols Reaction-Curve Method

**Given:** FOPDT process $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p \, e^{-\theta s}}{\tau s + 1}$.

**Step 1 — Apply an open-loop step test** of magnitude $M$ to the process. Plot the output $y(t)$ — the famous **S-shaped reaction curve**.

**Step 2 — Draw the tangent line** at the inflection point of the S-curve. The tangent intersects the initial value and the final asymptote. The intersection with the initial value gives the **apparent dead time $\theta$**, and the slope of the tangent, divided by the step size, gives the inverse of the apparent time constant.

**Step 3 — Compute the process slope (reaction rate) $R$.**
$$R = \frac{\Delta y_{\text{ss}}}{\tau} = \frac{K_p M}{\tau}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Ziegler–Nichols (Reaction-Curve) tuning formulas for a PI controller:**
$$K_c = \frac{0.9}{K_p} \cdot \frac{\tau}{\theta}$$
$$\tau_I = 3.0 \, \theta$$

**Step 5 — Sanity check:** The ratio $\tau / \theta$ should lie between **3 and 10** for ZN to give acceptable results. Outside this range, use **Cohen–Coon** or **ITAE-based** tuning.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Cascade vs. Feedback Trim

> [!NOTE]
> This table is **frequently asked** as a 3-mark question in KTU exams.

| Feature | Cascade Control | Feedback Trim |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Number of explicit setpoints** | Two (primary + secondary) | One (primary), secondary is implicit |
| **Secondary variable has a fixed setpoint?** | Yes | No — it is *allowed to float* |
| **Typical secondary sensor** | Flow, pressure, temperature | Quality variable (O$_2$, CO, viscosity) |
| **Trim action direction** | Modifies primary controller output | Modifies the actuator *or* secondary loop's setpoint |
| **Primary purpose** | Speed up inner loop | Compensate for unmeasured disturbances |
| **Used when** | Secondary variable must be held at a target | Secondary variable has an *optimum*, not a target |
| **Example** | Furnace temperature → fuel flow (set) | Steam pressure → fuel (O$_2$ trim) |
| **Industrial analogy** | Cruise control + traction control | Cruise control + hill-climb assist |

## 3.5 Numerical Example: Step-Response Calculation for a First-Order Process

**Problem:** A thermocouple measuring a furnace has dynamics $G(s) = \dfrac{0.8}{5s+1}$. The furnace temperature changes by $50^{\circ}\text{C}$ as a step. Find the temperature reading after 5 s and the time to reach $40^{\circ}\text{C}$ (i.e., $80\%$ of final value).

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Identify parameters: $K_p = 0.8$, $\tau = 5$ s, $M = 50^{\circ}\text{C}$.

Step 2 — Final steady-state value: $y(\infty) = K_p M = 0.8 \times 50 = 40^{\circ}\text{C}$.

Step 3 — Temperature at $t = 5$ s (= $\tau$):
$$y(5) = 40 \left( 1 - e^{-5/5} \right) = 40 \left( 1 - e^{-1} \right) = 40 (1 - 0.3679) = 40 \times 0.6321 = 25.28^{\circ}\text{C}$$

Step 4 — Time to reach $40^{\circ}\text{C}$ (note: $40^{\circ}\text{C}$ here is interpreted as $40^{\circ}\text{C}$ *rise* — i.e., $100\%$ of $40^{\circ}\text{C}$ final value, but the typical question is for $40^{\circ}\text{C}$ rise = $40/40 = 100\%$ which is impossible at finite time; we interpret as $40/40 = 1$ in normalized units — i.e., the full $40^{\circ}\text{C}$ above initial):

$$1 = 1 - e^{-t/5} \implies e^{-t/5} = 0 \implies t = \infty$$

Reinterpreting as *reading 40% of final* (i.e., $16^{\circ}\text{C}$ reading), we solve:
$$0.4 = 1 - e^{-t/5}$$
$$e^{-t/5} = 0.6$$
$$-t/5 = \ln(0.6) = -0.5108$$
$$t = 5 \times 0.5108 = 2.55 \text{ s}$$

**Final Answer:** $y(5) = 25.28^{\circ}\text{C}$, and time to reach $40\%$ of final value $= 2.55$ s.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Generic Feedback Trim Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Primary Setpoint R1] --> SUM1{{Sum Node 1}}
    Y1[Primary Output Y1] --> SUM1
    SUM1 --> E1[Primary Error E1]
    E1 --> GC1[Master Controller Gc1]
    GC1 --> ADD{{Summing Junction}}
    T[Trim Signal T] --> ADD
    ADD --> ACT[Final Actuator Signal C]
    ACT --> GP1[Primary Process Gp1]
    GP1 --> Y1
    GP1 --> GP2[Secondary Process Gp2]
    GP2 --> Y2[Secondary Output Y2]
    R2[Secondary Setpoint R2] --> SUM2{{Sum Node 2}}
    Y2 --> SUM2
    SUM2 --> E2[Secondary Error E2]
    E2 --> GC2[Trim Controller Gc2]
    GC2 --> T
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Industrial Boiler Combustion Control with O$_2$ Trim (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SP1[Steam Pressure Setpoint] --> S1{{Error 1}}
    PT[Pressure Transmitter] --> S1
    S1 --> MIC[Master PI Steam Pressure Controller]
    MIC --> FS[Signal to Fuel Valve Actuator]
    FS --> FV[Fuel Valve]
    FV --> BOIL[Industrial Boiler]
    BOIL --> SP_OUT[Steam Pressure Output]
    SP_OUT --> PT
    O2SP[Implicit O2 Target] --> S2{{Error 2}}
    O2T[Oxygen Analyzer in Flue Gas] --> S2
    S2 --> AIC[Trim PI Oxygen Controller]
    AIC --> TRIM[Trim Signal Adds to Air Flow Setpoint]
    TRIM --> AV[Air Damper Actuator]
    AV --> BLOW[Blower Air Supply]
    BLOW --> BOIL
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture Flow: Signal Path in Feedback Trim

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    DEMAND[Process Demand Signal] --> PC[Primary Controller Stage]
    PC --> SUMJ[Summing Node for Trim Injection]
    DISO[Disturbance Acting on Process] --> PROC[Industrial Process Unit]
    PROC --> SENSE1[Primary Sensor PV1]
    PROC --> SENSE2[Secondary Sensor PV2]
    SENSE1 --> PC
    SENSE2 --> TC[Trim Controller Stage]
    TC --> SUMJ
    SUMJ --> ACT[Final Control Element Valve]
    ACT --> PROC
    OPT[Implicit Optimization Target] -.-> TC
```

## 4.4 Process Dynamics — Response Classification Map (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START[Process Dynamics Family] --> FOP[First Order Plus Dead Time FOPDT]
    START --> SOP[Second Order Process]
    START --> INT[Integrating Process]
    START --> DIST[Distributed Parameter Process]
    FOP --> FOP1[Self Regulating: Step Rises to Steady State]
    SOP --> SOP1[Three Response Types: Underdamped Critically Damped Overdamped]
    INT --> INT1[Ramps Continuously When Input Applied]
    DIST --> DIST1[Exhibits Spatial Variation Along Length]
    FOP1 --> TUNE1[Tune Using ZN Reaction Curve]
    SOP1 --> TUNE2[Tune Using ZN Ultimate Gain Method]
    INT1 --> TUNE3[Must Use PI with External Feedback or PD]
    DIST1 --> TUNE4[Requires PDE Modeling or Lumped Approximation]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The trimming controller's output does **not** directly drive the actuator. It *modifies* the signal *before* the actuator (or in some configurations, *modifies* the secondary setpoint). The student should be able to draw the *signal-flow arrows* correctly in the KTU exam — direction matters!

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between Cascade Control and Feedback Trim Control. Give one industrial example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Cascade Control | Feedback Trim Control |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Setpoints | Two explicit setpoints (primary + secondary) | One explicit setpoint (primary); secondary is *implicit* |
| Secondary variable role | Must be held to a fixed value | Allowed to *float* to compensate for disturbances |
| Industrial example | Furnace temperature control with inner fuel-flow loop | Boiler steam-pressure control with flue-gas O$_2$ trim |

The **key difference** is that in cascade control, the secondary variable is *tightly regulated* to its own setpoint, whereas in feedback trim, the secondary variable is *guided toward an optimum* rather than a target. (3 marks)

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**A first-order process has $K_p = 2.5$ and $\tau = 8$ min. Find the value of the process variable after 4 min, if subjected to a unit-step input.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Using $y(t) = K_p (1 - e^{-t/\tau})$:
$$y(4) = 2.5 \left( 1 - e^{-4/8} \right) = 2.5 (1 - e^{-0.5}) = 2.5 (1 - 0.6065) = 2.5 \times 0.3935 = 0.984$$
Hence $y(4) \approx 0.984$ units. (3 marks)

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question B1 — Option A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **Feedback Trim control system**. Clearly label the primary controller, trim controller, primary process, secondary process, and the trim signal injection point. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A first-order process with dead time has the transfer function $G_p(s) = \dfrac{1.6 \, e^{-2s}}{6s + 1}$. Design a PI controller using the **Ziegler–Nichols reaction-curve method** and calculate the controller settings. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Block Diagram & Explanation (7 marks):**

The Feedback Trim architecture has the following components (see Section 4.1 of these notes for the Mermaid block diagram):

1. **Primary setpoint $R_1$** and **primary measurement $Y_1$** form the primary error $E_1 = R_1 - Y_1$.
2. **Master controller $G_{c1}$** (typically PI or PID) processes $E_1$ and outputs a controller signal $C_m$.
3. The **trim signal $T$** is generated by a **trim (slave) controller $G_{c2}$** that operates on the *secondary error* $E_2 = R_2 - Y_2$, where $Y_2$ is measured downstream of the primary process.
4. The two signals are **summed**: $C = C_m + T$, and this combined signal drives the **final control element** (valve, damper, etc.).
5. The secondary measurement $Y_2$ is typically a *quality variable* (O$_2$, CO, viscosity) and the trim setpoint $R_2$ is often *implicit* — i.e., the controller drives $Y_2$ toward an *optimum* rather than a fixed target.
6. **Key advantage:** the trim loop compensates for disturbances (e.g., changing fuel composition) that are not visible to the primary loop.

**Mark distribution:** (i) Block diagram — 3 marks, (ii) Labelled components — 2 marks, (iii) Operational explanation — 2 marks.

**Part (b) — PI Tuning using ZN Reaction-Curve (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify FOPDT parameters:**
$$K_p = 1.6, \quad \tau = 6 \text{ min}, \quad \theta = 2 \text{ min}$$

**Step 2 — Verify tuning validity:**
$$\frac{\tau}{\theta} = \frac{6}{2} = 3$$
This is at the lower acceptable boundary for ZN. Acceptable.

**Step 3 — Apply ZN reaction-curve PI formulas:**
$$K_c = \frac{0.9 \, \tau}{K_p \, \theta} = \frac{0.9 \times 6}{1.6 \times 2} = \frac{5.4}{3.2} = 1.6875$$
$$\tau_I = 3 \, \theta = 3 \times 2 = 6 \text{ min}$$

**Step 4 — Final PI controller transfer function:**
$$G_c(s) = K_c \left( 1 + \frac{1}{\tau_I s} \right) = 1.6875 \left( 1 + \frac{1}{6 s} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:** The proportional band $P_B = 100 / K_c = 59.3\%$. The integral time $\tau_I = 6$ min removes the steady-state offset in approximately $4 \tau_I = 24$ min. (1 mark)

**Mark distribution:** (i) Parameter identification — 1 mark, (ii) Ratio check — 1 mark, (iii) $K_c$ derivation — 2 marks, (iv) $\tau_I$ derivation — 1 mark, (v) Final expression and interpretation — 2 marks.

---

### Question B1 — Option B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the **closed-loop transfer function** of a feedback trim system. Show clearly how the trim loop modifies the characteristic equation compared to a single-loop system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **furnace temperature** is to be controlled by adjusting fuel flow. The fuel-flow loop has dynamics $G_{p2}(s) = \dfrac{0.8}{3s+1}$. The furnace has dynamics $G_{p1}(s) = \dfrac{2.0}{10s+1}$. Design a **PI controller** for the outer (temperature) loop using the **Direct Synthesis** method for a desired closed-loop time constant $\tau_c = 4$ min. Assume the inner fuel-flow loop is tuned to give a **fast effective gain of 1.0** in the trim signal. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Closed-Loop Transfer Function Derivation (7 marks):**

Refer to the derivation in Section 3.2 of these notes. The final result:

$$\frac{Y_1(s)}{R_1(s)} = \frac{G_{c1}(s) G_{p1}(s)}{1 + G_{c1}(s) G_{p1}(s) + G_{c1}(s) G_{c2}(s) G_{p1}(s) G_{p2}(s)}$$

**Key comparative points (board-mark worthy):**
- A single-loop system has a characteristic equation $1 + G_{c1} G_{p1} = 0$.
- The feedback trim system modifies this to $1 + G_{c1} G_{p1} + G_{c1} G_{c2} G_{p1} G_{p2} = 0$.
- The additional term $G_{c1} G_{c2} G_{p1} G_{p2}$ acts as an *extra damping/integrity term* in the denominator.
- This **increases robustness** to disturbances and reduces the sensitivity of the closed loop to model uncertainty in $G_{p1}$.
- The trim loop effectively *lowers the closed-loop time constant* and *reduces the overshoot*.

**Mark distribution:** (i) Block diagram — 1 mark, (ii) Signal equations — 2 marks, (iii) Final CLTF — 2 marks, (iv) Comparison with single-loop — 2 marks.

**Part (b) — Direct Synthesis Tuning (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify the equivalent process** (after trim loop, $G_{p1,\text{eff}} = 1.0$ as per assumption):
$$G_{p,\text{eq}}(s) = G_{p1} \cdot G_{p2,\text{eff}} = \frac{2.0}{10s + 1}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Direct Synthesis formula for a first-order desired closed loop:**
$$G_c(s) = \frac{1}{G_p(s)} \cdot \frac{1}{\tau_c s}$$
(with unity feedback, no time-delay compensation).

$$G_c(s) = \frac{10s + 1}{2.0} \cdot \frac{1}{4s} = \frac{10s + 1}{8s}$$

**Step 3 — Separate into P and I components:**
$$G_c(s) = \frac{10}{8} + \frac{1}{8s} = 1.25 + \frac{0.125}{s}$$

Comparing with $G_c(s) = K_c + \dfrac{K_c}{\tau_I s} = K_c \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I s}\right)$:

$$K_c = 1.25, \quad \tau_I = \frac{K_c}{0.125} = \frac{1.25}{0.125} = 10 \text{ min}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:** $K_c = 1.25$ is a moderate proportional gain. $\tau_I = 10$ min is the integral reset time. The closed-loop response will have a time constant of $4$ min as desired. (1 mark)

**Mark distribution:** (i) Equivalent process — 1 mark, (ii) Direct synthesis formula — 2 marks, (iii) Separation into P and I — 2 marks, (iv) Final values — 1 mark, (v) Interpretation — 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Process Dynamics with Feedback Trim:**
>
> 1. **Confusing cascade and feedback trim** — If the student writes *"secondary loop is also given a fixed setpoint"*, the examiner will deduct 1 mark immediately. **Correct answer:** the secondary setpoint is *implicit* in feedback trim.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the $e^{-s\theta}$ term in FOPDT** — When a question gives $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p e^{-\theta s}}{\tau s + 1}$, students often write the step response as $K_p M (1 - e^{-t/\tau})$, **missing the $u(t - \theta)$ factor**. Full marks only if the response is written as $K_p M (1 - e^{-(t-\theta)/\tau}) \cdot u(t - \theta)$.
>
> 3. **Sign of the trim signal** — Some students wrongly assume the trim *subtracts* from the master output. In most industrial implementations, the trim **adds** to the master output. Read the problem statement carefully.
>
> 4. **Ziegler–Nichols formula confusion** — KTU expects $K_c = \dfrac{0.9 \, \tau}{K_p \, \theta}$ for PI in reaction-curve method. Students often confuse this with the **ultimate-gain** method formula $K_c = 0.5 K_u$. Read which method is asked.
>
> 5. **Units in FOPDT parameters** — $\tau$ and $\theta$ *must* be in the same time unit (minutes or seconds). Mixing them is a 1-mark deduction.
>
> 6. **Drawing block diagrams without arrows** — Block diagrams in KTU answer scripts must show *clear signal-flow arrows*. A box without an arrow is 0 marks.
>
> 7. **Forgetting to specify the secondary measurement variable** — When asked for an example, students write *"feedback trim is used in industry"* but don't name the *secondary variable* (O$_2$, viscosity, etc.). Examiner will not give full marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Process Dynamics** describes the time-domain behavior of a process when subjected to inputs/disturbances.
- **First-order lag:** $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{\tau s + 1}$, step response $y(t) = K_p M (1 - e^{-t/\tau})$.
- **FOPDT:** $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p e^{-\theta s}}{\tau s + 1}$, step response delayed by $\theta$.
- **Second-order:** $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{\tau^2 s^2 + 2\zeta\tau s + 1}$; underdamped when $\zeta < 1$.
- **Integrating:** $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{s}$, output *ramps* with input.
- **Time-constant thumb rule:** $63\%$ at $t = \tau$, $95\%$ at $t = 3\tau$, $99\%$ at $t = 5\tau$.
- **Feedback Trim** has ONE explicit setpoint (primary); secondary variable is *implicit* / *optimized*.
- **Difference from Cascade:** Cascade = two explicit setpoints; Trim = one explicit + one implicit.
- **Closed-loop TF of feedback trim:**
$$\frac{Y_1}{R_1} = \frac{G_{c1} G_{p1}}{1 + G_{c1} G_{p1} + G_{c1} G_{c2} G_{p1} G_{p2}}$$
- **Characteristic equation:** $1 + G_{c1} G_{p1} + G_{c1} G_{c2} G_{p1} G_{p2} = 0$.
- **Ziegler–Nichols (PI, reaction-curve):** $K_c = \dfrac{0.9 \tau}{K_p \theta}$, $\tau_I = 3\theta$.
- **Cohen–Coon (PI):** faster, better for processes with $\tau/\theta < 3$.
- **Direct Synthesis:** $G_c(s) = \dfrac{1}{G_p(s)} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\tau_c s}$ (for first-order desired response).
- **Industrial applications:** Boiler O$_2$ trim, refinery tray-temperature trim, kiln torque trim, HVAC CO$_2$ trim.
- **Performance metrics to know by heart:** $t_r$, $t_s$, $M_p$, DR, IAE, ISE, ITAE.
- **Trim signal injection point:** summed with master output *before* the final control element.
- **Primary purpose of trim:** compensate for *unmeasured disturbances* and improve *energy efficiency*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** In 14-mark questions, always:
> 1. **Draw the block diagram first** (3 marks reserved).
> 2. **Write the differential equation / transfer function** (1–2 marks).
> 3. **Show all derivations step-by-step** (don't skip algebra).
> 4. **Mention real-world industrial context** (gives a *good impression* and can earn 1 grace mark from the examiner).
> 5. **End with a one-line conclusion** stating the design's *usefulness*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steam Enthalpy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT864 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN LARGE SCALE INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Steam Enthalpy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Enthalpy

In classical process thermodynamics applied to large-scale industrial plants (power, petrochemical, refinery, fertilizer), **Steam Enthalpy** is defined as the **specific total heat content (energy per unit mass)** carried by water vapour (steam) at a defined thermodynamic state, measured relative to a reference datum (conventionally saturated liquid water at **0 °C / 273.15 K** with h = 0 kJ/kg).

Mathematically, for a closed system containing steam:

$$
H \;=\; U \;+\; P\,V
$$

Where:
- **H** = Total Enthalpy (kJ)
- **U** = Internal Energy (kJ)
- **P** = Absolute Pressure (kPa or bar)
- **V** = Volume (m³)

The corresponding **specific enthalpy** (per unit mass) used universally in steam engineering is:

$$
h \;=\; u \;+\; P\,v
$$

Where the lower-case letters denote the property per kilogram of working fluid (kJ/kg, m³/kg).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Note:** For the PEICT864 module, the examiner expects students to state that **h** is a *point function* (state function), dependent ONLY on the instantaneous state (P, T, or P, x or P, v) — *not* on the path of heating.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a kettle boiling on a stove. The water absorbs heat first (sensible heating to 100 °C), then changes phase (latent heating to steam). The steam rushing out has absorbed a *huge* amount of energy — enough to scald you. This stored energy is the **enthalpy**.
