# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following questions are modeled strictly on the KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE) pattern. Marks are split as **Part A (3 marks × 2)** and **Part B (14 marks — internal choice)**. The internal choice in Part B means the student answers *either* Question A *or* Question B in full.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**
**Define a cleanroom as per ISO 14644-1. List any three parameters that are controlled inside a cleanroom.**

**Model Answer:**

A cleanroom is defined by **ISO 14644-1:2015** as:
> *"A room in which the concentration of airborne particles is controlled, and which is constructed and operated in a manner to minimize the introduction, generation and retention of particles inside the room, and in which other relevant parameters such as temperature, humidity, and pressure are controlled as necessary."*

The three main parameters controlled in a cleanroom are:

1. **Particulate count** (number of particles per m³ at reference size, e.g., 0.5 µm)
2. **Temperature** (typically 21 °C ± 2 °C for comfort and process stability)
3. **Relative Humidity (RH)** (30–55% to control static and biological growth)

*(Other acceptable parameters: pressure differential, air velocity, air changes per hour.)*

**Marks Distribution:** Definition – 2 marks; List – 1 mark.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]**
**State the need for cleanrooms in any three major industries.**

**Model Answer:**

A cleanroom is essential wherever the *presence of microscopic particles* can compromise **product quality, safety, or scientific validity**.

1. **Semiconductor Industry (Wafer Fabrication):** Modern chips have features as small as 5–14 nm. A single 0.5 µm particle can short-circuit a transistor, causing yield loss. Cleanrooms of ISO Class 3–5 are mandatory.

2. **Pharmaceutical Industry (Sterile Injectables):** Injectable drugs bypass the body's natural defenses. Any microbial or particulate contamination can cause septic shock or death. USFDA and EU GMP mandate ISO Class 5 environments for aseptic filling.

3. **Aerospace Industry (Satellite Assembly):** Particle contamination on optical sensors or solar cells degrades performance. ISO Class 7–8 cleanrooms are used during satellite integration.

*(Other acceptable answers: HDD manufacturing, biotech tissue culture, hospital OT, optical lens manufacturing.)*

**Marks Distribution:** One industry explained – 1 mark × 3.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 2024 ESE, for each Part B question the student is given an **internal choice**. They answer **either** Question A *or* Question B in full. Each part (a) and (b) carries **7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **need for cleanrooms** in the **semiconductor and pharmaceutical industries**. Discuss the impact of contamination on yield and patient safety. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the **ISO 14644-1 classification system** for cleanrooms. Compare it with the older **FED-STD-209E** standard. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Need in Semiconductor Industry (3.5 marks):**

Semiconductor fabrication involves processes such as **photolithography, etching, deposition (CVD/PVD), and chemical-mechanical polishing (CMP)** at feature sizes of **5 nm to 14 nm**. At this scale, a single particle of size **0.1 µm – 0.5 µm** (which is 7× to 35× the smallest feature) can:

- Block UV light during photolithography → incomplete patterning
- Bridge two metal lines → electrical short
- Create a pinhole in the gate oxide → device failure
- Introduce dopant at wrong location → parametric failure

The **defect density** $D_0$ directly controls yield $Y$ through the Poisson model:

$$Y = e^{-D_0 \times A}$$

where $A$ is the chip area. A fab producing 300 mm wafers with 100 chips/wafer at 5 nm cannot tolerate any class lower than **ISO Class 3**, which restricts particles ≥ 0.5 µm to **35.2 / m³**.

**Need in Pharmaceutical Industry (3.5 marks):**

For **sterile injectable drugs (parenterals)**, the route of administration bypasses the skin — the body's primary defense. Hence:

- USFDA 21 CFR Part 211.113 mandates control of microbiological contamination
- **Grade A** (ISO 5) air supply is required at the point of fill
- Any viable particle (CFU) can cause **septicemia, endotoxin shock, or death**
- **HEPA-filtered laminar airflow** at fill line is a legal requirement
- Risk-based approach per **EU GMP Annex 1 (2022)** mandates continuous particle monitoring

**Conclusion:** A cleanroom is the *single non-negotiable enabler* for both industries to deliver safe, defect-free products.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**ISO 14644-1 Classification (4 marks):**

ISO 14644-1 is the **current global standard** for cleanroom classification, published by the International Organization for Standardization. The classification is based on the **maximum permitted concentration of particles per cubic meter** at the **Most Penetrating Particle Size (MPPS)** and is given by:

$$C_n = 10^N \times \left(\frac{0.1}{D}\right)^{2.08}$$

Nine classes are defined, from **ISO 1 (cleanest)** to **ISO 9 (room air)**. The standard also mandates:
- Monitoring at **three particle sizes** (0.1, 0.5, 5.0 µm)
- Periodic **re-qualification** every 6 or 12 months
- **Installation qualification, operational qualification, and performance qualification (IQ/OQ/PQ)**
- Continuous monitoring using a **calibrated particle counter** (per ISO 21501-4)

**FED-STD-209E Comparison (3 marks):**

| Feature | FED-STD-209E | ISO 14644-1 |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | U.S. Federal (1963, cancelled 2001) | International (ISO, 1999, revised 2015) |
| Classes | Class 1 to 100,000 | ISO 1 to ISO 9 |
| Reference Size | Only 0.5 µm and 5.0 µm | 0.1 µm, 0.5 µm, 5.0 µm |
| Unit | Particles per **ft³** | Particles per **m³** |
| Status | Cancelled but still in colloquial use | Active global standard |
| Industries still using it | HDD, paint, some pharma | Semiconductor, biotech, advanced pharma |

Conversion formula between the two:

$$C_{m^3} = C_{ft^3} \times 35.3147$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Pattern]**

**(a)** Discuss the **different types of contamination** encountered in cleanrooms. Explain how each type is measured and controlled. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Describe the **key parameters used to characterize a cleanroom**. Derive the **formula for air changes per hour (ACH)** and explain its significance. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

There are **four major types of contamination** in a cleanroom:

**1. Particulate Contamination (2 marks)**
- **Definition:** Solid or liquid particles suspended in the air, sized from 0.001 µm to 1000 µm.
- **Source:** Skin flakes, clothing fibers, equipment wear, outdoor dust.
- **Measurement:** Optical particle counter (OPC) using laser light scattering; expressed in **counts per m³** at calibrated sizes (0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 5.0 µm).
- **Control:** HEPA/ULPA filtration, laminar airflow, gowning, sticky mats.

**2. Biological Contamination (Bioburden) (2 marks)**
- **Definition:** Viable microorganisms — bacteria, yeasts, molds, viruses, spores.
- **Source:** Human skin, breath, raw materials, water.
- **Measurement:** **Active air samplers** (e.g., Andersen sampler) draw a known volume of air onto agar; colonies counted as **CFU/m³**. Settle plates measure **CFU/4-hour exposure**.
- **Control:** UV sterilization, disinfectant fogging, autoclaving of materials, restricted access, positive pressure.

**3. Molecular / Chemical Contamination (1.5 marks)**
- **Definition:** Airborne Molecular Contaminants (AMC) — hydrocarbons, acids, bases, siloxanes, dopants.
- **Source:** Cleanroom construction materials, paints, outgassing from plastic, exhaust from equipment.
- **Measurement:** **GC-MS** (Gas Chromatography–Mass Spectrometry), **IMS** (Ion Mobility Spectrometry); expressed in **ppb (parts per billion)**.
- **Control:** Chemical filters (carbon, potassium permanganate), low-outgassing materials, dedicated exhaust.

**4. Electrostatic Contamination (1.5 marks)**
- **Definition:** Static charge on surfaces that attracts airborne particles.
- **Source:** Triboelectric charging of insulators (plastics, synthetic fabric).
- **Measurement:** **Surface resistance** (ohms/sq) and **static charge** (volts).
- **Control:** **Conductive flooring** (10⁶ Ω), **ESD-safe garments**, **ionizers** (AC or DC corona discharge), and **humidity control** (40–55% RH).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Key Parameters to Characterize a Cleanroom (3 marks):**

1. **Cleanroom Class (ISO 14644-1)** — based on particle count.
2. **Air Changes per Hour (ACH)** — rate of air replacement.
3. **Airflow Pattern** — Laminar (unidirectional) vs Turbulent (mixed).
4. **Filter Efficiency** — HEPA / ULPA percentage at MPPS.
5. **Pressure Differential** — typically 10–15 Pa between adjacent zones.
6. **Temperature and Humidity** — process stability and static control.
7. **Recovery Time** — time to clear particles after release of smoke.

**Derivation of ACH (4 marks):**

**Definition:** ACH is the number of times the entire air volume of the cleanroom is replaced per hour.

**Step 1:** Volumetric flow rate from filters:

$$Q = A \times v$$

where $A$ is the filter area in m² and $v$ is the face velocity in m/min or m/s.

**Step 2:** Convert $Q$ to per-hour basis:

$$Q_{hour} = A \times v \times 60 \quad (\text{if } v \text{ is in m/min})$$

**Step 3:** Cleanroom volume:

$$V = L \times B \times H$$

**Step 4:** Apply the ACH definition:

$$ACH = \frac{Q_{hour}}{V} = \frac{A \times v \times 60}{V}$$

**Significance of ACH:**

- **Low ACH (10–20):** Used in ISO 8 / ISO 9 — ordinary clean zones.
- **Medium ACH (20–60):** Used in ISO 7 (e.g., hospital OT, satellite assembly).
- **High ACH (240–600):** Used in ISO 5 (e.g., aseptic filling, semiconductor backend).
- **Very High ACH (>600):** Used in ISO 3–ISO 4 (front-end semiconductor fabs).

Higher ACH = faster particle removal = lower steady-state particle concentration. However, ACH alone does not define the class; it is a *supporting parameter*.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes — Where You Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing sterility with cleanliness.** A cleanroom is *not* the same as a sterile room. Sterility is the *absence of viable microorganisms*, while a cleanroom controls *all particles* including non-living ones. Examiners will deduct 1–2 marks if you use the terms interchangeably.
> 
> 2. **Writing only "Class 100" without reference to ISO 14644-1.** In KTU 2024, ISO nomenclature is preferred. If you write Class 100, *always* write **(ISO 5)** alongside it. The reverse is also true.
> 
> 3. **Skipping the units in ACH derivation.** Marks are reserved for the explicit step where you convert m/min to m/hour (multiply by 60). Forgetting this loses 1 mark.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting to mention HEPA filter efficiency at 0.3 µm MPPS.** Saying "HEPA filters 99.97% particles" without specifying *the size* is incomplete and loses 1 mark.
> 
> 5. **Writing "positive pressure" without specifying the value (10–15 Pa).** Vague statements cost marks. Always state the *magnitude* of the pressure differential.
> 
> 6. **Conflating FED-STD-209E with ISO 14644-1.** FED-STD-209E is **cancelled** since 2001. Always mention the cancellation and then justify that the industry still uses the old nomenclature colloquially.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Cleanroom = controlled environment** for airborne particles per **ISO 14644-1**.
- [x] **Need arises** from three failure modes: physical defects (industry), biological hazard (pharma), and scientific error (research).
- [x] **Three control pillars:** introduction, generation, retention of particles.
- [x] **Four contamination types:** particulate, biological, molecular, electrostatic.
- [x] **ISO 14644-1 formula:** $C_n = 10^N \times (0.1/D)^{2.08}$.
- [x] **HEPA filter:** ≥ 99.97% at **0.3 µm MPPS**; ULPA: ≥ 99.999% at 0.12 µm.
- [x] **ACH formula:** $ACH = (A \times v \times 60)/V$; ISO 5 needs 240–600 ACH.
- [x] **FED-STD-209E** is cancelled (2001) but Class 100 = ISO 5, Class 10 = ISO 4, etc.
- [x] **Pressure cascade:** 10–15 Pa between adjacent zones; higher class = higher pressure.
- [x] **Industries:** semiconductor (ISO 3–5), pharma (ISO 5–8), aerospace (ISO 7–8), HDD (ISO 4–5), hospital OT (ISO 7).
- [x] **Human shedding rate:** ~1,000,000 particles/min when moving — hence strict gowning and minimal movement.
- [x] **MPPS** = Most Penetrating Particle Size = 0.3 µm — the *worst-case* size for HEPA.
- [x] **Recovery time** is a key performance metric — typically ≤ 15 minutes for ISO 5.
- [x] **GMP & USFDA 21 CFR Part 211** mandate Grade A (ISO 5) air for aseptic fill.
- [x] **Conversion:** $1 \, m^3 = 35.3147 \, ft^3$.
- [x] **Always quote particle limit in m³** (ISO standard) — never in ft³.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Clean Room
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT864 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN LARGE SCALE INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Clean Room

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Clean Room** is a specially constructed, highly controlled enclosed environment in which the concentration of airborne particulates (aerosols, dust, microbes, chemical vapors) is maintained within strictly defined limits, typically governed by **ISO 14644-1** or the older **US Federal Standard 209E (FS 209E)**. The room is engineered to minimize the introduction, generation, and retention of contaminants, while precisely controlling parameters such as **temperature, relative humidity, differential pressure, airflow velocity, and air exchange rate**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A Clean Room is a **HEPA/ULPA filtered, positively pressurized, unidirectional or turbulent airflow-controlled environment** designed to maintain particulate contamination at or below specified class limits — essential for **semiconductor fabrication, pharmaceutical aseptic processing, biotechnology, aerospace assembly, and medical device manufacturing**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a Clean Room like a **hospital operating theater** — but for microchips and medicines. Just as a surgical team scrubs in, wears gloves, and uses sterile tools to prevent bacteria from entering a patient's open wound, a clean room uses **special garments (bunny suits)**, **air showers at entry**, and **unidirectional filtered air** to prevent invisible dust particles (often smaller than a human hair) from landing on a silicon wafer or contaminating a vaccine.

Imagine pouring a glass of clean water in a dusty desert. It will immediately catch dust particles. A clean room is like a **glove box filled with constantly moving pure air** — any particle that enters is swept away by the laminar airflow before it can settle on a sensitive product. The room is essentially **"breathing" filtered air** 24/7.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Metrics in Bold:**
> - **HEPA Filter Efficiency:** $\geq 99.97\%$ retention of particles $\geq 0.3\ \mu m$
> - **ULPA Filter Efficiency:** $\geq 99.999\%$ retention of particles $\geq 0.12\ \mu m$
> - **Standard Reference Particle:** $0.5\ \mu m$ (for FS 209E) and $0.1\ \mu m$ (for ISO 14644-1)
> - **Air Velocity (Laminar):** $0.3$ to $0.5\ m/s$ ($60$ to $90\ ft/min$)
> - **Air Changes Per Hour (ACH):** $20$ to $600+$ depending on class
> - **Differential Pressure:** Typically $+10$ to $+15\ Pa$ (positive) for cleanliness cascade

### 1.4 Clean Room Classification Standards

| Standard | Classification Basis | Particle Limit (max per m³) |
|----------|----------------------|------------------------------|
| **FS 209E** (superseded) | Class 1, 10, 100, 1000, 10000, 100000 | E.g., Class 100 = 100 particles $\geq 0.5\ \mu m$ per ft³ |
| **ISO 14644-1** (current) | ISO Class 1 to 9 | E.g., ISO 5 $\equiv$ Class 100 |
| **EU GMP** (Pharma) | Grade A, B, C, D | Based on both at-rest and in-operation states |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** The industry has migrated from FS 209E to **ISO 14644-1**. An **ISO Class 5** clean room corresponds to the older **Class 100** (Federal Standard). The conversion formula is: $\text{Class Number} = 10^{(N-1)} \times 100$ where $N$ is the ISO class number for $0.1\ \mu m$ particles.

### 1.5 Why Clean Rooms Are Critical in Large-Scale Industries

1. **Semiconductor Industry:** A single $0.1\ \mu m$ particle can ruin an entire microchip die. Modern chips have features smaller than $7\ nm$.
2. **Pharmaceuticals:** Aseptic manufacturing of injectables requires ISO 5 / Grade A conditions to prevent microbial contamination.
3. **Aerospace:** Satellite assembly demands particulate control to ensure optical and mechanical precision.
4. **Biotechnology & Medical Devices:** Tissue culture, gene therapy, and implantable devices require sterile, controlled environments.
5. **Optics & Lasers:** Precision lenses and laser mirrors need defect-free surfaces.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principles

A clean room operates on **four core engineering principles**:

1. **Filtration** — Air entering the room passes through **HEPA (High-Efficiency Particulate Air)** or **ULPA (Ultra-Low Particulate Air)** filters that trap particles using three mechanisms:
   - **Interception** — Particles follow airstream but get caught on filter fibers.
   - **Impaction** — Larger particles cannot follow curved airstreams and collide with fibers.
   - **Diffusion** — Sub-micron particles undergo Brownian motion and impact fibers randomly.

2. **Airflow Pattern** — Two main patterns:
   - **Laminar (Unidirectional) Flow:** Air moves in parallel streams at uniform velocity (typically $0.45\ m/s$). Used in ISO Class 1–5.
   - **Turbulent (Non-unidirectional) Flow:** Air is mixed by dilution; used in ISO Class 6–9.

3. **Pressure Cascade** — Adjacent rooms maintain a **differential pressure** so that air always flows *from cleaner to less clean* zones, preventing backflow of contaminants.

4. **Personnel and Material Protocols** — Bunny suits, gloves, masks, air showers, sticky mats, and pass boxes.

### 2.2 Clean Room Design Architecture (Step-by-Step Logic)

**Step 1: Determine Cleanliness Class**
- Identify the industry's regulatory requirement (e.g., ISO 5 for semiconductors, Grade A for sterile injectables).

**Step 2: Calculate Air Volume and Required ACH**
- $V = A \times H$ where $A$ is floor area, $H$ is ceiling height.
- Required supply airflow: $Q = V \times \text{ACH}/60$ (in m³/min or CFM).

**Step 3: Specify Filter Type and Quantity**
- HEPA for ISO 5–7; ULPA for ISO 1–3.
- Filter face area is calculated to maintain face velocity within manufacturer limits (typically $0.5\ m/s$).

**Step 4: Design Air Handling Unit (AHU)**
- Includes pre-filter, cooling/heating coils, humidifier/dehumidifier, fan, and HEPA terminal filter.

**Step 5: Establish Pressure Cascade**
- Calculate leakage area and fan capacity to maintain $\Delta P = +10$ to $+15\ Pa$.

**Step 6: Implement Monitoring and Control**
- Integrate **particle counters, temperature/humidity sensors, differential pressure transmitters, and airflow velocity sensors** into a **DCS/SCADA/BMS** system.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **exam-critical** and frequently tested in KTU ESE papers.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|-------------------|------------|---------------|
| 1 | Volume of clean room | $V = A \times H$ | m³ |
| 2 | Supply airflow rate | $Q = \dfrac{V \times \text{ACH}}{60}$ | m³/min |
| 3 | HEPA filter face velocity | $V_f = \dfrac{Q}{A_{filter}}$ | m/s (typically $\leq 0.5$) |
| 4 | Particle concentration (FS 209E) | $C_N = 10^N \times \left(\dfrac{0.5}{d}\right)^{2.2}$ | particles/ft³ at size $d\ \mu m$ |
| 5 | Filter efficiency | $\eta = \left(1 - \dfrac{C_{downstream}}{C_{upstream}}\right) \times 100\%$ | % |
| 6 | Differential pressure | $\Delta P = P_{clean} - P_{dirty}$ | Pa (positive = clean side higher) |
| 7 | Air changes per hour (ACH) | $\text{ACH} = \dfrac{Q \times 60}{V}$ | hr⁻¹ |
| 8 | Recovery time | $t_r = \dfrac{-V}{Q}\ln\left(\dfrac{C_{final}}{C_{initial}}\right)$ | seconds |
| 9 | ISO Class to FS 209E | $\text{ISO Class } N \approx \text{FS 209E Class } 10^{(N-1)} \times 100$ for $0.1\ \mu m$ | — |
| 10 | Filter pressure drop | $\Delta P_{filter} = K \cdot \rho \cdot \dfrac{V_f^2}{2}$ | Pa (using Darcy's law) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Avoid the pipe symbol `|` in your answer scripts** when writing absolute values or conditions — use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX, or simply write *"mod"* or *"absolute value of"*.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Semiconductor Fabs:** TSMC, Intel, and Samsung operate ISO Class 3–4 cleanrooms covering thousands of square meters.
- **Pharma Blockbusters:** COVID-19 vaccine manufacturing (Pfizer, Moderna) required ISO 5 / Grade A environments.
- **Space Industry:** NASA's Goddard Space Flight Center cleanrooms assemble satellites in ISO Class 7–8.
- **Hospital Pharmacies:** IV admixture rooms use ISO Class 5 laminar flow hoods.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Conversion Between FS 209E and ISO 14644-1 Classes

The **Federal Standard 209E** classifies clean rooms by the maximum number of particles $\geq 0.5\ \mu m$ per cubic foot. The **ISO 14644-1** standard classifies by particles $\geq 0.1\ \mu m$ per cubic meter. To convert:

**Step 1:** Start with the FS 209E particle count formula:

$$C_N = 10^N \cdot \left(\frac{0.5}{d}\right)^{2.2}$$

This formula gives the *allowable number of particles per cubic foot* of size $d\ \mu m$ or larger in a Class $N$ clean room.

**Step 2:** Recognize that particle counts are normalized per unit volume. The exponent **2.2** comes from empirical data on typical particle size distributions in clean rooms.

**Step 3:** Convert particles per ft³ to particles per m³. Since $1\ ft^3 = 0.0283\ m^3$:

$$C_{m^3} = \frac{C_{ft^3}}{0.0283} \approx 35.3 \times C_{ft^3}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute and equate. For ISO Class $M$ (which counts particles $\geq 0.1\ \mu m$):

$$C_{ISO,M} = 10^M \cdot \left(\frac{0.1}{0.1}\right)^{2.2} \cdot 35.3 = 35.3 \times 10^M \quad \text{(particles/m}^3)$$

**Step 5:** Equate to the FS 209E expression at the same cutoff size $d = 0.1\ \mu m$ for a Class $N$ room:

$$C_{FS,N}(0.1) = 10^N \cdot \left(\frac{0.5}{0.1}\right)^{2.2} = 10^N \cdot 5^{2.2}$$

**Step 6:** Compute $5^{2.2}$:

$$5^{2.2} = e^{2.2 \cdot \ln 5} = e^{2.2 \cdot 1.6094} = e^{3.541} \approx 34.5$$

**Step 7:** Set equal:

$$35.3 \times 10^M = 34.5 \times 10^N \cdot \frac{1}{0.0283} \rightarrow 10^M \approx 10^N \cdot 3.5$$

**Step 8:** Taking $\log_{10}$:

$$M \approx N + \log_{10}(3.5) \approx N + 0.544$$

**Step 9:** Round to nearest standard class. For example:
- **ISO Class 5** ($\approx$ Class 100) ↔ FS 209E Class 100
- **ISO Class 7** ↔ FS 209E Class 10000

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Reference:** $\text{ISO Class} = N + 0.5$ (approximately), where $N$ is the logarithm of the FS 209E class number for $0.5\ \mu m$ particles. So FS 100 ($\log_{10} = 2$) $\rightarrow$ ISO 5; FS 10000 ($\log_{10} = 4$) $\rightarrow$ ISO 7.

### 3.2 Derivation: Filter Face Velocity and Pressure Drop

**Step 1:** A HEPA filter is rated for a nominal airflow $Q_{rated}$ at a face velocity $V_{f,r} = 0.45\ m/s$.

**Step 2:** Face area is:

$$A_{face} = \frac{Q_{rated}}{V_{f,r}}$$

**Step 3:** Initial pressure drop across the filter is given by:

$$\Delta P_{initial} = K \cdot \rho \cdot \frac{V_f^2}{2}$$

where $K$ is the resistance coefficient (typically 10–20 for HEPA), $\rho$ is air density ($1.2\ kg/m^3$).

**Step 4:** As the filter loads with particles, $\Delta P$ increases. The filter is replaced when $\Delta P \approx 2.5 \times \Delta P_{initial}$ (typically $250$ to $500\ Pa$).

**Step 5:** The actual face velocity during operation:

$$V_{f,actual} = \frac{Q_{actual}}{A_{face}}$$

**Step 6:** Maintain $V_{f,actual}$ within $\pm 20\%$ of rated for proper filtration.

### 3.3 Derivation: Recovery Time

When contamination is introduced (e.g., door opens, operator enters), the recovery time to reach the design class is:

$$t_r = -\frac{V}{Q} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{C_{final}}{C_{initial}}\right)$$

where:
- $V$ = room volume (m³)
- $Q$ = supply airflow (m³/s)
- $C_{final}$ = target particle concentration
- $C_{initial}$ = current (elevated) concentration

**Example:** A $100\ m^3$ cleanroom with $Q = 0.5\ m^3/s$ (30 m³/min) goes from $C_i = 10^7$ to $C_f = 10^5$ particles/m³.

$$t_r = -\frac{100}{0.5} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{10^5}{10^7}\right) = -200 \cdot \ln(0.01) = -200 \cdot (-4.605) \approx 921\ s \approx 15.4\ min$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Cleanroom Types

| Feature | Class 1 / ISO 3 | Class 100 / ISO 5 | Class 10000 / ISO 7 | Class 100000 / ISO 8 |
|---------|-----------------|-------------------|----------------------|------------------------|
| **Air changes/hr** | 500–750 | 240–600 | 60–150 | 20–60 |
| **Airflow pattern** | Laminar (vertical) | Laminar (vertical/horizontal) | Mixed | Turbulent |
| **Filter type** | ULPA | HEPA/ULPA | HEPA | HEPA |
| **Pressure (Pa)** | +15 to +20 | +12 to +15 | +10 to +12 | +5 to +10 |
| **Temperature** | $21 \pm 1^\circ C$ | $21 \pm 1^\circ C$ | $21 \pm 2^\circ C$ | $18$–$26^\circ C$ |
| **Humidity (%RH)** | $45 \pm 5$ | $45 \pm 5$ | $45 \pm 10$ | $30$–$70$ |
| **Industry use** | Semiconductor fab | Pharma aseptic, biotech | Pharma, hospital | General mfg, assembly |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define a clean room. List any **four** industry applications where clean rooms are mandatory.

**Model Answer:**
A clean room is a controlled environment in which the concentration of airborne particulates is maintained within specified limits (per ISO 14644-1 or FS 209E), along with controlled temperature, humidity, pressure, and airflow. Key parameters include HEPA filtration, laminar/turbulent flow, and positive pressure.

**Four industry applications:**
1. Semiconductor wafer fabrication (ISO Class 3–5)
2. Pharmaceutical aseptic manufacturing (GMP Grade A/B)
3. Aerospace satellite assembly (ISO Class 7–8)
4. Medical device and biotechnology production (ISO Class 5)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between **HEPA** and **ULPA** filters with respect to efficiency and particle size.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | HEPA | ULPA |
|-----------|------|------|
| **Full form** | High-Efficiency Particulate Air | Ultra-Low Particulate Air |
| **Efficiency** | $\geq 99.97\%$ | $\geq 99.999\%$ |
| **Particle size** | $\geq 0.3\ \mu m$ | $\geq 0.12\ \mu m$ |
| **Application** | ISO Class 5–8 | ISO Class 1–4 |
| **Pressure drop** | Lower | Higher |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks – Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** A cleanroom measures **$10\ m \times 8\ m \times 3\ m$** (L × W × H). It is to be maintained at **ISO Class 5**, requiring a **laminar airflow** of $0.45\ m/s$ over the entire ceiling. The HEPA filter bank has a face area of $4\ m^2$. Calculate: **(7 marks)**
   (i) The room volume.
   (ii) The total supply airflow in m³/s.
   (iii) Verify if the HEPA filter face velocity is within the manufacturer rating.

**(b)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **functional architecture of a clean room**, including AHU, filtration stages, and BMS integration. Discuss the role of **pressure cascade** and **recovery time** in maintaining class limits. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) (i) Volume:**

$$V = 10 \times 8 \times 3 = 240\ m^3$$

**(ii) Supply airflow:**

$$Q = A_{ceiling} \times V_{air} = (10 \times 8) \times 0.45 = 80 \times 0.45 = 36\ m^3/s$$

**(iii) Face velocity on HEPA filter:**

$$V_f = \frac{Q}{A_{filter}} = \frac{36}{4} = 9\ m/s$$

**Result:** This is **far above** the manufacturer rating of $0.45\ m/s$. Hence, the **HEPA filter bank must be enlarged** to:

$$A_{required} = \frac{36}{0.45} = 80\ m^2$$

This indicates the actual installation needs approximately $80\ m^2$ of HEPA filter face area, distributed across the ceiling as a **grid of terminal filters** — typical of laminar flow cleanrooms.

**(b) Block Diagram & Discussion:** Refer to the diagrams in Section 4.1 and 4.2. Key points:
- AHU stages: Pre-filter → Cooling coil → Fan → Secondary filter → HEPA terminal.
- **Pressure cascade** ensures air flows from clean to less clean, preventing ingress.
- **Recovery time** is the time to restore class limits after a contamination event, governed by $Q$ and $V$. Faster recovery is achieved by higher ACH.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** With a suitable sketch, describe the construction and working of a **HEPA filter**. State the **three filtration mechanisms** and explain why HEPA filters are preferred in cleanrooms. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cleanroom of volume $V = 500\ m^3$ is supplied with $Q = 2.5\ m^3/s$ of HEPA-filtered air. After a contamination event, the particle count rises to $C_i = 10^8$ particles/m³. Compute the **recovery time** required to reach $C_f = 10^6$ particles/m³. Comment on whether this recovery time is acceptable for an **ISO Class 5** semiconductor cleanroom. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) HEPA Filter Construction and Working:**

A HEPA filter is a **pleated, dry-type, fibrous filter** made of randomly arranged borosilicate microfibers, pleated to maximize surface area. The pleats are separated by **aluminum corrugated spacers** and sealed in a metal or plastic frame. Air is forced through the filter medium; particles are trapped by:

1. **Interception** — Mid-sized particles ($0.1$–$0.4\ \mu m$) follow airstream but touch fibers and adhere.
2. **Impaction** — Larger particles ($> 0.5\ \mu m$) have too much inertia to follow curved airstreams and crash into fibers.
3. **Diffusion** — Sub-micron particles ($< 0.1\ \mu m$) undergo Brownian motion and randomly strike fibers.

HEPA filters achieve $\geq 99.97\%$ efficiency at $0.3\ \mu m$ (the most penetrating particle size, MPPS). They are preferred because they:
- Have high single-pass efficiency
- Operate at low pressure drop
- Can handle high airflow rates
- Are robust and have long service life (typically 5–10 years)

**(b) Recovery Time Calculation:**

$$t_r = -\frac{V}{Q} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{C_f}{C_i}\right)$$

$$t_r = -\frac{500}{2.5} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{10^6}{10^8}\right) = -200 \cdot \ln(0.01)$$

$$t_r = -200 \times (-4.605) = 921\ s \approx 15.4\ minutes$$

**Comment:** For an **ISO Class 5** semiconductor cleanroom, recovery should typically be **under 5–10 minutes** to avoid production loss and contamination-related yield drop. Since $15.4$ minutes is **above** the ideal, the system should either:
- Increase supply airflow $Q$ (larger fans or HEPA area), or
- Reduce room volume, or
- Upgrade to ULPA filters to reduce initial contamination source.

A revised design target: $t_r \leq 5$ min requires $Q \geq 500/300 \times 4.605 \approx 7.7\ m^3/s$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Clean Room Questions:**
> 1. **Unit confusion** between m³ and ft³ when converting FS 209E to ISO. Always convert $1\ ft^3 = 0.0283\ m^3$.
> 2. **Forgetting to specify the particle size** (0.1, 0.3, or 0.5 µm) when stating class limits — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark per missing parameter.
> 3. **Sign of differential pressure:** Positive = clean room is at higher pressure than surrounding; negative = containment (e.g., for pathogens). Students often confuse these.
> 4. **MPPS confusion:** The Most Penetrating Particle Size for HEPA is **0.3 µm**, NOT 0.1 µm. Below this, diffusion dominates; above it, impaction dominates.
> 5. **Skipping the assumption** in numerical problems (e.g., "Assuming uniform laminar flow"). This costs 1–2 marks routinely.
> 6. **Confusing ACH with face velocity** — ACH is room-level, face velocity is filter-level. KTU frequently tests this distinction.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**
> - Clean Room = controlled particulate + T + RH + pressure + airflow environment per ISO 14644-1.
> - **FS 209E Class N** $\approx$ **ISO Class (N+0.5)** roughly; e.g., Class 100 ↔ ISO 5.
> - **HEPA:** $\geq 99.97\%$ at $0.3\ \mu m$; **ULPA:** $\geq 99.999\%$ at $0.12\ \mu m$.
> - **Three filtration mechanisms:** Interception, Impaction, Diffusion.
> - **Laminar flow velocity:** $0.3$–$0.5\ m/s$; **ACH:** $20$–$600+$ depending on class.
> - **Pressure cascade:** Clean room must be at **+10 to +15 Pa** higher than adjacent.
> - **MPPS** (Most Penetrating Particle Size) for HEPA = **$0.3\ \mu m$**.
> - **Recovery time formula:** $t_r = -(V/Q) \cdot \ln(C_f/C_i)$ — KTU favorite.
> - **Differential pressure transmitter + particle counter + T/RH sensor** = core instrumentation in cleanroom BMS.
> - **Bunny suits, air showers, pass boxes, sticky mats** = contamination control protocols.
> - **Industries:** Semiconductor, Pharma, Biotech, Aerospace, Medical Devices, Optics.
> - **EU GMP grades** (A/B/C/D) apply to pharma; **ISO Classes** apply to general/electronics.

---

**END OF NOTES — CLEAN ROOM (MODULE 3)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Subzone Optimisation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT864 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN LARGE SCALE INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Subzone Optimisation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Subzone Optimisation** is a hierarchical, intermediate-level control and optimisation strategy used in large-scale continuous process industries (refineries, petrochemical complexes, integrated chemical plants, and power plants). In this framework, the entire plant is *decomposed* into several **subzones**, where each subzone is a logical grouping of functionally coupled process units that share material, energy, or information streams. The objective of subzone optimisation is to determine the *optimal setpoints* for the local controllers (typically regulatory PID loops) within that subzone so that a subzone-level economic or operational objective is maximised (or minimised), while satisfying local and inter-unit process constraints.

Formally, for a subzone $Z_k$ containing $n_k$ process units:

$$\max_{u_k \in U_k} \; J_k(u_k, d_k, y_k)$$

subject to:

$$g_k(u_k, d_k, y_k) \leq 0, \quad h_k(u_k, d_k, y_k) = 0$$

where $u_k$ = manipulated variables, $d_k$ = disturbances, $y_k$ = controlled variables, and $J_k$ is the subzone economic objective.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Subzone Optimisation bridges the gap between *unit-level regulatory control* (Layer 0/1) and *plant-wide optimisation* (Layer 4) in the **Perkins/Walsh Hierarchical Control Pyramid** widely used in KTU-endorsed process control curricula.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine managing a **massive international airport**:

- The **whole airport** = the *entire plant* (plant-wide optimisation)
- Each **terminal building** = a *subzone* (e.g., the crude distillation subzone, the hydrocracker subzone)
- Each **check-in counter and gate** = a *process unit* with its own local controller

You wouldn't have one person making every decision across the whole airport. Instead, each **terminal manager** optimises their own terminal — gate assignments, baggage flow, security queues — then reports up to an **airport director** who coordinates the terminals.

Similarly, a refinery **subzone manager** looks at a cluster of units (say, the entire Naphtha Hydrotreating block) and decides the best operating points for that block, *not* for the whole refinery. This is Subzone Optimisation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A subzone is defined by **strong internal coupling** (units exchange a lot of material/energy) and **relatively weak external coupling** (only a few streams connect it to other subzones). This structural property is what makes decomposition mathematically tractable.

## 1.3 Why Subzones Exist in Large-Scale Industry

A modern refinery may have **hundreds of process units** and **thousands of control loops**. Solving a single, monolithic optimisation problem with all variables is:

- **Computationally intractable** (curse of dimensionality)
- **Practically unreliable** (one failed sensor could invalidate the whole solution)
- **Organisationally unrealistic** (different engineering groups own different units)

Subzone decomposition solves all three problems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** Industry-standard control hierarchy has **5 layers** per the ISA-95 / Purdue model: Field I/O (L0) → Regulatory Control (L1) → Supervisory / Advanced Control (L2) → Plant-Wide Optimisation (L3) → Corporate Planning (L4). Subzone Optimisation sits at **L2-L3 interface**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Subzone Decomposition Principle

Large-scale process plants are decomposed into subzones based on **process topology** (P&ID connectivity), **material balance closure**, and **control interaction strength** (Relative Gain Array, RGA analysis).

### 2.1.1 Steps in Subzone Definition

1. **Graph-Theoretic Partitioning:** Construct a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ where vertices $V$ are process units and edges $E$ are interconnecting streams. Use community-detection algorithms (Louvain, spectral clustering) to identify natural subzone boundaries.

2. **Interaction Analysis:** Compute the **Relative Gain Array (RGA)** for each candidate subzone:

$$\Lambda_{ij} = \frac{\left(\partial y_i / \partial u_j\right)_G}{\left(\partial y_i / \partial u_j\right)_{all\;loops\;open}}$$

3. **Cut-Set Identification:** Identify **cut-stream** variables — the streams that cross subzone boundaries. These become the **coordination variables** in the upper level.

4. **Subzone Validation:** Ensure each subzone is *operationally meaningful* (e.g., a hydrotreater + reactor + separator train forms a natural subzone, not a random split).

## 2.2 Mathematical Formulation (Detailed)

The general subzone optimisation problem is a **constrained nonlinear program (NLP)**. For subzone $Z_k$:

$$\max_{u_k, x_k} \; \Phi_k(x_k, u_k) = c_k^T u_k + \frac{1}{2} u_k^T Q_k u_k$$

subject to:

$$\dot{x}_k = f_k(x_k, u_k, d_k) \quad \text{(dynamic model)}$$

$$g_k(x_k, u_k) \leq 0 \quad \text{(inequality constraints)}$$

$$h_k(x_k, u_k) = 0 \quad \text{(equality constraints)}$$

$$u_k^{\min} \leq u_k \leq u_k^{\max}$$

For the **steady-state** case (which is the most common in KTU problems):

$$f_k(x_k, u_k, d_k) = 0 \implies x_k = \text{steady-state map of } (u_k, d_k)$$

The objective $\Phi_k$ is typically a **profit function**: revenue from products minus cost of feedstocks, energy, and utilities.

## 2.3 Coordination Variables and the Cut-Stream Concept

When a stream connects subzone $Z_1$ to $Z_2$, its flow rate $F_{12}$ appears as:
- An **output (disturbance)** for $Z_1$
- An **input (decision variable)** for $Z_2$

The coordination problem is: choose $F_{12}$ such that *both* subzones are optimal simultaneously. This is the **price-based / Lagrangian coordination** approach.

### 2.3.1 Lagrangian Coordination

Define the augmented subzone Lagrangian:

$$L_k = \Phi_k + \lambda^T (F_k^{in} - F_k^{out}) - \mu^T (c_k^{cut})$$

where $\lambda$ is a vector of **transfer prices** for cut streams and $\mu$ are Lagrange multipliers for resource coupling. The upper-level **coordinator** updates $\lambda, \mu$ via gradient ascent:

$$\lambda^{(i+1)} = \lambda^{(i)} + \alpha_i \cdot \frac{\partial L_{total}}{\partial \lambda}$$

This is the **Interaction-Balance Method** or **Price Decomposition**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\max \Phi_k(u_k) = \sum_j p_j P_j - \sum_i f_i F_i$ | Subzone profit objective | Economic optimisation |
| 2 | $\Lambda_{ij} = \dfrac{K_{ij}}{K_{ij}^{eff}}$ | Relative Gain Array element | Loop pairing / subzone definition |
| 3 | $F_{cut}^{(i+1)} = F_{cut}^{(i)} + \alpha \cdot \nabla_\lambda L$ | Coordination update (gradient method) | Multi-subzone coordination |
| 4 | $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu$ | Linear subzone state-space model | MPC design |
| 5 | $y = Cx + Du$ | Output equation | MPC / observer design |
| 6 | $J_{MPC} = \sum_{k=0}^{H_p} \Vert y(k) - y_{ref} \Vert_Q^2 + \sum_{k=0}^{H_c-1} \Vert \Delta u(k) \Vert_R^2$ | Model Predictive Control cost | Subzone MPC |
| 7 | $u^* = \arg\min_u J_{MPC}$ | MPC QP/NLP solution | Setpoint tracking |
| 8 | $K_p, T_i, T_d$ (Ziegler-Nichols) | PID tuning for inner loop | Regulatory layer |
| 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{\Delta P_{useful}}{\Delta P_{total}}$ | Pump/turbine isentropic efficiency | Energy cost in objective |
| 10 | $Q = m C_p \Delta T$ | Heat duty (utility cost) | Energy subzone cost term |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL TYPESETTING NOTE:** In the table above, all vertical bars use the latex `\Vert` command (e.g., $\Vert y \Vert_Q^2$) instead of the raw `|` character — this is the **KTU-2024 Safe Table Syntax** and prevents markdown table corruption in PDF export.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Subzone Optimisation is **the workhorse layer** of modern refinery advanced process control (APC). Industry leaders like **Honeywell Profit Suite**, **Aspen DMCplus**, **Yokogawa APC**, and **ABB Optimize** all implement subzone-level MPC that is then coordinated at the plant-wide RTO level.

Typical industries deploying it:

- **Crude oil refineries** (CDU/VDU subzone, FCC subzone, hydrocracker subzone, blending subzone)
- **Petrochemical complexes** (ethylene cracker subzone, polymerisation subzone)
- **LNG plants** (liquefaction subzone, fractionation subzone)
- **Power plants** (boiler subzone, turbine subzone, HRSG subzone)
- **Pulp & Paper mills** (digester subzone, dryer subzone)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real industrial metric:** A well-tuned subzone MPC on a CDU can deliver **$1–5 million USD/year** in additional margin per unit (per industry white papers, e.g., Honeywell 2018 APC Impact Survey).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Subzone Profit Objective from First Principles

Consider a hydrotreating subzone with:
- Feed: Naphtha at flow rate $F_N$ (m³/h), price $c_N$ ($/m³)
- Product: Treated Naphtha at flow rate $P_T$ (m³/h), price $p_T$ ($/m³)
- Utility: Steam at $Q_s$ (kg/h), price $c_s$ ($/kg)
- Conversion efficiency: $\eta = P_T / F_N$

**Step 1:** Define revenue from product sales.
The instantaneous revenue is price × flow:

$$R = p_T \cdot P_T = p_T \cdot \eta \cdot F_N$$

**Step 2:** Define raw material cost.
The cost of feedstock is:

$$C_{feed} = c_N \cdot F_N$$

**Step 3:** Define utility cost.
Steam cost is:

$$C_{steam} = c_s \cdot Q_s = c_s \cdot m_s C_p \Delta T$$

**Step 4:** Construct the profit objective.
The subzone profit per unit time is revenue minus total costs:

$$\Phi = R - C_{feed} - C_{steam} - C_{fixed}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute the variables and simplify.
Substituting $P_T = \eta F_N$ and $Q_s = m_s C_p (T_{out} - T_{in})$:

$$\Phi = p_T \cdot \eta \cdot F_N - c_N \cdot F_N - c_s \cdot m_s C_p (T_{out} - T_{in}) - C_{fixed}$$

**Step 6:** Group terms by decision variable.
Collecting the terms involving $F_N$ and $T_{out}$:

$$\Phi(F_N, T_{out}) = F_N (p_T \eta - c_N) - c_s m_s C_p (T_{out} - T_{in}) - C_{fixed}$$

**Step 7:** Identify the optimal direction.
For a maximisation problem, the profit increases with $F_N$ as long as the **margin** $(p_T \eta - c_N) > 0$, and decreases with $T_{out}$ (since heating costs more). Thus, intuitively, the subzone controller should:

$$\text{Set } F_N \uparrow \text{ (within limits)}, \quad T_{out} \downarrow \text{ (within product spec)}$$

This is the fundamental insight of subzone optimisation: **the optimal operating point is constrained by product specifications and equipment limits, not by open-loop maximum throughput.**

## 3.2 Derivation: Lagrangian Coordination Update

We have two subzones $Z_1$ and $Z_2$ coupled by a single cut stream with flow $F_{12}$.

**Step 1:** Subzone 1's problem.
Treating $F_{12}$ as a decision variable of $Z_1$ (it can choose how much to send):

$$\max_{F_{12}} \Phi_1(F_1^{in}, F_{12})$$

subject to $F_{12}^{\min} \leq F_{12} \leq F_{12}^{\max}$.

**Step 2:** Subzone 2's problem.
Treating $F_{12}$ as a *given input*:

$$\max_{u_2} \Phi_2(F_2^{in} = F_{12}, u_2)$$

**Step 3:** Define the overall plant objective.
Plant profit is the sum:

$$\Phi_{plant} = \Phi_1 + \Phi_2$$

**Step 4:** Apply Lagrangian dual decomposition.
Introduce multiplier $\lambda$ for the coupling constraint $F_{12} = F_{21}$ (conservation). The dual problem:

$$L = \Phi_1(F_{12}) + \Phi_2(F_{12}) + \lambda \cdot (\text{imbalance})$$

**Step 5:** Decompose into two independent subproblems.

$$L = \underbrace{[\Phi_1(F_{12}) - \lambda F_{12}]}_{L_1} + \underbrace{[\Phi_2(F_{12}) + \lambda F_{12}]}_{L_2}$$

Each subzone can now be solved independently — $Z_1$ sees $\lambda$ as a *cost per unit* of the cut stream it sends, and $Z_2$ sees $\lambda$ as a *revenue per unit* of cut stream it receives.

**Step 6:** Coordinator updates $\lambda$.
The upper-level coordinator maximises the dual:

$$\lambda^{(i+1)} = \lambda^{(i)} + \alpha \cdot [F_{12}^{(Z_1)} - F_{12}^{(Z_2)}]$$

where $\alpha$ is the step size. At convergence, $F_{12}^{(Z_1)} = F_{12}^{(Z_2)}$ (i.e., supply equals demand at the cut stream).

**Step 7:** Convergence check.
The dual variables $\lambda^*$ are interpreted as **transfer prices** — the implicit cost of material flowing between subzones. At optimum, $\lambda^*$ equals the marginal value of the cut stream.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A subzone consists of a reactor and separator. The reactor converts feed A → product B with yield $y = 0.85$. Feed A costs **$800/ton**, product B sells for **$1500/ton**. Maximum feed rate is **100 ton/h**. What is the unconstrained optimal feed rate?

**Solution:**

Step 1: Margin per ton of feed:
$$M = y \cdot p_B - c_A = 0.85 \times 1500 - 800 = 1275 - 800 = 475 \text{ \$/ton}$$

Step 2: Unconstrained optimum:
Since $M > 0$, profit is maximised at maximum feed:
$$F_A^* = F_A^{\max} = 100 \text{ ton/h}$$

Step 3: Maximum profit:
$$\Phi^{\max} = 475 \times 100 = 47{,}500 \text{ \$/h}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** Always check if the unconstrained optimum violates equipment constraints (compressor capacity, heat exchanger area, etc.). In KTU questions, the *real* optimum is almost always at a constraint boundary, not in the interior.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Control Pyramid (Purdue / ISA-95)

The following Mermaid block illustrates where Subzone Optimisation lives in the standard industrial control hierarchy. This is one of the most exam-asked diagrams in KTU Module 3.

```mermaid

    L4["LAYER 4 CORPORATE PLANNING"]:::lvl4
    L3["LAYER 3 PLANT WIDE OPTIMISATION RTO"]:::lvl3

    L2B["LAYER 2 SUBZONE OPTIMISATION ZONE B"]:::lvl2b
    L2C["LAYER 2 SUBZONE OPTIMISATION ZONE C"]:::lvl2c

    L1B["LAYER 1 REGULATORY CONTROL ZONE B"]:::lvl1b
    L1C["LAYER 1 REGULATORY CONTROL ZONE C"]:::lvl1c

    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2A
    L3 --> L2B
    L3 --> L2C
    L2A --> L1A
    L2B --> L1B
    L2C --> L1C
    L1A --> L0
    L1B --> L0
    L1C --> L0
```

This figure shows how a large refinery is partitioned into 4 subzones with explicit cut streams. This is the **canonical KTU Module 3 figure**.

## 4.3 Subzone Optimisation Information Flow

This shows the data and command flow between a subzone, its MPC, and the upper-level plant optimiser.

```mermaid
graph TD
    PLTOP["PLANT WIDE RTO LAYER 3"]:::top
    SZOPT["SUBZONE OPTIMISER LAYER 2"]:::sz
    MPC["SUBZONE MPC LAYER 2"]:::mpc
    REG["REGULATORY PID LAYER 1"]:::reg
    FLD["FIELD PLANT LAYER 0"]:::fld

    PLTOP -- "Setpoints and Economic Targets" --> SZOPT
    SZOPT -- "Optimal Steady State Targets" --> MPC
    MPC -- "Dynamic Setpoint Trajectories" --> REG
    REG -- "MV signals 4 to 20 mA" --> FLD
    FLD -- "Process measurements" --> REG
    REG -- "PV feedback" --> MPC
    MPC -- "Status and constraints" --> SZOPT
    SZOPT -- "Economic performance" --> PLTOP
```

## 4.4 Cut-Stream Coordination Architecture

This is the **price-based / Lagrangian coordination** flow, exactly matching Section 3.2 derivation.

```mermaid
graph TD
    COORD["UPPER COORDINATOR"]:::coord
    SZ1A["SUBZONE 1 SOLVER"]:::sz1
    SZ2A["SUBZONE 2 SOLVER"]:::sz2
    PLANT["PHYSICAL PLANT CUT STREAMS"]:::plant

    COORD -- "lambda 1 transfer price" --> SZ1A
    COORD -- "lambda 2 transfer price" --> SZ2A
    SZ1A -- "F12 demand" --> COORD
    SZ2A -- "F21 supply" --> COORD
    COORD -- "Imbalance F12 minus F21" --> COORD
    SZ1A -- "Setpoints" --> PLANT
    SZ2A -- "Setpoints" --> PLANT
    PLANT -- "Actual flow data" --> SZ1A
    PLANT -- "Actual flow data" --> SZ2A
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: Define subzone optimisation in large-scale process industries. How does it differ from plant-wide optimisation?**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):** Subzone optimisation is a hierarchical control strategy in which a large continuous process plant is partitioned into smaller, logically coupled groups of process units called *subzones*. The objective is to compute optimal steady-state operating setpoints for the regulatory control loops *within* a subzone so as to maximise a subzone-level economic or performance index, subject to local process constraints.

**Difference (1.5 Marks):**

| Aspect | Subzone Optimisation | Plant-Wide Optimisation |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | A group of related units (e.g., CDU block) | Entire plant |
| **Time-scale** | Hours to minutes | Days to hours |
| **Coordination** | Uses local subzone models | Coordinates subzone outputs |
| **Solver** | Subzone-level NLP/LP | Plant-wide NLP with cut-stream coupling |

> [!TIP]
> **Marking Pattern:** Examiners award 1 mark for a clean definition, 1 mark for an example, and 1 mark for the differentiating table. Always include an example in your answer.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: List and briefly explain any three criteria used to define a subzone in a refinery.**

**Model Answer (1 mark each):**

1. **Process Topological Coupling:** Units that are directly connected by material or energy streams (sharing feed, product, or recycle) are grouped into the same subzone. Example: Reactor + Separator + Recycle Compressor in a hydrotreater block.

2. **Material Balance Closure:** A subzone should be selected such that most of its material balance can be closed internally, with only a few streams crossing the subzone boundary. This minimises cut-stream coordination complexity.

3. **Control Interaction Strength (RGA-based):** Units that exhibit strong dynamic interaction (high RGA values, $\Lambda_{ij} \to 1$) should reside in the same subzone, while weakly interacting units (low RGA) should be separated. This reduces the dimensionality of the subzone optimisation problem.

> [!NOTE]
> **Optional 4th criterion** (for extra credit): *Operator/Engineering Ownership* — subzones often align with the shift teams or process engineering groups responsible for the units.

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: A refinery has a Catalytic Cracking Subzone consisting of a Reactor, a Regenerator, and a Main Fractionator. The feed to the reactor is vacuum gas oil (VGO) at flow rate $F_{VGO}$ (ton/h), cost = $400/ton. The main product is gasoline at flow rate $F_g$ (ton/h), selling price = $850/ton$. The conversion yield of VGO to gasoline is $y = 0.78$. A catalyst regeneration air blower consumes electrical power $P$ (kW) at $0.10/kWh. The relation between blower power and VGO feed is $P = 5 + 0.04 F_{VGO}$. The product separator also requires steam at $Q_s = 0.5 F_g$ (ton/h) at a cost of $30/ton of steam. Maximum feed capacity is 200 ton/h due to reactor constraints. Minimum gasoline production for downstream commitments is 100 ton/h.**

**(a) Formulate the subzone optimisation problem as a mathematical program with the profit objective and all constraints. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Solve the problem to find the optimal feed rate and the maximum subzone profit. (7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Formulation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Identify Decision Variables (1 Mark)**
The main decision variable is the VGO feed rate $F_{VGO}$. The gasoline production is then determined by the yield: $F_g = 0.78 \cdot F_{VGO}$.

**Step 2: Construct the Profit Function (3 Marks)**
Profit = Revenue − Raw Material Cost − Electricity Cost − Steam Cost

Revenue from gasoline:
$$R = 850 \cdot F_g = 850 \times 0.78 F_{VGO} = 663 F_{VGO}$$

Raw material cost:
$$C_{VGO} = 400 \cdot F_{VGO}$$

Electricity cost (per hour, since $P$ is in kW):
$$C_{elec} = 0.10 \cdot P = 0.10 (5 + 0.04 F_{VGO}) = 0.5 + 0.004 F_{VGO}$$

Steam cost:
$$C_{steam} = 30 \cdot Q_s = 30 \times 0.5 F_g = 15 F_g = 15 \times 0.78 F_{VGO} = 11.7 F_{VGO}$$

Total profit per hour:
$$\Phi = 663 F_{VGO} - 400 F_{VGO} - 0.5 - 0.004 F_{VGO} - 11.7 F_{VGO}$$

Simplifying by collecting $F_{VGO}$ terms:
$$\Phi(F_{VGO}) = (663 - 400 - 0.004 - 11.7) F_{VGO} - 0.5$$

$$\Phi(F_{VGO}) = 251.296 F_{VGO} - 0.5$$

**Step 3: Write Constraints (3 Marks)**

1. **Maximum feed constraint** (reactor capacity):
$$F_{VGO} \leq 200$$

2. **Minimum gasoline commitment** ($F_g \geq 100$):
$$0.78 F_{VGO} \geq 100 \implies F_{VGO} \geq 128.21$$

3. **Non-negativity:**
$$F_{VGO} \geq 0$$

**Final Formulation:**

$$\max_{F_{VGO}} \; \Phi = 251.296 F_{VGO} - 0.5$$

subject to:
$$F_{VGO} \leq 200, \quad F_{VGO} \geq 128.21, \quad F_{VGO} \geq 0$$

---

**Part (b) — Solution (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Analyse the Objective (2 Marks)**
Since the coefficient of $F_{VGO}$ in the objective (251.296) is **positive**, the profit is monotonically increasing in $F_{VGO}$. Therefore, the unconstrained optimum is at $F_{VGO} \to \infty$, but constraints will bound it.

**Step 2: Check Feasible Region (2 Marks)**
The feasible region is:
$$128.21 \leq F_{VGO} \leq 200$$

**Step 3: Find Constrained Optimum (2 Marks)**
Because profit increases with $F_{VGO}$, the maximum is at the **upper bound**:
$$F_{VGO}^* = 200 \text{ ton/h}$$

**Step 4: Compute Maximum Profit (1 Mark)**
$$\Phi^{\max} = 251.296 \times 200 - 0.5 = 50{,}259.2 - 0.5 = 50{,}258.7 \text{ \$/h}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students incorrectly write the profit function with a single $F_{VGO}$ term and forget to include the **steam cost term** $C_{steam}$ in the objective. This loses 1–2 marks. **Always itemise every cost component** (feedstock, utilities, energy) before simplifying.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: Two subzones $Z_1$ and $Z_2$ in a petrochemical complex are coupled by a single intermediate stream of flow rate $F$ (ton/h). Each subzone's profit is a quadratic function of the cut-stream flow it handles:**

$$\Phi_1(F) = 500F - 2F^2 \quad \text{(Subzone 1, supplier)}$$

$$\Phi_2(F) = 800F - 3F^2 \quad \text{(Subzone 2, receiver)}$$

**There is no upper limit on $F$, but the operating cost to $Z_1$ of producing $F$ rises steeply, so an internal constraint exists: $F \leq 100$ ton/h for $Z_1$.**

**(a) Without coordination, what flow rate $F$ would each subzone choose in isolation? (4 Marks)**

**(b) Apply Lagrangian price-based coordination. Set up the dual problem, derive the coordination update equation, and find the optimal $F$ and transfer price $\lambda$ after 3 iterations using a step size of $\alpha = 0.05$. (10 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Uncoordinated Solutions (4 Marks)**

**Subzone 1 (Supplier) Uncoordinated (2 Marks):**
Maximise $\Phi_1(F)$ by taking the derivative and setting it to zero:

$$\frac{d\Phi_1}{dF} = 500 - 4F = 0$$

$$F_1^* = 125 \text{ ton/h}$$

But this violates the constraint $F \leq 100$. So the constrained optimum is at the boundary:
$$F_1^{constrained} = 100 \text{ ton/h}$$

**Subzone 2 (Receiver) Uncoordinated (2 Marks):**
Maximise $\Phi_2(F)$:

$$\frac{d\Phi_2}{dF} = 800 - 6F = 0$$

$$F_2^* = 133.33 \text{ ton/h}$$

No internal constraint, so $F_2^* = 133.33$ ton/h.

**Observation (Not required but good for marks):** $F_1^{constrained} < F_2^*$ — the two subzones are **mismatched**. This is exactly the situation where coordination is needed.

---

**Part (b) — Lagrangian Coordination (10 Marks)**

**Step 1: Set up the dual problem (2 Marks)**
Introduce a coordination variable $F$ (the cut stream) and a price $\lambda$ such that:

- $Z_1$ sees: $\Phi_1(F) - \lambda F$ (price reduces $Z_1$'s effective profit as $F$ increases — the "marginal cost" of supplying)
- $Z_2$ sees: $\Phi_2(F) + \lambda F$ (price adds to $Z_2$'s effective profit as $F$ increases — the "marginal value" of receiving)

The dual objective:
$$L(\lambda) = \max_F [\Phi_1(F) - \lambda F] + \max_F [\Phi_2(F) + \lambda F]$$

**Step 2: Solve each subzone as a function of $\lambda$ (3 Marks)**

For $Z_1$ (subject to $F \leq 100$):
$$\frac{d}{dF}[\Phi_1 - \lambda F] = 500 - 4F - \lambda = 0$$

$$F_1(\lambda) = \min\left[\frac{500 - \lambda}{4}, \; 100\right]$$

For $Z_2$:
$$\frac{d}{dF}[\Phi_2 + \lambda F] = 800 - 6F + \lambda = 0$$

$$F_2(\lambda) = \frac{800 + \lambda}{6}$$

**Step 3: Derive the coordination update equation (2 Marks)**
At optimum, $F_1(\lambda) = F_2(\lambda)$. Use gradient ascent on the dual:

$$\lambda^{(i+1)} = \lambda^{(i)} + \alpha \cdot [F_2(\lambda^{(i)}) - F_1(\lambda^{(i)})]$$

**Step 4: Iterate starting from $\lambda^{(0)} = 0$ with $\alpha = 0.05$ (3 Marks)**

**Iteration 1:**
$$F_1(0) = \min[125, 100] = 100$$
$$F_2(0) = 800/6 = 133.33$$
$$\lambda^{(1)} = 0 + 0.05 \times (133.33 - 100) = 0 + 0.05 \times 33.33 = 1.667$$

**Iteration 2:**
$$F_1(1.667) = \min[(500-1.667)/4, 100] = \min[124.58, 100] = 100$$
$$F_2(1.667) = (800+1.667)/6 = 133.61$$
$$\lambda^{(2)} = 1.667 + 0.05 \times (133.61 - 100) = 1.667 + 1.681 = 3.348$$

**Iteration 3:**
$$F_1(3.348) = \min[(500-3.348)/4, 100] = \min[124.16, 100] = 100$$
$$F_2(3.348) = (800+3.348)/6 = 133.89$$
$$\lambda^{(3)} = 3.348 + 0.05 \times (133.89 - 100) = 3.348 + 1.695 = 5.043$$

**Step 5: Conclude (implicitly clear from the trend)**
$\lambda$ is rising — the **transfer price is increasing** because the supplier ($Z_1$) is constrained at 100 ton/h. The coordinator is signalling to $Z_2$ that the marginal value of receiving more than 100 is high. The flow converges to $F^* = 100$ ton/h with $\lambda$ rising. In a full implementation, $\lambda$ would continue to grow until the receiver $Z_2$ voluntarily reduces its demand (via its own constraints, not shown here).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Part b):** Students frequently (1) forget the **sign convention** in the Lagrangian update ($\lambda^{(i+1)} = \lambda^{(i)} + \alpha[F_2 - F_1]$ where the receiver is $Z_2$), and (2) fail to apply the **constraint** $F \leq 100$ when computing $F_1(\lambda)$ in iterations 2 and 3. These two errors lose up to 3 marks cumulatively.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep):**

- [x] **Subzone = a cluster of strongly coupled process units** that share material/energy and are weakly coupled to the rest of the plant.
- [x] **Subzone Optimisation sits at Layer 2 (Supervisory/Advanced Control)** of the Purdue / ISA-95 hierarchy — *above* regulatory PID, *below* plant-wide RTO.
- [x] **Three criteria for subzone definition:** process topological coupling, material balance closure, and RGA-based interaction strength.
- [x] **Mathematical form:** Constrained nonlinear program (NLP) maximising $\Phi_k(u_k)$ subject to $f_k = 0$, $g_k \leq 0$, $h_k = 0$, with $u_k^{\min} \leq u_k \leq u_k^{\max}$.
- [x] **Objective function structure:** Revenue (products × price) − Raw Material Cost (feedstock × cost) − Utility Cost (steam, electricity, cooling water) − Fixed Cost.
- [x] **Cut streams** are variables that flow between subzones; they become **coordination variables** (Lagrange multipliers / transfer prices) in the upper level.
- [x] **Lagrangian coordination update:** $\lambda^{(i+1)} = \lambda^{(i)} + \alpha [F_{receiver} - F_{supplier}]$.
- [x] **MPC cost function:** $J = \sum \Vert y - y_{ref} \Vert_Q^2 + \sum \Vert \Delta u \Vert_R^2$ — quadratic in tracking error and control moves.
- [x] **RGA formula:** $\Lambda_{ij} = K_{ij} / K_{ij}^{eff}$ — used for loop pairing and subzone definition.
- [x] **Industrial impact:** A well-tuned subzone MPC on a refinery CDU delivers **$1–5 million USD/year** per unit.
- [x] **Always check constraints before declaring the optimum** — the unconstrained optimum is rarely the true answer in KTU questions.
- [x] **In Lagrangian coordination, $\lambda$ = transfer price = marginal value of the cut stream** at equilibrium.
- [x] **Coordinator step size $\alpha$** is critical: too large → oscillation, too small → slow convergence. Industrial implementations use variable step sizes (e.g., $\alpha^{(i+1)} = \alpha^{(i)} / i$).
- [x] **Purdue hierarchy layers (memorise!):** L0 = Field, L1 = Regulatory, L2 = Advanced/Subzone, L3 = Plant-Wide Optimisation, L4 = Corporate Planning.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-Line Definition for Quick Recall:** *Subzone optimisation is the intermediate-level control strategy that partitions a large plant into coupled unit-clusters and solves an economic NLP for each cluster, coordinated at the plant level via transfer prices on cut streams.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pressure Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT864 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN LARGE SCALE INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pressure Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pressure Control** is a fundamental regulatory process in large-scale process industries (refineries, petrochemical plants, power stations, and chemical manufacturing units) wherein the pressure of a process fluid (gas, vapor, or liquid) within a vessel, pipeline, or column is maintained at a desired setpoint by automatically manipulating a final control element—typically a control valve—based on feedback from a pressure-sensing transmitter.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, pressure control is classified as a **single-loop regulatory control strategy** belonging to the family of **Process Variable (PV) – Manipulated Variable (MV)** pairings, formally expressed through **Process Gain ($K_p$)**, **Time Constant ($\tau_p$)**, and **Dead Time ($\theta_p$)** transfer function parameters.

The general block representation is:

$$G_p(s) = \frac{K_p \cdot e^{-\theta_p s}}{\tau_p s + 1}$$

where $G_p(s)$ is the open-loop process transfer function relating pressure deviation to manipulated flow.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Kitchen Tap and Pressure Cooker:**
> Imagine a pressure cooker on a gas stove. The pressure inside builds up as steam is generated. You (the controller) listen to the pressure cooker whistle (the sensor). If the whistle is too shrill (high pressure), you reduce the gas supply (manipulated variable). If the pressure drops too low (whistle stops), you increase the gas. This continuous human feedback loop is exactly what a **PID-based pressure controller** does automatically in a plant.

In a refinery's **distillation column overhead receiver**, the system must maintain a near-constant pressure (typically **1.5 – 4.5 bar gauge**) so that downstream condensation and separation occur at predictable boiling points.

### 1.3 Engineering Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Industrial Range |
|---|---|
| Standard Process Pressure | **0.1 – 100 bar** |
| Differential Pressure (DP) across orifice | **0 – 100 kPa** |
| ISA Standard Pressure Signal (Transmitter) | **3 – 15 psig** or **4 – 20 mA DC** |
| HART Protocol Layer | **1200 baud FSK** |
| Typical Loop Power Supply | **24 V DC** |

> [!NOTE]
> All modern KTU-relevant pressure transmitters operate on the **two-wire 4–20 mA standard** defined by **ISA-50.1**, allowing long cable runs (up to 1 km) with strong noise immunity.

### 1.4 Why Pressure Control is Critical in Large-Scale Industries

- **Safety**: Over-pressurization can cause vessel rupture, fires, or explosions.
- **Product Quality**: Many reactions (e.g., catalytic cracking) are pressure-sensitive.
- **Throughput**: Pumping and compression costs scale steeply with pressure drops.
- **Equipment Protection**: Compressors, turbines, and heat exchangers have strict pressure envelopes.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Breakdown of Pressure Control Concepts

#### 2.1.1 Classification by Process Dynamics

- **Self-Regulating Pressure Process:** A process where pressure naturally reaches a steady state when the manipulated flow is held constant (e.g., gas discharge to atmosphere through a fixed restriction). Such processes follow a **First-Order Plus Dead Time (FOPDT)** model.
- **Integrating (Non-Self-Regulating) Pressure Process:** A process where pressure continues to rise (or fall) indefinitely if the manipulated and disturbance flows are not balanced (e.g., a closed vessel with no outlet, or a compressor discharge into a constant-volume tank). This is modeled as a pure integrator with gain.

#### 2.1.2 Classification by Loop Configuration

1. **Direct Pressure Control (Pressure-to-Flow)**
   - Pressure transmitter (PT) → Controller (PIC) → Control Valve (PV) on the discharge line.
   - The valve manipulates the *outflow* of the vessel.

2. **Indirect Pressure Control (Pressure-to-Heat / Surge Control)**
   - For centrifugal compressors, pressure is controlled by manipulating **compressor speed** via a Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) or **anti-surge valve** recycle line.
   - This is critical to avoid the **surge line** region.

3. **Cascade Pressure Control**
   - Master loop: Pressure controller sets the setpoint of a slave (flow) controller.
   - Improves disturbance rejection.

4. **Split-Range Pressure Control**
   - Used when two or more valves are required to operate in complementary pressure ranges (e.g., one valve opens on high pressure, another closes).
   - Common in compressor anti-surge and reactor pressure control.

#### 2.1.3 Pressure Control Loops in a Typical Refinery

- **Distillation Column Overhead Pressure** → Condenser duty / reflux drum vent valve.
- **Steam Header Pressure** → Boiler firing rate / letdown valve.
- **Reactor Pressure** → Block valve on product takeoff.
- **Compressor Discharge Pressure** → Anti-surge recycle + speed control.

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' — Core Physics Behind Pressure Control

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The fundamental energy balance governing pressure dynamics in a fixed-volume vessel is derived from the **Ideal Gas Law** $PV = nRT$ and the **Conservation of Mass**:

$$\frac{dP}{dt} = \frac{R \cdot T}{V} \cdot \left( F_{in} - F_{out} \right)$$

- Why is this important? Because the **rate of change of pressure** is directly proportional to the **net molar flow imbalance**.
- Therefore, the controller's job is to drive $F_{in} - F_{out} \to 0$ in steady state.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p \, e^{-\theta_p s}}{\tau_p s + 1}$ | FOPDT process model (self-regulating) | — |
| 2 | $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p \, e^{-\theta_p s}}{s}$ | Pure integrator model (tank) | — |
| 3 | $PV = nRT$ | Ideal gas law (constant volume) | P in Pa, V in m³ |
| 4 | $\dfrac{dP}{dt} = \dfrac{RT}{V}(F_{in} - F_{out})$ | Vessel pressure dynamic equation | Pa/s |
| 5 | $C_{max} = K_p \cdot K_c \cdot \dfrac{\tau_p}{\tau_p + \theta_p}$ | Maximum allowable controller gain (stability bound) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $K_c = \dfrac{0.5}{K_p} \cdot \dfrac{\tau_p}{\theta_p}$ | Ziegler–Nichols PI tuning (reaction curve) | — |
| 7 | $\tau_I = 3 \cdot \theta_p$ | Z–N integral time (PI) | seconds |
| 8 | $q = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot \Delta P}{\rho \cdot K_v^{-2}}}$ | Valve flow through $K_v$ coefficient | m³/h |
| 9 | $P_{setpoint} = P_{nominal} \pm \Delta P_{deadband}$ | Controller setpoint with deadband | bar |
| 10 | $F = C_v \sqrt{\Delta P / G_f}$ | Standard valve sizing equation (ISA) | varies |

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute values to prevent Markdown table breakage (e.g., $\vert K_p \vert$ instead of $\vert K_p \vert$).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil & Gas:** Maintains pipeline pressure for **Multi-Phase Flow Meters (MPFM)**.
- **Power Plants:** Boiler drum pressure triple-redundant control using **3-out-of-2 voting**.
- **Pharmaceutical:** Clean-in-Place (CIP) loop pressure integrity testing.
- **HVAC:** Building static pressure control for cleanrooms (ISO Class 5/7).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Dynamic Pressure Equation for a Gas-Filled Vessel

**Assumptions:**
- Ideal gas behaviour.
- Constant volume $V$ and constant temperature $T$ (isothermal simplification).
- Uniform composition.

**Step 1 — Start from the ideal gas law.**

$$P \cdot V = n \cdot R \cdot T$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate both sides with respect to time $t$.**

$$V \cdot \frac{dP}{dt} = R \cdot T \cdot \frac{dn}{dt}$$

The vessel volume is constant, so $V$ remains outside the derivative.

**Step 3 — Express the molar accumulation as net inflow.**

$$\frac{dn}{dt} = F_{in} - F_{out}$$

where $F_{in}$ and $F_{out}$ are molar flow rates (kmol/s) entering and leaving.

**Step 4 — Substitute back.**

$$V \cdot \frac{dP}{dt} = R \cdot T \cdot (F_{in} - F_{out})$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the rate of pressure change.**

$$\boxed{\frac{dP}{dt} = \frac{R \cdot T}{V} \cdot \left( F_{in} - F_{out} \right)}$$

This is the **linearized governing differential equation** for gas-pressure dynamics in a fixed-volume vessel.

**Interpretation for Control:**
- If $F_{in} > F_{out}$, pressure **rises** at a rate proportional to $\dfrac{RT}{V}$.
- If $F_{in} < F_{out}$, pressure **falls**.
- The constant $\dfrac{RT}{V}$ acts as the **process gain** $K_p$ for the integrator model.

### 3.2 Derivation: Linearized FOPDT Model for Pressure-to-Valve Dynamics

A common scenario: a gas vessel with outlet flow through a control valve. The valve has a linear installed characteristic:

$$F_{out} = K_v \cdot u(t)$$

where $u(t)$ is the controller output (%) and $K_v$ is the valve flow coefficient (kmol/s per % opening). The inlet flow is treated as a disturbance $F_{in} = F_{in,0} + d(t)$.

**Step 1 — Apply small-signal linearization around steady state.**

$$P(t) = P_0 + p'(t), \quad F_{in} = F_{in,0} + d(t), \quad F_{out} = F_{v,0} + K_v \cdot u'(t)$$

**Step 2 — Substituting into the governing equation (assuming $T$ and $V$ constant).**

$$\frac{dP'}{dt} = \frac{RT}{V} \left( d(t) - K_v \cdot u'(t) \right)$$

**Step 3 — Take the Laplace transform with zero initial conditions.**

$$s \cdot P'(s) = \frac{RT}{V} \left( D(s) - K_v \cdot U'(s) \right)$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange for the process transfer function $G_p(s)$.**

$$G_p(s) = \frac{P'(s)}{U'(s)} = -\frac{RT \cdot K_v}{V \cdot s}$$

The negative sign confirms the *reverse-acting* nature: opening the valve (increasing $u$) decreases pressure. For control design, we take the magnitude $\vert G_p(s) \vert$ and invert the controller action (use a *decrease* direct/reverse setting as required).

**Step 5 — Add dead time and process lag for realism.**

Many processes exhibit measurement delay and thermal lag. The full **FOPDT** model becomes:

$$G_p(s) = \frac{K_p \, e^{-\theta_p s}}{\tau_p s + 1}$$

where $K_p = \dfrac{RT \cdot K_v}{V}$, $\tau_p$ = thermal/process lag, $\theta_p$ = measurement + transport dead time.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Pressure Control Strategy

| Process Type | Vessel Geometry | Strategy | Controller Action | Typical Final Element |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Liquid storage tank | Horizontal/vertical | Direct pressure | Reverse (PID) | Globe valve on outlet |
| Gas receiver | Spherical/cylindrical | Direct pressure | Reverse (PI) | Ball valve on vent line |
| Compressor discharge | Cylindrical | Anti-surge + speed | Cascade | Recycle valve + VFD |
| Steam boiler drum | Cylindrical | Three-element (drum level dominant) | PI | Feedwater control valve |
| Distillation column | Tower overhead | Condenser duty | Reverse (PI) | Condenser water valve |
| Reactor | Jacketed vessel | Pressure-Temp cascade | Cascade | Coolant valve + vent |
| Vacuum chamber | Sealed vessel | Inlet bleed | Direct (PI) | Air-in bleeder valve |

### 3.4 Derivation: Pressure Surge in Compressor Systems (Surge Limit)

The **Greitzer Surge Margin** for a centrifugal compressor is:

$$SM = \frac{\Delta P_{operating} - \Delta P_{surge}}{\Delta P_{surge}}$$

A positive surge margin (typically **SM ≥ 10%**) is required for stable operation. Anti-surge controllers open a recycle valve to maintain $P_{discharge} - P_{suction}$ above the surge line.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
