<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define self-regulating and non-self-regulating pressure processes with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Self-Regulating Process:** A pressure process that naturally settles to a new steady state after a disturbance, due to an internal negative feedback mechanism. Example: Gas discharge from a vessel through a fixed restriction to atmosphere (3 marks for definition + example).
- **Non-Self-Regulating (Integrating) Process:** A pressure process that continues to drift (rise/fall) indefinitely until the controller intervenes. Example: Closed gas cylinder being filled by a compressor with no outlet (3 marks).

#### **Question A2:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any three industrial applications of pressure control.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. Distillation column overhead pressure control (1 mark)
2. Boiler drum pressure regulation in thermal power plants (1 mark)
3. Compressor anti-surge pressure control (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a single-loop pressure control system used in a process industry. Identify each block and the signal flow.

**Model Answer:**

A single-loop pressure control system consists of the following elements (1 mark each, total 7):

1. **Setpoint (SP):** The desired pressure value entered by the operator in the DCS/HMI.
2. **Process Variable (PV):** Actual pressure measured by a **PT (Pressure Transmitter)** sensing element such as a strain-gauge or piezoresistive diaphragm.
3. **Comparator:** Computes the **error** $e(t) = SP - PV$ continuously.
4. **Controller (PIC):** A **PID controller** that applies the proportional, integral, and derivative action:
$$u(t) = K_c \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{\tau_I}\int e(t)dt + \tau_D \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$$
5. **Final Control Element (PCV — Pressure Control Valve):** Pneumatically actuated globe/ball valve that throttles the flow.
6. **I/P Converter:** Converts 4–20 mA DC signal to 3–15 psig pneumatic pressure for valve actuation.
7. **Process (Vessel):** The physical system whose pressure is being controlled; the loop closes back to the transmitter.

**Signal flow:** SP → Comparator → PIC → I/P → PCV → Vessel → PT → Comparator.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** A gas cylinder of volume 2 m³ is filled at a constant temperature of 300 K. The inlet molar flow is 0.5 kmol/h. Find the time required for the pressure to rise from 1 bar to 5 bar. Take R = 8.314 kJ/kmol·K.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Convert units and write governing equation.**

$$V = 2 \, m^3, \quad T = 300 \, K, \quad F_{in} = 0.5 \, \text{kmol/h}$$
$$R = 8.314 \, \frac{kJ}{kmol \cdot K} = 8.314 \times 10^3 \, \frac{Pa \cdot m^3}{kmol \cdot K}$$

**Step 2 — Express pressure rise rate.**

$$\frac{dP}{dt} = \frac{RT}{V} \cdot F_{in} = \frac{8314 \times 300}{2} \times \frac{0.5}{3600}$$

Compute numerator: $8314 \times 300 = 2,494,200 \, \frac{Pa \cdot m^3}{kmol}$

$$\frac{2,494,200}{2} = 1,247,100 \, \frac{Pa \cdot m^3}{kmol \cdot m^3} = 1,247,100 \, \frac{Pa}{kmol}$$

Then $\dfrac{0.5}{3600} = 1.389 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{kmol/s}$

$$\frac{dP}{dt} = 1,247,100 \times 1.389 \times 10^{-4} \approx 173.2 \, \frac{Pa}{s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute required $\Delta P$.**

$$\Delta P = 5 - 1 = 4 \, \text{bar} = 4 \times 10^5 \, \text{Pa}$$

**Step 4 — Time required.**

$$t = \frac{\Delta P}{dP/dt} = \frac{4 \times 10^5}{173.2} \approx 2309.5 \, \text{s} \approx 38.5 \, \text{minutes}$$

**Answer:** Approximately **38.5 minutes (7 marks — 1 mark for setup, 2 marks for derivative, 2 marks for substitution, 1 mark for unit conversion, 1 mark for final answer).**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Explain the cascade pressure control strategy used for a centrifugal compressor. How does it improve over single-loop control?

**Model Answer:**

In a centrifugal compressor, the **discharge pressure** must be controlled while avoiding the **surge region**. A cascade loop consists of (1 mark each):

1. **Master (Primary) Loop:** Pressure transmitter (PT) → Pressure controller (PIC) outputs the *flow setpoint* to the slave.
2. **Slave (Secondary) Loop:** Flow controller (FIC) on the recycle line → positions the anti-surge control valve.
3. **Variable Frequency Drive (VFD):** Compressor speed is adjusted based on demand.

**Advantages over single-loop (3 marks):**
- **Faster disturbance rejection:** The flow loop reacts within 1–2 seconds, while pressure changes take 10–30 seconds to propagate.
- **Anti-surge protection:** Decouples the slow pressure dynamics from the fast surge dynamics.
- **Better linearity:** The inner flow loop linearizes the valve characteristic.
- **Improved stability:** Higher closed-loop bandwidth without oscillation.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** For a pressure process with $K_p = 2.5$ %/%, $\tau_p = 120$ s, and $\theta_p = 15$ s, determine PI controller settings using the Ziegler-Nichols reaction curve method.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Recall Z–N reaction curve formulas for PI controller:**

$$K_c = \frac{0.5}{K_p} \cdot \frac{\tau_p}{\theta_p}$$

$$\tau_I = 3 \cdot \theta_p$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$K_c = \frac{0.5}{2.5} \times \frac{120}{15} = 0.2 \times 8 = 1.6 \, \text{%/%}$$

$$\tau_I = 3 \times 15 = 45 \, \text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Express the controller transfer function.**

$$G_c(s) = K_c \left(1 + \frac{1}{\tau_I s}\right) = 1.6 \left(1 + \frac{1}{45s}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Compute derivative time (zero for PI).**

$$\tau_D = 0$$

**Answer:** $K_c = 1.6$ %/%, $\tau_I = 45$ s, $\tau_D = 0$ (7 marks — 2 marks for formula, 2 marks for substitution, 2 marks for final values, 1 mark for transfer function).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Leading to Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Confusing self-regulating with integrating processes** — Loss of 1–2 marks in Part A.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units** (e.g., bar to Pa, kmol/h to kmol/s) — Loss of 1 mark in Part B calculations.
> 3. **Missing the negative sign** in the transfer function derivation — Examiners deduct 1 mark if sign convention is not explained.
> 4. **Omitting block diagram labels** in descriptive answers — Loss of up to 2 marks.
> 5. **Not specifying controller action (direct/reverse)** — Always state: "Since increasing valve opening *decreases* pressure, use **reverse-acting** controller."

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Pressure Control Definition:** Automatic regulation of fluid pressure in a process using a control loop (PT → PIC → PCV).
- **Process Types:** Self-regulating (FOPDT) and non-self-regulating (integrator).
- **Governing Equation:** $\dfrac{dP}{dt} = \dfrac{RT}{V}(F_{in} - F_{out})$ for gas vessels.
- **FOPDT Model:** $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p e^{-\theta_p s}}{\tau_p s + 1}$.
- **Controller Action:** **Reverse-acting** for valve-on-outlet; **Direct-acting** for valve-on-inlet.
- **Z–N PI Tuning:** $K_c = \dfrac{0.5}{K_p} \cdot \dfrac{\tau_p}{\theta_p}$, $\tau_I = 3\theta_p$.
- **Cascade Control:** Master (pressure) + Slave (flow) — used in compressors for anti-surge.
- **Split-Range:** Two valves act in complementary 4–12 mA and 12–20 mA ranges.
- **Safety Devices:** **PSV (Pressure Safety Valve)** is mandatory in all pressure vessels as per ASME Section VIII.
- **Standard Signals:** 4–20 mA DC, 3–15 psig pneumatic, HART protocol overlay.
- **Anti-Surge:** Maintain $\Delta P$ across compressor above surge line; surge margin $\geq$ 10%.
- **High-Pressure Applications:** Refinery distillation, power plant boilers, gas processing, LNG storage.
- **Common Sensors:** Strain gauge, piezoresistive, capacitive, piezoelectric, resonant (high precision).
- **Valve Sizing:** $F = C_v \sqrt{\Delta P / G_f}$ — must consider choked flow for gases when $P_{downstream} < 0.53 P_{upstream}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Tip:** Memorize the governing equation, the FOPDT transfer function, and the Z–N tuning formulas. These three items alone account for approximately **60% of marks** in any pressure control exam question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Elimination of Draft
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT864 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN LARGE SCALE INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Elimination of Draft

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Draft" in Large Scale Industries?

In the context of large-scale industrial processes (especially **thermal power plants**, **boilers**, **furnaces**, and **process heaters**), **Draft** is defined as the **small negative gauge pressure (sub-atmospheric pressure)** that is intentionally maintained inside a combustion chamber, furnace, or boiler to ensure the proper, controlled flow of air for combustion and flue gases through the system toward the stack (chimney).

Mathematically, it is the pressure difference between two points in the gas flow path:

$$
\Delta P = P_{\text{ambient}} - P_{\text{inside furnace}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Draft is always expressed in **millimeters of water column (mm WC)** or **inches of water column (in. WC)** — NOT in Pascals — because the magnitudes are extremely small (typically **–5 mm WC to –50 mm WC** for balanced draft furnaces).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine you are sipping a milkshake through a straw. The reason the milkshake rises is because **your mouth creates a lower pressure (suction)** than the atmospheric pressure pushing down on the milkshake. The pressure difference = "draft" of your suction.

In a boiler:
- The **atmospheric pressure pushes air in** through the FD (Forced Draft) fan.
- The **ID (Induced Draft) fan pulls flue gases out** through the stack.
- The tiny pressure difference (draft) ensures the **gases flow smoothly in the desired direction** without leaking out of the boiler setting or pulling in unwanted cold air.

## 1.3 Why Do We Need to "Eliminate" Draft Effects?

The word **"Elimination"** here does **NOT** mean removing the draft itself (draft is essential!). It refers to **eliminating the MEASUREMENT ERRORS and UNWANTED SIDE-EFFECTS** that arise from the draft, namely:

1. **Zero Drift** in draft gauges due to ambient temperature/pressure variations.
2. **Manometric fluid density errors** caused by temperature changes.
3. **Condensation of moisture** in the connecting impulse lines.
4. **Asymmetric flow disturbances** causing measurement inaccuracy.
5. **Static head errors** when measuring draft at elevated locations.

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU Board Exam Purposes:** When the question says "Elimination of Draft", interpret it as **"Methods/Techniques to correctly measure and control draft by eliminating measurement errors and using suitable instrumentation."**

## 1.4 Types of Draft in Industries

| Type of Draft | Mechanism | Typical Pressure Range | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Natural Draft** | Buoyancy of hot flue gases in chimney | –10 to –40 mm WC | Small boilers, domestic heaters |
| **Forced Draft (FD)** | FD Fan pushes air into furnace | +50 to +200 mm WC | Modern utility boilers |
| **Induced Draft (ID)** | ID Fan pulls flue gases out | –100 to –300 mm WC | Required to overcome gas path resistance |
| **Balanced Draft** | Combination of FD + ID | ≈ **0 mm WC** at furnace | **Most common in large plants** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Draft Generation

The draft in any furnace/boiler is governed by the **Stack Effect (Chimney Effect)**, derived from the density difference between hot flue gases and ambient air:

$$
\Delta P_{\text{draft}} = H \cdot g \cdot (\rho_{\text{air}} - \rho_{\text{gas}})
$$

Where:
- $H$ = Effective height of the chimney (m)
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- $\rho_{\text{air}}$ = Density of ambient air (kg/m³)
- $\rho_{\text{gas}}$ = Density of hot flue gas (kg/m³)

The gas density at temperature $T$ (in Kelvin) is given by the **Ideal Gas Law**:

$$
\rho_{\text{gas}} = \frac{P \cdot M}{R \cdot T}
$$

Where:
- $P$ = Absolute pressure (Pa)
- $M$ = Molecular mass of flue gas (kg/kmol)
- $R$ = Universal gas constant = **8314 J/(kmol·K)**

## 2.2 Why Errors Arise in Draft Measurement — The Core Problem

When a draft gauge (typically a **differential pressure manometer** or **DPT — Differential Pressure Transmitter**) is connected between the furnace interior and the atmosphere, several errors creep in:

### 2.2.1 The Manometer Fluid Density Error
The manometer reading $h$ in mm of fluid at density $\rho_f$ is conventionally converted to "mm of water column" using:

$$
h_{\text{wc}} = h \cdot \left(\frac{\rho_f}{\rho_{\text{water}}}\right)
$$

But if the **manometer fluid (mercury, oil, or alcohol) changes temperature**, its density changes, giving an erroneous reading.

### 2.2.2 The Condensation Error (Most Common in Industry)
The connecting tubes (called **"impulse lines"**) carry hot, moist flue gases. When these gases cool inside the impulse line, **water vapor condenses** and accumulates, creating a **false head of water** in the line:

$$
P_{\text{false}} = \rho_{\text{water}} \cdot g \cdot h_{\text{condensate}}
$$

This adds/subtracts a phantom pressure to the actual draft.

### 2.2.3 The Ambient Pressure Correction
Draft gauges measure **gauge pressure**, but the reference (atmosphere) changes with weather. A standard draft of "–20 mm WC" at sea level becomes "–18.5 mm WC" at higher altitude.

### 2.2.4 The Static Head Error
If the draft tapping is located at an elevation different from the gauge, the **hydrostatic head of the air column in the impulse line** itself causes an error:

$$
\Delta P_{\text{head}} = (\rho_{\text{air}} - \rho_{\text{gas}}) \cdot g \cdot \Delta z
$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Draft Definition** | $\Delta P = P_{\text{atm}} - P_{\text{furnace}}$ | mm WC | Basic draft |
| 2 | **Stack Effect (Natural Draft)** | $\Delta P = H \cdot g \cdot (\rho_a - \rho_g)$ | Pa | Chimney design |
| 3 | **Ideal Gas Density** | $\rho = \frac{PM}{RT}$ | kg/m³ | Density at temp. |
| 4 | **Manometer Conversion** | $h_{\text{wc}} = h \cdot \frac{\rho_f}{\rho_w}$ | mm WC | Gauge calibration |
| 5 | **Venturi for FD Fan** | $Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho(1-\beta^4)}}$ | m³/s | Air flow measurement |
| 6 | **Pitot Static Equation** | $v = \sqrt{\frac{2(P_t - P_s)}{\rho}}$ | m/s | Flue gas velocity |
| 7 | **Balanced Draft Condition** | $P_{\text{furnace}} \approx 0$ (gauge) | mm WC | Set-point |
| 8 | **Density Correction Factor** | $K = \frac{T_{\text{ref}}}{T_{\text{actual}}} \cdot \frac{P_{\text{actual}}}{P_{\text{ref}}}$ | – | Compensation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Vertical pipes / absolute values:** The notation for absolute pressure is written as $\vert P \vert$ in answers. KTU examiners **will** deduct marks if you confuse gauge and absolute pressures in draft problems.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Thermal Power Plants** (NTPC, Tata Power, KSEB stations in Kerala): Draft control determines combustion efficiency, heat transfer, and crucially, **prevents flame blow-outs** and **furnace implosions**.
- **Cement Plants** (e.g., Malabar Cements, Kerala Minerals): Rotary kiln draft control optimizes clinker quality.
- **Steel Plants** (SAIL, JSPL): Blast furnace top-pressure control is essentially draft control.
- **Petroleum Refineries**: Process heaters and crackers use balanced draft for safety.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: The Natural Draft (Chimney) Equation

**Starting Point:** Consider a vertical chimney of height $H$ containing hot flue gas of density $\rho_g$ at temperature $T_g$, surrounded by ambient air of density $\rho_a$ at temperature $T_a$.

**Step 1:** The pressure at the **bottom of the chimney (inside)** is:
$$
P_{\text{inside, bottom}} = P_{\text{top}} + \rho_g \cdot g \cdot H
$$

**Step 2:** The pressure at the **bottom of the chimney (outside, in atmosphere)** is:
$$
P_{\text{outside, bottom}} = P_{\text{top}} + \rho_a \cdot g \cdot H
$$

**Step 3:** The pressure difference (draft available) is:
$$
\Delta P = P_{\text{outside, bottom}} - P_{\text{inside, bottom}}
$$

**Step 4:** Substituting:
$$
\Delta P = (\rho_a - \rho_g) \cdot g \cdot H
$$

**Step 5:** Applying the ideal gas law for both fluids:
$$
\rho_a = \frac{P_{\text{atm}} \cdot M_a}{R \cdot T_a} \quad ; \quad \rho_g = \frac{P_{\text{atm}} \cdot M_g}{R \cdot T_g}
$$

(Here we assume both are at approximately atmospheric pressure at the chimney base.)

**Step 6:** Substituting back:
$$
\Delta P = \frac{P_{\text{atm}} \cdot g \cdot H}{R} \cdot \left( \frac{M_a}{T_a} - \frac{M_g}{T_g} \right)
$$

This is the **complete natural draft equation** in SI units.

**Step 7:** Converting to mm of water column (the industrial unit), using $1 \text{ mm WC} = 9.806 \text{ Pa}$:

$$
\Delta P_{\text{mmWC}} = \frac{\Delta P_{\text{Pa}}}{9.806}
$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Compensation of Density Error in a Manometer

**Problem Context:** A U-tube manometer uses **mercury** ($\rho_{Hg} = 13{,}600$ kg/m³) and is calibrated at **20 °C**. It reads a draft of **$h = 15$ mm Hg** at an actual operating fluid temperature of **55 °C**. The mercury density at 55 °C is **13{,}500 kg/m³**. Find the **corrected draft in mm WC**.

**Step 1 — Write the actual pressure indicated:**
$$
P_{\text{ind}} = h \cdot \rho_{Hg,\,55°C} \cdot g
$$

$$
P_{\text{ind}} = 0.015 \times 13{,}500 \times 9.81
$$

$$
P_{\text{ind}} = 1986.4 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to mm WC using water density at 4 °C (1000 kg/m³):**
$$
h_{\text{wc, actual}} = \frac{P_{\text{ind}}}{\rho_{\text{water}} \cdot g} = \frac{1986.4}{1000 \times 9.81}
$$

$$
h_{\text{wc, actual}} = 0.2025 \text{ m WC} = 202.5 \text{ mm WC}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the error (what the gauge "should" read at 20 °C):**
$$
h_{\text{wc, calibrated}} = h \times \frac{\rho_{Hg,\,20°C}}{\rho_{\text{water}}} = 15 \times \frac{13{,}600}{1000}
$$

$$
h_{\text{wc, calibrated}} = 204 \text{ mm WC}
$$

**Step 4 — Determine the error to be eliminated:**
$$
E = h_{\text{wc, calibrated}} - h_{\text{wc, actual}} = 204 - 202.5 = 1.5 \text{ mm WC}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Answer Writing Tip:** Always explicitly state the **density value used, the temperature of the manometer, and the reference water density** when answering such questions. Examiners award method marks for these.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sources of Error and Their Elimination Techniques

| # | Source of Error | Magnitude (Typical) | **Elimination / Compensation Technique** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Manometer fluid density change with temperature | 1–5 mm WC | Use **temperature-compensated manometer fluids**; use **DPT with electronic temperature compensation** |
| 2 | **Condensation in impulse lines** | 5–20 mm WC (worst case) | Install **drip pots / condensate pots**; use **purging with dry air**; **heat tracing** of impulse lines |
| 3 | Ambient pressure variation (altitude/weather) | 2–10 mm WC | Use **absolute pressure transmitters** with **barometric pressure compensation** |
| 4 | Wind effects on stack (cross-winds) | Up to 15 mm WC | Install **wind shields**; use **averaging draft probes** |
| 5 | Static head error (elevation difference) | ~0.12 mm WC per metre | Apply **height correction** mathematically; mount gauge **at the same level as tapping** |
| 6 | Zero drift in electronic transmitter | 0.1–0.5% of span | Periodic **re-zeroing**; use **smart transmitters with auto-zero** |
| 7 | Asymmetric plugging of impulse lines | Erratic | **Regular blow-down** of impulse lines; use **dual-leg purging** |
| 8 | Pulsation from fans | High frequency noise | Install **damping / snubber** in lines; use **low-pass filter** in transmitter |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Draft Measurement and Elimination System

The following **Mermaid Block Diagram** depicts the complete instrumentation chain for measuring and eliminating errors in draft measurement in a typical balanced-draft boiler:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Furnace / Boiler Combustion Chamber] -->|Flue Gas Tap| B[Impulse Line - Hot Side]
    A1[Atmosphere Reference] -->|Reference Tap| C[Impulse Line - Cold Side]
    B --> D[Condensate Pot / Drip Pot]
    C --> E[Condensate Pot - Reference]
    D --> F[Differential Pressure Transmitter DPT]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Signal Conditioner with Temperature Compensation]
    G --> H[PID Controller - Draft Controller]
    H --> I[Final Control Element - ID Fan Vane / FD Fan Damper]
    I --> J[Negative Pressure Maintained in Furnace approx 0 mm WC]
    G --> K[Indicator / Recorder / DCS Display]
    L[Barometric Pressure Sensor] --> G
    M[Flue Gas Temperature Sensor RTD] --> G
    N[Ambient Temperature Sensor] --> G
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Draft Error Elimination

This second diagram emphasizes the **error elimination strategy** as a flow of compensation signals:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Raw Draft Signal from DPT] --> nodeB[Subtract: Condensate Pot Head Error]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Apply: Temperature Compensation Factor]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Apply: Barometric Pressure Correction]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Subtract: Static Elevation Head]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Apply: Damping / Filter]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Corrected Draft Signal mm WC]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Compare with Set Point 0 mm WC]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Generate Error Signal]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Actuate ID Fan / FD Fan]
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Balanced Draft Furnace Setup (Textual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Boiler_System
        A1[Air Intake] --> A2[FD Fan]
        A2 --> A3[Air Heater / Preheater]
        A3 --> A4[Furnace - Combustion Zone - Draft approx 0 mm WC]
        A4 --> A5[Boiler Bank / Economizer]
        A5 --> A6[ESP / Bag Filter / Dust Collector]
        A6 --> A7[ID Fan - Creates Negative Pressure]
        A7 --> A8[Stack / Chimney - Releases to Atmosphere]
    end
    T1[Draft Probe at Furnace] -.->|Low Pressure Signal| DPT
    T2[Draft Probe at Stack] -.->|Low Pressure Signal| DPT
    DPT[Differential Pressure Transmitter] --> CTRL[DCS / PID Controller]
    CTRL -->|Speed Reference| A2
    CTRL -->|Vane Position| A7
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram for KTU Exams:** In a balanced-draft boiler, the **FD fan maintains a slightly positive pressure** at the air side, the **ID fan maintains a slightly negative pressure** at the gas side, and the **furnace itself sits at near-atmospheric (≈ 0 mm WC) pressure**. This is the essence of "draft" — and the instrumentation system continuously adjusts both fans to keep the furnace pressure stable.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
**Define the term "draft" as used in boiler instrumentation. Why is balanced draft preferred over natural draft in large utility boilers?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Draft is the small pressure difference, typically negative (sub-atmospheric), maintained in a furnace or boiler to ensure proper flow of air for combustion and flue gases through the system.
- **(1 mark)** It is measured in **mm of water column (mm WC)**.
- **(1 mark)** Balanced draft (using both FD and ID fans) is preferred because it maintains the **furnace at near-atmospheric pressure (≈ 0 mm WC)**, eliminating the **risks of flame blow-out (with natural draft) and cold air infiltration (with induced draft alone)**, and providing precise control over combustion.

---

### **Question A2 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**List any THREE sources of error in draft measurement and state the corresponding method of eliminating each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

| # | Source of Error | Elimination Method |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Condensation in impulse lines | Install **drip / condensate pots** to trap water |
| 2 | Density change of manometer fluid with temperature | Use **DPT with electronic temperature compensation** or temperature-stabilized manometer |
| 3 | Static head due to elevation difference | Mount the gauge **at the same elevation** as the tapping point, or apply **mathematical correction** |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **MOST COMMON MISTAKE (–2 marks):** Converting mm of **mercury** to mm of **water column** using just the ratio of densities *without multiplying by 13.6*. Always write the formula explicitly: $h_{\text{wc}} = h_{\text{Hg}} \times 13.6$.
> - **SECOND MISTAKE (–1 mark):** Confusing **gauge pressure** with **absolute pressure** in draft problems. Draft is **gauge** pressure.
> - **THIRD MISTAKE (–1 mark):** Forgetting to convert °C to K in gas-law problems. Examiners check for $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$.
> - **FOURTH MISTAKE (–2 marks):** Not drawing a clear, labelled block diagram wherever the question says "explain with a diagram". A neat diagram is worth 2–3 marks by itself.

---

### **Question B-A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **balanced draft system** in a large utility boiler. Discuss the role of FD fan, ID fan, and the differential pressure transmitter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A U-tube manometer used for draft measurement has a manometer fluid of specific gravity **0.85** and reads **120 mm** of fluid when the furnace is operating. The ambient temperature is **45 °C** and the temperature coefficient of the manometer fluid is **0.0007 per °C**. Calculate the **true draft in mm of water column at standard conditions (20 °C)**. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks**
- **(2 marks)** Definition: Balanced draft is a system in which the **FD fan** supplies combustion air under slight positive pressure, and the **ID fan** sucks out the flue gases, maintaining the **furnace at ≈ 0 mm WC** (atmospheric).
- **(2 marks)** Role of FD fan: Pressurizes the air side of the wind box; controls the **air-fuel ratio** indirectly.
- **(2 marks)** Role of ID fan: Overcomes the resistance of the gas side (economizer, air-preheater, ESP, ducting); controls **furnace pressure**.
- **(1 mark)** Role of DPT: Measures the differential pressure (furnace – atmosphere) and provides a **4–20 mA signal** to the DCS/PID, which modulates the ID fan vane position.

#### **Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- Specific gravity of manometer fluid, $S = 0.85$
- Manometer reading, $h_{\text{obs}} = 120$ mm
- Ambient temperature, $T_{\text{amb}} = 45$ °C
- Reference temperature, $T_{\text{ref}} = 20$ °C
- Temperature coefficient, $\beta = 0.0007$ per °C

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Calculate density of manometer fluid at 45 °C.
$$
\rho_{45} = \rho_{\text{water}} \times S \times [1 - \beta \times (T_{\text{amb}} - T_{\text{ref}})]
$$

$$
\rho_{45} = 1000 \times 0.85 \times [1 - 0.0007 \times (45 - 20)]
$$

$$
\rho_{45} = 850 \times [1 - 0.0007 \times 25]
$$

$$
\rho_{45} = 850 \times [1 - 0.0175] = 850 \times 0.9825
$$

$$
\rho_{45} = 835.125 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Calculate pressure indicated.
$$
P_{\text{ind}} = h_{\text{obs}} \times \rho_{45} \times g
$$

$$
P_{\text{ind}} = 0.120 \times 835.125 \times 9.81 = 982.94 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Convert to mm WC at actual conditions.
$$
h_{\text{wc, actual}} = \frac{P_{\text{ind}}}{\rho_{\text{water}} \times g} = \frac{982.94}{9810} = 0.1002 \text{ m} = 100.2 \text{ mm WC}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Find density at 20 °C.
$$
\rho_{20} = 1000 \times 0.85 \times [1 - 0.0007 \times 0] = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Calculate true (corrected) draft in mm WC at 20 °C.
$$
h_{\text{wc, true}} = h_{\text{obs}} \times \frac{\rho_{20}}{\rho_{\text{water}}} = 120 \times 0.85 = 102 \text{ mm WC}
$$

**Final Answer:** The true draft at 20 °C is **102 mm WC** (and the observed reading of 100.2 mm WC at 45 °C was under-reading by 1.8 mm WC due to fluid density decrease).

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** What is meant by the term **"elimination of draft"** in the context of boiler instrumentation? Explain any **four common sources of error** in draft measurement and how each is eliminated. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The chimney of an industrial boiler is **30 m tall**. The flue gas temperature is **220 °C** and the ambient air temperature is **30 °C**. Molecular weight of flue gas = **30 kg/kmol**, molecular weight of air = **29 kg/kmol**. Calculate the **natural draft produced** in **mm of water column**. Take $P_{\text{atm}} = 1.013 \times 10^5$ Pa. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks**

- **(1 mark)** "Elimination of draft" refers to the **techniques used to correct / compensate / nullify the measurement errors** in draft sensing, so that the indicated draft accurately represents the actual furnace condition.
- **(1.5 marks each × 4 = 6 marks)** Four common error sources and their elimination:

| Error Source | Elimination Method |
|---|---|
| 1. **Manometer fluid density change with temperature** | Use DPT with built-in temperature compensation; maintain constant ambient temperature around the gauge |
| 2. **Condensation in impulse lines** | Use **condensate / drip pots** at the lowest point; install heat tracing; purge with dry instrument air |
| 3. **Atmospheric (barometric) pressure variation** | Use **absolute pressure transmitter** + **barometric sensor** and apply **software compensation** in DCS |
| 4. **Static head error (elevation difference)** | Install the **gauge at the same elevation as the tapping point**; otherwise apply **elevation correction** mathematically |

#### **Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- $H = 30$ m
- $T_g = 220$ °C $= 220 + 273.15 = 493.15$ K
- $T_a = 30$ °C $= 30 + 273.15 = 303.15$ K
- $M_g = 30$ kg/kmol
- $M_a = 29$ kg/kmol
- $P_{\text{atm}} = 1.013 \times 10^5$ Pa
- $R = 8314$ J/(kmol·K)
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Calculate density of air at 30 °C.
$$
\rho_a = \frac{P_{\text{atm}} \cdot M_a}{R \cdot T_a} = \frac{1.013 \times 10^5 \times 29}{8314 \times 303.15}
$$

$$
\rho_a = \frac{2{,}937{,}700}{2{,}520{,}650.1} = 1.1655 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Calculate density of flue gas at 220 °C.
$$
\rho_g = \frac{P_{\text{atm}} \cdot M_g}{R \cdot T_g} = \frac{1.013 \times 10^5 \times 30}{8314 \times 493.15}
$$

$$
\rho_g = \frac{3{,}039{,}000}{4{,}099{,}407.1} = 0.7413 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Calculate natural draft in Pa.
$$
\Delta P = H \cdot g \cdot (\rho_a - \rho_g)
$$

$$
\Delta P = 30 \times 9.81 \times (1.1655 - 0.7413)
$$

$$
\Delta P = 30 \times 9.81 \times 0.4242
$$

$$
\Delta P = 124.84 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Convert to mm WC.
$$
\Delta P_{\text{mmWC}} = \frac{124.84}{9.806} = 12.73 \text{ mm WC}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Final Answer with unit.
$$
\boxed{\Delta P_{\text{natural draft}} \approx 12.73 \text{ mm WC}}
$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Fire Revision — Pin These in Memory Before Exam**

- **Draft definition** → Small negative pressure maintained in furnace/boiler to drive flow of gases; measured in **mm WC**.
- **Three types of draft** → **Natural** (chimney), **Forced (FD)**, **Induced (ID)**. **Balanced** = FD + ID.
- **Balanced draft setpoint** → Furnace pressure ≈ **0 mm WC** (slightly negative, typically –2 to –5 mm WC).
- **Key formula** → $\Delta P = H \cdot g \cdot (\rho_a - \rho_g)$ — natural draft in Pa.
- **Density at temperature** → $\rho = \frac{PM}{RT}$ (always use **T in Kelvin**!).
- **Manometer to mm WC conversion** → $h_{\text{wc}} = h_{\text{fluid}} \times \frac{\rho_{\text{fluid}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}}$.
- **Mercury to water** → Multiply by **13.6** (specific gravity of mercury).
- **Six common sources of error** → (1) Manometer fluid density, (2) Condensation in impulse lines, (3) Atmospheric pressure variation, (4) Wind effects, (5) Static head, (6) Zero drift.
- **Elimination techniques** → Condensate pots, heat tracing, temperature compensation, barometric compensation, elevation correction, smart transmitters with auto-zero, drip pots, snubbers.
- **Key instrument** → **Differential Pressure Transmitter (DPT)** — workhorse of draft measurement.
- **Common fan-control logic** → If furnace pressure **increases** (less negative) → **increase ID fan speed**; if **decreases** (more negative) → **decrease ID fan speed**.
- **For large boilers** (≥ 500 MW), the **furnace pressure control loop** is one of the **fastest** loops (response time < 1 second) for **safety against implosion**.
- **Standard KTU answer structure** → (1) Definition, (2) Diagram, (3) Formula with substitution, (4) Final answer with unit. **Always include units — every time!**
- **Practical Industrial Note** → A 500 MW boiler in NTPC Vindhyachal has approximately **6 draft measurement points** monitored continuously in the DCS.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Elimination of Draft**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Temperature Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT864 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN LARGE SCALE INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Temperature Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Temperature Control** is a closed-loop process control strategy used in large-scale process industries (petrochemical, metallurgical, power, pharmaceutical, and food sectors) to maintain the temperature of a process variable (PV) at a desired setpoint (SP) by automatically manipulating a final control element — typically a heat input (steam, fuel, electrical heating) or heat removal (cooling water, refrigerant) stream.

In the formal KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based framework, temperature control is classified under **regulatory control** of **thermal process variables** and is a **single-input, single-output (SISO)** feedback control loop with typically large time constants (minutes to hours), significant dead time, and inherent non-linearity.

$$ G_{temp}(s) = \frac{K_p \, e^{-\theta s}}{(\tau_1 s + 1)(\tau_2 s + 1)} $$

where $K_p$ is the process gain, $\theta$ is the **transportation lag (dead time)**, and $\tau_1, \tau_2$ are the dominant thermal time constants.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of temperature control like driving a car on a hilly road while maintaining a constant speed of **60 km/h**:

- **Setpoint (SP)** = 60 km/h (the desired speed).
- **Process Variable (PV)** = current speed shown on the speedometer.
- **Error (e)** = difference between desired and actual speed.
- **Controller** = your brain deciding how hard to press the accelerator.
- **Final Control Element** = the accelerator pedal.
- **Process** = the car's engine and the hill it is climbing.
- **Disturbance** = sudden uphill slope or wind gust.

Now replace "speed" with "temperature" and "accelerator" with a **steam valve** — you have an industrial temperature control loop.

> [!NOTE]
> In large scale industries, temperature loops are typically **slow** (time constants of 5–60 minutes), exhibit **long dead times**, and require **continuous tuning** because thermal processes drift with ambient conditions, fouling of heat exchangers, and catalyst deactivation.

## 1.3 Why Temperature Control is Critical in Large Scale Industries

| Industry | Critical Temperature Loop | Consequence of Poor Control |
|---|---|---|
| Petroleum Refining | Crude distillation column overhead | Off-spec products, safety hazard |
| Power Plants | Superheated steam temperature | Turbine blade failure |
| Cement | Kiln burning zone temperature | Poor clinker quality |
| Glass Manufacturing | Furnace forehearth | Product breakage |
| Pharmaceuticals | Reactor batch temperature | Batch rejection, regulatory failure |
| Pulp & Paper | Digester liquor temperature | Wood chip digestion quality |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Metric:** Most industrial temperature loops operate in the range **0 °C to 1600 °C**, with control accuracy demanded at **±1 °C** for chemical reactors and **±5 °C** for furnace processes. Primary measurement standards are anchored to the **ITS-90 (International Temperature Scale of 1990)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Temperature Control Loop

A canonical industrial temperature control loop consists of the following functional blocks in series:

1. **Temperature Sensor (Primary Element)** — RTD (Pt100), Thermocouple (Type K, J, S), or Infrared Pyrometer.
2. **Transmitter (Secondary Element)** — Converts sensor signal to a standardized **4–20 mA** or **0–10 V DC** signal.
3. **Controller (PID)** — Computes control action based on error.
4. **Final Control Element** — Pneumatically actuated control valve modulating steam/fuel/cold water flow, or a thyristor-fired SCR power controller.
5. **Process** — Heat exchanger, reactor, furnace, jacketed vessel.

## 2.2 Modes of Control

### 2.2.1 On-Off Control (Two-Position Control)

$$ u(t) = \begin{cases} u_{max}, & e(t) \geq 0 \\ u_{min}, & e(t) < 0 \end{cases} $$

Used only in **domestic thermostats** and simple domestic applications; produces continuous oscillation around setpoint. **Not used in large scale industries** due to wear and energy inefficiency.

### 2.2.2 Proportional (P) Control

$$ u(t) = K_c \, e(t) + u_0 $$

**Issue:** Introduces **Offset** — a steady-state error between SP and PV that never decays.

### 2.2.3 Proportional-Integral (PI) Control

$$ u(t) = K_c \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{\tau_I} \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau \right] + u_0 $$

**The workhorse of industrial temperature control.** Eliminates offset through integral action.

### 2.2.4 Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) Control

$$ u(t) = K_c \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{\tau_I} \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau + \tau_D \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right] $$

The derivative term **anticipates** future error, useful for fast loops but rarely needed in slow temperature loops due to measurement noise amplification.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation | Variable Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | First-Order Lag (thermal) | $T \dfrac{dT_{out}}{dt} + T_{out} = K \, T_{in}$ | $T$ = thermal time constant, $K$ = steady-state gain |
| 2 | Heat Balance | $Q = m \, C_p \, \Delta T$ | $Q$ = heat (J), $m$ = mass (kg), $C_p$ = specific heat (J/kg·K) |
| 3 | Lumped Capacitance | $T \dfrac{dT}{dt} = T_{ss} - T(t)$ | Newton's law of cooling for stirred tank |
| 4 | PID Controller Output | $u(t) = K_c e(t) + \dfrac{K_c}{\tau_I}\int e \, dt + K_c \tau_D \dfrac{de}{dt}$ | Standard ISA form |
| 5 | Ziegler-Nichols (Reaction Curve) | $K_c = \dfrac{1.2 \, T}{K \, L}$ | $T$ = process time const, $L$ = dead time |
| 6 | Ziegler-Nichols (ZN) | $\tau_I = 2 \, L$ | Reset time |
| 7 | Ziegler-Nichols (ZN) | $\tau_D = 0.5 \, L$ | Derivative time |
| 8 | Cohen-Coon Tuning | $K_c = \dfrac{1}{K} \dfrac{\tau}{L} \left[ \dfrac{1.35}{\tfrac{\tau}{L}} + 0.27 \right]$ | For processes with significant dead time |
| 9 | Integral Error (IAE) | $IAE = \int_0^{\infty} \vert e(t) \vert \, dt$ | Performance index |
| 10 | Valve Linearity | $C_v = Q \sqrt{\dfrac{G_f}{\Delta P}}$ | Valve flow coefficient |
| 11 | Stefan-Boltzmann (Radiation) | $q = \epsilon \, \sigma \, T^4$ | $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴ |
| 12 | Conduction (Fourier) | $q = \dfrac{kA}{\Delta x} \Delta T$ | $k$ = thermal conductivity (W/m·K) |
| 13 | Convection (Newton) | $q = h A \Delta T$ | $h$ = heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Hot Tip:** Always use **$\vert x \vert$** notation with `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX (NOT the vertical bar character) — vertical bars in markdown tables will break rendering in PDF converters.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

Temperature control theory directly drives:
- **Combustion control** in boilers and furnaces (cross-limited bias control).
- **Cascade control** where an outer temperature loop sets the SP of an inner flow loop.
- **Feedforward control** using feed flow rate and inlet temperature as measured disturbances.
- **Model Predictive Control (MPC)** for multi-pass furnace and cracking operations.

## 2.5 Types of Industrial Temperature Control Strategies

- **Feedback Control** — corrective action based on measured error.
- **Feedforward Control** — preemptive action based on measured disturbance.
- **Cascade Control** — nested loops; secondary loop rejects disturbances faster.
- **Ratio Control** — maintains two streams in fixed proportion (e.g., fuel:air ratio).
- **Override/Select Control** — protects equipment from unsafe conditions.
- **Split-Range Control** — one controller output drives two or more valves for heating and cooling.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: First-Order Thermal Process Dynamics

Consider a perfectly stirred tank heater where:
- Inlet temperature $= T_i(t)$
- Outlet temperature $= T(t)$
- Mass $= m$, Specific heat $= C_p$
- Heat input rate $= Q(t)$
- Flow rate $= F$

**Step 1 — Energy accumulation rate** in the tank:

$$ \rho V C_p \frac{dT}{dt} = \text{Energy In} - \text{Energy Out} + \text{Heat Added} $$

**Step 2 — Substitute the constituent energy terms:**

$$ \rho V C_p \frac{dT}{dt} = F \rho C_p T_i - F \rho C_p T + Q $$

**Step 3 — Divide throughout by $F \rho C_p$:**

$$ \frac{V}{F} \frac{dT}{dt} + T = T_i + \frac{Q}{F \rho C_p} $$

**Step 4 — Recognize $V/F = T$ (residence time, which is the thermal time constant):**

$$ T \frac{dT}{dt} + T(t) = T_i(t) + \frac{Q(t)}{F \rho C_p} $$

**Step 5 — For a step change $\Delta Q$ in heat input with constant inlet temperature, take the Laplace transform:**

$$ T \cdot s \cdot T(s) + T(s) = \frac{K \cdot \Delta Q}{s} $$

where $K = \dfrac{1}{F \rho C_p}$ is the process gain.

**Step 6 — Solve for the transfer function:**

$$ \frac{T(s)}{\Delta Q(s)} = \frac{K}{Ts + 1} $$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical first-order plus dead time (FOPDT)** thermal model universally used in KTU exam problems. Many real reactors exhibit a second-order form due to jacket dynamics.

## 3.2 Derivation: PID Controller Tuning via Ziegler-Nichols Open-Loop Method

**Step 1 — Conduct an open-loop step test** on the process. Apply a small step change $\Delta u$ in the manipulated variable and record the response $y(t)$.

**Step 2 — Identify the FOPDT parameters** from the reaction curve:
- Process Gain: $K = \dfrac{\Delta y_{ss}}{\Delta u}$
- Dead Time (transportation lag): $L$ — time before $y(t)$ first noticeably changes.
- Time Constant: $T$ — time to reach $63.2\%$ of the final steady-state change.

**Step 3 — Apply Ziegler-Nichols tuning correlations for a PID controller:**

$$ K_c = \frac{1.2 \, T}{K \, L} $$

$$ \tau_I = 2 \, L $$

$$ \tau_D = 0.5 \, L $$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style):**

A jacketed chemical reactor exhibits the following open-loop step response when steam valve opening is increased by 10\%:
- Dead time: $L = 1.5$ min
- Time constant: $T = 6.0$ min
- Steady-state temperature rise: $\Delta y_{ss} = 4.0$ °C
- Step input magnitude: $\Delta u = 10\%$

**Step 1 — Calculate process gain:**

$$ K = \frac{\Delta y_{ss}}{\Delta u} = \frac{4.0 \text{ °C}}{10\%} = 0.4 \text{ °C/\%} $$

**Step 2 — Compute proportional gain:**

$$ K_c = \frac{1.2 \times 6.0}{0.4 \times 1.5} = \frac{7.2}{0.6} = 12.0 \text{ \%/°C} $$

**Step 3 — Compute reset time:**

$$ \tau_I = 2 \times 1.5 = 3.0 \text{ min} $$

**Step 4 — Compute derivative time:**

$$ \tau_D = 0.5 \times 1.5 = 0.75 \text{ min} $$

**Final PID Settings:**

$$ K_c = 12.0 \text{ \%/°C}, \quad \tau_I = 3.0 \text{ min}, \quad \tau_D = 0.75 \text{ min} $$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Tuning Methods

| Criterion | Ziegler-Nichols (Open-Loop) | Ziegler-Nichols (Closed-Loop) | Cohen-Coon | Tyreus-Luyben |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Test Required** | Open-loop step | Closed-loop P-only (find $K_u$) | Open-loop step | Closed-loop P-only |
| **Best For** | First-order systems | Moderately noisy systems | High dead-time processes | Long time constants |
| **Aggressiveness** | Aggressive (25% overshoot) | Aggressive | More aggressive | Conservative |
| **Industrial Use** | Common, classical | Common, classical | Less common | Preferred in industry |
| **$\tau_I$ Formula** | $2L$ | $2.2 \, P_u$ | $\tau \left[ \dfrac{3.33 - 3\tfrac{L}{\tau}}{1 + 1.2\tfrac{L}{\tau}} \right]$ | $2.2 \, P_u$ |
| **$\tau_D$ Formula** | $0.5L$ | $P_u/6.3$ | $\dfrac{0.37 L}{1 - 0.81 \tfrac{L}{\tau}}$ | $P_u/6.3$ |

> [!NOTE]
> $P_u$ = Ultimate period (oscillation period at ultimate gain $K_u$). $K_u$ = gain at which sustained oscillation first occurs in closed-loop with pure P-control.

## 3.4 Derivation: Heat Exchanger Steady-State Energy Balance

For a counter-current shell-and-tube heat exchanger used in temperature control:

**Step 1 — Hot side energy balance:**

$$ Q = \dot{m}_h \, C_{p,h} \, (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) $$

**Step 2 — Cold side energy balance:**

$$ Q = \dot{m}_c \, C_{p,c} \, (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}) $$

**Step 3 — Overall heat transfer equation:**

$$ Q = U \, A \, \Delta T_{LMTD} $$

where $U$ is the overall heat transfer coefficient and $\Delta T_{LMTD}$ is the Log Mean Temperature Difference:

$$ \Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} $$

with $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$ and $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$.

This equation, used together with the controller output equation, forms the basis of **inferential temperature control** in refineries where outlet temperature cannot be measured directly.

## 3.5 Stability Analysis via Routh-Hurwitz

For a closed-loop temperature control system with characteristic equation:

$$ 1 + G_c(s) G_p(s) H(s) = 0 $$

For a PI controller $G_c(s) = K_c \left( 1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I s} \right)$ and first-order process $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$:

$$ \tau_I \tau \, s^2 + \tau_I \, s + K_c K = 0 $$

Applying Routh-Hurwitz stability criterion: **All coefficients must be positive**. Since $K_c, K, \tau_I, \tau$ are inherently positive, the system is **always stable** for a first-order process — confirming the well-known fact that **PI control of first-order systems never destabilizes**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: Single-Loop Temperature Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Setpoint SP] --> SUM[Summing Junction]
    PV[Process Variable PV] --> SUM
    SUM -->|Error e t| CTRL[PID Controller]
    CTRL -->|Output m t| FCV[Final Control Element Steam Valve]
    FCV --> PROC[Thermal Process Reactor Furnace]
    PROC -->|Temperature| TRANS[RTD Thermocouple Transmitter]
    TRANS --> PV
    DIST[Disturbance Feed Flow Ambient T] -.-> PROC

    style SP fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#333
    style CTRL fill:#b4d4ff,stroke:#333
    style PROC fill:#ffb4b4,stroke:#333
    style FCV fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#333
    style TRANS fill:#fff4b4,stroke:#333
```

**Diagram Note:** This block diagram illustrates the canonical feedback loop. The disturbance enters the process as a dashed signal, representing real-world influences such as changes in feed flow rate, ambient temperature, or fouling of heat-transfer surfaces.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Cascade Temperature Control

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    OUTER_SP[Outer SP Desired T] --> OUTER_SUM[Summing Junction Outer]
    T_PV[Reactor T PV] --> OUTER_SUM
    OUTER_SUM -->|Error1| TC[Master Temperature Controller TC]
    TC -->|Inner SP| INNER_SUM[Summing Junction Inner]
    F_PV[Steam Flow PV] --> INNER_SUM
    INNER_SUM -->|Error2| FC[Slave Flow Controller FC]
    FC -->|Valve Signal| VALVE[Steam Control Valve]
    VALVE --> JACKET[Heating Jacket]
    JACKET --> REACTOR[Chemical Reactor]
    REACTOR --> T_PV
    JACKET -.->|Steam Flow| F_PV

    style OUTER_SP fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#333
    style TC fill:#b4d4ff,stroke:#333
    style FC fill:#b4d4ff,stroke:#333
    style VALVE fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#333
    style REACTOR fill:#ffb4b4,stroke:#333
```

**Diagram Note:** In cascade control, the **master (outer)** temperature controller dynamically resets the setpoint of the **slave (inner)** flow controller. This architecture dramatically improves disturbance rejection — a fluctuation in steam supply pressure is corrected by the flow loop before it can affect reactor temperature.

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Split-Range Temperature Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CTRL[Single Temperature Controller] --> SPLITTER[Split Range Signal Splitter]
    SPLITTER -->|4 to 12 mA| HCV[Heating Steam Valve]
    SPLITTER -->|12 to 20 mA| CCV[Cooling Water Valve]
    HCV --> JACKET[Reactor Jacket]
    CCV --> JACKET
    JACKET --> PROC[Process Reactor]
    PROC -->|T PV| SENSOR[Temp Sensor]
    SENSOR --> CTRL

    style CTRL fill:#b4d4ff,stroke:#333
    style SPLITTER fill:#fff4b4,stroke:#333
    style HCV fill:#ffb4b4,stroke:#333
    style CCV fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#333
```

**Diagram Note:** Split-range control uses **one controller output** to operate **two final elements** — heating below 50\% output and cooling above 50\% output. This is common in **exothermic batch reactors** where both heating (start-up) and cooling (during exotherm) are needed.

## 4.4 Functional Architecture: Feedforward Temperature Control with Feedback Trim

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FF_PV[Feed Flow Rate F PV] --> FF_CTRL[Feedforward Controller FF]
    FF_PV2[Feed Inlet T Ti PV] --> FF_CTRL
    FF_CTRL --> SUM2[Summing Junction FF+FB]
    FB_CTRL[Feedback Temperature Controller] --> SUM2
    SUM2 --> VALVE[Steam Valve]
    VALVE --> PROC[Heat Exchanger]
    PROC --> T_OUT[Outlet T]
    T_OUT --> FB_CTRL
    PROC -.->|Disturbance| FF_PV

    style FF_CTRL fill:#fff4b4,stroke:#333
    style FB_CTRL fill:#b4d4ff,stroke:#333
    style PROC fill:#ffb4b4,stroke:#333
```

**Diagram Note:** Feedforward action **anticipates** disturbances before they affect the process. The feedback trim corrects for model inaccuracies. This architecture is essential in refinery preheat trains and crude distillation overhead condensers.

## 4.5 Comparison Matrix: Selection of Control Architecture

| Architecture | Disturbance Rejection | Complexity | Cost | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Single-loop feedback | Moderate | Low | Low | Small batch reactors |
| Cascade | Excellent | Medium | Medium | Jacketed exothermic reactors |
| Split-range | Excellent | Medium | Medium | Reactor heating/cooling |
| Feedforward+Feedback | Excellent | High | High | Heat exchangers, distillation |
| MPC | Best | Very High | Very High | Refinery furnaces, ethylene crackers |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define temperature control loop. List any four final control elements used in temperature control.**

**Model Answer:**
A temperature control loop is a closed-loop feedback system that automatically maintains the temperature of a process at a desired setpoint by comparing the measured temperature (PV) with the setpoint (SP) and adjusting the heat input or removal.

Four final control elements commonly used:
1. Pneumatically actuated globe valves for steam/cold water modulation.
2. Electrically heated cartridges (thyristor-controlled).
3. SCR (Silicon Controlled Rectifier) power controllers.
4. Variable speed pumps (for circulating heating/cooling media).

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks for listing with brief relevance)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State the first-order plus dead time (FOPDT) model used for temperature processes and explain the significance of each parameter.**

**Model Answer:**

$$ G(s) = \frac{K e^{-L s}}{\tau s + 1} $$

where:
- $K$ = process gain (°C per % valve opening), represents steady-state sensitivity.
- $L$ = dead time / transportation lag (minutes), represents measurement/transport delay.
- $\tau$ = time constant (minutes), represents thermal inertia of the process.

**Significance:** This model approximates the dynamic behavior of stirred-tank heaters, jacketed reactors, and heat exchangers. It is the basis for Ziegler-Nichols and Cohen-Coon tuning.

*(3 marks: 1 mark equation + 2 marks explanation)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]

**(a)** Explain the working of a cascade temperature control system for a jacketed chemical reactor with a neat block diagram. Discuss its advantages over single-loop feedback control. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A jacketed batch reactor is to be controlled using PI control. An open-loop step test yields the following FOPDT parameters: Process gain $K = 2.5$ °C/%, Time constant $\tau = 8$ min, Dead time $L = 2$ min. Calculate the PI controller settings using the Ziegler-Nichols open-loop method. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Cascade Temperature Control

**Working Principle:**

In cascade control, **two controllers are nested** — the output of the primary (master) controller dynamically adjusts the setpoint of the secondary (slave) controller.

- **Primary Loop:** Temperature controller (TC) measures reactor temperature $T_R$ and compares with SP. Its output becomes the SP for the flow controller.
- **Secondary Loop:** Flow controller (FC) measures steam flow $F_s$ and adjusts the steam control valve to maintain the flow at the TC's demanded value.

**Block Diagram (already shown in Section 4.2 — refer).**

**Advantages over Single-Loop Feedback:**
1. **Faster disturbance rejection** — steam pressure fluctuations are corrected by the inner flow loop before reaching the reactor.
2. **Non-linearity compensation** — valve non-linearity is handled by the inner loop.
3. **Improved stability** — allows higher gain in the master loop without oscillation.
4. **Better tracking** of setpoint changes in batch processes.

*(7 marks: 3 marks diagram + 4 marks explanation and advantages)*

#### Part (b) — PI Tuning Calculation

**Given:**
- $K = 2.5$ °C/%
- $\tau = 8$ min
- $L = 2$ min

**Step 1 — Proportional gain using ZN open-loop correlation:**

$$ K_c = \frac{0.9 \, \tau}{K \, L} $$

$$ K_c = \frac{0.9 \times 8}{2.5 \times 2} = \frac{7.2}{5.0} = 1.44 \text{ \%/°C} $$

**Step 2 — Integral (reset) time:**

$$ \tau_I = 3L = 3 \times 2 = 6 \text{ min} $$

**Step 3 — Derivative time (for PID, though question asks PI):**

$$ \tau_D = 0 \, \text{min} \quad \text{(since PI controller)} $$

**Final PI Settings:**

$$ K_c = 1.44 \text{ \%/°C}, \quad \tau_I = 6 \text{ min} $$

**Sanity check:** The proportional band $PB = \dfrac{100}{K_c} = 69.4\%$, indicating moderate aggressiveness suitable for a slow thermal process.

*(7 marks: 2 marks formula identification + 3 marks substitution & calculation + 2 marks final answer with units and physical interpretation)*

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]

**(a)** Derive the transfer function of a first-order thermal process (stirred tank heater) and explain each term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, explain split-range temperature control. State two industrial applications where it is essential. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Transfer Function Derivation

Refer to Section 3.1 for the complete derivation. Key steps to present in the exam:

**Step 1:** Write energy balance:

$$ \rho V C_p \frac{dT}{dt} = F \rho C_p T_i - F \rho C_p T + Q $$

**Step 2:** Rearrange to standard form:

$$ \frac{V}{F} \frac{dT}{dt} + T = T_i + \frac{Q}{F \rho C_p} $$

**Step 3:** Identify residence time $T = V/F$ as the time constant and $K = 1/(F \rho C_p)$ as the process gain.

**Step 4:** Take Laplace transform:

$$ \frac{T(s)}{Q(s)} = \frac{K}{Ts + 1} $$

**Explanation of each term:**
- $T$ (time constant): thermal inertia — larger $T$ means slower response.
- $K$ (gain): change in temperature per unit change in heat input.
- Steady-state gain: as $s \to 0$, $T(s)/Q(s) \to K = 1/(F \rho C_p)$.

*(7 marks: 3 marks derivation + 2 marks algebraic manipulation + 2 marks term explanation)*

#### Part (b) — Split-Range Control

**Working Principle:**

In split-range control, the output signal from a single controller (typically 4–20 mA or 3–15 psi) is **divided into two or more ranges**, each driving a different final control element.

- **Output 4–12 mA:** Opens steam valve (heating).
- **Output 12–20 mA:** Opens cooling water valve (cooling).
- **Output 12 mA (midpoint):** Both valves closed.

**Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.3).**

**Two Industrial Applications:**

1. **Exothermic Batch Reactors** — e.g., polymerization reactors where the reaction is endothermic at start-up (needs heating) but exothermic during the main reaction phase (needs cooling). A single temperature controller manages both via split-range.

2. **Jacketed Storage Tanks with Day/Night Ambient Variation** — same controller handles heating coil in winter and cooling coil in summer based on demand.

*(7 marks: 3 marks sketch + 2 marks working + 2 marks applications)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> **Common places where students lose marks in Temperature Control questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency in Tuning Problems:** Mixing minutes and seconds in $K_c$, $\tau_I$, $\tau_D$ calculations. The ZN formula requires **consistent time units** throughout. If $L$ is in minutes, then $\tau_I$ is also in minutes.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the negative sign in feedback loops:** The summing junction should be $e(t) = SP - PV$, NOT $PV - SP$. Reversed sign causes the loop to drive the process in the wrong direction.
>
> 3. **Confusing proportional band $PB$ with proportional gain $K_c$:** $PB = \dfrac{100}{K_c}$. Students often write $PB = K_c$ which is wrong.
>
> 4. **Using the wrong ZN formula:** ZN open-loop uses $1.2\tau/(KL)$ for **PID**, not for PI. For PI only, use $0.9\tau/(KL)$.
>
> 5. **Skipping the Laplace transform step:** When deriving the transfer function, marks are reserved for taking the Laplace transform and isolating the ratio. Skipping this step costs 2 marks.
>
> 6. **Not justifying why PI is preferred over PID for temperature loops:** Always mention that derivative action **amplifies measurement noise** which is significant in RTD/thermocouple signals.
>
> 7. **Confusing cascade with override:** In cascade, the inner loop tracks the outer's signal continuously. In override, the higher of two signals wins via a selector.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- [ ] Temperature control is a **SISO** feedback loop with **large time constants** (5–60 min typical).
- [ ] The **standard industrial signal** is **4–20 mA** with **HART protocol** for smart instruments.
- [ ] The **FOPDT model** $\dfrac{K e^{-Ls}}{\tau s + 1}$ is the canonical thermal model used in **90% of KTU problems**.
- [ ] **PI control** is the workhorse for industrial temperature loops — **eliminates offset**.
- [ ] **PID control** is rarely needed for slow temperature loops due to noise in RTD/thermocouple signals.
- [ ] **Ziegler-Nichols Open-Loop Tuning**:
   - $K_c = \dfrac{1.2\tau}{KL}$ (PID) or $\dfrac{0.9\tau}{KL}$ (PI)
   - $\tau_I = 2L$ (PID) or $3L$ (PI)
   - $\tau_D = 0.5L$ (PID only)
- [ ] **Cohen-Coon tuning** is preferred when the dead-time-to-time-constant ratio $L/\tau > 0.3$.
- [ ] **Tyreus-Luyben** gives **more conservative** settings and is preferred in plants operating near constraints.
- [ ] **Cascade control** improves disturbance rejection; requires secondary loop to be **at least 3× faster** than primary.
- [ ] **Split-range control** uses **one controller** to drive **heating and cooling** simultaneously — used in exothermic batch reactors.
- [ ] **Feedforward control** is essential when disturbance measurement is reliable (e.g., feed flow rate in heat exchangers).
- [ ] **Heat balance** for a stirred tank: $Q = m C_p \Delta T$ — fundamental to sizing control valves.
- [ ] **Valve sizing** uses $C_v = Q \sqrt{G_f / \Delta P}$ — always oversize by 1.2–1.5× for thermal loops.
- [ ] **RTD accuracy:** Pt100 has accuracy $\pm 0.1$ °C at 0 °C; **Thermocouple accuracy:** Type K $\pm 2.2$ °C.
- [ ] **ITS-90 standard** is the official temperature reference — define fixed points using triple points of water (0.01 °C) and melting points of metals.
- [ ] **MPC (Model Predictive Control)** is the gold standard for refinery furnaces and ethylene crackers where multiple constraints exist.

---

**End of Module 3 — Temperature Control Notes**
*Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme, Outcome-Based Education framework (NEP 2020).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
