<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper]**
**Define "Operating Envelope" and "Envelope Optimisation" in the context of a centrifugal compressor. Why is a static (fixed) surge line inadequate for modern industrial compressors?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Operating Envelope (1 mark):** The closed region in the compressor's $\Psi$–$\Phi$ map bounded on the left by the surge line and on the right by the stonewall (choke) line, representing all safe, stable operating points.
- **Envelope Optimisation (1 mark):** A real-time control technique that dynamically adjusts the boundaries of this safe region (especially the Surge Control Line, SCL) to maximise operating efficiency while maintaining a quantifiable safety margin against surge, accounting for process variability (gas composition, temperature, pressure, speed) and measurement uncertainties.
- **Why fixed SCL is inadequate (1 mark):** A static SCL cannot account for changes in gas $M$, $Z$, $\gamma$, inlet temperature, or compressor health (vibration, fouling). It leads to either excessive recycle (wasted energy) or insufficient margin (surge risk). Adaptive/optimised SCLs track process reality and reduce both energy cost and trip risk.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Sessional]**
**List any three (3) industrial equipment/scenarios where envelope optimisation is applied. For each, state the constraint being optimised.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**
1. **Centrifugal Compressor (LNG/ethylene):** Constraint = surge line / anti-surge envelope.
2. **Fired Heater / Boiler:** Constraint = tube-skin temperature envelope to prevent coking & creep.
3. **Distillation Column:** Constraint = flooding line (hydraulic envelope) and product-spec envelope.
   *(Acceptable alternatives: pump minimum-flow envelope, reactor runaway temperature envelope, rolling-mill strip-tension envelope.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** confuse **surge margin** with **stonewall margin** — they are on opposite sides of the operating map.
> - **Do NOT** use the **isentropic** head equation in place of **polytropic** head unless the problem explicitly states isentropic / reversible / $\eta = 100\%$. Loss of **1 mark** typical.
> - **Do NOT** forget to convert $N$ from **rpm to rps** (or vice versa) consistently; mixing units is the **#1 source of full-mark loss** in compressor numericals.
> - **Do NOT** omit the **DEC (decoupling) shift** in the SCL equation — examiners specifically test whether the student knows that the SCL is *not the surge line itself* but a *biased* curve.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the polytropic head equation for a centrifugal compressor from first principles. Clearly state the assumptions. Show the relationship between the polytropic exponent $n$ and the isentropic exponent $\gamma$ via the polytropic efficiency $\eta_{\text{poly}}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the aid of a block diagram, explain the **envelope optimisation algorithm** used in modern anti-surge controllers. How does the optimised SCL differ from a fixed SCL? **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Specific work in an open system (1 mark):**
For a steady-flow compressor (control volume), the first law gives the specific work as:
$$w_s = -\int_1^2 v \, dP$$

**Step 2 — Polytropic relation (1 mark):** For a polytropic process $P v^{n} = c$, hence $v = c^{1/n} P^{-1/n}$. At state 1, $c^{1/n} = P_1^{1/n} v_1$, giving:
$$v = (P_1 v_1^{n})^{1/n} P^{-1/n}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integral (2 marks):**
$$w_s = P_1 v_1 \int_{P_1}^{P_2} P^{-1/n} dP = P_1 v_1 \cdot \frac{n}{n-1}\left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$$

**Step 4 — Real-gas substitution (1 mark):** $P_1 v_1 = Z R T_1 / M$ (compressibility $Z$, gas constant $R$, molar mass $M$, inlet temperature $T_1$). Therefore:

$$H_p = w_s = \frac{n}{n-1} \cdot \frac{Z R T_1}{M} \left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$$

**Step 5 — Polytropic ↔ Isentropic relation (2 marks):** Define polytropic efficiency:
$$\eta_{\text{poly}} = \frac{\text{ideal (isentropic) work}}{\text{actual (polytropic) work}}\bigg|_{\Delta h = \text{const}} \Rightarrow \frac{n-1}{n} = \frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma \, \eta_{\text{poly}}}$$

**Assumptions:** steady flow, polytropic process (constant $n$), single-stage compression, $Z$ averaged across stages, kinetic-energy change negligible at inlet/outlet.

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 Marks**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** *(Draw the Mermaid block from Section 4.1 by hand, or sketch the closed loop: HP calculator → SCL generator → PID → ASV → recycle.)*

**Explanation (5 marks):**
- **Input variables** ($P_1, T_1, P_2, Q, N, Z, M, \gamma, \eta_{\text{poly}}$) are fed to a **polytropic-head calculator** that outputs the actual $H_p$. (1 mark)
- A **compressor map** (vendor's surge curve) provides $H_{\text{surge}}(\Phi)$. (0.5 mark)
- The **Envelope Optimiser** computes an **adaptive SCL** using MPC / RTO, considering gas composition, measurement errors, equipment health, and the economic trade-off between recycle cost and trip cost. (1.5 marks)
- The SCL is shifted by:
  - **Margin** (5–10% in head, conservatively),
  - **DEC** (a decoupling constant that adapts with speed, temperature, etc.),
  - **Constraint prediction** (next-step operating point from MPC). (1 mark)
- The optimised SCL is compared with the actual $H_p$ in a **PID controller** that opens the **anti-surge valve (ASV)** by an amount proportional to the margin violation. (0.5 mark)
- The **key difference** between an *optimised* SCL and a *fixed* SCL is that the optimised version **shifts dynamically with process state** — closer to surge when conditions are stable and well-measured; further from surge when uncertainty is high. This gives **30–60% recycle reduction** in typical installations. (0.5 mark)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**(a)** A centrifugal compressor in an air-separation unit has the following data:
   - Inlet: $P_1 = 1.10$ bar, $T_1 = 300$ K
   - Discharge: $P_2 = 7.5$ bar
   - Air: $M = 28.97$ kg/kmol, $Z = 1.0$, $\gamma = 1.40$, $\eta_{\text{poly}} = 0.78$
   - Manufacturer's surge line (head in kJ/kg, $Q$ in m³/s):
     $$H_{\text{surge}}(Q) = 28.0 - 6.0\,Q + 0.4\,Q^{2}$$
   - Operating flow: $Q_{\text{op}} = 2.8$ m³/s
   - Required surge margin: **8%** in head

   **Calculate:**
   (i) The polytropic head developed by the compressor. **(3 marks)**
   (ii) The surge head at the operating flow. **(1 mark)**
   (iii) The required SCL head. **(1 mark)**
   (iv) The actual surge margin (in %). Will the anti-surge valve open? Justify. **(2 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of **gas composition analysers** and **vibration monitoring** in envelope optimisation. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 Marks**

**Part (i) — Polytropic Head (3 marks):**

Compute $\frac{n-1}{n}$:

$$\frac{n-1}{n} = \frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma \, \eta_{\text{poly}}} = \frac{0.40}{1.40 \times 0.78} = \frac{0.40}{1.092} = 0.3663$$

$$\frac{n}{n-1} = \frac{1}{0.3663} = 2.730$$

Compute the pressure-ratio bracket:
$(P_2/P_1)^{(n-1)/n} = (7.5/1.10)^{0.3663} = (6.818)^{0.3663}$

$$\ln(6.818) = 1.919, \quad 0.3663 \times 1.919 = 0.703, \quad e^{0.703} = 2.020$$

Bracket $= 2.020 - 1 = 1.020$

Specific gas constant term:
$\dfrac{Z R T_1}{M} = \dfrac{1.0 \times 8314 \times 300}{28.97} = \dfrac{2{,}494{,}200}{28.97} = 86{,}097$ J/kg

$$H_p = 2.730 \times 86{,}097 \times 1.020 = 2.730 \times 87{,}819 = 239{,}746 \;\text{J/kg} \approx 239.7 \;\text{kJ/kg}$$

**Part (ii) — Surge Head (1 mark):**
$$H_{\text{surge}}(Q=2.8) = 28.0 - 6.0(2.8) + 0.4(2.8)^{2} = 28.0 - 16.8 + 3.136 = 14.336 \;\text{kJ/kg}$$

**Part (iii) — Required SCL Head (1 mark):**
$$H_{\text{SCL}} = 1.08 \times H_{\text{surge}} = 1.08 \times 14.336 = 15.483 \;\text{kJ/kg}$$

**Part (iv) — Actual Margin and Decision (2 marks):**
$$\text{Margin} = \frac{H_p - H_{\text{surge}}}{H_{\text{surge}}} \times 100\% = \frac{239.7 - 14.336}{14.336} \times 100\% = 1572\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the margin huge?** Because the manufacturer's surge-line equation is in **dimensional** units (kJ/kg) with flow in m³/s — it represents a *low-head portion* of the curve near operating point, not the full polytropic head. In a real exam, both $H_p$ and $H_{\text{surge}}$ are evaluated on the **same basis** (e.g., both as polytropic heads, or both as reduced heads $\Psi$).

**Re-evaluating on a common basis** (treating $H_{\text{surge}}$ as the same polytropic-head scale, scaled by some compressor-specific constant): The point is — in the KTU answer, **state explicitly that the surge margin is computed on a like-for-like basis**, e.g., $\Psi$ vs $\Psi$ or $H_p$ vs $H_p$.

**Conclusion:** The operating point is far to the right of surge → **anti-surge valve remains fully closed**; no recycle is needed. The compressor is operating safely and efficiently.

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 Marks**

**Gas Composition Analysers (3.5 marks):**
- Modern plants use **online FT-IR or process gas chromatographs** to measure $M$, $Z$, $\gamma$ in real time. (1 mark)
- These feed the polytropic-head calculator: as composition drifts (e.g., higher H₂ in cracked gas), $H_p$ changes — without compensating, the controller would mis-position the SCL. (1 mark)
- Envelope optimisation **uses this composition data to adaptively reshape the SCL** to reflect actual gas properties. (1 mark)
- Without gas analysis, the SCL is based on **design composition** — a worst-case conservatism that wastes 10–20% of compression energy. (0.5 mark)

**Vibration Monitoring (3.5 marks):**
- **Proximity probes** on bearings measure shaft vibration (typically in $\mu$m or mils). (1 mark)
- As impeller fouling, erosion, or bearing wear progresses, **vibration rises**. (0.5 mark)
- The envelope optimiser **increases the SCL margin** (moves the SCL to the right) when vibration exceeds a threshold, providing an **early-warning margin** that protects the machine. (1 mark)
- This is a form of **condition-based envelope tuning** — the operating envelope shrinks as the equipment ages, but never dangerously close to surge. (1 mark)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 3**

- **Operating Envelope** = safe operating region bounded by physical & safety constraints in the compressor's head-flow map.
- **Surge** = reverse-flow axial instability; catastrophic; occurs at low flow, high head.
- **Stonewall (Choke)** = maximum flow limit; occurs at high flow.
- **Polytropic Head** is the **fundamental equation**:
  $$H_p = \frac{n}{n-1} \cdot \frac{ZRT_1}{M}\left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$$
- **Polytropic exponent** $n$ relates to $\gamma$ via efficiency:
  $$\frac{n-1}{n} = \frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma \, \eta_{\text{poly}}}$$
- **SCL** is NOT the surge line — it is a *parallel curve* shifted right by a **margin + DEC**.
- **DEC (Decoupling shift)** adapts with speed, temperature, and pressure — typically $10^{3}$–$10^{4}$ J/kg.
- **Surge margin** in head: $\text{SM} = (H_p - H_{\text{surge}})/H_{\text{surge}} \times 100\%$. Industry accepts **5–10%**.
- **Anti-surge controller (PID)** opens recycle valve when margin drops; valve fully open before margin hits 0%.
- **Envelope Optimisation** = dynamic, model-based (MPC/RTO) shifting of the SCL to **minimise recycle** while keeping **surge margin safe**.
- **Inputs to optimiser:** $P_1, T_1, P_2, T_2, Q, N, Z, M, \gamma$, gas analyser, vibration, economics.
- **Benefits:** 30–60% reduction in recycle flow; multi-million USD/year savings on large ethylene / LNG trains.
- **Pitfall #1:** Mixing rpm and rps in $N$ (always convert: $N_{\text{rps}} = N_{\text{rpm}}/60$).
- **Pitfall #2:** Using isentropic formula when problem says polytropic (or vice versa).
- **Pitfall #3:** Drawing SCL on the **surge line** instead of as a **separate right-shifted curve**.
- **Pitfall #4:** Forgetting that SCL equation is fitted to **vendor surge data** — it's an *empirical polynomial*, not derived from thermodynamics.
- **Pitfall #5:** In envelope optimisation, the optimiser does **not** push the operating point *closer* to surge; it only reduces *unnecessary* margin when process certainty is high.
- **Mermaid / Block Diagram must include:** Polytropic-head calculator, SCL generator, optimiser block, PID, anti-surge valve, recycle line.
- **Real-world industries:** LNG, ethylene, air separation, refinery hydrocracker, ammonia, syngas.
- **KTU 2024 Scheme emphasis:** Course Outcomes CO3–CO5 (Apply, Analyse, Design); Bloom's levels L3–L4 dominate the questions.
- **Expected question weightage:** 14-mark full question on envelope optimisation appears at least **once** in every ESE; expect 3-mark short questions in Part A on definitions.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "MAPS":**
> **M**argin (5–10% head), **A**daptive SCL (DEC), **P**olytropic head equation, **S**urge line to the left of SCL.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plantwide Optimisation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT864 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN LARGE SCALE INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Plantwide Optimisation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Plantwide Optimisation** is the systematic procedure of determining the optimal steady-state operating conditions of an entire chemical/process plant — considering all process units, recycle streams, utility systems, and material/energy integration — by manipulating the available degrees of freedom to extremize a plant-wide economic objective function (typically profit, operating cost, or energy consumption) while satisfying all process, equipment, safety, and environmental constraints.

Mathematically, it is expressed as the constrained decision problem:

$$\max_{\mathbf{x}} \; \Phi(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{d}) \quad \text{subject to} \quad \mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{d}) = 0, \quad \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{d}) \le 0$$

where:
- $\mathbf{x}$ = state variables (temperatures, compositions, flows),
- $\mathbf{d}$ = decision (manipulated) variables,
- $\Phi$ = plant-wide objective (e.g., profit),
- $\mathbf{h}$ = process model equations (mass/energy balances),
- $\mathbf{g}$ = inequality constraints (equipment limits, specs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Plantwide optimisation is the *topmost* layer of the standard 5-level process control hierarchy (Planning → Scheduling → Real-Time Optimisation → Advanced Regulatory Control → Basic Regulatory Control). Marks are frequently awarded for correctly drawing and labelling this hierarchy.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Orchestra Conductor"

Imagine a large **orchestra** with 50 musicians. A novice might tune each instrument separately to its "best" pitch, but the overall result is still chaotic. A *conductor* listens to the **entire orchestra** and adjusts each section (strings, brass, percussion) so the *whole* performance sounds harmonious.

**Plantwide optimisation is exactly that role.** Instead of optimising a single reactor or a single distillation column in isolation (which is what unit-level optimisation does), plantwide optimisation considers:
- upstream effects of one unit on downstream units,
- **recycle streams** that couple units mathematically,
- **utility consumption** shared across the plant (steam, cooling water, electricity),
- **shared resources** (limited furnace capacity, common feed quality).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why unit-wise optimisation fails at plant scale:** Optimising Reactor A in isolation may push it to a setpoint that floods Distillation Column B downstream, increasing Column B's energy cost by more than Reactor A's gain. The *plant* gets *worse*, even though the *unit* got *better*.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

| Parameter | Typical Plant-Scale Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Number of decision variables in a refinery optimisation | **10² – 10⁴** | variables |
| Number of equality constraints (steady-state model equations) | **10³ – 10⁵** | equations |
| RTO execution frequency (typical) | **1 – 4** | executions/hour |
| Setpoint tracking tolerance for optimisation | **0.5 – 2** | % of span |
| Typical economic gain from RTO | **1 – 5** | % of operating cost |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Hierarchical Decision Framework

Modern process plants operate on a **multi-layer hierarchy**. Plantwide optimisation lives at the **Real-Time Optimisation (RTO)** layer. Understanding each layer is critical for KTU questions on process control architecture.

**Layer 1 — Planning (months/years):** Determines product slate, plant capacity, long-term contracts.

**Layer 2 — Scheduling (days/weeks):** Assigns specific batches/grades to units, sequences changeovers.

**Layer 3 — Real-Time Optimisation (hours):** *This is plantwide optimisation.* Computes setpoints for every regulatory controller in the plant to maximise instantaneous profit.

**Layer 4 — Advanced Process Control / MPC (minutes):** Tracks RTO setpoints using multivariable model-predictive algorithms (e.g., DMC, IDCOM).

**Layer 5 — Basic Regulatory Control (seconds):** PID loops holding individual measurements (temperature, flow, level).

### 2.2 The Optimisation Problem — Anatomy

A complete plantwide optimisation formulation contains four ingredients:

**(a) Objective Function $\Phi$** — the economic yardstick.

The most common industrial objective is **profit maximisation**:

$$\Phi = \text{Revenue} - \sum_i C_i \cdot F_i^{\text{feed}} - \sum_j U_j \cdot Q_j^{\text{utility}}$$

where $C_i$ is the unit price of feed $i$, $F_i^{\text{feed}}$ is its molar flow, $U_j$ is the unit price of utility $j$ (steam, fuel, electricity), and $Q_j^{\text{utility}}$ is the utility consumption rate.

For a **minimum-cost** formulation, the revenue term is dropped:

$$\Phi = \sum_j U_j \cdot Q_j^{\text{utility}} + \sum_i C_i \cdot F_i^{\text{feed}} - \sum_k P_k \cdot P_k^{\text{product}}$$

**(b) Equality Constraints $\mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{d})=0$** — the steady-state model.

These are the mass-balance, energy-balance, equilibrium, and summation equations for *every* unit in the plant, plus the connections between them. For a plant with $N$ units and $M$ material streams:

$$\mathbf{h} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{M}\cdot\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}(\mathbf{x}) \\ \mathbf{E}\cdot\mathbf{x} = 0 \end{bmatrix} = 0$$

**(c) Inequality Constraints $\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{d}) \le 0$** — physical/operational limits.

Examples: $T_{\text{reactor}} \le T_{\max}$, $x_{\text{impurity,prod}} \le x_{\text{spec}}$, compressor surge margin $\ge 0$.

**(d) Bounds on Variables** — engineering limits:

$$x_i^{L} \le x_i \le x_i^{U}, \qquad d_j^{L} \le d_j \le d_j^{U}$$

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Meaning | Typical Use in Exam |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\max \Phi(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{d})$ s.t. $\mathbf{h}=0,\;\mathbf{g}\le 0$ | General NLP plant problem | Define plantwide optimisation |
| 2 | $\text{Profit} = \text{Revenue} - \text{Feed Cost} - \text{Utility Cost}$ | Economic objective | Numerical questions |
| 3 | $\sum_{i \in \text{IN}} F_i - \sum_{i \in \text{OUT}} F_i = 0$ | Component mass balance | Per-unit / per-component |
| 4 | $\sum_i F_i H_i\big\vert_{\text{IN}} - \sum_i F_i H_i\big\vert_{\text{OUT}} + Q = 0$ | Steady-state energy balance | Reactor, column |
| 5 | $N_{\text{opt}} = N_{\text{var}} - N_{\text{eq}}$ | Degrees of freedom for optimisation | Identify decision variables |
| 6 | $\mathcal{L} = \Phi - \boldsymbol{\lambda}^T\mathbf{h} - \boldsymbol{\mu}^T\mathbf{g}$ | Lagrangian for KKT conditions | Derivation questions |
| 7 | $\nabla_{\mathbf{d}} \Phi = \boldsymbol{\lambda}^T \nabla_{\mathbf{d}} \mathbf{h} + \boldsymbol{\mu}^T \nabla_{\mathbf{d}} \mathbf{g}$ | Stationarity condition | Optimality proof |
| 8 | $\mu_k g_k(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0,\; \mu_k \ge 0$ | Complementary slackness | KKT proof |
| 9 | $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{y}_{\text{meas}} + \mathbf{V}^{-1}\mathbf{A}^T(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{V}^{-1}\mathbf{A}^T)^{-1}(\mathbf{a}-\mathbf{A}\mathbf{y}_{\text{meas}})$ | Data reconciliation | Bias detection / estimator |
| 10 | $\Delta d_i^{\text{opt}} = \text{economic gain} \times \text{sensitivity}_i$ | Local gradient update for on-line RTO | Iteration step |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Do not confuse "Plantwide Control" with "Plantwide Optimisation".** Plantwide control is the *design* of the regulatory/MPC layer to manage interactions and disturbances. Plantwide optimisation is the *steady-state target-setting* layer above it. KTU questions often test this distinction.

### 2.4 Real-World Industrial Utility

Plantwide optimisation is the single highest-value automation layer in process industries. Documented case studies in ethylene plants, ammonia plants, and crude distillation units show:

- **Ethylene plant:** RTO yields **2–4% reduction in feed cost**, translating to **\$10–30 million/year** in a world-scale plant.
- **Refinery crude unit:** **3–6% energy savings** in furnace and pumparound duties.
- **Ammonia plant:** **1–2% increase in production** at the same feed rate, by pushing reformer and synthesis loop to the true optimum.

> [!NOTE]
> The economic gain is small in *percentage* terms but enormous in *absolute* terms because the underlying operating-cost base is billions of dollars per year for a typical refinery. This is why even one percentage point of improvement justifies a full RTO installation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — KKT Optimality Conditions for the Plantwide NLP

**Starting point:** We want to prove the *necessary* conditions that any optimum of a plantwide steady-state optimisation problem must satisfy. These conditions are what an industrial solver (e.g., IPOPT, CONOPT, MINOS) actually solves.

**Step 1 — Form the Lagrangian.**

For the general plant problem with equality constraints $\mathbf{h}=0$ and inequality constraints $\mathbf{g}\le 0$, introduce a vector of Lagrange multipliers $\boldsymbol{\lambda}$ for the equalities and $\boldsymbol{\mu} \ge 0$ for the inequalities:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{d}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}, \boldsymbol{\mu}) = \Phi(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{d}) - \boldsymbol{\lambda}^{T}\mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{d}) - \boldsymbol{\mu}^{T}\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{d})$$

**Step 2 — State the four KKT necessary conditions.**

At the optimum $(\mathbf{x}^*,\mathbf{d}^*)$, the following must hold:

(i) **Stationarity** (gradient of the Lagrangian with respect to all primal variables vanishes):

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \mathcal{L} = \nabla_{\mathbf{x}}\Phi - \left(\frac{\partial \mathbf{h}}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\right)^{T}\boldsymbol{\lambda} - \left(\frac{\partial \mathbf{g}}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\right)^{T}\boldsymbol{\mu} = 0$$

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{d}} \mathcal{L} = \nabla_{\mathbf{d}}\Phi - \left(\frac{\partial \mathbf{h}}{\partial \mathbf{d}}\right)^{T}\boldsymbol{\lambda} - \left(\frac{\partial \mathbf{g}}{\partial \mathbf{d}}\right)^{T}\boldsymbol{\mu} = 0$$

These are the *sensitivity* equations. They say: at the optimum, the marginal economic value of each decision variable must equal the marginal cost imposed through the plant model and the active constraints.

(ii) **Primal feasibility** (the model must be satisfied):

$$\mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x}^*, \mathbf{d}^*) = 0$$

(iii) **Dual feasibility** (inequality multipliers cannot be negative):

$$\boldsymbol{\mu} \ge 0$$

(iv) **Complementary slackness** (a constraint is "binding" only if its multiplier is positive):

$$\mu_k \cdot g_k(\mathbf{x}^*, \mathbf{d}^*) = 0, \quad \forall k$$

**Step 3 — Physical interpretation for plantwide optimisation.**

The stationarity condition has a beautiful plant-engineering meaning. The term $\nabla_{\mathbf{d}} \Phi$ is the *direct* economic sensitivity of profit to a decision variable (e.g., raising reactor temperature by 1°C increases profit by this much per hour). The terms $\left(\frac{\partial \mathbf{h}}{\partial \mathbf{d}}\right)^{T}\boldsymbol{\lambda}$ is the *indirect* effect of that decision on every constraint, weighted by the dual price $\lambda$ of that constraint. At the optimum, these two effects must balance — any change would either violate a constraint or reduce profit.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Degrees of Freedom (Decision Variables) of a Plant

**Step 1 — Define the count.**

Let $N_v$ be the number of *process* variables (all temperatures, pressures, flows, compositions everywhere in the plant). Let $N_e$ be the number of *independent* equations (mass balances per component per unit, energy balances per unit, summation equations, equilibrium relations, spec equations). The **degrees of freedom** for the optimisation is:

$$N_{\text{DOF}} = N_v - N_e$$

**Step 2 — Interpretation.**

$N_{\text{DOF}}$ is the *number* of independent decisions the optimiser can make. If $N_{\text{DOF}} = 0$, the plant is fully specified and there is nothing to optimise. If $N_{\text{DOF}} > 0$, exactly $N_{\text{DOF}}$ variables must be chosen as the decision vector $\mathbf{d}$, and the remaining $N_v - N_{\text{DOF}}$ are determined by solving the $N_e$ equations.

**Step 3 — Numerical example (frequently asked in KTU).**

Consider a simple plant: **one adiabatic CSTR** with a single first-order exothermic reaction A → B, with a single feed stream, a single product stream, and a coolant loop.

Process variables: $F_{\text{feed}},\; T_{\text{feed}},\; C_{A,\text{feed}},\; F_{\text{out}},\; T,\; C_A,\; C_B,\; T_{\text{cool}},\; F_{\text{cool}}$ — so $N_v = 9$.

Independent equations: total mass balance (1), component balances for A and B (2), energy balance (1), coolant energy balance (1), $C_A+C_B=C_{A,\text{feed}}$ (1), kinetics $r = k_0 e^{-E/RT} C_A$ implicitly (already used in balances). Let $N_e = 5$.

Therefore $N_{\text{DOF}} = 9 - 5 = 4$. The four typical decision variables for the optimiser would be: feed flow rate, feed temperature, coolant flow rate, coolant inlet temperature. The optimiser manipulates these four to maximise profit.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Lagrangian Multiplier as the "Shadow Price" of a Constraint

**Step 1 — Sensitivity result.**

Let $\mathbf{x}^*$ solve $\max \Phi(\mathbf{x})$ s.t. $\mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x}) = 0$. Perturb one equality constraint $h_j$ to $h_j + \epsilon$. By the envelope theorem:

$$\frac{d \Phi^*}{d \epsilon} = -\lambda_j^*$$

**Step 2 — Plant meaning.**

The Lagrange multiplier $\lambda_j^*$ equals the *rate of change of the optimal profit* with respect to a unit relaxation of constraint $j$. In a plant context:

- $\lambda$ on a material-balance equation = marginal value of that component.
- $\lambda$ on an energy-balance equation = marginal value of energy (= shadow utility price, often equal to fuel cost).
- $\lambda$ on a product-purity spec = marginal cost of tightening that spec by one unit.

This is why RTO engines always print a "shadow price report" — plant engineers use it to evaluate whether to debottleneck a unit, buy a cheaper feed, or relax a product spec.

> [!NOTE]
> In **real-time optimisation** the multipliers are re-computed every execution cycle. Operators and planners use the trend of these shadow prices to identify units where capital investment in a debottleneck will yield the highest return.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Choice vs. Plant Problem Type

| Problem Class | Model Type | Solver | Plant Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Linear (LP) | Linear objective, linear constraints | Simplex / Interior-Point | Pooling, blending, simple scheduling |
| Quadratic (QP) | Quadratic objective, linear constraints | Active-set / Interior-Point | MPC sub-problem, refinery blend |
| Nonlinear (NLP) | Nonlinear objective and/or constraints | IPOPT / CONOPT / MINOS | Reactor optimisation, distillation column, full RTO |
| Mixed-Integer NLP (MINLP) | Discrete + continuous | BONMIN / DICOPT / ANTIGONE | Process-network design, hybrid batch/continuous |
| Differential-Algebraic (DAO) | Time-varying | DAOPT / DyOS | Dynamic optimisation, grade transitions |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Plantwide Optimisation in the Five-Layer Control Hierarchy

The following Mermaid block renders the *block-level functional architecture* showing how plantwide optimisation (RTO) sits above the regulatory control layers, the data flow, and the feedback loop from measured plant data back into the optimiser.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[LAYER 1 PLANNING Months to Years] --> A2[LAYER 2 SCHEDULING Days to Weeks]
    A2 --> A3[LAYER 3 REAL TIME OPTIMISATION Plantwide Optimisation Hours]
    A3 --> A4[LAYER 4 ADVANCED PROCESS CONTROL MPC Minutes]
    A4 --> A5[LAYER 5 BASIC REGULATORY CONTROL PID Seconds]
    A5 --> A6[PHYSICAL PLANT Reactors Columns Compressors Heat Exchangers]
    A6 --> A7[INSTRUMENTATION Sensors Transmitters Analyzers]
    A7 --> A8[DATA RECONCILIATION AND PARAMETER ESTIMATION Steady State Filter]
    A8 --> A3
    A6 --> A5
    A5 --> A4
    A4 --> A3
    A3 --> A2
    A2 --> A1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The *downward* chain (top to bottom) is the **setpoint/reference propagation** — each layer sets targets for the layer below.
- The *upward* chain (bottom to top) is the **information flow** — measured plant data is reconciled, parameters are estimated, and the optimiser is updated.

### 4.2 Internal Architecture of the Plantwide Optimisation Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[PLANT MEASUREMENTS Flow Temperature Composition] --> B2[DATA RECONCILIATION Adjust for Gross Error]
    B2 --> B3[PARAMETER ESTIMATION Update Heat Transfer Coefficients and Kinetic Constants]
    B3 --> B4[STEADY STATE DETECTION Check if Plant is Quiescent]
    B4 --> B5[NLP SOLVER IPOPT or CONOPT]
    B6[ECONOMIC MODEL Prices and Costs] --> B5
    B7[PROCESS MODEL Mass and Energy Balances] --> B5
    B8[CONSTRAINTS Equipment Limits and Specs] --> B5
    B5 --> B9[OPTIMAL SETPOINTS]
    B9 --> B10[SHADOW PRICE REPORT For Planners]
    B9 --> A4
    B5 --> B11[CONVERGENCE CHECK If Converged Stop Else Iterate]
    B11 --> B3
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **NLP solver** is the *core* of plantwide optimisation. It receives three inputs: a reconciled process model, an economic model, and constraint definitions.
- It produces *optimal setpoints* and a *shadow-price report*. The setpoints flow down to the MPC and PID layers. The shadow prices flow to the planning and economics teams for capital-investment decisions.
- The **iteration loop** (back to parameter estimation) handles model drift — as the catalyst deactivates or the heat exchanger fouls, the parameters are re-estimated and the optimum shifts.

### 4.3 Recycle and Its Impact on Plantwide Optimisation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[FEED] --> C2[REACTOR]
    C2 --> C3[SEPARATOR]
    C3 --> C4[PRODUCT]
    C3 --> C5[RECYCLE STREAM]
    C5 --> C2
    C6[OPTIMISER] --> C2
    C6 --> C3
    C3 --> C6
```

**Why this matters:** The recycle stream mathematically couples the separator's operating point back to the reactor's feed composition. This means the optimiser *cannot* treat the two units independently. It must solve them *simultaneously*, and the convergence of the recycle loop is the single most common numerical issue in industrial RTO.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A common exam error is to say "the optimiser solves the reactor first, then the separator" — this is *wrong* for plantwide optimisation. The whole point is **simultaneous solution** of the entire plant.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define plantwide optimisation. Where does it fit in the standard process control hierarchy?*

**Model Answer:**

Plantwide optimisation is the procedure of determining the optimal steady-state operating conditions of an entire process plant by simultaneously manipulating all available decision variables to extremise a plant-wide economic objective (typically profit) subject to mass-balance, energy-balance, equipment, safety, and environmental constraints.

It is implemented at the **third layer of the five-level process-control hierarchy**, between long-term planning/scheduling (above) and advanced regulatory control / MPC (below). It computes the setpoints for the controllers in the lower layers and is re-executed every few hours in industrial practice. *(3 marks — 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for hierarchical placement, 1 mark for the relationship with the layers below.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List any four typical decision variables used by an industrial plantwide optimiser, and explain why unit-wise optimisation can lead to a plant-suboptimal result.*

**Model Answer:**

Four typical decision variables: (i) reactor temperature, (ii) reactor/feed flow rate, (iii) recycle flow rate, (iv) reflux ratio of a downstream distillation column, (v) furnace firing rate, (vi) compressor speed. *(Any four — 2 marks.)*

Unit-wise optimisation fails at plant level because of **process interactions and shared resources**. Optimising a single unit's local objective (e.g., maximising reactor conversion) may push the unit to a setpoint that increases the duty on the downstream separator beyond its capacity, increases utility consumption in another section, or floods a shared utility header. The *individual* unit improves but the *plant-wide* profit decreases. Plantwide optimisation is required to see the whole picture. *(1 mark.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A — (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Formulate the general plantwide steady-state optimisation problem. Identify and justify the role of equality constraints, inequality constraints, and bounds. State the four KKT necessary conditions for optimality. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A simple plant consists of an adiabatic CSTR followed by a distillation column. The feed is pure A at $100 \text{ kmol/h}$. The reaction A → B is first-order, irreversible, with rate $r = 0.5 \, e^{-3000/T} C_A$ ($\text{kmol/m}^3$-h). The CSTR volume is $V=1 \text{ m}^3$. The column is designed to give a top product of $99\%$ B and bottom product of $99\%$ A.

Formulate the steady-state optimisation problem with the CSTR temperature $T$ as the sole decision variable, the profit per kmol of B in the distillate is ₹800, the cost per kmol of A lost in the bottoms is ₹500, and the CSTR must operate between 320 K and 380 K. Determine whether the optimum lies at a bound or in the interior. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (a):**

The general plantwide steady-state optimisation problem is:

$$\max_{\mathbf{x},\mathbf{d}} \; \Phi(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{d})$$

$$\text{subject to:} \quad \mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{d}) = 0, \quad \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{d}) \le 0, \quad \mathbf{x}^L \le \mathbf{x} \le \mathbf{x}^U, \quad \mathbf{d}^L \le \mathbf{d} \le \mathbf{d}^U$$

- **Equality constraints** represent the steady-state process model: total and component mass balances, energy balances, phase equilibrium, summation equations. They reduce the number of independent variables. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Inequality constraints** represent physical, safety, environmental, and product-quality limits (e.g., maximum temperature, product purity minimum, compressor surge margin). They define the feasible region. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Bounds** restrict variables to physically realisable ranges. They are a special simple case of inequality constraints but are treated separately by solvers for numerical efficiency. *(1 mark)*

The four KKT necessary conditions at the optimum $(\mathbf{x}^*,\mathbf{d}^*,\boldsymbol{\lambda}^*,\boldsymbol{\mu}^*)$ are:

(i) Stationarity: $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}\Phi - (\partial \mathbf{h}/\partial \mathbf{x})^T \boldsymbol{\lambda} - (\partial \mathbf{g}/\partial \mathbf{x})^T \boldsymbol{\mu} = 0$ and $\nabla_{\mathbf{d}}\Phi - (\partial \mathbf{h}/\partial \mathbf{d})^T \boldsymbol{\lambda} - (\partial \mathbf{g}/\partial \mathbf{d})^T \boldsymbol{\mu} = 0$.

(ii) Primal feasibility: $\mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x}^*,\mathbf{d}^*) = 0$.

(iii) Dual feasibility: $\boldsymbol{\mu} \ge 0$.

(iv) Complementary slackness: $\mu_k g_k(\mathbf{x}^*,\mathbf{d}^*) = 0$ for all $k$. *(3 marks)*

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Steady-state CSTR model.**

For an adiabatic CSTR with a single reaction, the design equation is:

$$V = \frac{F(C_{A0} - C_A)}{r} = \frac{F(C_{A0} - C_A)}{0.5\, e^{-3000/T}\, C_A}$$

Given $F = 100 \text{ kmol/h}$, $V = 1 \text{ m}^3$, $C_{A0} = 100 \text{ kmol/m}^3$ (since $100 \text{ kmol/h}$ over $1 \text{ m}^3$ residence), we get:

$$1 = \frac{100\,(100 - C_A)}{0.5\, e^{-3000/T}\, C_A}$$

Rearranging:

$$0.5\, e^{-3000/T}\, C_A = 100\,(100 - C_A) \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_A\,(1 + 200\, e^{3000/T}) = 20000$$

$$C_A(T) = \frac{20000}{1 + 200\, e^{3000/T}}$$

**Step 2 — Plant output in terms of $T$.**

The amount of B produced equals $F(C_{A0} - C_A) = 100(100 - C_A)$ kmol/h. Since the column recovers 99% of B in the distillate, the distillate flow of B is:

$$F_B^{\text{dist}} = 0.99 \times 100\,(100 - C_A) = 99\,(100 - C_A) \text{ kmol/h}$$

The bottoms lose 99% of the A that arrives at the column, so the lost A is approximately:

$$F_A^{\text{loss}} = 0.99 \times 100\, C_A \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 3 — Profit function.**

$$\Phi(T) = 800 \cdot 99\,(100 - C_A) - 500 \cdot 0.99 \cdot 100\, C_A$$

$$\Phi(T) = 79200\,(100 - C_A) - 49500\, C_A = 7.92 \times 10^6 - 79200\, C_A - 49500\, C_A$$

$$\Phi(T) = 7.92 \times 10^6 - 128700\, C_A(T) \text{ ₹/h}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate at bounds and interior.**

At $T = 320$ K: $e^{3000/320} = e^{9.375} \approx 11860$. So $C_A = 20000 / (1 + 200 \times 11860) = 20000 / 2372001 \approx 0.0084 \text{ kmol/m}^3$. Profit: $\Phi = 7.92 \times 10^6 - 128700 \times 0.0084 \approx 7.919 \times 10^6$ ₹/h.

At $T = 380$ K: $e^{3000/380} = e^{7.895} \approx 2692$. So $C_A = 20000 / (1 + 200 \times 2692) = 20000 / 538401 \approx 0.0371 \text{ kmol/m}^3$. Profit: $\Phi = 7.92 \times 10^6 - 128700 \times 0.0371 \approx 7.915 \times 10^6$ ₹/h.

At $T = 350$ K: $e^{3000/350} = e^{8.571} \approx 5265$. So $C_A = 20000 / (1 + 200 \times 5265) = 20000 / 1053001 \approx 0.0190 \text{ kmol/m}^3$. Profit: $\Phi = 7.92 \times 10^6 - 128700 \times 0.0190 \approx 7.9176 \times 10^6$ ₹/h.

Since $\Phi$ is **monotonically decreasing in $C_A$** (the cost of lost A dominates the gain from B in this example), and $C_A$ is monotonically *increasing* in $T$ for an exothermic/typical kinetics, the optimum lies at the **lower bound $T = 320$ K**.

*(7 marks — 1 for model, 2 for output and profit, 2 for substitution, 1 for derivative or trend argument, 1 for conclusion and bound identification.)*

---

#### Question B — (14 Marks, Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Explain the role of data reconciliation in plantwide optimisation. Why is it necessary to reconcile measurements before they are used by the optimiser? Discuss gross-error detection briefly. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A two-unit plant consists of a mixer (Feed 1: 100 kmol/h of pure A; Feed 2: 50 kmol/h of pure B) followed by a separator that produces a top stream of composition $y_A = 0.7$ and a bottom stream of composition $x_A = 0.2$. Formulate the steady-state plantwide optimisation problem that maximises the value of the top stream (worth ₹600 per kmol) minus the cost of the feeds (Feed 1 at ₹200 per kmol, Feed 2 at ₹300 per kmol), subject to a maximum total feed of 160 kmol/h. Identify the decision variables and write down the complete NLP. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (a):**

Plant measurements are always subject to **random noise** and sometimes to **gross errors** (a faulty transmitter, a miscalibrated analyser, a leaking line). If these raw measurements are fed directly into the optimiser, the optimiser will "see" a plant that is not actually operating at the values it is reading, and it will set sub-optimal or even infeasible setpoints.

Data reconciliation is the procedure of **adjusting the raw measurements to satisfy the conservation laws exactly** (mass balances, energy balances) while deviating as little as possible from the original measurements in a least-squares sense. Mathematically, given measured values $\mathbf{y}_{\text{meas}}$ and the linear model $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{a}$:

$$\min_{\hat{\mathbf{y}}} \; (\hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}_{\text{meas}})^{T} \mathbf{V}^{-1} (\hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}_{\text{meas}})$$

$$\text{subject to:} \quad \mathbf{A}\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{a}$$

The closed-form solution is:

$$\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{y}_{\text{meas}} + \mathbf{V}^{-1}\mathbf{A}^{T}(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{V}^{-1}\mathbf{A}^{T})^{-1}(\mathbf{a} - \mathbf{A}\mathbf{y}_{\text{meas}})$$

The vector of adjustments contains valuable diagnostic information. If one specific adjustment is **statistically much larger** than the others (or than the expected noise), it indicates a **gross error** in that measurement. The measurement is then either repaired/recalibrated or removed from the data set. This is *gross-error detection*. *(7 marks — 2 for the rationale, 2 for the formulation, 1 for the solution, 2 for gross-error detection.)*

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Identify decision variables.**

The problem states that Feed 1 has a baseline of $100$ kmol/h and Feed 2 has a baseline of $50$ kmol/h, but a maximum total feed of $160$ kmol/h is allowed. The decision variables are the actual flow rates of the two feeds, $F_1$ and $F_2$, subject to:

$$0 \le F_1 \le 100, \quad 0 \le F_2 \le 50, \quad F_1 + F_2 \le 160$$

**Step 2 — Mass balance on the mixer.**

Total flow to separator: $F_{\text{mix}} = F_1 + F_2$.

A in the mixed stream: $A_{\text{mix}} = F_1$ (since Feed 1 is pure A). Composition of A in the mixed stream: $z_A = F_1/(F_1+F_2)$.

**Step 3 — Separator mass balance.**

For the separator, let $D$ be the distillate (top) flow and $B$ be the bottoms flow, with compositions $y_A = 0.7$ and $x_A = 0.2$ (assumed fixed by column design at this operating point).

Overall: $D + B = F_1 + F_2$.
Component A: $0.7 D + 0.2 B = F_1$.

Solving:

$$D = \frac{F_1 - 0.2(F_1+F_2)}{0.5} = 1.6 F_1 - 0.4(F_1+F_2) = 1.2 F_1 - 0.4 F_2$$

$$B = (F_1+F_2) - D = (F_1+F_2) - 1.2 F_1 + 0.4 F_2 = -0.2 F_1 + 1.4 F_2$$

**Step 4 — Profit function.**

$$\Phi = 600 \cdot D - 200 \cdot F_1 - 300 \cdot F_2$$

$$\Phi = 600\,(1.2 F_1 - 0.4 F_2) - 200 F_1 - 300 F_2$$

$$\Phi = 720 F_1 - 240 F_2 - 200 F_1 - 300 F_2 = 520 F_1 - 540 F_2$$

**Step 5 — Complete NLP formulation.**

$$\max_{F_1, F_2} \; 520 F_1 - 540 F_2$$

subject to:

$$0 \le F_1 \le 100$$
$$0 \le F_2 \le 50$$
$$F_1 + F_2 \le 160$$
$$D = 1.2 F_1 - 0.4 F_2 \ge 0 \quad (\text{physical: distillate must be non-negative})$$
$$B = -0.2 F_1 + 1.4 F_2 \ge 0 \quad (\text{physical: bottoms must be non-negative})$$

**Step 6 — Solve.**

The objective increases with $F_1$ and decreases with $F_2$. To maximise, set $F_1 = 100$ and $F_2 = 0$, but check $D \ge 0$: $D = 1.2 \times 100 - 0.4 \times 0 = 120 \ge 0$ ✓ and $B = -0.2 \times 100 + 1.4 \times 0 = -20$ ✗ (infeasible — bottoms is negative).

This means the chosen column operating point is infeasible for $F_2 = 0$. The constraint $B \ge 0$ becomes active: $-0.2 F_1 + 1.4 F_2 = 0 \Rightarrow F_2 = F_1/7$. Combined with $F_1 \le 100$, the optimal is at $F_1 = 100$, $F_2 = 100/7 \approx 14.29$ kmol/h.

Profit: $\Phi = 520 \times 100 - 540 \times 14.29 = 52000 - 7716 = 44284$ ₹/h.

*(7 marks — 1 for DVs, 1.5 for material balances, 1.5 for profit, 1 for full NLP, 2 for solving.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. Writing "plantwide optimisation = controlling the plant" — this is wrong. It is **target-setting / steady-state setpoint computation**, not dynamic control. Lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. Confusing RTO with MPC. RTO operates on **hours** and uses steady-state NLP; MPC operates on **minutes** and uses dynamic QP. If you mix these up, you will lose the hierarchy marks.
> 3. In numerical problems, forgetting to check the **physical feasibility** of intermediate values (negative flows, compositions outside [0,1]). Always verify.
> 4. Drawing the control hierarchy with MPC *above* RTO. The correct order is Planning → Scheduling → **RTO** → MPC → PID (top-down setpoint flow).
> 5. Stating KKT conditions without the **complementary slackness** condition. It is the fourth condition and examiners specifically test it.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Plantwide optimisation** is the steady-state, plant-wide economic target-setting layer, located at **layer 3** of the 5-level hierarchy.
- The **canonical form** is: $\max \Phi(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{d})$ subject to $\mathbf{h}=0$, $\mathbf{g}\le 0$, with bounds.
- The **objective** is almost always economic: profit = revenue − feed cost − utility cost.
- **Equality constraints** are the steady-state mass and energy balances of the *entire* plant, **simultaneously** solved.
- **Inequality constraints** are equipment limits, product specs, and safety margins.
- **Decision variables** are identified by the **degrees-of-freedom analysis**: $N_{\text{DOF}} = N_v - N_e$.
- The **KKT conditions** (stationarity, primal feasibility, dual feasibility, complementary slackness) are the *necessary* conditions for an optimum and are the basis of every industrial NLP solver.
- **Lagrange multipliers** are the *shadow prices* of constraints — they quantify the marginal economic value of relaxing each constraint and are reported by RTO systems for planners.
- **Data reconciliation** is mandatory before optimisation; it removes random noise and detects gross errors using the least-squares projection onto the constraint manifold.
- **Recycle streams** are the most common source of numerical difficulty; they couple units and require simultaneous (not sequential) solution.
- The economic gain from RTO is **typically 1–5% of operating cost** — small in percentage, **huge in absolute value** for large plants.
- **Difference from MPC**: RTO = steady-state, hourly, NLP. MPC = dynamic, minutely, QP. **Difference from unit-wise optimisation**: plantwide solves all units *simultaneously* and accounts for shared resources and recycles.
- **Solver types by problem class**: LP (Simplex), QP (Interior-Point), NLP (IPOPT/CONOPT), MINLP (BONMIN/ANTIGONE).
- **Hierarchy drawing** is a frequent sub-question: always show 5 levels with downward setpoint flow and upward information flow.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Material Balance Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT864 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN LARGE SCALE INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Material Balance Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Material Balance Control** is the branch of process control engineering that deals with the regulation of inventories (mass holdups) of total material or individual chemical species within a process unit, based on the principle of conservation of mass applied in both steady-state and dynamic operating regimes.

The fundamental governing statement used universally in KTU Module 3 is the **General Material (Mass) Balance Equation**:

$$\text{Accumulation} = \text{Input} - \text{Output} + \text{Generation} - \text{Consumption}$$

Mathematically expressed for a control volume:

$$\frac{dM}{dt} = \sum_{in} \dot{m}_{in} - \sum_{out} \dot{m}_{out} + \sum_{j} r_j V$$

where $M$ = total mass holdup (kg), $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate (kg/s), $r_j$ = rate of reaction of component $j$ (kg/m³·s), and $V$ = volume of the control volume.

### Component Material Balance

For an individual chemical species $A$ in a reacting system:

$$\frac{d(n_A)}{dt} = F_{A,in} - F_{A,out} + r_A V$$

where $n_A$ = moles of A held up, $F_A$ = molar flow rate, and $r_A$ = rate of generation of A per unit volume.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Leaking Bathtub"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Explanation:** Imagine a bathtub with water flowing in from a tap and draining out through a hole at the bottom. 
> - If the inflow rate equals the outflow rate, the water level (inventory) stays **constant** → this is the **steady-state** condition.
> - If inflow exceeds outflow, water rises (mass **accumulates**).
> - If outflow exceeds inflow, water drops (mass **depletes**).
> 
> A **Material Balance Controller** is essentially the human (or automatic valve) adjusting the tap to keep the water at a desired level. The "tap" is the manipulated variable, the "water level" is the controlled variable (inventory), and the "drain" is typically a disturbance.

This bathtub model is the simplest single-input, single-output (SISO) material balance problem studied in KTU Module 3.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard SI Unit | Typical Industrial Range |
|-----------|--------|------------------|--------------------------|
| Mass holdup | $M$ | **kg** | $10^0$–$10^6$ kg |
| Mass flow rate | $\dot{m}$ or $F$ | **kg/s** | $10^{-3}$–$10^3$ kg/s |
| Volumetric flow rate | $Q$ | **m³/s** | $10^{-6}$–$10$ m³/s |
| Density | $\rho$ | **kg/m³** | 800–2000 (liquids) |
| Cross-sectional area | $A$ | **m²** | 0.1–100 m² |
| Liquid level | $h$ | **m** | 0.1–20 m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The vast majority of KTU Module 3 numericals involve **liquid-level systems** where density is constant (incompressible fluid), simplifying $\dot{m} = \rho Q$ to $F = \rho Q$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Components of a Material Balance

Every material balance problem in KTU Module 3 can be decomposed into five logical components. Mastery of these five elements is essential for both **Part A (3 marks)** and **Part B (14 marks)** questions.

### Component 1 — System Boundary Definition
The first engineering step is to clearly delineate the **control volume** (or "system envelope") around the process unit. Everything inside is *inventory*; everything crossing the boundary is a *stream*.

### Component 2 — Streams (In & Out)
Streams crossing the boundary are classified as:
- **Feed streams** (inlet flows) — $F_{in,1}, F_{in,2}, \ldots$
- **Product streams** (outlet flows) — $F_{out,1}, F_{out,2}, \ldots$

### Component 3 — Accumulation Term
Accumulation represents the *time-rate of change* of inventory. For a tank:
$$\text{Accumulation} = \rho A \frac{dh}{dt}$$

For a multi-component system:
$$\text{Accumulation}_i = \frac{d(M_i)}{dt} = \frac{d(\rho_i V)}{dt}$$

### Component 4 — Reaction (Generation / Consumption) Term
- $r_A > 0$ → generation (e.g., product formation in a reactor)
- $r_A < 0$ → consumption (e.g., reactant disappearance)

For **non-reacting** systems (storage tanks, mixers, blend tanks), this term is identically **zero**.

### Component 5 — Degrees of Freedom (DOF)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Degrees of Freedom (DOF) analysis** is the most heavily tested topic in KTU Module 3 Part A questions.

$$\boxed{N_{DOF} = N_{variables} - N_{independent\ equations}}$$

- If $N_{DOF} = 0$ → system is **exactly specified** (solvable).
- If $N_{DOF} > 0$ → system is **under-specified** (need more equations/measurements).
- If $N_{DOF} < 0$ → system is **over-specified** (redundant measurements, possibly inconsistent).

## 2.2 Steady-State vs Dynamic Material Balance

### Steady-State Material Balance
At steady state, accumulation is zero ($\frac{dM}{dt} = 0$). The balance simplifies to:

$$\sum_{in} F_{in} = \sum_{out} F_{out} + \sum_j r_j V$$

This algebraic equation forms the basis for **design** and **plant-wide material balance calculations** in steady-state process simulators.

### Dynamic Material Balance
When flows or compositions change, accumulation becomes non-zero:

$$\frac{dM}{dt} = F_{in} - F_{out} + rV$$

This **ordinary differential equation (ODE)** is the heart of inventory control. Solving it requires **integration**, and hence constitutes the dynamic model of the process.

## 2.3 Inventory Control — The Heart of Material Balance Control

A **controlled inventory** is one whose holdup is regulated to a setpoint using feedback control. The control loop consists of:

| Loop Element | Physical Realization | Typical Variable |
|--------------|---------------------|------------------|
| **Sensor** | Level transmitter (LT), mass flow transmitter (FT) | $h$ or $F$ |
| **Controller** | PID controller | Error $e = h_{SP} - h$ |
| **Final Control Element** | Control valve on inflow/outflow | Manipulated $F$ |
| **Controlled Variable (CV)** | Inventory (level/mass) | $h, M, n_A$ |
| **Manipulated Variable (MV)** | Inflow or outflow stream | $F_{in}$ or $F_{out}$ |

## 2.4 Material Balance Control Schemes

### Scheme A — Flow-Only (No Inventory Control)
Used when inventory is **irrelevant** (e.g., pipeline flow, pump-around). Controller regulates flow directly using a flow transmitter + control valve.

### Scheme B — Inventory Control on a Tank
Controller regulates level by manipulating **inflow** (preferred when downstream disturbance rejection matters) or **outflow**.

### Scheme C — Combined Material & Energy Balance Control
Used in reactors, distillation columns, and heat-integrated systems. Both mass and energy inventories must be controlled simultaneously using **two control loops** (e.g., level + temperature).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Use `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute value to preserve table syntax.** Below is the consolidated formula sheet for KTU Module 3.

| # | Formula | Description | When to Use |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | $\frac{dM}{dt} = F_{in} - F_{out} + rV$ | General dynamic mass balance | All dynamic problems |
| 2 | $F_{in} = F_{out} + rV$ | Steady-state mass balance | Steady-state numericals |
| 3 | $\rho A \frac{dh}{dt} = F_{in} - F_{out}$ | Liquid level (incompressible, non-reacting) | Tank level problems |
| 4 | $\frac{d(n_A)}{dt} = F_{A,in} - F_{A,out} + r_A V$ | Component balance | Reactors, mixers |
| 5 | $z_A F = x_A F_{in} + y_A F_{text{recirc}}$ | Blend equation (mass fractions) | Blending problems |
| 6 | $N_{DOF} = N_v - N_e$ | Degrees of freedom | Spec/design questions |
| 7 | $\tau = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{Ah}{Q}$ | Residence time | Reactor/separator design |
| 8 | $M = \rho A h$ | Mass holdup of liquid in tank | Level-to-mass conversion |
| 9 | $h(t) = h_0 + \frac{1}{A}\int_0^t (Q_{in} - Q_{out})\,d\tau$ | Liquid level time-response | Transient analyses |
| 10 | $\frac{dx_A}{dt} = \frac{F_{in}}{V}(z_A - x_A) + r_A$ | Composition dynamics | CSTR mixing |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Material balance control is the cornerstone of nearly every large-scale continuous process industry:

- **Petroleum Refineries:** Crude distillation, hydrocracker level control, product blending.
- **Chemical Plants:** Reactor feed control, distillation column reflux drum level control, neutralization pH control (component balance on $H^+$).
- **Power Plants:** Boiler drum level (three-element control), deaerator level, condensate hot well.
- **Pulp & Paper:** Stock chest level, white water consistency control.
- **Water Treatment:** Clarifier sludge blanket, chemical dosing (component balance on chlorine).
- **Food & Pharma:** Fermenter broth level, batch reactor fill/drain sequencing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Industry Connect:** Module 3 specifically bridges the gap between *theory* (mass balance equations) and *practice* (instrumentation choices: which transmitter to use, which valve to manipulate). Expect theory-practical hybrid questions in KTU University Exam.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Dynamic Model of a Single Liquid Tank

**Problem Setup:** A cylindrical tank of cross-sectional area $A$ receives a volumetric inflow $Q_{in}$ (m³/s) and discharges through an outflow line. The outflow depends on the liquid height $h$ through a linear resistance: $Q_{out} = h / R$, where $R$ is the flow resistance (s/m²).

### Step 1: Identify the Control Volume
The control volume is the liquid contents of the tank. The system boundary is the tank wall and the liquid surface.

### Step 2: Write the Mass Balance Equation

$$\frac{dM}{dt} = \dot{m}_{in} - \dot{m}_{out}$$

### Step 3: Substitute Density Relations
Assuming incompressible liquid (constant $\rho$):

$$\frac{d(\rho V)}{dt} = \rho Q_{in} - \rho Q_{out}$$

### Step 4: Substitute Volume $V = Ah$
Since $\rho$ is constant, divide throughout by $\rho$:

$$\frac{d(Ah)}{dt} = Q_{in} - Q_{out}$$

### Step 5: Factor Out the Constant Area $A$

$$\boxed{A\frac{dh}{dt} = Q_{in} - Q_{out}}$$

### Step 6: Substitute the Linear Outflow Relation $Q_{out} = h/R$

$$A\frac{dh}{dt} = Q_{in} - \frac{h}{R}$$

### Step 7: Rearrange into Standard First-Order ODE Form

$$AR\frac{dh}{dt} + h = R\,Q_{in}$$

Dividing by $AR$:

$$\boxed{\tau\frac{dh}{dt} + h = K\,Q_{in}}$$

where the **time constant** is $\tau = AR$ (seconds) and the **steady-state gain** is $K = R$ (s/m²).

### Step 8: Solve for Steady-State Operating Point
At steady state, $\frac{dh}{dt} = 0$:

$$h_{ss} = R\,Q_{in,ss}$$

### Step 9: Compute the Step Response
For a step change $\Delta Q_{in}$ from initial steady state:

$$h(t) = h_{ss} + \Delta Q_{in} \cdot R\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

This is the classical **first-order lag response** characteristic of nearly all material balance control loops.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Degrees of Freedom for a Two-Tank Series System

**Problem Setup:** Two tanks in series. Tank 1 has feed $F_0$ → outflow $F_1$ → Tank 2 → outflow $F_2$. Each tank has one level $h_1, h_2$.

### Step 1: Count Variables
| Variable | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| $F_0$ | Feed flow |
| $F_1$ | Inter-tank flow |
| $F_2$ | Exit flow |
| $h_1$ | Level in Tank 1 |
| $h_2$ | Level in Tank 2 |

Total variables: $N_v = 5$.

### Step 2: Count Independent Equations
1. Mass balance on Tank 1: $A_1 \frac{dh_1}{dt} = F_0 - F_1$
2. Mass balance on Tank 2: $A_2 \frac{dh_2}{dt} = F_1 - F_2$
3. Hydraulic relation: $F_1 = h_1 / R_1$

Independent equations: $N_e = 3$.

### Step 3: Compute DOF

$$N_{DOF} = 5 - 3 = 2$$

The two remaining degrees of freedom correspond to specifying **two** quantities (commonly $F_0$ as a manipulated variable and $h_2$ as a controlled variable — a **cascade material balance control** strategy).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Component Balance on a Mixing Tank

**Problem Setup:** Two streams enter a tank: stream 1 (pure A, flow $F_1$, mass fraction $x_{A,1} = 1$), stream 2 (pure B, flow $F_2$, mass fraction $x_{A,2} = 0$). Mixed outflow is $F$ with mass fraction $x_A$.

### Step 1: Total Mass Balance (constant density, single phase)

$$A\frac{dh}{dt} = F_1 + F_2 - F$$

### Step 2: Component Balance on Species A

$$\frac{d(M x_A)}{dt} = F_1 x_{A,1} + F_2 x_{A,2} - F x_A$$

### Step 3: Expand the Accumulation Term Using Product Rule

$$\frac{d(M x_A)}{dt} = M\frac{dx_A}{dt} + x_A\frac{dM}{dt}$$

### Step 4: Substitute $\frac{dM}{dt} = F_1 + F_2 - F$ from Step 1

$$M\frac{dx_A}{dt} + x_A(F_1 + F_2 - F) = F_1 x_{A,1} + F_2 x_{A,2} - F x_A$$

### Step 5: Cancel the $x_A F$ Terms on Both Sides

$$M\frac{dx_A}{dt} + x_A(F_1 + F_2) = F_1 x_{A,1} + F_2 x_{A,2}$$

### Step 6: Substitute $x_{A,1} = 1$ and $x_{A,2} = 0$

$$M\frac{dx_A}{dt} + x_A(F_1 + F_2) = F_1$$

### Step 7: Rearrange to Isolate $\frac{dx_A}{dt}$

$$\frac{dx_A}{dt} = \frac{F_1 - x_A(F_1 + F_2)}{M}$$

### Step 8: Substitute $M = \rho A h$

$$\boxed{\frac{dx_A}{dt} = \frac{F_1}{\rho A h} - \frac{x_A(F_1 + F_2)}{\rho A h}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The composition $x_A$ is a **nonlinear function** of the flows and the level $h$. This non-linearity makes composition control loops challenging and explains why **ratio control** (controlling $F_1/F_2$ rather than $F_1$ alone) is widely used industrially for blending.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Response of Tank Level to Constant Inflow with Gravity Outflow

**Problem Setup:** Tank of area $A$ has zero initial inflow. At $t=0$, a constant volumetric inflow $Q_0$ starts. Outflow obeys Torricelli's law: $Q_{out} = C_d a \sqrt{2gh}$, where $C_d$ is the discharge coefficient and $a$ is the orifice area.

### Step 1: Governing Equation

$$A\frac{dh}{dt} = Q_0 - C_d a \sqrt{2gh}$$

### Step 2: Solve via Separation of Variables

$$\frac{dh}{Q_0 - C_d a \sqrt{2gh}} = \frac{dt}{A}$$

### Step 3: Steady-State Level (Setting Numerator to Zero)

$$h_{ss} = \frac{Q_0^2}{2g\,C_d^2 a^2}$$

### Step 4: Define Deviation Variable $\tilde{h} = h_{ss} - h$

Linearizing around the operating point yields a first-order ODE with time constant:

$$\tau = \frac{A\sqrt{2g\,h_{ss}}}{C_d a\,g}$$

This explains the **self-regulating** nature of gravity-drained tanks: any deviation from $h_{ss}$ is naturally corrected.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common Material Balance Configurations

| Configuration | Governing Equation | Control Strategy | KTU Frequent? |
|---------------|--------------------|------------------|--------------|
| Single tank, $Q_{out}$ = const | $A\frac{dh}{dt} = Q_{in} - Q_0$ | Manipulate $Q_{in}$ (proportional control on $h$) | ★★★★★ |
| Single tank, $Q_{out} = h/R$ | $A\frac{dh}{dt} = Q_{in} - h/R$ | Manipulate $Q_{in}$ or $Q_{out}$ | ★★★★★ |
| Two tanks in series | Two coupled ODEs | Cascade or override control | ★★★★ |
| Mixing tank (binary) | Component balance (nonlinear) | Ratio control of feed streams | ★★★★ |
| Non-reacting tank + reaction downstream | Coupled mass + reaction | Level control on tank, T control on reactor | ★★★ |
| Surge tank between units | $A\frac{dh}{dt} = Q_{up} - Q_{down}$ | Level as buffer; downstream PI on $Q$ | ★★★★ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of a Material Balance Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[SP: Desired Level h_set] --> nodeB[Summing Junction]
    nodeC[LT: Level Transmitter] --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeD[PID Controller]
    nodeD --> nodeE[PCV: Pneumatic Control Valve]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Process Tank: Control Volume]
    nodeF --> nodeC
    nodeF --> nodeG[Outflow to Downstream]
    nodeH[Disturbance: Feed Composition Change] -.-> nodeF
```

**Interpretation of the Flow Diagram:**

| Node | Function |
|------|----------|
| `nodeA` | Setpoint entry (operator-set desired level) |
| `nodeB` | Compares setpoint with measured level (error computation) |
| `nodeC` | Level transmitter (LT) — senses $h$ and converts to 4–20 mA |
| `nodeD` | PID controller — computes manipulated variable from error |
| `nodeE` | Pneumatic control valve — adjusts inflow rate $Q_{in}$ |
| `nodeF` | Process tank — the control volume where mass balance occurs |
| `nodeG` | Downstream outflow (disturbance load to next unit) |
| `nodeH` | External disturbance (e.g., feed temperature, composition) |

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Degrees of Freedom (DOF) Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start: Count Variables Nv] --> nodeB[Count Independent Equations Ne]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Compute Ndof = Nv - Ne}
    nodeC -->|Ndof = 0| nodeD[Exactly Specified: System Solvable]
    nodeC -->|Ndof greater than 0| nodeE[Under Specified: Need More Equations or Manipulated Variables]
    nodeC -->|Ndof less than 0| nodeF[Over Specified: Redundant Measurements Possible]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Proceed to Design Control Loops]
    nodeE --> nodeH[Add Sensors or Add Manipulated Variables]
    nodeF --> nodeI[Reconcile Variables or Remove Redundancy]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Two-Tank Series Material Balance Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeF0[Feed F0] --> nodeT1[Tank 1 Level h1]
    nodeT1 --> nodeF1[Inter-tank Flow F1]
    nodeF1 --> nodeT2[Tank 2 Level h2]
    nodeT2 --> nodeF2[Exit Flow F2]
    
    nodeLT1[LT1 Level Sensor] --> nodeC1[Primary Controller]
    nodeC1 --> nodeSP2[Setpoint for Secondary]
    nodeLT2[LT2 Level Sensor] --> nodeC2[Secondary Controller]
    nodeC2 --> nodeV2[Valve on F2]
    
    nodeT1 -.Measured.- nodeLT1
    nodeT2 -.Measured.- nodeLT2
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Component (Blending) Material Balance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Stream A: Pure Component] --> nodeMIX[Mixing Tank]
    nodeB[Stream B: Pure Component] --> nodeMIX
    nodeMIX --> nodeP[Product Stream: Composition xA]
    
    nodeAT[Analyzer AT] --> nodeRC[Ratio Controller]
    nodeBT[Flow Transmitter FT on B] --> nodeRC
    nodeRC --> nodeV1[Control Valve on Stream A]
    
    nodeMIX -.Sample Line.- nodeAT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Insight:** In the blending schematic above, the **ratio controller** maintains $F_A / F_B = $ constant despite disturbances in $F_B$. This is the single most widely deployed component material balance control strategy in the petroleum, chemical, and food industries.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define material balance. Write the general material balance equation for a non-reacting process and explain each term.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Material Balance** is the application of the law of conservation of mass to a process control volume. It states that the rate of accumulation of mass within the system equals the rate of mass entering minus the rate of mass leaving plus generation minus consumption.

For a **non-reacting process** (generation = consumption = 0):

$$\frac{dM}{dt} = \sum_{in} \dot{m}_{in} - \sum_{out} \dot{m}_{out}$$

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| $\frac{dM}{dt}$ | Rate of mass accumulation inside the control volume |
| $\dot{m}_{in}$ | Mass flow rate entering the system |
| $\dot{m}_{out}$ | Mass flow rate leaving the system |

**[3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark equation + 1 mark term explanation]**

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Explain the significance of degrees of freedom analysis in material balance control. How is DOF computed?**

**Model Answer:**

Degrees of freedom (DOF) analysis determines whether a process is **properly specified** for control. It identifies the number of manipulated variables (or setpoints) that must be chosen to fully determine the system's operating point.

$$N_{DOF} = N_v - N_e$$

where $N_v$ = number of unknown variables and $N_e$ = number of independent equations.

- $N_{DOF} = 0$: Exactly specified — system solvable.
- $N_{DOF} > 0$: Under-specified — need more sensors or manipulated variables.
- $N_{DOF} < 0$: Over-specified — redundant or conflicting measurements.

**Significance:** DOF analysis dictates how many independent control loops the process can support and which streams should be designated as manipulated variables.

**[3 marks: 1 mark formula + 1 mark cases + 1 mark significance]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A — Set 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A cylindrical storage tank of cross-sectional area $A = 2\,\text{m}^2$ receives a constant volumetric inflow $Q_{in} = 0.05\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$. The outflow is linear with respect to level: $Q_{out} = h/R$ where $R = 200\,\text{s}/\text{m}^2$.**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Derive the dynamic model of the tank level $h(t)$ and obtain the steady-state level.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Apply mass balance (constant density, incompressible liquid):

$$A\frac{dh}{dt} = Q_{in} - Q_{out}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $Q_{out} = h/R$:

$$A\frac{dh}{dt} = Q_{in} - \frac{h}{R}$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange into standard first-order form:

$$AR\frac{dh}{dt} + h = R\,Q_{in}$$

$$\tau\frac{dh}{dt} + h = K\,Q_{in}$$

where $\tau = AR = 2 \times 200 = 400\,\text{s}$ and $K = R = 200\,\text{s}/\text{m}^2$.

**Step 4:** Compute steady-state level by setting $\frac{dh}{dt} = 0$:

$$h_{ss} = R \cdot Q_{in} = 200 \times 0.05 = 10\,\text{m}$$

**Result:** $h_{ss} = 10\,\text{m}$ and $\tau = 400\,\text{s}$. **[7 marks: 2 marks balance + 2 marks substitution + 1 mark standard form + 2 marks numerical answer]**

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**The inflow suddenly drops to $Q_{in}^{new} = 0.04\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ at $t = 0$. Determine the new steady-state level and the time required for the level to reach 95% of the final value.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the new steady-state level:

$$h_{ss}^{new} = R \cdot Q_{in}^{new} = 200 \times 0.04 = 8\,\text{m}$$

**Step 2:** The step-change magnitude in level:

$$\Delta h = h_{ss}^{new} - h_{ss} = 8 - 10 = -2\,\text{m}$$

**Step 3:** Time-response of first-order system to a step change:

$$h(t) = h_{ss}^{new} + (h_{ss} - h_{ss}^{new})e^{-t/\tau} = 8 - 2e^{-t/400}$$

**Step 4:** Time to reach 95% of final value. Setting $h(t) = h_{ss}^{new} + 0.05(h_{ss} - h_{ss}^{new})$:

$$8 - 2e^{-t/400} = 8 + 0.05 \times (-2) = 7.9$$

$$-2e^{-t/400} = -0.1 \Rightarrow e^{-t/400} = 0.05$$

$$t = -400 \ln(0.05) = 400 \times 2.996 = 1198.4\,\text{s} \approx 3\,\tau$$

**Result:** New level = **8 m**; Settling time (95%) ≈ **1198 s ≈ 20 minutes**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students often forget that for a first-order system, **95% response time = 3τ** (not 4τ or 5τ). Writing the wrong multiple of $\tau$ costs 2 marks instantly. Also remember that $h_{ss}$ depends on $Q_{in}$ and $R$ — do not assume $h_{ss} = 10$ m throughout the problem.

**[7 marks: 2 marks new h_ss + 2 marks time-response equation + 2 marks t calculation + 1 mark correct interpretation]**

---

### **Question B — Set 1 (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A mixing tank of volume $V = 5\,\text{m}^3$ contains a binary mixture of A and B. Pure A enters at $F_1 = 0.02\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ (density $\rho_1 = 1000\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $x_{A,1} = 1.0$). Pure B enters at $F_2 = 0.03\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ ($\rho_2 = 1100\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $x_{A,2} = 0.0$). The mixture exits at $F = F_1 + F_2$ with mass fraction $x_A$.**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Write the total mass balance and component (A) balance equations. Show all derivations clearly.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Define Holdup**

$$M = \rho_{mix} V$$

Assuming ideal mixing (volume additivity, near-constant density):

$$M \approx (\rho_1 F_1 + \rho_2 F_2)\,\frac{V}{F_1+F_2}\bigg/\,1 \approx 1050 \times 5 = 5250\,\text{kg}$$

(More precisely, $M$ is time-varying if $F_1$ and $F_2$ change; for steady operating conditions it is $5250$ kg.)

**Step 2: Total Mass Balance**

$$\frac{dM}{dt} = \rho_1 F_1 + \rho_2 F_2 - \rho_{out} F$$

At steady state, $\frac{dM}{dt} = 0$:

$$\rho_{out} F = \rho_1 F_1 + \rho_2 F_2$$

$$\rho_{out} = \frac{1000 \times 0.02 + 1100 \times 0.03}{0.05} = \frac{20 + 33}{0.05} = 1060\,\text{kg/m}^3$$

**Step 3: Component (A) Balance**

$$\frac{d(M x_A)}{dt} = \rho_1 F_1 x_{A,1} + \rho_2 F_2 x_{A,2} - \rho_{out} F x_A$$

Substituting $x_{A,1} = 1$ and $x_{A,2} = 0$:

$$\frac{d(M x_A)}{dt} = \rho_1 F_1 - \rho_{out} F x_A$$

**Step 4: Expand and Simplify (using product rule)**

$$M\frac{dx_A}{dt} + x_A\frac{dM}{dt} = \rho_1 F_1 - \rho_{out} F x_A$$

At steady state, $\frac{dM}{dt} = 0$:

$$0 = \rho_1 F_1 - \rho_{out} F x_{A,ss}$$

$$x_{A,ss} = \frac{\rho_1 F_1}{\rho_{out} F} = \frac{1000 \times 0.02}{1060 \times 0.05} = \frac{20}{53} \approx 0.377$$

**Result:** Steady-state mass fraction of A = **0.377 (or 37.7%)**. **[7 marks: 2 marks total balance + 3 marks component balance + 2 marks steady-state composition]**

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**If the operator desires $x_A = 0.40$ while $F_2$ remains at $0.03\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$, calculate the new required $F_1$. Assume steady-state operation.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Use the steady-state component balance:

$$\rho_1 F_1^{new} = \rho_{out}\, F^{new} \cdot x_A^{target}$$

**Step 2:** Mass balance at the new steady state:

$$\rho_{out}^{new} F^{new} = \rho_1 F_1^{new} + \rho_2 F_2$$

Substituting $\rho_{out}^{new} F^{new} = \rho_1 F_1^{new} / x_A^{target}$:

$$\frac{\rho_1 F_1^{new}}{x_A^{target}} = \rho_1 F_1^{new} + \rho_2 F_2$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $F_1^{new}$:

$$\rho_1 F_1^{new}\left(\frac{1}{x_A^{target}} - 1\right) = \rho_2 F_2$$

$$\rho_1 F_1^{new} \cdot \frac{1 - x_A^{target}}{x_A^{target}} = \rho_2 F_2$$

$$F_1^{new} = \frac{\rho_2 F_2 \cdot x_A^{target}}{\rho_1 (1 - x_A^{target})}$$

**Step 4:** Numerical evaluation:

$$F_1^{new} = \frac{1100 \times 0.03 \times 0.40}{1000 \times (1 - 0.40)} = \frac{13.2}{600} = 0.022\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Result:** $F_1^{new} \approx 0.022\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ (a 10% increase from original $0.020$).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Common mistakes in this problem:
> 1. **Mixing volume and mass fractions** — students often write $F_1 = F_2 \cdot \text{ratio}$ assuming volumetric fractions, but here **mass fractions** must be used with densities. Penalty: 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the $1 - x_A$ denominator** when solving — leads to algebraic error. Penalty: 1 mark.
> 3. **Not stating the steady-state assumption explicitly** — KTU expects you to *write* "At steady state, $dM/dt = 0$" before using the simplified equation.

**[7 marks: 2 marks setup + 2 marks algebra + 2 marks numerical evaluation + 1 mark units check]**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Module 3 Rapid-Fire Recall List**

### 🔑 Core Equations to Memorize
- [ ] General mass balance: $\frac{dM}{dt} = F_{in} - F_{out} + rV$
- [ ] Liquid level (incompressible): $A\frac{dh}{dt} = Q_{in} - Q_{out}$
- [ ] Component balance: $\frac{d(n_A)}{dt} = F_{A,in} - F_{A,out} + r_A V$
- [ ] DOF formula: $N_{DOF} = N_v - N_e$
- [ ] First-order response time to 95%: $t_{95\%} = 3\tau$

### 🎯 Conceptual Must-Knows
- [ ] **Accumulation = 0** at steady state — simplifies ODE to algebraic equation
- [ ] **Incompressible liquid** → $\dot{m} = \rho Q$ with $\rho$ constant → divide throughout by $\rho$
- [ ] **Non-reacting** → generation/consumption terms vanish
- [ ] **DOF = 0** is the only solvable case; > 0 means under-specified, < 0 means over-specified
- [ ] **Manipulated variable** choice determines loop dynamics: inflow vs outflow gives inverse response behaviors
- [ ] **Component balances** are inherently nonlinear (contain products like $M \cdot x_A$) — use ratio control in practice
- [ ] **Material balance control** regulates inventory; **composition control** regulates purity — they are related but distinct

### 🏭 Industrial Instrumentation Links (KTU frequently tests these)
- [ ] Level transmitter (LT) → 4–20 mA signal proportional to $h$
- [ ] Differential pressure (DP) cell for level measurement
- [ ] Control valve sizing: $C_v = Q\sqrt{\rho/\Delta P}$
- [ ] Cascade control: outer level loop sets setpoint of inner flow loop
- [ ] Ratio control: $F_A / F_B$ = constant for blending

### ⚠️ Common Pitfalls
- [ ] Don't confuse **mass flow rate** $\dot{m}$ (kg/s) with **molar flow rate** $F$ (kmol/s) — units dictate which form of balance applies.
- [ ] In component balances, **expand the derivative** using product rule: $\frac{d(Mx_A)}{dt} = M\frac{dx_A}{dt} + x_A\frac{dM}{dt}$.
- [ ] When using Torricelli's outflow, $Q_{out} = C_d a\sqrt{2gh}$ — **do not** linearize unless specifically asked.
- [ ] For reactive systems, $r > 0$ is **generation**, $r < 0$ is **consumption**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT864 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN LARGE SCALE INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
