> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The phrase *alternative* in the syllabus title is deliberate — it signifies that the student must understand **both** strategies, the **conditions under which each is preferred**, and the **industrial compromise (combined control)** that is used in nearly every large utility boiler.

The trade-off can be summarised as:

| Master | Speed of Response | Steady-State Accuracy | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pressure** | Slow (large thermal inertia) | Excellent | Steady base-load utility boilers |
| **Flow** | Fast (direct measurement of demand) | Poor (open-loop without pressure trim) | Rapidly fluctuating process-steam boilers |
| **Combined** | Fast + accurate | Excellent | All large utility / industrial boilers |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Logic of Pressure-Master Control

In the **pure pressure-master scheme**, the loop is single-element:

1. Steam pressure transmitter (PT) measures $P_s(t)$ in the steam header.
2. The pressure controller (PC) compares $P_s$ with set-point $P_{sp}$.
3. Controller output $u(t)$ drives the *fuel valve positioner* (and, via a *cross-limiting* relay, the *air damper*).
4. At steady state, $P_s \rightarrow P_{sp}$ exactly — meaning the **mass balance is enforced implicitly by pressure equality**.

**Why does it work?**
$$P_s \downarrow \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{demand} > \text{supply} \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{increase fuel}$$
$$P_s \uparrow \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{supply} > \text{demand} \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{decrease fuel}$$

**The "Why" – Energy Storage Argument:**
The boiler drum + steam piping acts as a *capacitor* storing steam. A sudden load change is *absorbed* by the stored steam, causing a transient pressure excursion. The PC then compensates. The pressure variable thus inherently integrates the supply-demand imbalance.

**Limitation:** The time-constant of the pressure loop is **30 s to 3 minutes** for large utility boilers. During this dead-time, a load step causes a measurable pressure swing and a transient *mis-match* between fuel and air, producing smoke, soot, and possible CO spikes.

### 2.2 Operating Logic of Flow-Master Control

Here the controlled variable is the **measured steam mass-flow rate** $\dot{m}_s$:

$$\dot{m}_{s,\text{meas}} = K \cdot \sqrt{\Delta P_{\text{orifice}}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{\rho_{\text{steam}}}}$$

or from an annubar / vortex / Coriolis meter.

1. Steam flow transmitter (FT) sends $\dot{m}_s$ to the flow controller (FC).
2. FC output sets the **fuel flow set-point** $F_{sp}$.
3. A *slave* fuel-flow loop (FFC) tightens the actual fuel delivery.

**Advantage:** Response time is essentially the **scan time of the flow meter (≈ 1 s)**, so load changes are tracked *immediately*.

**Fatal Flaw – No Pressure Trim:**
If the fuel valve sticks, or if boiler water level falls exposing heating surface, the pressure will drift away from set-point *without the flow controller noticing*, because the flow is still being delivered. Hence flow-master alone is **never used in safety-critical service** — it is always wrapped with a pressure limiter.

### 2.3 The Combined / Hybrid Master (Fire-Floor Pressure)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Standard:** IEEE 1222 / IEC 61500 recommend the **Combined Master** as best practice for boilers above ~50 TPH.

**Operating regions:**

- **Below cross-over firing rate (low fire):** Pressure controller is active. Steam flow signal is used only for *feed-forward trim*.
- **Above cross-over firing rate (high fire):** Steam-flow controller is active. Steam pressure is used as a *limiting / override* variable.
- At the **cross-over point**, both signals are blended smoothly (typically a 10 % overlap window) using *high/low signal selectors* — this prevents *bump-less transfer* is ensured by matching the secondary variable at switch-over.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the *complete cheat-sheet* a KTU student is expected to know verbatim for Module 4 derivations.

| # | Equation | Symbols / Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{m}_s \cdot (h_s - h_{fw}) = \dot{m}_f \cdot \text{GCV} \cdot \eta_b$ | $\dot{m}$ in kg/s, $h$ in kJ/kg, GCV in kJ/kg, $\eta_b$ dimensionless | Energy balance across boiler |
| 2 | $\text{AFR}_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{32}{12}\frac{y_{O_2}}{1 - y_{O_2}}$ for hydrocarbons | – | Theoretical air |
| 3 | $\text{AFR}_{\text{actual}} = (1 + \lambda)\,\text{AFR}_{\text{stoich}}$ | $\lambda \in [0.10,\,0.50]$ | With excess air |
| 4 | $\dot{m}_{\text{air}} = K \cdot \dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} \cdot \text{AFR}_{\text{actual}}$ | kg/s | Air demand from fuel |
| 5 | $\dot{V}_{\text{steam}} = C_d A \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho}$ | m³/s | Orifice steam flow |
| 6 | $\tau_p \dfrac{dP_s}{dt} = \dot{m}_f \cdot \text{GCV} \cdot \eta_b - \dot{m}_s \cdot (h_s - h_{fw})$ | $\tau_p$ in s | Pressure dynamics |
| 7 | $K_p \left(e + \dfrac{1}{\tau_i}\int e\,dt + \tau_d \dfrac{de}{dt}\right)$ | PID controller output | Standard PID law |
| 8 | $\dot{m}_{f,\text{ref}} = \dot{m}_s \cdot \dfrac{(h_s - h_{fw})}{\text{GCV} \cdot \eta_b}$ | kg/s | Feed-forward fuel demand |
| 9 | $O_2\% = 0.21\lambda / (\lambda + 0.21) \cdot 100$ for fuel-rich control | – | Flue-gas O₂ ↔ excess air |
| 10 | $\Delta P_{\text{cross}} = 0.05\,P_{sp}$ typical | bar | Cross-over window width |

> **Notation key:** $h_s$ – steam specific enthalpy, $h_{fw}$ – feed-water enthalpy, GCV – gross calorific value, $\eta_b$ – boiler thermal efficiency, $\tau_p$ – process time constant, $K_p,\tau_i,\tau_d$ – PID gains, $\lambda$ – excess-air fraction, $C_d$ – discharge coefficient.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power-generation utility boilers (BHEL, Tata Power, NTPC):** Always use the combined master with cross-over at ~30 % MCR (Maximum Continuous Rating).
- **Process-industry boilers (refineries, petrochemicals):** Often flow-master on steam header with pressure override, because steam demand is highly cyclic.
- **Recovery boilers (pulp & paper):** Pressure-master due to slow, steady load from the recovery process.
- **Waste-heat boilers:** Flow-master on the gas-side with pressure limiter, since the energy source is variable and not controllable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy Balance Master Equation

**Goal:** Derive the relationship between steam flow demand $\dot{m}_s$ and the required fuel flow $\dot{m}_f$.

**Step 1 – State the First Law for the boiler (control volume):**
$$\dot{Q}_{\text{in}} = \dot{m}_s (h_s - h_{fw}) + \dot{Q}_{\text{loss}}$$

Here $\dot{Q}_{\text{in}}$ is the heat released by fuel per second, and $\dot{Q}_{\text{loss}}$ is the convective/radiative stack loss plus blow-down loss.

**Step 2 – Express $\dot{Q}_{\text{in}}$ in terms of fuel mass flow:**
$$\dot{Q}_{\text{in}} = \dot{m}_f \cdot \text{GCV} \cdot \eta_b$$

where $\eta_b$ absorbs both combustion completeness and the heat-loss term.

**Step 3 – Rearrange to obtain the *fuel-demand equation*:**
$$\dot{m}_f = \frac{\dot{m}_s (h_s - h_{fw})}{\text{GCV} \cdot \eta_b}$$

**Step 4 – Insert numerical typical values (assume 100 TPH boiler):**
$\dot{m}_s = 100{,}000/3600 = 27.78\ \text{kg/s}$, $h_s = 2{,}800\ \text{kJ/kg}$, $h_{fw} = 900\ \text{kJ/kg}$, GCV $= 42{,}000\ \text{kJ/kg}$ (FO), $\eta_b = 0.88$.

$$\dot{m}_f = \frac{27.78 \times (2800 - 900)}{42000 \times 0.88}$$

**Step 5 – Compute numerator:**
$$27.78 \times 1900 = 52{,}782\ \text{kW (thermal)}$$

**Step 6 – Compute denominator:**
$$42000 \times 0.88 = 36{,}960\ \text{kJ/kg}$$

**Step 7 – Final fuel flow:**
$$\dot{m}_f = \frac{52782}{36960} = 1.428\ \text{kg/s} \approx 5{,}141\ \text{kg/h}$$

This is the **feed-forward fuel demand** that the flow-master controller enforces instantaneously.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pressure-Dynamics First-Order Model

**Step 1 – Define the *pressurisation* rate of the steam drum:**
$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\rho V \right) = \dot{m}_f^{*} - \dot{m}_s$$

where $\dot{m}_f^{*}$ is the *effective* mass flow of steam produced from fuel (= $\dot{m}_f\,\text{GCV}\,\eta_b / \Delta h_{\text{latent}}$), and $V$ is the steam-space volume.

**Step 2 – Use the ideal-gas-like relation for low-pressure swing around operating point:**
$$\frac{dP_s}{dt} = \frac{R T}{M V}\left(\dot{m}_f^{*} - \dot{m}_s\right)$$

**Step 3 – Substitute and define the process time-constant $\tau_p = MV/(RT)$:**
$$\tau_p \frac{dP_s}{dt} = \dot{m}_f^{*} - \dot{m}_s$$

**Step 4 – Linearise around steady-state operating point $P_{s0}$:**
$$\tau_p \frac{d\Delta P_s}{dt} = K_f \Delta \dot{m}_f - \Delta \dot{m}_s$$

This is the **standard first-order plus dead-time (FOPDT)** model:
$$\dfrac{\Delta P_s(s)}{\Delta \dot{m}_f(s)} = \dfrac{K_f}{\tau_p s}$$

**Step 5 – Take the Laplace transform and identify the *process gain*:**
$$K_p = \dfrac{\Delta P_s(\infty)}{\Delta \dot{m}_f(\infty)} = \dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_s}\cdot P_{s0}$$

For a 100-bar drum with $\dot{m}_s = 28$ kg/s:
$$K_p = \dfrac{100}{28} \approx 3.57\ \text{bar per (kg/s)}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cross-Over Selection Logic (High/Low Signal Selector)

**Step 1 – Pressure controller output (in % of stroke):**
$$u_P = 100 \cdot K_{pP}\left[(P_{sp} - P_s) + \dfrac{1}{\tau_{iP}}\int (P_{sp} - P_s)\,dt\right]$$

**Step 2 – Flow controller output (in % of stroke):**
$$u_F = 100 \cdot K_{pF}\left[(\dot{m}_{s,sp} - \dot{m}_s) + \dfrac{1}{\tau_{iF}}\int (\dot{m}_{s,sp} - \dot{m}_s)\,dt\right]$$

**Step 3 – Low-signal-selector outputs the smaller demand at LOW FIRE; high-signal-selector outputs the larger at HIGH FIRE:**
$$u_{\text{final}} = \begin{cases} \min(u_P, u_F), & \dot{m}_f < \dot{m}_{f,\text{xover}} \quad (\text{low fire})\\ \max(u_P, u_F), & \dot{m}_f \ge \dot{m}_{f,\text{xover}} \quad (\text{high fire})\end{cases}$$

**Step 4 – Anti-bump condition at cross-over:**
For *bump-less transfer*, the integrator of the *inactive* controller is *track-held* by an external feedback loop such that $u_{\text{inactive}} = u_{\text{active}}$ at the moment of switch-over. This guarantees no actuator step.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use What

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The matrix below is a high-yield exam table. KTU typically asks to justify the choice in 7-mark sub-questions.

| Operating Condition | Preferred Master | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Steady base-load, utility | **Pressure** | Load is predictable; accuracy of pressure > speed of response |
| Cyclic / fast-varying load | **Flow** | Direct measurement of demand, low dead-time |
| Variable fuel quality (coal / bagasse) | **Combined** | Flow-master tracks demand; pressure-master trims composition changes |
| Multiple boilers on common header | **Pressure** (master-slave) | Header pressure is the only common variable |
| Start-up / low fire | **Pressure** | Flow signal unreliable below ~20 % MCR (noisy, low $\Delta P$ at orifice) |
| Supercritical once-through | **Flow** | No drum; pressure is no longer an integrator, so pressure signal lags |

### 3.5 Stability Analysis — Why Pressure-Master can Oscillate

**Step 1 – Closed-loop transfer function with PI controller:**
$$G_{CL}(s) = \dfrac{K_p K_c \left(\tau_i s + 1\right) / \tau_i}{s\left(\tau_p s + 1\right) + K_p K_c \left(\tau_i s + 1\right) / \tau_i}$$

**Step 2 – Characteristic equation (denominator = 0):**
$$\tau_p s^2 + s + \dfrac{K_p K_c}{\tau_i}\left(\tau_i s + 1\right) = 0$$
$$\tau_p s^2 + \left(1 + K_p K_c\right) s + \dfrac{K_p K_c}{\tau_i} = 0$$

**Step 3 – For Routh-Hurwitz stability, both coefficients must be positive — which is automatic. But the *damping ratio* determines whether oscillation occurs:**
$$\zeta = \dfrac{1 + K_p K_c}{2\sqrt{\tau_p K_p K_c / \tau_i}}$$

**Step 4 – A typical set of values $K_p = 3.57$, $K_c = 0.5$, $\tau_i = 60$ s, $\tau_p = 90$ s gives:**
$$\zeta = \dfrac{1 + 1.785}{2\sqrt{90 \cdot 1.785 / 60}} = \dfrac{2.785}{2\sqrt{2.678}} = \dfrac{2.785}{3.273} \approx 0.85$$

$\zeta = 0.85$ is *under-damped but stable*. KTU students should know that for large utility boilers, $\zeta \in [0.6, 0.9]$ is the practical design target.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Pressure-Master Control Loop (Single-Element)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Demand["LOAD DEMAND"]
        LoadUser["Process Steam Consumer"]
    end

    subgraph Boiler["BOILER PLANT"]
        Drum["Drum / Steam Space"]
        Furnace["Furnace / Combustion"]
        Stack["Flue Gas Stack"]
    end

    subgraph Instrument["INSTRUMENTATION & CONTROL"]
        PT["PT - Steam Pressure Transmitter"]
        PC["PC - Pressure Controller PID"]
        FV["FV - Fuel Valve with Positioner"]
        AV["AV - Air Damper Actuator"]
        FTfuel["FT - Fuel Flow Transmitter"]
        FFC["FFC - Slave Fuel Flow Controller"]
    end

    LoadUser -->|"Steam drawn"| Drum
    Drum -->|"Steam Pressure Signal 4-20 mA"| PT
    PT -->|"PV to PC"| PC
    PC -->|"MV to FV"| FV
    FV -->|"Fuel"| Furnace
    Furnace -->|"Heat"| Drum
    Furnace -->|"Exhaust"| Stack
    FV -->|"Flow Signal"| FTfuel
    FTfuel -->|"PV to FFC"| FFC
    FFC -->|"Trim Signal"| FV
    PC -.->|"Cross-Limited Lead"| AV
    Furnace -.->|"Air Demand"| AV
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Red path (PC → FV):** master loop on pressure.
- **Blue path (FTfuel → FFC → FV):** inner slave flow loop for fuel trimming.
- **Dashed path (PC → AV):** the same pressure controller output also leads the air damper through a *cross-limiting* ratio relay so that AFR is preserved.

### 4.2 Flow-Master Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Header["STEAM HEADER"]
        SteamFlow["Steam Flow Element Orifice / Vortex"]
    end

    subgraph Measure["MEASUREMENT"]
        FTs["FT - Steam Flow Transmitter"]
        PTs["PT - Steam Pressure Transmitter - OVERRIDE"]
    end

    subgraph Control["CONTROL LAYER"]
        FC["FC - Steam Flow Controller"]
        PC["PC - Steam Pressure Controller - OVERRIDE"]
        HS["HS - High Signal Selector"]
        FFC["FFC - Fuel Flow Controller"]
    end

    subgraph Final["ACTUATION"]
        FuelValve["Fuel Control Valve"]
        AirValve["Air Damper"]
    end

    SteamFlow --> FTs
    FTs --> FC
    PTs --> PC
    FC --> HS
    PC --> HS
    HS --> FFC
    FFC --> FuelValve
    HS -.-> AirValve
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **flow controller FC** is the primary master — its output drives the fuel valve.
- The **pressure controller PC** acts only as an *override limiter*: if $P_s$ exceeds a safety threshold (typically 105 % of set-point), the PC output rises above the FC output and the high-selector switches to pressure control, *clamping* the fuel demand.
- This architecture is sometimes called **Flow-Master with Pressure Override**.

### 4.3 Combined Master — Fire-Floor Pressure Control (Industrial Standard)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SteamSide["STEAM SIDE"]
        SteamOut["Steam to Process / Turbine"]
        Pm["PT - Steam Pressure"]
        Fs["FT - Steam Flow"]
    end

    subgraph MasterCtrl["MASTER CONTROL LAYER"]
        PCm["PC - Pressure Master Controller"]
        FCm["FC - Flow Master Controller"]
        HSx["HS - High Signal Selector HIGH FIRE"]
        LSx["LS - Low Signal Selector LOW FIRE"]
        XOver["XOVER - Cross-Over Logic Block"]
    end

    subgraph SlaveCtrl["SLAVE LAYER"]
        FFCs["FFC - Fuel Flow Slave Controller"]
        AFCs["AFC - Air Flow Slave Controller"]
    end

    subgraph Actuators["FINAL CONTROL ELEMENTS"]
        FuelV["Fuel Valve"]
        AirV["Air Damper / FD Fan Vane"]
    end

    Pm --> PCm
    Fs --> FCm
    PCm --> LSx
    FCm --> HSx
    LSx --> XOver
    HSx --> XOver
    XOver -->|"Fuel Demand Signal"| FFCs
    XOver -->|"Air Demand via AFR Relay"| AFCs
    FFCs --> FuelV
    AFCs --> AirV
    FuelV -.->|"Steam Generation"| SteamOut
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Low Fire region:** Pressure controller output dominates (LS picks the smaller of PC and FC outputs because the FC output is artificially *backed off* during low fire).
- **High Fire region:** Flow controller output dominates (HS picks the larger of PC and FC outputs because at high fire PC backs off to its lower saturation).
- **XOVER block** implements the *bumpless transfer* with overlap window of typically 5 %–10 % of firing rate.

### 4.4 Signal-Selector Truth Table

| Region | PC output | FC output | Selector | Master Active | Output to Fuel Slave |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Low fire | High (>FC) | Low | Low select | PC | PC output |
| Cross-over zone | ≈ FC | ≈ FC | Either (bump-less) | Blended | min / max ≈ equal |
| High fire | Low (<FC) | High | High select | FC | FC output |
| Pressure override | PC saturated high | FC normal | High select | PC | PC = clamp |

### 4.5 Complete Utility-Boiler Control Schematic (Block-Level Functional Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1["SENSORS"]
        PTm["Steam Pressure PT"]
        FTsm["Steam Flow FT"]
        FTf["Fuel Flow FT"]
        FTa["Air Flow FT"]
        AT["O2 Analyser AT in Flue Gas"]
        LT["Drum Level LT"]
    end

    subgraph S2["CONTROLLERS"]
        PCc["Pressure Controller"]
        FCc["Flow Controller"]
        FFCc["Fuel Slave Controller"]
        AFCc["Air Slave Controller"]
        OXc["O2 Trim Controller"]
        LCc["Drum Level Controller"]
        XOc["Cross-Over and Selector"]
    end

    subgraph S3["ACTUATORS"]
        FVc["Fuel Valve"]
        AVc["Air Damper"]
        FDVc["Feedwater Valve"]
    end

    PTm --> PCc
    FTsm --> FCc
    PCc --> XOc
    FCc --> XOc
    XOc --> FFCc
    XOc --> AFCc
    FTf --> FFCc
    FTa --> AFCc
    AT --> OXc
    OXc -.-> AFCc
    FFCc --> FVc
    AFCc --> AVc
    LT --> LCc
    LCc --> FDVc
```

This block map ties **all Module-4 loops together**: combustion control (pressure / flow master), air trimming via oxygen trim, and drum-level cascade. It is the single picture a student should be able to draw free-hand in a KTU exam for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between Pressure-Master and Flow-Master control strategies in a boiler, listing one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Pressure-Master:** Steam pressure is the controlled variable. *Advantage:* inherently enforces the energy balance in steady state — no offset in pressure. (1.5 marks)
- **Flow-Master:** Steam flow is the controlled variable. *Advantage:* direct, fast measurement of load demand — rapid tracking of load changes with low dead-time. (1.5 marks)

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**What is the role of a high/low signal selector in the Combined Master boiler control scheme?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A *high-signal selector* chooses the higher of the pressure and flow controller outputs at **high fire**, ensuring flow-master dominance. (1.5 marks)
- A *low-signal selector* chooses the lower at **low fire**, ensuring pressure-master dominance. (1.5 marks)
- Together they provide **bump-less cross-over** between the two masters as firing rate changes.

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 4, 14 Marks]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Pressure-Master boiler control** scheme. Derive the closed-loop characteristic equation and discuss its stability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 100 TPH boiler operates at 100 bar drum pressure. The steam flow changes from 25 kg/s to 30 kg/s in 10 seconds. Estimate (i) the process gain $K_p$, (ii) the controller gain $K_c$ for critical damping, given $\tau_p = 90$ s and $\tau_i = 60$ s. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Pressure-Master Scheme (7 marks):**
- Draw the block diagram showing **PT → PC → FV** with steam drum pressure as the controlled variable. (1.5 marks)
- Energy balance and pressure dynamics:
$$\tau_p \dfrac{d\Delta P_s}{dt} = K_f \Delta \dot{m}_f - \Delta \dot{m}_s$$ (1 mark)
- Closed-loop characteristic equation with PI controller: (2 marks)
$$\tau_p s^2 + (1 + K_p K_c)s + \dfrac{K_p K_c}{\tau_i} = 0$$
- Damping ratio and stability discussion (oscillatory if $\zeta < 1$, unstable if $\tau_p \tau_i K_p K_c < 0$, etc.). For $\zeta = 1$ (critical damping): (1.5 marks)
$$1 + K_p K_c = 2\sqrt{\dfrac{\tau_p K_p K_c}{\tau_i}}$$
- Conclusion: a PI controller is sufficient for the pressure loop; derivative action is rarely needed because the drum acts as a natural integrator-like capacitor. (1 mark)

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**
(i) **Process gain:**
$$K_p = \dfrac{\Delta P_s}{\Delta \dot{m}_f}\bigg|_{\dot{m}_s = \text{const}}$$
For step in *load* $\Delta \dot{m}_s$ that the master must offset:
$$K_p = \dfrac{P_{s0}}{\dot{m}_{s0}} = \dfrac{100}{27.78} = 3.60\ \text{bar·s/kg}$$ (2 marks)

(ii) **Critical damping condition** $\zeta = 1$:
$$\left(1 + K_p K_c\right)^2 = 4\,\dfrac{\tau_p K_p K_c}{\tau_i}$$ (2 marks)
Substitute $K_p = 3.60$, $\tau_p = 90$ s, $\tau_i = 60$ s:
$$\left(1 + 3.6 K_c\right)^2 = 4 \times \dfrac{90 \times 3.6 K_c}{60} = 21.6 K_c$$
Let $x = 1 + 3.6 K_c$:
$$x^2 = 21.6(x - 1)/3.6 = 6(x - 1)$$
$$x^2 - 6x + 6 = 0$$
$$x = \dfrac{6 \pm \sqrt{36 - 24}}{2} = \dfrac{6 \pm \sqrt{12}}{2} = \dfrac{6 \pm 3.464}{2}$$

Taking the smaller root (realistic $K_c$):
$$x = 1.268 \;\Longrightarrow\; K_c = \dfrac{1.268 - 1}{3.6} = 0.0744\ \text{(bar/bar)}$$ (3 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget the *linearisation assumption* around steady state. If you write $K_p = P_{s0}/\dot{m}_{s0}$, **state explicitly** that this is the linearised gain around the operating point, otherwise you lose 1 mark. Also, the question asks for *critical damping*, not *optimal* — do not introduce Ziegler-Nichols tuning here unless the question demands it.

---

#### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Module 4, 14 Marks]`

**(a)** Describe the **Flow-Master boiler control** scheme with a block diagram. Explain why it is always paired with a **pressure override**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A combined-master boiler has the following data: cross-over firing rate = 30 % of MCR = 9 TPH of fuel; steam flow set-point at high fire = 28 kg/s; pressure set-point = 100 bar. If the actual steam flow drops to 26 kg/s while the pressure rises to 102 bar, determine which controller is active and compute the required *fuel demand* using the energy balance equation. Take $h_s - h_{fw} = 1900$ kJ/kg, GCV = 42 000 kJ/kg, $\eta_b = 0.88$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Flow-Master with Pressure Override (7 marks):**
- Block diagram with **FT_s → FC → HS → FFC → fuel valve**. (1.5 marks)
- **Pressure override** branch: PT → PC → HS (high-select). (1 mark)
- Explanation: Flow-master alone cannot detect a stuck fuel valve or a low-drum-level condition because the *measured* steam flow remains close to set-point. (2 marks)
- Pressure override *clamps* the fuel demand when $P_s$ exceeds a safe limit (typically 105 % of $P_{sp}$), preventing over-pressure and ensuring safety. (2 marks)
- Mention the bypass / override release: once pressure normalises, control returns smoothly to flow-master. (0.5 marks)

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**
- Determine region: actual firing rate unknown, but $\dot{m}_s = 26$ kg/s is *below* set-point, indicating load has dropped. Pressure *above* set-point means supply exceeds demand. (1 mark)
- Since we are at *higher-than-set pressure* but *lower-than-set flow*, the boiler is operating in the **flow-master region** (high fire, because the firing rate needed to push pressure up is high). The pressure controller will be *backed off*, and the flow controller's output will dominate via the high-selector. (2 marks)
- Apply energy balance for fuel demand corresponding to *steam demand* of 26 kg/s:
$$\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{\dot{m}_s (h_s - h_{fw})}{\text{GCV} \cdot \eta_b} = \dfrac{26 \times 1900}{42000 \times 0.88}$$ (2 marks)
- Numerator: $26 \times 1900 = 49{,}400$ kW
- Denominator: $42000 \times 0.88 = 36{,}960$ kJ/kg
$$\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{49400}{36960} = 1.337\ \text{kg/s} \approx 4812\ \text{kg/h} \approx 4.81\ \text{TPH}$$ (2 marks)
- Compare with cross-over: 4.81 TPH < 9 TPH → the boiler is actually in the **low-fire region**, so the *pressure controller* is the active master. The flow controller's output is being *clamped down* by the low-signal selector to prevent over-firing. (Bonus 0 mark — but conceptually the student must reconcile.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** In sub-part (b), many students blindly compute the fuel demand and stop. They lose **2 marks** because they did not explicitly identify which controller is active. **Always state the operating region (low/high fire) first, then compute.** A common KTU board trick is to give numbers that look like high-fire but actually place the boiler in low-fire; the student must reconcile *both* the flow and the pressure observations.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — must-know points for KTU Module 4:**

- ☐ **Master variable** is the *load-representative* variable; in boilers it is either steam pressure or steam flow.
- ☐ **Pressure-Master** = single-element PI on pressure transmitter → fuel valve; slow but accurate.
- ☐ **Flow-Master** = direct measurement of steam flow → fuel demand; fast but unsafe alone.
- ☐ **Combined Master** = pressure-master below cross-over, flow-master above cross-over, with high/low signal selectors.
- ☐ **Cross-over firing rate** is typically 25 %–35 % of MCR; a 5 %–10 % overlap window prevents bumps.
- ☐ **Energy-balance equation** (must be memorised): $\dot{m}_f = \dot{m}_s (h_s - h_{fw}) / (\text{GCV} \cdot \eta_b)$.
- ☐ **Pressure dynamics first-order**: $\tau_p \dfrac{dP_s}{dt} = \dot{m}_f^{*} - \dot{m}_s$.
- ☐ **Damping ratio** for PI on pressure loop: $\zeta = (1 + K_p K_c) / \left(2 \sqrt{\tau_p K_p K_c / \tau_i}\right)$.
- ☐ **Excess air** $\lambda$ typically 15 %–25 % at full load, rising to 30 %–50 % at low load.
- ☐ **Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR)** is preserved by *cross-limiting*: fuel leads air on demand increase; air leads fuel on demand decrease (this prevents smoke).
- ☐ **Pressure override** is mandatory in flow-master schemes for safety.
- ☐ **Drum level** is controlled by a separate *three-element* (or two-element) cascade on feed-water — *do not confuse* with combustion control.
- ☐ **Supercritical once-through boilers** use **flow-master only** (no drum, no usable pressure storage).
- ☐ **Signal selector types**: low-signal-select for low-fire, high-signal-select for high-fire.
- ☐ **Bump-less transfer** requires integrator track-hold on the inactive controller.
- ☐ **Typical industrial set-point conventions**: 4–20 mA electronic or 3–15 psi pneumatic; safety valve at +5 % to +10 % MWP.

> **Final one-liner:** *Choose pressure-master when load is steady and you need accuracy; choose flow-master when load is cyclic and you need speed; choose combined master when you cannot compromise on either — which is the reality of every large-scale industrial boiler.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feedforward Control with Feedback Trim
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT864 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN LARGE SCALE INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Feedforward Control with Feedback Trim

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Feedforward Control (FFC)** is an *anticipatory* (open-loop) control strategy that measures one or more **load disturbances** before they propagate through the process and computes a corrective manipulated variable action *in parallel with* — rather than in response to — the resulting process error.

**Feedback Trim (FB-Trim)** is a *corrective* (closed-loop) secondary loop overlaid on the FFC. Its sole purpose is to drive the residual steady-state and dynamic error (caused by model mismatch, unmeasured disturbances, and parameter drift) to zero.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Feedforward Control with Feedback Trim** is a **composite (hybrid) control architecture** in which the FFC path provides fast, model-based disturbance rejection while the feedback path provides robust, error-driven correction. This combination is the **industry standard** for processes dominated by measurable, frequent disturbances (e.g., heat exchangers, distillation columns, HVAC systems, boiler drum level).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Driving a Car in Hilly Terrain**
>
> - **Feedforward action** = *Looking ahead* at a hill and *pre-emptively pressing the accelerator* before the car actually slows down. You are reacting to the **cause** (the slope), not the effect.
> - **Feedback action** = *Looking at the speedometer* and adjusting the accelerator to keep the cruise speed at exactly the setpoint. You are reacting to the **effect** (speed drop).
> - **Feedforward + Feedback Trim** = Using *both* — your eyes on the road (FFC) and your eyes on the speedometer (FB) — to maintain a perfectly constant speed. The FFC does ~90% of the work; the FB trims out the last 10% caused by wind, road surface, and estimation errors.
>
> **Industrial example: Shell-and-Tube Heat Exchanger**
> The most common KTU exam context. The disturbance is the **inlet temperature of the cold process stream** ($T_{c,in}$). The FFC measures $T_{c,in}$ and adjusts the **steam flow** setpoint *before* the outlet hot-stream temperature ($T_{h,out}$) drifts. The FB trim, using a temperature transmitter on $T_{h,out}$, makes a small correction to the steam setpoint to eliminate the residual offset.

## 1.3 Key Terminology & Physical Metrics

| Term | Symbol | Industrial Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Manipulated Variable | $M$ or $U$ | Valve stem position / flow controller setpoint |
| Controlled Variable | $C$ | Process output being regulated |
| Load Disturbance | $D$ | Unwanted input entering the process |
| Process Dead Time | $\theta_p$ | Pure delay in the process path |
| Disturbance Dead Time | $\theta_d$ | Pure delay in the disturbance path |
| Process Time Constant | $\tau_p$ | First-order lag of the process |
| Disturbance Time Constant | $\tau_d$ | First-order lag of the disturbance path |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why combine them?** Pure FFC is **open-loop** and *cannot* correct for unmeasured disturbances or model error. Pure FB is **reactive** and *cannot* anticipate. The hybrid scheme gives **anticipation + robustness** — this is the KTU-favoured answer to "why not use only one?"
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Bulleted Breakdown

The composite controller operates on **two parallel paths** that merge at a single summing junction before the final control element (valve):

1. **The Disturbance Path (Measurement Side):**
   A sensor (e.g., RTD, orifice plate) continuously measures the disturbance $D(s)$.
   The signal passes through a **disturbance measurement transfer function** $G_{md}(s)$ (which includes transmitter dynamics, filter, and dead time).

2. **The Feedforward Path (Anticipatory Action):**
   The measured disturbance signal is fed into the **Feedforward Controller** $G_{ff}(s)$.
   $G_{ff}(s)$ is a *model-based* compensator that mathematically computes the exact $M$ required to cancel the disturbance.
   Output of this path: $M_{ff}(s) = G_{ff}(s) \cdot G_{md}(s) \cdot D(s)$.

3. **The Feedback Path (Corrective Action):**
   A second sensor measures the controlled variable $C$ through $G_{mp}(s)$ (output measurement dynamics).
   The error $E = R - C_{measured}$ enters a standard **PID controller** $G_c(s)$.
   Output of this path: $M_{fb}(s) = G_c(s) \cdot [R(s) - G_{mp}(s) \cdot C(s)]$.

4. **The Summing Junction (Hybrid Command):**
   The two controller outputs are added algebraically: $U(s) = M_{ff}(s) + M_{fb}(s)$.
   This combined signal drives the **final control element** $G_v(s)$ (valve + positioner dynamics).

5. **The Process:**
   The process $G_p(s)$ receives the manipulated input and the disturbance: $C(s) = G_p(s) \cdot G_v(s) \cdot U(s) + G_d(s) \cdot D(s)$.

6. **Steady-State Trim:**
   In the long run, the integral action inside the PID forces the residual error to zero, regardless of how inaccurate the FFC model is.

## 2.2 Why and How Each Step Works

- **Why measure the disturbance?** Because if you can *see* the cause, you can act *before* the effect (temperature, level, pressure) deviates.
- **Why a model-based $G_{ff}$?** Because the disturbance and process have *different dynamics* (different time constants, gains, and dead times). The FFC must "invert" the process and "replicate" the disturbance path to produce a perfectly cancelling signal.
- **Why add feedback trim?** Because *no model is perfect*. Real processes have nonlinearity, drift, and unmeasured disturbances. The FB trim acts as an *adaptive corrector*.
- **Why a single summing junction?** It keeps the two paths *non-interacting* in the ideal case: FFC handles $D$, FB handles *everything else*.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbols convention:** All transfer functions are in the **Laplace domain** (variable $s$). Negative signs arise from the convention that the controller must oppose the disturbance.

| # | Equation | Meaning | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C(s) = G_p(s)\,U(s) + G_d(s)\,D(s)$ | Linear process model | Any single-input single-output (SISO) process with one measurable disturbance |
| 2 | $G_{ff,ideal}(s) = -\dfrac{G_d(s)}{G_p(s)}$ | **Ideal FFC** (unity valve, unity sensor) | Theoretical derivation / static design |
| 3 | $G_{ff}(s) = -\dfrac{G_d(s)}{G_v(s)\,G_p(s)}$ | **Practical FFC** including valve dynamics | When $G_v(s) \neq 1$ (almost always in real plants) |
| 4 | $G_{ff}(s) = -\dfrac{G_d(s)\,G_{mp}(s)}{G_v(s)\,G_p(s)\,G_{md}(s)}$ | **Complete FFC** with measurement dynamics | Full industrial design (most exam-correct) |
| 5 | $K_{ff,static} = -\dfrac{K_d}{K_p}$ | Static (DC) FFC gain | First-order check; steady-state offset elimination |
| 6 | $C(s) = \left[G_p\,G_v\,G_{ff}\,G_{md} + G_d\right] D(s) + G_p\,G_v\,G_c\,[R - G_{mp}\,C]$ | Closed-loop output (composite) | Final block-diagram reduction |
| 7 | $E_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s\,E(s) = 0$ | Steady-state error with integral FB | Proves that FB trim removes residual FFC error |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Distillation columns**: Feed composition is the dominant disturbance; FFC on reflux vs. FB on top product composition is the textbook case.
- **Boiler drum level**: FFC on steam flow + FB on level (the famous "shrink-and-swell" compensation).
- **HVAC chilled-water systems**: FFC on outdoor wet-bulb temperature + FB on supply air temperature.
- **Chemical reactors**: FFC on feed concentration + FB on reactor temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners reward diagrams that **explicitly show both sensor paths** ($G_{md}$ and $G_{mp}$) and **both controller paths** ($G_{ff}$ and $G_c$). Marks are routinely lost when students draw only a single loop.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Ideal Feedforward Controller Transfer Function

**Goal:** Find $G_{ff}(s)$ such that the controlled variable $C(s)$ is *independent* of the disturbance $D(s)$.

**Step 1 — Write the unforced process equation.**
A linear SISO process with one measured disturbance is governed by the superposition principle:

$$C(s) = G_p(s)\,U(s) + G_d(s)\,D(s)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the feedforward law.**
The feedforward controller acts on the measured disturbance alone (ignoring the setpoint), so:

$$U(s) = G_{ff}(s)\,G_{md}(s)\,D(s)$$

For ideal design we first assume $G_{md}(s) = 1$ and $G_v(s) = 1$ (perfect sensors and unity valve).

**Step 3 — Substitute $U(s)$ into the process equation.**

$$C(s) = G_p(s)\,G_{ff}(s)\,D(s) + G_d(s)\,D(s)$$

Factor out $D(s)$:

$$C(s) = \left[\,G_p(s)\,G_{ff}(s) + G_d(s)\,\right]\,D(s)$$

**Step 4 — Apply the *perfect disturbance rejection* condition.**
For $C(s)$ to be completely decoupled from $D(s)$, the bracketed term must equal zero:

$$G_p(s)\,G_{ff}(s) + G_d(s) = 0$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $G_{ff}(s)$.**

$$G_{ff}(s) = -\frac{G_d(s)}{G_p(s)}$$

The **negative sign** is essential: if a positive disturbance pushes the output up, the FFC must push the manipulated variable *down* (or in the opposite direction) to cancel it.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Practical FFC Including Valve and Sensor Dynamics

**Step 1 — Start from the full block diagram.**
With realistic valve $G_v(s)$, disturbance sensor $G_{md}(s)$, and output sensor $G_{mp}(s)$, the manipulated signal reaching the process is:

$$U(s) = G_v(s)\,\left[\,G_{ff}(s)\,G_{md}(s)\,D(s) + G_c(s)\,\bigl(R(s) - G_{mp}(s)\,C(s)\bigr)\,\right]$$

**Step 2 — Write the process output.**

$$C(s) = G_p(s)\,U(s) + G_d(s)\,D(s)$$

**Step 3 — Set $R(s) = 0$** (we are analyzing the disturbance-rejection path only) and substitute:

$$C(s) = G_p(s)\,G_v(s)\,G_{ff}(s)\,G_{md}(s)\,D(s) - G_p(s)\,G_v(s)\,G_c(s)\,G_{mp}(s)\,C(s) + G_d(s)\,D(s)$$

**Step 4 — Collect $C(s)$ on the LHS and $D(s)$ on the RHS.**

$$C(s)\,\left[\,1 + G_p(s)\,G_v(s)\,G_c(s)\,G_{mp}(s)\,\right] = D(s)\,\left[\,G_p(s)\,G_v(s)\,G_{ff}(s)\,G_{md}(s) + G_d(s)\,\right]$$

**Step 5 — To make $C(s)$ independent of $D(s)$** (perfect FFC decoupling), the RHS must be zero:

$$G_p(s)\,G_v(s)\,G_{ff}(s)\,G_{md}(s) + G_d(s) = 0$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $G_{ff}(s)$.**

$$G_{ff}(s) = -\frac{G_d(s)\,G_{mp}(s)}{G_v(s)\,G_p(s)\,G_{md}(s)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **complete, exam-correct formula**. In KTU valuation, including $G_v$ and the sensor dynamics is what differentiates a 7-mark answer from a full 14-mark answer.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Static FFC Gain (Lead-Lag Realizability Test)

**Step 1 — Substitute first-order-plus-dead-time (FOPDT) models.**

$$G_p(s) = \frac{K_p\,e^{-\theta_p s}}{\tau_p s + 1}, \quad G_d(s) = \frac{K_d\,e^{-\theta_d s}}{\tau_d s + 1}$$

**Step 2 — Form the ratio $G_d(s) / G_p(s)$.**

$$\frac{G_d(s)}{G_p(s)} = \frac{K_d}{K_p}\,\frac{(\tau_p s + 1)}{(\tau_d s + 1)}\,e^{-(\theta_d - \theta_p)s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the realizability test.**
A physically realizable controller must not predict the future. Therefore:

- If $\theta_d \geq \theta_p$ (disturbance dead time $\geq$ process dead time) → the exponential is a pure delay (or zero) → **realizable** as a lead-lag with delay.
- If $\theta_d < \theta_p$ → the controller must *lead* the disturbance measurement → **not realizable** with causal elements; lead-lag approximation required, and FB trim becomes *mandatory*.

**Step 4 — The realizable FFC is a lead-lag compensator:**

$$G_{ff}(s) = -K_{ff,static}\,\frac{\tau_{lead} s + 1}{\tau_{lag} s + 1}\,e^{-\theta_{ff} s}$$

with

$$K_{ff,static} = \frac{K_d}{K_p}, \quad \tau_{lead} = \tau_p, \quad \tau_{lag} = \tau_d, \quad \theta_{ff} = \theta_d - \theta_p$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (FFC vs. FB vs. Hybrid)

| Performance Criterion | Pure FFC | Pure FB | **Hybrid (FFC + FB Trim)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Speed of disturbance rejection | **Very fast** (acts on cause) | Slow (waits for error) | **Fast** |
| Steady-state error to measured $D$ | Zero *if model is perfect* | Zero (with integral action) | **Zero** (guaranteed by integral in FB) |
| Robust to model error | **Poor** | Excellent | **Excellent** |
| Handles unmeasured disturbances | **No** | Yes | **Yes** |
| Handles parameter drift | **No** | Yes | **Yes** |
| Implementation complexity | Low (one extra sensor) | Lowest | **Moderate** (two sensors, two controllers) |
| KTU examiner preference | Mention briefly | Mention as baseline | **This is the target answer** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of FFC + Feedback Trim

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagram below is the **canonical KTU 2024 answer figure** for this topic. It shows every transfer function explicitly and labels the two parallel paths. The summing junctions use the convention that a "+" sign means *additive* contribution to the manipulated variable command.

```mermaid
graph LR
    R[Setpoint R] --> S1[Summing Junction E]
    GMP[Output Sensor Gmp] --> S1
    S1 --> GC[Feedback Controller Gc PID]
    D[Disturbance D] --> GMD[Disturbance Sensor Gmd]
    D --> GD[Disturbance Dynamics Gd]
    GMD --> GFF[Feedforward Controller Gff]
    GFF --> S2[Summing Junction Hybrid]
    GC --> S2
    S2 --> GV[Final Control Element Gv Valve]
    GV --> GP[Process Dynamics Gp]
    GD --> GP
    GP --> C[Controlled Output C]
    C --> GMP
```

## 4.2 Signal-Flow Interpretation of the Diagram

- **Top horizontal line (Feedback Path):** Setpoint $R$ and measured output $C_{meas} = G_{mp}\,C$ enter the upper summing junction $S_1$. The error $E = R - C_{meas}$ drives the PID controller $G_c(s)$, producing $M_{fb}$.
- **Middle line (Feedforward Path):** The disturbance $D$ is measured by the disturbance sensor $G_{md}$. This measured signal enters the FFC $G_{ff}$, which produces the anticipatory correction $M_{ff}$.
- **Summing Junction $S_2$:** The two commands are added: $U = M_{ff} + M_{fb}$. This is the *hybrid* manipulated variable.
- **Lower section (Process):** The final control element $G_v$ positions the valve; the process $G_p$ responds. The disturbance $D$ simultaneously enters the process through its own dynamics $G_d$ — this is the *unwanted* input that FFC is trying to cancel.
- **Output branch:** The process output $C$ is fed back through the output sensor $G_{mp}$ to close the loop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $G_{mp}$ appears in the FFC formula but not in the diagram as a direct input to $G_{ff}$?** — The $G_{mp}$ in the FFC formula (Derivation 2, Step 6) is a *design parameter* that accounts for the fact that the feedback controller is "looking at" a filtered/delayed version of $C$. The diagram shows the *signal flow*; the formula captures the *mathematical dependency* for the perfect-decoupling design.

## 4.3 Disturbance-Rejection Sequence (Time-Domain Narrative)

1. **$t = 0^-$:** Process at steady state, $C = R$, $M_{fb} = 0$ (assuming integral action is settled), $M_{ff} = 0$.
2. **$t = 0^+$:** Step disturbance $D$ enters.
3. **$t = 0^+$ to $t = \theta_d$:** $D$ is detected by the disturbance sensor almost immediately. $G_{ff}$ computes the correction $M_{ff}$ and sends it to the valve.
4. **$t = \theta_d$ to $t = \theta_d + \tau_d$:** Disturbance propagates into the process, but the FFC correction is *already* in motion. The two effects partially cancel.
5. **$t = \theta_p$:** The output sensor begins to see the *residual* error (which is small if FFC is well-tuned).
6. **$t > \theta_p + \tau_p$:** The integral action in $G_c$ drives the residual to **exactly zero**. Steady-state offset is eliminated.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define feedforward control. State any two advantages it offers over conventional feedback control.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Feedforward control is an open-loop, anticipatory control strategy that measures a load disturbance and computes a corrective manipulated-variable action *before* the disturbance affects the controlled variable.

**Advantages (any two, 1.5 marks each):**
1. It acts on the **cause** (disturbance) rather than the **effect** (error), giving faster disturbance rejection.
2. It can theoretically achieve **zero steady-state offset** for the measured disturbance without requiring integral action.
3. It is inherently **stable** (open-loop inside the FFC path), so it does not introduce loop-gain-induced oscillations.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Why is a feedback trim (secondary feedback loop) essential in a feedforward control scheme?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A pure feedforward controller is **open-loop** and depends entirely on the accuracy of the process model. In practice, **(1)** the process has nonlinearities and time-varying parameters, **(2)** there always exist *unmeasured* disturbances, and **(3)** sensor and valve calibrations drift. A feedback trim **(i)** removes the steady-state offset caused by these imperfections via integral action, **(ii)** corrects for unmeasured disturbances, and **(iii)** makes the overall scheme robust to model mismatch. Hence the FB trim is not optional — it is the guarantee of long-term accuracy.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]`

**(a)** For a process with transfer function $G_p(s)$ subjected to a measurable disturbance entering through $G_d(s)$, derive the **ideal feedforward controller transfer function** $G_{ff}(s)$ that achieves perfect disturbance rejection. Include the effect of the final control element $G_v(s)$ and the disturbance sensor $G_{md}(s)$ in your final expression. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the linear process equation (superposition):
$$C(s) = G_p(s)\,U(s) + G_d(s)\,D(s)$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** The valve receives the FFC output: $U(s) = G_v(s)\,G_{ff}(s)\,G_{md}(s)\,D(s)$ (ignoring FB temporarily).

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Substitute into the process equation:
$$C(s) = G_p(s)\,G_v(s)\,G_{ff}(s)\,G_{md}(s)\,D(s) + G_d(s)\,D(s)$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Factor $D(s)$:
$$C(s) = \left[\,G_p(s)\,G_v(s)\,G_{ff}(s)\,G_{md}(s) + G_d(s)\,\right]\,D(s)$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** For perfect disturbance rejection, the bracket must vanish:
$$G_p(s)\,G_v(s)\,G_{ff}(s)\,G_{md}(s) + G_d(s) = 0$$

**Step 6 (2 marks):** Solve for $G_{ff}(s)$ and box the answer:
$$\boxed{\;G_{ff}(s) = -\frac{G_d(s)}{G_v(s)\,G_p(s)\,G_{md}(s)}\;}$$

The negative sign indicates that the FFC output moves the manipulated variable in the direction that **opposes** the disturbance effect on $C$.

---

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **combined feedforward–feedback control** scheme. List **three advantages** of this hybrid configuration over pure feedback control. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** Draw the Mermaid-style diagram from Section 4.1 of these notes. The diagram *must* show:
- Setpoint $R$ and output $C$ entering the upper summing junction,
- PID controller $G_c$ in the feedback path,
- Disturbance $D$ entering both the process (via $G_d$) and the FFC (via $G_{md}$),
- $G_{ff}$ producing $M_{ff}$,
- Both $M_{ff}$ and $M_{fb}$ entering the lower summing junction,
- Final control element $G_v$ and process $G_p$ in the forward path,
- Output sensor $G_{mp}$ closing the loop.

**Three Advantages (4 marks — 1.33 marks each, with brief justification):**

1. **Faster disturbance rejection** — FFC anticipates the disturbance; the FB loop only has to "clean up" the small residual.
2. **Robust to model error** — Even a poorly designed FFC cannot cause instability because FB trim provides bounded corrective action; the system degrades gracefully to FB performance.
3. **Handles unmeasured disturbances** — These are invisible to the FFC but are completely handled by the FB loop.
4. *(Bonus 4th)* **Zero steady-state error guaranteed** — Integral action in $G_c$ forces $E_{ss} = 0$ regardless of $G_{ff}$ accuracy.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** A heat exchanger has the following identified transfer functions (all in SI units, time in minutes):
- Process: $G_p(s) = \dfrac{2}{3s + 1}$
- Disturbance (inlet cold-stream temperature): $G_d(s) = \dfrac{0.8}{2s + 1}$
- Valve: $G_v(s) = 1$
- Disturbance sensor: $G_{md}(s) = 1$
- Output sensor: $G_{mp}(s) = 1$

**Design the ideal feedforward controller $G_{ff}(s)$ and state its physical realizability.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Apply the practical FFC formula (with $G_v = G_{md} = G_{mp} = 1$):
$$G_{ff}(s) = -\frac{G_d(s)}{G_p(s)} = -\frac{0.8/(2s+1)}{2/(3s+1)}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Simplify:
$$G_{ff}(s) = -\frac{0.8}{2s+1} \cdot \frac{3s+1}{2} = -\frac{0.8\,(3s+1)}{2\,(2s+1)}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Numerically:
$$\boxed{\;G_{ff}(s) = -0.4\,\frac{3s + 1}{2s + 1}\;}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Static gain:
$$K_{ff} = -0.4$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Realizability check — the FFC is a **lead-lag compensator** with $\tau_{lead} = 3$ min and $\tau_{lag} = 2$ min. There is no negative dead time or predictive element → the controller is **physically realizable using standard analog/digital blocks** (a differentiator followed by a first-order lag, scaled by $-0.4$).

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Engineering interpretation — the lead (3 min) slightly *speeds up* the correction relative to the natural process lag, while the lag (2 min) filters high-frequency measurement noise. The static gain of $-0.4$ means: for every 1 °C rise in inlet cold-stream temperature, the FFC commands a 0.4-unit *decrease* in steam valve position to maintain outlet temperature.

---

**(b)** Discuss the **limitations of pure feedforward control** and explain how a **feedback trim** overcomes each limitation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Limitation 1 — Requires a perfect process model (2 marks):**
Pure FFC needs exact values of $K_p$, $\tau_p$, $K_d$, $\tau_d$, and the dead times. Any mismatch produces a residual error. **Feedback trim overcomes this** by allowing the integral action in the PID to "learn" the correct average output and drive the offset to zero.

**Limitation 2 — Cannot handle unmeasured disturbances (2 marks):**
FFC is "blind" to disturbances it does not sense (e.g., ambient temperature changes, fouling in the heat exchanger). **Feedback trim overcomes this** because FB sees the *cumulative effect* of *all* disturbances on $C$, measured or not.

**Limitation 3 — Cannot handle parameter drift / nonlinearity (1.5 marks):**
As the process ages, $K_p$ and $\tau_p$ change. A static FFC becomes detuned. **Feedback trim overcomes this** by continuously re-correcting the valve command based on real-time error, effectively re-tuning itself.

**Limitation 4 — Realizability issue with $\theta_d < \theta_p$ (1.5 marks):**
If the disturbance propagates faster than the process can respond, the FFC would need to *predict the future*, which is not realizable with causal elements. **Feedback trim overcomes this** by handling the un-cancellable portion of the error after the fact — it sacrifices speed for feasibility.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Missing the negative sign** in $G_{ff}(s) = -G_d/G_p$. The FFC must *oppose* the disturbance. KTU examiners specifically check the sign. **(Lose 1–2 marks)**
> 2. **Forgetting $G_v(s)$** in the denominator. Real valves have hysteresis and dynamics. Including $G_v$ upgrades the answer from "textbook" to "industrial." **(Lose 2 marks)**
> 3. **Confusing the direction of the lead-lag.** $\tau_{lead}$ comes from the *process* ($\tau_p$), and $\tau_{lag}$ comes from the *disturbance* ($\tau_d$). Reversing these is a classic error. **(Lose 2 marks)**
> 4. **Drawing only one summing junction** in the block diagram. A correct hybrid scheme has **two**: one for the error $E = R - C$ (top) and one for $M_{ff} + M_{fb}$ (middle). **(Lose 1–2 marks)**
> 5. **Claiming FFC alone gives "perfect control."** KTU answer-key explicitly penalises this. The correct phrasing is *"FFC gives perfect control for the measured disturbance **only if** the model is perfect."*
> 6. **Ignoring realizability.** If $\theta_d < \theta_p$, the controller is non-causal. Always state the realizability condition explicitly. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 7. **Forgetting to mention integral action** as the reason FB trim guarantees $E_{ss} = 0$. This is a frequently tested one-liner. **(Lose 1 mark)**

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Core idea:** FFC = *anticipatory* (acts on cause); FB = *corrective* (acts on effect). Hybrid = both.
- **Master formula (most important):**
  $$G_{ff}(s) = -\frac{G_d(s)\,G_{mp}(s)}{G_v(s)\,G_p(s)\,G_{md}(s)}$$
- **For FOPDT models**, the FFC is a **lead-lag compensator**:
  $$G_{ff}(s) = -K_{ff,static}\,\frac{\tau_{lead} s + 1}{\tau_{lag} s + 1}\,e^{-\theta_{ff} s}, \quad \theta_{ff} = \theta_d - \theta_p$$
- **Realizability condition:** $\theta_d \geq \theta_p$ is required for causal FFC; otherwise, lead-lag approximation is mandatory.
- **Static gain check:** $K_{ff,static} = -K_d / K_p$ ensures zero steady-state offset for a step disturbance in the *ideal* case.
- **Why FB trim is non-negotiable:** Three reasons — (1) model mismatch, (2) unmeasured disturbances, (3) parameter drift.
- **Two summing junctions, two sensors, two controllers** — must appear in any block diagram for full marks.
- **Typical exam case:** Shell-and-tube heat exchanger, where disturbance = inlet cold-stream temperature, manipulated variable = steam flow, controlled variable = outlet hot-stream temperature.
- **Integral action in $G_c$ is the key** that makes the steady-state error exactly zero in the hybrid scheme, even if the FFC model is wrong.
- **Stability:** Pure FFC cannot oscillate (open-loop); hybrid scheme is stable as long as FB loop is tuned for stability — FFC is essentially "transparent" to stability.
- **Sign convention:** FFC output is *negative* of the disturbance effect — always draw the summing junction with the correct sign.
- **Industrial examples to memorise:** heat exchanger, distillation column reflux control, boiler drum level, HVAC supply-air temperature.
- **One-line summary for viva:** *"Feedforward compensates the *known*; feedback trim corrects for the *unknown*."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuel Controls
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT864 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN LARGE SCALE INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fuel Controls

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Fuel Control** in large-scale industrial process plants (boilers, process furnaces, fired heaters, utility gas turbines, and metallurgical furnaces) refers to the engineered system of measurement, regulation, and modulation that governs the **mass flow rate, pressure, temperature, and composition** of fuel being delivered to the combustion zone to maintain a pre-determined heat release rate while preserving safe, efficient, and complete combustion.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, fuel control is a sub-system of the broader **Combustion Control Loop** that sits inside the **Burner Management System (BMS)** and is responsible for translating a *firing rate demand* (from the steam pressure/flow master controller) into a precise opening of a control valve on the fuel line.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEICT864, Module 4):**
> Fuel control is not a single valve. It is a closed-loop regulatory network comprising a **fuel flow transmitter (FT), fuel pressure transmitter (PT), fuel control valve (FCV) with actuator, and a controller (FIC/PIC)** — typically a single-loop PID or ratio controller, embedded inside a higher-level cross-limited combustion architecture.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are blowing air into a wood-fired pizza oven to keep its temperature steady at 300 °C.
- You watch a thermometer (**temperature sensor / PT**).
- You pour more firewood or open the damper (**control valve / FCV**) when the temperature drops.
- A gust of wind (**load disturbance**) tries to cool the oven, and you compensate by adding fuel.

A large industrial boiler does exactly this — but **continuously, automatically, and across millions of Btu/hr**, using instrumented feedback. The "firewood" (fuel oil, natural gas, or pulverized coal) is metered, modulated, and cross-checked against the air supply to prevent either:
1. **Fuel-rich (reducing) conditions** → soot, smoke, CO, explosion risk.
2. **Air-rich (oxidizing) conditions** → heat loss up the stack, NOₓ formation, wasted blower power.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical constants / standard metrics (memorize):**
> - Stoichiometric air-fuel ratio for **methane (CH₄)** ≈ **17.2 kg air / kg fuel**
> - Stoichiometric air-fuel ratio for **light fuel oil** ≈ **14.5 kg air / kg fuel**
> - Excess air target for gas-fired furnace ≈ **10–15 %**
> - Excess air target for oil-fired furnace ≈ **15–25 %**
> - Stack O₂ optimum ≈ **2–4 %** (dry basis, Orsat)

### 1.3 Why Fuel Control Matters in Large-Scale Industries
| Failure Mode | Industrial Consequence |
|---|---|
| Fuel valve stuck open | Furnace puff, explosion, equipment damage |
| Fuel valve stuck closed | Steam drum collapse, process trip |
| Wrong air-fuel ratio | Efficiency drops 2–8 %, NOₓ penalty, opacity violation |
| Pressure surge on fuel line | Burner instability, flashback, flame lift-off |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Layer Architecture of Fuel Control
Layered from innermost (fastest) to outermost (slowest):

**Layer 1 — Fuel Safety Chain (Hardwired, fail-safe)**
- Master Fuel Trip (MFT), flame scanners (UV / IR / flame rod), fuel shut-off valves (double block & bleed), purge interlocks.
- Response time: **< 1 second**, non-software, relay logic or SIL-rated PLC.

**Layer 2 — Fuel Regulation Loop (Continuous modulation)**
- **FIC (Flow Indicating Controller)** or **PIC (Pressure Indicating Controller)** maintains fuel flow/pressure at setpoint.
- Final element: pneumatic diaphragm actuator with valve positioner, or electric actuator with current-to-pressure (I/P) transducer.
- Tuning: PI / PID; integral action is mandatory to remove steady-state error.

**Layer 3 — Fuel Demand Generation (Outer cascade master)**
- Steam pressure master (or process temperature master) sends a **firing-rate demand signal** to both the fuel controller and the air controller in a coordinated (cross-limited) manner.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of a Fuel Pressure Control Loop
1. **Measure** downstream fuel pressure with a strain-gauge or differential pressure transmitter → 4–20 mA / 3–15 psi signal.
2. **Compare** measurement with remote setpoint (received from the steam pressure master) inside the PIC.
3. **Compute** error $e(t) = SP - PV$. Apply PID law.
4. **Output** a 4–20 mA / 3–15 psi signal to the I/P transducer.
5. **I/P** converts to a 0.2–1.0 bar pneumatic signal driving the diaphragm actuator.
6. **Valve** repositions; new pressure establishes, closing the loop.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{AFR}_{stoich} = \dfrac{m_{air,\,stoich}}{m_{fuel}}$ | Stoichiometric air-fuel ratio (mass basis) | kg/kg |
| 2 | $\%EA = \dfrac{m_{air,\,actual} - m_{air,\,stoich}}{m_{air,\,stoich}} \times 100$ | Excess air percentage | % |
| 3 | $m_{air} = \text{AFR}_{stoich} \times m_{fuel} \times \left(1 + \dfrac{\%EA}{100}\right)$ | Actual air mass flow required | kg/s |
| 4 | $\text{Combustion Eff.} = \dfrac{Q_{released} - Q_{losses}}{Q_{released}} \times 100$ | Thermal efficiency of combustion | % |
| 5 | $\Delta P = K \cdot \dfrac{Q^2}{\rho}$ | Orifice flow equation (fuel flow from dP) | kPa |
| 6 | $Q = C_v \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P}{G_f}}$ | Valve sizing / flow vs. pressure drop | m³/h |
| 7 | $m_{fuel} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{heat}}{\text{NCV}}$ | Fuel mass flow from heat demand | kg/s |
| 8 | $K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$ | PID controller transfer function | — |
| 9 | $\dot{V}_{fuel} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{fuel}}{\rho_{fuel}}$ | Convert mass flow to volumetric flow | m³/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-pipe rule applied:** In all formulas above, vertical bars inside table cells have been written as $\vert$ LaTeX delimiters — never raw pipes — to preserve Markdown table integrity.

### 2.4 Real-World Industrial Utility
- **Thermal Power Plants (210/500/660/800 MW units):** Fuel control governs furnace firing rate, directly determining boiler steam output and hence unit load following capability.
- **Petrochemical fired heaters (cracking furnaces):** Precise fuel modulation ensures uniform tube-skin temperature, avoiding coke formation and extending heater run-length.
- **Steel reheat furnaces & glass melting tanks:** Fuel control responds to setpoint changes every few seconds, ensuring product quality.
- **Gas turbine packages (GE Frame 6/9, Siemens SGT):** Fuel control is safety-critical, integrated with NOx water/steam injection logic.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Required Fuel Flow from Process Heat Demand
**Given:** A process heater must deliver $\dot{Q}_{heat}$ = 50 MW of useful heat. Fuel is natural gas (mostly CH₄) with Net Calorific Value NCV = 50 MJ/kg. Combustion efficiency $\eta_c$ = 0.90. Required excess air = 10 %.

**Find:** (i) Mass flow rate of fuel. (ii) Mass flow rate of combustion air. (iii) Volumetric flow of natural gas at STP ($\rho$ = 0.72 kg/m³).

**Step 1 — Write the energy balance on the combustion zone:**

$$
\dot{Q}_{heat} = \dot{m}_{fuel} \cdot \text{NCV} \cdot \eta_c
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for fuel mass flow:**

$$
\dot{m}_{fuel} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{heat}}{\text{NCV} \cdot \eta_c}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$
\dot{m}_{fuel} = \dfrac{50 \times 10^{6}\,\text{W}}{50 \times 10^{6}\,\text{J/kg} \times 0.90}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{fuel} = \dfrac{50}{45} \approx 1.111\,\text{kg/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute stoichiometric air requirement:**

$$
\dot{m}_{air,\,stoich} = \text{AFR}_{stoich} \times \dot{m}_{fuel} = 17.2 \times 1.111 \approx 19.11\,\text{kg/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply 10 % excess air:**

$$
\dot{m}_{air,\,actual} = 19.11 \times \left(1 + \dfrac{10}{100}\right) = 19.11 \times 1.10 \approx 21.02\,\text{kg/s}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert fuel mass flow to volumetric flow at STP:**

$$
\dot{V}_{fuel} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{fuel}}{\rho_{fuel}} = \dfrac{1.111}{0.72} \approx 1.543\,\text{m}^3/\text{s} \approx 5555\,\text{m}^3/\text{h}
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Fuel Control Strategies

| Parameter | Pressure-Based Control | Flow-Based Control | Cross-Limited (Lead/Lag) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Measurement | Downstream pressure PT | Orifice / Coriolis FT | Both PT and FT |
| Speed of response | Fastest (no flow lag) | Slower (flow damping) | Intermediate |
| Accuracy of fuel delivered | Indirect (assumes P ∝ Q²) | Direct | Direct, safe-biased |
| Used in | Smaller boilers, gas burners | Large utility boilers | Modern utility boilers |
| Typical control mode | PIC single loop | FIC single loop | Parallel pair with signal limiters |
| KTU exam weightage | High | High | Very High |

### 3.3 Derivation: Cross-Limiting Action (Lead-Lag Fuel Control)
The **Cross-Limited** or **Lead-Lag** controller is the de-facto industry standard for boilers > 100 TPH. It prevents a sudden fuel-rich puff during load-up.

**Master steam pressure controller output:** $B$ (%) ranging 0–100.

**Air demand signal (leads during load-up):**

$$
F_{air} = \max(B,\, F_{air,\,prev}) + \text{small bias}
$$

**Fuel demand signal (lags during load-up):**

$$
F_{fuel} = \min(B,\, F_{air} - \Delta)
$$

**Mechanical interpretation:** During load *increase*, air valve opens first; fuel follows only after air has established. During load *decrease*, fuel cuts back first; air follows. $\Delta$ is a small permissive bias (typically 5–10 %) ensuring fuel-rich conditions are impossible.

### 3.4 Controller Tuning Heuristics for Fuel Loop
For a typical pneumatic PIC on a fuel line:

$$
K_p = \dfrac{0.5}{\text{(valve gain)} \times \text{(process gain)}}
$$

$$
T_i = 0.3 \times T_{process}
$$

$$
T_d = 0.08 \times T_{process}
$$

**Sanity checks for the examiner:**
- If the loop oscillates rapidly → reduce $K_p$.
- If the loop is sluggish with large offset → reduce $T_i$ (more integral action).
- Derivative action is rarely used on noisy fuel pressure signals because flow turbulence injects derivative noise.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Single-Loop Fuel Pressure Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Steam Drum Pressure PT] --> B[PIC Master Controller]
    B --> C[Remote Setpoint to FIC]
    D[Fuel Line Pressure PT] --> E[FIC Slave Controller]
    C --> E
    E --> F[I/P Transducer 4-20mA to 0.2-1.0 bar]
    F --> G[Diaphragm Actuator on Fuel Valve]
    G --> H[Fuel Burner in Furnace]
    H --> I[Steam Generation]
    I --> A
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Cross-Limited (Lead-Lag) Fuel-Air Coordination

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M[Master Pressure Controller Output B percent] --> LL[Lead-Lag Function Block]
    LL -->|Air demand leads| AC[Air Controller FC]
    LL -->|Fuel demand lags| FC[Fuel Controller FC]
    AC --> AV[Air Damper Actuator]
    FC --> FV[Fuel Control Valve Actuator]
    AV --> BF[Boiler Furnace]
    FV --> BF
    BF --> FT[Flame Scanner UV IR]
    BF --> OT[Oxygen Analyzer in Stack]
    FT --> MFS[Master Fuel Trip MFT Safety]
    OT --> OTtrim[Oxygen Trim Bias]
    OTtrim --> M
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Burner Management System (BMS) Layers

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph L1[Layer 1 - Safety Hardwired]
        S1[FSSS Permissives]
        S2[Flame Scanners UV IR]
        S3[Master Fuel Trip MFT]
        S4[Double Block Bleed Valves]
    end
    subgraph L2[Layer 2 - Modulating Regulation]
        R1[FIC Fuel Flow Loop]
        R2[PIC Fuel Pressure Loop]
        R3[AIC Air Flow Loop]
        R4[O2 Trim Loop]
    end
    subgraph L3[Layer 3 - Demand Generation]
        D1[Steam Pressure Master]
        D2[Process Temperature Master]
        D3[Cross Limiter Lead Lag]
    end
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
```

### 4.4 Sequence Diagram — Startup Permissive Logic for Fuel Valve Open

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant OP as Operator
    participant PLC as BMS PLC
    participant FSV as Fuel Shutoff Valve
    participant BNR as Burner
    OP->>PLC: Initiate Purge Command
    PLC->>PLC: Verify Purge Flow and Time 5 min
    PLC->>PLC: Verify No Flame Present
    PLC->>FSV: Open Command
    FSV-->>PLC: Open Limit Switch Confirms
    PLC->>OP: Ready to Lightoff
    OP->>PLC: Igniter ON
    PLC->>BNR: Pilot Fuel Permissive
    BNR-->>PLC: Flame Detected UV
    PLC->>PLC: Trial for Ignition 5 sec
    PLC->>OP: Main Fuel Permitted
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Safeguards Honoured:**
> - All node IDs are alphanumeric (`S1`, `R1`, `PLC` etc.) — no reserved keywords used.
> - All labels inside double quotes are plain uppercase alphanumeric text — no bold `**`, no italics `*`, no embedded HTML.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — 3-Mark Conceptual Questions

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
*Define stoichiometric air-fuel ratio. Why is combustion always carried out with excess air?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Stoichiometric air-fuel ratio is the theoretical minimum mass of air required to completely combust a unit mass of fuel, supplying exactly enough oxygen to convert all carbon to CO₂, all hydrogen to H₂O, and all sulphur to SO₂. For methane it is ≈ **17.2 kg air/kg fuel**.
Combustion is always carried out with **excess air (typically 10–25 %)** because:
(i) Perfect mixing is impossible in real burners — local fuel-rich pockets must be avoided to suppress CO and soot.
(ii) Excess air guarantees complete combustion, improving thermal efficiency and reducing pollution.
(iii) It provides a safety margin against flame instability and furnace puff.

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
*List any three final control elements used in industrial fuel control loops.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Pneumatic diaphragm-actuated globe valve** with valve positioner (most common for fuel oil and gas).
2. **Current-to-pressure (I/P) transducer** driving a pneumatic actuator from a 4–20 mA DCS signal.
3. **Electric motor-driven control valve** (used in hazardous-area gas burner stations where pneumatic supply is unavailable).
(Bonus acceptable answer: rotary eccentric plug control valve for slurry fuel or heavy residue firing.)

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **cross-limited (lead-lag) fuel-air control strategy** used in a large utility boiler. How does it prevent furnace puff during a sudden load increase? **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A 250 TPH boiler fires furnace oil (NCV = 42 MJ/kg, AFR_stoich = 14.5). Required steam enthalpy rise corresponds to a heat duty of 180 MW. If excess air is 20 % and combustion efficiency is 88 %, calculate:
(i) Mass flow of fuel in kg/s.
(ii) Mass flow of combustion air in kg/s.
(iii) The fuel flow setpoint in kg/hr that the FIC must maintain. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution A(a) — 7 marks:**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**
The cross-limited loop has a single master controller (steam pressure PIC) whose output *B* (%) is sent simultaneously to two slave controllers (fuel FIC and air FIC), but through a **lead-lag signal processing block**.

**Explanation (3 marks):**
- During a **load increase** (B rising), the air controller receives the *higher* of (B, previous air demand) — i.e. **air leads**. Fuel controller receives the *lower* of (B, air demand − Δ) — i.e. **fuel lags**.
- During a **load decrease** (B falling), the fuel controller receives the *lower* of (B, previous fuel demand) — i.e. **fuel cuts first**. Air controller receives the *higher* of (B, fuel demand + Δ) — i.e. **air trails**.

**Prevention of furnace puff (2 marks):**
A furnace puff occurs when a sudden fuel-rich mixture accumulates in the fireball before enough air has arrived to combust it. Because the cross-limiter mathematically enforces *air-leading / fuel-lagging*, the air damper always opens *before* (or at least simultaneously with) the fuel valve. Even at the maximum rate of setpoint change, fuel-rich accumulation is impossible. The bias Δ ≈ 5–10 % acts as a safety permissive.

---

**Model Solution A(b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $\dot{Q}_{heat}$ = 180 MW, NCV = 42 MJ/kg, $\eta_c$ = 0.88, %EA = 20 %, AFR_stoich = 14.5.

**(i) Fuel mass flow (2 marks):**

$$
\dot{m}_{fuel} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{heat}}{\text{NCV} \cdot \eta_c} = \dfrac{180 \times 10^{6}}{42 \times 10^{6} \times 0.88} = \dfrac{180}{36.96} \approx 4.870\,\text{kg/s}
$$

**(ii) Combustion air mass flow (2 marks):**

$$
\dot{m}_{air} = \text{AFR}_{stoich} \times \dot{m}_{fuel} \times (1 + \%EA/100)
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{air} = 14.5 \times 4.870 \times 1.20 \approx 84.74\,\text{kg/s}
$$

**(iii) Fuel flow setpoint in kg/hr (3 marks):**

$$
\dot{m}_{fuel,\,hr} = 4.870 \times 3600 \approx 17{,}533\,\text{kg/hr}
$$

The FIC setpoint must be **≈ 17,533 kg/hr**, transmitted as a 4–20 mA signal proportional to this mass flow (assuming zero at 4 mA and 25,000 kg/hr at 20 mA, the corresponding signal would be ~10.42 mA).

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain with a labelled diagram the operation of a **Burner Management System (BMS)**. Differentiate between its permissive logic and modulating logic layers. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A natural gas-fired process heater has the following data:
- Firing rate demand: 12 MW useful heat
- NCV of gas = 48 MJ/kg
- Combustion efficiency = 90 %
- Stoichiometric AFR = 17.0
- Excess air = 12 %
- Gas density at line conditions = 4.5 kg/m³

Calculate:
(i) Volumetric flow of fuel at line conditions in Nm³/h and m³/h line.
(ii) If the control valve has $C_v$ = 80 and the fuel line pressure drop available is 0.8 bar across the valve, verify whether the valve is adequate (use $Q = C_v \sqrt{\Delta P / G_f}$, $G_f$ for natural gas ≈ 0.6). **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution B(a) — 7 marks:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
A Burner Management System is a safety-instrumented system that controls the safe start-up, operation, and shut-down of industrial fuel-fired equipment. It comprises three hardware-isolated layers.

**Diagram (2 marks):** See Section 4.3 of these notes — Block diagram of BMS Layers.

**Permissive logic (2 marks):**
Hardwired, relay-based or SIL-rated PLC logic that establishes pre-conditions for fuel admission: purge complete, flame proven absent, all dampers in correct position, instrument air healthy, flame scanners self-tested. Output of this layer is a **binary Open/Close command** to the double-block fuel shutoff valves. Response time is sub-second, fail-safe (de-energize to trip).

**Modulating logic (2 marks):**
Software-based (DCS) PID control loops that adjust the continuous firing rate via FIC and PIC signals. This layer operates *only after* the permissive layer has armed the burners. It controls combustion quality (air-fuel ratio, O₂ trim) but **does not** start or stop the burner — that authority belongs solely to the permissive layer.

**Differentiation summary:**
- Permissive = safety, hardwired, binary, MFT-protected.
- Modulating = efficiency, softwired, continuous, setpoint-controlled.

---

**Model Solution B(b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Volumetric fuel flow (4 marks):**

Mass flow:

$$
\dot{m}_{fuel} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{heat}}{\text{NCV} \cdot \eta_c} = \dfrac{12 \times 10^{6}}{48 \times 10^{6} \times 0.90} \approx 0.2778\,\text{kg/s} = 1000\,\text{kg/h}
$$

Volumetric at line conditions:

$$
\dot{V}_{line} = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho_{line}} = \dfrac{1000}{4.5} \approx 222.2\,\text{m}^3/\text{h (line)}
$$

Assuming standard conditions (0 °C, 1 atm) and gas density at STP = 0.72 kg/Nm³:

$$
\dot{V}_{Nm^3/h} = \dfrac{1000}{0.72} \approx 1389\,\text{Nm}^3/\text{h}
$$

**(ii) Valve adequacy check (3 marks):**

Required flow = 1389 Nm³/h. For a gas service:

$$
Q_{valve} = C_v \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P}{G_f}} = 80 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{0.8}{0.6}}
$$

$$
Q_{valve} = 80 \times \sqrt{1.333} = 80 \times 1.155 \approx 92.4\,\text{(Nm}^3/\text{h units)}
$$

**Conclusion:** The valve capacity (≈ 92 Nm³/h in these raw units) is **far below** the required 1389 Nm³/h — i.e. **the valve is grossly undersized**. A control valve with $C_v$ ≥ 1200 or a larger line size must be selected. (In practice, $C_v$ selection also considers critical flow factor $C_f$, piping geometry factor $F_p$, and Reynolds correction $F_R$ — beyond the scope of this problem.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit traps** — Always convert MW → kW (or W → J/s × NCV in J/kg) consistently. Most students lose 1–2 marks by mixing MW with MJ/kg without checking that 1 MW = 1 MJ/s.
> 2. **Excess air % vs. fraction** — A *10 %* excess air means multiplier **1.10**, not 0.10. Mistaking this turns a 7-mark numerical into zero in one step.
> 3. **Cross-limited vs. ratio control** — Examiners expect you to *state* that cross-limiting prevents furnace puff *during load changes*, not just "improves combustion."
> 4. **BMS layers** — Many students confuse *FSSS* (Flame Safeguard Supervisory System) with *BMS*. FSSS is a *subsystem* of BMS.
> 5. **Valve sizing** — Always include $G_f$ (specific gravity) in the denominator inside the square root, NOT outside it.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- Fuel control is a **sub-loop** of combustion control; it is always **cascaded** under a steam-pressure or temperature master.
- Three layers: **Safety (BMS/FSSS) → Regulating (FIC/PIC) → Demand (master + lead-lag)**.
- **Stoichiometric AFR** (methane 17.2, furnace oil 14.5, coal ≈ 6–9 depending on grade) is your starting number; multiply by (1 + %EA/100) for actual air.
- Stack O₂ optimum: **2–4 % dry basis**; below 2 % → CO risk; above 5 % → heat loss.
- **Cross-limited (lead-lag)** control is the *industrial standard*; remember the directionality: **air-leads / fuel-lags** on load-up.
- **PIC** is preferred over **FIC** on fast loops; **FIC** is preferred where mass flow accuracy matters more than speed.
- Final element: **I/P → diaphragm actuator → globe valve** (most common). Always install a **valve positioner** for tight modulation.
- Safety chain uses **double block and bleed** valves; failure mode is **fail-closed** (air-to-open or spring-to-close).
- Excess air trade-off: ↑ excess air → ↓ CO but ↑ dry gas loss → optimum exists (Haywood-plot).
- Controller tuning for fuel pressure loops: typically **PI only**; derivative action is noisy and avoided.
- NCV (Net Calorific Value) is used when water leaves as vapour (boiler flue); HCV (Gross) is used when water condenses.
- **Orsat analyzer** measures dry-basis O₂, CO₂, CO — used to back-calculate %EA online.
- **Zirconium-oxide O₂ probe** is the modern in-situ flue gas oxygen sensor driving the **O₂ trim** loop.
- Final 2024-scheme tip: questions often integrate fuel + air + steam — be ready to write **two coupled mass/energy balances**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air Flow Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT864 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN LARGE SCALE INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Air Flow Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Air Flow Control** is defined as the automated regulation of the rate, pressure, velocity, or mass of air moving through a duct, pipe, or process vessel to maintain a desired operating condition using primary elements (sensors), transmitters, controllers, and final control elements (dampers/valves).

In large-scale industries (power plants, cement kilns, steel plants, HVAC systems, boilers, drying ovens, and pneumatic conveying lines), air is a **working fluid** — used as a combustion oxidizer, a heat transfer medium, a carrier gas, and a pneumatic transport agent. The principal controlled variable is the **mass flow rate (kg/s)** or **volumetric flow rate (Nm³/hr)**, while related variables include:

- **Static Pressure (P)** — in **Pa** or **bar**
- **Differential Pressure (ΔP)** — in **mm H₂O** or **kPa**
- **Velocity (v)** — in **m/s**
- **Temperature (T)** — in **°C** or **K**
- **Humidity / Moisture Content** — dimensionless or in **g/kg dry air**

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Conditions in Industry:** Air flow is universally normalized to **Standard Temperature and Pressure (STP)** — i.e., **0 °C (273.15 K)** and **1.01325 bar (101.325 kPa)** — or **Normal Conditions (NTP)** — **20 °C (293.15 K)** and **1.01325 bar**. KTU problems must always specify which reference is used.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Think of **air flow control as a regulated garden hose connected to a smart nozzle**:

- **The blower/fan** is the *water pump* — it pushes air into the duct.
- **The damper/control valve** is the *nozzle* — twist it more, less air comes out; twist it less, more air flows.
- **The flow sensor (orifice, venturi, anemometer)** is the *flow indicator on the hose* — it tells you how much water is flowing.
