- **The controller (PID)** is *your brain* — it watches the flow indicator and adjusts the nozzle knob to keep flow exactly where you want it.

Now scale this up to a **500 MW boiler** that needs precisely **250 tonnes of air per hour** to combust coal cleanly. Too little air → smoke and soot. Too much air → heat loss up the chimney. The control loop must react within **seconds** to load changes from the grid. That is the job of **Air Flow Control**.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exam:** Air flow control is fundamentally a **dynamic, multivariable, non-linear** control problem. The dominant non-linearity comes from the **square-root relationship** between differential pressure and volumetric flow rate through a primary element.

---

### 1.3 Classification of Air Flow Variables

| Variable | Symbol | Engineering Unit | Typical Range in Industry |
|----------|--------|------------------|---------------------------|
| Volumetric Flow Rate | Q | Nm³/hr, m³/s | 100 – 5,00,000 Nm³/hr |
| Mass Flow Rate | ṁ | kg/s, kg/hr | 0.1 – 50,000 kg/hr |
| Velocity | v | m/s | 5 – 40 m/s (duct) |
| Static Pressure | P | kPa, mbar | 0.5 – 50 kPa (gauge) |
| Differential Pressure | ΔP | kPa, mmH₂O | 0.1 – 50 kPa |
| Temperature | T | °C | 20 – 800 °C (hot air) |
| Density | ρ | kg/m³ | 0.6 – 1.2 kg/m³ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physics Behind Air Flow Measurement

All primary air flow elements (orifice, venturi, pitot, nozzle) ultimately rely on **two foundational equations of fluid mechanics** — Bernoulli's equation and the Continuity equation.

#### 2.1.1 Continuity Equation (Conservation of Mass)

For an **incompressible** fluid in a closed duct:

$$Q = A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$$

where:
- $A_1, A_2$ = cross-sectional areas at two points (m²)
- $v_1, v_2$ = velocities at those points (m/s)
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (m³/s)

> [!NOTE]
> Air at low velocities (< 100 m/s) and moderate temperatures behaves approximately as an **incompressible fluid**. At high speeds (> 0.3 × Mach number) or high temperatures, **compressibility corrections** become mandatory.

#### 2.1.2 Bernoulli's Equation (Energy Conservation)

For an **ideal, steady, incompressible, inviscid** flow along a streamline:

$$P_1 + \frac{1}{2} \rho v_1^2 + \rho g z_1 = P_2 + \frac{1}{2} \rho v_2^2 + \rho g z_2$$

When the duct is horizontal ($z_1 = z_2$), the gravitational term drops:

$$P_1 - P_2 = \frac{1}{2} \rho (v_2^2 - v_1^2)$$

This pressure drop is the **theoretical basis for differential-pressure-type flow meters**.

### 2.2 The Square-Root Law (Most Important Relationship in Air Flow Control)

For an **orifice plate**, **venturi**, **flow nozzle**, or **annubar**, the volumetric flow rate is given by:

$$Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{\frac{2 \, \Delta P}{\rho \, (1 - \beta^4)}}$$

where:
- $C_d$ = **discharge coefficient** (typically 0.6 – 0.65 for orifice)
- $A_2$ = throat area (m²)
- $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$ = differential pressure (Pa)
- $\rho$ = air density (kg/m³)
- $\beta = d/D$ = diameter ratio (throat / pipe)
- $1 - \beta^4$ = **velocity-of-approach factor**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Forgetting the **velocity-of-approach factor** $(1-\beta^4)^{-1/2}$. For $\beta > 0.5$, this correction can exceed **3–5 %** and is essential for high marks.

### 2.3 Mass Flow Formulation

Mass flow rate is obtained by multiplying volumetric flow by density:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{\frac{2 \, \rho \, \Delta P}{1 - \beta^4}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note that mass flow is proportional to **√ΔP × √ρ**, while volumetric flow is proportional to **√ΔP / √ρ**. This distinction is critical in **hot-air applications** where density changes with temperature.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $Q = A v$ | Continuity relation | m³/s |
| 2 | $\Delta P = \frac{1}{2}\rho v^2$ | Dynamic pressure (Pitot) | Pa |
| 3 | $Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho(1-\beta^4)}}$ | Orifice / venturi volumetric flow | m³/s |
| 4 | $\dot{m} = C_d A_2 \sqrt{\frac{2 \rho \Delta P}{1-\beta^4}}$ | Orifice / venturi mass flow | kg/s |
| 5 | $Q \propto \sqrt{\Delta P}$ | Square-root law (linearization target) | — |
| 6 | $P = \rho R_s T$ | Ideal gas law for air | Pa, kg/m³, J/(kg·K), K |
| 7 | $R_{s,\text{air}} = 287.058$ | Specific gas constant for dry air | J/(kg·K) |
| 8 | $C_v = \frac{Q}{N_1} \sqrt{\frac{\rho_1}{\Delta P}}$ | Valve flow coefficient | — |
| 9 | $\tau \frac{dQ}{dt} + Q = K \cdot u$ | First-order air dynamics model | s, kg/s |
| 10 | $G_c(s) = K_c \left(1 + \frac{1}{\tau_I s} + \tau_D s\right)$ | PID controller (ideal form) | — |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** The square-root law drives the requirement for **square-root extractors** (hardware modules or software functions) in the DCS. Without linearization, the loop gain varies with operating point, making PID tuning very difficult. KTU questions frequently ask why square-root extraction is needed — the answer is **loop-gain stabilization for linear control**.

### 2.5 Real-World Importance in Large-Scale Industries

- **Boiler Combustion Control (Power Plants):** Air-to-fuel ratio must be maintained within **±2 %** to comply with NOₓ and CO emission norms. Air flow control is a major sub-loop of the **cascaded boiler control strategy**.
- **Cement & Lime Kilns:** Secondary and tertiary air flow is controlled to maintain **calcination temperature** (~1450 °C) and **clinker quality**.
- **Steel Industry (Blast Furnace):** Hot blast air flow (stoves) regulates tuyère combustion and tuyère raceway temperature.
- **HVAC of Pharma/Cleanrooms:** Laminar flow, ΔP cascades, and air-change-per-hour (ACH) — often **20–600 ACH** depending on cleanliness class.
- **Pneumatic Conveying (Food, Plastic, Cement):** Carrier-air velocity in the **dilute-phase** regime must be maintained above saltation velocity (typically 15–25 m/s for cement).

---

### 2.6 Control Valve / Damper Sizing (Brief)

The control element for air flow is usually a **butterfly valve, louver damper, or variable-pitch fan**. The valve authority and $C_v$ sizing are crucial:

$$C_v = \frac{Q}{N_1} \sqrt{\frac{\rho / \rho_{\text{ref}}}{\Delta P / \Delta P_{\text{ref}}}}$$

For **inherent equal-percentage characteristic**, the installed flow at lift $l$ is:

$$Q(l) = Q_{\max} \cdot R^{\,(l - 1)}$$

where $R$ is the turn-down ratio (typically 30–50 for butterfly dampers).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Volumetric Flow Through an Orifice Plate (with Velocity-of-Approach Correction)

We will derive the equation governing the flow of air through a **concentric sharp-edged orifice plate**, the most common primary element in industrial air flow measurement.

#### Step 1 — Ideal (Inviscid) Bernoulli Equation

Consider air flowing in a horizontal duct of diameter $D$, with an orifice plate creating a throat of diameter $d$. Apply Bernoulli's equation between point 1 (upstream, area $A_1$) and point 2 (vena contracta, area $A_2$):

$$P_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 = P_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_2^2$$

#### Step 2 — Rearrange for the Pressure Drop

$$P_1 - P_2 = \frac{1}{2}\rho (v_2^2 - v_1^2)$$

#### Step 3 — Apply the Continuity Equation

Since the fluid is incompressible, the mass flow is conserved:

$$\rho A_1 v_1 = \rho A_2 v_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_1 = v_2 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$

#### Step 4 — Substitute the Continuity Result

$$P_1 - P_2 = \frac{1}{2}\rho \, v_2^2 \left(1 - \frac{A_2^2}{A_1^2}\right)$$

Solve for $v_2$:

$$v_2 = \sqrt{\frac{2 (P_1 - P_2)}{\rho \left(1 - \frac{A_2^2}{A_1^2}\right)}}$$

#### Step 5 — Introduce the Diameter Ratio

Since $A = \pi D^2 / 4$, the area ratio squared equals $\beta^4$:

$$\frac{A_2^2}{A_1^2} = \frac{d^4}{D^4} = \beta^4$$

Hence:

$$v_2 = \sqrt{\frac{2 (P_1 - P_2)}{\rho (1 - \beta^4)}}$$

#### Step 6 — Account for Real-Fluid Losses (Discharge Coefficient)

The ideal flow theory assumes a perfect fluid. Real fluids experience friction and the **vena contracta** lies slightly downstream of the throat. The **discharge coefficient** $C_d$ corrects for both:

$$v_2 = C_d \sqrt{\frac{2 (P_1 - P_2)}{\rho (1 - \beta^4)}}$$

#### Step 7 — Multiply by Throat Area to Get Volumetric Flow

$$Q = A_2 \cdot v_2 = C_d A_2 \sqrt{\frac{2 (P_1 - P_2)}{\rho (1 - \beta^4)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Result — Orifice Equation:** $Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho (1-\beta^4)}}$. This is the **canonical KTU formula** for orifice-based air flow measurement.

#### Step 8 — Express in Terms of Pipe Diameter

Using $A_2 = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$ and $\beta = d/D$:

$$Q = \frac{\pi \, d^2}{4} \, C_d \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho \left(1 - \beta^4\right)}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Trick:** If a KTU problem gives $D = 100$ mm, $d = 60$ mm, then $\beta = 0.6$, $\beta^4 = 0.1296$, and the velocity-of-approach factor is $(1 - 0.1296)^{-1/2} = 1.0735$. Always show this intermediate calculation in the answer script for full marks.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Square-Root Linearization of the Flow Signal

#### Step 1 — Process Variable in Differential-Pressure Form

The differential pressure transmitter outputs a current (4–20 mA) proportional to the **measured ΔP**:

$$I_{\text{DPT}} = 4 + 16 \cdot \frac{\Delta P - \Delta P_{\min}}{\Delta P_{\max} - \Delta P_{\min}}$$

#### Step 2 — True Flow is Non-Linear in ΔP

From Section 2.2:

$$Q = K \sqrt{\Delta P} \quad \text{where} \quad K = C_d A_2 \sqrt{\frac{2}{\rho(1-\beta^4)}}$$

#### Step 3 — Expand the Square Root Using Taylor Series about Operating Point $\Delta P_0$

$$\sqrt{\Delta P} = \sqrt{\Delta P_0} + \frac{1}{2\sqrt{\Delta P_0}} (\Delta P - \Delta P_0) - \frac{1}{8(\Delta P_0)^{3/2}} (\Delta P - \Delta P_0)^2 + \cdots$$

#### Step 4 — Implement Hardware Square-Root Extractor

Modern DCS performs exact extraction:

$$I_{\text{out}} = 4 + 16 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\Delta P} - \sqrt{\Delta P_{\min}}}{\sqrt{\Delta P_{\max}} - \sqrt{\Delta P_{\min}}}$$

After square-root extraction:

$$I_{\text{out}} \propto Q \quad (\text{linear in flow})$$

#### Step 5 — Why Linearization is Critical for Control

The open-loop gain of a flow loop without linearization is:

$$K_{\text{process}} = \frac{dQ}{d(\Delta P)} = \frac{K}{2\sqrt{\Delta P}}$$

At low flow, $K_{\text{process}}$ is **large**; at high flow, it is **small**. This means:
- Loop becomes **sluggish at high flow**
- Loop becomes **unstable / oscillatory at low flow**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** Examiners specifically test whether students can explain **why** square-root extraction is needed. The answer is **process-gain linearization for constant controller tuning across the operating range**. Many students incorrectly answer "to convert signal to engineering units" — that is the **range conversion** job, not square-root extraction.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Mass Flow Through a Thermal Mass Flow Meter (TMFM)

#### Step 1 — Principle of Operation

A small known amount of heat $P_h$ (Watts) is added to the flowing air stream. The temperature difference $\Delta T$ between two resistance temperature detectors (RTDs) is inversely proportional to mass flow:

$$\dot{m} = \frac{P_h}{C_p \, \Delta T}$$

#### Step 2 — Derivation from Energy Balance

The energy added by the heater equals the enthalpy gain of the air:

$$P_h = \dot{m} C_p (T_{\text{out}} - T_{\text{in}}) = \dot{m} C_p \Delta T$$

Rearranging:

$$\dot{m} = \frac{P_h}{C_p \Delta T}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this is powerful:** TMFM gives **direct mass flow** without needing pressure or temperature compensation. It is the preferred method for **clean dry air** in semiconductor, pharmaceutical, and laboratory applications.

#### Step 3 — Numerical Example for KTU Practice

Given: $P_h = 50$ W, $C_p = 1005$ J/(kg·K), $\Delta T = 10$ K. Find $\dot{m}$.

$$\dot{m} = \frac{50}{1005 \times 10} = 4.975 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s} \approx 17.91 \text{ kg/hr}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Air Flow Measurement Technology

| Technology | Accuracy | Pressure Loss | Turndown | Cost | Best Application |
|------------|----------|---------------|----------|------|------------------|
| Orifice Plate | ±1–2 % | High | 3:1 | Low | Large ducts, low maintenance |
| Venturi | ±0.5–1 % | Very Low | 4:1 | High | Large coal-fired boiler FD/ID fans |
| Annubar | ±1–1.5 % | Low | 4:1 | Medium | Retrofit installations |
| Pitot-Static | ±2–3 % | Negligible | 3:1 | Low | Spot measurements |
| Vortex Shedding | ±0.5–1 % | Moderate | 10:1 | Medium | Steam & clean gases |
| Thermal Mass | ±1–2 % | Very Low | 50:1 | High | Clean dry compressed air |
| Ultrasonic | ±1–2 % | None | 10:1 | Very High | Large stacks, dirty air |
| Coriolis | ±0.1–0.5 % | High | 20:1 | Very High | Custody transfer, high accuracy |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Air Flow Control Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the **functional architecture** of a typical air flow control loop, including measurement, linearization, control, and final-element stages.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Setpoint SP] --> SUM1((Summing Junction))
    FB1[Flow Transmitter FT] --> SUM1
    SUM1 --> EC[Error e t]
    EC --> PID[PID Controller Gc s]
    PID --> I2P[Current to Pressure Converter I P]
    I2P --> PCV[Pneumatic Control Valve PCV]
    PCV --> PROC[Air Flow Process Gp s]
    PROC --> DUCT[(Duct / Pipe)]
    DUCT --> PO[Primary Element Orifice]
    PO --> DPT[Differential Pressure Transmitter DPT]
    DPT --> SQR{Square Root Extractor}
    SQR --> FT[Flow Transmitter FT 4 to 20 mA]
    FT --> FB1
    DPT --> DPFB[Differential Pressure Feedback]

    classDef sensor fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef controller fill:#A0D8B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef final fill:#FF9AA2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef process fill:#B5C7FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px

    class PO,DPT,FT sensor
    class PID,EC,SQR controller
    class PCV,I2P final
    class PROC,DUCT process
```

### 4.2 Cascade Control Strategy for Boiler Air Flow

In a large-scale **coal-fired boiler**, air flow is rarely controlled as a single loop. Instead, a **cascade control** strategy is used, where the **master loop** (air flow) sets the setpoint of a **slave loop** (fan speed or damper position).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MS[Master Setpoint Air Flow] --> MSJ((Summing Junction 1))
    FM[Flow Measurement] --> MSJ
    MSJ --> MC[Master Controller Typically PI]
    MC --> SSSP[Slave Setpoint]
    SSSP --> SSJ((Summing Junction 2))
    FBM[Forced Draft Fan Speed Feedback] --> SSJ
    SSJ --> SC[Slave Controller Typically P or PI]
    SC --> AF[Actuator Damper or VFD]
    AF --> FAN[Forced Draft Fan]
    FAN --> DUCT2[(Combustion Air Duct)]
    DUCT2 --> ORIF[Orifice Flow Element]
    ORIF --> DPT2[DP Transmitter]
    DPT2 --> SQR2{Square Root}
    SQR2 --> FM

    classDef master fill:#FFE699,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef slave fill:#B6D7A8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef plant fill:#A4C2F4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px

    class MS,MSJ,MC,FM master
    class SSSP,SSJ,SC,FBM,AF slave
    class FAN,DUCT2,ORIF,DPT2,SQR2 plant
```

### 4.3 Feedforward–Feedback Air/Fuel Ratio Control

For boilers, the **best practice** is to use **feedforward control** based on fuel flow, with **feedback trim** based on measured excess oxygen or measured air flow.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FF[Fuel Flow Signal] --> FFGAIN[Feedforward Gain Kff]
    FFGAIN --> FFJ((Summing Junction))
    FBK[Air Flow Feedback] --> FBC[Feedback Controller PI]
    FBC --> FFJ
    FFJ --> DAMPER[Air Damper Actuator]
    DAMPER --> BOILER((Boiler Furnace))
    BOILER --> O2[Oxygen Analyzer ZrO2 Sensor]
    O2 --> TRIM[Trim Signal Optional]
    TRIM --> FBC

    classDef feed fill:#FCE5CD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef fb fill:#D9EAD3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef phys fill:#C9DAF8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px

    class FF,FFGAIN feed
    class FBK,FBC,TRIM,O2 fb
    class DAMPER,BOILER phys
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture — Square-Root Extraction & Signal Conditioning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Upstream Tap] --> IMP[Impulse Line 1]
    P2[Throat Tap] --> IMP2[Impulse Line 2]
    IMP --> HS1[High Side of DPT]
    IMP2 --> LS2[Low Side of DPT]
    HS1 --> DP[Differential Pressure Cell]
    LS2 --> DP
    DP --> AMP[Amplifier and Linearization]
    AMP --> SQRT[Square Root Function]
    SQRT --> RANGE[Range Conversion to 4 to 20 mA]
    RANGE --> OUT[Output to DCS or Controller]
    OUT --> HMI[Operator HMI Display in Nm3 per hr]

    classDef phys fill:#F4CCCC,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef sig fill:#D9D2E9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef ctrl fill:#D0E0E3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px

    class P1,P2,IMP,IMP2,HS1,LS2,DP phys
    class AMP,SQRT,RANGE sig
    class OUT,HMI ctrl
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why these diagrams matter for KTU:** The 14-mark questions often ask the student to "draw and explain the air flow control loop" or "describe the cascade control strategy for boiler air flow". A clean, well-labelled block diagram is worth **3–4 marks** out of 14 in itself. Always draw the **measurement → transmitter → controller → final element → process → measurement** chain.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Explain why square-root extraction is essential in differential-pressure-based air flow measurement.**

**Model Answer:**

In a primary element such as an orifice plate, venturi, or flow nozzle, the volumetric flow rate $Q$ is proportional to the **square root** of the measured differential pressure $\Delta P$, i.e., $Q = K \sqrt{\Delta P}$.

Without square-root extraction, the **process gain** of the flow loop is non-linear:

$$K_p = \frac{dQ}{d(\Delta P)} = \frac{K}{2\sqrt{\Delta P}}$$

This means the loop gain is **high at low flow** and **low at high flow**, making it impossible to tune a PID controller that gives uniform performance across the operating range.

**Square-root extraction linearizes** the transmitter output, so 4–20 mA is directly proportional to $Q$, enabling **constant controller tuning**, improved setpoint tracking, and stable regulatory performance. **(3 marks)**

---

#### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between volumetric flow rate and mass flow rate. Why is mass flow preferred in combustion air control?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Volumetric Flow Rate (Q) | Mass Flow Rate (ṁ) |
|--------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| Definition | Volume of air per unit time | Mass of air per unit time |
| Units | Nm³/hr, m³/s | kg/s, kg/hr |
| Dependence on T, P | Yes — changes with T and P | No — invariant with T, P |

In combustion air control, the **stoichiometric air–fuel ratio is expressed in mass terms**. The chemical reaction $C + O_2 \to CO_2$ requires a specific **mass** of O₂ per kg of fuel, regardless of how hot or pressurized the air is. Therefore, controlling **mass flow** directly ensures correct stoichiometry independent of temperature or pressure variations across FD fans and preheaters. Volumetric flow control would lead to **air deficiency at high temperatures** (due to density drop) and cause incomplete combustion. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A** [KTU University Exam — June 2024] — 14 Marks

**(a)** Derive the volumetric flow equation for an orifice plate, including the velocity-of-approach factor. Define all terms and state their SI units. **— 7 marks**

**(b)** A 250 mm diameter duct carries air at 30 °C and 1.01325 bar. An orifice plate with a 150 mm bore is installed. A differential pressure transmitter reads **ΔP = 2.5 kPa**. Given: discharge coefficient $C_d = 0.62$, $R = 287$ J/(kg·K). Calculate the **volumetric flow rate in Nm³/hr** and the **mass flow rate in kg/hr**. **— 7 marks**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation — Orifice Equation**

Refer to **Section 3.1, Steps 1 to 7** of these notes for the full derivation. The result is:

$$Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{\frac{2 \, \Delta P}{\rho \, (1 - \beta^4)}}$$

where:
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate, m³/s
- $C_d$ = discharge coefficient, dimensionless
- $A_2$ = throat cross-section area, m²
- $\Delta P$ = differential pressure, Pa
- $\rho$ = air density, kg/m³
- $\beta = d/D$ = diameter ratio, dimensionless

**Each term explained with units gets 1 mark each. The 7-step derivation carries 3 marks. The final boxed equation and statement of the velocity-of-approach factor carries 1 mark.**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution**

**Step 1 — Compute the diameter ratio $\beta$**

$$\beta = \frac{d}{D} = \frac{0.150}{0.250} = 0.600$$

$$\beta^4 = (0.600)^4 = 0.1296$$

$$1 - \beta^4 = 1 - 0.1296 = 0.8704$$

**Step 2 — Compute the density of air at 30 °C and 1.01325 bar**

$$T = 30 + 273.15 = 303.15 \text{ K}$$

$$\rho = \frac{P}{R T} = \frac{101325}{287 \times 303.15} = \frac{101325}{87004.05} = 1.1646 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Compute the throat area $A_2$**

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.150)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.0225}{4} = 0.01767 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Apply the orifice equation**

$$Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho(1 - \beta^4)}}$$

$$Q = 0.62 \times 0.01767 \times \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 2500}{1.1646 \times 0.8704}}$$

Compute the inner fraction:

$$\frac{2 \times 2500}{1.1646 \times 0.8704} = \frac{5000}{1.0134} = 4933.9$$

Square root:

$$\sqrt{4933.9} = 70.24 \text{ m/s}$$

Therefore:

$$Q = 0.62 \times 0.01767 \times 70.24 = 0.7694 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Convert volumetric flow to Nm³/hr**

**Standard conditions:** $T_{\text{std}} = 273.15$ K, $P_{\text{std}} = 101325$ Pa.

$$Q_{\text{std}} = Q \times \frac{T_{\text{std}}}{T} \times \frac{P}{P_{\text{std}}}$$

Since $P = P_{\text{std}}$ in this case:

$$Q_{\text{std}} = 0.7694 \times \frac{273.15}{303.15} = 0.6932 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{s}$$

Convert to Nm³/hr:

$$Q_{\text{std}} = 0.6932 \times 3600 = 2495.5 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{hr}$$

**Step 6 — Compute mass flow rate**

$$\dot{m} = \rho Q = 1.1646 \times 0.7694 = 0.8961 \text{ kg/s}$$

Convert to kg/hr:

$$\dot{m} = 0.8961 \times 3600 = 3226.0 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**Final Answer:**
- $Q \approx 2495.5$ **Nm³/hr**
- $\dot{m} \approx 3226.0$ **kg/hr**

**Mark distribution (Part b):** Diameter ratio + β⁴ (1 mark), density (1 mark), throat area (1 mark), substitution (1 mark), numerical evaluation (1 mark), STP conversion (1 mark), final answer with units (1 mark).

---

#### **Question B** [KTU University Exam — May 2023] — 14 Marks

**(a)** Explain the **cascade control strategy** for boiler combustion air flow. Draw a neat block diagram and justify why cascade control is preferred over single-loop control. **— 7 marks**

**(b)** A thermal mass flow meter applies a constant heater power of **120 W** across a flowing air stream. The two RTDs record an inlet temperature of **28 °C** and an outlet temperature of **38 °C**. The specific heat of air is $C_p = 1005$ J/(kg·K). Calculate the **mass flow rate in kg/hr**. State two industrial applications where thermal mass flow meters are preferred over orifice plates. **— 7 marks**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Cascade Control of Boiler Air Flow**

**Definition:** Cascade control uses **two controllers in series** — a **master (primary) controller** that controls the **outer variable** (air flow) and a **slave (secondary) controller** that controls the **inner variable** (fan speed / damper position / FD fan vane).

**Block Diagram:** Refer to **Section 4.2** (Mermaid cascade diagram).

**Master Loop (Outer):**
- **PV:** Measured air flow (Nm³/hr)
- **CV Output:** Slave setpoint (fan speed setpoint)
- **Controller Type:** PI (slow but accurate)

**Slave Loop (Inner):**
- **PV:** Fan speed (rpm) or damper position (%)
- **CV Output:** Actuator signal to VFD / damper motor
- **Controller Type:** P or PI (fast)

**Why cascade is preferred over single-loop control:**

1. **Faster disturbance rejection:** Disturbances in fan speed (motor voltage dips, fan load changes) are corrected by the **inner loop** before they propagate to the air flow. The single-loop control would have to wait until the air flow changed to react.
2. **Non-linearity isolation:** Damper and valve non-linearities are absorbed in the **inner loop**.
3. **Improved stability:** Inner loop reduces the effective time constant seen by the outer loop, allowing **higher master-loop gain** without oscillation.
4. **Better setpoint tracking:** Master setpoint changes are realized rapidly via the inner loop.

**Mark distribution (Part a):** Definition + diagram (3 marks), Master and slave loop details (2 marks), At least 3 justifications (2 marks).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution — Thermal Mass Flow Meter**

**Step 1 — Calculate temperature rise**

$$\Delta T = T_{\text{out}} - T_{\text{in}} = 38 - 28 = 10 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the thermal mass flow equation**

$$\dot{m} = \frac{P_h}{C_p \Delta T} = \frac{120}{1005 \times 10} = \frac{120}{10050} = 0.01194 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to kg/hr**

$$\dot{m} = 0.01194 \times 3600 = 42.99 \text{ kg/hr} \approx 43.0 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**Step 4 — Industrial applications where TMFM is preferred over orifice plates:**

1. **Semiconductor clean-room air flow monitoring** — requires very low pressure drop and high accuracy at low flow.
2. **Pharmaceutical process air and fermenter aeration** — needs sanitary, crevice-free designs.
3. **Compressed-air distribution networks in factories** — direct mass flow reading without T/P compensation.
4. **Laboratory and medical gas flow** — bi-directional capability and excellent low-flow sensitivity.

(Any two are sufficient.)

**Mark distribution (Part b):** ΔT (1 mark), equation (1 mark), numerical evaluation (1 mark), unit conversion (1 mark), applications (3 marks — 1.5 marks each).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Ways Students Lose Marks in Air Flow Control Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the velocity-of-approach factor** $(1-\beta^4)^{-1/2}$ — costs up to **2 marks** in 14-mark problems.
> 2. **Confusing volumetric and mass flow** — if the question asks for Nm³/hr but you give kg/hr, you lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Not stating the standard reference conditions** (0 °C vs 20 °C, 1.01325 bar) — examiners specifically deduct marks for ambiguity.
> 4. **Writing the Bernoulli equation without specifying assumptions** (steady, incompressible, inviscid) — the assumptions themselves carry 1 mark.
> 5. **Drawing a control-loop block diagram with feedback arrow missing or in the wrong direction** — examiners refuse to award diagram marks without a clear feedback path.
> 6. **Mixing up Cv and Cv_kv** — these are American and metric valve-sizing coefficients; the relationship is $C_v = 1.156 \, K_v$.
> 7. **Applying the orifice equation to a compressible flow** (M > 0.3) without compressibility correction — examiners may deduct if M > 0.1 in the question.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Air Flow Control**

- ✅ **Continuity:** $Q = A v$ (mass conservation for incompressible flow).
- ✅ **Bernoulli:** $P + \frac{1}{2}\rho v^2 + \rho g z = \text{constant}$ along a streamline.
- ✅ **Master Equation:** $Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{2 \Delta P / [\rho(1-\beta^4)]}$ — the **canonical formula**.
- ✅ **Mass flow variant:** $\dot{m} = C_d A_2 \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P / (1-\beta^4)}$.
- ✅ **Square-root law:** $Q \propto \sqrt{\Delta P}$ — drives the need for **square-root extraction**.
- ✅ **Density of air:** $\rho = P / (R_s T)$ with $R_s = 287.058$ J/(kg·K).
- ✅ **Standard reference:** STP (0 °C, 1.01325 bar) and NTP (20 °C, 1.01325 bar).
- ✅ **Thermal mass flow:** $\dot{m} = P_h / (C_p \Delta T)$ — direct mass flow, no T/P compensation.
- ✅ **Cascade control** = Master (flow) + Slave (fan/damper) — faster disturbance rejection.
- ✅ **Feedforward air control** = use fuel flow signal to compute air demand; trim with O₂ feedback.
- ✅ **Turndown ratio** of orifice ≈ 3:1; venturi ≈ 4:1; vortex ≈ 10:1; thermal ≈ 50:1.
- ✅ **Selection rule:** Orifice = cheapest; Venturi = lowest pressure loss; TMFM = clean air, low flow; Coriolis = highest accuracy.
- ✅ **ΔP transmitter taps:** D and D/2 (orifice), throat taps (venturi).
- ✅ **Loop non-linearity** without square-root extraction = $K_p = K / (2\sqrt{\Delta P})$ — controller tuning is operating-point dependent.
- ✅ **Industrial application hierarchy:** Power plant boiler > cement kiln > blast furnace > HVAC > pneumatic conveying.
- ✅ **Combustion air–fuel ratio stoichiometry:** For coal, theoretical O₂ ≈ 1.4–1.7 Nm³ per kg of coal; practical excess air = 15–25 %.
- ✅ **Flow regimes:** Laminar (Re < 2300), transitional (2300 < Re < 4000), turbulent (Re > 4000).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-line takeaway for the KTU exam:** *Air flow control is a non-linear control problem governed by the square-root law; effective control requires square-root extraction, primary-element selection based on accuracy and turndown, and advanced strategies like cascade or feedforward control to handle multi-variable interactions in large-scale industrial processes.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Damper and Fan Controls
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT864 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN LARGE SCALE INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Damper and Fan Controls

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Damper?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> A **damper** is a mechanical device installed in a duct, stack, or process line that modulates the cross-sectional area available for fluid flow, thereby controlling the volumetric flow rate of gases (most commonly air) in industrial systems.

**Industrial Context:** Dampers are ubiquitous in large-scale plants — cement kilns, power station boiler air/flue gas paths, steel mill ventilation, and HVAC systems of process industries.

**Physical Constants / Standards Used:**
- Standard damper leakage class: **Class I, II, III, IV** (per **AMCA 500-D**)
- Typical industrial air density: **ρ = 1.20 kg/m³** at NTP (**20 °C, 101.325 kPa**)
- Damper leakage target for tight shut-off: **≤ 1 % of nominal flow (Class IV)**

## 1.2 What is a Fan?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> A **fan** is a rotary-bladed machine that imparts kinetic energy to a continuous stream of gas (usually air), increasing its static pressure or velocity to move it through a ducted system against resistance.

**Industrial Context:** Fans are the workhorses of induced-draft (ID), forced-draft (FD), and primary-air (PA) systems in power plants, process air supplies in petrochemical units, and cooling-air circulation in large rotating machinery.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Tap" and the "Water Pump"

| Component | Domestic Analogy | Industrial Equivalent |
|-----------|------------------|------------------------|
| **Damper** | The **tap/faucet** on a kitchen sink | Restricts flow by narrowing passage |
| **Fan** | The **water pump** in a building's overhead tank | Provides the driving pressure head |
| **Duct** | The **pipeline** carrying water | Carries the air to the process point |
| **System Resistance** | Friction in pipe walls + bend losses | Static back-pressure of filters, heat exchangers, dampers, duct friction |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world feel:** Imagine you have a powerful water pump (the fan) feeding a network of pipes (the duct). When you partially close a valve (the damper) downstream, the flow drops — but the pump still spins at full speed and "wastes" energy fighting its own discharge restriction. A better way is to *slow the pump* (VSD) or *tilt the impeller blades* (IGV) — saving electricity. This is the central engineering dilemma of fan-damper control.

## 1.4 Classification Snapshot

> [!NOTE]
> **Damper Types (most common in industry):**
> 1. **Louver dampers** — multi-blade, used for modulating control
> 2. **Butterfly dampers** — single disc, used for isolation
> 3. **Guillotine dampers** — sliding plate, for tight shut-off (e.g., economiser bypass)
> 4. **Radial / sector dampers** — for circular ducts, especially ID fans
> 5. **Poppet dampers** — fast-acting, for emergency isolation

> [!NOTE]
> **Fan Types (industrial relevance):**
> 1. **Centrifugal (radial) fans** — backward-curved, forward-curved, radial-bladed
> 2. **Axial fans** — propeller, tube-axial, vane-axial
> 3. **Mixed-flow fans** — hybrid characteristic

---

# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet
<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2.1 Damper Construction and Characteristics

### 2.1.1 Louver Damper Geometry

A louver damper consists of multiple flat blades mounted on parallel shafts, linked together by a jack-shaft and crank-arm linkage. Rotation of the linkage moves all blades in unison.

**Two Sub-Types:**

| Feature | **Parallel Blade Damper** | **Opposed Blade Damper** |
|---|---|---|
| **Blade motion** | All blades rotate in the **same direction** | Adjacent blades rotate in **opposite directions** |
| **Flow profile at partial opening** | Non-uniform; jet stream cross-section small | Uniform across the duct |
| **ΔP vs. position characteristic** | Non-linear (sharp drop early) | Closer to **linear** (proportional) |
| **Preferred use** | Tight shut-off applications | **Modulating flow control** (preferred for control loops) |
| **Equal-percentage behaviour** | No | Approximately yes |

### 2.1.2 Damper Flow Equation

The flow through a damper follows the standard orifice equation:

$$Q = C_d \cdot A_{eff} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, \Delta P_{damper}}{\rho}}$$

where
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (m³/s)
- $C_d$ = discharge coefficient (**typically 0.6 – 0.65** for a sharp-edged damper)
- $A_{eff}$ = effective open area (m²)
- $\Delta P_{damper}$ = pressure drop across damper (Pa)
- $\rho$ = gas density (**1.20 kg/m³** for air at NTP)

## 2.2 Fan — Operating Principle

A fan imparts **energy per unit mass** (specific work) to the gas:

$$W_{fan} = \dfrac{\Delta P_{total}}{\rho} \quad [\text{J/kg}]$$

The shaft power required (mechanical input to the fan):

$$HP = \dfrac{Q \cdot \Delta P_{total}}{\eta_{fan}} \quad [\text{W}]$$

where $\eta_{fan}$ = **fan efficiency (typically 70 % – 85 % for industrial units)**.

## 2.3 System Resistance Curve

The plant/duct system presents a back-pressure that varies with flow:

$$\Delta P_{system} = K \cdot Q^{n}$$

- For purely **frictional** systems: $n = 2$ (parabolic)
- For systems with significant **lift/height** component: $n \approx 1.5$ – 1.8
- The **operating point** of the fan is the intersection of the **fan curve** and the **system resistance curve**

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU numerical.**

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_2/Q_1 = N_2/N_1$ | Flow scales **linearly** with speed | $D$ = constant |
| 2 | $\Delta P_2 / \Delta P_1 = (N_2/N_1)^2$ | Pressure scales with **speed squared** | $D$ = constant |
| 3 | $HP_2 / HP_1 = (N_2/N_1)^3$ | Power scales with **speed cubed** | $D$ = constant |
| 4 | $Q_2/Q_1 = (D_2/D_1)^3 (N_2/N_1)$ | General flow affinity | Variable $D$ |
| 5 | $\Delta P_2 / \Delta P_1 = (D_2/D_1)^2 (N_2/N_1)^2$ | General pressure affinity | Variable $D$ |
| 6 | $HP_2 / HP_1 = (D_2/D_1)^5 (N_2/N_1)^3$ | General power affinity | Variable $D$ |
| 7 | $\Delta P_{sys} = K \cdot Q^n$ | System resistance law | $n = 2$ for parabolic |
| 8 | $HP_{throttle} \approx HP_{rated} \cdot (Q/Q_{rated})$ | Throttling power law (linear) | Approximate |
| 9 | $HP_{VSD} = HP_{rated} \cdot (Q/Q_{rated})^3$ | VSD power law (cubic) | Parabolic system |
| 10 | $Q = C_d A_{eff} \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho}$ | Orifice/flow equation | $\rho = 1.20$ kg/m³ for air |
| 11 | $W_{fan} = \Delta P_{total} / \rho$ | Specific work on gas | J/kg |
| 12 | $HP = Q \cdot \Delta P_{total} / \eta_{fan}$ | Fan shaft power | Watts |
| 13 | $\eta_{fan} = (Q \cdot \Delta P) / (HP_{shaft})$ | Fan efficiency | 0.70 – 0.85 typical |
| 14 | $\% \text{Savings} = \dfrac{HP_{throttle} - HP_{VSD}}{HP_{throttle}} \times 100$ | Energy savings comparison | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility of these relations:**
> In a typical thermal power station, the **Induced Draft (ID) fan** and **Forced Draft (FD) fan** together consume **20 % – 25 %** of the plant's auxiliary power. Switching from throttling (damper) control to VSD control at partial load can save **30 % – 60 %** of fan energy — translating to **several crore ₹ per year** in a 500 MW unit.

## 2.5 Three Major Fan Flow Control Methods — Conceptual Summary

| Method | Mechanism | Energy Efficiency | Capital Cost | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Discharge / Inlet Damper** | Throttles the gas; fan runs at constant speed | **Poor** (linear $HP$–$Q$) | **Low** | High (numericals) |
| **Inlet Guide Vanes (IGV)** | Pre-swirls flow entering impeller; reduces work input | **Moderate** (≈ linear–cubic) | Medium | High |
| **Variable Speed Drive (VSD / VFD)** | Varies fan shaft speed directly | **Best** (cubic $HP$–$Q$) | **High** (but best ROI) | High |
| **Variable Pitch (axial fans)** | Adjusts blade angle at hub | Best (after VSD) | High | Moderate |

---

# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix
<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3.1 Derivation of the Fan Affinity Laws (Speed Variation, D = constant)

### 3.1.1 Dimensional / Geometric Foundation

For a fan geometrically similar to a reference fan (same impeller diameter $D$, same blade angles), the velocity of gas at any point on the impeller is proportional to the tip speed:

$$V_{tip} = \pi D N / 60 \quad [\text{m/s}]$$

where $N$ is in **rpm** and $D$ is in metres.

> **Step 1:** Since all linear dimensions and velocities in the impeller scale with $V_{tip}$, and $V_{tip} \propto N$ (for fixed $D$), the **volumetric flow rate** must scale linearly with $N$:

$$Q \propto N \quad \text{(at constant } D \text{)} \implies \boxed{\dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}}$$

> **Step 2:** Pressure rise in the fan comes from the change in angular momentum of the gas, which depends on $V_{tip}^2$. Hence:

$$\Delta P \propto V_{tip}^2 \propto N^2 \quad \text{(at constant } D \text{)} \implies \boxed{\dfrac{\Delta P_2}{\Delta P_1} = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{2}}$$

> **Step 3:** Shaft power = (Flow) × (Pressure) / efficiency, so it scales as $N \times N^2 = N^3$:

$$HP = \dfrac{Q \cdot \Delta P}{\eta} \propto N^3 \quad \text{(at constant } D \text{)} \implies \boxed{\dfrac{HP_2}{HP_1} = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{3}}$$

### 3.1.2 Generalised Affinity Laws (Variable D and N)

When both impeller diameter $D$ and speed $N$ change, the velocity scales as $V_{tip} \propto D \cdot N$, leading to:

$$\dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \left(\dfrac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^{3} \cdot \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}$$

$$\dfrac{\Delta P_2}{\Delta P_1} = \left(\dfrac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^{2} \cdot \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{2}$$

$$\dfrac{HP_2}{HP_1} = \left(\dfrac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^{5} \cdot \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{3}$$

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Affinity Law Numerical (Typical 7-Mark KTU Style)

**Problem Statement:**
A centrifugal fan delivers **$Q_1 = 12{,}000$ m³/hr** of air at a static pressure of **$\Delta P_1 = 1800$ Pa** when running at **$N_1 = 1450$ rpm**, drawing **$HP_1 = 9.5$ kW** from the motor. Determine the new flow, pressure, and power when the fan speed is changed to **$N_2 = 1100$ rpm**. Assume fan efficiency is unchanged.

**Step 1 — Compute the speed ratio:**

$$\dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{1100}{1450} = 0.7586$$

> **Step 2 — Apply the flow affinity law:**

$$Q_2 = Q_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right) = 12000 \times 0.7586$$

$$Q_2 = 9103.4 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{hr}$$

> **Step 3 — Apply the pressure affinity law:**

$$\Delta P_2 = \Delta P_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{2} = 1800 \times (0.7586)^2$$

$$\Delta P_2 = 1800 \times 0.5755 = 1035.9 \;\; \text{Pa}$$

> **Step 4 — Apply the power affinity law:**

$$HP_2 = HP_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{3} = 9.5 \times (0.7586)^3$$

$$HP_2 = 9.5 \times 0.4366 = 4.148 \;\; \text{kW}$$

**Verification (Sanity Check):**

$$\dfrac{HP_2}{HP_1} = \dfrac{4.148}{9.5} = 0.4366 \;\;\checkmark \text{ matches } (0.7586)^3$$

**Final Tabulated Answer:**

| Parameter | Rated | Reduced Speed | % Change |
|---|---|---|---|
| Speed, $N$ | 1450 rpm | 1100 rpm | −24.14 % |
| Flow, $Q$ | 12 000 m³/hr | **9103 m³/hr** | −24.14 % |
| Pressure, $\Delta P$ | 1800 Pa | **1036 Pa** | −42.45 % |
| Power, $HP$ | 9.50 kW | **4.15 kW** | −56.34 % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pedagogical Insight:** Notice the *cubic* drop in power (56 %) versus the *linear* drop in flow (24 %). This is the entire economic basis for VSD fan control — small flow reductions yield disproportionately large energy savings.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Energy Savings Comparison: Damper vs. VSD

**Problem Statement:**
A forced-draft fan in a boiler draws **200 kW** at full load (100 % flow). Estimate the shaft power required at **70 % flow** when controlled by:
**(a)** a discharge damper (throttling), and
**(b)** a variable speed drive (VSD).

Assume the duct system has a parabolic resistance characteristic. Compute the energy savings in kW and as a percentage.

**Step 1 — Define the flow ratio:**

$$x = \dfrac{Q}{Q_{rated}} = 0.70$$

> **Step 2 — Throttling Control:**
> For damper control on a parabolic system, the fan still runs at full speed; the operating point shifts down along the fan curve. Approximation (KTU board level):

$$HP_{throttle} \approx HP_{rated} \cdot x = 200 \times 0.70$$

$$\boxed{HP_{throttle} = 140 \;\; \text{kW}}$$

> **Step 3 — VSD Control:**
> For a parabolic system, the affinity law gives the cubic relation (system pressure $\propto Q^2$, fan pressure matches it):

$$HP_{VSD} = HP_{rated} \cdot x^{3} = 200 \times (0.70)^3$$

$$HP_{VSD} = 200 \times 0.343 = 68.6 \;\; \text{kW}$$

$$\boxed{HP_{VSD} = 68.6 \;\; \text{kW}}$$

> **Step 4 — Compute the savings:**

$$\Delta HP = 140 - 68.6 = 71.4 \;\; \text{kW}$$

$$\% \text{Savings} = \dfrac{71.4}{140} \times 100 = 51.0 \;\; \%$$

**Final Tabulated Answer:**

| Control Method | Power at 70 % flow | Energy Saved vs. Throttling |
|---|---|---|
| Discharge Damper | 140.0 kW | — (baseline) |
| Variable Speed Drive | 68.6 kW | **71.4 kW (51 %)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Footnote:** If the fan runs at 70 % flow for **6000 hours/year**, the monetary saving at ₹6/kWh is:
> $71.4 \times 6000 \times 6 = $ **₹ 25.7 lakh/year** per fan — a strong ROI case for retrofitting VSDs.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choice of Fan Control Method

| Plant Condition | Recommended Control | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Constant flow, fixed load | **Damper (90 % open)** | No need for variable control |
| Frequent load changes, 60–100 % flow | **VSD** | Cubic energy savings dominate |
| High-temperature, dusty flue gas (ID fan) | **Hydraulic-coupled VSD** | Heat isolation; VFD electronics protected |
| Limited budget, low hours/year | **IGV (variable)** | Moderate saving, low CapEx |
| Turndown > 4:1 with high pressure rise | **VSD + IGV (combined)** | Stays on fan's best-efficiency curve |

---

# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics
<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.1 Damper and Fan Control — Functional Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    SPnodeA[SET POINT FROM DCS OR PLC] --> CTRLnodeB[PID CONTROLLER IN CONTROL ROOM]
    CTRLnodeB --> ACTnodeC[PNEUMATIC OR ELECTRIC ACTUATOR]
    ACTnodeC --> DMnodeD[CONTROL DAMPER IN DUCT]
    DMnodeD --> FNnodeE[CENTRIFUGAL OR AXIAL FAN]
    FNnodeE --> PROCnodeF[PROCESS DUCTWORK OR BOILER]
    PROCnodeF --> SNnodeG[FLOW TRANSMITTER OR DP TRANSMITTER]
    SNnodeG --> TXnodeH[4 TO 20 MA SIGNAL]
    TXnodeH --> CTRLnodeB
    SPnodeA -.-> ALMnodeI[ALARM AND INTERLOCK LOGIC]
    PROCnodeF -.-> ALMnodeI
```

> **Figure Explanation (for KTU answer sheets):**
> The diagram above represents a **closed-loop fan-damper control system**. The DCS issues a set-point (flow or pressure) to the PID controller. The controller output drives the actuator (pneumatic diaphragm or electric motor) which positions the damper. The flow sensor (orifice + DPT) measures the actual flow and feeds back to the controller, completing the loop. Alarm logic monitors process upsets and triggers protective actions (e.g., trip, MFT in boilers).

## 4.2 Damper Classification Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    ROOTnodeA[INDUSTRIAL DAMPERS] --> LBnodeB[LOUVER DAMPERS]
    ROOTnodeA --> BFnodeC[BUTTERFLY DAMPERS]
    ROOTnodeA --> GNnodeD[GUILLOTINE DAMPERS]
    ROOTnodeA --> RDnodeE[RADIAL DAMPERS]
    ROOTnodeA --> IGVnodeF[INLET GUIDE VANES]
    LBnodeB --> PBnodeG[PARALLEL BLADE TYPE]
    LBnodeB --> OBnodeH[OPPOSED BLADE TYPE]
    IGVnodeF --> FIGNV[FIXED PITCH IGV]
    IGVnodeF --> VIGNV[VARIABLE PITCH IGV]
    GNnodeD --> SGDNV[SLIDING GATE CONFIGURATION]
```

> **Figure Explanation:**
> This taxonomy tree is what KTU examiners expect in **5-mark descriptive answers**. Note that **opposed-blade louver dampers** are universally preferred for modulating control because of their near-linear installed characteristic, which matches well with PID controllers.

## 4.3 Fan Control Strategy — Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    STARTnodeA[FAN FLOW CONTROL REQUIRED] --> QnodeB[IS TURNDOWN RATIO LESS THAN 2 TO 1]
    QnodeB -- YES --> DMPRnodeC[USE DAMPER CONTROL]
    QnodeB -- NO --> QnodeD[IS HIGH ENERGY EFFICIENCY PRIORITY]
    QnodeD -- YES --> VSDnodeE[USE VARIABLE SPEED DRIVE VSD]
    QnodeD -- NO --> IGV2nodeF[USE INLET GUIDE VANES IGV]
    VSDnodeE --> COSTnodeG[HIGH CAPITAL COST BUT BEST ROI]
    IGV2nodeF --> COST2nodeH[MEDIUM CAPITAL COST MEDIUM SAVINGS]
    DMPRnodeC --> COST3nodeI[LOW CAPITAL COST POOR EFFICIENCY]
```

> **Figure Explanation:**
> This decision tree directly answers a common KTU short-note question: *"Select a fan control method for a given application."* Justify your choice by quoting the **turndown ratio**, **efficiency priority**, and **capital cost** trade-off.

## 4.4 Typical ID Fan & Damper Layout (Power Plant Schematic)

```mermaid
graph LR
    ECONO1[BOILER ECONOMISER OUTLET] --> GD1[GUILLOTINE DAMPER GT 101]
    GD1 --> ESP1[ELECTROSTATIC PRECIPITATOR INLET]
    ESP1 --> IDAMPR1[CONTROL DAMPER FOR ID FAN]
    IDAMPR1 --> IDFAN1[INDUCED DRAFT FAN]
    IDFAN1 --> STACK1[STACK DISCHARGE TO ATMOSPHERE]
    IDFAN1 -. SHAFT COUPLED TO .-> MOTOR1[VFD DRIVEN MOTOR 11 KV]
    MOTOR1 -. SPEED REF FROM .-> DCS1[BOILER DCS CONTROLLER]
    ESP1 -. DPT FEEDBACK .-> DCS1
    IDFAN1 -. BEARING TEMP AND VIBRATION .-> DCS1
```

> **Figure Explanation (Important for KTU 14-mark answers):**
> In a 500 MW thermal power unit, the ID fan path is: **Economiser → Guillotine Damper (isolation) → ESP → Control Damper (modulating) → ID Fan (VFD driven) → Stack**. The DCS receives feedback from ESP outlet DP transmitter and modulates both the **damper position** and the **VFD frequency** to maintain furnace draft pressure at **−50 mmH₂O (approx. −490 Pa)**. Bearing temperature and vibration signals are routed to trip logic.

---

# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap
<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between parallel-blade and opposed-blade louver dampers. State where each is preferred.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Parallel-Blade Damper | Opposed-Blade Damper |
|---|---|---|
| Blade motion | All blades rotate in the same direction | Adjacent blades rotate in opposite directions |
| Flow profile at partial opening | Non-uniform; central jet | Uniform across the duct |
| Installed characteristic vs. flow | Non-linear (quick drop, then flat) | Approximately linear (proportional) |
| **Preferred application** | Two-position or isolation service | **Modulating control in feedback loops** (1 mark for difference, 1 mark for preference, 1 mark for reasoning) |

> **Valuation tip:** Examiner's key awards 1 mark for each correct row, with an **extra mark for stating that opposed-blade is preferred in PID loops** because of its linear installed characteristic.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is an Inlet Guide Vane (IGV)? How does it save energy compared to a discharge damper?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> An **Inlet Guide Vane (IGV)** is a set of adjustable swirl vanes installed **upstream of the fan impeller** that pre-rotate the incoming air in a direction **opposite to the impeller rotation**. This pre-swirl reduces the work that the impeller must do on the gas, thereby **shifting the fan pressure–flow curve downward** without changing fan speed. **(1.5 marks)**
>
> Compared to a discharge damper, which **wastes energy as heat in the throttled gas**, the IGV achieves part-load reduction by **reducing the energy input at the source (impeller)**, not by dissipating it. The result is a **30 %–50 % energy saving at 50 % flow**, significantly better than a discharge damper's performance. **(1.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a centrifugal fan. How does it differ from an axial fan? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A centrifugal fan consists of:
- **Impeller** — a rotor with backward-curved, forward-curved, or radial blades mounted on a shaft
- **Casing** (volute or scroll-shaped) — converts kinetic energy of the gas leaving the impeller tips into static pressure
- **Inlet** (suction eye) — axial entry of gas into the impeller hub
- **Shaft and bearings** — supported by pillow blocks; coupled to motor via flexible coupling
- **Drive assembly** — direct, V-belt, or gear-driven

**Working (2.5 marks):**
Gas enters axially at the impeller eye, is flung radially outward by centrifugal force imparted by the rotating blades, and decelerates in the volute casing where its kinetic energy is converted to **static pressure**. The high-pressure gas then exits the discharge flange tangentially.

**Difference from Axial Fan (1.5 marks):**

| Feature | Centrifugal | Axial |
|---|---|---|
| Flow direction | Radial (90° turn) | Parallel to shaft |
| Pressure capability | High (up to 15 kPa) | Low to moderate (up to 2.5 kPa) |
| Flow rate | Moderate | Very high |
| Typical use | FD/ID fans, bag filters, ESP | Cooling towers, mine ventilation, AHU |

---

#### **(b)** A centrifugal fan delivers **15 000 m³/hr** of flue gas at a static pressure of **2 500 Pa** and runs at **1 200 rpm**, drawing **11 kW** from the motor. Calculate: **(i)** the new flow, **(ii)** the new pressure, and **(iii)** the new power when the fan is slowed to **900 rpm**. Use the fan affinity laws. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Speed ratio:**

$$\dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{900}{1200} = 0.75$$

> **Step 2 — New flow (linear affinity):**

$$Q_2 = Q_1 \times \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right) = 15000 \times 0.75 = 11250 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{hr}$$

$$\boxed{Q_2 = 11{,}250 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{hr}}$$

> **Step 3 — New static pressure (square affinity):**

$$\Delta P_2 = \Delta P_1 \times \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 = 2500 \times (0.75)^2 = 2500 \times 0.5625$$

$$\boxed{\Delta P_2 = 1406.25 \;\; \text{Pa}}$$

> **Step 4 — New power (cubic affinity):**

$$HP_2 = HP_1 \times \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 = 11 \times (0.75)^3 = 11 \times 0.421875$$

$$\boxed{HP_2 = 4.64 \;\; \text{kW}}$$

**Summary Table (expected in answer):**

| Quantity | Original | New (900 rpm) | % Change |
|---|---|---|---|
| Speed, $N$ | 1200 rpm | 900 rpm | −25 % |
| Flow, $Q$ | 15 000 m³/hr | **11 250 m³/hr** | −25 % |
| Pressure, $\Delta P$ | 2500 Pa | **1406.25 Pa** | −43.75 % |
| Power, $HP$ | 11 kW | **4.64 kW** | −57.8 % |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### **(a)** With suitable diagrams, describe the three common methods of controlling the flow of a centrifugal fan: (i) discharge damper, (ii) inlet guide vanes, and (iii) variable speed drive. Compare their energy efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Discharge Damper Control (1.5 marks):**
A throttling valve is installed in the fan discharge duct. Closing the damper increases the system resistance, shifting the operating point along the fan curve to lower flow. The fan still runs at constant (full) speed, and the surplus pressure is **dissipated as heat in the throttled gas**.

**(ii) Inlet Guide Vanes (IGV) (1.5 marks):**
Adjustable swirl vanes are placed at the fan inlet. By pre-rotating the incoming gas **opposite to the impeller's rotation**, the vanes reduce the pressure rise across the impeller for the same flow. The fan curve is **shifted downward** without changing speed. Energy is conserved at the source (impeller) rather than wasted downstream.

**(iii) Variable Speed Drive (VSD) (1.5 marks):**
The fan motor speed is varied electronically (VFD/AC drive) or hydraulically (fluid coupling). The fan curve itself shrinks, and the operating point follows the affinity laws. No energy is wasted — the input to the motor is reduced in proportion to $N^3$.

**Energy Efficiency Comparison (2.5 marks):**

| Method | Power Law at partial load | Relative Efficiency at 50 % flow |
|---|---|---|
| Discharge damper | $HP \propto Q$ (linear) | **Poor** — fan still consumes high power |
| IGV | $HP \approx HP_{rated} \cdot (0.4 + 0.6 \cdot Q/Q_{rated})$ (sub-linear) | **Moderate** — better than damper, less than VSD |
| VSD | $HP \propto Q^3$ (cubic) | **Best** — 87.5 % saving at 50 % flow |

> At **50 % flow**: Damper ≈ 50 % power, IGV ≈ 70 % power, VSD ≈ **12.5 % power** (huge saving).

---

#### **(b)** A forced-draft fan draws **320 kW** at **100 % flow** (rated). Compute the shaft power at **75 % flow** using (i) damper control, and (ii) VSD control. Calculate the energy saved in kW and as a percentage. Take the system resistance as parabolic. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $HP_{rated} = 320$ kW; $x = Q/Q_{rated} = 0.75$

> **Step 1 — Damper (throttling) control power:**

$$HP_{damper} = HP_{rated} \cdot x = 320 \times 0.75$$

$$\boxed{HP_{damper} = 240 \;\; \text{kW}}$$

> **Step 2 — VSD control power (cubic law for parabolic system):**

$$HP_{VSD} = HP_{rated} \cdot x^3 = 320 \times (0.75)^3 = 320 \times 0.421875$$

$$\boxed{HP_{VSD} = 135 \;\; \text{kW}}$$

> **Step 3 — Energy saved:**

$$\Delta HP = 240 - 135 = 105 \;\; \text{kW}$$

$$\% \text{Savings} = \dfrac{105}{240} \times 100 = 43.75 \;\; \%$$

> **Step 4 — Annual energy savings (assuming 7000 hr/year operation):**

$$E_{saved} = 105 \times 7000 = 7{,}35{,}000 \;\; \text{kWh/year}$$

> At ₹7/kWh, monetary saving = **₹ 51.45 lakh/year** — strong economic justification for VSD retrofit.

**Conclusion:** VSD is far more efficient than damper control for partial-load operation of fans in industrial systems.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points in Damper & Fan Control Questions:**
> 1. **Mixing up the three affinity laws** — students often write $Q \propto N^2$ (wrong). It is **$Q \propto N$, $\Delta P \propto N^2$, $HP \propto N^3$**. *(–2 marks typical)*
> 2. **Forgetting to cube the speed ratio for power** in numericals — the cubic relationship is the most "KTU-favourite" and examiners specifically look for it. *(–2 marks)*
> 3. **Using $Q \propto Q^3$ for damper control** — this is for VSD. Damper control follows the **linear** law $HP \propto Q$. Mixing these up loses **full 7 marks** in Part B.
> 4. **No units in final answer** — KTU explicitly requires SI units (m³/hr for $Q$, Pa for $\Delta P$, kW for $HP$). *(–1 mark per numerical)*
> 5. **Omitting the diagram in 7-mark questions** — a "neat sketch" carries **at least 1–2 marks** by itself.
> 6. **Writing "damper and fan are the same thing"** — penalty of **–2 marks** for conceptual error.
> 7. **Not justifying the choice of control method** with turndown ratio or efficiency — write *both* reasons.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (High-Density Summary):**

- **Damper:** A flow-modulating device in a duct. Most common industrial type is the **louver damper** (parallel-blade for isolation, **opposed-blade for modulating control**).
- **Damper flow equation:** $Q = C_d A_{eff} \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho}$ with $C_d \approx 0.6$–$0.65$ and $\rho_{air} = 1.20$ kg/m³ at NTP.
- **Fan:** Rotary device imparting energy to gas. Types: **centrifugal** (high $\Delta P$), **axial** (high $Q$, low $\Delta P$), **mixed-flow**.
- **Fan Affinity Laws (Constant $D$):**
  - $Q \propto N$
  - $\Delta P \propto N^2$
  - $HP \propto N^3$ ← **most important for energy-saving arguments**
- **Fan Affinity Laws (Variable $D$):** $Q \propto D^3 N$, $\Delta P \propto D^2 N^2$, $HP \propto D^5 N^3$.
- **System resistance:** $\Delta P_{sys} = K \cdot Q^n$ where **$n = 2$ for parabolic systems** (most ducts).
- **Operating point:** Intersection of fan curve and system resistance curve.
- **Damper control:** Fan at constant speed; power follows $HP \propto Q$ (linear). **Poor efficiency** at part load.
- **IGV control:** Pre-swirl at inlet; sub-linear $HP$–$Q$ law. **Moderate efficiency.**
- **VSD control:** Fan speed varied; power follows $HP \propto Q^3$ (cubic). **Best efficiency**, highest CapEx, best ROI.
- **Typical industrial savings:** Switching from damper to VSD at 70 % flow saves **≈ 50 %** of fan power.
- **Standards to mention:** **AMCA 500-D** (damper leakage classes), **ISO 5801** (fan testing), **API 673** (forced-draft fans in power).
- **Mnemonic:** "**Q-lin, P-quad, HP-cube**" → quickly recall all three affinity laws.
- **In an exam:** always state **units**, draw a **diagram**, and **verify the speed ratio cubed** for power calculations.
- **Real-world impact:** A 500 MW unit's ID/FD/PA fans can consume **30–40 MW** of auxiliary power; VSD retrofit payback is typically **2–3 years**.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 4 Notes — Damper and Fan Controls.**
> Practice at least two affinity-law numericals and one energy-savings comparison numerical before the KTU ESE for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Furnace Draft Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT864 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN LARGE SCALE INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Furnace Draft Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Furnace Draft** is defined as the small negative pressure (typically **-2 mm to -10 mm of water column**, i.e., **-19.6 Pa to -98 Pa**) maintained inside a furnace or combustion chamber relative to the atmospheric pressure, for the purpose of safely drawing combustion air into the firebox, ensuring complete combustion of fuel, and safely exhausting the flue gases through the stack.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board-Exam Ready):**
> *Furnace Draft is the differential pressure between the furnace/combustion chamber interior and the surrounding atmosphere, created and regulated to control the flow of combustion air and flue gases. It is the primary controlled variable in boiler/furnace combustion control loops.*

## 1.2 The Three Reference Pressures in a Furnace

To understand draft control, you must remember these **three distinct pressures**:

| Zone | Pressure Status | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Atmosphere** | Reference (Zero) | 0 mm WC |
| **Furnace / Firebox** | Slightly **negative** | **-2 to -10 mm WC** |
| **Combustion Air Duct (Windbox)** | Slightly **positive** | **+5 to +25 mm WC** |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "Drinking Through a Straw"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:**
> Think of drinking water through a straw. When you **suck air out** of the straw (creating a slight negative pressure inside), the atmospheric pressure on the water in the glass **pushes** the water up into the straw. Similarly, in a furnace:
> - The **Induced Draft (ID) Fan** "sucks out" flue gases, creating a slight vacuum (negative draft).
> - Atmospheric pressure then **pushes fresh air** into the furnace through the FD fan and air-preheater.
> - The **Forced Draft (FD) Fan** further pressurizes this incoming air into the windbox.
>
> Without the negative draft, smoke and unburnt fuel would leak OUT through furnace doors (dangerous!). With too much draft, heat would be lost too quickly, and fuel consumption would skyrocket.

## 1.4 Why Draft Control is a Critical Control Loop

Draft control is **non-negotiable** in large-scale industrial furnaces because:

1. **Safety** — Prevents flame blow-out AND prevents hot gases/soot leakage into the working area.
2. **Combustion Efficiency** — Right draft = right air-fuel ratio = complete combustion = **less CO, less soot, less unburnt carbon**.
3. **Heat Transfer** — Proper draft maintains flame shape and heat-release pattern, optimizing convective and radiative heat transfer in the heat-transfer surfaces (water walls, superheaters).
4. **Environmental Compliance** — Reduces CO, NOx, and particulate emissions.
5. **Equipment Longevity** — Prevents back-fire, pulsation, and over-pressure that damage refractories.

## 1.5 Classification of Draft Systems

There are three primary classifications used across KTU Board examinations:

### A) Natural Draft
- Uses a **tall chimney/stack** (typically 30–60 m).
- Draft is created by the buoyancy of **hot flue gases** versus cold ambient air.
- **No moving parts**, but **limited and uncontrollable** — once chimney height is fixed, draft is fixed.
- Used in small boilers, domestic furnaces.

### B) Forced Draft (Positive Pressure Furnace)
- Uses an **FD Fan** to push air *into* the windbox and furnace.
- Furnace pressure is maintained **positive** (above atmospheric).
- High risk of hot gas leakage; not preferred for fuel-handling areas.
- Used in some industrial heaters where leakage is acceptable.

### C) Induced Draft (Negative Pressure Furnace)
- Uses an **ID Fan** at the flue-gas outlet to *suck out* gases.
- Furnace pressure is maintained **negative** relative to atmosphere.

### D) Balanced Draft (Most Common in Industry) ⭐
- Combines **both** FD Fan (pushes air in) **AND** ID Fan (pulls gases out).
- Furnace is maintained at a **slight negative pressure** (typically **-2 to -5 mm WC**).
- **Independent control** of air flow and flue gas flow — this is the KTU-favourite configuration.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theory of Draft Generation

The draft is fundamentally a manifestation of **Bernoulli's equation** applied to a flowing gas column (combustion products in the stack vs. cold air outside).

The theoretical draft generated by a chimney of height *H* is given by:

$$H_d = H \cdot (\rho_{air} - \rho_{gas}) \cdot g$$

where:
- $H_d$ = Theoretical draft in Pascals (Pa) or mm WC
- $H$ = Effective chimney height (m)
- $\rho_{air}$ = Density of ambient air (~ **1.225 kg/m³ at NTP**)
- $\rho_{gas}$ = Density of hot flue gas (~ **0.5 kg/m³ at 200 °C**)
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**

### Logic Behind the Equation:
1. Hot gases inside the chimney are **lighter** (lower density) than ambient air outside.
2. This creates a **column imbalance** — ambient air weighs more than the gas column.
3. The heavier outside air pushes **downward**, displacing the lighter gas **upward**.
4. This pressure differential at the bottom is the **natural draft**.

## 2.2 The Three Critical Draft Pressures (Measured Variables)

A typical balanced-draft boiler has **three differential pressure (dP) measurements**:

| Measurement | Symbol | Typical Range | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Furnace Draft** | $P_{FD}$ | **-2 to -10 mm WC** | Main controlled variable |
| **Air-Flow Differential** | $P_{Air}$ | **+5 to +50 mm WC** | Proportional to air flow |
| **Windbox-to-Furnace dP** | $P_{WB}$ | **+5 to +25 mm WC** | Indicates FD fan output |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All draft pressures are measured using a **Differential Pressure Transmitter (DPT)** connected across two pressure tappings via an **impulse line**. In KTU boards, you must mention the unit **mm WC (water column)** or **in WC** — both are acceptable, but mm WC is SI-preferred in India.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat-Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — Memorize for KTU 2024 Exams**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H_d = H(\rho_a - \rho_g)g$ | Theoretical natural draft | $H$ = chimney height; $\rho$ = density | Pa or mm WC |
| 2 | $F_{air} = \frac{138 \cdot O_2}{21 - O_2}$ (Orsat) | Excess air from Orsat analysis | $O_2$ = % O₂ in flue gas | % |
| 3 | $\Delta P_{fan} = K \cdot Q^2 \cdot \rho$ | Fan pressure rise | $Q$ = volume flow; $\rho$ = density | Pa |
| 4 | $P_{fan} \propto N^2$ | Fan affinity law (pressure) | $N$ = fan speed | RPM |
| 5 | $Q_{fan} \propto N$ | Fan affinity law (flow) | — | m³/s |
| 6 | $\text{Stack Loss \%} = \frac{T_g - T_a}{CO_2\%} \cdot k$ | Siegert's empirical formula | $T_g$ = flue gas temp; $T_a$ = ambient | % |
| 7 | $\eta_{comb} = 100 - \text{Losses}$ | Combustion efficiency | — | % |
| 8 | $A:F = \frac{11.6 \cdot O_2}{21 - O_2}$ | Air-Fuel ratio from Orsat | — | kg/kg |

> [!CAUTION]
> When writing formulas in markdown tables, **never** use the vertical bar `|` inside expressions. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead. Example: write $y \mid x$ not $y \mid x$ in tables.

## 2.4 Excess Air and Orsat Analysis

Orsat analysis is a wet-chemical gas analysis method that measures the **percentage composition** of dry flue gas: typically **% CO₂, % O₂, % CO**.

The **excess air percentage** is calculated as:

$$\% \text{Excess Air} = \frac{O_2 \times 100}{21 - O_2} \text{ (approximately, for O}_2\text{-only flue gas)}$$

$$\% \text{Excess Air} = \frac{O_2 - 0.5 \cdot CO}{0.264 \cdot N_2 - (O_2 - 0.5 \cdot CO)} \times 100$$

### Real-World Interpretation:
- **0–5% excess air** → Efficient but risk of incomplete combustion (CO spikes).
- **10–20% excess air** → **Optimal range** for most industrial boilers.
- **>30% excess air** → Excessive draft, heat loss through stack, low efficiency.

## 2.5 Draft Control Loop Components

A complete KTU-recognizable draft control loop comprises:

1. **Primary Element (Sensor):** Differential Pressure Transmitter (DPT) — e.g., Rosemount 3051CD, Honeywell ST3000.
2. **Signal Conditioner / Converter:** I/P (Current-to-Pressure) transducer for pneumatic actuators.
3. **Controller:** PID controller (electronic or pneumatic) — historically, Bailey, Foxboro, Yokogawa.
4. **Final Control Element:** **Dampers** (butterfly or louvre type) on FD/ID fan inlet or outlet ducts.
5. **Actuator:** Pneumatic diaphragm or electric motor-operated actuator (MOA).
6. **Auxiliary:** Furnace pressure transmitter, air-flow transmitter, fuel-flow transmitter.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** When asked to "explain a draft control loop", always draw **the closed loop with arrows showing signal flow**: Sensor → Controller → I/P → Actuator → Damper → Process (Furnace) → back to Sensor. **Direction of arrows = direction of signal/pneumatic flow**, NOT process flow.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Theoretical Natural Draft

**Starting Principle:** A column of hot gas in a chimney of height *H* is lighter than an equal column of ambient cold air. The pressure difference at the base of the chimney equals the weight difference of these two columns.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** Pressure at the base of a static gas column of height *H* and density *ρ* is:

$$P_{base} = \rho \cdot g \cdot H$$

**Step 2:** Apply this to **both** the inside (hot flue gas) and outside (ambient air) columns of the chimney:

$$P_{inside, base} = \rho_{gas} \cdot g \cdot H$$

$$P_{outside, base} = \rho_{air} \cdot g \cdot H$$

**Step 3:** The **theoretical draft** is defined as the pressure difference that drives flue gas up the stack. This is the *outside* pressure minus the *inside* pressure (since the draft "pulls" the gas up):

$$H_d = P_{outside} - P_{inside}$$

$$H_d = \rho_{air} \cdot g \cdot H - \rho_{gas} \cdot g \cdot H$$

**Step 4:** Factor out the common terms ($g \cdot H$):

$$H_d = g \cdot H \cdot (\rho_{air} - \rho_{gas})$$

This is the final **natural-draft equation**.

### Numerical Evaluation (Worked Example for KTU Board):

**Given:**
- Chimney height, $H = 40$ m
- Flue gas temperature, $T_g = 250°$C $= 523$ K
- Ambient temperature, $T_a = 30°$C $= 303$ K
- Ambient pressure = 1 atm

**Step 5:** Calculate ambient air density using ideal gas law:

$$\rho_{air} = \frac{P \cdot M}{R \cdot T_a} = \frac{101325 \cdot 0.029}{8.314 \cdot 303}$$

$$\rho_{air} = \frac{2938.4}{2519.1} = 1.166 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 6:** Assuming flue gas molecular weight $M_g \approx 0.030$ kg/mol (mostly CO₂, H₂O, N₂):

$$\rho_{gas} = \frac{101325 \cdot 0.030}{8.314 \cdot 523} = \frac{3039.75}{4348.22}$$

$$\rho_{gas} = 0.699 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 7:** Substitute into the draft equation:

$$H_d = 9.81 \cdot 40 \cdot (1.166 - 0.699)$$

$$H_d = 9.81 \cdot 40 \cdot 0.467$$

$$H_d = 183.25 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 8:** Convert to mm WC (1 mm WC = 9.81 Pa):

$$H_d = \frac{183.25}{9.81} = 18.68 \text{ mm WC}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** A 40 m tall chimney with 250 °C flue gases generates roughly **18.7 mm WC** of theoretical draft — sufficient to draw combustion air through a small industrial furnace. Notice that draft increases with **(a)** chimney height, **(b)** density difference (i.e., hotter gases), and **(c)** lower ambient temperature.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Fan Affinity (Fan Laws)

The Fan Affinity Laws are the foundation for variable-speed draft control. They relate fan performance to rotational speed.

### Assumption: Geometrically similar fans operating at the same point of rating.

**Law 1 — Flow proportionality:**

$$\frac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2}$$

(Volume flow rate *Q* is directly proportional to speed *N*.)

**Law 2 — Pressure proportionality:**

$$\frac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2$$

(Pressure rise *P* is proportional to the square of speed.)

**Law 3 — Power proportionality:**

$$\frac{W_1}{W_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^3$$

(Power consumed *W* is proportional to the cube of speed.)

### Step-by-Step Derivation of Law 1:

Consider two geometrically similar fans. The fluid velocity at the impeller tip is:

$$v = \pi \cdot D \cdot N$$

where *D* is the impeller diameter. By geometric similarity, $D_1 = D_2$. So:

$$v \propto N$$

Since volumetric flow $Q = A \cdot v$ and $A$ is constant:

$$\therefore Q \propto N$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation of Law 2:

By Euler's turbomachine equation, the pressure rise across an impeller is:

$$\Delta P = \rho \cdot u \cdot \Delta v_\theta$$

where *u* is the blade speed and $\Delta v_\theta$ is the change in tangential velocity of the fluid. Both *u* and *v* are proportional to *N*, so:

$$\Delta P \propto N^2$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation of Law 3:

Power is flow times pressure:

$$W = Q \cdot \Delta P$$

Substituting $Q \propto N$ and $\Delta P \propto N^2$:

$$W \propto N \cdot N^2 = N^3$$

$$\therefore W \propto N^3$$

### Practical KTU Insight:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why variable-speed drives save energy:**
> If a fan runs at **50% speed**, it consumes only **12.5% power** (not 50%!). This is why modern large-scale industries (e.g., 500 MW power stations) use **VFD (Variable Frequency Drives)** on FD and ID fans instead of inlet damper control — energy savings of 30–40% are typical.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Draft Control Loop Equations

The standard PID controller output equation used in draft control is:

$$m(t) = K_c \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{\tau_i}\int e(t)\,dt + \tau_d \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$$

where:
- $m(t)$ = Controller output (3–15 psi pneumatic or 4–20 mA electronic)
- $K_c$ = Proportional gain
- $\tau_i$ = Integral time (reset time) in minutes
- $\tau_d$ = Derivative time in minutes
- $e(t) = SP - PV$ = Setpoint minus measured furnace pressure

### Typical Tuning Values for Furnace Draft Control:

| Parameter | Typical Range (Pneumatic Controller) | Typical Range (Electronic Controller) |
|---|---|---|
| **Proportional Band (PB)** | **100–300 %** | $K_c = 2$ to $10$ |
| **Integral Time ($\tau_i$)** | **0.5–5 min/repeat** | $0.1$ to $2$ min |
| **Derivative Time ($\tau_d$)** | **0 (usually OFF)** | $0$ (usually OFF) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Pitfall:** Draft control loops almost NEVER use derivative action because furnace pressure is a **noisy signal** (flame turbulence causes rapid fluctuations). Derivative action would amplify this noise and cause damper chatter. **Use PI control only** — derivative is OFF.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Balanced Draft Furnace System

This is the **most-frequently-asked diagram** in KTU exams on furnace draft. Please redraw it during your exam for full marks.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Atmospheric Air Inlet] --> A2[Air Pre-Heater]
    A2 --> A3[Forced Draft FD Fan]
    A3 --> A4[Windbox]
    A4 --> A5[Furnace Firebox]
    A5 --> A6[Boiler Bank / Heat Transfer Tubes]
    A6 --> A7[Economizer]
    A7 --> A8[Induced Draft ID Fan]
    A8 --> A9[Electrostatic Precipitator ESP]
    A9 --> A10[Stack Chimney]
    A10 --> A11[Atmosphere Exhaust]

    subgraph Sensors[Sensors]
        S1[Furnace Draft DPT]
        S2[Air Flow DPT across nozzle]
        S3[Fuel Flow Transmitter FT]
    end

    A5 -.measured by.-> S1
    A4 -.measured by.-> S2
    FUEL[Fuel Supply] --> A5
    FUEL -.measured by.-> S3

    subgraph Control[Control Room]
        C1[Master Controller]
        C2[Fuel Controller]
        C3[Air Flow Controller]
        C4[Draft Controller]
    end

    S1 --> C4
    S2 --> C3
    S3 --> C2

    C2 --> AC1[Fuel Valve Actuator]
    C3 --> AC2[FD Fan Inlet Damper]
    C4 --> AC3[ID Fan Inlet Damper]

    AC1 --> FUEL
    AC2 --> A3
    AC3 --> A8
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Three-Element Draft Control Strategy

In large utility boilers, a **cascaded three-element control** is used. The master controller (furnace pressure) sets the setpoint of the slave controller (air flow).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SP1[Setpoint Furnace Pressure -3 mm WC] --> SUM1((Sum))
    PT[Furnace Draft DPT] --> SUM1
    SUM1 --> FC1[Furnace Draft PID Controller Master]
    FC1 -->|Remote SP| SUM2((Sum))
    FT[Air Flow Transmitter] --> SUM2
    SUM2 --> FC2[Air Flow PID Controller Slave]
    FC2 --> IP1[I/P Converter]
    IP1 --> AC1[Damper Actuator on ID Fan]
    AC1 --> PROC[ID Fan / Furnace]
    PROC --> PT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:**
> - **Outer (Master) loop:** Furnace pressure is the **primary** controlled variable.
> - **Inner (Slave) loop:** Air flow is **faster** and is the secondary variable.
> - Cascade structure gives **faster disturbance rejection** and **smoother control**.

## 4.3 Fan Curve — Operating Point Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fan Curve: ΔP vs Q] --> B[System Resistance Curve: ΔP = K*Q squared]
    B --> C[Intersection = Operating Point]
    C --> D[At Speed N1: Operating Point OP1]
    C --> E[At Speed N2 N1: Operating Point OP2 higher flow]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always mark the **operating point** as the intersection of the fan curve and system resistance curve. Changing fan speed shifts the fan curve vertically (ΔP ∝ N²); changing damper opening changes the slope of the system curve. The new operating point is where they intersect.

## 4.4 Signal Flow Diagram — Complete Closed-Loop Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP((Setpoint -3 mm WC)) --> SUM((Summing Junction e = SP - PV))
    PV[Process Variable Furnace Pressure] --> SUM
    SUM --> PID[PID Controller Kc, Ti, Td]
    PID --> IP[I/P Converter 4-20mA to 3-15 psi]
    IP --> ACT[Damper Actuator Pneumatic]
    ACT --> DAMP[Inlet Damper on ID Fan]
    DAMP --> FURN[Furnace Process]
    FURN --> TRANSD[DPT Differential Pressure Transmitter]
    TRANSD --> PV
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU exam, when you draw this loop, you **must label each block** with its function. Marks are awarded for: (1) correct direction of signal flow, (2) all six components shown, (3) feedback loop closed.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## **PART A — 3-Mark Questions**

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define furnace draft. Why is a slight negative pressure maintained in an industrial furnace?**

**Model Answer:**

**Furnace Draft:** Furnace draft is the small negative pressure maintained inside the furnace/combustion chamber relative to atmospheric pressure, typically in the range of **-2 to -10 mm of water column**.

**Reasons for maintaining negative pressure (3 points):**

1. **Safety:** Prevents hot flue gases, flame, and soot from leaking out of the furnace through observation doors, inspection ports, and seams, thereby protecting operating personnel from burns and the working area from smoke contamination.

2. **Flue gas removal:** Ensures that combustion products (CO₂, H₂O, SO₂, N₂) are continuously drawn towards the ID fan and discharged up the stack, preventing accumulation that would suffocate the flame.

3. **Controlled air ingress:** Slight negative pressure allows a controlled, predictable amount of combustion air to enter through the FD fan pathway, ensuring stable air-fuel ratio for complete combustion.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List the differences between Forced Draft and Induced Draft systems.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Forced Draft (FD) | Induced Draft (ID) |
|---|---|---|
| **Location of fan** | Before the furnace (inlet air side) | After the furnace (flue gas side) |
| **Furnace pressure** | **Positive** (above atmospheric) | **Negative** (below atmospheric) |
| **Function** | **Pushes** combustion air into furnace | **Sucks out** flue gases from furnace |
| **Leakage direction** | Hot gases leak OUT through doors (dangerous) | Cold air leaks IN through doors (safer) |
| **Typical use** | Small heaters, gas turbines | Utility boilers, large furnaces |
| **Draft value** | +5 to +25 mm WC | -2 to -10 mm WC |
| **Power consumption** | Lower | Higher (handles hot gases, larger volume) |

## **PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)**

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** Explain the working of a balanced draft furnace with a neat block diagram. How is furnace draft pressure measured and controlled?

#### **Model Solution:**

**Introduction [1 Mark]:**
A balanced draft furnace uses **two fans — a Forced Draft (FD) fan** at the air-inlet side and an **Induced Draft (ID) fan** at the flue-gas-outlet side — to maintain the furnace at a slight negative pressure (typically -3 to -5 mm WC).
