$$\Delta P = P_L - P_H = \rho_w \, g \, h_2$$

Hmm — this only gives the water-column height. To measure *interface position*, we must subtract the dry-boot offset.

### Step 5 — Subtract the Zero (Suppression) and Span (Elevation)

Industrial transmitters use the equation:

$$\Delta P_{measured} = \rho_o \, g \, h_{above\,interface} + \rho_w \, g \, h_{water\,column} - \rho_{calibration\,fluid} \, g \, h_{full}$$

The **zero** is suppressed when the boot is full of water only. The **span** corresponds to going from "boot full of water" to "interface at the top of the boot".

### Step 6 — Engineering Calibration (Standard Industry Practice)

Most interface D/P cells are calibrated using **water** as the fill fluid on bench, then *corrected* for the actual operating SG:

$$\Delta P_{operating} = \Delta P_{calibrated} \times \frac{(\rho_w - \rho_o)_{operating}}{(\rho_w - \rho_o)_{water}}$$

Since water-on-water calibration has the same density on both sides, the calibrated span for the *interface* is:

$$\Delta P_{span} = (\rho_w - \rho_o) \, g \, h_{boot}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** If oil density $\rho_o = 850$ kg/m³ and water density $\rho_w = 1020$ kg/m³, then $(\rho_w - \rho_o) = 170$ kg/m³. For a 1 m boot, $\Delta P_{span} = 170 \times 9.81 \times 1 = 1667.7$ Pa $\approx 6.7$ inches H₂O. This is the typical low-span range of an interface D/P cell — which is why interface transmitters are *much more sensitive* than vessel-level D/P cells.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choice of Level / Interface Technology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison Table — Memorize This for KTU Board Exams.**

| Technology | Working Principle | Best Suited For | Advantages | Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **D/P Cell (Wet-Wet)** | Hydrostatic head difference | Level, interface (3-phase) | Simple, robust, industry-proven | Density variation errors |
| **Displacer-Type** | Buoyancy force on immersed displacer | Interface in narrow boot | Mechanical, no calibration drift | Moving parts, vibration issues |
| **Capacitance Probe** | Dielectric change between oil and water | Interface in non-foaming service | No moving parts, fast response | Sensitive to coating, buildup |
| **Guided Wave Radar (GWR)** | Time-of-flight of EM pulse | Level despite foam/turbulence | Unaffected by foam/density | Cost, interface only in clean service |
| **Non-Contact Radar** | FMCW microwave reflection | Top-mounted level | No contact, no fouling | Foam can damp signal |
| **Float / Sight Glass** | Mechanical float or sight glass | Local indication | Simple, cheap, visual | No remote signal, prone to leak |
| **Ultrasonic** | Echo time of sound wave | Open vessels | Cheap | Foam defeats accuracy |
| **Conductivity Probe** | Electrical continuity in water | Simple water detection | Cheap, reliable | Single point, water-only |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (For Numerical Questions in KTU)

**Problem:** A 3-phase horizontal separator has oil of density **880 kg/m³** sitting above water of density **1020 kg/m³**. The D/P cell measuring the oil–water interface has a low-side tap at 1.2 m below the high-side tap. When the interface is exactly at the high-side tap level, the reading is **4.50 kPa**. What is the new interface position if the reading becomes **2.25 kPa**?

### Solution

**Step 1.** Compute the effective density differential:

$$\Delta\rho = \rho_w - \rho_o = 1020 - 880 = 140 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 2.** At zero interface position (water fills entire 1.2 m), the theoretical maximum DP is:

$$\Delta P_{max} = \Delta\rho \cdot g \cdot h_{max} = 140 \times 9.81 \times 1.2 = 1648.1 \text{ Pa} \approx 1.648 \text{ kPa}$$

But the actual reading at "interface at high-tap" is 4.50 kPa, which includes the **wet-leg suppression pressure** (water column above the low-side tap).

**Step 3.** The *change* in DP per metre of interface rise:

$$\frac{d(\Delta P)}{dh} = -\Delta\rho \cdot g = -140 \times 9.81 = -1373.4 \text{ Pa/m}$$

(Interface rising means the water column below the high tap is *shrinking* — hence the negative sign.)

**Step 4.** Change in DP observed:

$$\delta(\Delta P) = 2.25 - 4.50 = -2.25 \text{ kPa} = -2250 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 5.** Height the interface has risen:

$$\Delta h = \frac{-2250}{-1373.4} = 1.638 \text{ m}$$

This is **greater than the 1.2 m tap-to-tap distance**, which indicates the interface has moved *above* the high-side tap — meaning **oil carryover into the water outlet is imminent** (a real-world alarm condition).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Forgetting to convert kPa to Pa before dividing by $(\rho_w - \rho_o) g$. This single error costs 2 marks in the KTU board valuation scheme.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

    LT1 --> LCV1["LCV-101 Level Ctrl Valve (Oil Outlet)"]:::control

    LIR1 --> LCV2["LCV-102 Interface Ctrl Valve (Water Drain)"]:::control
    TT1 --> TCV1["TCV-101 Temp Ctrl Valve (Heater)"]:::control

### How to Read the Diagram
- **Yellow nodes** = Process Input (wellhead stream).
- **Grey central node** = The separator vessel itself.
- **Blue nodes** = Field sensors (transmitters).
- **Green nodes** = Control valves (final control elements).
- **Gold node** = The DCS/PLC controller (the "brain").
- **Red nodes** = Safety/Emergency Shutdown system.
- **Lavender node** = Operator Human-Machine Interface.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram — Interface Level Control Modes

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NormalOperation: System Start
    
    NormalOperation --> HighInterfaceAlarm: Interface rises above HiHi setpoint
    NormalOperation --> LowInterfaceAlarm: Interface falls below LoLo setpoint
    NormalOperation --> HighPressureAlarm: PT reading > Hi setpoint
    NormalOperation --> LowPressureAlarm: PT reading < Lo setpoint
    
    HighInterfaceAlarm --> NormalOperation: Operator Ack & Auto-Correct
    HighInterfaceAlarm --> ESD1: Sustained trip > 30 sec
    LowInterfaceAlarm --> NormalOperation: Auto-Correct via LCV-102
    LowInterfaceAlarm --> ESD2: Sustained trip > 30 sec
    
    HighPressureAlarm --> ESD3: PSV Opens, then ESD
    LowPressureAlarm --> NormalOperation: Auto pressure recovery
    
    ESD1 --> SafeShutdown: All Outlets Close, Vent Opens
    ESD2 --> SafeShutdown
    ESD3 --> SafeShutdown
    
    SafeShutdown --> ManualReset: Operator field verification
    ManualReset --> NormalOperation: Reset & Restart Sequence
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: With a neat sketch, explain the working of a three-phase horizontal separator used in the oil industry.**

**Model Answer (3 Mark Key Points):**

1. **Construction**: Horizontal cylindrical pressure vessel with two chambers — the *main vessel* (gas/oil separation) and the *boot* (water collection). Inlet diverter, vane pack, mist extractor, weir plates, and coalescer pads are fitted internally.

2. **Working**: Multiphase feed enters through the inlet diverter (1), sudden momentum drop separates gas, which flows to the top via vane pack (2), oil flows over the weir into the oil chamber (3), and water settles in the boot (4). Gas exits via top outlet, oil via weir-controlled outlet, water via boot drain.

3. **Instrumentation**: LT for oil level, LIR for oil–water interface, PT for pressure, TT for temperature. ESD valves on all three outlets.

> **Valuation tip**: A labeled sketch (even rough) carries **at least 1 mark** in KTU marking.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between level measurement and interface measurement in an oil–water separator. Which instruments are typically used?**

**Model Answer:**

- **Level measurement** determines the height of a *single liquid* (e.g., oil) above a reference datum.
- **Interface measurement** determines the *boundary between two immiscible liquids* (oil above, water below).
- In a 3-phase separator, **D/P cell** with two impulse taps is the most common, calibrated using the relation $\Delta P = (\rho_w - \rho_o) g h_{interface}$.
- **Displacer-type** and **capacitance probes** are also used. The D/P cell measures differential hydrostatic head, displacer measures buoyancy force change as the boot fills with water vs oil, and capacitance probes exploit the dielectric contrast ($\varepsilon_{oil} \approx 2.2$ vs $\varepsilon_{water} \approx 80$).

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module-Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** *Choose either Question A or Question B. Each has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.*

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a labeled block diagram, explain the **complete instrumentation and control scheme** for a 3-phase horizontal oil–gas–water separator. Include all four primary measurements, their transmitters, controllers, and final control elements.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Primary Measurements & Transmitters (3 Marks):**

| Variable | Transmitter | Tag | Location |
|---|---|---|---|
| Oil level | D/P Cell | LT-101 | Top of main vessel |
| Oil-water interface | D/P Cell (Wet-Wet) | LIT-102 | Boot section |
| Pressure | Pressure Tx | PT-101 | Top of main vessel |
| Temperature | RTD / Thermocouple | TT-101 | Mid-vessel |

**2. Controllers (2 Marks):**
- **LIC-101**: Level controller — oil outlet.
- **LIC-102 (Interface)**: Interface controller — water drain from boot.
- **PIC-101**: Pressure controller — gas outlet back-pressure valve.
- **TIC-101**: Temperature controller — heater bypass valve.

**3. Final Control Elements (1 Mark):**
- Three pneumatic globe valves (oil, gas, water) with diaphragm actuators and I/P converters.
- One ESD block valve on each outlet, spring-return, fail-close.

**4. Block Diagram (1 Mark):**
Refer to the architecture shown in Section 4.1 (Mermaid block diagram). Each transmitter → DCS → PID controller → I/P → actuator → valve.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 3-phase separator handles **5000 bbl/day** of crude (SG = 0.88) with a water cut of **30%**. The interface D/P cell has a calibrated span of **0–1.5 m of water**. Compute (i) the interface-level percentage when the D/P reads **7.2 kPa**, and (ii) the operating DP span in operating conditions. Assume $\rho_w = 1020$ kg/m³.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute effective density differential at operating conditions.**

$$\Delta\rho = \rho_w - \rho_o = 1020 - 880 = 140 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 2: Compute operating DP span (ii).**

$$\Delta P_{span} = \Delta\rho \cdot g \cdot h_{max} = 140 \times 9.81 \times 1.5 = 2060.1 \text{ Pa} \approx 2.06 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3: Compute the percentage level for the given DP reading (i).**

$$\text{Level \%} = \frac{\Delta P_{reading}}{\Delta P_{span}} \times 100 = \frac{7.2}{2.06} \times 100 \approx 349.5\%$$

This is **greater than 100%** — physically impossible.

**Step 4: Engineering interpretation.**

The reading of 7.2 kPa exceeds the maximum span (2.06 kPa). This indicates the D/P cell is **saturated** (likely due to gas breakthrough into the boot, or the boot is completely filled with water above the lower tap). **Operator action required**: Reduce water outlet flow, investigate gas carryover.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. ❌ Forgetting to convert **0–1.5 m of *water*** to *operational fluid* span using $\Delta\rho$ instead of full $\rho_w$ — costs **2 marks**.
> 2. ❌ Not commenting on the **physical impossibility** of > 100% level — costs **1 mark** for engineering judgment.
> 3. ❌ Failing to state the **operating SG correction formula** — costs **1 mark**.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **principle, construction, and working of a capacitance-type interface level probe** used in oil industry separators. List two advantages over a D/P cell.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Principle (2 Marks):**
A capacitance probe consists of two concentric electrodes separated by a dielectric. The capacitance is:

$$C = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L}{\ln(r_o / r_i)}$$

When the dielectric between the electrodes changes from oil ($\varepsilon_r \approx 2.2$) to water ($\varepsilon_r \approx 80$), the capacitance increases sharply — providing a clean, large-magnitude signal for interface detection.

**2. Construction (2 Marks):**
- Outer rod (ground electrode) and inner rod (live electrode) insulated with PTFE/Teflon.
- Mounted vertically in the boot section, spanning the expected interface range.
- Connected to a capacitance-to-current transmitter (Rosemount 3300 series as an industry reference example — students need not memorize model numbers).

**3. Working (2 Marks):**
The high-frequency excitation (~500 kHz) prevents ionic conduction through the water. The varying capacitance is converted to 4–20 mA. The signal is linearized to give interface height.

**4. Advantages over D/P cell (1 Mark):**
- ✅ No impulse lines → no plugging / no heat tracing required.
- ✅ Insensitive to density changes of the fluids (only dielectric matters).

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Emergency Shutdown (ESD) logic** for a high-pressure production separator. List the trip initiators and the actions taken.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Purpose of ESD (1 Mark):**
To bring the separator to a safe state automatically when any pre-defined unsafe condition is detected, preventing vessel rupture, fire, or explosion.

**2. Trip Initiators (3 Marks):**

| Trip Condition | Instrument | Setpoint Logic |
|---|---|---|
| High-High Pressure | PSHH-101 | > 110% design pressure |
| High-High Level | LSHH-101 | Liquid carryover to gas outlet |
| Low-Low Level | LSLL-101 | Gas blow-by into oil outlet |
| High-High Temperature | TSHH-101 | Above autoignition |
| Low-Low Pressure | PSLL-101 | Risk of collapse / implosion |
| Fire detected | Flame detector / Fusible plug | Field-mounted |
| Manual ESD pushbutton | HS-1001 | Operator initiated |

**3. ESD Actions (2 Marks):**
- ✅ ESD-1: Inlet ESD valve closes (fail-close, spring return).
- ✅ ESD-2: Gas outlet valve closes.
- ✅ ESD-3: Oil outlet valve closes.
- ✅ Open blowdown valve to flare (depressurize vessel).
- ✅ Activate firewater deluge (if fire detected).
- ✅ Send alarm to DCS and shutdown downstream pumps.

**4. Voting Logic (1 Mark):**
Use **2-out-of-3 (2oo3)** voting on critical trips (pressure and level) to prevent spurious trips.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often forget to specify **SIL (Safety Integrity Level)** rating of the ESD loop. KTU expects you to mention at least **SIL 2** for production separators per **IEC 61511**.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullet List:**

- ⭐ A **3-phase separator** separates gas, oil, and water using **gravity**, **coalescence**, and **chemical/electrostatic** enhancement.
- ⭐ The **boot section** is dedicated for water collection and houses the **oil–water interface probe**.
- ⭐ The four core measurements are: **Level (LT)**, **Interface (LIT)**, **Pressure (PT)**, **Temperature (TT)**.
- ⭐ **Interface measurement equation** (memorize): $\Delta P_{span} = (\rho_w - \rho_o) \, g \, h_{boot}$.
- ⭐ **Stokes' Law** gives droplet settling velocity: $v_t = \dfrac{d^2 g (\rho_L - \rho_G)}{18\mu}$.
- ⭐ **Capacitance probe** uses dielectric contrast (oil 2.2 vs water 80).
- ⭐ **D/P cell** uses hydrostatic head difference.
- ⭐ **Displacer** uses Archimedes buoyancy principle.
- ⭐ **GWR (Guided Wave Radar)** is preferred for foaming crudes.
- ⭐ **ESD trips** typically use **2oo3 voting** at **SIL 2** per IEC 61511.
- ⭐ Separator **inlet diverter**, **vane pack**, **mist extractor**, and **weir plate** are the four canonical internals — know their functions.
- ⭐ Common SG values: Light crude ≈ 0.82, Heavy crude ≈ 0.95, Brine ≈ 1.02–1.15.
- ⭐ **BS&W** (Basic Sediment & Water) export spec is typically < 1% — your control loop must hold this.
- ⭐ Always cite **mass balance** and **Stokes' settling** in derivation questions.
- ⭐ Drawing a **labeled block diagram** in any 14-mark question scores you easy 2–3 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Test Separators and Well Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT864 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN LARGE SCALE INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Test Separators and Well Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> A **Test Separator** is a specialized pressure vessel installed at the wellhead or gathering station, used during well-testing operations to physically segregate the total well stream into measurable oil, gas, and water phases under controlled conditions of pressure and temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Well Testing** is a controlled, time-bound diagnostic procedure performed on an oil/gas well using a temporary surface-test package (choke manifold + separator + metering) to evaluate reservoir and wellbore productivity, fluid properties, and the individual phase flow rates.

### 1.1 Real-World Analogy — The "Well as a Patient"

Imagine a doctor examining a patient. The patient (the well) produces a mixture of fluids — crude oil, natural gas, water, and sand. Just as a doctor needs to draw blood, run tests in a lab, and measure each parameter separately, the petroleum engineer cannot deploy a single measuring device that simultaneously quantifies oil, gas, and water from a mixed stream.

So the engineer routes the entire well stream into a **test separator** (the "laboratory centrifuge"), which — using gravity — cleanly splits the mixture into its constituent phases. Each phase is then routed through its own dedicated flow meter (the "lab equipment") so the engineer can obtain a precise diagnostic report (productivity index, GOR, water cut, etc.).

### 1.2 Standard Performance Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The four "vital signs" measured during every KTU-style well-test problem are:
> - **Oil Flow Rate (q_o)** in **stock-tank barrels per day (STB/D)** — **bold** standard unit.
> - **Gas Flow Rate (q_g)** in **million standard cubic feet per day (MMSCFD)** or **SCF/D**.
> - **Gas–Oil Ratio (GOR)** in **SCF/STB** — typical value: **500 to 2000 SCF/STB**.
> - **Water Cut (WC)** in **%** by volume.

### 1.3 Why Test Separators Are Crucial in Large-Scale Industries

In offshore platforms, LNG plants, and remote upstream gathering stations, a wrong GOR measurement can lead to incorrect allocation of revenue among partner companies worth **millions of dollars per month**. Hence the test separator acts as the **"revenue accountable meter"** of the upstream industry. Without precise instrumentation on the test separator, fiscal metering, custody transfer, and reservoir management decisions would all be in error.

### 1.4 Types of Test Separators

| Type | Phases Separated | Typical Capacity | KTU Exam Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| Two-Phase Horizontal | Gas + Liquid (oil or oil+water) | 5,000 – 20,000 BOPD | High |
| Two-Phase Vertical | Gas + Liquid | 1,000 – 5,000 BOPD | Medium |
| Three-Phase Horizontal | Gas + Oil + Water | 5,000 – 25,000 BOPD | Very High |
| Three-Phase Spherical | Gas + Oil + Water | 500 – 3,000 BOPD | Low |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 2024 Scheme, **three-phase horizontal test separators** are the most frequently examined configuration because they demonstrate the complete instrument suite (level, interface, pressure, flow, and ESD).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Gravity Settling (Stokes' Regime)

When the well stream enters the separator, it undergoes a sudden pressure drop. Gas flashes out of solution and rises to the top. The remaining liquid — oil and water — is given a **residence time** long enough for water droplets to settle below oil droplets because of density differences.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does separation work?**
> Because $ \rho_{water} > \rho_{oil} > \rho_{gas} $, gravity acts more strongly on denser phases. The terminal settling velocity of a water droplet inside an oil continuum (the "creaming inversion" of emulsion) is governed by **Stokes' Law**.

### 2.2 Internal Architecture of a Three-Phase Test Separator

The flow path follows a strict sequence:

1. **Inlet Diverter** (or "splash plate") — absorbs momentum and initiates primary gas–liquid separation.
2. **Deflection Baffle / Vane Pack / Mist Extractor** — removes entrained liquid droplets from the gas stream (target droplet size: **10 µm or larger**).
3. **Liquid Section** — contains a **weir** and **baffle plates** that direct oil over the top weir and water under the bottom weir.
4. **Interface Level Region** — the liquid–liquid interface is maintained at a controlled elevation by an **interface level controller**.
5. **Outlet Nozzles** — separate nozzles for gas, oil, and water, each with its own control valve.

### 2.3 Instrumentation and Control Loops

A standard KTU-exam test separator is governed by **four critical control loops**:

- **Pressure Control Loop (PCL)**: PIC on gas outlet → drives a back-pressure control valve on the gas line. Maintains separator at a set pressure (typically **50 to 150 psig**).
- **Oil-Level Control Loop (LCL)**: LIC on oil leg → drives a control valve on the oil outlet line. Maintains oil level at set point.
- **Water–Oil Interface Control Loop (WIL)**: LIC on interface → drives a control valve on the water outlet line. Maintains interface at a set point **between** the oil and water weirs.
- **Temperature Monitoring Loop**: TI/TC on the vessel body, used for viscosity correction of flow meters and to prevent hydrate formation.

> [!NOTE]
> **ESD (Emergency Shutdown) System**: In case of high-high level (HH), high-high pressure (HH), or fire, the **ESD valve** at the inlet closes within **≤ 3 seconds**, and the **blowdown valve** depressurizes the vessel safely.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v_s = \dfrac{g \cdot d^2 \cdot (\rho_w - \rho_o)}{18 \cdot \mu_o}$ | Stokes settling velocity of water droplet in oil | ft/s |
| 2 | $t_{res} = \dfrac{V_{liq}}{q_{liq}}$ | Liquid residence time | min |
| 3 | $J = \dfrac{q_o}{\bar{p}_r - p_{wf}}$ | Productivity Index | STB/D/psi |
| 4 | $GOR = \dfrac{q_g}{q_o}$ | Gas–Oil Ratio | SCF/STB |
| 5 | $WC (\%) = \dfrac{q_w}{q_w + q_o} \times 100$ | Water Cut | % |
| 6 | $BS\&W = \dfrac{V_{sediment+water}}{V_{total}} \times 100$ | Basic Sediment & Water | % |
| 7 | $q_{total} = q_o + q_w$ (volumetric) | Total liquid rate | STB/D |
| 8 | $V_{vessel} = q_o \cdot t_{res}$ | Vessel sizing equation | bbl |
| 9 | $N_{Re} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v \cdot D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number for flow meter | dimensionless |
| 10 | $q_{actual} = q_{std} \cdot \dfrac{z \cdot T}{P}$ | Gas-law conversion | MMSCFD |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constants Used**: $g = 32.174 \;\text{ft/s}^2$, $1 \;\text{bbl} = 5.615 \;\text{ft}^3$, $1 \;\text{psi} = 6.895 \;\text{kPa}$.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility in Large-Scale Industries

- **Offshore Platforms** (e.g., Mumbai High, North Sea, Persian Gulf): Test separators are skid-mounted and dual-train for redundancy.
- **Custody Transfer**: Fiscal metering of oil and gas for inter-company revenue split.
- **Reservoir Engineering**: Calculates reservoir pressure $\bar{p}_r$, permeability $k$, and skin factor $s$ using transient well-test equations.
- **Process Optimization**: Provides live data to DCS for choke-management decisions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Productivity Index (J)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Productivity Index (J) is the most important well-test performance metric for KTU board exams. It is a direct measure of how much oil the well produces per psi of drawdown.

**Step 1 — Write Darcy's radial flow equation for an oil well in pseudo-steady state:**

$$
q_o = \dfrac{0.00708 \cdot k \cdot h \cdot (\bar{p}_r - p_{wf})}{\mu_o \cdot B_o \cdot \left[\ln\left(\dfrac{r_e}{r_w}\right) - 0.75 + s\right]}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the Productivity Index (J) as the flow rate per unit drawdown:**

$$
J \equiv \dfrac{q_o}{\bar{p}_r - p_{wf}}
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to express J explicitly in terms of reservoir properties:**

$$
J = \dfrac{0.00708 \cdot k \cdot h}{\mu_o \cdot B_o \cdot \left[\ln\left(\dfrac{r_e}{r_w}\right) - 0.75 + s\right]}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate units:**
- $k$ → millidarcies (mD)
- $h$ → feet (ft)
- $\mu_o$ → centipoise (cP)
- $B_o$ → reservoir bbl / STB (rbbl/STB)
- $J$ → STB/D/psi

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**
A high $J$ value means a **strong well** (high deliverability). A declining $J$ over time indicates **reservoir depletion** or **skin damage** (e.g., scale, asphaltene deposition).

### 3.2 Derivation of Gas-Oil Ratio from Test-Separator Measurements

**Step 1 — Measure gas flow at standard conditions (60°F, 14.7 psia):**

$$
q_{g,\text{std}} = C_d \cdot A_o \cdot Y \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P \cdot \rho_g}{1 - \beta^4}}
$$

**Step 2 — Measure oil flow at standard conditions (after stock-tank):

$$
q_{o,\text{std}} = \dfrac{N_{pulses}}{K_{\text{meter}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute GOR:**

$$
GOR = \dfrac{q_{g,\text{std}}}{q_{o,\text{std}}} \quad [\text{SCF/STB}]
$$

**Step 4 — Real-world interpretation:**
If $GOR$ increases monotonically with time → reservoir gas cap is expanding, indicating **gas breakthrough**.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Exam Style

**Problem Statement:**
A test separator receives a well stream. The oil flow meter reads **1500 STB/D**, the water flow meter reads **300 STB/D**, and the gas flow meter reads **900,000 SCF/D** at standard conditions. Compute (a) water cut, (b) GOR, and (c) total liquid rate.

**Solution:**

**(a) Water Cut (WC):**

$$
WC = \dfrac{q_w}{q_w + q_o} \times 100
$$

$$
WC = \dfrac{300}{300 + 1500} \times 100 = \dfrac{300}{1800} \times 100
$$

$$
WC = 16.67\%
$$

**(b) Gas-Oil Ratio (GOR):**

$$
GOR = \dfrac{q_g}{q_o} = \dfrac{900{,}000}{1500}
$$

$$
GOR = 600 \;\text{SCF/STB}
$$

**(c) Total Liquid Rate:**

$$
q_{total} = q_o + q_w = 1500 + 300
$$

$$
q_{total} = 1800 \;\text{STB/D}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Two-Phase vs Three-Phase Separators

| Parameter | Two-Phase Test Separator | Three-Phase Test Separator |
|---|---|---|
| Number of level instruments | 1 (oil level only) | 2 (oil level + interface level) |
| Type of level instrument | Float / DP / Displacer | DP / Guided-wave radar + interface probe |
| Cost | ~$200,000 | ~$400,000 |
| ESD complexity | Medium | High (HH level + HH pressure + fire) |
| Typical KTU mark allocation | 7 marks | 14 marks |
| Real-world application | Gas wells / dry-oil wells | Wells producing water |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Insight**: Three-phase separators carry **higher marks** in KTU 2024 ESE because they require explanation of *two simultaneous* level loops (oil level + interface level). Always draw both control loops to score full marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Well Test Package — Functional Block Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the complete interaction of equipment, instruments, and control loops during a well-test operation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Wellhead Christmas Tree] --> B[Choke Manifold Adjustable Choke]
    B --> C[Flowline Heater Sand Probe SP]
    C --> D[Three-Phase Test Separator]
    D --> E[Gas Outlet PCV Pressure Control Valve]
    D --> F[Oil Outlet LCV Level Control Valve]
    D --> G[Water Outlet LCV2 Interface Control Valve]
    E --> H[Gas Flow Meter Orifice Turbine]
    F --> I[Oil Flow Meter PD Turbine Coriolis]
    G --> J[Water Flow Meter PD Turbine]
    H --> K[Gas Scrubber Flare Stack]
    I --> L[Production Manifold to Storage]
    J --> L
    M[PT Pressure Transmitter on Vessel] --> N[DCS Distributed Control System]
    O[LT Oil Level Transmitter] --> N
    P[IT Interface Level Transmitter] --> N
    Q[TT Temperature Transmitter] --> N
    N --> E
    N --> F
    N --> G
    R[ESD Push Button HH Level HH Pressure Fire] --> S[SDV Shutdown Valve Closes less than 3 seconds]
    S --> B
```

### 4.2 Three-Phase Test Separator — Internal Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    InletStream[Well Stream Inlet Nozzle] --> Diverter[Inlet Diverter Splash Plate Primary Gas Liquid Separation]
    Diverter --> GasSection[Gas Section Mist Extractor Vane Pack]
    GasSection --> GasOut[Gas Outlet to PCV]
    Diverter --> LiquidSection[Liquid Section Baffles Weir Plates]
    LiquidSection --> OilTop[Oil Layer Top Weir]
    LiquidSection --> InterfaceZone[Oil Water Interface Region]
    InterfaceZone --> WaterBottom[Water Layer Bottom Weir]
    OilTop --> OilOut[Oil Outlet to LCV]
    WaterBottom --> WaterOut[Water Outlet to LCV2]
    subgraph Instruments
        PT[PT Vessel Pressure]
        TT[TT Vessel Temperature]
        LT1[LT Oil Level]
        LT2[IT Interface Level Oil Water]
    end
    Instruments --> DCS[Distributed Control System DCS]
```

### 4.3 Control Loop Strategy — Pressure, Level, and Interface

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP1[Pressure Set Point psig] --> PIC[Pressure Indicating Controller PIC]
    PT[Pressure Transmitter on Vessel Top] --> PIC
    PIC --> PCV[Pressure Control Valve on Gas Outlet]
    PCV --> Vessel[(Test Separator Vessel)]

    SP2[Oil Level Set Point percent] --> LIC[Level Indicating Controller LIC]
    LT1[Oil Level Transmitter Differential Pressure Type] --> LIC
    LIC --> LCV[Level Control Valve on Oil Outlet]
    LCV --> Vessel

    SP3[Interface Set Point percent] --> ILC[Interface Level Controller ILC]
    LT2[Interface Level Transmitter Guided Wave Radar] --> ILC
    ILC --> LCV2[Interface Control Valve on Water Outlet]
    LCV2 --> Vessel
```

### 4.4 ESD Logic — Trip Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HH1[HH High High Pressure greater than 200 psig] --> OR1{OR Logic}
    HH2[HH High High Oil Level greater than 90 percent] --> OR1
    HH3[HH High High Interface Level] --> OR1
    F[Fire Signal Flame Detector] --> OR1
    OR1 --> ESD[ESD Controller Logic Solver]
    ESD --> SDV1[SDV1 Inlet Shutdown Valve Close]
    ESD --> SDV2[SDV2 Gas Outlet Shutdown Valve Close]
    ESD --> BDV[Blowdown Valve Open to Flare]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Diagram Tip**: Always label your set-point values, transmitter types, and final-control-element names. Examiners reward **complete ISA-standard P&ID symbols** with full tag letters.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Similar Model]**

**Q1. Define a Test Separator. List any four instruments mounted on it.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**
A test separator is a pressure vessel used during well-testing to separate the incoming well stream into measurable oil, gas, and water phases under controlled pressure and temperature.

Four instruments mounted on it:
1. Pressure Transmitter (PT) on vessel top
2. Level Transmitter (LT) on oil leg
3. Interface Level Transmitter (IT) on water leg
4. Temperature Transmitter (TT) on vessel body
5. (Optional) Sight glass, sampling port, relief valve

> [!NOTE]
> For 3 marks, naming **4 distinct instruments** with full ISA tag notation is sufficient.

---

**Q2. Define Productivity Index (J). State its SI unit.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**
Productivity Index is defined as the oil flow rate produced per unit pressure drawdown between the average reservoir pressure and the flowing bottomhole pressure.

$$
J = \dfrac{q_o}{\bar{p}_r - p_{wf}}
$$

**Unit:** STB/D/psi (field unit) or m³/d/kPa (SI unit).

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU End Semester Examination – Model Paper Pattern, Module 4 Choice]**

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**Q (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a three-phase test separator. List the four critical control loops and explain any **two** in detail. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

A three-phase test separator receives a multi-phase well stream through an inlet diverter that performs primary gas–liquid separation. Gas rises to the top, passes through a mist extractor, and exits through the gas outlet. The liquid section contains oil and water, which separate by gravity. Oil overflows the top weir; water exits through the bottom weir. An interface-level transmitter maintains the oil–water boundary at a fixed elevation.

**Four Critical Control Loops:**
1. **Pressure Control Loop (PCL)**
2. **Oil-Level Control Loop (LCL)**
3. **Interface Control Loop (ICL)**
4. **Temperature Monitoring Loop**

**Detailed explanation of two loops:**

**(i) Pressure Control Loop (PCL):**
- Transmitter: PT mounted on the top of the vessel.
- Controller: PIC receives signal (4–20 mA) and compares to set point.
- Final Control Element: PCV on the gas outlet line.
- Function: Maintains vessel pressure at safe operating pressure (typically 50–150 psig). When upstream pressure rises, PIC closes PCV slightly to vent less gas.

**(ii) Interface Control Loop (ICL):**
- Transmitter: IT (guided-wave radar or differential-pressure type).
- Controller: ILC.
- Final Control Element: LCV2 on the water outlet line.
- Function: Maintains the oil–water interface at a fixed elevation between the two weirs. If interface rises → water carryover into oil → ILC opens LCV2 to remove more water.

---

**Q (b)** During a well test, the following data were obtained from a test separator:
- Oil flow rate = **2500 STB/D**
- Water flow rate = **500 STB/D**
- Gas flow rate = **1.75 MMSCFD**
- Average reservoir pressure $\bar{p}_r = 3000 \;\text{psi}$
- Flowing bottomhole pressure $p_{wf} = 2500 \;\text{psi}$

Calculate:
1. Water cut (WC)
2. Gas-oil ratio (GOR)
3. Productivity Index (J) `[7 Marks]`

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Water Cut:**

$$
WC = \dfrac{q_w}{q_w + q_o} \times 100 = \dfrac{500}{500 + 2500} \times 100
$$

$$
WC = \dfrac{500}{3000} \times 100 = 16.67\%
$$

**Step 2 — Gas-Oil Ratio:**

$$
q_g = 1.75 \;\text{MMSCFD} = 1{,}750{,}000 \;\text{SCF/D}
$$

$$
GOR = \dfrac{q_g}{q_o} = \dfrac{1{,}750{,}000}{2500}
$$

$$
GOR = 700 \;\text{SCF/STB}
$$

**Step 3 — Productivity Index:**

$$
\Delta p = \bar{p}_r - p_{wf} = 3000 - 2500 = 500 \;\text{psi}
$$

$$
J = \dfrac{q_o}{\Delta p} = \dfrac{2500}{500}
$$

$$
J = 5 \;\text{STB/D/psi}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake #1**: Students forget to convert MMSCFD to SCF/D (multiply by 10⁶). This single error loses 3 marks.
> - **Common Mistake #2**: Confusing the Productivity Index formula with the **flow rate** itself. Always write $J = q_o / \Delta p$, not $J = q_o$.
> - **Common Mistake #3**: Omitting the units in the final answer. KTU examiners award **0.5 marks** for writing units explicitly.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 Model]**

**Q (a)** Explain the purpose of well testing. Describe any **four** types of well tests used in the oil and gas industry. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

**Purpose of Well Testing:**
Well testing is performed to obtain quantitative data on the reservoir's ability to deliver fluids to the wellbore and to characterize the produced fluids. Specific purposes include:
1. Determining reservoir pressure, permeability, and skin factor.
2. Measuring individual phase flow rates (oil, gas, water).
3. Computing Productivity Index (J) and GOR.
4. Identifying reservoir boundaries (faults, fluid contacts).
5. Sampling fluids for PVT analysis in the laboratory.

**Four Types of Well Tests:**

1. **Drill Stem Test (DST):** Conducted immediately after drilling to evaluate the productivity of a formation before completion. Uses a downhole test tool with packers and a downhole shut-in valve.

2. **Production (Flow) Test:** A long-duration test (typically 24 to 72 hours) at multiple flow rates to obtain stabilized deliverability. Uses the **four-point flow-after-flow test** or **modified isochronal test**.

3. **Pressure Build-Up Test:** Well is produced at constant rate, then shut in at the surface. The bottomhole pressure recovery is recorded and analyzed (using **Horner plot**) to determine $\bar{p}_r$ and skin factor $s$.

4. **Pressure Draw-Down Test:** Well is opened at constant rate from a shut-in condition; the declining bottomhole pressure is recorded.

5. **Wireline Formation Test (WFT):** In-situ downhole fluid sampling and mini-DST performed with a wireline-conveyed probe (e.g., MDT, RFT).

---

**Q (b)** A horizontal three-phase test separator is to be sized for the following service:
- Liquid flow rate $q_{liq} = 5000 \;\text{bbl/D}$
- Required liquid retention time $t_{res} = 3 \;\text{min}$
- Length-to-diameter ratio $L/D = 3$

Calculate:
1. Required vessel volume
2. Vessel diameter and length
3. Comment on whether the sizing is adequate if the actual flow surges to 8000 bbl/D for 2 hours per day. `[7 Marks]`

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Required Vessel Volume:**

Convert flow rate to bbl/min:
$$
q_{liq} = \dfrac{5000 \;\text{bbl/D}}{1440 \;\text{min/D}} = 3.472 \;\text{bbl/min}
$$

Required vessel volume:
$$
V_{vessel} = q_{liq} \times t_{res} = 3.472 \times 3 = 10.42 \;\text{bbl}
$$

Convert to ft³ (since $1 \;\text{bbl} = 5.615 \;\text{ft}^3$):
$$
V_{vessel} = 10.42 \times 5.615 = 58.51 \;\text{ft}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Vessel Diameter and Length:**

For a horizontal cylinder, $V = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot L$

Using $L = 3D$:
$$
V = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot 3D = \dfrac{3\pi D^3}{4}
$$

Solving for D:
$$
D^3 = \dfrac{4V}{3\pi} = \dfrac{4 \times 58.51}{3 \times 3.1416} = \dfrac{234.04}{9.4248}
$$

$$
D^3 = 24.83 \;\text{ft}^3 \Rightarrow D = 2.92 \;\text{ft} \approx 35 \;\text{in}
$$

$$
L = 3D = 8.76 \;\text{ft} \approx 105 \;\text{in}
$$

**Step 3 — Surge Comment:**

At surge flow of 8000 bbl/D:
$$
q_{surge} = \dfrac{8000}{1440} = 5.556 \;\text{bbl/min}
$$

Required vessel volume at surge:
$$
V_{surge} = 5.556 \times 3 = 16.67 \;\text{bbl} = 93.6 \;\text{ft}^3
$$

Since $V_{surge} = 93.6 \;\text{ft}^3 > V_{vessel} = 58.51 \;\text{ft}^3$, the vessel is **undersized during surge** by approximately **60%**. Recommendations:
- (i) Increase vessel diameter to ~3.5 ft and length to ~10.5 ft, OR
- (ii) Install a **surge tank** upstream of the test separator, OR
- (iii) Reduce retention time set point to 1.8 min during surge (compromising water-droplet separation).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake #1**: Students often forget to convert bbl/D to bbl/min. This is a **1-mark deduction** every time.
> - **Common Mistake #2**: Students use the wrong formula for cylinder volume. Always state $V = (\pi D^2 / 4) \cdot L$ and substitute $L = 3D$ explicitly.
> - **Common Mistake #3**: Students forget to comment on the surge condition. The comment is worth **2 marks** by itself — don't skip it!

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Exam Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Test Separator Definition**: Pressure vessel that separates well stream into **oil + gas + water** under controlled P, T.
- ✅ **Primary Instruments**: PT (pressure), TT (temperature), LT (oil level), IT (interface), FT (flow on each outlet).
- ✅ **Four Critical Loops**: PCL, LCL, ICL, and temperature monitoring.
- ✅ **ESD Response Time**: Must close inlet SDV within **≤ 3 seconds**.
- ✅ **Productivity Index Formula**: $J = q_o / (\bar{p}_r - p_{wf})$ — units: **STB/D/psi**.
- ✅ **GOR Formula**: $q_g / q_o$ — units: **SCF/STB**.
- ✅ **Water Cut Formula**: $q_w / (q_w + q_o) \times 100$ — units: **%**.
- ✅ **Stokes' Law**: $v_s = g \cdot d^2 \cdot \Delta \rho / (18 \mu)$ — remember the **18 μ** denominator, not 18ρ.
- ✅ **Vessel Sizing**: $V_{vessel} = q_{liq} \times t_{res}$; $L/D = 3$ for horizontal; $L/D = 2$ for vertical.
- ✅ **ISA Tag Letters**: PT, TT, LT, IT, FT, PIC, LIC, ILC, PCV, LCV.
- ✅ **Blowdown**: During ESD, depressurize vessel to flare in **≤ 15 minutes** (API 521 standard).
- ✅ **Mist Extractor**: Removes droplets **≥ 10 µm** from gas phase.
- ✅ **Sand Probe (SP)**: Mounted upstream of separator to detect sand erosion.
- ✅ **Common KTU Pitfall**: Never confuse **standard conditions** (60°F, 14.7 psia) with **operating conditions**.
- ✅ **High-yield diagram for exam**: Always draw a three-phase test separator with **two weir plates**, **inlet diverter**, and **mist extractor**, labeled with **ISA tags**.

---

**End of Module 4 Notes — Test Separators and Well Test**

*Prepared in accordance with the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned), Outcome-Based Education framework, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive levels.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: First Stage Separator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT864 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN LARGE SCALE INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** First Stage Separator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **First Stage Separator (FSS)** is a primary pressure vessel installed immediately downstream of the wellhead choke in a crude oil production train. It performs the **bulk separation** of the multi-phase well stream (oil, gas, free water, and entrained solids) into two or three distinct phases by exploiting density differences under elevated pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A first stage separator is a high-pressure, two-phase or three-phase pressure vessel that receives the total well stream from the wellhead and performs the first major phase split — separating free gas from the liquid (oil + water) — at substantially wellhead pressure and at the operating temperature of the flowing stream.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Oil & Vinegar Bottle" Intuition

Imagine a freshly shaken bottle of salad dressing (oil + vinegar) right after the salad. If you let it rest on the table, the heavier vinegar slowly settles to the bottom, while the lighter oil floats to the top, and a thin layer of bubbles escapes upward. A **First Stage Separator does exactly this** — but at industrial scale, at high pressure, and in seconds rather than minutes.

- The **bottle** = separator shell (vessel)
- The **salad dressing** = well stream (oil + gas + water)
- The **table** = gravity field (~**9.81 m/s²**)
- The **settling time** = residence time (typically **1–3 minutes**)

> [!NOTE]
> The first stage operates at the **highest pressure** in the separation train because reducing pressure earlier would cause excessive gas flashing, which would:
> 1. Waste useful well pressure energy (which is later used to drive the sales gas pipeline).
> 2. Cause artificial cooling (Joule–Thomson effect), which can form hydrates — a major operational hazard.

### 1.3 Position in the Process Train

```
[Wellhead] → [Choke] → [1st Stage Sep] → [2nd Stage Sep] → [3rd Stage Sep] → [Stock Tank]
              (P↓)        (High P)          (Medium P)         (Low P)           (Atm)
                            ↑
                       (THIS TOPIC)
```

### 1.4 Why It Must Be the *First* Stage?

| Reason | Engineering Justification |
|---|---|
| Maximum gas recovery | Captures dissolved gas at high pressure for sale |
| Energy conservation | Uses well's natural pressure to drive downstream flow |
| Stabilization | Stabilizes flow before subsequent stages |
| Heat integration | Avoids re-heating that would be needed if flashed prematurely |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Phases Inside the Vessel

A typical 3-phase horizontal first stage separator performs four sequential operations inside a single shell:

1. **Inlet Diverter / Cyclonic Inlet Device (CID):** Causes an abrupt change in direction → primary separation of bulk gas from liquid by centrifugal force and momentum change. This is called **Primary Separation (Stage 1)**.
2. **Gravity Settling Section (Coalescing Zone):** Liquid moves horizontally across a calm settling section. Gas migrates upward (vapor space), free water settles downward. This is **Secondary Separation (Stage 2)**.
3. **Mist Extractor / Coalescer:** Tiny liquid droplets (typically **> 10 μm**) entrained in the gas are captured by a wire-mesh or vane pack. This is **Tertiary Separation (Stage 3)**.
4. **Liquid-Liquid Coalescer (Weir & Boot):** Oil and water are separated by gravity; clean oil overflows an oil weir; water is drained from a water boot.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **KTU 2024 module** specifically emphasizes the **mechanism of phase disengagement** — students must know the role of gravity, droplet size, residence time, and density difference.

### 2.2 Governing Physics — Gravity Settling

A liquid droplet falling (or rising) through a continuous fluid is governed by **Stokes' Law** (laminar regime, Re < 1):

$$ v_t = \frac{g \, d_p^2 \, (\rho_L - \rho_G)}{18 \, \mu_G} $$

Where:
- $v_t$ = terminal settling velocity of droplet (m/s)
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration (**9.81 m/s²**)
- $d_p$ = droplet diameter (m)
- $\rho_L$ = density of liquid droplet (kg/m³)
- $\rho_G$ = density of gas/vapor phase (kg/m³)
- $\mu_G$ = dynamic viscosity of gas (Pa·s)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** The key insight from Stokes' law is the **squared dependence on droplet diameter** — doubling the droplet size **quadruples** the settling velocity. This is why inlet devices and mist extractors focus on **coalescence** (growing small droplets into big droplets) rather than on chemical change.

### 2.3 Souders–Brown Equation (Vessel Sizing)

The **maximum allowable superficial gas velocity** is governed by the Souders–Brown correlation, the industry-standard equation for separator sizing:

$$ v_G = K \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho_L - \rho_G}{\rho_G}} $$

Where:
- $v_G$ = maximum allowable superficial gas velocity (m/s)
- $K$ = Souders–Brown coefficient (m/s) — depends on pressure, separator type, and separation efficiency
- $\rho_L, \rho_G$ = liquid and gas densities (kg/m³)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **K-factor** for a **first stage (high-pressure) separator** is typically **0.107 m/s (≈ 0.35 ft/s)**, whereas a second-stage separator uses **K = 0.076 m/s**, and a third-stage uses **K = 0.061 m/s**. The K-factor *decreases* with lower pressure because gas density drops, making separation harder.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbol | Typical KTU Value | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stokes terminal velocity | $v_t = \frac{g \cdot d_p^2 (\rho_L - \rho_G)}{18 \mu_G}$ | — | Droplet settling |
| 2 | Souders–Brown gas velocity | $v_G = K \sqrt{(\rho_L - \rho_G)/\rho_G}$ | $K = 0.107$ m/s (1st stage) | Vessel diameter sizing |
| 3 | Reynolds number (settling) | $Re = \rho_G v_t d_p / \mu_G$ | Re < 1 (laminar) | Validates Stokes regime |
| 4 | Retention time for oil | $t_{oil}$ | **1–3 min** | Liquid section length |
| 5 | Retention time for water | $t_{water}$ | **3–5 min** (if 3-phase) | Water boot sizing |
| 6 | Gas retention time | $t_{gas}$ | **0.5–1 min** | Vapor space volume |
| 7 | Separator pressure | $P$ | **30–100 bar** (offshore) | First stage |
| 8 | Operating temperature | $T$ | **60–90 °C** | Same as wellhead |
| 9 | API gravity of crude | $°API$ | **30–40** | Standard metric |

> [!NOTE]
> Always use the **continuous phase** (gas for primary separation) in the denominator of viscosity and density terms. A common KTU mistake is to use liquid viscosity, which gives a settling velocity 1000× too high.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Offshore platforms:** First stage separators are critical because real estate is limited; the FSS is sized for **maximum** reservoir deliverability.
- **Gas processing plants:** The FSS is the de-facto **slug catcher** when the well stream is slug-prone.
- **Refineries (crude unit):** A FSS is the **desalter** in crude distillation, where brine is removed.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Stokes' Terminal Velocity (Falling Droplet Model)

Consider a small liquid droplet of diameter $d_p$ falling under gravity through a quiescent gas. A force balance on the droplet gives:

**Step 1 — Identify the three forces acting on the droplet**

$$ \sum F = F_g - F_b - F_d = m \cdot \frac{dv}{dt} $$

Where:
- $F_g$ = gravitational force (downward)
- $F_b$ = buoyant force (upward)
- $F_d$ = drag force (upward, opposes motion)

**Step 2 — Express each force in terms of measurable quantities**

The gravitational force:
$$ F_g = m \cdot g = \rho_L \cdot V_p \cdot g = \rho_L \cdot \frac{\pi d_p^3}{6} \cdot g $$

The buoyant force (Archimedes):
$$ F_b = \rho_G \cdot V_p \cdot g = \rho_G \cdot \frac{\pi d_p^3}{6} \cdot g $$

The viscous drag force (Stokes drag, valid for Re < 1):
$$ F_d = 3 \pi \mu_G d_p v $$

**Step 3 — Apply the steady-state (terminal) condition**

At terminal velocity, the droplet no longer accelerates, so $\frac{dv}{dt} = 0$. Thus:

$$ F_g - F_b = F_d $$

**Step 4 — Substitute the expressions**

$$ \rho_L \cdot \frac{\pi d_p^3}{6} \cdot g - \rho_G \cdot \frac{\pi d_p^3}{6} \cdot g = 3 \pi \mu_G d_p v_t $$

**Step 5 — Factor common terms**

$$ \frac{\pi d_p^3 g}{6} \cdot (\rho_L - \rho_G) = 3 \pi \mu_G d_p v_t $$

**Step 6 — Solve for $v_t$**

$$ v_t = \frac{\pi d_p^3 g (\rho_L - \rho_G)}{6 \cdot 3 \pi \mu_G d_p} = \frac{d_p^2 g (\rho_L - \rho_G)}{18 \mu_G} $$

**Final Result:**

$$ v_t = \frac{g \cdot d_p^2 (\rho_L - \rho_G)}{18 \mu_G} \quad \text{(m/s)} $$

This is the **Stokes' terminal velocity** used in KTU problems on first-stage separator design.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Vessel Diameter from Souders–Brown Equation

The superficial gas velocity in a vertical separator must not exceed the Souders–Brown velocity. The volumetric gas flow is related to the vessel cross-section as:

$$ v_G = \frac{Q_G}{A} = \frac{4 Q_G}{\pi D^2} $$

Where $Q_G$ is the gas volumetric flow (m³/s) and $D$ is the vessel diameter (m).

**Step 1 — Rearrange for D**

$$ D = \sqrt{\frac{4 Q_G}{\pi v_G}} $$

**Step 2 — Substitute Souders–Brown**

$$ D = \sqrt{\frac{4 Q_G}{\pi K \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L - \rho_G}{\rho_G}}}} $$

**Step 3 — Compute numerically for a typical KTU problem**

Given:
- $Q_G = 0.5 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$ (gas flow at operating conditions)
- $K = 0.107 \, \text{m/s}$ (first-stage K-factor)
- $\rho_L = 850 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ (oil density)
- $\rho_G = 40 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ (gas density at 50 bar, 70 °C)

**Step 4 — Compute the density term**

$$ \frac{\rho_L - \rho_G}{\rho_G} = \frac{850 - 40}{40} = 20.25 $$

**Step 5 — Take the square root**

$$ \sqrt{20.25} = 4.5 $$

**Step 6 — Compute the Souders–Brown velocity**

$$ v_G = 0.107 \times 4.5 = 0.4815 \, \text{m/s} $$

**Step 7 — Compute the diameter**

$$ D = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.5}{\pi \times 0.4815}} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{1.5125}} = \sqrt{1.3223} = 1.150 \, \text{m} $$

**Step 8 — Apply the design safety factor (typical 1.2)**

$$ D_{design} = 1.2 \times 1.15 = 1.38 \, \text{m} \approx 1.4 \, \text{m} $$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Vertical vs Horizontal FSS

| Parameter | Vertical FSS | Horizontal FSS (3-Phase) | KTU Preference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bulk separation efficiency | High (long fall path) | Moderate (sluggish for large droplets) | Vertical for gas-heavy |
| Liquid–liquid separation | Poor (no calm settling) | Excellent (long settling length) | Horizontal for 3-phase |
| Slug handling | Poor (level swings) | Excellent (long vessel damps slugs) | Horizontal for slug-prone |
| Footprint | Small diameter, tall | Wide diameter, short | Horizontal offshore |
| Foamy crude performance | Moderate | Better (longer residence) | Horizontal for foamy |
| K-factor (Souders–Brown) | 0.107 m/s | 0.107 m/s (vertical eq.) | Same physics |
| Typical KTU exam use | 2-phase only | 3-phase (oil + water + gas) | Both asked |

> [!NOTE]
> For **offshore platforms**, horizontal separators dominate because they handle **slugs** better. For **onshore gas plants**, vertical separators dominate because the well stream is gas-rich.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a First Stage Separator

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **functional flow** of fluids, instruments, and control loops inside a typical 3-phase horizontal first stage separator. This is the KTU-recommended diagrammatic form.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inletA[Well Stream Inlet] --> diverterA[Cyclonic Inlet Diverter]
    diverterA --> vaporSpaceA[Vapor Space Bulk Separation]
    vaporSpaceA --> mistExtractA[Mist Extractor Wire Mesh Pack]
    mistExtractA --> gasOutletA[Gas Outlet to Scrubber]
    diverterA --> liqSectionA[Liquid Settling Section]
    liqSectionA --> oilWeirA[Oil Weir Overflow]
    liqSectionA --> waterBootA[Water Boot]
    oilWeirA --> oilOutletA[Oil Outlet to 2nd Stage]
    waterBootA --> waterOutletA[Water Outlet to Treatment]
    gasOutletA --> gasPIDC[Gas Outlet PCV Control Valve]
    oilOutletA --> oilLIC[Oil Level LIC Control Valve]
    waterOutletA --> waterLIC[Water Level LIC Interface]
    liqSectionA --> pressureTapA[Pressure Transmitter PT]
    mistExtractA --> temperatureTapA[Temperature Transmitter TT]
    inletA --> safetyReliefA[Pressure Safety Valve PSV]
```

### 4.2 Detailed Three-Phase Horizontal Separator — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Wellhead Choke] -->|Multiphase Flow| B[Inlet Diverter Device]
    B -->|Primary Gas Separation| C[Vapor Space]
    B -->|Liquid Slug| D[Settling Section]
    C -->|Demisted Gas| E[Mist Extractor]
    E -->|Sales Gas| F[Gas Outlet]
    D -->|Oil + Water| G[Oil Weir]
    D -->|Free Water| H[Water Boot]
    G -->|Stabilized Oil| I[Oil Outlet LC]
    H -->|Produced Water| J[Water Outlet LC]
    subgraph Control_Loops
        K[Pressure Controller PC] --> F
        L[Oil Level Controller LC] --> I
        M[Water Level Controller LC] --> J
    end
    subgraph Safety_Instruments
        N[PSV Pressure Safety Valve]
        O[LSL Low Level Switch]
        P[LSH High Level Switch]
        Q[PG Pressure Gauge]
    end
    F -.Pressure Feedback.-> K
    I -.Level Feedback.-> L
    J -.Level Feedback.-> M
```

### 4.3 Instrumentation & Control Loops — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PT1[PT Pressure Transmitter] -->|4-20 mA| PIC1[PIC Pressure Indicator Controller]
    PIC1 -->|Setpoint 50 bar| PCV1[PCV Pressure Control Valve on Gas Outlet]
    LT1[LT Level Transmitter Oil] -->|4-20 mA| LIC1[LIC Level Indicator Controller]
    LIC1 -->|Setpoint 50 percent| LCV1[LCV Level Control Valve on Oil Outlet]
    LT2[LT Level Transmitter Water Boot] -->|4-20 mA| LIC2[LIC Water Level Controller]
    LIC2 -->|Setpoint 50 percent| LCV2[LCV Water Dump Valve]
    TT1[TT Temperature Transmitter] -->|4-20 mA| TIC1[TIC Temperature Indicator Controller]
    TIC1 -->|Signal| HE[Heat Exchanger Trim Heater]
    FT1[FT Flow Transmitter Gas] -->|4-20 mA| FI1[FI Flow Indicator]
    FT2[FT Flow Transmitter Oil] -->|4-20 mA| FI2[FI Flow Indicator]
    LSH1[LSH High Level Alarm] -->|Trip| SD[Shutdown System ESD]
    PSV1[PSV Pressure Safety Valve] -->|Mechanical| VENT[Atmosphere Vent]
```

### 4.4 Key Components — Quick-Reference Schematic Table

| Symbol | Component | Function in FSS | KTU Exam Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| CID | Cyclonic Inlet Diverter | Primary bulk separation | High-weightage |
| WP | Wire-Mesh Mist Extractor | Coalesce fine droplets | High-weightage |
| OW | Oil Weir | Oil–water interface control | High-weightage |
| WB | Water Boot | Water collection & dump | Moderate |
| SS | Sand Jet / Sand Wash | Solid removal | Moderate |
| PSV | Pressure Safety Valve | Overpressure protection | Mandatory |
| LG | Level Glass | Local level indication | Mandatory |
| PI | Pressure Indicator | Local pressure | Mandatory |
| TI | Temperature Indicator | Local temperature | Mandatory |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **KTU 2024 scheme** diagrams, students are expected to use **ISA-standard symbols** (circles for instruments, lines for signals, dashed for pneumatic, solid for electronic). Mermaid approximates this; the actual exam requires hand-drawing the **P&ID** (Piping and Instrumentation Diagram).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a First Stage Separator. State its function in a crude oil production train.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **First Stage Separator (FSS)** is a pressure vessel installed immediately downstream of the wellhead choke valve, designed to perform the **primary bulk separation** of the multi-phase well stream (crude oil, free gas, free water, and sand) into two or three distinct phases.

**Functions:**
1. **Primary phase split** — separates free gas from the liquid hydrocarbon stream at the highest available pressure (1 mark).
2. **Pressure stabilization** — provides a stable operating pressure to the downstream low-pressure separation train (1 mark).
3. **Bulk water removal** — removes free (not emulsified) water from the crude to protect downstream equipment (1 mark).

> **Marking key:** Definition (1M) + two distinct functions (1M each).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Why is the first stage separator operated at high pressure? What happens if the pressure is set too low?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The first stage separator is operated at **high pressure (typically 30–100 bar)** to:

1. **Maximize gas recovery** — Captures dissolved methane and ethane at high pressure, increasing sales-gas revenue (1 mark).
2. **Conserve pressure energy** — The well's natural pressure is used to drive flow through downstream stages, eliminating intermediate pumps (1 mark).
3. **Avoid Joule–Thomson cooling** — Premature pressure reduction causes sharp temperature drops, leading to **gas hydrate formation** (e.g., methane hydrates at ~7 °C), which plug pipelines (1 mark).

> **Marking key:** Three reasons × 1 mark each.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024]*

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working of a 3-phase horizontal first stage separator**. Highlight the role of each internal component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A first-stage separator handles a gas flow rate of $0.6 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$ at operating conditions. The oil density is $860 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ and the gas density is $45 \, \text{kg/m}^3$. Using the Souders–Brown coefficient $K = 0.107 \, \text{m/s}$, determine the **minimum vessel diameter**. Apply a safety factor of **1.2** to your result. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

A 3-phase horizontal first stage separator is a horizontal cylindrical pressure vessel (typically **L/D = 2.5 to 4**) with two dished heads.

**Key internal components and their roles (each 1 mark):**

1. **Inlet Diverter (Cyclonic Inlet Device):** A tangential or deflector-type inlet that forces an abrupt 180° change in direction, exploiting centrifugal force to throw bulk liquid down and let gas rise. *(1 mark)*
2. **Vapor Space:** The upper part of the vessel where disengaged gas collects. The interface level is controlled by a level transmitter (LT) on the oil outlet. *(1 mark)*
3. **Mist Extractor (Wire Mesh Pad or Vane Pack):** A coalescing element near the gas outlet that captures droplets typically **> 10 μm** to prevent liquid carry-over in the gas. *(1 mark)*
4. **Liquid Settling Section:** A calm horizontal section where oil and water separate by gravity. Water (denser) settles down, oil overflows the weir. *(1 mark)*
5. **Oil Weir:** A fixed-height overflow weir that maintains the oil–water interface at a controlled level. *(1 mark)*
6. **Water Boot:** A small-diameter extension at the bottom of the vessel that collects water and dumps it via a level-controlled valve. *(1 mark)*
7. **Safety and Instrument Devices:** PSV, level glasses, pressure gauges, and shutdown level switches (LSHH, LSLL). *(1 mark)*

> **Sketch:** Show horizontal cylinder, two heads, inlet diverter at one end, mist extractor near gas outlet, oil weir in the middle, water boot at the bottom, with all ISA instrumentation symbols.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 — Write Souders–Brown equation (1 mark)**

$$ v_G = K \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho_L - \rho_G}{\rho_G}} $$

**Step 2 — Substitute the density term (1 mark)**

$$ \frac{\rho_L - \rho_G}{\rho_G} = \frac{860 - 45}{45} = \frac{815}{45} = 18.11 $$

**Step 3 — Take the square root (1 mark)**

$$ \sqrt{18.11} = 4.255 $$

**Step 4 — Compute maximum allowable gas velocity (1 mark)**

$$ v_G = 0.107 \times 4.255 = 0.4553 \, \text{m/s} $$

**Step 5 — Relate gas velocity to vessel cross-section (1 mark)**

$$ v_G = \frac{4 Q_G}{\pi D^2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad D = \sqrt{\frac{4 Q_G}{\pi v_G}} $$

**Step 6 — Substitute numerical values (1 mark)**

$$ D = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.6}{\pi \times 0.4553}} = \sqrt{\frac{2.4}{1.430}} = \sqrt{1.678} = 1.295 \, \text{m} $$

**Step 7 — Apply the design safety factor (1 mark)**

$$ D_{design} = 1.2 \times 1.295 = 1.554 \, \text{m} \approx 1.6 \, \text{m} $$

> **Answer:** The minimum vessel diameter is **1.3 m** and the **design diameter with 1.2 safety factor is 1.6 m**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget to **square-root the density ratio** and end up with a gas velocity 4× too low, which inflates the diameter. Also, do not confuse **mass density (kg/m³)** with **molar density (kmol/m³)** — the Souders–Brown equation strictly uses mass density.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]*

**(a)** Explain the **principle of gravity separation** in a first stage separator. Derive the **Stokes' terminal velocity equation** for a liquid droplet settling in a gas. State the conditions under which Stokes' law is valid. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A first-stage separator handles an oil flow of $5000 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day}$ with a **residence time of 2 minutes**. The vessel has an **L/D ratio of 3** and the oil occupies **50%** of the vessel volume. Calculate the **diameter and length** of the separator. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

**Principle of gravity separation (2 marks):**

Gravity separation exploits the **density difference** between two immiscible phases. The denser phase (e.g., water) settles downward, the lighter phase (e.g., oil) rises, and a clean interface is established. The separation rate depends on (i) density difference, (ii) droplet size, and (iii) viscosity of the continuous phase.

**Stokes' derivation (4 marks):** *(See Section 3.1 of these notes for the full derivation.)*

Force balance: $F_g - F_b = F_d$

$$ \rho_L \cdot \frac{\pi d_p^3}{6} g - \rho_G \cdot \frac{\pi d_p^3}{6} g = 3 \pi \mu_G d_p v_t $$

Solving:

$$ v_t = \frac{d_p^2 g (\rho_L - \rho_G)}{18 \mu_G} $$

**Validity conditions (1 mark):**
- **Reynolds number Re < 1** (laminar flow around droplet).
- **Spherical, rigid, non-interacting droplets** (i.e., low droplet concentration, typically < 1% by volume).
- **No slip at the droplet surface** (continuous fluid viscosity dominates).

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 — Convert oil flow to m³/s (1 mark)**

$$ Q_L = \frac{5000}{24 \times 3600} = \frac{5000}{86400} = 0.05787 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s} $$

**Step 2 — Relate residence time to vessel liquid volume (1 mark)**

$$ V_{liquid} = Q_L \times t = 0.05787 \times 120 = 6.944 \, \text{m}^3 $$

**Step 3 — Total vessel volume (50% fill) (1 mark)**

$$ V_{vessel} = \frac{V_{liquid}}{0.5} = \frac{6.944}{0.5} = 13.888 \, \text{m}^3 $$

**Step 4 — Apply L/D = 3 (1 mark)**

$$ V_{vessel} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \times L = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \times 3D = \frac{3\pi}{4} D^3 $$

**Step 5 — Solve for D (1 mark)**

$$ D^3 = \frac{4 \times 13.888}{3 \pi} = \frac{55.55}{9.425} = 5.894 $$

$$ D = \sqrt[3]{5.894} = 1.804 \, \text{m} \approx 1.8 \, \text{m} $$

**Step 6 — Compute L (1 mark)**

$$ L = 3D = 3 \times 1.804 = 5.412 \, \text{m} \approx 5.4 \, \text{m} $$

**Step 7 — Verify liquid volume (1 mark)**

$$ V_{liquid} = 0.5 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times 1.8^2 \times 5.4 = 0.5 \times 2.5447 \times 5.4 = 6.87 \, \text{m}^3 \approx 6.94 \, \text{m}^3 \, \checkmark $$

> **Answer:** **Diameter D ≈ 1.8 m**, **Length L ≈ 5.4 m**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Many students write "50% fill" but then use the **total vessel volume** as the liquid volume. Always re-read the problem: oil *occupies* 50% — so the **liquid volume is half the vessel volume**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Definition:** FSS = primary bulk phase separator at high pressure, immediately downstream of choke.
- ✅ **Position:** Always *first* in the separation train; operates at the *highest* pressure.
- ✅ **Three internal mechanisms:** **Inlet Diverter → Gravity Settling → Mist Extractor** (in that order).
- ✅ **Stokes' Law:** $v_t = \dfrac{d_p^2 g (\rho_L - \rho_G)}{18 \mu_G}$ — valid for Re < 1.
- ✅ **Souders–Brown:** $v_G = K \sqrt{(\rho_L - \rho_G)/\rho_G}$, with $K \approx 0.107$ m/s for 1st stage.
- ✅ **Vessel sizing:** Diameter from Souders–Brown; length from retention time × L/D ratio.
- ✅ **3-phase vs 2-phase:** 3-phase has oil weir + water boot; 2-phase has only oil outlet.
- ✅ **Vertical vs Horizontal:** Vertical for gas-rich; horizontal for slug-prone / 3-phase.
- ✅ **Critical instruments:** PIC on gas outlet, LIC on oil and water boots, PSV (mandatory), LSHH/LSLL shutdown.
- ✅ **Common KTU errors:** wrong density used (gas instead of liquid, or vice-versa); missing safety factor; forgetting square root in Souders–Brown.
- ✅ **Hydrate warning:** Never let first stage operate below hydrate formation pressure/temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Memory Aid — The "4 Ds" of First Stage Separator:**
> **D**eflect (inlet diverter) → **D**isengage (vapor space) → **D**ry (mist extractor) → **D**ump (water boot).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Second Stage Separator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT864 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN LARGE SCALE INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Second Stage Separator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Second Stage Separator (Stage-2 Separator):** A pressurized process vessel installed immediately downstream of a primary (first stage) separator, whose function is to perform a *secondary phase-separation* on a partially separated multiphase stream. It operates at a **lower pressure** than the first stage, recovering dissolved/entrapped light hydrocarbons (vapor) from the liquid stream and removing entrained liquid droplets from the gas stream, thereby upgrading the product quality and protecting downstream equipment such as compressors, dehydrators, and stabilizers.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context of **PEICT864**, the Second Stage Separator is treated as a representative example of a *multi-stage process unit*, allowing students to study how field instrumentation (level, pressure, temperature, flow) and control loops interact with process dynamics.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Two-Step Coffee Filter:**
> Imagine pouring a freshly brewed mixture of coffee and fine grounds (analogous to a wellhead multiphase stream — oil, water, and natural gas) through a coarse filter. The first filter catches the large grounds (this is the *First Stage Separator*). However, the liquid passing through still looks hazy because of *fine suspended particles* (analogous to small liquid droplets entrained in the gas and dissolved gas in the oil). When this hazy liquid is poured through a finer, slower filter, the result is crystal-clear coffee (the *Second Stage Separator* output). The two-stage approach is more efficient and produces a cleaner, more usable final product than a single aggressive filter could.

### Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard values are conventionally used in separator design and must be memorized for KTU evaluation:

- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$
- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P_{\text{atm}} = 101.325 \; \text{kPa}$
- **Standard temperature:** $T_{\text{std}} = 15.56 \; ^{\circ}\text{C} = 288.71 \; \text{K}$
- **Air density at STP:** $\rho_{\text{air}} = 1.225 \; \text{kg/m}^3$
- **Water density at 4 °C:** $\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3$
- **Minimum gas–liquid separation residence time:** typically **45 s to 3 min** (vessel dependent)
- **Design operating pressure drop (first → second stage):** usually **30–70 %** of the first-stage pressure
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Role in the Process Train

The Second Stage Separator is positioned in the process flow as follows:

1. **Upstream:** Receives the partially separated stream from the first stage. This stream still contains (a) dissolved gases in the liquid phase, and (b) entrained liquid droplets in the gas phase.
2. **Downstream:** Delivers:
   - A **dry, near-pure gas** to a compressor or gas-processing unit.
   - A **degassed liquid** to a stabilizer, treater, or storage tank.

### 2.2 Operating Principles — The "Why" and "How"

The vessel exploits **two physical phenomena simultaneously**:

- **Vapor-Liquid Equilibrium (VLE) shift due to pressure reduction.** When the liquid reaches a lower-pressure vessel, dissolved gases flash out of solution. The "How": Henry's law and Raoult's law govern the quantity of gas released. The "Why": Lower partial pressure of each component in the gas phase allows more molecules to escape the liquid.
- **Gravitational settling of entrained droplets.** A small droplet of liquid in a continuous gas phase falls under gravity but is opposed by drag. The "How": The droplet accelerates until drag balances gravity, reaching a *terminal velocity*. The "Why": Droplets that fall faster than the gas's *upward velocity* are captured; the rest are carried out.

> [!NOTE]
> **Internal Hardware You Will Be Tested On (KTU Module 4):**
> - **Inlet Diverter / Cyclone Inlet Device** — eliminates bulk momentum.
> - **Settling / Disengagement Section** — provides residence time.
> - **Mist Extractor (Vane / Wire-Mesh / Cyclonic)** — captures droplets of 10 µm and above.
> - **Weir / Boot / Coalescer Section** — separates two immiscible liquids (oil/water) in a three-phase separator.
> - **Splash Plate / Deflector** — prevents re-entrainment.

### 2.3 Instrumentation and Control Loops (KTU Core Focus)

The standard instrumentation package on a Second Stage Separator includes:

| Tag | Instrument | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **PT** | Pressure Transmitter | Monitors vessel pressure |
| **PC** | Pressure Controller | Holds back-pressure on gas outlet via PCV |
| **PSV / PSHH** | Pressure Safety Valve / High-High Switch | Overpressure protection |
| **LT** | Level Transmitter | Measures liquid level |
| **LC** | Level Controller | Modulates liquid dump valve |
| **LSH / LSL** | Level Switch High / Low | Alarms & ESD trips |
| **TT** | Temperature Transmitter | Process temperature |
| **FT** | Flow Transmitter | Inlet & both outlet flows |
| **SG** | Sight Glass | Local visual level |
| **XV** | Motor-Operated ESD Valve | Block valves for shutdown |

### 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Control Loop

- **Level Control Loop:** Without it, the liquid would either flood the gas space (causing liquid carryover to the compressor — extremely damaging) or run dry (allowing gas to leak into the liquid dump line, creating a two-phase flow problem downstream). Therefore, **LT → LC → Dump Valve** is a *non-negotiable* regulatory loop.
- **Pressure Control Loop:** Protects vessel integrity, maintains separation efficiency (VLE is pressure-dependent), and provides a stable flow to the downstream gas system. **PT → PC → Back-Pressure Control Valve** on the gas outlet.
- **ESD (Emergency Shutdown) System:** Hardwired, fail-safe, independent of the DCS. Activated by *LSHH* (high-high level) or *PSHH* (high-high pressure). Closes the inlet ESD valve and dumps liquid inventory.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for numerical problems. Note: Vertical bars are rendered using `\vert` to keep markdown table syntax intact.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Stokes' Law** (terminal velocity of a small droplet) | $v_t = \dfrac{g \cdot d^2 \cdot (\rho_L - \rho_G)}{18 \,\mu}$ | m/s | Droplet settling, mist extractor design |
| 2 | **Newton's Law Regime** (large droplets, $Re > 1$) | $v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \, g \, d \, (\rho_L - \rho_G)}{3 \, C_D \, \rho_G}}$ | m/s | Larger droplet capture |
| 3 | **Reynolds Number** for droplet | $Re = \dfrac{\rho_G \, v_t \, d}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | Regime identification |
| 4 | **Residence Time** | $\tau = \dfrac{V_{\text{vessel}}}{Q_{\text{liquid}}}$ | seconds | Vessel sizing |
| 5 | **Superficial Gas Velocity** (limiting) | $v_{s,\text{lim}} = K \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L - \rho_G}{\rho_G}}$ | m/s | Maximum allowable gas velocity (Souders–Brown) |
| 6 | **Souders–Brown K-Factor** selection | $K = 0.07$–$0.15 \; \text{ft/s}$ for wire-mesh | ft/s | Mist-eliminator sizing |
| 7 | **Material Balance (2-phase)** | $F_{\text{in}} = G_{\text{out}} + L_{\text{out}}$ | kg/h or kmol/h | Mass conservation |
| 8 | **Component Mass Balance (i)** | $F \cdot z_i = G \cdot y_i + L \cdot x_i$ | mol/h | Per-component check |
| 9 | **Ideal Gas Law (Vapor Outlet)** | $G = \dfrac{PV}{R T} \cdot M$ | kg/h | Vapor density, mass flow |
| 10 | **Separation Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}}}{C_{\text{in}}} \times 100\%$ | % | Performance assessment |
| 11 | **Vessel Cross-Section Area** | $A = \dfrac{Q_G}{v_{s,\text{lim}}}$ | m² | Diameter calculation |
| 12 | **Interface Level (3-phase)** | $h_{\text{interface}} = f(\Delta P, \rho_{\text{oil}}, \rho_{\text{water}})$ | m | Water–oil interface location |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are used by **process design engineers** to size the vessel diameter and length, by **control engineers** to tune the level/pressure loops, and by **safety engineers** to specify PSV setpoints. A KTU student who internalizes this table can answer nearly any quantitative question on the topic.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Terminal Velocity of a Liquid Droplet (Stokes' Regime)

This is the single most important derivation. It explains **how a mist extractor works** mathematically.

**Starting Assumptions:**
- Spherical droplet of diameter $d$, density $\rho_L$, falling under gravity $g$.
- Continuous gas phase of density $\rho_G$ and dynamic viscosity $\mu$.
- Flow regime: **Laminar** ($Re \leq 1$).

**Step 1 — Identify the three forces acting on the droplet:**

- **Gravity (downward):**
$$F_g = \dfrac{\pi}{6} \, d^3 \, \rho_L \, g$$

- **Buoyancy (upward):**
$$F_b = \dfrac{\pi}{6} \, d^3 \, \rho_G \, g$$

- **Drag (opposes motion; for laminar Stokes flow around a sphere):**
$$F_d = 3 \pi \, \mu \, d \, v_t$$

**Step 2 — Apply steady-state (terminal) condition. Acceleration = 0, so net force = 0:**

$$F_g - F_b - F_d = 0$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the three force expressions:**

$$\dfrac{\pi}{6} \, d^3 \, \rho_L \, g - \dfrac{\pi}{6} \, d^3 \, \rho_G \, g - 3 \pi \, \mu \, d \, v_t = 0$$

**Step 4 — Group the first two terms (effective weight):**

$$\dfrac{\pi}{6} \, d^3 \, (\rho_L - \rho_G) \, g = 3 \pi \, \mu \, d \, v_t$$

**Step 5 — Isolate $v_t$ (terminal velocity):**

$$v_t = \dfrac{ \dfrac{\pi}{6} \, d^3 \, (\rho_L - \rho_G) \, g }{ 3 \pi \, \mu \, d }$$

**Step 6 — Simplify the constants ($\pi/6$ over $3\pi = 1/18$):**

$$v_t = \dfrac{g \, d^2 \, (\rho_L - \rho_G)}{18 \, \mu}$$

This is the famous **Stokes' Law** used universally in separator and mist-extractor design.

**Step 7 — Cross-check with a numerical example (KTU-style):**

Given: $d = 100 \, \mu\text{m} = 1 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}$, $\rho_L = 800 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ (light condensate), $\rho_G = 5 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ (natural gas at 5 bar), $\mu = 1.2 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$, $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$.

$$v_t = \dfrac{9.81 \times (1 \times 10^{-4})^2 \times (800 - 5)}{18 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$v_t = \dfrac{9.81 \times 1 \times 10^{-8} \times 795}{2.16 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$v_t = \dfrac{7.80 \times 10^{-5}}{2.16 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.361 \; \text{m/s}$$

> **Interpretation:** A 100-micron droplet settles at 0.36 m/s. The vessel's upward gas velocity must therefore be **less than 0.36 m/s** for this droplet to be captured. This number drives the vessel diameter.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Vessel Cross-Section from Souders–Brown Correlation

**Step 1 — Define the design gas velocity** using Souders–Brown K-factor:

$$v_{s} = K \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L - \rho_G}{\rho_G}}$$

**Step 2 — Define the volumetric gas flow rate at operating conditions:**

$$Q_G = \dfrac{G \, R \, T}{P \, M}$$

where $G$ is the gas mass flow rate, $R = 8.314 \; \text{J/(mol·K)}$ is the universal gas constant, $T$ is absolute temperature, $P$ is absolute pressure, and $M$ is gas molecular weight.

**Step 3 — Relate $Q_G$ to the vessel cross-section:**

By continuity, the volumetric gas flow must equal the velocity × area:

$$Q_G = v_s \cdot A$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the cross-sectional area $A$:**

$$A = \dfrac{Q_G}{v_s}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the vessel diameter $D$:**

$$A = \dfrac{\pi \, D^2}{4} \;\Rightarrow\; D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 A}{\pi}}$$

**Step 6 — Combine with step 1's expression for $v_s$:**

$$D = \sqrt{ \dfrac{4 Q_G}{\pi \, K} \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_G}{\rho_L - \rho_G}} }$$

This is the **master equation** used in KTU design problems. Note the embedded $\sqrt{\rho_G / (\rho_L - \rho_G)}$ term — a higher-pressure separator (denser gas) requires a *larger* diameter.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Material Balance Across the Second Stage Separator

**Step 1 — Total Mass Balance** (steady state, no accumulation):

$$F_{\text{in}} = G_{\text{out}} + L_{\text{out}}$$

**Step 2 — Component Mass Balance** for component $i$:

$$F_{\text{in}} \cdot z_i = G_{\text{out}} \cdot y_i + L_{\text{out}} \cdot x_i$$

**Step 3 — Express in mole-fraction form** (if mole basis):

$$\sum_i z_i = 1, \quad \sum_i y_i = 1, \quad \sum_i x_i = 1$$

**Step 4 — In a 3-phase separator (gas + oil + water),** an additional water stream $W_{\text{out}}$ appears:

$$F_{\text{in}} = G_{\text{out}} + L_{o,\text{out}} + W_{\text{out}}$$

**Step 5 — Worked numerical example:**

Inlet flow: $F_{\text{in}} = 10\,000 \; \text{kg/h}$ (50 % gas, 50 % liquid by mass).
