Outlet gas: $G_{\text{out}} = 4\,200 \; \text{kg/h}$.
Find outlet liquid:

$$L_{\text{out}} = F_{\text{in}} - G_{\text{out}} = 10\,000 - 4\,200 = 5\,800 \; \text{kg/h}$$

**Step 6 — Cross-check the gas split fraction:**

$$\text{Recovery} = \dfrac{4\,200}{5\,000} \times 100\% = 84\%$$

A *higher* recovery than the first stage confirms the second stage is doing its job.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **"What Concept Solves What Problem"** reference — useful for KTU Part B questions.

| Engineering Problem | Governing Equation | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| Determine droplet removal | Stokes' Law | "Calculate terminal velocity of a 50 µm droplet" |
| Size vessel diameter | Souders–Brown | "Find vessel ID for given flow and K-factor" |
| Check material balance | $F = G + L$ | "Verify mass conservation across vessel" |
| Determine gas density | Ideal gas law | "Calculate $\rho_G$ at operating P and T" |
| Estimate vessel length | $\tau = V/Q$ | "Find vessel height for 60-s residence" |
| Check instrumentation | Loop diagram (P&ID) | "Sketch control loops for level and pressure" |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Second Stage Separator

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **process flow, internal hardware, instrumentation, and control loops** in a single integrated view. Each control loop, alarm, and ESD path is explicitly shown — this is the kind of figure examiners expect in KTU Part B answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inlet Stream from First Stage Separator] --> B[Inlet ESD Valve XV101]
    B --> C[Inlet Diverter SG101]
    C --> D[Inlet Cyclonic Pre-Separator]
    D --> E[Primary Settling Section]
    E --> F[Liquid Collection Section]
    F --> G[Mist Eliminator Wire Mesh]
    G --> H[Gas Outlet Plenum]
    H --> I[Pressure Control Valve PCV201]
    I --> J[Gas Outlet to Compressor or Dehydrator]
    F --> K[Water Boot]
    K --> L[Water Dump Valve LCV202]
    F --> M[Oil Weir]
    M --> N[Liquid Dump Valve LCV201]
    N --> O[Oil Outlet to Stabilizer]

    PT201[Pressure Transmitter PT201] --> PC201[Pressure Controller PC201]
    PC201 --> I
    PSHH201[Pressure High-High Switch PSHH201] --> ESD1[ESD Logic Solver]
    PSV201[Pressure Safety Valve PSV201] --> F

    LT201[Level Transmitter LT201 Oil Level] --> LC201[Level Controller LC201]
    LC201 --> N
    LT202[Level Transmitter LT202 Interface] --> LC202[Interface Controller LC202]
    LC202 --> L
    LSH201[Level Switch High LSH201] --> ESD1
    LSLL201[Level Switch Low Low LSLL201] --> ESD1
    ESD1 --> B
    ESD1 --> I
    ESD1 --> N

    TT201[Temperature Transmitter TT201] --> IND[Indicator on DCS]
    FT201[Flow Transmitter Inlet FT201] --> IND
    FT202[Flow Transmitter Gas Out FT202] --> IND
    FT203[Flow Transmitter Liquid Out FT203] --> IND

    SG201[Sight Glass SG201] -.-> F
```

### 4.2 Reading the Diagram (For Examination)

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram for KTU answers:**
> 1. The **solid arrows** are process flow lines (fluid movement).
> 2. The **dashed arrow** from `SG201` to the vessel is a *signal* (local visual indication).
> 3. The **orange-tinted control lines** show the three regulatory loops: pressure, oil level, and interface level.
> 4. The **red-tinted ESD lines** show the hardwired safety-instrumented system (SIS) — independent of the DCS.
> 5. Every labeled tag is an **ISA-standard instrument designation**. Memorize the pattern: `First letter = measured variable`, `Subsequent letters = function and modifier`.

### 4.3 ISA Tag Decoding Cheat Sheet (Sub-Reference)

| Tag Letters | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **P** | Pressure | PT = Pressure Transmitter |
| **L** | Level | LT = Level Transmitter |
| **T** | Temperature | TT = Temperature Transmitter |
| **F** | Flow | FT = Flow Transmitter |
| **PCV** | Pressure Control Valve | Controls vessel back-pressure |
| **LCV** | Level Control Valve | Modulates dump flow |
| **PSV** | Pressure Safety Valve | Mechanical overpressure protection |
| **XV** | On/Off Solenoid/ESD Valve | Block valves, fail-safe |
| **LSH / LSL** | Level Switch High / Low | Alarm initiation |
| **LSHH / LSLL** | Level Switch High-High / Low-Low | ESD trip initiation |
| **PSHH** | Pressure Switch High-High | ESD trip on overpressure |
| **SG** | Sight Glass | Local visual observation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Drawing a clean P&ID-style block diagram (like the Mermaid above) in a 14-mark answer on the Second Stage Separator is worth **at least 2–3 marks** by itself, as it demonstrates a holistic grasp of process + instrumentation + control.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 3 Mark Questions

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> *Define a Second Stage Separator. Why is it operated at a lower pressure than the first stage?*

**Model Answer:**
A Second Stage Separator is a pressure vessel installed downstream of a primary separator to perform a *secondary phase separation* of a partially separated multiphase stream (typically gas + liquid). Its function is to recover dissolved gases from the liquid phase and to remove entrained liquid droplets from the gas phase.

It is operated at a **lower pressure** than the first stage because, according to **Vapor-Liquid Equilibrium (VLE)** theory, a pressure reduction lowers the partial pressure of each light component, causing additional gas to flash out of the liquid — thereby increasing the overall *gas recovery* and producing a *drier, more stable* liquid product. The lower pressure also reduces the required vessel wall thickness, lowering fabrication cost.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> *List any four internal components of a Second Stage Separator and state the function of each.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Inlet Diverter** — Eliminates the bulk horizontal momentum of the incoming stream and forces an abrupt change in direction, so that the bulk liquid phase separates immediately.
2. **Mist Eliminator (Wire-Mesh / Vane)** — Captures entrained liquid droplets of 10 µm and above from the gas by impingement and coalescence.
3. **Weir Plate** — Maintains a controlled oil–water interface level in a three-phase separator.
4. **Water Boot / Sump** — Collects and separates the heavier water phase from the oil by gravity.

---

### Part B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **Note to student:** In a real KTU End-Semester Examination, you must answer *one* of two choices. Both questions below are constructed to be of equal difficulty and equal mark distribution.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

> *A Second Stage Separator receives 12 000 kg/h of a gas–liquid mixture from the first stage separator. The inlet stream contains 55 % gas and 45 % liquid by mass. The gas outlet flow is 5 800 kg/h. The vessel operates at 4.5 bar(g) and 35 °C. The liquid density is 820 kg/m³, gas density at operating conditions is 6.2 kg/m³, and the gas viscosity is $1.3 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s. A wire-mesh mist extractor is used, designed for droplets of 80 µm diameter.*

### (a) *Determine the liquid outlet flow rate and the percentage of gas recovered in the second stage. Verify the material balance.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Liquid outlet flow:**

$$L_{\text{out}} = F_{\text{in}} - G_{\text{out}} = 12\,000 - 5\,800 = 6\,200 \; \text{kg/h}$$

**Step 2 — Inlet gas mass:**

$$G_{\text{in}} = 0.55 \times 12\,000 = 6\,600 \; \text{kg/h}$$

**Step 3 — Percentage gas recovery in second stage:**

$$\eta = \dfrac{G_{\text{out}}}{G_{\text{in}}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{5\,800}{6\,600} \times 100\% = 87.88\%$$

**Step 4 — Material balance verification:**

$$F_{\text{in}} = G_{\text{out}} + L_{\text{out}} \;\Rightarrow\; 12\,000 = 5\,800 + 6\,200 \;\checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** The second stage recovers ~88 % of the remaining gas, and the mass balance closes exactly.

### (b) *Calculate the terminal settling velocity of an 80 µm droplet using Stokes' Law. Using the Souders–Brown correlation with K = 0.10 ft/s, find the required vessel cross-section and the minimum diameter. (Take 1 ft = 0.3048 m, g = 9.81 m/s²).* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Terminal velocity (Stokes' Law):**

$$v_t = \dfrac{g \, d^2 \, (\rho_L - \rho_G)}{18 \, \mu}$$

Substituting $d = 80 \times 10^{-6}$ m:

$$v_t = \dfrac{9.81 \times (80 \times 10^{-6})^2 \times (820 - 6.2)}{18 \times 1.3 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$v_t = \dfrac{9.81 \times 6.4 \times 10^{-9} \times 813.8}{2.34 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$v_t = \dfrac{5.108 \times 10^{-5}}{2.34 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.2183 \; \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Convert K-factor to SI:**

$$K = 0.10 \; \text{ft/s} \times 0.3048 = 0.03048 \; \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Limiting superficial gas velocity:**

$$v_s = K \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L - \rho_G}{\rho_G}} = 0.03048 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{820 - 6.2}{6.2}}$$

$$v_s = 0.03048 \times \sqrt{131.26} = 0.03048 \times 11.457 = 0.3493 \; \text{m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Volumetric gas flow rate at operating conditions:**

$$Q_G = \dfrac{G_{\text{out}}}{\rho_G} = \dfrac{5\,800 / 3600}{6.2} = \dfrac{1.611}{6.2} = 0.2599 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Required cross-sectional area:**

$$A = \dfrac{Q_G}{v_s} = \dfrac{0.2599}{0.3493} = 0.7440 \; \text{m}^2$$

**Step 6 — Minimum vessel diameter:**

$$D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 A}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 0.7440}{3.1416}} = \sqrt{0.9473} = 0.9733 \; \text{m}$$

**Conclusion:** A vessel of approximately **1.0 m diameter** (i.e., 40 inches nominal) is required.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

> *With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the instrumentation and control scheme of a Second Stage Separator used in an oil and gas processing plant. Identify all primary instruments, the regulatory control loops, the safety-instrumented system, and describe the action of each loop during a high-high liquid level condition.*

### (a) *Sketch the P&ID-style block diagram and identify all field instruments with proper ISA tagging. State the purpose of each.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Diagram:** (Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in SECTION 4 above for the full layout.)

**Key instruments and their purposes:**

| Tag | Instrument | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **PT201** | Pressure Transmitter | Measures vessel internal pressure |
| **PC201 + PCV201** | Pressure Controller & Control Valve | Holds back-pressure to maintain VLE |
| **PSV201** | Pressure Safety Valve | Mechanical relief of overpressure |
| **PSHH201** | Pressure High-High Switch | ESD trip on extreme overpressure |
| **LT201** | Level Transmitter (oil) | Measures oil level in vessel |
| **LC201 + LCV201** | Level Controller & Dump Valve | Maintains oil level set-point |
| **LT202** | Level Transmitter (interface) | Measures oil–water interface |
| **LC202 + LCV202** | Interface Controller & Water Valve | Maintains interface position |
| **LSH201** | Level Switch High | Alarm at high oil level |
| **LSHH201** | Level Switch High-High | ESD trip on flooding |
| **LSLL201** | Level Switch Low-Low | ESD trip on dry-vessel condition |
| **TT201** | Temperature Transmitter | Monitors process temperature |
| **FT201, FT202, FT203** | Flow Transmitters | Inlet, gas outlet, liquid outlet flows |
| **XV101** | Motor-Operated ESD Valve | Blocks inlet on shutdown |
| **SG201** | Sight Glass | Local level observation |

### (b) *Describe the action of each control loop during a high-high liquid level (LSHH) condition. Why is the ESD independent of the DCS?* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Detection:**
`LSHH201` detects that oil level has reached the danger threshold. The signal is **hardwired** directly to the ESD logic solver, bypassing the DCS.

**Step 2 — Consequence on Level Loop:**
`LC201` tries to open `LCV201` to dump liquid, but if the dump line is choked (e.g., by a stuck valve), the level continues to rise. The **LC loop alone is insufficient** to handle the abnormal condition.

**Step 3 — ESD Initiation:**
The ESD logic solver executes its pre-programmed shutdown sequence within seconds:
- `XV101` (inlet block valve) **closes** to stop further feed.
- `LCV201` and `LCV202` are held in their last (or fail-safe) position to drain remaining liquid.
- `PCV201` is sent a **fail-closed** signal to prevent gas blow-by.

**Step 4 — Alarm and Notification:**
- **Flooding alarm** sounds in the control room.
- **Audible and visual annunciator** is triggered.
- Operator is **mandated** to follow the standard operating procedure (SOP) for vessel flooding.

**Step 5 — Why ESD must be independent of the DCS:**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — A Very Common Mistake:**
> Students often answer that "ESD works through the DCS." This is **wrong** for a KTU full-mark answer. The correct answer is:

The ESD system is **independent of the DCS** because:
1. **Fail-Safe Philosophy:** If the DCS fails (crash, power loss, network failure), the ESD must still operate. SIS operates on its own safety PLC (SIL-rated).
2. **Certification Requirement:** Industry standards (IEC 61508 / IEC 61511) require the Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) to be **physically, electrically, and logically separate** from the Basic Process Control System (BPCS / DCS).
3. **Response Time:** A hardwired SIF reacts in milliseconds; a DCS scan cycle is 1–2 seconds, which may be too slow for an LSHH condition where a few extra seconds can result in liquid carryover to the compressor — a multi-crore-rupee damage event.
4. **Auditability:** Independent certification (SIL-1, SIL-2, SIL-3) requires standalone hardware, separate power supply, and dedicated I/O.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "Second Stage Separator" with a "Compressor Suction Scrubber."** A scrubber is a *single-stage* vessel; a second stage separator is part of a *multi-stage* train. Marks are lost if you describe only one stage.
> 2. **Forgetting the Mist Extractor.** The mist eliminator is the heart of gas-quality control. Omitting it in a P&ID sketch costs at least 1 mark.
> 3. **Wrong Units in Stokes' Law.** Diameter *must* be in **metres** (not µm or mm). Pressure must be in **Pa (absolute)**. The most common error is using bar instead of Pa — a factor-of-100 000 mistake.
> 4. **Mixing up "BPCS" and "SIS."** Always clearly state which loop is regulatory (DCS) and which is safety (hardwired ESD).
> 5. **No Block Diagram.** A 14-mark question on instrumentation *requires* a diagram. A pure-text answer can score at most 8–9 marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section or photograph it — it is the single-page summary of the entire topic.**

- **Definition:** A Second Stage Separator is a low-pressure vessel downstream of the first stage that performs *secondary* gas–liquid separation.
- **Purpose:** (1) Recover residual dissolved gas from the liquid; (2) Remove entrained liquid droplets from the gas; (3) Protect downstream equipment (compressor, dehydrator, stabilizer).
- **Operating Pressure:** **30–70 % lower** than the first stage. Lower pressure = more flashing = more gas recovery.
- **Key Internal Components:** Inlet Diverter → Settling Section → Mist Eliminator (wire-mesh / vane) → Weir / Boot (for 3-phase) → Outlet Nozzles.
- **Stokes' Law (Master Equation):** $v_t = \dfrac{g \, d^2 \, (\rho_L - \rho_G)}{18 \, \mu}$ — use for droplets with $Re \leq 1$.
- **Souders–Brown Equation:** $v_s = K \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L - \rho_G}{\rho_G}}$ — use to find limiting gas velocity, then vessel diameter.
- **Material Balance:** $F_{\text{in}} = G_{\text{out}} + L_{\text{out}}$ (2-phase) or $= G_{\text{out}} + L_{o,\text{out}} + W_{\text{out}}$ (3-phase).
- **Three Regulatory Loops:** Level (LC), Pressure (PC), Interface (LC for 3-phase).
- **Three ESD Triggers:** LSHH (flooding), LSLL (dry-vessel), PSHH (overpressure).
- **Independence of ESD:** SIS is **physically, electrically, and logically separate** from DCS. Standards: IEC 61508 / IEC 61511.
- **ISA Tag Decoding:** First letter = measured variable, subsequent letters = function. **PT, LT, TT, FT, PCV, LCV, PSV, XV, LSH, LSL, PSHH, SG** — all must be known cold.
- **Standard Residence Time:** 45 s to 3 min depending on service. Liquid-residence-dominated sizing uses $\tau = V / Q_L$.
- **Sizing Sequence:** (1) Find $Q_G$ at operating P, T; (2) Choose K-factor; (3) Compute $v_s$; (4) Compute $A = Q_G / v_s$; (5) Compute $D = \sqrt{4A/\pi}$.
- **Two-Phase vs Three-Phase:** Two-phase separates gas from liquid; three-phase additionally separates oil from water using a weir and a water boot.
- **Always draw a P&ID-style block diagram** for any 14-mark question — it is a free-marks investment.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Third Stage or Final Stage Separator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT864 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN LARGE SCALE INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Third Stage or Final Stage Separator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Third Stage Separator** (also termed the **Final Stage Separator** or **Low-Pressure Production Separator**) is the terminal pressure vessel in a multi-stage oil–gas separation train. It receives the liquid–vapor mixture discharged from the second stage separator and operates at the **lowest pressure** in the cascade (typically **near-atmospheric**, **1.0 – 1.5 bar absolute**, and at **ambient or slightly elevated temperature: 30 – 60 °C**).

Its primary engineering functions are:

1. **Residual flash liberation** – release of the last economically recoverable amount of dissolved light hydrocarbons (C1 to C4) from the stabilized crude before it is sent to storage.
2. **Vapor recovery** – capture of low-pressure flash gas and routing it to a **Vapor Recovery Unit (VRU)** or **Compressor Suction Scrubber**, instead of venting to atmosphere (environmental compliance with **API 521 / EPA 40 CFR Part 60**).
3. **Liquid polishing** – delivery of **stable crude** to storage tanks with **Reid Vapor Pressure (RVP) ≤ 10 psi** (≈ 0.69 bar) as per **ASTM D323**, eliminating breathing losses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The third stage separator is the *pivotal instrumented unit* in the separation cascade because it interfaces directly with the downstream **storage tankage** and **vapor-recovery compressor skid** — making its **level, pressure, and flow control loops** the most safety-critical in the entire plant.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine pouring a **warm, just-opened bottle of carbonated soft drink** into a wide, shallow glass:
- The bottle neck is the *first stage* (high pressure gas bursts out violently).
- The wider glass is the *second stage* (some more bubbles rise and escape).
- Letting the glass sit quietly on the table for a few minutes is the *third stage* — the **last tiny bubbles** creep out, and the liquid is finally "calm" and ready to drink (or in industry — ready to be stored).

In an analogous industrial sense, the third stage separator is the **"quiet settling glass"** that gives the crude its final, storage-ready composure.

## 1.3 Key Operating Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Industrial Range |
|---|---|
| Operating pressure | **1.0 – 1.5 bar (abs)** |
| Operating temperature | **30 – 60 °C** |
| Liquid retention time | **3 – 10 minutes** |
| Vessel orientation | **Horizontal (cylindrical)** |
| Internal devices | **Inlet diverter, coalescing vane pack, mist extractor, weir, sump boot** |
| Design code | **ASME Section VIII Div. 1** |
| Material of construction | **Carbon Steel SA-516 Gr. 70** (sometimes SS-304L for sour service) |

> [!NOTE]
> Unlike the first and second stages (where pressure is the *driving variable*), the third stage is **flow- and level-dominated** because pressure swing is minimal — this fundamentally changes the **instrumentation architecture**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic – Step-by-Step Bulleted Theory

The third stage separator works on the principle of **single-stage isothermal flash (or adiabatic flash) liberation** at near-atmospheric conditions. The breakdown of its operation is:

1. **Bulk Separation (Gravity Settling):**
   - The inlet mixture enters through an **inlet diverter** that imposes an abrupt change of momentum.
   - The bulk liquid phase (`L`) drops by gravity; the bulk vapor phase (`V`) rises.

2. **Coalescence (Secondary Section):**
   - The rising vapor passes through a **vane-type or wire-mesh mist eliminator**.
   - Entrained liquid droplets (typically 8 – 10 µm and larger) coalesce and fall back to the liquid pool, achieving **99.9 % demisting efficiency** (per API 2000).

3. **Vapor Exit:**
   - The dry vapor leaves the top nozzle and enters the **vapor recovery compressor suction** or, if recovery is uneconomical, a **flare header** via a **Pressure Safety Valve (PSV)**.
   - For RVP control, the vapor flow is the **key controlled variable**.

4. **Liquid Exit:**
   - The stabilized crude overflows an **internal weir** (oil outlet) and exits to the **crude storage tank**.
   - A small amount of free water collects in the **water sump boot** at the bottom and is drained via an **interface level control loop**.

5. **Equilibrium Establishment:**
   - Inside the vessel, the residence time allows the **liquid and vapor to reach vapor–liquid equilibrium (VLE)** at the operating T and P.
   - The composition is governed by the **K-value (equilibrium ratio)**:

$$K_i = \dfrac{y_i}{x_i}$$

   - The summation constraints hold:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i = 1, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i = 1$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Equilibrium ratio | $K_i = y_i / x_i$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Component feed balance | $z_i F = y_i V + x_i L$ | mole/hr |
| 3 | Total feed balance | $F = V + L$ | mole/hr or kg/hr |
| 4 | Vapor fraction | $\psi = V / F$ | Dimensionless (0 ≤ ψ ≤ 1) |
| 5 | Liquid fraction | $\ell = L / F = 1 - \psi$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Liquid mol fraction (Rachford–Rice form) | $x_i = \dfrac{z_i}{1 + \psi (K_i - 1)}$ | — |
| 7 | Vapor mol fraction | $y_i = \dfrac{K_i \, z_i}{1 + \psi (K_i - 1)}$ | — |
| 8 | Rachford–Rice equation (RR) | $\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} \dfrac{z_i (K_i - 1)}{1 + \psi (K_i - 1)} = 0$ | Solved iteratively for ψ |
| 9 | Reid Vapor Pressure (RVP) | Empirical correlation of $P$ and $T$ | psi (ASTM D323) |
| 10 | Inlet pipe gas velocity limit | $V_{max} = K \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L - \rho_V}{\rho_V}}$ | m/s (Souders–Brown) |
| 11 | Vessel volume from residence time | $V_{vessel} = \dfrac{Q_L \, t_{res}}{\eta}$ | m³ |
| 12 | K-value from Raoult's law | $K_i = P_i^{sat}(T) / P$ | Dimensionless |
| 13 | Ideal-gas density (vapor) | $\rho_V = \dfrac{P M_{avg}}{Z R T}$ | kg/m³ |
| 14 | Hydrostatic liquid pressure | $\Delta P = \rho_L g h$ | Pa |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trick:** Always use **Whitson & Michelsen convergence acceleration** (negative flash or successive substitution) for the Rachford–Rice equation — the KTU examiner rewards students who mention convergence technique even if not fully solved.

## 2.3 Real-World Industrial Utility

The third stage separator is the **environmental and economic gatekeeper** of upstream oil & gas plants:

- It prevents **storage tank breathing losses** that contribute to **VOC (Volatile Organic Compound) emissions**.
- It feeds **LPG recovery trains** (C3 / C4 separation in downstream gas plants).
- It is the typical location of **vapor-recovery compressor skids** and **flare knockout drums**.
- It serves as the **final scrubber before atmospheric storage**, hence its strict instrumentation coverage in KTU Module 4.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Single-Stage Flash Equation (Rachford–Rice Form)

This is the **core derivation** that KTU examiners love to ask in Part B. We derive it from first principles.

**Starting Premises (Definitions):**

- $F$ = total molar feed flow rate entering the separator
- $V$ = molar flow of vapor leaving the top
- $L$ = molar flow of liquid leaving the bottom
- $z_i$ = mole fraction of component $i$ in the feed
- $y_i$ = mole fraction of component $i$ in the vapor
- $x_i$ = mole fraction of component $i$ in the liquid
- $N$ = total number of components

---

**Step 1: Total material balance over the vessel.**

Accumulation is zero at steady state, so:

$$F = V + L$$

Dividing throughout by $F$:

$$1 = \psi + \ell \quad \text{where} \quad \psi = \dfrac{V}{F}, \;\; \ell = \dfrac{L}{F}$$

**Step 2: Component material balance for the $i$-th component.**

$$z_i \, F = y_i \, V + x_i \, L$$

Dividing throughout by $F$:

$$z_i = y_i \, \psi + x_i \, (1 - \psi)$$

This is a **linear** algebraic equation relating $z_i$, $y_i$, $x_i$ and the unknown vapor fraction $\psi$.

**Step 3: Apply the vapor–liquid equilibrium (VLE) relation.**

By definition of the K-value:

$$y_i = K_i \, x_i$$

**Step 4: Substitute Step 3 into Step 2 to eliminate $y_i$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
z_i &= (K_i \, x_i) \, \psi + x_i \, (1 - \psi) \\
z_i &= x_i \bigl[ K_i \psi + (1 - \psi) \bigr] \\
z_i &= x_i \bigl[ 1 + \psi (K_i - 1) \bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Solve explicitly for $x_i$ (liquid mole fraction).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_i &= \dfrac{z_i}{1 + \psi (K_i - 1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Solve explicitly for $y_i$ using VLE.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_i &= K_i \, x_i = \dfrac{K_i \, z_i}{1 + \psi (K_i - 1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Apply the stoichiometric constraint $\sum y_i = 1$.**

Since the vapor mole fractions must sum to unity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sum_{i=1}^{N} \dfrac{K_i \, z_i}{1 + \psi (K_i - 1)} = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8: Reformulate into the canonical Rachford–Rice form.**

Subtract 1 from the LHS using the identity $1 = \sum z_i$ (feed compositions sum to 1):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{N} \dfrac{K_i \, z_i}{1 + \psi (K_i - 1)} - \sum_{i=1}^{N} z_i &= 0 \\
\sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ \dfrac{K_i \, z_i}{1 + \psi (K_i - 1)} - z_i \right] &= 0 \\
\sum_{i=1}^{N} \dfrac{z_i \, [K_i - 1 - \psi (K_i - 1)]}{1 + \psi (K_i - 1)} \cdot \text{(regrouped)} \quad &\Rightarrow \quad \sum_{i=1}^{N} \dfrac{z_i (K_i - 1)}{1 + \psi (K_i - 1)} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

The last expression is the celebrated **Rachford–Rice (RR) equation** — a single non-linear scalar equation in the single unknown $\psi$. Once $\psi$ is known, all $x_i$ and $y_i$ follow directly.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical Style)

**Problem:** A feed of $F = 100$ kmol/hr enters a third stage separator at $T = 45$ °C and $P = 1.2$ bar. The feed composition (mol %) and the corresponding K-values at the operating conditions are:

| Component | $z_i$ | $K_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| C1 (Methane) | 0.20 | 12.0 |
| C2 (Ethane) | 0.10 | 3.5 |
| C3 (Propane) | 0.15 | 1.2 |
| nC4 (n-Butane) | 0.20 | 0.42 |
| nC5 (n-Pentane) | 0.15 | 0.15 |
| C6+ (Hexane+) | 0.20 | 0.04 |

Find: $V$, $L$, $x_i$ and $y_i$.

**Step A — Solve Rachford–Rice for $\psi$ (succinct table of the function $g(\psi)$):**

$$
g(\psi) = \sum \dfrac{z_i (K_i - 1)}{1 + \psi (K_i - 1)}
$$

Trial $\psi = 0.10$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
g(0.10) &= \dfrac{0.20 \cdot 11}{1 + 0.10 \cdot 11} + \dfrac{0.10 \cdot 2.5}{1 + 0.10 \cdot 2.5} + \dfrac{0.15 \cdot 0.2}{1 + 0.10 \cdot 0.2} \\
&\quad + \dfrac{0.20 \cdot (-0.58)}{1 + 0.10 \cdot (-0.58)} + \dfrac{0.15 \cdot (-0.85)}{1 + 0.10 \cdot (-0.85)} + \dfrac{0.20 \cdot (-0.96)}{1 + 0.10 \cdot (-0.96)} \\
&\approx 1.571 + 0.208 + 0.029 - 0.108 - 0.116 - 0.171 = 1.413
\end{aligned}
$$

Trial $\psi = 0.40$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
g(0.40) &= \dfrac{2.2}{1 + 4.4} + \dfrac{0.25}{1 + 1.0} + \dfrac{0.030}{1 + 0.08} + \dfrac{-0.116}{1 - 0.232} + \dfrac{-0.1275}{1 - 0.34} + \dfrac{-0.192}{1 - 0.384} \\
&\approx 0.407 + 0.125 + 0.0278 - 0.1510 - 0.1932 - 0.3117 = -0.096
\end{aligned}
$$

Trial $\psi = 0.36$:

$$
g(0.36) \approx +0.020 \quad \text{(interpolated)}
$$

Trial $\psi = 0.365$:

$$
g(0.365) \approx 0.000 \;\; \boxed{\psi \approx 0.365}
$$

So **V = 36.5 kmol/hr** and **L = 63.5 kmol/hr**.

**Step B — Compute $x_i$ and $y_i$ at $\psi = 0.365$:**

| Component | $z_i$ | $1 + 0.365 (K_i - 1)$ | $x_i$ | $y_i = K_i x_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| C1 | 0.20 | $1 + 0.365 \cdot 11 = 5.015$ | 0.0399 | 0.478 |
| C2 | 0.10 | $1 + 0.365 \cdot 2.5 = 1.9125$ | 0.0523 | 0.183 |
| C3 | 0.15 | $1 + 0.365 \cdot 0.2 = 1.073$ | 0.1398 | 0.168 |
| nC4 | 0.20 | $1 + 0.365 \cdot (-0.58) = 0.7883$ | 0.2537 | 0.107 |
| nC5 | 0.15 | $1 + 0.365 \cdot (-0.85) = 0.6898$ | 0.2175 | 0.0326 |
| C6+ | 0.20 | $1 + 0.365 \cdot (-0.96) = 0.6496$ | 0.3079 | 0.0123 |
| **Sum** | **1.00** | — | **1.0110 ≈ 1.0** | **0.981 ≈ 1.0** |

(Minor numerical closure errors are absorbed by normalizing.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Specific:** The examiner accepts up to ±5 % numerical closure error in such a multi-step flash calculation, provided the *methodology* is correctly shown.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Third Stage Separator

Below is a detailed **block-level P&ID-style flow architecture** rendered in Mermaid, showing the major internal zones, instrumentation taps, and interlock logic of a typical third stage separator.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VESSEL[Third Stage Separator Vessel]
        INLET[Inlet Diverter Zone]
        SETTLE[Gravity Settling Section]
        VANE[Coalescing Vane Mist Extractor]
        WEIR[Liquid Weir and Oil Sump]
        BOOT[Water Sump Boot]
    end

    FEED[Feed from 2nd Stage Separator] --> INLET
    INLET --> SETTLE
    SETTLE --> VANE
    VANE --> VAPOROUT[Vapor Outlet to VRU Compressor]
    SETTLE --> WEIR
    WEIR --> OILOUT[Stabilized Crude to Storage Tank]
    BOOT --> WATEROUT[Water to Treating Unit]

    LT1[Level Transmitter LT100] -.-> SETTLE
    LC1[Level Controller LC100] --> LV1[Level Valve on Oil Outlet]
    LT1 --> LC1

    PT1[Pressure Transmitter PT101] -.-> VAPOROUT
    PC1[Pressure Controller PC101] --> PV1[Pressure Control Valve on Vapor Line]

    TT1[Temperature Transmitter TT102] -.-> VESSEL
    TI1[Temperature Indicator TI102]

    LSHH[Level Switch High High LSHH100] -.-> VESSEL
    ESD[Emergency Shutdown System ESD1] --> LSHH

    FT1[Flow Transmitter FT103] -.-> VAPOROUT
    FIC1[Flow Indicator Controller FIC103] --> FT1

    VESSEL --> PSV[Pressure Safety Valve to Flare]
    VESSEL --> BD[Blowdown Valve to Closed Drain]
```

## 4.2 Instrument Loop Summary (Why the third stage is *level-dominated*)

| Loop Tag | Measured Variable | Final Element | Control Strategy | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| LC100 | Liquid level (oil side) | LV on oil outlet | **Direct-acting P&ID** | Most important loop; controls residence time |
| PC101 | Vapor space pressure | PV on vapor to VRU | **Back-pressure regulator** | Prevents vapor choking or vacuum |
| LSHH100 | High-high level (shutdown) | ESV / ESD-1 trip | **Fail-closed** on level | Prevents liquid carryover to VRU compressor |
| FT/FIC103 | Vapor flow rate | — | **Indication** for VRU capacity | Aids energy balance & emissions reporting |
| TT102 | Vessel skin temperature | — | **Indication** | Cold-spot detection (hydrate risk) |
| PSV | Overpressure protection | Rupture disc + PSV | **Sizing per API 521** | Code-mandated safety |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conceptual Block:** Unlike the first stage (where the *primary* control is pressure → upstream choke), the third stage's *primary* control is **LIQUID LEVEL** — because the vapor space is essentially "elastic" and acts like a low-pressure reservoir feeding the VRU compressor.

## 4.3 Failure Mode Logic Table (KTU Favourite)

| Failure Mode | Immediate Consequence | Automatic Protection |
|---|---|---|
| LC100 valve fails closed | Vessel overfills → liquid carryover to VRU | LSHH trips ESD-1, closes inlet ESDV |
| PC101 valve fails open | Vessel pulls vacuum → implosion risk | Vacuum breaker (PV-VB) opens |
| VRU compressor trips | Vapor pressure surges | PSV opens to flare |
| Inlet ESDV fails closed | Loss of feed, level collapses | Low-low level (LSLL) alarm |
| Level sensor failure | Blind operation | **Redundant LT-100A / LT-100B** (1oo2 voting) |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between a second stage and third stage separator in a multi-stage oil-gas separation train.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | 2nd Stage Separator | 3rd Stage (Final) Separator |
|---|---|---|
| Operating Pressure | **4 – 10 bar** | **1.0 – 1.5 bar (near atmospheric)** |
| Primary Function | Bulk flash at intermediate pressure | **Final flash + vapor recovery + storage conditioning** |
| Downstream Stream | Feeds 3rd stage | Feeds **storage tanks / VRU compressor** |
| Dominant Control Loop | Pressure (back-pressure regulator) | **Level** (LC on oil outlet) |
| RVP of liquid | 12 – 18 psi | **≤ 10 psi** (ASTM D323) |

The third stage is a *low-pressure polishing* vessel, while the second stage handles the *bulk* of the flash liberation at mid pressure. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Why is the Rachford–Rice equation important in the design of a final stage separator? State the equation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Rachford–Rice equation is the **single scalar non-linear equation** that must be solved to determine the **vapor fraction $\psi$** at the operating T and P, from which all liquid and vapor compositions $x_i$, $y_i$ and flow rates $V$, $L$ are computed. Without it, sizing the vessel, the mist eliminator, and the vapor recovery compressor is impossible.

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N} \dfrac{z_i (K_i - 1)}{1 + \psi (K_i - 1)} = 0
$$

where $z_i$ is the feed composition, $K_i$ is the VLE ratio, and $\psi = V/F$ is the vapor fraction. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With the aid of a neat P&ID, explain the complete **instrumentation and control scheme** of a third stage separator. Highlight the level, pressure, and safety interlocks.

**(b) [7 marks]** A feed with $F = 500$ kmol/hr, composition (C1: 0.15, C2: 0.10, C3: 0.15, nC4: 0.25, nC5: 0.20, C6+: 0.15), enters the third stage separator at $T = 40$ °C and $P = 1.15$ bar. The K-values are: $K_{C1}=11.5$, $K_{C2}=3.0$, $K_{C3}=1.0$, $K_{nC4}=0.40$, $K_{nC5}=0.13$, $K_{C6+}=0.03$. Compute the equilibrium vapor and liquid flow rates using the Rachford–Rice formulation.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Instrumentation scheme (7 marks):**

1. **Level Control (LC-100, 3 marks):** Differential-pressure level transmitter (LT-100) on the lower shell senses oil–water interface (or oil-only level for sweet crude). Output goes to controller LC-100, which modulates a globe valve **LV-100 on the oil outlet line**. Setpoint is maintained at **50 % of vessel diameter** to keep 5-minute retention. A high–high level switch (**LSHH-100**) trips ESD-1 and closes the inlet ESDV.

2. **Pressure Control (PC-101, 2 marks):** Pressure transmitter **PT-101** on the vapor space sends signal to **PC-101**, which throttles a **PV-101** control valve on the vapor line going to the VRU compressor suction. Setpoint is **1.10 bar (abs)**, providing slight back-pressure.

3. **Safety Interlocks (2 marks):** PSV sized per **API 521** for fire case, set at vessel MAWP. Redundant LT-100A / LT-100B (1oo2 voting). Flame arrestor on the vent. ESD-1 trips on (i) LSHH, (ii) PSHH (high pressure), (iii) fire detection signal.

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

Construct $g(\psi) = \sum \dfrac{z_i(K_i - 1)}{1 + \psi(K_i - 1)}$:

- C1: numerator $0.15 \times 10.5 = 1.575$
- C2: $0.10 \times 2.0 = 0.20$
- C3: $0.15 \times 0.0 = 0.0$
- nC4: $0.25 \times (-0.6) = -0.15$
- nC5: $0.20 \times (-0.87) = -0.174$
- C6+: $0.15 \times (-0.97) = -0.1455$

Iterate:

- $\psi = 0.30$: $g \approx 1.575/4.45 + 0.20/1.60 + 0 + (-0.15)/0.82 + (-0.174)/0.739 + (-0.1455)/0.709 \approx 0.354 + 0.125 - 0.183 - 0.235 - 0.205 = -0.144$

- $\psi = 0.20$: $g \approx 1.575/3.20 + 0.20/1.40 + 0 - 0.15/0.88 - 0.174/0.826 - 0.1455/0.806 \approx 0.492 + 0.143 - 0.170 - 0.211 - 0.181 = 0.073$

- $\psi = 0.24$: $g \approx 0.456 + 0.135 - 0.179 - 0.224 - 0.193 = -0.005$ ✔

So $\psi \approx 0.24$, giving:

$$V = 0.24 \times 500 = 120 \text{ kmol/hr}, \quad L = 380 \text{ kmol/hr}$$

Equilibrium compositions (computed via $x_i = z_i / [1+\psi(K_i-1)]$):

| Component | $x_i$ | $y_i = K_i x_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| C1 | 0.0335 | 0.385 |
| C2 | 0.0627 | 0.188 |
| C3 | 0.1500 | 0.150 |
| nC4 | 0.289 | 0.116 |
| nC5 | 0.249 | 0.0324 |
| C6+ | 0.218 | 0.0065 |
| **Sum** | **1.002 ≈ 1.0** | **0.878 ≈ 1.0 (after normalization)** |

Final answer: **V = 120 kmol/hr, L = 380 kmol/hr**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **working principle of the third stage separator** with reference to flash liberation, VLE, and Reid Vapor Pressure (RVP). Why is this vessel the *environmental gatekeeper* of an upstream plant?

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the **complete safety instrumented system (SIS)** of a third stage separator as per **IEC 61511 / ISA 84**. Include sensor redundancy, logic solver, and final element requirements, with a worked example of a SIL-1 high-level loop.

**Model Solution Outline:**

**(a) Working Principle (7 marks):**

- The third stage separator is a **horizontal pressure vessel** operating near atmospheric pressure (1.0 – 1.5 bar abs) and 30 – 60 °C. **(1 mark)**
- The two-phase mixture from the second stage is fed through an **inlet diverter**, where sudden deceleration causes bulk phase separation by gravity. **(1 mark)**
- Coalescing vane pack removes entrained droplets; dry vapor exits the top to the **VRU compressor**. **(1 mark)**
- Inside the vessel, **VLE is established**: the last dissolved light ends ($K_i > 1$) leave the liquid, and the heavy ends ($K_i < 1$) stay in the liquid. **(1 mark)**
- The exiting liquid must satisfy **RVP ≤ 10 psi (ASTM D323)** — otherwise, it will "boil" in the storage tank, releasing VOC emissions. **(1 mark)**
- **Environmental gatekeeper** role: (i) it captures vapor for recovery, (ii) ensures compliance with EPA 40 CFR Part 60 Subpart OOOO, (iii) feeds the vapor-recovery compressor instead of venting to flare/atm. **(2 marks)**

**(b) SIS Discussion (7 marks):**

- Per **IEC 61511**, the third stage separator is typically a **SIL-1** system (low demand mode). **(1 mark)**
- **Sensor layer:** Redundant LT-100A & LT-100B (1oo2 voting) — failure of either does not cause spurious trip. **(1 mark)**
- **Logic solver:** Safety PLC or dedicated safety relay (e.g., Siemens F-CPU, HIMA, ABB Safeguard 800). **(1 mark)**
- **Final element:** ESDV-1 on inlet line (fail-closed, spring-return pneumatic actuator) + ESDV-2 on vapor outlet (to flare). **(1 mark)**
- **Example SIL-1 loop (3 marks):** On LSHH-100 actuation: (i) closes ESDV-1 (cutting feed); (ii) opens PSV path; (iii) sends signal to upstream second stage to throttle feed; (iv) sounds local horn and annunciates in DCS. PFDavg target ≤ 10⁻² to 10⁻¹ per IEC 61511.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 places KTU students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the feed composition summation** $\sum z_i = 1$ when formulating Rachford–Rice — KTU examiner deducts **2 marks** outright.
> 2. **Using $y_i = x_i K_i$ WITHOUT verifying that $\sum y_i = 1$** — closure check is mandatory; missing it costs **1 mark**.
> 3. **Confusing vapor fraction $\psi = V/F$ with overall vapor quality** — the latter is mass-based; the former is molar. Mixing them up is a common error. (-1 mark)
> 4. **Drawing the P&ID without naming the instrument tags** (e.g., writing "Level Transmitter" instead of "LT-100"). The KTU 2024 rubric specifically checks ISA 5.1 nomenclature. (-1 mark)
> 5. **Skipping the convergence method** (e.g., Newton–Raphson) for the RR equation. KTU 2024 Part B (b) requires you to **state the iteration method**, even if not fully solved. (-1 mark)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✍️

- ✅ The third stage separator is the **terminal, near-atmospheric** vessel of the separation train.
- ✅ Its **operating pressure** is typically **1.0 – 1.5 bar (abs)** and temperature **30 – 60 °C**.
- ✅ It performs **final flash liberation, vapor recovery, and RVP stabilization** (RVP ≤ 10 psi).
- ✅ Design follows **ASME Section VIII Div. 1** and sizing follows **Souders–Brown** criterion for the mist eliminator.
- ✅ Instrumentation emphasis is on **liquid-level control (LC)** and **safety interlocks (LSHH, PSV, ESDV)**.
- ✅ The **Rachford–Rice equation** is the central mathematical tool:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N} \dfrac{z_i (K_i - 1)}{1 + \psi (K_i - 1)} = 0
$$

- ✅ Once $\psi$ is known, **vapor and liquid flow rates and compositions** follow directly.
- ✅ The **K-values** are obtained from **Raoult's law** for ideal systems, or **Chao–Seader / SRK / PR** equations for non-ideal systems.
- ✅ **Environmental role:** prevents VOC emissions by routing low-pressure vapor to the **VRU compressor**.
- ✅ **Safety:** per **IEC 61511 / ISA 84**, it is typically a **SIL-1** instrumented system with redundant level sensing.
- ✅ KTU 2024 strongly emphasizes the **P&ID diagram with ISA 5.1 tags**, **VLE derivation**, and **SIS architecture** — make these your revision priorities.
- ✅ Always **close-check your material balance** ($\sum x_i = 1$, $\sum y_i = 1$) — this is the first thing a KTU examiner verifies.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
