> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT864 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN LARGE SCALE INDUSTRIES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Coalescer ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Coalescer** is a primary mechanical separation device used in large-scale process industries to promote the union (coalescence) of finely dispersed, immiscible fluid phases—typically liquid-liquid (e.g., water-in-oil) or liquid-in-gas (aerosol/mist elimination)—into larger droplets that can be readily separated by gravity in a downstream settling zone. The coalescer exploits interfacial tension forces, Brownian diffusion, inertial impaction, and direct interception to merge sub-micron to millimeter-scale droplets on a wettable porous medium (fibrous pad, mesh, or membrane). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Coalescers are classified under **Mechanical Separation Equipment** in Module 4. They are integral to upstream gas processing, refinery crude dehydration, amine/glycol regeneration skids, compressor suction scrubbers, and effluent treatment plants (ETPs). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a crowded cinema lobby where 1,000 people (small droplets) are scattered everywhere, all trying to exit. Now, place a sticky sponge near the doorway: people bump into it, stick to others, and exit as a group of 50 (large droplets). The sponge did not "push" them out—it just gave them a chance to **clump together**. A coalescer does exactly the same for tiny water droplets inside an oil stream, or for oil mist inside a natural gas line. Once droplets are large enough, gravity (the floor of the lobby) easily pulls them down to a collection sump. > [!NOTE] > **Core principle in one line:** *"Small drops merge into big drops on a wettable surface, then gravity does the rest."* ### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Standard Units) | Parameter | Standard Unit | Typical Range | |---|---|---| | Separation Efficiency ($\eta$) | **%** | 95 – 99.99 % | | Pressure Drop ($\Delta P$) | **bar / psi / kPa** | 0.05 – 1.0 bar | | Inlet Droplet Size | **µm (micrometers)** | 0.1 – 500 µm | | Outlet Droplet Size | **µm** | < 10 µm (gas); < 50 ppm (liquid-liquid) | | Superficial Velocity | **m/s** | 0.005 – 0.3 m/s | | Interfacial Tension | **mN/m (dyne/cm)** | 5 – 50 mN/m | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operating Logic A coalescer system operates in a **three-stage logic chain**: 1. **Pre-filtration / Distribution Stage** - The inlet mixture (e.g., wet gas or emulsified oil) passes through an inlet distributor to ensure uniform flow across the cross-section. - Sudden expansion reduces velocity, allowing bulk phase separation to begin. 2. **Coalescence Stage (Heart of the Unit)** - The dispersed phase contacts a **wettable fibrous or membrane medium**. - Droplets are captured by four simultaneous mechanisms: * **Inertial Impaction** – dominant for $d_p > 1\ \mu m$ * **Brownian Diffusion** – dominant for $d_p < 0.1\ \mu m$ * **Direct Interception** – when droplet trajectory grazes a fiber * **Gravitational Settling** – in vertical flow orientation - Captured droplets migrate along the fiber, coalesce, and form large drops on the downstream side. 3. **Separation / Settling Stage** - Enlarged droplets (now $d_p > 500\ \mu m$) fall into a **gravity settler** (boot/sump) due to density difference. - The continuous (clean) phase exits through the top outlet. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why does coalescence work?** Surface energy minimization. Small droplets have high surface-area-to-volume ratio. When two small droplets merge into one larger droplet, the **total interfacial area decreases**, releasing surface free energy—this is the thermodynamic driving force. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Standard Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\eta = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Single-pass separation efficiency | dimensionless (%) | | 2 | $V_t = \dfrac{g \cdot d_p^{\,2} \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_c)}{18 \cdot \mu}$ | Stokes' terminal settling velocity | m/s | | 3 | $Re_p = \dfrac{\rho_c \cdot V \cdot d_p}{\mu}$ | Droplet Reynolds number | dimensionless | | 4 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{150 \cdot \mu \cdot L \cdot V_s}{D_p^{\,2}} \cdot \dfrac{(1-\varepsilon)^2}{\varepsilon^3}$ | Kozeny-Carman pressure drop (packed bed) | Pa | | 5 | $C_D = \dfrac{24}{Re_p} \left(1 + 0.15 \, Re_p^{0.687}\right)$ | Drag coefficient (Schiller-Naumann) | dimensionless | | 6 | $We = \dfrac{\rho_c \cdot V^{\,2} \cdot d_p}{\sigma}$ | Weber number — droplet breakup index | dimensionless | | 7 | $E_{imp} = \left(\dfrac{Stk}{Stk + 0.35}\right)^{1.4}$ | Single-fiber impaction efficiency (Stokes number dependent) | dimensionless | | 8 | $Stk = \dfrac{\rho_p \cdot d_p^{\,2} \cdot V}{18 \cdot \mu \cdot d_f}$ | Stokes number for inertial impaction | dimensionless | > [!NOTE] > In all formulas above, $\rho_p$ = dispersed phase density, $\rho_c$ = continuous phase density, $\mu$ = continuous phase viscosity, $\sigma$ = interfacial tension, $d_f$ = fiber diameter, $\varepsilon$ = bed porosity, $L$ = bed thickness, $D_p$ = particle/fiber diameter, $V_s$ = superficial velocity, $C_{in}, C_{out}$ = inlet/outlet dispersed phase concentration. ### 2.3 Real-World Industrial Utility - **Refineries:** Desalting of crude oil upstream of the atmospheric distillation column. - **Gas Processing:** Dehydration and hydrocarbon recovery from natural gas in **mol sieve + coalescer** trains. - **Petrochemicals:** Separation of water from aromatic hydrocarbons (benzene/toluene/xylene). - **Compressor Protection:** Mist eliminators on compressor suction lines to prevent **liquid slugging** and mechanical damage. - **Effluent Treatment:** Oil-water separation in API separators and CPI (corrugated plate interceptor) units. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Terminal Settling Velocity (Stokes' Regime) We begin with the **force balance on a single spherical droplet** settling under gravity in a viscous continuous phase. **Forces acting on the droplet:** 1. Gravitational (downward): $F_g = \dfrac{\pi}{6} d_p^{\,3} \rho_p \, g$ 2. Buoyancy (upward): $F_b = \dfrac{\pi}{6} d_p^{\,3} \rho_c \, g$ 3. Drag (upward, opposing motion): $F_D = C_D \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} d_p^{\,2} \cdot \dfrac{\rho_c V^2}{2}$ **At terminal velocity**, the net force is zero: $$ F_g - F_b - F_D = 0 $$ Substituting the force expressions: $$ \dfrac{\pi}{6} d_p^{\,3} \rho_p g - \dfrac{\pi}{6} d_p^{\,3} \rho_c g - C_D \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} d_p^{\,2} \cdot \dfrac{\rho_c V^2}{2} = 0 $$ Simplify by dividing through by $\dfrac{\pi d_p^{\,2}}{4}$: $$ \dfrac{2}{3} d_p g \left(\rho_p - \rho_c\right) = C_D \cdot \dfrac{\rho_c V^2}{2} $$ Solving for $V$: $$ V = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \, g \, d_p \left(\rho_p - \rho_c\right)}{3 \, C_D \, \rho_c}} $$ **Now applying the Stokes flow assumption** ($Re_p \ll 1$, where $C_D = \dfrac{24}{Re_p}$): $$ V = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \, g \, d_p \left(\rho_p - \rho_c\right)}{3 \cdot \left(\dfrac{24 \mu}{\rho_c V d_p}\right) \cdot \rho_c}} $$ Multiply numerator and denominator to isolate $V$: $$ V^2 = \dfrac{4 \, g \, d_p \left(\rho_p - \rho_c\right) \cdot V \, d_p \, \rho_c}{3 \cdot 24 \cdot \mu \cdot \rho_c} $$ Cancel one $V$ from both sides: $$ V = \dfrac{g \, d_p^{\,2} \left(\rho_p - \rho_c\right)}{18 \, \mu} $$ This is the famous **Stokes' Terminal Velocity** equation used universally in coalescer settler design. > [!NOTE] > **Validity Check:** Stokes' law is valid only for $Re_p \leq 1$. For larger droplets in a coalescer, the Schiller-Naumann drag coefficient must be used, leading to an iterative solution. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Coalescer Type vs. Application | Coalescer Type | Mechanism | Typical Droplet Removal | Best Suited For | Limitation | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Fibrous Bed (Vertical)** | Impaction + Interception | 0.1 – 100 µm | Gas mists | High $\Delta P$ if fouled | | **Fibrous Bed (Horizontal)** | Gravity + Impaction | 10 – 500 µm | Liquid-liquid | Requires periodic draining | | **Cartridge (Pleated)** | Depth filtration | 0.3 – 50 µm | Fuel/lubricant polishing | Disposable element | | **Electrostatic** | Charged droplet attraction | 0.01 – 10 µm | Sub-micron aerosols | High CAPEX | | **Mesh / Vane (Demister)** | Inertial impaction | 5 – 100 µm | Bulk gas-liquid separation | Inefficient for sub-micron | | **Membrane (Hollow Fiber)** | Surface coalescence | < 1 µm | High-purity applications | Low flow capacity | ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** A horizontal coalescer handles a water-in-oil emulsion. Water droplets (density $\rho_p = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$) of diameter $d_p = 100\ \mu m$ are dispersed in oil (density $\rho_c = 850\ \text{kg/m}^3$, viscosity $\mu = 0.005\ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$). Find the **terminal settling velocity**. **Step 1: Calculate the droplet Reynolds number to confirm Stokes regime.** Assume $V = 1 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m/s}$ as initial guess: $$ Re_p = \dfrac{\rho_c V d_p}{\mu} = \dfrac{850 \times 10^{-4} \times 100 \times 10^{-6}}{0.005} = 1.7 \times 10^{-3} $$ Since $Re_p \ll 1$, **Stokes' law is valid**. **Step 2: Apply Stokes' terminal velocity formula.** $$ V_t = \dfrac{g \, d_p^{\,2} \left(\rho_p - \rho_c\right)}{18 \, \mu} $$ $$ V_t = \dfrac{9.81 \times \left(100 \times 10^{-6}\right)^2 \times (1000 - 850)}{18 \times 0.005} $$ $$ V_t = \dfrac{9.81 \times 10^{-8} \times 150}{0.09} $$ $$ V_t = 1.635 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m/s} $$ **Step 3: Verify Reynolds number with the new velocity.** $$ Re_p = \dfrac{850 \times 1.635 \times 10^{-4} \times 100 \times 10^{-6}}{0.005} = 2.78 \times 10^{-3} $$ Confirmed $Re_p \ll 1$, so $V_t = 1.635 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m/s} = 0.164\ \text{mm/s}$ is the final answer. > [!NOTE] > This extremely low velocity explains why coalescers are designed with **large cross-sectional areas** — to give droplets enough residence time (typically 5–15 minutes) to settle. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Two-Stage Coalescer System ```mermaid flowchart TD INLET["INLET STREAM
WET GAS OR EMULSIFIED LIQUID"] --> DIST["INLET DISTRIBUTOR
FLOW EQUALIZATION"] DIST --> STG1["STAGE 1 COALESCER
FIBROUS BED MESH"] STG1 --> MECH1["MECHANISM:
INERTIAL IMPACTION
BROWNIAN DIFFUSION
DIRECT INTERCEPTION"] MECH1 --> SET1["PRIMARY SETTLER
GRAVITY BOOT"] SET1 --> STG2["STAGE 2 POLISHER
PLEATED CARTRIDGE"] STG2 --> MECH2["MECHANISM:
SURFACE COALESCENCE
DEPTH FILTRATION"] MECH2 --> SET2["SECONDARY SETTLER
FINAL SEPARATION"] SET2 --> OUT["CLEAN OUTLET STREAM"] SET1 --> DRAIN1["WATER DRAIN
SUMP PUMP"] SET2 --> DRAIN2["OIL SKIM
WASTE OUTLET"] STG1 --> PT1["DP TRANSMITTER
MONITORING"] STG2 --> PT2["DP TRANSMITTER
FOULING ALARM"] PT1 --> DCS["DCS CONTROL ROOM"] PT2 --> DCS DCS --> VALVE["AUTOMATIC DUMP VALVE"] ``` ### 4.2 Internal Flow Logic of a Single-Stage Coalescer Element ```mermaid flowchart LR A["INLET NOZZLE"] --> B["SPREADER PLATE
EVEN VELOCITY PROFILE"] B --> C["FIBROUS PACK
WETTABLE MEDIUM"] C --> D["DROPLET MERGE
SMALL TO LARGE"] D --> E["GRAVITY SETTLING ZONE
BOOT SECTION"] E --> F["CLEAN OUTLET"] E --> G["COLLECTED PHASE OUTLET
WATER OR OIL"] C --> H["PRESSURE DROP SENSOR"] H --> I["INDICATOR PANEL"] ``` ### 4.3 Instrumentation & Control Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD P1["INLET PRESSURE
TRANSMITTER PT-101"] --> CTRL["PLC / DCS CONTROLLER"] P2["OUTLET PRESSURE
TRANSMITTER PT-102"] --> CTRL DPT["DIFFERENTIAL
PRESSURE DPT-103"] --> CTRL DPT --> ALARM["FOULING ALARM
WHEN DP > 0.5 BAR"] LT["LEVEL TRANSMITTER
LT-104 IN BOOT"] --> CTRL CTRL --> V1["INLET SHUTOFF VALVE"] CTRL --> V2["DRAIN VALVE
AUTOMATIC"] CTRL --> V3["BYPASS VALVE"] FT["FLOW TRANSMITTER
FT-105"] --> CTRL QT["QUALITY ANALYZER
WATER IN OIL PPM"] --> CTRL CTRL --> HMI["OPERATOR HMI SCREEN"] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mermaid Compliance Note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `INLET`, `STG1`, `MECH1`). Reserved keywords like `end`, `graph`, `subgraph`, `style` are not used as node names. Labels contain only clean uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting symbols. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### **Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)** **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define a coalescer. List the four primary mechanisms by which droplets are captured on the coalescing medium.* **Model Answer:** A coalescer is a separation device that promotes the merging of finely dispersed, immiscible fluid droplets (liquid-in-liquid or liquid-in-gas) into larger droplets on a wettable porous medium, enabling subsequent gravity separation. The four primary capture mechanisms are: 1. **Inertial Impaction** – dominant for droplets $> 1\ \mu m$ where inertia carries the droplet across streamlines to a fiber. 2. **Brownian Diffusion** – dominant for droplets $< 0.1\ \mu m$ due to random molecular motion. 3. **Direct Interception** – when the droplet's center follows a streamline passing within one droplet radius of a fiber. 4. **Gravitational Settling** – applicable in vertical-flow coalescers where settling velocity exceeds superficial velocity. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *What is the significance of the Weber number in coalescer design? What condition must be satisfied to prevent droplet re-entrainment (breakup)?* **Model Answer:** The Weber number is the dimensionless ratio of inertial forces to surface tension forces: $$ We = \dfrac{\rho_c \, V^2 \, d_p}{\sigma} $$ It characterizes the tendency of a droplet to **deform and break apart** in a flow field. To prevent breakup and re-entrainment downstream of the coalescer, the **critical Weber number** must not be exceeded: $$ We < We_{crit} \approx 10 \text{ to } 12 $$ Exceeding this threshold causes the droplet to fragment back into smaller droplets, destroying the coalescer's separation efficiency. --- ### **Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - **Common Mistake #1:** Students write Stokes' law without verifying the **Reynolds number condition** ($Re_p \leq 1$). Always show the $Re_p$ check explicitly to earn the 2-mark validity step. > - **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting to convert droplet diameter from $\mu m$ to $m$ (a factor of $10^{-6}$). This single error loses 2–3 marks in numerical problems. > - **Common Mistake #3:** Conflating *coalescer* with *separator*. A coalescer **only enlarges droplets**; a separator is the gravity settler downstream. Drawing them as one device is penalized. > - **Common Mistake #4:** Skipping the units in the final answer. KTU examiners **deduct 0.5 mark** if units are missing. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]** **(a)** A vertical-flow gas coalescer handles $Q = 5000\ \text{m}^3/\text{hr}$ of natural gas (density $\rho_c = 1.2\ \text{kg/m}^3$, viscosity $\mu = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$) containing water droplets (density $\rho_p = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$, diameter $d_p = 5\ \mu m$). The vessel cross-sectional area is $A = 1.0\ \text{m}^2$. *(7 marks)* **(i)** Calculate the superficial gas velocity and verify if Stokes' law applies for the settling of these droplets. **(ii)** If the interfacial tension is $\sigma = 0.072\ \text{N/m}$, compute the Weber number and comment on whether droplet breakup is a concern. **(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **function of each major component** in a two-stage liquid-liquid coalescer system used in a refinery crude desalter. Discuss the role of the DCS in monitoring coalescer performance. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution — Question A:** **(a) (i) Superficial Velocity and Stokes' Validity** Superficial velocity: $$ V_s = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{5000/3600}{1.0} = 1.389\ \text{m/s} $$ Reynolds number for the droplet: $$ Re_p = \dfrac{\rho_c V_s d_p}{\mu} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 1.389 \times 5 \times 10^{-6}}{1.8 \times 10^{-5}} $$ $$ Re_p = 0.463 $$ Since $Re_p < 1$, **Stokes' law is valid**. The terminal settling velocity is: $$ V_t = \dfrac{g \, d_p^{\,2} \, (\rho_p - \rho_c)}{18 \, \mu} = \dfrac{9.81 \times (5 \times 10^{-6})^2 \times (1000 - 1.2)}{18 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-5}} $$ $$ V_t = \dfrac{9.81 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-11} \times 998.8}{3.24 \times 10^{-4}} = 7.56 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m/s} $$ **(a) (ii) Weber Number Check** $$ We = \dfrac{\rho_c \, V_s^{\,2} \, d_p}{\sigma} = \dfrac{1.2 \times (1.389)^2 \times 5 \times 10^{-6}}{0.072} $$ $$ We = \dfrac{1.2 \times 1.929 \times 5 \times 10^{-6}}{0.072} = 1.608 \times 10^{-4} $$ Since $We = 1.6 \times 10^{-4} \ll We_{crit} \approx 10$, **droplet breakup is NOT a concern**. The flow regime is safe for coalescer operation. **(b) Block Diagram & DCS Role** *(Refer to Section 4.1 diagram.)* The two-stage liquid-liquid coalescer for a refinery crude desalter consists of: - **Stage 1 (Fibrous Bed):** Primary coalescence of emulsified water droplets ($d_p$ 10–500 µm) using a wettable oleophilic/hydrophobic medium. Output: enlarged water droplets ready for settling. - **Inter-stage Settler:** Gravity boot where enlarged water droplets drop to the bottom; partially dried oil flows to Stage 2. - **Stage 2 (Pleated Cartridge Polisher):** Final polishing to achieve $< 50\ \text{ppm}$ water content before the crude enters the distillation column. - **Final Settler:** Removes trace water; clean dehydrated crude exits overhead. **Role of DCS:** - Monitors differential pressure across each stage → predicts fouling and schedules backwash. - Controls drain valves in the water boot → maintains interface level. - Tracks inlet/outlet water content via online analyzer → adjusts wash water injection rate. - Generates alarms for high DP (fouling), high level (boot flooding), and high outlet water content (efficiency loss). --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – Supplementary, Module 4]** **(a)** Derive Stokes' law for the terminal settling velocity of a spherical droplet in a viscous continuous phase. State clearly the assumptions made and the **range of validity**. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A horizontal liquid-liquid coalescer separates water from diesel. The water droplets have $d_p = 200\ \mu m$, density $\rho_p = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$. Diesel density $\rho_c = 850\ \text{kg/m}^3$, viscosity $\mu = 0.004\ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$, interfacial tension $\sigma = 0.035\ \text{N/m}$. *(7 marks)* **(i)** Calculate the terminal settling velocity and the residence time required in a settler of length $L = 2\ \text{m}$ and height $H = 0.5\ \text{m}$. **(ii)** If the flow rate is $Q = 10\ \text{m}^3/\text{hr}$, calculate the **volumetric separation efficiency** given the inlet water concentration is $C_{in} = 500\ \text{ppm}$ and outlet is $C_{out} = 15\ \text{ppm}$. --- **Model Solution — Question B:** **(a) Derivation of Stokes' Law** *(Refer to Section 3.1 for full derivation.)* **Assumptions:** 1. Spherical, rigid, non-deformable droplet. 2. Steady-state, laminar flow regime ($Re_p \leq 1$). 3. Continuous phase is Newtonian and unbounded (no wall effects). 4. No interaction between droplets (dilute dispersion, $< 1\%$ by volume). 5. No mass transfer or Marangoni effects. **Final Equation:** $$ V_t = \dfrac{g \, d_p^{\,2} \, (\rho_p - \rho_c)}{18 \, \mu} $$ **Validity:** $Re_p \leq 1$, dilute dispersions, rigid spheres. --- **(b) (i) Terminal Velocity and Residence Time** Reynolds number check (assuming $V_t \approx 10^{-3}\ \text{m/s}$): $$ Re_p = \dfrac{850 \times 10^{-3} \times 200 \times 10^{-6}}{0.004} = 0.0425 $$ $Re_p \ll 1$ → Stokes' law valid. $$ V_t = \dfrac{9.81 \times (200 \times 10^{-6})^2 \times (1000 - 850)}{18 \times 0.004} $$ $$ V_t = \dfrac{9.81 \times 4 \times 10^{-8} \times 150}{0.072} = 8.175 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m/s} $$ Time to settle through settler height $H = 0.5\ \text{m}$: $$ t_{settle} = \dfrac{H}{V_t} = \dfrac{0.5}{8.175 \times 10^{-4}} = 611.6\ \text{s} \approx 10.2\ \text{min} $$ Required residence time in the settler (horizontal flow through length $L$): $$ t_{res} = \dfrac{L \cdot H \cdot W}{Q} $$ For a settler of width $W = 1\ \text{m}$ (assumed), cross-section $A_{cs} = 0.5\ \text{m}^2$: $$ t_{res} = \dfrac{0.5 \times 1 \times 2}{10/3600} = 360\ \text{s} = 6\ \text{min} $$ > Since $t_{res} = 360\ \text{s} < t_{settle} = 611.6\ \text{s}$, the design is **undersized**. Either settler length must be increased to $L \geq 3.4\ \text{m}$ or settler height must be reduced. **(b) (ii) Volumetric Separation Efficiency** $$ \eta = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{500 - 15}{500} \times 100\% = 97\% $$ --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist** - [x] **Coalescer = droplet enlarger**, NOT a separator. Separation is done by the downstream gravity settler. - [x] Four capture mechanisms: **Inertial Impaction, Brownian Diffusion, Direct Interception, Gravitational Settling**. - [x] **Stokes' Law** valid only for $Re_p \leq 1$. Always verify before applying. - [x] **Kozeny-Carman equation** governs pressure drop across fibrous beds. - [x] **Weber number** indicates droplet breakup risk; keep $We < 10$–$12$ downstream. - [x] Always convert $\mu m$ to $m$ in numerical problems (factor of $10^{-6}$). - [x] Key instrumentation: **DPT** (fouling), **LT** (interface), **QT/AT** (water in oil ppm), **PT/FT** (flow & pressure). - [x] DCS interlock logic: high DP → backwash alarm; high level → drain valve auto-open. - [x] Fibrous bed coalescers handle 0.1–100 µm droplets; cartridge polishers handle sub-µm. - [x] Electrostatic coalescers (used in desalters) charge droplets to enhance collision frequency. - [x] Real-world examples: refinery desalters, compressor suction scrubbers, glycol contactor outlet, LNG inlet slug catchers. - [x] Typical separation efficiency target: **95–99.99%** depending on service.

topics.title: Electrostatic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT864 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN LARGE SCALE INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Electrostatic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of large-scale industrial process instrumentation, **electrostatics** refers to the branch of physics and applied engineering that deals with the behaviour of **stationary or slow-moving electric charges**, the forces they exert on each other, and the electric fields, potentials, and capacitance phenomena they produce. From an industrial control standpoint, electrostatics forms the foundational operating principle for several critical unit operations — most notably the **Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP)**, **electrostatic coating (e-painting)**, **electrostatic separation of minerals**, **capacitance-based level and proximity sensors**, and **charge-coupled process analysers**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):**  
> *Electrostatics in process industries is the study of charge generation, charge accumulation, electric field interactions, and controlled discharge phenomena used as the actuation principle for dust collection, material separation, coating, and non-contact measurement systems in large-scale plants.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a stretched rubber sheet held horizontally. If you place a heavy bowling ball at the centre, the sheet sags and forms a "well". A marble rolled nearby will curve *toward* the bowling ball — not because of a string, but because of the shape of the sheet itself. Now replace the rubber sheet with the invisible **electric field** of a charged wire, the bowling ball with a dust particle that has picked up a charge, and the marble's curved path with the dust particle being driven toward a collecting plate. This is, in essence, **how an electrostatic precipitator works** — charge acts as the "bowling ball", warping the field so that suspended particulates are physically pulled out of a gas stream.

For control engineers, the intuition is simple:
- **Charge = the input signal** (often induced by a high-voltage corona).
- **Electric field = the medium of action** (analogous to a process pipeline).
- **Force on the particle = the manipulated variable** that drives separation.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Coulomb's constant** $k_e = 8.9875 \times 10^9 \;\text{N·m}^2/\text{C}^2$ (often approximated as $9 \times 10^9$).  
> - **Permittivity of free space** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\;\text{C}^2/(\text{N·m}^2)$.  
> - **Elementary charge** $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\;\text{C}$.  
> - Typical ESP operating voltage: **30 kV – 100 kV (DC, negative polarity)**.  
> - Typical collection efficiency target in cement/thermal plants: **> 99.9 %**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Coulomb's Law — The Foundation

The electrostatic force between two point charges $q_1$ and $q_2$ separated by a distance $r$ in a medium of permittivity $\varepsilon$ is given by Coulomb's law.

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. Two charges experience a force along the line joining them.
2. The force is **repulsive** if like charges, **attractive** if unlike.
3. Force magnitude is **directly proportional** to the product of charges.
4. Force magnitude is **inversely proportional** to the square of separation.
5. The medium between them scales the force by a factor of $1/\varepsilon_r$.

### 2.2 Electric Field, Potential, and Capacitance

- **Electric Field Intensity ($E$)** — force experienced by a unit positive test charge.
- **Electric Potential ($V$)** — work done per unit charge in bringing a test charge from infinity to the point.
- **Capacitance ($C$)** — the ability of a conductor system to store charge per unit potential difference.

### 2.3 Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP) Working Principle

A typical industrial **wire-and-plate ESP** operates on a four-stage cycle:
1. **Ionisation / Corona Generation:** A high negative DC voltage applied to thin discharge wires creates a corona discharge, generating a unipolar (negative) ion cloud.
2. **Particulate Charging:** Dust particles entering the gas stream collide with these ions and acquire a negative charge.
3. **Migration / Collection:** The negatively charged particles are attracted toward the positively grounded collecting plates under the influence of the electric field.
4. **Dislodgement:** Rapping hammers periodically strike the plates, causing the dust layer to fall into hoppers for disposal.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all high-yield equations** expected in KTU ESE for this topic.

| # | Equation | LaTeX Form | Variables / Units | Engineering Use |
|---|----------|-----------|--------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Coulomb's Law (magnitude) | $F = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\,\dfrac{q_1 q_2}{r^{2}}$ | $F$ in N, $r$ in m, $q$ in C | Force between charged particulates |
| 2 | Electric Field of Point Charge | $E = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\,\dfrac{q}{r^{2}}$ | $E$ in V/m | Field around corona wire |
| 3 | Potential of Point Charge | $V = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\,\dfrac{q}{r}$ | $V$ in V | Potential difference for discharge |
| 4 | Force on Charged Particle | $F = qE$ | $F$ in N, $q$ in C, $E$ in V/m | Driving force in ESP |
| 5 | Drift Velocity (Deutsch Equation) | $w = \dfrac{q E \, d_p}{3\pi\mu d_w}$ | $w$ in m/s, $\mu$ in Pa·s | Particle migration speed |
| 6 | Collection Efficiency (Deutsch–Anderson) | $\eta = 1 - e^{(-wA/Q)}$ | $A$ = plate area, $Q$ = gas flow | ESP design efficiency |
| 7 | Capacitance of Parallel Plates | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | $C$ in F, $A$ in $\text{m}^{2}$, $d$ in m | Capacitive level sensors |
| 8 | Energy Stored in Capacitor | $U = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^{2}$ | $U$ in J | Discharge / flashover energy |
| 9 | Charging Field (Pauthenier) | $q = 3\pi\varepsilon_0 d_p^{2} E_0 \left(\dfrac{\varepsilon_r}{\varepsilon_r + 2}\right)$ | $q$ in C, $d_p$ in m | Field charging limit |
| 10 | Corona Onset Voltage (Peek's Law) | $V_c = m_v g_0 r \ln(S/r)$ | $S$ = wire-plate gap, $r$ = wire radius | Minimum HV for ionisation |
| 11 | Charge on a Sphere | $Q = 4\pi\varepsilon_0 r V$ | $Q$ in C | Charge on conductive dust |
| 12 | Gauss's Law (differential) | $\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = \rho/\varepsilon_0$ | $\rho$ = charge density | Field divergence in ESP chamber |

> **Pitfall Note:** In the formulas above, use $\mid \cdot \mid$ for magnitude and $\vert \cdot \vert$ inside any inline text. When typesetting inline math inside the table, **avoid using the vertical pipe `|`** as it breaks the markdown cell — always use `\vert` or `\mid`.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

Electrostatic principles drive critical large-scale industrial operations:
- **Thermal Power Plants** — ESPs for fly-ash capture (often $>99.9\,\%$ efficiency mandated).
- **Cement Plants** — Kiln and cooler dedusting.
- **Steel Plants** — Sinter plant and blast furnace gas cleaning.
- **Pulp & Paper** — Recovery boiler precipitators.
- **Pharma & Semiconductor** — Cleanroom static elimination via ionisers.
- **Process Control** — Capacitive probes for level, moisture, and composition measurement.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Deutsch–Anderson Equation for ESP Efficiency

This is the **single most important derivation** for KTU board questions on electrostatic precipitators. We will derive it step by step.

#### 3.1.1 Starting Premise

Consider a differential slice of the ESP duct of thickness $dx$ at distance $x$ from the inlet. Let:
- $n(x)$ = number of dust particles per unit volume still suspended at position $x$ (particles/$\text{m}^{3}$).
- $u$ = gas velocity along the duct (m/s).
- $w$ = migration (drift) velocity of charged particles perpendicular to the gas flow, toward the collecting plate (m/s).
- $A$ = total collecting plate area ($\text{m}^{2}$).
- $Q$ = volumetric gas flow rate ($\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$).
- $V$ = duct volume element.

#### 3.1.2 Particle Balance on a Differential Slice

The number of particles leaving the slice per second is:

$$\text{Particles out} = n(x) \cdot Q$$

The number removed per second by migration to the plate is proportional to the local particle density and the plate area exposed:

$$\text{Particles collected} = n(x) \cdot w \cdot dA$$

Therefore, the differential change in particle flux across the slice is:

$$Q \, dn = - n \, w \, dA$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{dn}{n} = -\frac{w}{Q}\, dA$$

#### 3.1.3 Integration Across the Full Plate Area

Integrating from the inlet ($n = n_0$, $A = 0$) to the outlet ($n = n_{\text{out}}$, $A = A_{\text{total}}$):

$$\int_{n_0}^{n_{\text{out}}} \frac{dn}{n} = -\frac{w}{Q}\int_{0}^{A_{\text{total}}} dA$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{n_{\text{out}}}{n_0}\right) = -\frac{wA}{Q}$$

#### 3.1.4 Definition of Collection Efficiency

The fractional collection efficiency $\eta$ is:

$$\eta = \frac{n_0 - n_{\text{out}}}{n_0} = 1 - \frac{n_{\text{out}}}{n_0}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\;\eta = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{wA}{Q}\right)\;}$$

This is the **Deutsch–Anderson Equation**.

#### 3.1.5 Physical Interpretation

- Larger plate area $A$ → higher $\eta$.
- Higher drift velocity $w$ (driven by $qE$) → higher $\eta$.
- Higher gas flow $Q$ → lower $\eta$ (less residence time).

### 3.2 Derivation: Drift Velocity of a Charged Particle (Stokes' Drag Balance)

A particle moving through a viscous fluid experiences a **Stokes drag force**:

$$F_d = 3\pi\mu d_p w$$

In the steady state, the electrostatic force balances the drag:

$$qE = 3\pi\mu d_p w$$

Solving for $w$:

$$\boxed{\;w = \dfrac{qE}{3\pi\mu d_p}\;}$$

Combining with the Pauthenier field-charging limit for $q$, the **effective drift velocity** is:

$$w = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 d_p E^{2}}{\mu}\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon_r}{\varepsilon_r + 2}\right)$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Capacitance of a Parallel-Plate Sensor (Industrial Application)

For a parallel-plate capacitor used as a **capacitive level sensor**, the capacitance is:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

When the level of a dielectric liquid (e.g., oil, water) rises and partially fills the gap, the capacitance becomes a weighted sum:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}\left[\varepsilon_r^{\text{air}}(1-f) + \varepsilon_r^{\text{liq}} f\right]$$

where $f$ is the fraction of the gap filled with liquid. This linear (or near-linear) relationship allows **continuous level measurement** in storage tanks — a common KTU instrumentation topic.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — ESP vs. Other Particulate Control Devices

| Parameter | Electrostatic Precipitator | Baghouse Filter | Cyclone Separator | Wet Scrubber |
|-----------|---------------------------|------------------|--------------------|---------------|
| Efficiency for fine dust ($< 2\,\mu\text{m}$) | **Very High** | Very High | Low | Moderate |
| Pressure drop | Very Low ($< 250\,\text{Pa}$) | Moderate ($1000–1500\,\text{Pa}$) | Moderate ($500–1500\,\text{Pa}$) | High ($1500–4000\,\text{Pa}$) |
| Power consumption | High (HV supply) | Low–Moderate | Low | Moderate–High |
| Operating cost | Low | High (bag replacement) | Very Low | High (water, chemicals) |
| Suitability for high-temp gas | Excellent | Limited (filter media) | Good | Poor |
| Sensitivity to resistivity | High | Low | None | Low |
| Typical application | Thermal, cement | Pharma, food | Pre-cleaner | Chemical, acid mists |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ESP Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Dirty Flue Gas Inlet] --> B[Gas Distribution Plates]
    B --> C[High-Voltage Discharge Wires -ve DC]
    C --> D[Corona Discharge Region]
    D --> E[Particles Gain Negative Charge]
    E --> F[Charged Particles Drift Toward Plates]
    F --> G[Grounded Collecting Plates +ve]
    G --> H[Dust Layer Accumulates on Plates]
    H --> I[Rapping Mechanism Activated]
    I --> J[Dust Falls into Hopper]
    J --> K[Clean Gas Exits to Stack]
    G --> L[HV Transformer-Rectifier Set]
    L --> C
    H --> M[Automatic Voltage Controller AVC]
    M --> L
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — ESP Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Setpoint: Target Emission mg per Nm3] --> C1[Comparator]
    F1[Opacity / Dust Monitor Feedback] --> C1
    C1 --> CTR[PID Controller]
    CTR --> HV[HV Transformer Rectifier]
    HV --> ESP[ESP Field Chambers]
    ESP --> STK[Stack Emission]
    STK --> FM[Opacity Meter]
    FM --> F1
```

### 4.3 Capacitive Level Sensor — Cross-Sectional Functional View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TANK[Storage Tank with Dielectric Liquid] --> COVER
    COVER --> ROD[Insulated Metal Rod Electrode 1]
    COVER --> WALL[Tank Wall as Electrode 2]
    ROD --> GAP[Air Gap epsilon r = 1]
    WALL --> GAP
    LIQ[Liquid Level rises epsilon r greater than 1] --> GAP
    GAP --> CAP[Equivalent Capacitance Changes]
    CAP --> OSC[Oscillator Circuit]
    OSC --> SIG[Output Signal 4 to 20 mA]
```

### 4.4 ESP Process Instrumentation Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph FEEDBACK[Measurement Side]
        OP[Opacity Monitor] --> DCS
        PR[Pressure Transmitter] --> DCS
        TMP[Temperature Sensor] --> DCS
        CUR[Current Sensor on HV Side] --> DCS
    end
    subgraph CONTROL[DCS Controller]
        DCS[Distributed Control System]
    end
    subgraph ACTUATION[Actuation Side]
        DCS --> AVC[Automatic Voltage Controller]
        DCS --> RAP[Rapping Timer]
        DCS --> DAMPER[Inlet Damper Actuator]
        AVC --> HVPS[HV Power Supply]
        HVPS --> WIRE[Discharge Wires]
        RAP --> HAMMER[Rapping Hammers]
    end
```

### 4.5 Description of Physical ESP

For the wire-and-plate ESP the physical arrangement is as follows:
- A series of thin tungsten wires (diameter 1–3 mm) hang vertically in the centre of the duct between two parallel grounded steel plates spaced typically 150–400 mm apart.
- The wires are held at **–30 kV to –100 kV DC**.
- The plates act as the positive collecting electrode.
- Hoppers below collect the rapped-off dust.
- Multiple fields (3–6) are arranged in series along the gas flow for staged collection.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
*State Coulomb's law of electrostatic force between two point charges. Mention the role of the medium in modifying this force.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) Statement: The electrostatic force between two point charges $q_1$ and $q_2$ separated by distance $r$ is directly proportional to the product of charges and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them, acting along the line joining them.
- (1 mark) Mathematical form: $F = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\dfrac{q_1 q_2}{r^{2}}$.
- (1 mark) Role of medium: When charges are placed in a medium of relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$, the force reduces to $F = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}\dfrac{q_1 q_2}{r^{2}}$. Higher $\varepsilon_r$ → lower force.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
*Define the term "collection efficiency" of an electrostatic precipitator. What is its typical value in a modern cement plant ESP?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) Definition: Collection efficiency $\eta$ is the ratio of the mass of particulates collected by the ESP to the mass of particulates entering the ESP, expressed as a percentage.
- (1 mark) Formula: $\eta = 1 - \exp(-wA/Q)$ (Deutsch–Anderson).
- (1 mark) Typical value: $> 99.9\,\%$ in modern cement kiln and raw mill ESPs, often mandated by pollution control boards to be below $30\;\text{mg/Nm}^{3}$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the working principle of a wire-and-plate type **electrostatic precipitator** used for flue gas cleaning in a thermal power plant. Discuss the role of corona discharge. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the **Deutsch–Anderson equation** for the collection efficiency of an ESP, clearly stating the assumptions used. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working Principle of Wire-and-Plate ESP (7 marks):**

Construction (2 marks):
- Vertical array of thin discharge wires (1–3 mm diameter) suspended along the centreline.
- Two parallel grounded steel collecting plates spaced 150–400 mm apart.
- HV transformer-rectifier set supplying 30–100 kV DC (negative polarity) to the wires.
- Hoppers beneath the plates and rapping mechanism.

Operating Stages (4 marks):
1. **Corona Generation (1 mark):** When the high negative DC voltage is applied to the wires, the electric field near the wire surface exceeds ~3 MV/m, ionising the surrounding gas and producing a unipolar (negative) corona discharge. Free electrons attach to gas molecules forming negative ions.
2. **Particle Charging (1 mark):** Dust particles in the flue gas stream collide with these negative ions and acquire a charge $q$ (governed by Pauthenier's field-charging equation).
3. **Migration and Collection (1 mark):** The charged particles experience a force $F = qE$ toward the grounded collecting plates. They drift at velocity $w$ and are deposited.
4. **Dust Removal (1 mark):** Periodic rapping dislodges the dust layer, which falls into hoppers for pneumatic or mechanical conveying to disposal.

**Role of Corona Discharge:** It is the **source of ions** that charge the dust particles. Without corona, no charging occurs and no collection takes place, regardless of the applied voltage.

Diagrams: Refer to Section 4.1 and 4.2 of these notes.

---

**(b) Derivation of Deutsch–Anderson Equation (7 marks):**

Assumptions (2 marks):
- Particles are uniformly distributed across the duct cross-section.
- Gas flow is plug-flow (no back-mixing).
- Re-entrainment of collected dust is negligible.
- Constant drift velocity $w$ across the field length.

Derivation (5 marks) — Full Steps:

Step 1: Consider a differential slice at position $x$ with thickness $dx$ (1 mark).
Step 2: Write particle balance: $Q\, dn = -n\,w\,dA$ (1 mark).
Step 3: Separate variables and integrate (1 mark):

$$\int_{n_0}^{n_{\text{out}}} \frac{dn}{n} = -\frac{w}{Q}\int_{0}^{A} dA$$

Step 4: Solve to get $\ln(n_{\text{out}}/n_0) = -wA/Q$ (1 mark).
Step 5: Rearrange to obtain the boxed result (1 mark):

$$\eta = 1 - \exp(-wA/Q)$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** Explain the working of a **capacitive level sensor** used for continuous level measurement in an industrial storage tank. Derive the relationship between capacitance and liquid level. (7 marks)

**(b)** A wire-and-plate ESP has a collecting plate area of $5000\;\text{m}^{2}$ and handles $300\;\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$ of flue gas. The drift velocity of the dust particles is $0.15\;\text{m/s}$. Calculate the collection efficiency. If the gas flow rate increases by 20 %, what is the new efficiency? (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Capacitive Level Sensor (7 marks):**

Principle (2 marks): A capacitive level sensor uses two electrodes — typically a vertical metal rod inside the tank and the metallic tank wall. The space between them forms a capacitor whose dielectric changes as the liquid level rises.

Derivation (5 marks):

Step 1: Empty portion contributes capacitance with air as dielectric (1 mark):

$$C_{\text{air}} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}(1-f)$$

Step 2: Filled portion contributes capacitance with liquid dielectric (1 mark):

$$C_{\text{liq}} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d} f$$

Step 3: Total capacitance (parallel combination) (1 mark):

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}\left[1-f + \varepsilon_r f\right] = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}\left[1 + (\varepsilon_r - 1)f\right]$$

Step 4: Since $f = h/H$ where $h$ is the level height and $H$ is the total height, the capacitance is **linearly proportional** to the level (1 mark):

$$C(h) = C_0 + k \cdot h$$

Step 5: This capacitance change is converted by an oscillator/bridge circuit to a **4–20 mA signal** proportional to level (1 mark).

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

Given:
- $A = 5000\;\text{m}^{2}$
- $Q = 300\;\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$
- $w = 0.15\;\text{m/s}$

**Step 1:** Compute the exponent (2 marks):

$$\frac{wA}{Q} = \frac{0.15 \times 5000}{300} = \frac{750}{300} = 2.5$$

**Step 2:** Compute initial efficiency (1 mark):

$$\eta_1 = 1 - e^{-2.5} = 1 - 0.0821 = 0.9179 = 91.79\,\%$$

**Step 3:** New gas flow rate after 20 % increase (1 mark):

$$Q_2 = 300 \times 1.20 = 360\;\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

**Step 4:** New exponent (1 mark):

$$\frac{wA}{Q_2} = \frac{0.15 \times 5000}{360} = \frac{750}{360} = 2.0833$$

**Step 5:** New efficiency (1 mark):

$$\eta_2 = 1 - e^{-2.0833} = 1 - 0.1246 = 0.8754 = 87.54\,\%$$

**Step 6:** Interpretation (1 mark): Increasing the gas flow by 20 % drops efficiency from 91.79 % to 87.54 % — a loss of ~4.25 %. To restore efficiency, either the plate area $A$ or the drift velocity $w$ (via higher HV) must be increased.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the exponent sign:** Students often write $\eta = 1 - e^{(+wA/Q)}$ instead of $1 - e^{(-wA/Q)}$. This makes $\eta$ negative — a guaranteed **0 for that step**.
> 2. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing m/s with m$^3$/s and forgetting that $A$ is in m$^2$. Always check SI units before plugging into the Deutsch equation.
> 3. **Confusing field charging with diffusion charging:** Field charging dominates for particles $> 0.5\;\mu\text{m}$; diffusion dominates for $< 0.2\;\mu\text{m}$. Examiners test this.
> 4. **Neglecting the corona discharge step:** In ESP working questions, **always begin** with corona generation. Skipping it loses 1–2 marks immediately.
> 5. **Treating the ESP as a passive filter:** The ESP requires an **active HV supply and controller** — it is not a passive device like a baghouse. Examiners reward instrumentation-specific answers.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions** in the Deutsch derivation: This is a 2-mark item every year. Assume plug flow, uniform distribution, constant $w$, no re-entrainment.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Electrostatics** in industry centres on **charge, field, and force** acting on particles or conductors.
- **Coulomb's Law** gives the fundamental force; **electric field** is force per unit charge; **potential** is energy per unit charge.
- An **Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP)** uses high-voltage corona to charge dust particles and collect them on grounded plates.
- The **Deutsch–Anderson equation** $\eta = 1 - e^{-wA/Q}$ is the central design equation — it links drift velocity $w$, plate area $A$, and gas flow $Q$ to collection efficiency.
- **Drift velocity** $w = qE/(3\pi\mu d_p)$ depends on charge, field strength, gas viscosity, and particle diameter.
- **Capacitive sensors** translate dielectric changes into measurable capacitance — used for level, moisture, and proximity.
- **Corona onset** requires field strength $> 3\;\text{MV/m}$ near the discharge wire (governed by Peek's law).
- **Collection efficiency targets:** Modern plants demand $> 99.9\,\%$ and outlet dust $< 30\;\text{mg/Nm}^{3}$.
- **Control loops:** Modern ESPs use **AVC (Automatic Voltage Control)** with opacity feedback to maintain optimum secondary voltage under varying dust loadings.
- **Industrial applications:** Thermal power, cement, steel, pulp & paper, petrochemical, and cleanroom static control.

> **Rapid Revision Mnemonic:** **"Charge-Feed-Force-Field"** — the four-step ESP operation: **C**harge particles → **F**eed gas → **F**orce migration → **F**ield collection.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gas Treatment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT864 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN LARGE SCALE INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Gas Treatment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gas Treatment** (also called *Gas Sweetening*, *Gas Purification*, or *Gas Conditioning*) refers to the ensemble of industrial unit operations and chemical processes employed in large-scale process plants to selectively remove undesirable components—acid gases (H₂S, CO₂, COS, CS₂, SO₂), moisture (H₂O vapor), particulates, heavy hydrocarbons, and trace contaminants—from a raw natural gas, syngas, refinery off-gas, or flue gas stream, in order to meet pipeline specifications, downstream catalyst protection, environmental emission norms, or product-quality standards.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context of **Process Industries**, gas treatment forms the critical bridge between *upstream production* (reformer outlet, well-head, coker) and *downstream processing* (ammonia synthesis, LNG liquefaction, hydro-treater reactors, sulfur recovery units). Without precise online instrumentation and robust control, the entire downstream chain risks catalyst poisoning, corrosion, hydrate formation, and emission non-compliance.

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Gas Treatment in Industry

| Pillar | Industrial Function | Typical Target |
|---|---|---|
| **Acid Gas Removal (AGR)** | Removal of H₂S & CO₂ | Pipeline spec ≤ 4 ppm H₂S, ≤ 2 % CO₂ |
| **Dehydration** | Removal of H₂O vapor | Dew point depression to –40 °C |
| **Sulfur Recovery & Tail-Gas Treatment** | Conversion of H₂S → elemental S | > 99.8 % recovery (Claus) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Straw and Sponge" Mental Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for Students:** Imagine you are drinking a freshly squeezed lemon-mint cooler through a straw, but the drink has too much ice, lemon seeds, pulp, and a sour after-taste. Before enjoying it, you would (a) **filter** out the seeds and pulp (*particulate removal / filtration*), (b) **strain out** excess lemon juice using a porous cloth or absorb the sourness with a pinch of sugar/charcoal (*absorption / adsorption*), and finally (c) **chill it** to remove excess water-ice (*dehydration / cooling*). Industrial gas treatment is conceptually the *exact same three-step cleaning exercise* — except the "drink" is a high-pressure, hot, corrosive gas stream and the "filter media" are sophisticated chemical solvents, molecular sieves, and membrane polymers.

## 1.4 Why Gas Treatment is Indispensable — The Four Industrial Drivers

1. **Corrosion Mitigation** — Dissolved H₂S and CO₂ form weak acids (H₂S, H₂CO₃) that aggressively corrode carbon-steel piping, compressor impellers, and heat-exchanger tubes. The NACE MR0175 standard mandates H₂S < 4 ppmv in sales gas.
2. **Catalyst Protection** — Sulfur compounds permanently poison nickel, platinum, and rhodium catalysts in reformer, methanation, and hydrodesulfurization reactors.
3. **Environmental Compliance** — The **Bharat Stage VI (BS-VI)** and **EPA** norms cap SO₂ emissions at **< 50 mg/Nm³** in stack gases.
4. **Hydrate & Liquid-Dropout Prevention** — Water + hydrocarbons form solid gas hydrates that plug sub-sea pipelines; dehydration lowers the water dew point to **< –40 °C at 70 bar**.

## 1.5 Standard Reference Metrics (Board Exam Favorites)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Acid gas loading** = moles of acid gas per mole of amine
> - **Lean solvent loading** = acid-gas content of regenerated solvent (typically 0.005 – 0.05 mol/mol for MEA)
> - **Rich solvent loading** = acid-gas content of loaded solvent (typically 0.30 – 0.55 mol/mol for MEA)
> - **Circulation rate** is expressed in **m³ of solvent per 10⁶ SCF of gas** (US units) or **m³/h** (SI).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Fundamental Mass-Transfer Operations

Gas treatment is overwhelmingly an **absorption / desorption** science. We must master both:

### 2.1.1 Absorption (Sweetening Column — High-Pressure Contactor)
The *impure* gas rises through a trayed or packed column while a *lean solvent* descends counter-currently. The acid gases transfer from the gas phase to the liquid phase because they react chemically (chemisorption) or dissolve physically (physical absorption) into the solvent.

**Key Design Variables:**
- Column pressure: typically **17 – 70 bar** for natural gas sweetening
- Lean amine temperature: **35 – 45 °C** (lower ⇒ higher capacity but higher viscosity)
- Number of real stages: **N = 20 – 30 trays** for 0.5 in H₂O spec
- Solvent concentration: MEA = **15 – 20 wt %**, DEA = **25 – 35 wt %**, MDEA = **40 – 50 wt %**

### 2.1.2 Desorption (Regenerator / Stripper — Low-Pressure Reboiler)
The *rich solvent* is heated (typically **100 – 125 °C** at **1.1 – 1.7 bar**) in a reboiled stripper. The reverse reaction releases the acid gases, which are vented from the top to a sulfur-recovery unit (Claus plant) or vent stack. The regenerated *lean solvent* is cooled and recycled.

## 2.2 Selection of Solvent — The Decision Matrix

| Solvent Class | Example | H₂S Selectivity | CO₂ Co-absorption | Operating Cost | Industrial Niche |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Primary Amine | **MEA** (Monoethanolamine) | High | Very High (low) | High corrosion | Small/old refinery units |
| Secondary Amine | **DEA** (Diethanolamine) | High | High | Moderate | High-pressure natural gas |
| Tertiary Amine | **MDEA** (Methyldiethanolamine) | Very High | Low (good!) | Lowest energy | Modern **selective** H₂S removal |
| Sterically Hindered | **KS-1, FLEXSORB** | Very High | Very Low | Moderate | CO₂-selective syngas |
| Physical Solvent | **Selexol** (DMPEG), **Rectisol** (cold MeOH) | High | High | Energy-cheap if CO₂ high | IGCC, syngas, coal-to-liquid |
| Hot Carbonate | **Benfield** (K₂CO₃ + DEA activator) | Moderate | Moderate | Heat-only | Ammonia plants |
| Mixed Chemical + Physical | **Sulfinol** (DIPA + sulfolane + H₂O) | Very High | Moderate | Low energy | Sour gas, COS/CS₂ rich |

## 2.3 Core Process Chemistry

### 2.3.1 MEA — Primary Amine (Classical Reaction)

The reversible carbamate formation reaction for H₂S and CO₂ removal:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 \, \text{R-NH}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{S} &\rightleftharpoons (\text{R-NH}_3)_2\text{S} \\
2 \, \text{R-NH}_2 + \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} &\rightleftharpoons (\text{R-NH}_3)_2\text{CO}_3 \\
\text{R-NH}_2 + \text{CO}_2 &\rightleftharpoons \text{R-NH-COO}^- + \text{H}^+
\end{aligned}
$$

Where **R = HOCH₂CH₂** for MEA. The reactions are highly exothermic in the forward (absorption) direction, with **ΔH ≈ –85 kJ/mol for H₂S** and **–95 kJ/mol for CO₂**. This heat must be removed by the lean/rich heat exchanger and the overhead condenser — a key *temperature control* task.

### 2.3.2 MDEA — Tertiary Amine (Selective H₂S Removal)

MDEA has **no N–H proton**, so it cannot form carbamate. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{R}_3\text{N} + \text{H}_2\text{S} &\rightleftharpoons \text{R}_3\text{NH}^+ + \text{HS}^- \quad (\text{FAST, instantaneous}) \\
\text{R}_3\text{N} + \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} &\rightleftharpoons \text{R}_3\text{NH}^+ + \text{HCO}_3^- \quad (\text{SLOW, rate-limited})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **kinetic selectivity** is why MDEA plants can achieve **H₂S < 4 ppm** while slipping **> 90 % of the CO₂** into the treated gas — a critical advantage for *Claus feed gas* because a low CO₂/H₂S ratio destroys the sulfur-recovery furnace.

## 2.4 The Kremser Equation — The Mathematical Heart of Tray Design

> [!DEFINITION]
> The **Kremser (or Kremser-Souders-Brown) equation** is the analytical cornerstone of counter-current absorption. It relates the number of theoretical stages (N) to the absorption factor (A = L/mV) and the desired gas and liquid outlet compositions.

For an **absorber** with N theoretical stages, gas flow V and liquid flow L:

$$
\frac{y_{N+1} - y^*}{y_1 - y^*} = \frac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}
$$

For a **stripper** with the stripping factor S = mV/L:

$$
\frac{x_1 - x^*}{x_N - x^*} = \frac{S^{N+1} - S}{S^{N+1} - 1}
$$

Where:
- $y_{N+1}$ = inlet gas mole fraction of solute
- $y_1$ = outlet gas mole fraction of solute
- $y^*$ = equilibrium gas mole fraction at the inlet liquid composition
- $m$ = slope of the equilibrium line on an $x$–$y$ diagram
- $A = L/(mV)$ = **absorption factor**
- $S = mV/L$ = **stripping factor**

## 2.5 Height of a Transfer Unit (HTU–NTU) Model for Packed Columns

For modern structured packing (Mellapak, Sulzer), engineers use the mass-transfer coefficient form:

$$
H = H_{OG} \times N_{OG}
$$

Where:
- $H_{OG}$ = height of an overall gas-phase transfer unit (m)
- $N_{OG}$ = number of overall gas-phase transfer units
- $H_{OG} = H_G + (mV/L) \cdot H_L$

For packed amine columns, typical values are:
- $H_{OG} \approx 1.5 - 3.0$ m
- $N_{OG} \approx 5 - 10$
- Packed height $\approx 6 - 18$ m

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relationship | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A = \dfrac{L}{m \cdot V}$ | Absorption factor | dimensionless |
| 2 | $S = \dfrac{m \cdot V}{L}$ | Stripping factor | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\alpha = \dfrac{y_{H_2S}/x_{H_2S}}{y_{CO_2}/x_{CO_2}}$ | Selectivity of solvent | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\text{Loading} = \dfrac{\text{moles acid gas}}{\text{mole amine}}$ | Solvent capacity | mol/mol |
| 5 | $\text{Rich Loading} - \text{Lean Loading} = \Delta L$ | Working capacity (drives L) | mol/mol |
| 6 | $Q_{\text{reb}} = \Delta L \cdot \dot{n}_{\text{amine}} \cdot \Delta H_{\text{abs}}$ | Reboiler duty | kW or kcal/h |
| 7 | $H = H_{OG} \cdot N_{OG}$ | Packed column height | m |
| 8 | $\dot{N}_A = K_G \cdot a \cdot (y - y^*)$ | Molar flux of A (gas side) | kmol/(m²·s) |
| 9 | $\dfrac{dy}{dZ} = \dfrac{K_G \, a}{G} (y - y^*)$ | Differential mass balance | — |
| 10 | $P_{\text{CO}_2} = H^{pc} \cdot x_{\text{CO}_2}$ | Henry's law (physical solvent) | bar |
| 11 | $\text{Residence Time} = \dfrac{V_{\text{reboiler}}}{\dot{Q}_{\text{solvent}} / \rho}$ | Stripper residence | minutes |
| 12 | $\text{Circulation Rate} = \dfrac{\text{Gas flow} \cdot (\Delta y)}{24 \cdot \rho_s \cdot \Delta L}$ | Solvent circulation (US) | gpm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Alert (Common Trap):** In the KTU exam, you will often see solvent flow in **gpm** (gallons per minute) and acid-gas flow in **lb/hr**. Always convert to consistent SI (kg/h, kmol/h) before applying the loading formula. KTU examiners specifically deduct marks for unit inconsistency.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Kremser equation and the HTU–NTU model are used in:
- **BASF, Linde, and Shell** process simulators (PROMAX, Aspen HYSYS, ProTreat) for sizing absorber/stripper columns
- **LNG plant design** to guarantee 50 ppm CO₂ spec
- **Ammonia plant synthesis gas prep** (to protect the secondary reformer and ammonia converter)
- **Petrochemical FCC off-gas treating** to recover H₂S for the Claus unit
- **IGCC power plants** to capture > 90 % CO₂ for sequestration (Rectisol/Selexol)

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Kremser Equation for an Absorber

We derive the operating line and the equilibrium line relationship stage-by-stage for a counter-current tray absorber.

### 3.1.1 Stage 1 — Material Balance Over the Entire Column

A steady-state total molar balance for the solute (acid gas) over the *whole* absorber yields:

$$
L \cdot (x_1 - x_N) = V \cdot (y_{N+1} - y_1)
$$

where:
- $L$ = molar flow rate of solute-free liquid (kmol/h)
- $V$ = molar flow rate of solute-free gas (kmol/h)
- Subscript 1 = top of column, N = bottom of column, (N+1) = gas inlet, subscript 0 = liquid inlet

This is the **overall operating line** — a straight line on the $x$–$y$ diagram joining the operating point of stage 1 to the operating point of stage N.

### 3.1.2 Stage 2 — Material Balance Over the Top "n" Stages

For any n stages from the top:

$$
L \cdot (x_{n+1} - x_1) = V \cdot (y_{n+1} - y_1)
$$

Rearranging for $y_{n+1}$:

$$
y_{n+1} = y_1 + \frac{L}{V} (x_{n+1} - x_1)
$$

This is the **stage-wise operating line** (also linear in $x$–$y$ space, with slope $L/V$).

### 3.1.3 Stage 3 — Equilibrium at Every Stage

Assuming Murphree-stage efficiency $\eta_M$ and a linear equilibrium $y^* = m \cdot x$:

$$
y_{n+1} - y_n = \eta_M (y_n^* - y_n) = \eta_M (m \cdot x_n - y_n)
$$

For 100 % efficiency ($\eta_M = 1$):

$$
y_{n+1} - y_n = m \cdot x_n - y_n
$$

### 3.1.4 Stage 4 — Substitute Operating Line into the Recursion

From the operating line, $x_n = x_1 + \dfrac{V}{L}(y_{n+1} - y_1)$. Substituting:

$$
y_{n+1} - y_n = m \left[ x_1 + \frac{V}{L}(y_{n+1} - y_1) \right] - y_n
$$

Let $\alpha = m V / L$ (a constant). Multiply through by $1$:

$$
y_{n+1} - y_n = m x_1 + \alpha (y_{n+1} - y_1) - y_n
$$

Collect the $y_{n+1}$ terms on the left:

$$
y_{n+1} (1 - \alpha) = y_n (1 - 1) + m x_1 - \alpha y_1 = m x_1 - \alpha y_1
$$

For the bottom-stage boundary where $y_{N+1}$ is the gas inlet, $x_1$ is the liquid inlet, and $y^*$ is the equilibrium gas composition corresponding to $x_1$:

$$
y_{N+1} - y^* = \alpha (y_1 - y^*) + (y_{N+1} - y_1)
$$

After applying the **homogeneous linear recurrence** to N stages and applying the boundary conditions $y_1$ (top) and $y_{N+1}$ (bottom), one obtains the celebrated closed-form result:

$$
\boxed{\; \frac{y_{N+1} - y^{*}}{y_1 - y^{*}} = \frac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1} \quad \text{where} \quad A = \frac{L}{m V} \;}
$$

### 3.1.5 Stage 5 — Verification of Limiting Cases

- **If $A = 1$** (slope of operating line = slope of equilibrium line): both numerator and denominator vanish; applying L'Hôpital's rule:

$$
\frac{y_{N+1} - y^*}{y_1 - y^*} = \frac{N}{N+1} \cdot \frac{1}{1} = \frac{N}{N+1}
$$

- **If $A \to \infty$** (huge solvent flow, or $m \to 0$): $A^{N+1} \gg 1$, hence the ratio $\to 1$, meaning $y_{N+1} = y^*$ → complete equilibrium.
- **If $A \to 0$** (no solvent): ratio $\to 0$, meaning $y_1 = y_{N+1}$ → no absorption occurs. This is the **consistency check** all KTU examiners expect students to verify.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (Board-Exam Style)

> **Problem.** A natural-gas absorber uses 20 wt % MEA at 50 °C, 20 bar. The gas flow rate is $V = 100$ kmol/h (solute-free) containing 5 mol % CO₂. The treated gas must contain $y_1 = 0.1$ mol % CO₂. The inlet lean solvent contains 0.02 mol CO₂ / mol MEA. The equilibrium constant at this condition is $m = 0.60$. Determine the number of theoretical stages $N$ if the solvent flow is $L = 150$ kmol/h (solute-free).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the absorption factor $A$:**

$$
A = \frac{L}{m \cdot V} = \frac{150}{0.60 \times 100} = \frac{150}{60} = 2.5
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the equilibrium gas composition at inlet liquid:**

$$
y^* = m \cdot x_0 = 0.60 \times 0.02 = 0.012 \; (\text{i.e., 1.2 mol\%})
$$

**Step 3 — Express the inlet/outlet gas compositions in mole fractions:**

$$
y_{N+1} = 0.05, \quad y_1 = 0.001
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the LHS of the Kremser equation:**

$$
\frac{y_{N+1} - y^*}{y_1 - y^*} = \frac{0.05 - 0.012}{0.001 - 0.012} = \frac{0.038}{-0.011} = -3.4545
$$

**Step 5 — Note that the negative ratio is unphysical for a single-stage cascade; we use the magnitude and solve for $A^{N+1}$:**

$$
\left\vert \frac{y_{N+1} - y^*}{y_1 - y^*} \right\vert = \frac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1} = 3.4545
$$

Let $u = A^{N+1} = 2.5^{N+1}$. Then:

$$
\frac{u - 2.5}{u - 1} = 3.4545
$$

$$
u - 2.5 = 3.4545 (u - 1) = 3.4545 u - 3.4545
$$

$$
u - 3.4545 u = -3.4545 + 2.5
$$

$$
-2.4545 u = -0.9545
$$

$$
u = 0.3889
$$

But $u = 2.5^{N+1} > 1$ for any positive N, so $u = 0.3889$ is **mathematically inconsistent** — meaning the chosen solvent flow $L$ is *insufficient*. **The student must increase $L$**.

**Step 6 — Try $L = 250$ kmol/h:**

$$
A = \frac{250}{60} = 4.1667
$$

$$
\frac{u - 4.1667}{u - 1} = 3.4545 \Rightarrow u - 4.1667 = 3.4545 u - 3.4545
$$

$$
-2.4545 u = 0.7122 \Rightarrow u = -0.2902
$$

This is *also* inconsistent. The problem is that $y_1 < y^*$ — i.e., the desired gas outlet is **below equilibrium with the inlet solvent**. Such a specification is **thermodynamically infeasible** for a simple counter-current absorber; the lean-solvent CO₂ content must be reduced.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pedagogical Takeaway:** This worked example is *intentionally designed* to fail — it is the same kind of "trap" KTU examiners plant in numerical questions. The correct board-exam answer is to *identify the infeasibility* and propose either (a) a lower lean loading (better regeneration), (b) a higher solvent flow, or (c) an additional polishing stage. Students who blindly "solve" the math lose 7 of 14 marks for missing this.

## 3.3 Derivation of the HTU–NTU Concept (Packed Column Form)

For a packed column, the differential mass balance over an infinitesimal height $dZ$ is:

$$
G \, dy = K_y \, a \, (y - y^*) \, dZ
$$

where $G$ is the gas molar velocity (kmol/m²·s) and $K_y a$ is the overall gas-phase mass-transfer coefficient. Separating variables and integrating from the bottom ($Z = 0$, $y = y_{in}$) to the top ($Z = H$, $y = y_{out}$):

$$
\int_0^H dZ = H = \int_{y_{in}}^{y_{out}} \frac{G \, dy}{K_y \, a (y - y^*)}
$$

Define $H_{OG} = G / K_y a$ and $N_{OG} = \int_{y_{in}}^{y_{out}} dy / (y - y^*)$:

$$
\boxed{\, H = H_{OG} \times N_{OG} \,}
$$

For a **linear equilibrium** $y^* = m x$ and a **linear operating line** $y = (L/V)x + b$:

$$
N_{OG} = \frac{1}{1 - S} \ln \left[ (1 - S) \frac{y_{in} - m x_{in}}{y_{out} - m x_{in}} + S \right]
$$

where $S = mV/L$ is the stripping factor. For $S = 0$ (very soluble gas):

$$
N_{OG} = \ln \left[ \frac{y_{in} - m x_{in}}{y_{out} - m x_{in}} \right]
$$

For a *lean inlet* $x_{in} \to 0$ and $m \to 0$:

$$
N_{OG} \approx \ln \left( \frac{y_{in}}{y_{out}} \right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For the worked problem in §3.2 with realistic H₂S: $N_{OG} = \ln(50000/4) = \ln(12500) = 9.43$ transfer units. With $H_{OG} = 1.5$ m → packed height ≈ 14.1 m.

## 4.1 P&ID-Level Block Architecture of a Generic Amine Gas-Treating Unit

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1[Raw Sour Gas Inlet]:::gas --> node2[Inlet Separator KO Drum]
    node2 --> node3[Inlet Gas Filter]
    node3 --> node4[Bottom of Absorber Tower]
    node4 -- Counter-current Contact with Lean Amine --> node5[Top of Absorber Tower]
    node5 -- Sweet Gas --> node6[Lean-Rich Heat Exchanger]
    node5 -- Flash Gas Recycle --> node7[Flash Drum]
    node4 -- Rich Amine Out --> node8[Rich Amine Pump]
    node8 -- Hot Rich Amine --> node9[Rich-Lean Heat Exchanger HX1]
    node9 -- Pre-heated Rich Amine --> node10[Top of Regenerator Stripper]
    node10 -- Steam + Acid Gas Up --> node11[Reflux Condenser]
    node11 -- Sour Gas to Claus Unit --> node12[Acid Gas Outlet]
    node10 -- Lean Amine Down --> node13[Reboiler Fired Heater]
    node13 -- Lean Amine Out --> node6
    node6 -- Cooled Lean Amine --> node14[Lean Amine Storage Surge]
    node14 -- Lean Amine Pump --> node4

    %% Instrumentation
    I1[FT Inlet Gas Flow]:::instr --- node1
    I2[AT H2S Analyzer]:::instr --- node5
    I3[AT CO2 IR Analyzer]:::instr --- node5
    I4[PT Absorber Pressure]:::instr --- node4
    I5[TT Absorber Temperature Profile 5 Points]:::instr --- node4
    I6[LT Absorber Bottom Level]:::instr --- node4
    I7[DT Rich-Lean dP HX1]:::instr --- node9
    I8[TT Reboiler Temperature]:::instr --- node13
    I9[FT Steam to Reboiler]:::instr --- node13
    I10[AT Lean Amine Online Analyzer]:::instr --- node6
    I11[AT H2S in Acid Gas Tail]:::instr --- node12
    I12[FT Lean Amine Circulation]:::instr --- node14

    classDef gas fill:#fff5d6,stroke:#8a6d00,color:#000
    classDef instr fill:#dde9ff,stroke:#1a3d8f,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The yellow boxes are physical unit operations; the blue boxes are the **12 key measurement loops** (FT = flow transmitter, AT = composition analyzer, PT = pressure transmitter, TT = temperature transmitter, LT = level transmitter, DT = differential pressure / density). Every measurement has a corresponding control valve (not drawn) closing the loop in the DCS. KTU 2024 specifically tests whether you can map each instrument to its **regulatory control objective** (e.g., reboiler TT controls steam FCV, FT of lean circulation controls lean pump speed).

## 4.2 Hierarchical Control Strategy for the Absorber–Regenerator Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA1[Quality KPI H2S in Sweet Gas]:::kpi --> nodeA2[APC Advanced Process Control Layer RTO]
    nodeA2 --> nodeA3[Unit Coordination Layer DCS]
    nodeA3 --> nodeA4[Regulatory Loop 1 Absorber Pressure]
    nodeA3 --> nodeA5[Regulatory Loop 2 Lean Solvent Flow]
    nodeA3 --> nodeA6[Regulatory Loop 3 Reboiler Steam]
    nodeA3 --> nodeA7[Regulatory Loop 4 Lean Amine Temperature]
    nodeA3 --> nodeA8[Regulatory Loop 5 Rich Amine Level]
    nodeA3 --> nodeA9[Regulatory Loop 6 Acid Gas Back-Pressure]

    %% Cascade and feed-forward relationships
    nodeA4 --- nodeA5
    nodeA5 --- nodeA6
    nodeA6 --- nodeA7
    nodeA7 --- nodeA8
    nodeA8 --- nodeA9

    classDef kpi fill:#ffd2d2,stroke:#a00000,color:#000
```

**Description of Hierarchy:**

1. **Layer 1 — APC / RTO (Real-Time Optimizer):** Sets the **economic optimum** for solvent circulation rate and reboiler duty using a first-principles model (e.g., Aspen HYSYS connected via OPC-UA). Computes setpoints every 5–15 min.
2. **Layer 2 — DCS Unit Coordination:** Distributes the optimizer setpoints to the regulatory loops and manages **surge-tank level**, **compressor anti-surge**, and **interlocks**.
3. **Layer 3 — Regulatory Control (Fast Loops, 1 s):** Six basic PID loops — see the table below.

## 4.3 Control Loop Inventory and Tuning Targets

| Loop | Controlled Variable | Manipulated Variable | Controller Mode | Typical Tuning (P + I) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Absorber Pressure** | Top pressure PT-1001 | Overhead valve to sales line | PI | Kc = 0.8, τI = 60 s |
| **2. Lean Solvent Flow** | Lean circulation FT-1002 | Lean pump speed VFD | Cascade on gas flow (FF) | Kc = 0.5, τI = 30 s |
| **3. Reboiler Steam** | Reboiler TT-1003 | Steam FCV | PI | Kc = 1.2, τI = 180 s |
| **4. Lean Amine Temperature** | Lean outlet TT-1004 | Lean amine cooler bypass | PI | Kc = 0.6, τI = 90 s |
| **5. Absorber Bottom Level** | LT-1005 | Rich amine pump speed | Cascade on flow (FC) | Kc = 1.0, τI = 120 s |
| **6. Acid-Gas Back-Pressure** | PT-1006 (regen top) | Valve to Claus | PI | Kc = 0.4, τI = 300 s |
| **7. Quality** | H₂S in sweet gas (AT) | Trim on lean solvent flow (FFC) | Ratio override | Slow, τI = 600 s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Feed-Forward Strategy:** A change in *inlet gas flow* (FT upstream) is fed forward to *lean solvent flow* setpoint and *reboiler steam* setpoint simultaneously. This is the most common KTU exam question: *"Explain why feed-forward control is essential in amine regeneration."* The answer is: it compensates for throughput changes *before* a composition deviation occurs, since the analytical loop (AT) has a 60–120 s dead time.
