# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Define the term *lean solvent loading* and *rich solvent loading* in an amine gas-treating unit. Why is the *difference* between the two a critical design parameter?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- *Lean solvent loading* is the moles of acid gas (H₂S + CO₂) remaining per mole of amine in the regenerated (lean) solvent leaving the stripper bottom. It is typically **0.005 – 0.05 mol/mol**.
- *Rich solvent loading* is the moles of acid gas per mole of amine in the loaded solvent leaving the absorber bottom, typically **0.30 – 0.55 mol/mol** for MEA.
- The *difference* $\Delta L = L_{\text{rich}} - L_{\text{lean}}$ is the *working capacity*. It directly determines the **solvent circulation rate** $L = \dot{n}_{\text{acid gas}} / \Delta L$ and hence the **pump and pipe sizing** and the **reboiler duty**. **Higher ΔL = lower operating cost.** *(1 mark for each definition, 1 mark for the working-capacity insight.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **List any three on-line composition analyzers used in a gas-treating plant and state what each one measures.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Lead-Acetate Tape Analyzer (AT-1007)** — measures H₂S concentration in treated gas in the **ppb range** using a colorimetric reaction. *(1 mark)*
2. **Non-Dispersive Infrared (NDIR) Analyzer** — measures CO₂ (and CH₄) in sweet gas and in absorber overhead; range 0 – 5000 ppm. *(1 mark)*
3. **Gas Chromatograph (GC)** — full multi-component analysis of feed, sweet gas, and acid-gas stream every 3 – 10 min; reports H₂S, CO₂, COS, CS₂, mercaptans, and hydrocarbons. *(1 mark)*

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

### ➤ Question A (14 Marks)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**
> **(a)** Derive the Kremser equation for a counter-current gas absorber with N theoretical stages, clearly defining the absorption factor $A$ and stating all assumptions. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A 20 wt % MEA absorber operates at 50 °C and 20 bar, treating $V = 100$ kmol/h of natural gas containing 5 mol % CO₂. The required treated-gas CO₂ is 0.1 mol %, the lean solvent loading is 0.02 mol/mol, and $m = 0.60$ at operating conditions. Determine (i) the minimum solvent flow rate $L_{\text{min}}$ and (ii) the actual solvent flow rate if you choose $L = 1.4 L_{\text{min}}$. Then find the number of theoretical stages required. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**
Write the four-step derivation exactly as in §3.1.1 – §3.1.5 of these notes:
1. Overall material balance to get the operating line.
2. Stage-wise material balance.
3. Equilibrium relation $y^* = m x$.
4. Combine to get the homogeneous recurrence and apply boundary conditions.
5. Final closed form: $\dfrac{y_{N+1} - y^*}{y_1 - y^*} = \dfrac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}$ with $A = L/(mV)$.

Mention all four assumptions: (i) isothermal, isobaric column, (ii) constant L and V, (iii) linear equilibrium (Henry's law region), (iv) Murphree efficiency = 1 (theoretical stages). Verify the two limiting cases ($A = 1$ and $A \to \infty$).

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute $y^*$ at the inlet liquid loading:**

$$
y^* = m \cdot x_0 = 0.60 \times 0.02 = 0.012
$$

**Step 2 — Find $L_{\text{min}}$.** At the *minimum solvent flow*, the operating line and equilibrium line intersect at the top of the column. At this pinch, $A_{\text{min}} = L_{\text{min}}/(mV)$ and the operating line is tangent to the equilibrium. Approximating the pinch condition $y_1 = y^*$ (operating point at top is on the equilibrium curve) is unphysical here because $y_1 < y^*$, so we use the **mass balance** form of $L_{\text{min}}$:

$$
L_{\text{min}} = \frac{V (y_{N+1} - y_1)}{x_{1,\text{max}} - x_0}
$$

The maximum liquid composition at the bottom is the *equilibrium* value corresponding to $y_{N+1}$:

$$
x_{1,\text{max}} = \frac{y_{N+1}}{m} = \frac{0.05}{0.60} = 0.0833 \; \text{mol/mol}
$$

Therefore:

$$
L_{\text{min}} = \frac{100 \times (0.05 - 0.001)}{0.0833 - 0.02} = \frac{4.9}{0.0633} = 77.4 \; \text{kmol/h}
$$

**Step 3 — Actual L:**

$$
L = 1.4 \times 77.4 = 108.4 \; \text{kmol/h}
$$

**Step 4 — Absorption factor:**

$$
A = \frac{L}{mV} = \frac{108.4}{0.60 \times 100} = 1.8067
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Kremser:**

$$
\frac{y_{N+1} - y^*}{y_1 - y^*} = \frac{0.05 - 0.012}{0.001 - 0.012} = \frac{0.038}{-0.011} = -3.4545
$$

Magnitude ratio = 3.4545. Solve:

$$
\frac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1} = 3.4545
$$

Let $u = A^{N+1}$:

$$
\frac{u - 1.8067}{u - 1} = 3.4545
$$

$$
u - 1.8067 = 3.4545 u - 3.4545
$$

$$
-2.4545 u = -1.6478 \Rightarrow u = 0.6713
$$

Again $u < 1$ — **infeasible at the chosen operating point** because the desired $y_1$ is *below* the equilibrium gas composition corresponding to the inlet liquid.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Valuation Warning):** The first time you compute $u = 0.6713$, you may be tempted to take $\log_{A}$ and report $N+1 = \log(0.6713)/\log(1.8067) = -0.398 / 0.589 = -0.676$, which gives a *negative* N. **This is the trap.** The correct conclusion is: **either increase L, decrease lean loading, or split the column.** In the KTU valuation key, students who blindly log the magnitude **lose 5 of 7 marks**. State the infeasibility, then re-solve for the *achievable* specification.

**Step 6 — Achievable specification at $L = 108.4$ kmol/h:**

If we accept $y_1 = 0.5$ mol % (a more realistic value, since 0.1 mol % is below the achievable equilibrium):

$$
\frac{0.05 - 0.012}{0.005 - 0.012} = \frac{0.038}{-0.007} = -5.4286
$$

$u = 0.7274$, still $< 1$ → still infeasible. So the solvent concentration must be increased.

> [!NOTE]
> The "trick" in the question is that the examiner wants you to demonstrate **engineering judgment** about infeasibility, not just arithmetic. In the KTU answer key, the expected final statement is: *"Since $y_1 < y^*$, the design point is infeasible for the given L. To achieve 0.1 mol % CO₂ in the treated gas, the lean-solvent loading must be reduced to $x_0 \le y_1/m = 0.00167$ mol/mol, requiring a deeper regeneration in the stripper."*

### ➤ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative

> **Q4. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]**
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **complete P&ID-level instrumentation scheme** for a MDEA-based selective H₂S removal unit, identifying at least 10 measurement loops and 4 advanced control strategies. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A hot potassium carbonate (Benfield) unit in an ammonia plant processes $1.2 \times 10^5$ Nm³/h of reformed gas containing 18 mol % CO₂ at 30 bar and 110 °C. The lean K₂CO₃ solution has 30 wt % K₂CO₃, the rich solution is loaded to 0.85 mol CO₂ / mol K₂CO₃, and the lean is stripped to 0.20 mol/mol. Estimate (i) the K₂CO₃ solution circulation rate in m³/h, (ii) the heat duty in the reboiler if $\Delta H_{rxn} = -90$ kJ/mol CO₂, and (iii) the packed-section height if $H_{OG} = 1.8$ m. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (b) — Numerical Approach:**

**Given data:**
- $V = 1.2 \times 10^5$ Nm³/h at NTP; at 30 bar and 110 °C, the actual volumetric flow is
$$
V_{\text{actual}} = 1.2 \times 10^5 \times \frac{110 + 273}{273} \times \frac{1.013}{30} = 5.30 \times 10^3 \; \text{Nm}^3/\text{h actual}
$$
- Molar flow of gas: $\dot{n}_{\text{gas}} = 1.2 \times 10^5 / 22.4 = 5357$ kmol/h
- CO₂ removed: $\dot{n}_{CO_2} = 0.18 \times 5357 = 964.3$ kmol/h

**Step (i) — K₂CO₃ solution circulation rate:**

Working capacity $\Delta L = 0.85 - 0.20 = 0.65$ mol CO₂ / mol K₂CO₃.

K₂CO₃ molar flow needed: $\dot{n}_{K_2CO_3} = 964.3 / 0.65 = 1483.5$ kmol/h.

Molar mass of K₂CO₃ = 138.21 g/mol. Mass flow = $1483.5 \times 138.21 = 205.0 \times 10^3$ kg/h = 205 t/h.

Solution is 30 wt % K₂CO₃ → total solution = $205.0 / 0.30 = 683.3$ t/h.

Density of 30 % K₂CO₃ solution at 110 °C ≈ 1280 kg/m³. So:

$$
\dot{V}_{\text{solution}} = \frac{683.3 \times 10^3}{1280} = 533.8 \; \text{m}^3/\text{h}
$$

**Step (ii) — Reboiler heat duty:**

$$
Q = \dot{n}_{CO_2} \times \Delta H_{rxn} = 964.3 \times 90 = 86.79 \times 10^3 \; \text{kW} = 86.8 \; \text{MW}
$$

*(Note: The reaction is exothermic in absorption, so desorption requires 86.8 MW of heat input.)*

**Step (iii) — Packed height:**

Use the simple log-mean driving-force form. Assume the gas CO₂ goes from 18 % to 0.5 % (typical Benfield spec) and the liquid $x$ ranges from 0.20 to 0.85:

- $y_{\text{in}} = 0.18$, $y_{\text{out}} = 0.005$
- $x_{\text{in}} = 0.20$, $x_{\text{out}} = 0.85$

With $m \approx 1.5$ at 30 bar for K₂CO₃ (typical value):

$$
N_{OG} = \ln \left[ \frac{y_{\text{in}} - m x_{\text{in}}}{y_{\text{out}} - m x_{\text{in}}} \right] = \ln \left[ \frac{0.18 - 0.30}{0.005 - 0.30} \right]
$$

Since $m x_{\text{in}} = 1.5 \times 0.20 = 0.30$ which exceeds $y_{\text{in}} = 0.18$ → the gas is *undersaturated* at the top and the absorption is mass-transfer-limited. Re-derive using $N_{OG}$ in a *non-linear* form is more rigorous, but for board purposes:

$$
N_{OG} \approx \ln \left( \frac{0.18}{0.005} \right) = \ln(36) = 3.58
$$

(using the lean-solvent approximation $m x_{\text{in}} \to 0$).

Therefore packed height $H = H_{OG} \times N_{OG} = 1.8 \times 3.58 = 6.45$ m → design with **7 m of packing** plus 1.5 m of distributor / demister space = **8.5 m total column height**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall in Benfield Calculations:** KTU examiners consistently deduct 2 marks if students use **wt % K₂CO₃ in mass flow** but then forget to convert to **mol CO₂ / mol K₂CO₃** for the loading. Always work in molar units for the loading formula.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Board Exam:**

- ☐ **Gas treatment** = bulk removal of acid gases (H₂S, CO₂) + dehydration + trace contaminant removal.
- ☐ **Absorption** uses counter-current contact; **desorption** uses heat + stripping steam.
- ☐ **Kremser equation** (most-tested formula): $\dfrac{y_{N+1} - y^*}{y_1 - y^*} = \dfrac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}$, with $A = L/(mV)$.
- ☐ **HTU–NTU** model for packed columns: $H = H_{OG} \cdot N_{OG}$.
- ☐ **Working capacity** $\Delta L = L_{\text{rich}} - L_{\text{lean}}$ drives the circulation rate and reboiler duty.
- ☐ **MEA** is generic; **DEA** is for high pressure; **MDEA** is *kinetically selective* for H₂S; **Selexol/Rectisol** are physical; **Sulfinol** is hybrid.
- ☐ **MDEA's selectivity** comes from its tertiary amine structure: it reacts with H₂S *fast*, with CO₂ *slow*.
- ☐ **Instrumentation** must include: FT (gas + solvent), PT (absorber + regenerator), TT (5-point profile + reboiler), AT (H₂S + CO₂ + GC), LT (rich solvent surge), DT (heat-exchanger dP).
- ☐ **Control hierarchy:** APC (RTO) → DCS coordination → 6 fast regulatory loops → safety interlocks.
- ☐ **Feed-forward** from inlet gas flow to lean solvent and steam setpoints is the most common advanced strategy — compensates for the 60–120 s analyzer dead time.
- ☐ **Infeasibility trap:** if the desired $y_1 < y^* = m x_0$, the design is thermodynamically infeasible; regenerate deeper.
- ☐ **Standard specs to memorize:** H₂S ≤ 4 ppmv (NACE), CO₂ ≤ 2 mol % (pipeline), water dew point < –40 °C at delivery pressure.
- ☐ **Energy benchmark:** modern MDEA plants need 1.8 – 2.5 GJ per tonne of CO₂ removed; Rectisol needs 2.5 – 3.5 GJ/t.
- ☐ **Safety instrumentation:** H₂S detectors (electrochemical), flame detectors on the Claus furnace, ESD-1 on the regenerator overhead, LEL detectors in the flash drum.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Exchanger
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT864 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN LARGE SCALE INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Heat Exchanger

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Heat Exchanger — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat Exchanger (HX):** A thermal process equipment designed to transfer heat efficiently between two or more fluids that are at different temperatures and separated by a solid wall, without allowing direct mixing of the fluids. The governing relationship is the fundamental heat-transfer rate equation:
>
> $$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_m$$
>
> where **Q** is the rate of heat transfer (**W** or **kW**), **U** is the overall heat transfer coefficient (**W/m²·K**), **A** is the effective heat transfer area (**m²**), and **ΔTₘ** is the appropriate mean temperature difference (**K** or **°C**).

In large-scale industries (petrochemical, power, fertilizer, refineries, HVAC), heat exchangers represent the **single largest capital and energy-recovery investment** on the plant side. Proper sizing, instrumentation (RTDs, thermocouples, flow transmitters, differential pressure cells), and control (bypass valves, TIC loops) are central to plant thermal efficiency.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Focus:** The course PEICT864 emphasizes the *instrumentation and control* viewpoint — meaning students must understand (i) how heat exchangers behave thermally, (ii) what variables are measured, and (iii) how feedback/feedforward control strategies are designed around them.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Think of a heat exchanger like **two people holding hands on a cold day, where one has warm hands and the other has cold hands**.

- The **warm hands** (hot fluid) transfer heat *through* the touching surface (the wall/tube) to the **cold hands** (cold fluid).
- The hands never actually mix — only heat crosses the boundary.
- The faster the contact (more surface area, better conductivity, higher turbulence), the faster heat moves across.
- If both hands eventually reach the *same* temperature, heat transfer stops — that is **thermal equilibrium**.

In an industrial sense, the "handshake" is replaced by metal tubes, plates, or shells engineered to maximize this controlled heat flow.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Typical Industrial Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heat duty | Q | kW or MW | 10 kW – 200 MW |
| Overall heat transfer coefficient | U | W/m²·K | 50 (gases) – 10,000 (condensing steam) |
| Heat transfer area | A | m² | 1 – 5,000 |
| LMTD | ΔTₘ | K | 5 – 200 |
| Effectiveness | ε | dimensionless | 0 – 1 |
| Fouling factor | R_f | m²·K/W | 0.0001 – 0.001 |

> [!NOTE]
> The **overall heat transfer coefficient U** is essentially a "quality grade" of the exchanger — the higher the U, the more efficient the heat transfer for a given area.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Heat Exchangers (KTU-Mandated)

Heat exchangers are broadly classified as follows:

1. **By Construction:**
   - **Shell-and-Tube Heat Exchanger (STHE)** — most common in process industries.
   - **Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE)** — gasketed plates; high heat transfer area per volume.
   - **Plate-Fin Heat Exchanger** — used in cryogenics and aerospace.
   - **Spiral Heat Exchanger** — handles fouling slurries.
   - **Double-Pipe Heat Exchanger** — simplest; one pipe inside another.

2. **By Flow Arrangement:**
   - **Parallel Flow (Co-current)** — both fluids enter at the same end and flow in the same direction.
   - **Counter Flow (Counter-current)** — fluids flow in opposite directions; most efficient.
   - **Cross Flow** — one fluid flows perpendicular to the other (typical in air-cooled exchangers).
   - **Multi-pass** — fluid changes direction multiple times inside the shell/tube side.

3. **By Heat Transfer Mechanism:**
   - **Direct-Contact Exchangers** (e.g., cooling towers) — fluids physically mix.
   - **Indirect-Contact / Recuperative** (covered here) — fluids separated by wall.
   - **Regenerative** — same surface alternately receives and releases heat (e.g., regenerator in a glass furnace).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Shell-and-Tube Heat Exchanger (STHE) — The Workhorse

A shell-and-tube heat exchanger consists of a bundle of tubes enclosed within a cylindrical shell. One fluid flows inside the tubes (**tube side**), and the other flows outside the tubes, across the shell (**shell side**). Baffles are installed inside the shell to:

- Direct the shell-side fluid across the tube bundle (increasing velocity and turbulence).
- Support the tubes mechanically.
- Reduce dead zones and improve heat transfer.

Key process variables that must be **instrumented** in a plant:
- **Inlet and outlet temperatures** of both fluids (RTD/TC-based TT).
- **Flow rates** on both sides (orifice, magnetic, Coriolis, vortex flowmeters → FT).
- **Pressure drop** (dP cells → PT) — for detecting fouling.
- **Bypass valve position** (valve positioner → ZT) — the manipulated variable in a control loop.

---

## 2.2 The Three Governing Equations

### (a) Heat Duty Equation

The rate of heat transfer from the hot fluid to the cold fluid:

$$Q = \dot{m}_h \cdot c_{p,h} \cdot (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = \dot{m}_c \cdot c_{p,c} \cdot (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$$

where:
- $\dot{m}_h, \dot{m}_c$ = mass flow rates of hot/cold fluid (kg/s)
- $c_{p,h}, c_{p,c}$ = specific heats (J/kg·K)
- $T$ = temperatures (K or °C)

### (b) Overall Heat Transfer Equation

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_m$$

The **overall heat transfer coefficient U** is the reciprocal of the *total thermal resistance*:

$$\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{x_w}{k_w} + \frac{1}{h_o} + R_{f,i} + R_{f,o}$$

where:
- $h_i, h_o$ = inside/outside convective coefficients (W/m²·K)
- $x_w, k_w$ = tube wall thickness and thermal conductivity
- $R_{f,i}, R_{f,o}$ = inside/outside **fouling factors** (m²·K/W)

### (c) LMTD (Log Mean Temperature Difference)

$$\Delta T_m = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}$$

For **counterflow**:
- $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$
- $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$

For **parallel flow**:
- $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}$
- $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,out}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Counterflow ALWAYS yields a higher LMTD than parallel flow for the same terminal temperatures** — which is why counterflow is preferred in almost all process industry applications.

---

## 2.3 Correction Factor F (for Multi-Pass and Crossflow)

When the flow is not a pure counterflow (e.g., 2-4 shell-and-tube, or crossflow), a **correction factor F** must be applied:

$$\Delta T_m^{actual} = F \cdot \Delta T_{m,counterflow}$$

where $F$ depends on two dimensionless groups:
- $P = \dfrac{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}}$  (cold-fluid temperature effectiveness ratio)
- $R = \dfrac{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}$  (capacity rate ratio)

F is read from standard charts (e.g., Kern's chart) for the given shell-and-tube configuration. Acceptable design range: **F ≥ 0.75**; below this, the design is rejected.

---

## 2.4 Effectiveness–NTU Method

An alternative design approach, especially useful when outlet temperatures are **not known a priori** but heat exchanger area and flow rates are given.

$$\varepsilon = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}} = \frac{Q_{actual}}{C_{min}(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})}$$

$$NTU = \frac{U \cdot A}{C_{min}}$$

where $C_{min} = (\dot{m} c_p)_{min}$ and $C_{max} = (\dot{m} c_p)_{max}$, with capacity ratio:

$$C_r = \frac{C_{min}}{C_{max}}$$

**Standard ε–NTU relations:**

| Configuration | Effectiveness Equation |
|---|---|
| Parallel flow | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp\!\left[-NTU(1 + C_r)\right]}{1 + C_r}$ |
| Counterflow | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp\!\left[-NTU(1 - C_r)\right]}{1 - C_r \cdot \exp\!\left[-NTU(1 - C_r)\right]}$ |
| Shell & tube (1 shell, 2, 3, 4… tube passes) | Tabular/Chart-based |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these formulas appear in almost every KTU end-semester question from this module.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heat duty | $Q = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T$ | W |
| 2 | Overall heat transfer | $Q = U A \Delta T_m$ | W |
| 3 | LMTD | $\Delta T_m = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$ | K |
| 4 | Overall resistance | $\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{x_w}{k_w} + \dfrac{1}{h_o} + R_{f,i} + R_{f,o}$ | m²·K/W |
| 5 | Multi-pass correction | $\Delta T_m = F \cdot \Delta T_{m,CF}$ | F dimensionless |
| 6 | P and R parameters | $P = \dfrac{T_{c,o} - T_{c,i}}{T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}}$ ; $R = \dfrac{T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}}{T_{c,o} - T_{c,i}}$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Effectiveness | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{C_{min}(T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})}$ | 0 – 1 |
| 8 | NTU | $NTU = \dfrac{U A}{C_{min}}$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Capacity ratio | $C_r = C_{min}/C_{max}$ | 0 – 1 |
| 10 | LMTD special case ($\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$) | $\Delta T_m = \Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$ | K |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When the temperature difference is constant (e.g., one fluid undergoing phase change like condensation or boiling), the LMTD collapses to the simple temperature difference, and U·A is computed directly. This is a frequently tested shortcut.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Industrial Utility

| Industry | Typical HX Application | Critical Control Variable |
|---|---|---|
| Petroleum refinery | Crude pre-heating, overhead condenser | Column overhead temperature |
| Power plant | Feedwater economizer, condenser | Boiler feedwater temperature |
| Chemical | Reactor feed preheat, reboiler | Reactor inlet T |
| LNG/Cryogenic | Plate-fin multi-stream | Product temperature / liquid level |
| HVAC | Chilled water coil, cooling tower | Zone temperature |
| Dairy/Food | Pasteurizer (PHE) | Product exit temperature |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: LMTD (Log Mean Temperature Difference)

The LMTD is derived by assuming:
1. The overall heat transfer coefficient U is **constant** along the length of the exchanger.
2. The specific heats of both fluids are **constant** (no phase change).
3. The flow is either pure counterflow or pure parallel flow (no mixing in the direction of flow).
4. Heat losses to the surroundings are **negligible**.
5. The temperature of each fluid varies only in the axial direction (1-D model).
6. Kinetic and potential energy changes are **negligible**.

Consider a differential element $dA$ at axial position $x$ inside a counterflow exchanger.

**Step 1:** Heat transferred across $dA$:

$$dQ = U \cdot dA \cdot (T_h - T_c)$$

**Step 2:** Heat lost by hot fluid over the same element:

$$dQ = -\dot{m}_h c_{p,h} \, dT_h \quad\Rightarrow\quad dT_h = -\frac{dQ}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}}$$

**Step 3:** Heat gained by cold fluid:

$$dQ = \dot{m}_c c_{p,c} \, dT_c \quad\Rightarrow\quad dT_c = \frac{dQ}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}}$$

**Step 4:** Take the differential of the temperature difference $\Delta T = T_h - T_c$:

$$d(\Delta T) = dT_h - dT_c = -dQ\!\left(\frac{1}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}}\right)$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $dQ = U \cdot dA \cdot \Delta T$ into Step 4:

$$d(\Delta T) = -U \cdot dA \cdot \Delta T \left(\frac{1}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}}\right)$$

**Step 6:** Separate variables:

$$\frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U\!\left(\frac{1}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}}\right) dA$$

**Step 7:** Integrate from inlet (where $\Delta T = \Delta T_1$ and $A = 0$) to outlet (where $\Delta T = \Delta T_2$ and $A = A$):

$$\int_{\Delta T_1}^{\Delta T_2} \frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U\!\left(\frac{1}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}}\right) \int_0^A dA$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right) = -U \cdot A \left(\frac{1}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}}\right)$$

**Step 8:** The total heat transfer equals both fluid side expressions:

$$Q = \dot{m}_h c_{p,h}(T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}) = \dot{m}_c c_{p,c}(T_{c,o} - T_{c,i})$$

For counterflow, the total $\Delta T$ change over the exchanger is $\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o} - (T_{h,o} - T_{c,i})$. With some algebra (substituting the energy balances and integrating):

$$\boxed{\;\Delta T_m = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** LMTD is the "effective average" temperature driving force. It is **always less than the arithmetic mean** $(\Delta T_1 + \Delta T_2)/2$ but always greater than the smaller of the two. The LMTD is the correct way to handle the non-linear temperature profile along the exchanger.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (Parallel vs Counterflow Comparison)

**Given:**
- Hot oil: $T_{h,i} = 200°C$, $T_{h,o} = 100°C$
- Cold water: $T_{c,i} = 30°C$, $T_{c,o} = 80°C$
- $\dot{m}_h c_{p,h} = \dot{m}_c c_{p,c} = 10,000$ W/K
- U = 500 W/m²·K

**Step 1: Heat duty** (identical for both fluids by energy balance):

$$Q = \dot{m}_h c_{p,h}(T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}) = 10{,}000 \times (200 - 100) = 1{,}000{,}000 \; W = 1 \; MW$$

**Step 2: LMTD for Parallel Flow:**
- $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,i} = 200 - 30 = 170°C$
- $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,o} = 100 - 80 = 20°C$

$$\Delta T_{m,parallel} = \frac{170 - 20}{\ln(170/20)} = \frac{150}{\ln(8.5)} = \frac{150}{2.1401} = 70.09°C$$

**Step 3: LMTD for Counter Flow:**
- $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o} = 200 - 80 = 120°C$
- $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i} = 100 - 30 = 70°C$

$$\Delta T_{m,counter} = \frac{120 - 70}{\ln(120/70)} = \frac{50}{\ln(1.7143)} = \frac{50}{0.5390} = 92.77°C$$

**Step 4: Area Required:**

$$A_{parallel} = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{m,parallel}} = \frac{10^6}{500 \times 70.09} = 28.53 \; m^2$$

$$A_{counter} = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{m,counter}} = \frac{10^6}{500 \times 92.77} = 21.56 \; m^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Counterflow needs **~24% less heat transfer area** than parallel flow for the same duty. This is the primary engineering reason counterflow exchangers dominate plant designs.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem: Effectiveness–NTU Method

**Given:**
- $C_h = \dot{m}_h c_{p,h} = 20{,}000$ W/K
- $C_c = \dot{m}_c c_{p,c} = 15{,}000$ W/K
- $T_{h,i} = 150°C$, $T_{c,i} = 30°C$
- $U \cdot A = 30{,}000$ W/K
- Configuration: Counterflow

**Step 1: Identify minimum and maximum capacity rates:**

$$C_{min} = 15{,}000 \; W/K, \quad C_{max} = 20{,}000 \; W/K$$

$$C_r = \frac{C_{min}}{C_{max}} = \frac{15{,}000}{20{,}000} = 0.75$$

**Step 2: Calculate NTU:**

$$NTU = \frac{U \cdot A}{C_{min}} = \frac{30{,}000}{15{,}000} = 2.0$$

**Step 3: Apply counterflow ε–NTU formula:**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{1 - \exp\!\left[-NTU(1 - C_r)\right]}{1 - C_r \cdot \exp\!\left[-NTU(1 - C_r)\right]}$$

$$NTU(1 - C_r) = 2.0 \times (1 - 0.75) = 0.5$$

$$\exp(-0.5) = 0.6065$$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{1 - 0.6065}{1 - 0.75 \times 0.6065} = \frac{0.3935}{1 - 0.4549} = \frac{0.3935}{0.5451} = 0.7220$$

**Step 4: Calculate the actual heat duty:**

$$Q_{max} = C_{min}(T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}) = 15{,}000 \times (150 - 30) = 1{,}800{,}000 \; W$$

$$Q_{actual} = \varepsilon \cdot Q_{max} = 0.7220 \times 1.8 \times 10^6 = 1.30 \; MW$$

**Step 5: Calculate outlet temperatures:**

$$T_{h,o} = T_{h,i} - \frac{Q_{actual}}{C_h} = 150 - \frac{1.30 \times 10^6}{20{,}000} = 150 - 65 = 85°C$$

$$T_{c,o} = T_{c,i} + \frac{Q_{actual}}{C_c} = 30 + \frac{1.30 \times 10^6}{15{,}000} = 30 + 86.67 = 116.67°C$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-check:** $C_h(T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}) = 20{,}000 \times 65 = 1.3 \times 10^6$ W ✓
>
> $C_c(T_{c,o} - T_{c,i}) = 15{,}000 \times 86.67 = 1.3 \times 10^6$ W ✓
>
> Energy balance is satisfied.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Design Methods

| Aspect | LMTD Method | ε–NTU Method |
|---|---|---|
| Typical use | Sizing a new exchanger | Rating an existing exchanger |
| Primary input | Inlet + outlet temperatures | Area, flow rates, inlet T |
| Primary output | Heat transfer area (A) | Outlet temperatures (and Q) |
| Iterative? | No (direct) | No (direct) |
| Used in | Design phase | Performance evaluation / control |
| Strength | Direct engineering design | Handles unknown outlets gracefully |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Instrumentation & Control of a Heat Exchanger

This diagram maps the control loop architecture, the physical measurement signals, the control valve, and the disturbance paths for a typical shell-and-tube heat exchanger (e.g., process fluid preheater using steam on the shell side).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Hot Process Fluid Inlet] --> A2[Tube Side of HX]
    A2 --> A3[Hot Process Fluid Outlet]
    B1[Steam Inlet from Header] --> B2[Steam Control Valve]
    B2 --> B3[Shell Side of HX]
    B3 --> B4[Condensate Outlet to Trap]
    A2 -. heat transfer through tube wall .-> B3
    TTH1[Temperature Transmitter Hot Inlet TT-101] --> A1
    TTH2[Temperature Transmitter Hot Outlet TT-102] --> A3
    TTC1[Temperature Transmitter Cold Outlet TT-103] --> A3
    FTH[Flow Transmitter Hot Side FT-101] --> A1
    PTH[Differential Pressure Transmitter dPT-101] --> A2
    TTC1 --> TIC[Temperature Indicator Controller TIC-100]
    TTH1 --> TIC
    TIC --> CV[Current to Pressure Converter I to P]
    CV --> B2
    SP[Setpoint from DCS Operator Station] --> TIC
    ALM[Alarm High Low on Outlet T] --> TIC
    ALM --> DCS[DCS Supervisory Layer]
    TIC --> DCS
    FTH --> DCS
    PTH --> DCS
    B4 --> CTL[Condensate Level Transmitter LT-101]
    CTL --> LCS[Level Control Switch LCS-100]
    LCS --> B4
```

**Interpretation of the flow:**

1. **Process fluid (hot)** enters the tube side and is cooled (or heated, depending on the application) by exchanging heat with **steam** flowing on the shell side.
2. **TT-101, TT-102, TT-103** measure inlet/outlet temperatures and feed the **TIC-100** controller.
3. The **setpoint (SP)** is loaded from the DCS operator station.
4. The controller output is sent through a **current-to-pressure (I/P) converter** to actuate the **steam control valve**.
5. **Differential pressure transmitter (dPT-101)** monitors fouling — a rise in dP indicates deposition on tubes.
6. **Condensate level switch (LCS-100)** protects against condensate flooding the shell, which would reduce heat transfer area.
7. All signals are mirrored to the **DCS** for trending, alarm annunciation, and historical logging.

---

## 4.2 Counterflow vs Parallel Flow — Temperature Profile Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PF[Parallel Flow Arrangement]
        direction LR
        P1[Hot fluid enters at T_hi 200 C] --> P2[Both flow left to right]
        P3[Cold fluid enters at T_ci 30 C] --> P2
        P2 --> P4[Hot fluid exits at T_ho 100 C]
        P2 --> P5[Cold fluid exits at T_co 80 C]
        P2 -. temperatures converge toward middle .-> P6[Common pinch point]
    end
    subgraph CF[Counterflow Arrangement]
        direction LR
        C1[Hot fluid enters at T_hi 200 C] --> C2[Hot flows left to right]
        C2 --> C3[Hot fluid exits at T_ho 100 C]
        C4[Cold fluid enters at T_ci 30 C from right] --> C5[Cold flows right to left]
        C5 --> C6[Cold fluid exits at T_co 80 C]
        C3 -. never meet in temperature .-> C6
    end
```

**Reading the schematic:**

- In **Parallel Flow**, hot and cold fluids enter the *same* end; their temperatures approach each other and meet at a "pinch point" inside the exchanger. The cold fluid outlet can never exceed the hot fluid outlet — this is the **thermodynamic limitation**.
- In **Counterflow**, hot and cold fluids flow in *opposite* directions. The cold fluid can be heated to a temperature higher than the hot fluid's outlet, breaking the parallel-flow limitation. This is why counterflow is the design default in industry.

---

## 4.3 Heat Transfer Resistance Network (Thermal Circuit Analogy)

This is the "thermal Ohm's law" representation — a classic KTU exam illustration.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[Hot fluid bulk T_h] --> R1[R_conv_i 1 divided by h_i]
    R1 --> R2[R_fouling_i R_f_i]
    R2 --> R3[R_wall x_w divided by k_w]
    R3 --> R4[R_fouling_o R_f_o]
    R4 --> R5[R_conv_o 1 divided by h_o]
    R5 --> T2[Cold fluid bulk T_c]
```

**Interpretation:** The temperature drops in a stepwise manner across each thermal resistance, exactly like voltage drops across series resistors in an electrical circuit. The "current" here is heat flux $q'' = Q/A$, and "voltage" is temperature difference.

$$\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + R_{f,i} + \frac{x_w}{k_w} + R_{f,o} + \frac{1}{h_o}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fouling increases with operating time.** The value of $R_f$ is a function of *service life*, fluid chemistry, temperature, and velocity. Design standards (e.g., TEMA) provide initial fouling allowances that engineers must include from day one — otherwise, the exchanger will underperform within months of service.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD). Why is it used in heat exchanger analysis instead of the arithmetic mean temperature difference?**

**Model Answer:**

LMTD is defined as the logarithmic mean of the temperature differences at the two ends of a heat exchanger:

$$\Delta T_m = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$$

where $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ are the terminal temperature differences (hot-inlet to cold-outlet, and hot-outlet to cold-inlet for counterflow).

**Why LMTD and not arithmetic mean:** The temperature profile along the length of a heat exchanger varies non-linearly (exponentially for constant U and constant $c_p$). The arithmetic mean overestimates the driving force, especially when $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ differ significantly. The LMTD correctly accounts for this exponential variation and provides the *true* mean driving force consistent with the differential heat transfer equation $dQ = U \cdot dA \cdot \Delta T$. LMTD also reduces to the arithmetic mean when $\Delta T_1 \approx \Delta T_2$ (e.g., during phase change), making it a general-purpose formula.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often compute LMTD with $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ taken in the *wrong* sequence. For counterflow, $\Delta T_1$ is **always** at the hot-inlet end (i.e., $T_{h,i} - T_{c,o}$). For parallel flow, $\Delta T_1$ is at the common inlet end ($T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}$). Swapping these yields a numerically different (and incorrect) result.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Explain the concept of fouling factor in a heat exchanger. How does it affect the design and operation of an industrial exchanger?**

**Model Answer:**

Fouling refers to the **unwanted accumulation of deposits** on the heat transfer surfaces of an exchanger. Common foulants include:
- Scale (calcium/magnesium salts from hard water)
- Sludge and biological growth
- Coke or polymer deposition (in hydrocarbon service)
- Corrosion products

The **fouling factor** $R_f$ (m²·K/W) is a numerical measure of the additional thermal resistance introduced by these deposits. It is included in the overall resistance equation:

$$\frac{1}{U_{dirty}} = \frac{1}{U_{clean}} + R_{f,i} + R_{f,o}$$

**Design impact:** Engineers must include a fouling allowance from day one. A clean exchanger with $U = 1000$ W/m²·K may degrade to $U_{dirty} = 400$ W/m²·K after six months — meaning the *actual* heat duty delivered drops significantly. To compensate, designers either (a) oversize the area initially, or (b) schedule periodic cleaning.

**Operational impact:** Fouling is monitored on-line using:
- **dP transmitters** (rising dP across the exchanger indicates deposit buildup).
- **Performance trending** — if outlet temperature drifts from setpoint despite constant flow, fouling is the prime suspect.
- Eventually, the heat exchanger is taken offline for chemical cleaning or hydroblasting.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuator's Note:** Students often treat fouling as a minor "design margin." In reality, fouling is a *variable resistance* that increases with time and is the most common reason process heat exchangers fail to meet nameplate duty. Mentioning the time-dependent nature of fouling will earn full marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice: Question A OR Question B)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Module 4]

**(a)** [7 Marks] A counterflow shell-and-tube heat exchanger heats water from 30°C to 80°C using engine oil that enters at 160°C and leaves at 100°C. The overall heat transfer coefficient is $U = 450 \; W/m^2 \cdot K$. Calculate:
1. The LMTD.
2. The heat transfer rate per unit area.
3. The required heat transfer area.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Explain in detail the instrumentation and control loop used to maintain the cold fluid outlet temperature of a shell-and-tube heat exchanger constant. Include a P&ID description and discuss the role of feedback, feedforward, and cascade strategies.

---

### Model Solution to Question A(a):

**Step 1: Identify the temperature differences (counterflow).**

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o} = 160 - 80 = 80°C$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i} = 100 - 30 = 70°C$$

**Step 2: Compute LMTD.**

$$\Delta T_m = \frac{80 - 70}{\ln(80/70)} = \frac{10}{\ln(1.1429)} = \frac{10}{0.1335} = 74.86°C$$

**Step 3: Compute heat transfer rate per unit area.**

$$\frac{Q}{A} = U \cdot \Delta T_m = 450 \times 74.86 = 33{,}687 \; W/m^2 \approx 33.69 \; kW/m^2$$

**Step 4: Compute heat duty Q.**

Since the cold-side water data is not given with mass flow, use the hot-side oil data — but we are not given the oil mass flow. So we instead compute *area* using a normalized quantity or use the cold water side — wait, the problem provides only temperatures. So the answer is expressed in terms of Q/A above, and the area requires Q. If the problem assumes the heat duty is computed by the LMTD × U × A equation directly, we present the formula:

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_m} = \frac{Q}{33{,}687} \; m^2$$

(If, for example, $\dot{m}_c = 5$ kg/s and $c_{p,c} = 4180$ J/kg·K, then $Q = 5 \times 4180 \times 50 = 1{,}045{,}000$ W, and $A = 31.02 \; m^2$.)

---

### Model Solution to Question A(b):

**Feedback Control Loop — Description:**

1. **Measurement:** A Resistance Temperature Detector (RTD) or thermocouple (TT-103) is installed at the **cold fluid outlet** to measure the controlled variable (CV = $T_{c,o}$).
2. **Transmission:** The TT converts the temperature into a 4–20 mA signal and sends it to the Temperature Indicator Controller (TIC-100).
3. **Comparison:** The TIC compares PV (process variable) to SP (setpoint), typically loaded from the DCS.
4. **Control Action:** The TIC implements a **PID algorithm** and outputs a 4–20 mA signal proportional to the error.
5. **Actuation:** The signal goes to a current-to-pressure (I/P) converter, which modulates a **pneumatic control valve** on the **hot fluid inlet** (or steam inlet). The valve position is the manipulated variable (MV).
6. **Final Control Element:** The valve adjusts hot fluid flow rate, which in turn changes the heat duty $Q = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T$, and hence the cold-side outlet temperature.

**Advanced Strategies:**

- **Feedforward Control:** A flow transmitter (FT-101) on the cold fluid inlet measures the load (disturbance) and directly adjusts the steam valve *before* the outlet temperature deviates. This anticipates load changes.
- **Cascade Control:** The *primary* loop is $T_{c,o}$ → TIC-100. The *secondary* loop is the hot fluid *temperature* (TT-102) → TIC-101 → steam valve. The primary's output becomes the secondary's setpoint. Cascade rejects disturbances entering the hot-side temperature far more effectively.

**P&ID Description (textual):**
- A vertical heat exchanger (vessel) with two process connections labeled "Hot In," "Hot Out," "Cold In," "Cold Out."
- TT-103 inserted into the cold-outlet nozzle.
- TIC-100 in the control room (or DCS faceplate).
- I/P converter near the steam control valve on the hot inlet line.
- FT-101 on the cold inlet for feedforward.
- PT-101 and dPT-101 for pressure/dP monitoring.
- All instruments tagged per ISA 5.1 standard.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks Lost:** Students often forget to (a) mention the **I/P converter** (mandatory between electronic controller and pneumatic valve), (b) distinguish between feedback and feedforward, and (c) explain *why* cascade is needed (to compensate for steam pressure/temperature disturbances *before* they reach the cold-side outlet). Examiners look for these depth elements.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 4]

**(a)** [7 Marks] A heat exchanger is to be designed to cool 10 kg/s of oil ($c_p = 2100$ J/kg·K) from 150°C to 60°C using cooling water that enters at 25°C and is allowed to exit at 70°C. The overall heat transfer coefficient is $U = 800$ W/m²·K. Determine:
1. The heat duty Q.
2. The required mass flow rate of cooling water ($c_{p,water} = 4180$ J/kg·K).
3. The LMTD and required area for both parallel and counterflow configurations. State which configuration is preferred and why.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Define effectiveness and NTU. For a counterflow heat exchanger with $C_r = 0.5$ and NTU = 2.5, calculate the effectiveness and explain the physical significance of the result.

---

### Model Solution to Question B(a):

**Step 1: Heat duty Q.**

$$Q = \dot{m}_{oil} c_{p,oil} (T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}) = 10 \times 2100 \times (150 - 60) = 10 \times 2100 \times 90 = 1{,}890{,}000 \; W = 1.89 \; MW$$

**Step 2: Mass flow rate of cooling water.**

$$Q = \dot{m}_{water} c_{p,water} (T_{c,o} - T_{c,i})$$

$$\dot{m}_{water} = \frac{Q}{c_{p,water}(T_{c,o} - T_{c,i})} = \frac{1{,}890{,}000}{4180 \times (70 - 25)} = \frac{1{,}890{,}000}{4180 \times 45} = \frac{1{,}890{,}000}{188{,}100} = 10.05 \; kg/s$$

**Step 3: LMTD for parallel flow.**

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,i} = 150 - 25 = 125°C$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,o} = 60 - 70 = -10°C$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Step:** $\Delta T_2$ is **negative** for parallel flow! This is a **thermodynamic impossibility** — you cannot cool a fluid to a temperature *below* the cold fluid's outlet using parallel flow, and a negative LMTD indicates the configuration is infeasible. This problem is a classic KTU trap.

**Step 4: LMTD for counter flow.**

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o} = 150 - 70 = 80°C$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i} = 60 - 25 = 35°C$$

$$\Delta T_{m,CF} = \frac{80 - 35}{\ln(80/35)} = \frac{45}{\ln(2.2857)} = \frac{45}{0.8267} = 54.43°C$$

**Step 5: Required area for counterflow.**

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{m,CF}} = \frac{1{,}890{,}000}{800 \times 54.43} = \frac{1{,}890{,}000}{43{,}544} = 43.40 \; m^2$$

**Conclusion:** Counterflow is the only feasible configuration for this duty — the parallel flow option is rejected because $T_{h,o} < T_{c,o}$, violating the second law of thermodynamics.

---

### Model Solution to Question B(b):

**Effectiveness (ε):**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}} = \frac{Q_{actual}}{C_{min}(T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})}$$

where $C_{min} = \min(\dot{m} c_p)$ on either side.

**Number of Transfer Units (NTU):**

$$NTU = \frac{U \cdot A}{C_{min}}$$

It represents the *dimensionless size* of the heat exchanger — a higher NTU means more heat transfer capacity per unit of $C_{min}$.

**Step 1: Apply counterflow ε–NTU formula.**

$$NTU(1 - C_r) = 2.5 \times (1 - 0.5) = 1.25$$

$$\exp(-1.25) = 0.2865$$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{1 - 0.2865}{1 - 0.5 \times 0.2865} = \frac{0.7135}{1 - 0.1433} = \frac{0.7135}{0.8567} = 0.8329$$

**Step 2: Physical interpretation.**

- $\varepsilon = 0.8329$ means the exchanger recovers **83.29%** of the *maximum possible* heat transfer (the maximum being limited by the smaller of the two fluids reaching the inlet temperature of the other).
- The remaining 16.71% is "lost" to thermodynamic irreversibility — there must always be a finite temperature difference for heat to flow.
- An effectiveness of 0.83 is typical of a *well-designed* industrial exchanger; values > 0.9 require either very large areas or near-equal flow rates.
- Increasing NTU further yields diminishing returns — the ε–NTU curve is asymptotic.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall in Part B(b):** Students often write $\varepsilon = Q / Q_{max}$ without defining $Q_{max}$ explicitly. The examiner will not award full credit unless you write $Q_{max} = C_{min}(T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})$ and explain *why* this is the maximum (i.e., the cold fluid cannot be heated above the hot fluid's inlet temperature). Always state the definition *and* the limiting physical interpretation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Points for Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision**

### Core Concepts
- [x] Heat exchanger transfers heat between fluids separated by a wall — **no mixing**.
- [x] Governing equation: $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_m$
- [x] LMTD: $\Delta T_m = (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)/\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)$
- [x] Counterflow is more efficient than parallel flow; preferred in 95%+ of plant designs.
- [x] Effectiveness–NTU method is used for rating (rather than sizing) an exchanger.
- [x] Fouling factor $R_f$ increases with operating time; always include in design.
- [x] Correction factor F is needed for multi-pass and crossflow arrangements.
- [x] $Q = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T$ must give the *same* Q on both fluid sides (energy balance).

### Instrumented Variables
- [x] **TT** — temperature transmitters on both inlet and outlet of both fluids.
- [x] **FT** — flow transmitters on both sides (for feedforward + energy balance).
- [x] **PT / dPT** — pressure and differential pressure (for fouling monitoring).
- [x] **LT** — level transmitter for condensate (in steam-heated units).
- [x] **Control Valve (with I/P)** — final control element on the heating/cooling medium.

### Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- [x] **Parallel flow can be infeasible** — check $T_{h,o} > T_{c,o}$.
- [x] **Fouling factor is not optional** — it is a mandatory design input.
- [x] **ε–NTU and LMTD give the same physical answer** — they are just two routes.
- [x] **Units consistency:** T in K or °C, but $\Delta T$ is the same in both; $Q$ in W; $U$ in W/m²·K; $A$ in m².
- [x] **F correction factor must be ≥ 0.75** — otherwise, change shell configuration.
- [x] **Sign convention** — $\Delta T_1$ always refers to the hot-inlet end of the exchanger (for counterflow).
- [x] **Effectiveness cannot exceed 1.0** — it is bounded by the second law of thermodynamics.

### KTU 2024 Examiner Mindset
- [x] Show full derivation steps, not just final answers.
- [x] Always state assumptions (constant U, constant $c_p$, no heat loss, negligible KE/PE).
- [x] Tag your work with the controlling equation (e.g., "Using the LMTD formula: ...").
- [x] Discuss both methods (LMTD and ε–NTU) and state when each is preferred.
- [x] For instrumentation questions, draw a labeled P&ID-style sketch — *not* a vague block diagram.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** In the 14-mark Part B questions, the (a) part is almost always a *numerical* design/calculation, and the (b) part is *descriptive* (instrumentation, control strategy, or theoretical comparison). Practice both styles — the descriptive part is where most students lose marks for lack of depth and proper P&ID-style thinking.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT864 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN LARGE SCALE INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** HVAC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HVAC (Heating, Ventilation, and Air-Conditioning)** is an integrated, multi-disciplinary branch of engineering that deals with the design, specification, installation, monitoring, and **automatic closed-loop control** of the thermal comfort, indoor air quality (IAQ), humidity, and air-distribution parameters inside large-scale industrial plants, clean rooms, process halls, pharmaceutical suites, data centres, and commercial high-rises.

In the context of **Instrumentation & Control Engineering**, the HVAC sub-system is treated as a **multi-variable, multi-loop, interacting MIMO (Multiple-Input Multiple-Output) plant** whose controlled variables are:

| Controlled Variable (CV) | Symbol | Typical Industrial Range | Sensor |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dry-Bulb Temperature | T<sub>db</sub> | 18 °C – 26 °C | RTD / PT-100, Thermistor |
| Relative Humidity | φ | 40 % – 60 % RH | Capacitive Polymer Sensor |
| CO₂ Concentration | C<sub>CO₂</sub> | < 800 ppm | NDIR Sensor |
| Differential Pressure | ΔP | 2.5 – 12.5 mm H₂O | Magnehelic / Transducer |
| Air Velocity at Duct | v | 2 – 8 m/s | Vane Anemometer / Pitot |

The **manipulated variables (MVs)** are chilled-water valve position, hot-water/steam valve position, fan/motor VFD frequency, and damper actuator stroke.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in "Large Scale Industries":** Unlike domestic AC, industrial HVAC must simultaneously satisfy **process requirements** (e.g., a semiconductor fab requiring ±0.1 °C stability and Class 100 cleanliness) **and** **occupant comfort** (ASHRAE Standard 55), while remaining **energy-efficient** under ASHRAE 90.1 and **safely interlocked** under NFPA 90A standards.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Human-Body" Intuition

Imagine an industrial plant as a **giant human body**:

| Human Body Function | Industrial HVAC Equivalent | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Skin blood vessels** dilating/constricting | Heating coil valve (steam/hot water) modulating | Regulate heat *gain* into the space |
| **Perspiration / sweat glands** | Cooling coil valve (chilled water) + humidifier | Remove latent & sensible heat |
| **Lungs breathing** | Fans, AHU blowers, exhaust ducts | Bring in fresh outside air (ventilation) |
| **Kidneys filtering toxins** | HEPA / ULPA filters + carbon beds | Clean contaminants from recirculated air |
| **Brain (hypothalamus)** | DDC Controller / PLC / BMS with PID loops | Sense & decide actuator positions |

Just as your body **automatically** sweats more when you run, an **AHU (Air-Handling Unit)** controlled by a PID loop **automatically** opens its chilled-water valve when the return-air temperature rises above the setpoint.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** Think **"HVAC = Thermo + Flow + Quality"** — Temperature, Humidity/Air-flow, and Air Quality. Every sensor, valve, and damper in the plant maps to one of these three pillars.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Industrial Units (KTU Highlight)

The following metrics are repeatedly used in KTU numericals and must be memorised:

- **TR (Ton of Refrigeration)** = **3.517 kW** = heat needed to melt **1 ton (2000 lb ≈ 907.18 kg)** of ice in 24 hours.
- **CFM (Cubic Feet per Minute)** — standard imperial airflow unit; 1 CFM ≈ **0.000472 m³/s**.
- **CMH (Cubic Metres per Hour)** — SI airflow unit; 1 CMH ≈ **0.5886 CFM**.
- **ACH (Air Changes per Hour)** = Volume of air supplied per hour / Volume of room. Clean rooms demand **20–600 ACH**.
- **BTU/hr** = 0.2931 W ; **1 kW** = 3412 BTU/hr.
- **Sensible Heat Factor (SHF)** = Sensible Heat Load / Total Heat Load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ASHRAE 62.1** mandates a minimum outside-air ventilation rate of approximately **8.5 L/s per person** (≈ 15 CFM/person) for office-type occupancies. KTU exam questions frequently test this standard figure.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block-Level Decomposition of an Industrial HVAC Plant

A typical large-scale HVAC system comprises **three tightly coupled sub-systems**. Treating them independently is the most common mistake students make in KTU board exams.

### 2.1.1 Sub-System A — The Thermal Subsystem (Heating + Cooling)

- **Heat Source Path:** Boiler → Hot-Water/Steam Header → Heating Coil (fin-tube heat exchanger) → Supply Air.
- **Heat Sink Path:** Chiller (vapour-compression or absorption) → Chilled-Water Pump → Cooling Coil → Return Air.
- **Primary Control Loop:** Temperature Transmitter (RTD/PT-100) → PID Controller → Pneumatic or Electric Actuator on **3-way mixing valve** modulating chilled-water flow.

### 2.1.2 Sub-System B — The Ventilation Subsystem (Air Movement + Filtration)

- **Forced-Air Loop:** SA Fan (Supply) + RA Fan (Return) + EA Fan (Exhaust) + MA Fan (Make-up Air).
- **Filtration Stages:**
  - Pre-filter (G4, MERV 7–8) — coarse dust.
  - Fine filter (F7–F9, MERV 13–14) — fine particulate.
  - **HEPA filter (H13–H14, ≥ 99.97 % at 0.3 µm)** — cleanroom.
  - **ULPA filter (U15–U17, ≥ 99.9995 %)** — pharma/semiconductor.
- **Damper Network:** Motorised opposed-blade or parallel-blade dampers (0–100 % stroke).

### 2.1.3 Sub-System C — The Air-Conditioning Subsystem (Humidity + IAQ)

- **Dehumidification:** Achieved by over-cooling the air below dew-point, condensing moisture on the cooling coil, then re-heating to supply temperature.
- **Humidification:** Steam injection, electric-resistance canister, or wetted-media pad (adiabatic).
- **IAQ Sensors:** CO₂ (NDIR), VOC (MOS), PM2.5 (laser scattering), CO (electrochemical).

## 2.2 Why These Equations? — Engineering Logic Behind Each Formula

The HVAC design engineer must compute three distinct loads to size equipment:

1. **Room Sensible Heat (RSH):** Heat gained through walls, roof, glass, infiltration, lights, equipment, occupants — measured in **kW or TR**.
2. **Room Latent Heat (RLH):** Moisture added by occupants, process operations, infiltration — measured in **kW or TR**.
3. **Outside Air Load:** Sensible + Latent portion of fresh air being introduced for ventilation.

Without these, the chiller could be undersized (room never cools) or oversized (energy waste, hunting in control loop).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The **Sensible Heat Factor (SHF)** uniquely determines the **psychrometric process line** on the chart. SHF = 1 → pure sensible cooling; SHF = 0 → pure dehumidification. Typical office SHF ≈ 0.75–0.85.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Print This Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **minimum** you must memorise. Every KTU 14-mark numerical on HVAC will use at least 3 of these.

| # | Formula Name | Equation (LaTeX) | Units | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sensible Heat (Air) | $Q_s = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | kW | Heating/cooling load on coil |
| 2 | Latent Heat (Air) | $Q_l = \dot{m} \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \Delta \omega$ | kW | Humidity control |
| 3 | Total Heat | $Q_t = Q_s + Q_l$ | kW | Chiller sizing |
| 4 | Sensible Heat Factor | $\text{SHF} = Q_s \div Q_t$ | dimensionless | Psychrometric process |
| 5 | SH Through Wall | $Q_{wall} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{cltd}$ | W | Cooling load envelope |
| 6 | Air Mass Flow | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot \dot{V} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v$ | kg/s | Duct sizing |
| 7 | Volume Flow | $\dot{V} = Q_s \div (\rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T)$ | m³/s | AHU sizing |
| 8 | CFM from kW | $\text{CFM} = Q_s \cdot 317.7 \div \Delta T$ | CFM | Imperial sizing |
| 9 | Fan Power | $P_{fan} = \Delta P \cdot \dot{V} \div \eta_{fan}$ | W | Energy audit |
| 10 | Pump Power | $P_{pump} = \rho \cdot g \cdot \dot{V} \cdot H \div \eta_{pump}$ | W | Chilled-water circuit |
| 11 | TR Definition | $1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW} = 12000 \text{ BTU/hr}$ | — | Equipment rating |
| 12 | ERSH (Effective Room Sensible Heat) | $\text{ERSH} = \text{RSH} + \text{RA Sensible} - \text{SA Sensible}$ | kW | Bypass-factor calculation |
| 13 | ADP (Apparatus Dew Point) | $\text{ADP} = T_{coil,surf} - \text{BF} \cdot (T_{coil,surf} - T_{inlet})$ | °C | Coil performance |
| 14 | Air Changes / Hour | $\text{ACH} = 60 \cdot \dot{V} \div V_{room}$ | hr⁻¹ | Cleanroom / hospital |
| 15 | Mixed Air Temp | $T_{mix} = (m_1 T_1 + m_2 T_2) \div (m_1 + m_2)$ | °C | AHU economiser |
| 16 | Dew Point | $T_{dp} = (b \cdot \gamma(T,RH)) \div (a - \gamma(T,RH))$ | °C | Humidity calc |
| 17 | Moist Air Density | $\rho = P \div [R_d \cdot T \cdot (1 + 1.6078 \cdot \omega)]$ | kg/m³ | Mass-flow balance |
| 18 | Specific Humidity | $\omega = 0.622 \cdot P_v \div (P - P_v)$ | kg/kg | Psychrometric chart |

Where:
- $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate (kg/s)
- $c_p$ = 1.005 kJ/kg·K for dry air, 1.02 for moist air
- $h_{fg}$ = 2501 kJ/kg (latent heat of vaporisation at 0 °C)
- $U$ = Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (W/m²K)
- $A$ = Area (m²)
- $\Delta T_{cltd}$ = Cooling Load Temperature Difference (from ASHRAE tables)
- $\rho$ = 1.204 kg/m³ at 20 °C sea level
- $\omega$ = humidity ratio (kg water / kg dry air)
- $\eta_{fan}$ ≈ 0.65–0.75 for forward-curved centrifugal
- $\Delta P$ = duct static pressure (Pa)

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for exam:** **"S L T"** → **S**ensible, **L**atent, **T**otal. Always compute RSH first, then RLH, then total, then SHF. Examiner's expect this exact order in valuation.

---

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Methodology:** Below is a **complete, from-scratch derivation** of an industrial cooling-load problem — exactly the style KTU board examiners award full marks for. Every algebraic step is explicitly shown. No equation is skipped.

---

## 3.1 Reference Numerical Problem

> *"A 20 m × 12 m × 4 m (L × W × H) electronics assembly hall has the following loads:*
> *(i) External wall area 96 m² with U = 0.6 W/m²K and CLTD = 11 K,*
> *(ii) Roof area 240 m² with U = 0.4 W/m²K and CLTD = 14 K,*
> *(iii) Glass window 24 m² with U = 3.5 W/m²K and CLTD = 10 K,*
> *(iv) Lights = 5 kW,*
> *(v) Equipment = 12 kW,*
> *(vi) Occupants = 40 persons (sensible 75 W/person, latent 55 W/person),*
> *(vii) Outside air 600 CFM at 35 °C DB / 28 °C WB mixing with return air at 25 °C DB / 17.5 °C WB.*
> *Room setpoint: 24 °C DB / 50 % RH.*
> *Compute: Total Sensible, Total Latent, Total Heat, SHF, and required tonnage in TR."*

---

## 3.2 Step 1 — Envelope (Fabric) Sensible Heat Gains

We use the standard KTU / ASHRAE formula:

$$Q_{fabric} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{cltd}$$

### 3.2.1 Through External Wall

Substitute the given values directly:

$$Q_{wall} = 0.6 \cdot 96 \cdot 11$$

Perform multiplication left-to-right:

$$Q_{wall} = 0.6 \cdot 96 = 57.6$$

$$Q_{wall} = 57.6 \cdot 11 = 633.6 \text{ W}$$

### 3.2.2 Through Roof

$$Q_{roof} = U_{roof} \cdot A_{roof} \cdot \Delta T_{cltd,roof}$$

$$Q_{roof} = 0.4 \cdot 240 \cdot 14$$

$$Q_{roof} = 0.4 \cdot 240 = 96$$

$$Q_{roof} = 96 \cdot 14 = 1344 \text{ W}$$

### 3.2.3 Through Glass

$$Q_{glass} = U_{glass} \cdot A_{glass} \cdot \Delta T_{cltd,glass}$$

$$Q_{glass} = 3.5 \cdot 24 \cdot 10$$

$$Q_{glass} = 3.5 \cdot 24 = 84$$

$$Q_{glass} = 84 \cdot 10 = 840 \text{ W}$$

### 3.2.4 Total Fabric Sensible Heat

$$Q_{fabric,total} = Q_{wall} + Q_{roof} + Q_{glass}$$

$$Q_{fabric,total} = 633.6 + 1344 + 840 = 2817.6 \text{ W}$$

---

## 3.3 Step 2 — Internal Sensible Heat Gains

### 3.3.1 Lights

Lighting contributes heat approximately equal to its rated wattage (assuming fluorescent/LED with ballast ≈ 1.2 multiplier, but here we take directly):

$$Q_{lights} = 5000 \text{ W} = 5.0 \text{ kW}$$

### 3.3.2 Equipment

For miscellaneous plug loads, we use rated power directly:

$$Q_{equip} = 12000 \text{ W} = 12.0 \text{ kW}$$

### 3.3.3 Occupant Sensible Heat

Sensible heat from people is per-person, scaled by population:

$$Q_{occ,s} = n_{occ} \cdot q_{s,person}$$

$$Q_{occ,s} = 40 \cdot 75 = 3000 \text{ W} = 3.0 \text{ kW}$$

### 3.3.4 Total Internal Sensible

$$Q_{int,s} = 5.0 + 12.0 + 3.0 = 20.0 \text{ kW} = 20000 \text{ W}$$

---

## 3.4 Step 3 — Total Room Sensible Heat (RSH)

$$Q_{RSH} = Q_{fabric,total} + Q_{int,s}$$

$$Q_{RSH} = 2817.6 + 20000 = 22817.6 \text{ W} \approx 22.82 \text{ kW}$$

---

## 3.5 Step 4 — Room Latent Heat (RLH)

Only occupants contribute latent load (assume process has no moisture):

$$Q_{RLH} = n_{occ} \cdot q_{l,person}$$

$$Q_{RLH} = 40 \cdot 55 = 2200 \text{ W} = 2.2 \text{ kW}$$

---

## 3.6 Step 5 — Total Room Heat

$$Q_{total,room} = Q_{RSH} + Q_{RLH}$$

$$Q_{total,room} = 22.82 + 2.20 = 25.02 \text{ kW}$$

---

## 3.7 Step 6 — Outside Air (Ventilation) Load

Convert CFM to m³/s:

$$\dot{V}_{OA} = 600 \text{ CFM} \cdot 0.0004719 \frac{\text{m}^3/\text{s}}{\text{CFM}}$$

$$\dot{V}_{OA} = 0.2832 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Mass flow at standard density 1.2 kg/m³:

$$\dot{m}_{OA} = \rho \cdot \dot{V}_{OA} = 1.2 \cdot 0.2832 = 0.3398 \text{ kg/s}$$

### 3.7.1 Outside-Air Sensible Heat

$$Q_{OA,s} = \dot{m}_{OA} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{OA} - T_{room})$$

$$Q_{OA,s} = 0.3398 \cdot 1.02 \cdot (35 - 24)$$

$$Q_{OA,s} = 0.3398 \cdot 1.02 = 0.3466$$

$$Q_{OA,s} = 0.3466 \cdot 11 = 3.813 \text{ kW}$$

### 3.7.2 Outside-Air Latent Heat

First find humidity ratios at the two states using standard psychrometric relations (approximated from chart):

- OA at 35 °C DB / 28 °C WB → $\omega_{OA} \approx 0.0200$ kg/kg
- Room at 24 °C DB / 50 % RH → $\omega_{room} \approx 0.0094$ kg/kg

$$Q_{OA,l} = \dot{m}_{OA} \cdot h_{fg} \cdot (\omega_{OA} - \omega_{room})$$

$$Q_{OA,l} = 0.3398 \cdot 2501 \cdot (0.0200 - 0.0094)$$

$$Q_{OA,l} = 0.3398 \cdot 2501 = 849.84$$

$$Q_{OA,l} = 849.84 \cdot 0.0106 = 9.008 \text{ kW}$$

### 3.7.3 Total Outside Air Load

$$Q_{OA,total} = Q_{OA,s} + Q_{OA,l} = 3.813 + 9.008 = 12.82 \text{ kW}$$

---

## 3.8 Step 7 — Grand Total Cooling Load

$$Q_{grand} = Q_{total,room} + Q_{OA,total}$$

$$Q_{grand} = 25.02 + 12.82 = 37.84 \text{ kW}$$

---

## 3.9 Step 8 — Sensible Heat Factor (SHF)

$$\text{SHF} = \frac{Q_{RSH}}{Q_{grand}}$$

> **Important:** SHF is calculated using **Room Sensible Heat only** divided by **Grand Total**, NOT using OA-included total. This is the KTU textbook convention.

$$\text{SHF} = \frac{22.82}{37.84} = 0.603$$

---

## 3.10 Step 9 — Convert to Tons of Refrigeration

$$\text{TR} = \frac{Q_{grand}}{3.517}$$

$$\text{TR} = \frac{37.84}{3.517} = 10.76 \text{ TR}$$

With a standard **20 % safety factor** (for future expansion and ASHRAE recommendation):

$$\text{TR}_{design} = 1.20 \cdot 10.76 \approx 12.91 \text{ TR} \approx 13 \text{ TR}$$

---

## 3.11 Analytical Matrix Summary Table

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fabric Sensible Heat | Q<sub>fabric</sub> | 2817.6 | W |
| Internal Sensible Heat | Q<sub>int,s</sub> | 20 000 | W |
| Room Sensible Heat | Q<sub>RSH</sub> | 22 817.6 | W |
| Room Latent Heat | Q<sub>RLH</sub> | 2 200 | W |
| Room Total Heat | Q<sub>total,room</sub> | 25 017.6 | W |
| OA Sensible | Q<sub>OA,s</sub> | 3 813 | W |
| OA Latent | Q<sub>OA,l</sub> | 9 008 | W |
| Grand Total Heat | Q<sub>grand</sub> | 37 838.6 | W |
| Sensible Heat Factor | SHF | 0.603 | — |
| Required Tonnage | TR | 10.76 | TR |
| Design Tonnage (+20 %) | TR<sub>design</sub> | 12.91 | TR |

---

## 3.12 Control-Loop Transfer Function Insight (Bonus)

For the closed-loop temperature control of the chilled-water valve, the simplified first-order plus dead-time (FOPDT) model is:

$$G_p(s) = \frac{K_p \cdot e^{-\theta s}}{\tau s + 1}$$

The standard Ziegler–Nichols PID tuning formulas are:

$$K_c = \frac{1.2 \cdot \tau}{K_p \cdot \theta}$$

$$T_i = 2 \cdot \theta$$

$$T_d = 0.5 \cdot \theta$$

Where:
- $K_p$ = process gain (°C/% valve)
- $\tau$ = process time constant (s)
- $\theta$ = dead time (s)
- $K_c$ = controller gain
- $T_i$ = integral time (s)
- $T_d$ = derivative time (s)

This is the heart of the **DDC (Direct Digital Control)** strategy used in modern BMS platforms like Honeywell EBI, Schneider EBO, Siemens Desigo CC, Johnson Controls Metasys.

> [!TIP]
> **Why Cascade?** The inner supply-air-temperature loop rejects disturbances (chilled-water temperature swings, fan speed changes) **before** they reach the room. This is why cascade control is the **industry standard** for large AHUs in KTU-grade industrial plants.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Define the term "HVAC". With a neat block diagram, explain the major sub-systems of a typical industrial HVAC plant.**

**Model Answer (10 lines):**

> HVAC stands for **Heating, Ventilation, and Air-Conditioning**. It is an integrated engineering system that controls **temperature, humidity, air purity, and air motion** inside a building or industrial process space to provide thermal comfort and process stability.
>
> The major sub-systems are:
>
> 1. **Heating subsystem** — Boiler, hot-water/steam piping, heating coils, modulating valves.
> 2. **Ventilation subsystem** — Fans (SA, RA, EA), ductwork, dampers, filtration stages.
> 3. **Air-conditioning subsystem** — Chiller, chilled-water pumps, cooling coils, humidifiers, dehumidifiers.
> 4. **Control subsystem** — Sensors (RTD, capacitive RH, NDIR CO₂), PID/DDC controllers, actuators.
>
> All four sub-systems work together to maintain the desired indoor environmental conditions as per **ASHRAE Standards 55 and 62.1**.

*(Diagram: Refer to Section 4.1 above.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the terms (i) Sensible Heat Factor (SHF) and (ii) Bypass Factor (BF) as applied to cooling-coil performance.**

**Model Answer:**

> **(i) Sensible Heat Factor (SHF):** SHF is the ratio of **sensible heat load** to the **total heat load** (sensible + latent) of a conditioned space. It determines the slope of the room process line on the psychrometric chart.
>
> $$\text{SHF} = \frac{Q_s}{Q_s + Q_l}$$
>
> A high SHF (≈ 1) indicates predominantly sensible cooling; a low SHF (≈ 0.5) indicates strong latent (dehumidification) requirement.
>
> **(ii) Bypass Factor (BF):** BF is the ratio of the **air that exits the cooling coil without undergoing any temperature change** to the **total air entering the coil**. It arises because cooling-coil fins cannot bring all the air into perfect contact with the cold surface.
>
> $$\text{BF} = \frac{T_2 - T_{ADP}}{T_1 - T_{ADP}}$$
>
> where $T_1$ = entering air DB, $T_2$ = leaving air DB, $T_{ADP}$ = apparatus dew-point temperature. A **lower BF** indicates a **more efficient coil** (closer to ideal).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has internal choice. **You must answer EITHER (a) OR (b), not both.** The 14 marks are split as **7 marks for sub-part (i)** and **7 marks for sub-part (ii)**.

---

### QUESTION A — **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **complete instrumentation and control scheme** for a **central Air-Handling Unit (AHU)** of a large pharmaceutical plant. Identify **all field sensors, controllers, final control elements**, and explain the **cascade control strategy** used for temperature regulation. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A cleanroom of dimensions 15 m × 10 m × 3.5 m must maintain 22 °C ± 1 °C and 55 % ± 5 % RH. The room houses 25 technicians and 30 kW of process equipment with motors (assume 75 % motor loading). Lighting load = 4 kW. Outside air is supplied at 800 CFM at 32 °C DB / 24 °C WB. Return air is at 24 °C DB / 16 °C WB.
*Assume:* U × A × CLTD for walls = 1500 W, for roof = 1800 W. Glass area negligible. Sensible heat per occupant = 80 W, latent = 60 W.
**Calculate:** (i) Room Sensible Heat, (ii) Room Latent Heat, (iii) Outside-air Sensible + Latent Heat, (iv) Grand Total Heat, (v) Sensible Heat Factor, (vi) Required tonnage (with 10 % safety factor). *(7 marks)*

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION A

#### Part (a) — AHU Instrumentation & Cascade Control

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    OA[Outside Air] --> DMP1[OA Motorised Damper]
    DMP1 --> MIX[Mixing Plenum]
    RA[Return Air] --> DMP2[RA Damper]
    DMP2 --> MIX
    MIX --> PFILT[Pre Filter]
    PFILT --> FFILT[Fine Filter]
    FFILT --> CC[Cooling Coil]
    CC --> HC[Heating Coil]
    HC --> HUM[Steam Humidifier]
    HUM --> FAN[Supply Fan VFD]
    FAN --> HEPA[HEPA H14]
    HEPA --> RM[Pharma Cleanroom]
    RM --> RA

    RM -.Temp Sensor PT100.-> TIC1
    TIC1{Master PID Room Temp} --> TIC2
    SA[Supply Duct Temp] -.PT100.-> TIC2
    TIC2{Slave PID SA Temp} --> CHV[Chilled Water Modulating Valve]
    CHV --> CC

    RM -.RH Sensor.-> HIC
    HIC{Humidity PID} --> HMV[Steam Valve]
    HMV --> HUM

    RM -.DP Sensor.-> PIC
    PIC{Pressure PID} --> DMP1
    PIC --> DMP2

    style TIC1 fill:#FFCCCC
    style TIC2 fill:#FFCCCC
    style HIC fill:#FFCCCC
    style PIC fill:#FFCCCC
```

**Explanation (7 marks distribution):**

1. **Sensors** (2 marks):
   - Room Temperature → PT-100 Class A RTD in thermowell.
   - Supply Air Temperature → PT-100 in duct.
   - Room Humidity → Capacitive polymer (e.g., Vaisala HMP110).
   - Differential Pressure → Electronic manometer (Setra 264).
   - CO₂ → NDIR sensor (e.g., Honeywell C7232).

2. **Controllers** (2 marks):
   - Master loop PIC/TIC: room temperature → cascade setpoint to slave.
   - Slave loop: supply-air temperature → chilled-water valve.
   - Independent humidity loop.
   - Pressure loop for damper modulation.

3. **Final Control Elements** (1.5 marks):
   - 3-way chilled-water valve with pneumatic or electric actuator.
   - Motorised opposed-blade dampers (Belimo or Honeywell make).
   - Steam solenoid valve for humidification.

4. **Cascade Strategy** (1.5 marks):
   - Master PID acts on room-temperature error and outputs a **varying setpoint** (typically 12–14 °C for supply air).
   - Slave PID controls the supply-air temperature by manipulating the chilled-water valve.
   - Slave rejects disturbances faster (chiller temp swings, fan speed changes).
   - Result: ± 0.3 °C room stability achievable.

---

#### Part (b) — Cooling Load Numerical

**Step 1 — Room Sensible Heat**

$$Q_{RSH} = Q_{walls} + Q_{roof} + Q_{lights} + Q_{equip} + Q_{occ,s}$$

Equipment with motor: 75 % of 30 kW = 22.5 kW becomes sensible heat in the room.

$$Q_{RSH} = 1500 + 1800 + 4000 + 22500 + (25 \cdot 80)$$

$$Q_{RSH} = 1500 + 1800 + 4000 + 22500 + 2000 = 31800 \text{ W} = 31.8 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Room Latent Heat**

$$Q_{RLH} = n_{occ} \cdot q_{l,person}$$

$$Q_{RLH} = 25 \cdot 60 = 1500 \text{ W} = 1.5 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Convert OA Flow to SI**

$$\dot{V}_{OA} = 800 \cdot 0.0004719 = 0.3775 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\dot{m}_{OA} = 1.2 \cdot 0.3775 = 0.453 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 4 — OA Sensible Heat**

$$Q_{OA,s} = \dot{m}_{OA} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{OA} - T_{room})$$

$$Q_{OA,s} = 0.453 \cdot 1.02 \cdot (32 - 22) = 0.462 \cdot 10 = 4.62 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 — OA Latent Heat**

Humidity ratios (from psychrometric chart):
- OA at 32 °C DB / 24 °C WB → $\omega_{OA} \approx 0.0173$ kg/kg
- Room at 24 °C DB / 55 % RH → $\omega_{room} \approx 0.0105$ kg/kg

$$Q_{OA,l} = 0.453 \cdot 2501 \cdot (0.0173 - 0.0105)$$

$$Q_{OA,l} = 0.453 \cdot 2501 = 1132.95$$

$$Q_{OA,l} = 1132.95 \cdot 0.0068 = 7.70 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6 — Grand Total Heat**

$$Q_{grand} = Q_{RSH} + Q_{RLH} + Q_{OA,s} + Q_{OA,l}$$

$$Q_{grand} = 31.8 + 1.5 + 4.62 + 7.70 = 45.62 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 7 — Sensible Heat Factor**

$$\text{SHF} = \frac{Q_{RSH}}{Q_{grand}} = \frac{31.8}{45.62} = 0.697$$

**Step 8 — Required Tonnage**

$$\text{TR}_{actual} = \frac{45.62}{3.517} = 12.97 \text{ TR}$$

Apply 10 % safety factor:

$$\text{TR}_{design} = 1.10 \cdot 12.97 = 14.27 \text{ TR}$$

**Recommended standard size: 15 TR chiller.**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Room Sensible Heat | 31.80 kW |
| Room Latent Heat | 1.50 kW |
| OA Sensible | 4.62 kW |
| OA Latent | 7.70 kW |
| Grand Total | 45.62 kW |
| SHF | 0.697 |
| Actual TR | 12.97 |
| Design TR (×1.10) | 14.27 ≈ 15 TR |

### QUESTION B — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4 Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **psychrometric processes** involved in:
*(i) Sensible heating, (ii) Sensible cooling, (iii) Cooling with dehumidification, (iv) Heating with humidification.*
Represent each on a psychrometric chart and identify the equipment used. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a **neat block diagram**, explain the **DDC (Direct Digital Control) architecture** used in a modern Building Management System (BMS) for a high-rise HVAC installation. Discuss the **BACnet/Modbus communication protocols** and the role of **variable frequency drives (VFDs)** in achieving **30–50 % energy savings**. *(7 marks)*

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION B

#### Part (a) — Psychrometric Processes

A psychrometric chart plots **dry-bulb temperature (X-axis)** vs **humidity ratio or wet-bulb temperature (Y-axis)**. Each HVAC process is a straight line.

**(i) Sensible Heating:** Air temperature rises, humidity ratio remains constant.
- Path: horizontal line moving **rightward** on the chart.
- Equipment: Heating coil (hot water / steam / electric).
- Examples: Winter pre-heating of outdoor air, reheat after cooling coil.

**(ii) Sensible Cooling:** Air temperature drops, humidity ratio remains constant (no moisture removed).
- Path: horizontal line moving **leftward**.
- Equipment: Dry cooling coil where surface temperature is **above dew-point**.
- Used when latent load is negligible (winter, very dry climate).

**(iii) Cooling + Dehumidification:** Air is cooled below dew-point; moisture condenses on coil.
- Path: line moving **leftward and downward** (toward saturation curve).
- Equipment: Chilled-water cooling coil with surface temperature **below dew-point**.
- The line connects entering air state to a point on the saturation curve at ADP.

**(iv) Heating + Humidification:** Air temperature rises **and** moisture is added.
- Path: line moving **rightward and upward**.
- Equipment: Heating coil + steam humidifier / wetted media.
- Typical in winter for occupied office spaces.

**Quick Table:**

| Process | T Change | ω Change | Equipment |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sensible Heating | ↑ | constant | Heating coil |
| Sensible Cooling | ↓ | constant | Dry coil |
| Cool + Dehumidify | ↓ | ↓ | Chilled coil (ADP < Tdp) |
| Heat + Humidify | ↑ | ↑ | Coil + humidifier |
| Adiabatic Saturation | ≈ const | ↑ | Spray washer |

---

#### Part (b) — DDC Architecture & Energy Savings

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Level 1 Field Devices Sensors and Actuators] --> L2
    L2[Level 2 DDC Controllers BACnet IP] --> L3
    L3[Level 3 Network Supervisor Niagara AX] --> L4
    L4[Level 4 Enterprise Server BMS Workstation] --> L5
    L5[Level 5 Cloud Analytics and Mobile App]

    L2 -.Modbus RTU.-> L2a[Chiller Plant Controller]
    L2 -.BACnet MS-TP.-> L2b[VAV Box Controllers]
    L2 -.LonWorks.-> L2c[Lighting and Shade Controller]

    L2 --> VFD1[Fan VFD]
    L2 --> VFD2[Chilled Water Pump VFD]
    L2 --> VFD3[Cooling Tower Fan VFD]

    style L1 fill:#C5E0B4
    style L2 fill:#9EC1CF
    style L3 fill:#FFD580
    style L4 fill:#FFCCCC
    style L5 fill:#D9D2E9
```

**Explanation (7 marks distribution):**

1. **Architecture Levels** (3 marks):
   - **Level 1 — Field devices:** PT100, RH sensors, CO₂ sensors, valve actuators, damper actuators.
   - **Level 2 — DDC Controllers:** Standalone microprocessor-based controllers (e.g., Honeywell Spyder, Schneider SE8000, Siemens RXC, Johnson FX-PCA). Execute PID locally.
   - **Level 3 — Network supervisor:** Niagara-based station aggregating data from multiple DDCs.
   - **Level 4 — BMS Server:** Long-term trending, alarms, energy dashboards.
   - **Level 5 — Cloud & mobile:** Remote monitoring, fault detection & diagnostics (FDD).

2. **Communication Protocols** (2 marks):
   - **BACnet (ASHRAE 135):** Open standard, OSI model, used for interoperability. Profiles: BACnet/IP (Ethernet), BACnet MS-TP (RS-485).
   - **Modbus (RTU/TCP):** Simple register-based protocol; widely used with VFDs and chillers.
   - **LonWorks:** Used in lighting + blind integration.
   - **KNX:** European building-automation standard.

3. **VFDs for Energy Savings** (2 marks):
   - **Affinity Law:** Fan/Pump power ∝ (speed)³.
   - Reducing fan speed by 20 % → power reduces to (0.8)³ = 51.2 % → **saves 48.8 %**.
   - Real-world HVAC retrofit studies (EPRI, ASHRAE) report **30–50 % plant energy savings** using VFD + static-pressure reset.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on HVAC questions — and how to avoid it:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 10–20 % safety factor.** Examiner awards 1 mark for design margin. Always multiply final TR by ≥ 1.10.
> 2. **Mixing up SHF formula.** SHF uses **Room Sensible Heat (RSH)** in the numerator — NOT total heat including OA. KTU graders check this carefully.
> 3. **Wrong unit conversions.** CFM × 0.0004719 = m³/s, NOT 0.0001. Memorise the exact constant.
> 4. **Ignoring latent heat of occupants.** Often students only do sensible load. Latent from occupants is a 2-marker question guaranteed in every KTU paper.
> 5. **Using 1.005 instead of 1.02 for $c_p$ of moist air.** In psychrometric calcs with humidity > 0.01 kg/kg, use **1.02 kJ/kg·K**.
> 6. **Not drawing the psychrometric chart path.** Even a hand-sketched process line gives 1–2 marks.
> 7. **Cascade loop without naming master/slave.** Examiners want explicit "Master PID outputs slave setpoint."
> 8. **Confusing ACH with CFM.** ACH = 60·V̇ / V_room. 1 CFM = 0.000472 m³/s.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Final 30-second revision before entering the exam hall:**

- **HVAC** = Heating + Ventilation + Air-Conditioning. Treat as one integrated MIMO plant.
- **Three Controlled Variables:** Temperature (°C), Humidity (% RH), Air Quality (ppm CO₂, PM).
- **Three Manipulated Variables:** Chilled-water valve, Hot-water/steam valve, Damper/Fan.
- **Four Load Components:** Fabric (walls/roof/glass), Internal (lights/equipment/occupants), Outside-air Sensible, Outside-air Latent.
- **Sensible Heat Formula:** $Q_s = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T$.
- **Latent Heat Formula:** $Q_l = \dot{m} h_{fg} \Delta \omega$.
- **Total Heat = SH + LH.**
- **SHF** = RSH / Total. Always use **Room Sensible** in numerator.
- **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 12 000 BTU/hr.**
- **1 CFM = 0.000472 m³/s ; 1 CMH = 0.5886 CFM.**
- **Cleanroom ACH = 20 to 600** depending on ISO class.
- **HEPA H13 = 99.97 % at 0.3 µm; ULPA U15 = 99.9995 %**.
- **ASHRAE 62.1** mandates **8.5 L/s per person** outside-air ventilation.
- **ASHRAE 55** defines thermal comfort (PMV between −0.5 and +0.5).
- **Cascade control** = Master (room T) + Slave (SA T). Always state explicitly in answers.
- **PID Tuning (ZN):** $K_c = 1.2\tau / (K_p \theta) ; T_i = 2\theta ; T_d = 0.5\theta$.
- **Affinity Law:** Power ∝ (speed)³ → 20 % speed reduction = ~50 % power saving.
- **BACnet / Modbus / LonWorks / KNX** are the four major BMS protocols.
- **Always** include **10–20 % safety factor** on the final TR.
- **Use $c_p = 1.02$ kJ/kg·K** for moist air (not 1.005).
- **Psychrometric chart** must be sketched whenever a process is described — even a rough line fetches 1–2 marks.
- **VFDs** + **economiser cycle** + **static-pressure reset** = the three pillars of modern HVAC energy savings (30–50 % reduction achievable).
